steps.explanation: ExtraTrees is **significantly faster** to train. Finding the optimal split requires sorting or histogram computation. Random thresholds skip this entirely - just pick a number and evaluate once!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Random Forest" RF1["For each feature:"] RF2["Sort values or
build histogram"] RF3["Try O(n) thresholds"] RF4["O(n × features)"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees" ET1["For each feature:"] ET2["Pick random number"] ET3["Evaluate once"] ET4["O(features)"] end RF4 --> SLOW["Slower"] ET4 --> FAST["Much Faster!"] style SLOW fill:#FFB6C1 style FAST fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Bias-Variance Trade-off
steps.explanation: ExtraTrees has **higher bias** (suboptimal splits) but **lower variance** (more randomization smooths predictions). For many problems, especially noisy data, this trade-off is favorable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Random Forest" RFB["Lower Bias
(optimal splits)"] RFV["Higher Variance
(less randomization)"] end subgraph "ExtraTrees" ETB["Higher Bias
(random splits)"] ETV["Lower Variance
(extreme randomization)"] end subgraph "When ExtraTrees Wins" W1["Noisy data"] W2["Risk of overfitting"] W3["Need fast training"] end style RFB fill:#90EE90 style RFV fill:#FFB6C1 style ETB fill:#FFFACD style ETV fill:#90EE90 style W1 fill:#ADD8E6 style W2 fill:#ADD8E6 style W3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Making Predictions
steps.explanation: Prediction works exactly like Random Forest: pass sample through all trees, collect predictions, use **majority vote** (classification) or **average** (regression). The ensemble mechanism is identical.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NEW["New Sample"] NEW --> T1["Tree 1: Class A"] NEW --> T2["Tree 2: Class B"] NEW --> T3["Tree 3: Class A"] NEW --> T4["Tree 4: Class A"] NEW --> T5["Tree 5: Class A"] T1 --> VOTE["Majority Vote"] T2 --> VOTE T3 --> VOTE T4 --> VOTE T5 --> VOTE VOTE --> PRED["Prediction: Class A
(4 out of 5 trees)"] style PRED fill:#90EE90

steps.title: No OOB Score by Default
steps.explanation: Since ExtraTrees uses all data (no bootstrap), there's no "out-of-bag" samples by default. To get OOB error, set **bootstrap=True**, but this makes it more like Random Forest.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "ExtraTrees (default)"
    ET1["bootstrap=False"]
    ET2["All samples used"]
    ET3["No OOB samples"]
    ET4["oob_score not available"]
    end
    subgraph "ExtraTrees (with bootstrap)"
    ETB1["bootstrap=True"]
    ETB2["~63% samples per tree"]
    ETB3["~37% OOB samples"]
    ETB4["oob_score available"]
    end
    ET1 --> ET2 --> ET3 --> ET4
    ETB1 --> ETB2 --> ETB3 --> ETB4
    style ET4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style ETB4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Importance
steps.explanation: ExtraTrees supports the same feature importance methods as Random Forest: **Gini importance** (mean decrease in impurity) and **permutation importance**. Interpretation is identical.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Feature Importance Methods" G["Gini Importance
feature_importances_"] P["Permutation Importance
sklearn.inspection"] end G --> R1["Sum impurity decrease
across all trees/splits"] P --> R2["Shuffle feature,
measure accuracy drop"] R1 --> IMP["Importance Ranking:
F5: 0.25
F2: 0.18
F8: 0.15
..."] R2 --> IMP style IMP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Side-by-Side: RF vs ExtraTrees
steps.explanation: Quick comparison of the two ensemble methods. Both are powerful; choice depends on your data and requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Random Forest"
    RF1["Bootstrap: Yes"]
    RF2["Split threshold: Optimal"]
    RF3["Training speed: Moderate"]
    RF4["Bias: Lower"]
    RF5["Variance: Higher"]
    RF6["OOB score: Yes"]
    end
    subgraph "ExtraTrees"
    ET1["Bootstrap: No (default)"]
    ET2["Split threshold: Random"]
    ET3["Training speed: Faster"]
    ET4["Bias: Higher"]
    ET5["Variance: Lower"]
    ET6["OOB score: No (default)"]
    end
    style RF2 fill:#FFFACD
    style ET2 fill:#90EE90
    style RF3 fill:#FFFACD
    style ET3 fill:#90EE90
    style RF5 fill:#FFB6C1
    style ET5 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use ExtraTrees
steps.explanation: Choose ExtraTrees when: (1) training speed is critical, (2) data is noisy, (3) you're overfitting with RF, (4) you have many features. Try both and compare validation scores!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{"Which to choose?"} Q -->|"Need fast training"| ET["ExtraTrees"] Q -->|"Noisy data"| ET Q -->|"Overfitting issues"| ET Q -->|"Need OOB score"| RF["Random Forest"] Q -->|"Interpretability matters"| RF Q -->|"Not sure"| BOTH["Try both!
Compare CV scores"] style ET fill:#90EE90 style RF fill:#ADD8E6 style BOTH fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Key Hyperparameters
steps.explanation: ExtraTrees shares most parameters with Random Forest. Key ones: **n_estimators**, **max_features**, **max_depth**, **min_samples_split**, **bootstrap** (False by default!).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Important Parameters" P1["n_estimators=100
Number of trees"] P2["max_features='sqrt'
Features per split"] P3["max_depth=None
Tree depth limit"] P4["min_samples_split=2
Min samples to split"] P5["bootstrap=False
Use all data (default)"] end subgraph "Tuning Tips" T1["More trees rarely hurts"] T2["Lower max_features =
more randomization"] T3["Set bootstrap=True
for OOB score"] end style P5 fill:#FFFACD style T3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: scikit-learn Implementation
steps.explanation: Using ExtraTrees is nearly identical to Random Forest. Just import **ExtraTreesClassifier** or **ExtraTreesRegressor** instead.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C1["from sklearn.ensemble import
ExtraTreesClassifier"] C2["et = ExtraTreesClassifier(
n_estimators=100,
max_features='sqrt',
n_jobs=-1,
random_state=42)"] C3["et.fit(X_train, y_train)"] C4["predictions = et.predict(X_test)"] C5["importance = et.feature_importances_"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 subgraph "For OOB Score" OOB["ExtraTreesClassifier(
bootstrap=True,
oob_score=True)"] end style C4 fill:#90EE90 style C5 fill:#FFFACD style OOB fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Decision Trees: Fundamentals
fields.description: Master the core concepts of decision trees: splits, impurity measures, tree structure, and prediction

steps.title: What is a Decision Tree?
steps.explanation: A **decision tree** is a flowchart-like structure where each internal node represents a test on a feature, each branch represents an outcome, and each leaf represents a prediction. It mimics human decision-making!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    ROOT["Is it raining?"]
    ROOT -->|Yes| L1["Take umbrella"]
    ROOT -->|No| R1["Is it sunny?"]
    R1 -->|Yes| L2["Wear sunglasses"]
    R1 -->|No| R2["Just go outside"]
    style ROOT fill:#E6E6FA
    style L1 fill:#90EE90
    style L2 fill:#90EE90
    style R2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Tree Anatomy
steps.explanation: A decision tree has: **Root node** (top, first split), **Internal nodes** (decision points), **Branches** (outcomes of tests), **Leaf nodes** (final predictions). Depth = longest path from root to leaf.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB ROOT["ROOT NODE
(First split)"] ROOT --> INT1["INTERNAL NODE
(Another split)"] ROOT --> LEAF1["LEAF NODE
(Prediction)"] INT1 --> LEAF2["LEAF NODE
(Prediction)"] INT1 --> LEAF3["LEAF NODE
(Prediction)"] style ROOT fill:#FFB6C1 style INT1 fill:#FFFACD style LEAF1 fill:#90EE90 style LEAF2 fill:#90EE90 style LEAF3 fill:#90EE90 NOTE["Depth = 2
Leaves = 3"]

steps.title: The Goal: Separate Classes
steps.explanation: For classification, the goal is to create **pure** leaf nodes where all samples belong to ONE class. We achieve this by finding splits that best separate the classes at each step.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before Split" MIXED["Mixed: 50 Red, 50 Blue"] end subgraph "After Good Split" LEFT["45 Red, 5 Blue
(mostly Red)"] RIGHT["5 Red, 45 Blue
(mostly Blue)"] end MIXED -->|"Feature X < 0.5"| LEFT MIXED -->|"Feature X >= 0.5"| RIGHT style MIXED fill:#E6E6FA style LEFT fill:#FFB6C1 style RIGHT fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Impurity: Measuring "Mixedness"
steps.explanation: **Impurity** measures how mixed the classes are in a node. Pure node (all same class) = 0 impurity. Evenly mixed = maximum impurity. We want splits that REDUCE impurity the most.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Pure Node" P["100% Class A
Impurity = 0"] end subgraph "Slightly Mixed" S["90% A, 10% B
Low Impurity"] end subgraph "Highly Mixed" H["50% A, 50% B
Max Impurity"] end style P fill:#90EE90 style S fill:#FFFACD style H fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Gini Impurity
steps.explanation: **Gini Impurity** = 1 - Σ(pᵢ²) where pᵢ is the proportion of class i. Range: 0 (pure) to 0.5 (binary) or higher (multiclass). Measures probability of misclassifying a random sample.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Gini Formula" F["Gini = 1 - Σ(pᵢ²)"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Pure: [100%, 0%]
Gini = 1 - (1² + 0²) = 0"] E2["Mixed: [50%, 50%]
Gini = 1 - (0.5² + 0.5²) = 0.5"] E3["Skewed: [90%, 10%]
Gini = 1 - (0.9² + 0.1²) = 0.18"] end style E1 fill:#90EE90 style E2 fill:#FFB6C1 style E3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Entropy (Information Gain)
steps.explanation: **Entropy** = -Σ(pᵢ × log₂(pᵢ)). Measures uncertainty/information content. Range: 0 (pure) to log₂(classes). **Information Gain** = entropy before split - weighted entropy after.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Entropy Formula" F["Entropy = -Σ(pᵢ × log₂(pᵢ))"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Pure: [100%, 0%]
Entropy = 0 bits"] E2["Mixed: [50%, 50%]
Entropy = 1 bit"] E3["Skewed: [90%, 10%]
Entropy ≈ 0.47 bits"] end style E1 fill:#90EE90 style E2 fill:#FFB6C1 style E3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Gini vs Entropy
steps.explanation: Both work well in practice. **Gini** is slightly faster (no log). **Entropy** is from information theory. Gini tends to isolate the most frequent class; Entropy produces more balanced trees.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Gini Impurity"
    G1["Faster computation"]
    G2["No logarithm needed"]
    G3["Default in scikit-learn"]
    G4["Favors larger partitions"]
    end
    subgraph "Entropy"
    E1["Information-theoretic"]
    E2["Slightly slower"]
    E3["More balanced splits"]
    E4["Used in ID3, C4.5"]
    end
    style G3 fill:#90EE90
    style E4 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Finding the Best Split
steps.explanation: For each feature, try different thresholds and calculate the **impurity reduction** (gain). The split with the HIGHEST gain wins. Repeat recursively for child nodes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    NODE["Node: 100 samples"]
    NODE --> TRY["Try all features & thresholds"]
    subgraph "Candidates"
    C1["F1 < 0.3: gain=0.15"]
    C2["F1 < 0.7: gain=0.22"]
    C3["F2 < 0.5: gain=0.31"]
    C4["F3 < 0.2: gain=0.18"]
    end
    TRY --> C1
    TRY --> C2
    TRY --> C3
    TRY --> C4
    C3 --> BEST["Winner: F2 < 0.5"]
    style C3 fill:#90EE90
    style BEST fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Regression Trees
steps.explanation: For regression, leaf nodes predict a **numeric value** (usually the mean of samples). Instead of impurity, we minimize **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) or **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Classification" CL["Leaf predicts CLASS
Majority vote"] CM["Minimize Gini/Entropy"] end subgraph "Regression" RL["Leaf predicts NUMBER
Mean of samples"] RM["Minimize MSE/MAE"] end subgraph "MSE Split" MSE["MSE = (1/n) Σ(yᵢ - ȳ)²
Find split that minimizes
weighted child MSE"] end style CL fill:#ADD8E6 style RL fill:#90EE90 style MSE fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Stopping Criteria
steps.explanation: Tree growth stops when: (1) **max_depth** reached, (2) node has fewer than **min_samples_split**, (3) leaf has fewer than **min_samples_leaf**, (4) node is **pure**, (5) no split improves impurity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    GROW["Growing tree..."]
    GROW --> CHECK{"Stop?"}
    CHECK -->|"max_depth reached"| STOP["Create leaf"]
    CHECK -->|"< min_samples_split"| STOP
    CHECK -->|"Pure node"| STOP
    CHECK -->|"No gain > 0"| STOP
    CHECK -->|"Otherwise"| CONT["Continue splitting"]
    CONT --> GROW
    style STOP fill:#90EE90
    style CONT fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Making Predictions
steps.explanation: To predict: start at root, evaluate the condition, follow the appropriate branch, repeat until reaching a leaf. The leaf's value is the prediction (class or number).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NEW["New sample:
F1=0.6, F2=0.3, F3=0.8"] NEW --> R["F2 < 0.5?
0.3 < 0.5? YES"] R -->|Yes| L1["F1 < 0.4?
0.6 < 0.4? NO"] R -.->|No| R1["..."] L1 -.->|Yes| L2["..."] L1 -->|No| LEAF["LEAF
Predict: Class A"] style NEW fill:#E6E6FA style LEAF fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Overfitting Problem
steps.explanation: Deep trees can **memorize** training data (overfit). A tree with one sample per leaf has 100% training accuracy but fails on new data. Solution: limit depth, prune, or use ensembles.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Overfit Tree"
    OF["Very deep"]
    OF1["1 sample per leaf"]
    OF2["100% train accuracy"]
    OF3["Poor test accuracy"]
    end
    subgraph "Good Tree"
    GD["Limited depth"]
    GD1["Many samples per leaf"]
    GD2["~95% train accuracy"]
    GD3["Good test accuracy"]
    end
    style OF3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style GD3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Pruning
steps.explanation: **Pruning** removes branches that don't improve generalization. **Pre-pruning**: stop early (max_depth, min_samples). **Post-pruning**: grow full tree, then remove unhelpful branches.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Pre-pruning (Early Stopping)"
    PRE1["Set max_depth=5"]
    PRE2["Set min_samples_leaf=10"]
    PRE3["Tree stops growing early"]
    end
    subgraph "Post-pruning"
    POST1["Grow full tree"]
    POST2["Evaluate each subtree"]
    POST3["Remove if no improvement"]
    POST4["Use validation set or CV"]
    end
    PRE1 --> PRE3
    PRE2 --> PRE3
    POST1 --> POST2 --> POST3
    style PRE3 fill:#90EE90
    style POST3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Strengths and Weaknesses
steps.explanation: Decision trees are interpretable and handle mixed data types, but they're prone to overfitting and are unstable (small data changes → different tree). Ensembles address weaknesses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Strengths"
    S1["Easy to interpret"]
    S2["Handles numeric & categorical"]
    S3["No feature scaling needed"]
    S4["Captures non-linear patterns"]
    S5["Fast prediction"]
    end
    subgraph "Weaknesses"
    W1["Prone to overfitting"]
    W2["Unstable (high variance)"]
    W3["Greedy, not globally optimal"]
    W4["Biased toward features with more levels"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S2 fill:#90EE90
    style S3 fill:#90EE90
    style W1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style W2 fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: CART: Classification and Regression Trees
fields.description: Deep dive into the CART algorithm used by scikit-learn, including binary splits, cost-complexity pruning, and implementation details

steps.title: What is CART?
steps.explanation: **CART** (Classification And Regression Trees) is a specific decision tree algorithm developed by Breiman et al. (1984). It's the algorithm used by scikit-learn's DecisionTreeClassifier and DecisionTreeRegressor.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Decision Tree Algorithms" ID3["ID3 (1986)
Categorical only
Multi-way splits"] C45["C4.5 (1993)
Handles continuous
Multi-way splits"] CART["CART (1984)
Numeric & categorical
BINARY splits only"] end CART --> SK["scikit-learn uses CART"] style CART fill:#90EE90 style SK fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: CART Key Feature: Binary Splits Only
steps.explanation: CART always creates **exactly two children** per split (binary tree). Even categorical features are split as "is X in {A,B}?" vs "is X in {C,D}?". This simplifies the algorithm and tree structure.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "CART (Binary)"
    CB["Color in {Red, Blue}?"]
    CB -->|Yes| CBL["Left child"]
    CB -->|No| CBR["Right child"]
    end
    subgraph "Other (Multi-way)"
    CM["Color = ?"]
    CM -->|Red| CMR["Child 1"]
    CM -->|Blue| CMB["Child 2"]
    CM -->|Green| CMG["Child 3"]
    end
    style CB fill:#90EE90
    style CM fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: CART for Classification
steps.explanation: For classification, CART uses **Gini impurity** by default (entropy is optional). At each node, it finds the binary split that maximizes the Gini gain (impurity reduction).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NODE["Node: 100 samples
60 Class A, 40 Class B
Gini = 0.48"] NODE --> SPLIT["Best split: X1 < 0.5"] SPLIT --> LEFT["Left: 50 samples
48 A, 2 B
Gini = 0.077"] SPLIT --> RIGHT["Right: 50 samples
12 A, 38 B
Gini = 0.355"] GAIN["Gini Gain = 0.48 - (0.5×0.077 + 0.5×0.355) = 0.264"] style NODE fill:#E6E6FA style LEFT fill:#ADD8E6 style RIGHT fill:#FFB6C1 style GAIN fill:#90EE90

steps.title: CART for Regression
steps.explanation: For regression, CART minimizes **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) by default. Each leaf predicts the **mean** of its samples. The split that minimizes weighted child MSE is chosen.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB NODE["Node: 100 samples
Mean y = 50
MSE = 400"] NODE --> SPLIT["Best split: X2 < 3.5"] SPLIT --> LEFT["Left: 40 samples
Mean y = 30
MSE = 100"] SPLIT --> RIGHT["Right: 60 samples
Mean y = 63
MSE = 150"] GAIN["MSE Reduction = 400 - (0.4×100 + 0.6×150) = 270"] style NODE fill:#E6E6FA style LEFT fill:#ADD8E6 style RIGHT fill:#90EE90 style GAIN fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Greedy Splitting Algorithm
steps.explanation: CART uses a **greedy** recursive algorithm: at each node, find the locally best split without looking ahead. This is fast but doesn't guarantee the globally optimal tree.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A["1. Start with all data at root"]
    B["2. For each feature:"]
    C["   Try all possible thresholds"]
    D["   Calculate Gini/MSE for each"]
    E["3. Select best feature + threshold"]
    F["4. Split node into two children"]
    G{"5. Stopping criteria met?"}
    H["6. Create leaf node"]
    I["7. Recurse on children"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H
    G -->|No| I
    I --> B
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Handling Continuous Features
steps.explanation: For a continuous feature with N unique values, CART tries N-1 possible thresholds (midpoints between sorted values). It picks the threshold with the best impurity reduction.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Feature X values" V["Sorted: [1.2, 2.5, 3.1, 4.8, 5.0]"] end subgraph "Candidate Thresholds" T1["X < 1.85"] T2["X < 2.80"] T3["X < 3.95"] T4["X < 4.90"] end V --> T1 V --> T2 V --> T3 V --> T4 T2 --> BEST["Best: X < 2.80
Gini gain = 0.35"] style BEST fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Handling Categorical Features
steps.explanation: For categorical features, CART tries all possible binary partitions of categories. With k categories, there are 2^(k-1) - 1 possible splits. For ordered targets, this can be optimized.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Categorical: Color = {R, G, B}"
    CAT["3 categories → 3 possible splits"]
    end
    subgraph "Possible Binary Splits"
    S1["{R} vs {G, B}"]
    S2["{G} vs {R, B}"]
    S3["{B} vs {R, G}"]
    end
    CAT --> S1
    CAT --> S2
    CAT --> S3
    S1 --> EVAL["Evaluate all, pick best"]
    S2 --> EVAL
    S3 --> EVAL
    style EVAL fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Cost-Complexity Pruning (CCP)
steps.explanation: CART's signature pruning method: **Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning**. It balances tree complexity against training error using parameter α (ccp_alpha). Higher α = simpler trees.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Cost-Complexity Formula"
    F["Rα(T) = R(T) + α × |T|"]
    F1["R(T) = training error"]
    F2["|T| = number of leaves"]
    F3["α = complexity penalty"]
    end
    subgraph "Effect of α"
    A0["α = 0: Full tree (no pruning)"]
    A1["α = 0.01: Some pruning"]
    A2["α = 0.1: Heavy pruning"]
    A3["α → ∞: Just root (1 leaf)"]
    end
    style A0 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A1 fill:#FFFACD
    style A2 fill:#ADD8E6
    style A3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: How CCP Works
steps.explanation: CCP works bottom-up: start with full tree, compute the "effective α" for each internal node, prune nodes with smallest α first. Repeat to get a sequence of trees, then pick best by cross-validation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB A["1. Grow full tree T_max"] B["2. For each internal node, compute:
α_eff = (error_leaf - error_subtree) / (leaves_subtree - 1)"] C["3. Prune node with smallest α_eff"] D["4. Repeat → sequence of trees"] E["5. Cross-validate to find best α"] F["6. Return pruned tree for that α"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F style E fill:#90EE90 style F fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Finding Optimal Alpha with CV
steps.explanation: Use **cross-validation** to find the best ccp_alpha. scikit-learn's `cost_complexity_pruning_path()` returns all α values and corresponding impurities. Plot and choose α with best CV score.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Process" P1["Get pruning path:
clf.cost_complexity_pruning_path(X, y)"] P2["Returns: ccp_alphas, impurities"] P3["For each alpha, train with CV"] P4["Plot alpha vs CV score"] P5["Pick alpha with best score
(or 1-SE rule)"] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 style P5 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: CART Leaf Predictions
steps.explanation: **Classification**: leaf predicts the majority class. Probabilities = class proportions in leaf. **Regression**: leaf predicts the mean (or median with MAE criterion).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Classification Leaf"
    CL["Samples: 45 A, 5 B"]
    CP["Prediction: Class A"]
    CPR["Probabilities: A=0.9, B=0.1"]
    end
    subgraph "Regression Leaf"
    RL["Samples: [10, 12, 15, 11, 14]"]
    RP["Prediction: mean = 12.4"]
    end
    CL --> CP --> CPR
    RL --> RP
    style CP fill:#90EE90
    style RP fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Feature Importance in CART
steps.explanation: CART computes feature importance as the **total impurity decrease** brought by each feature across all nodes, weighted by samples reaching each node, normalized to sum to 1.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Computing Importance" C1["For each node split on feature F:"] C2["Importance += (n_node/n_total) × Δimpurity"] C3["Sum across all nodes"] C4["Normalize so sum = 1"] end subgraph "Result" R["Feature Importances:
X1: 0.45
X2: 0.30
X3: 0.15
X4: 0.10"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> R style R fill:#90EE90

steps.title: CART in scikit-learn
steps.explanation: scikit-learn implements CART with additional hyperparameters for pre-pruning and CCP. Key parameters: criterion, max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, ccp_alpha.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Key Parameters" P1["criterion: 'gini'/'entropy'/'log_loss'
(classification)"] P2["criterion: 'squared_error'/'absolute_error'
(regression)"] P3["max_depth: int or None"] P4["min_samples_split: int (default=2)"] P5["min_samples_leaf: int (default=1)"] P6["ccp_alpha: float (default=0)"] end style P6 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: load data, train CART, prune with CCP, evaluate, and interpret with feature importances.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code Flow" C1["from sklearn.tree import
DecisionTreeClassifier"] C2["clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(
criterion='gini',
max_depth=10,
ccp_alpha=0.01)"] C3["clf.fit(X_train, y_train)"] C4["predictions = clf.predict(X_test)"] C5["proba = clf.predict_proba(X_test)"] C6["importance = clf.feature_importances_"] C7["from sklearn.tree import plot_tree
plot_tree(clf)"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 C3 --> C6 C3 --> C7 style C4 fill:#90EE90 style C7 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: CART vs Other Tree Algorithms
steps.explanation: CART's binary splits and Gini criterion make it simple and fast. ID3/C4.5 use entropy and allow multi-way splits. CHAID uses chi-square tests. Each has trade-offs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "CART"
    CART1["Binary splits only"]
    CART2["Gini (default) or Entropy"]
    CART3["Handles numeric & categorical"]
    CART4["Cost-complexity pruning"]
    end
    subgraph "C4.5"
    C451["Multi-way splits"]
    C452["Gain Ratio (normalized entropy)"]
    C453["Handles missing values"]
    C454["Error-based pruning"]
    end
    subgraph "CHAID"
    CH1["Multi-way splits"]
    CH2["Chi-square tests"]
    CH3["For categorical targets"]
    end
    style CART4 fill:#90EE90
    style C453 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Logistic Regression: Complete Guide
fields.description: Master logistic regression from sigmoid function to multi-class classification, including regularization and interpretation

steps.title: What is Logistic Regression?
steps.explanation: Despite its name, **Logistic Regression** is a **classification** algorithm. It predicts the probability of a binary outcome using a linear combination of features transformed by the sigmoid function.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Linear Regression" LR["Predicts continuous values
y = mx + b
Range: -∞ to +∞"] end subgraph "Logistic Regression" LOG["Predicts probabilities
P(y=1) = σ(mx + b)
Range: 0 to 1"] end LR --> |"Add sigmoid"| LOG style LOG fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Problem: Linear Output → Probability
steps.explanation: A linear model outputs any real number, but we need probabilities (0 to 1). We can't just clip values - we need a smooth transformation that maps (-∞, +∞) to (0, 1).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB LINEAR["Linear: z = w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + ... + b
Output: -∞ to +∞"] PROBLEM["Problem: z=5 means what probability?
z=-3 means what probability?"] SOLUTION["Solution: Transform with sigmoid
σ(z) squashes to (0, 1)"] LINEAR --> PROBLEM --> SOLUTION style PROBLEM fill:#FFB6C1 style SOLUTION fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function
steps.explanation: The **sigmoid function** σ(z) = 1 / (1 + e⁻ᶻ) transforms any real number to a value between 0 and 1. It's S-shaped: negative inputs → near 0, positive inputs → near 1, zero → exactly 0.5.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Sigmoid Function: σ(z) = 1 / (1 + e⁻ᶻ)"
    F["Input z → Output σ(z)"]
    end
    subgraph "Key Values"
    V1["z = -∞ → σ(z) ≈ 0"]
    V2["z = -2 → σ(z) ≈ 0.12"]
    V3["z = 0 → σ(z) = 0.5"]
    V4["z = +2 → σ(z) ≈ 0.88"]
    V5["z = +∞ → σ(z) ≈ 1"]
    end
    subgraph "Properties"
    P1["Always between 0 and 1"]
    P2["Smooth and differentiable"]
    P3["σ(-z) = 1 - σ(z)"]
    end
    style V3 fill:#FFFACD
    style P1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Complete Model
steps.explanation: Logistic regression combines linear combination with sigmoid: **P(y=1|X) = σ(wᵀX + b)**. The linear part z = wᵀX + b is called the **log-odds** or **logit**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR X["Features
X = [x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ]"] LINEAR["Linear combination
z = w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + ... + b"] SIGMOID["Sigmoid
σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ)"] PROB["Probability
P(y=1) ∈ (0,1)"] X --> LINEAR --> SIGMOID --> PROB style PROB fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Log-Odds (Logit) Interpretation
steps.explanation: The linear part z represents **log-odds**: z = log(P/(1-P)). This means odds = eᶻ. A coefficient wᵢ means: increasing xᵢ by 1 multiplies the odds by e^(wᵢ).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Transformations" P["Probability P"] ODDS["Odds = P / (1-P)"] LOGODDS["Log-odds = log(odds) = z"] end P -->|"P/(1-P)"| ODDS -->|"log()"| LOGODDS subgraph "Example: P = 0.75" E1["Odds = 0.75/0.25 = 3
(3:1 in favor)"] E2["Log-odds = log(3) ≈ 1.1"] end style LOGODDS fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Making Predictions
steps.explanation: Compute z = wᵀX + b, apply sigmoid to get probability P, then use a **threshold** (default 0.5) to classify. P ≥ 0.5 → Class 1, P < 0.5 → Class 0.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    X["Input: x₁=2, x₂=3"]
    Z["z = 0.5(2) + 0.8(3) - 1.5 = 1.9"]
    P["P = σ(1.9) = 1/(1+e⁻¹·⁹) ≈ 0.87"]
    T{"P ≥ 0.5?"}
    C1["Predict: Class 1"]
    C0["Predict: Class 0"]
    X --> Z --> P --> T
    T -->|"0.87 ≥ 0.5: Yes"| C1
    T -->|No| C0
    style C1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Decision Boundary
steps.explanation: The **decision boundary** is where P = 0.5, which means z = 0 (since σ(0) = 0.5). This is a **linear boundary**: w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + ... + b = 0 (a hyperplane).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Decision Boundary" DB["z = w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + b = 0
This is a straight line (2D)
or hyperplane (higher D)"] end subgraph "Classification Regions" R1["z > 0 → σ(z) > 0.5 → Class 1"] R2["z < 0 → σ(z) < 0.5 → Class 0"] R3["z = 0 → σ(z) = 0.5 → Boundary"] end style R1 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1 style R3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Training: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
steps.explanation: We find weights that **maximize the likelihood** of observing our training data. For each sample, the likelihood is P if y=1, or (1-P) if y=0. We maximize the product of all likelihoods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Likelihood for one sample" L1["If y=1: L = P(y=1) = σ(z)"] L2["If y=0: L = P(y=0) = 1 - σ(z)"] L3["Combined: L = σ(z)^y × (1-σ(z))^(1-y)"] end subgraph "Total Likelihood" TL["L_total = ∏ Lᵢ for all samples"] ML["Maximize L_total
(or minimize -log L_total)"] end L3 --> TL --> ML style ML fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Loss Function: Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)
steps.explanation: We minimize **cross-entropy loss** (negative log-likelihood): L = -[y·log(P) + (1-y)·log(1-P)]. This penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Cross-Entropy Loss" F["L = -[y·log(P) + (1-y)·log(1-P)]"] end subgraph "Intuition" I1["If y=1: L = -log(P)
Want P→1, so L→0"] I2["If y=0: L = -log(1-P)
Want P→0, so L→0"] I3["Wrong confident prediction
→ Very high loss"] end subgraph "Example" E1["y=1, P=0.9: L = -log(0.9) ≈ 0.1"] E2["y=1, P=0.1: L = -log(0.1) ≈ 2.3"] end style I3 fill:#FFB6C1 style E1 fill:#90EE90 style E2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Optimization: Gradient Descent
steps.explanation: Unlike linear regression, there's **no closed-form solution**. We use **gradient descent** (or variants like L-BFGS, Newton's method) to iteratively find optimal weights.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Gradient Descent"
    INIT["Initialize weights w, b"]
    COMPUTE["Compute predictions P = σ(Xw + b)"]
    LOSS["Compute loss L"]
    GRAD["Compute gradients ∂L/∂w, ∂L/∂b"]
    UPDATE["Update: w = w - α·∂L/∂w"]
    CHECK{"Converged?"}
    end
    INIT --> COMPUTE --> LOSS --> GRAD --> UPDATE --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|No| COMPUTE
    CHECK -->|Yes| DONE["Done!"]
    style DONE fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Regularization: Preventing Overfitting
steps.explanation: Regularization adds a penalty for large weights. **L2 (Ridge)** shrinks all weights. **L1 (Lasso)** can zero out weights (feature selection). **Elastic Net** combines both.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Regularized Loss" BASE["Base: Cross-entropy loss"] L2["L2 (Ridge): + λ·Σwᵢ²
Shrinks weights"] L1["L1 (Lasso): + λ·Σ|wᵢ|
Zeros out weights"] EN["Elastic Net: + λ₁·Σ|wᵢ| + λ₂·Σwᵢ²"] end subgraph "scikit-learn" SK["penalty='l2' (default)
penalty='l1'
penalty='elasticnet'
C = 1/λ (inverse regularization)"] end style L2 fill:#ADD8E6 style L1 fill:#90EE90 style SK fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multi-Class: One-vs-Rest (OvR)
steps.explanation: For K classes, train K binary classifiers: "Class k vs all others". To predict, run all K classifiers and pick the class with highest probability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "3-Class Problem"
    CLS["Classes: A, B, C"]
    end
    subgraph "Train 3 Binary Classifiers"
    M1["Model 1: A vs (B,C)"]
    M2["Model 2: B vs (A,C)"]
    M3["Model 3: C vs (A,B)"]
    end
    subgraph "Predict"
    P1["P(A) = 0.7"]
    P2["P(B) = 0.2"]
    P3["P(C) = 0.4"]
    PRED["Predict: Class A (highest)"]
    end
    CLS --> M1 & M2 & M3
    M1 --> P1
    M2 --> P2
    M3 --> P3
    P1 & P2 & P3 --> PRED
    style PRED fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Multi-Class: Softmax (Multinomial)
steps.explanation: **Softmax regression** trains one model with K sets of weights. The softmax function ensures probabilities sum to 1: P(k) = e^(zₖ) / Σe^(zⱼ). More efficient than OvR.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Softmax Function" F["P(class k) = e^(zₖ) / Σⱼ e^(zⱼ)"] end subgraph "Example: 3 classes" Z["z₁=2, z₂=1, z₃=0"] E["e²=7.4, e¹=2.7, e⁰=1.0
Sum = 11.1"] P["P₁=7.4/11.1=0.67
P₂=2.7/11.1=0.24
P₃=1.0/11.1=0.09"] SUM["Sum = 1.0 ✓"] end Z --> E --> P --> SUM style SUM fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Interpreting Coefficients
steps.explanation: Each coefficient wᵢ is the change in **log-odds** per unit increase in xᵢ. **Odds ratio** = e^(wᵢ) tells you how odds multiply. Positive w → increases probability of class 1.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Interpretation" W["Coefficient w₁ = 0.5"] LO["Log-odds increases by 0.5
when x₁ increases by 1"] OR["Odds ratio = e^0.5 ≈ 1.65
Odds multiply by 1.65"] end subgraph "Example" E1["w_age = 0.03
OR = 1.03"] E2["Each year older:
odds increase by 3%"] end W --> LO --> OR style OR fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Scaling Matters!
steps.explanation: Logistic regression is sensitive to feature scales. **Always scale features** (StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler) for faster convergence and meaningful coefficient comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without Scaling"
    WO1["Age: 0-100"]
    WO2["Income: 0-1,000,000"]
    WO3["Coefficients not comparable"]
    WO4["Slow convergence"]
    end
    subgraph "With StandardScaler"
    WS1["Age: mean=0, std=1"]
    WS2["Income: mean=0, std=1"]
    WS3["Coefficients comparable"]
    WS4["Fast convergence"]
    end
    style WO4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style WS4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Threshold Tuning
steps.explanation: Default threshold is 0.5, but you can adjust it. Lower threshold → more Class 1 predictions (higher recall). Higher threshold → more Class 0 predictions (higher precision).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Threshold Effects" T1["Threshold = 0.3
More Class 1 predictions
Higher recall, lower precision"] T2["Threshold = 0.5
Default balance"] T3["Threshold = 0.7
Fewer Class 1 predictions
Lower recall, higher precision"] end subgraph "Use Case" U1["Medical diagnosis: Low threshold
(don't miss positives)"] U2["Spam filter: High threshold
(don't block good email)"] end style U1 fill:#FFB6C1 style U2 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: scikit-learn Implementation
steps.explanation: Use LogisticRegression with key parameters: C (inverse regularization), penalty, solver, multi_class, max_iter. Access coefficients with coef_ and intercept_.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C1["from sklearn.linear_model import
LogisticRegression"] C2["lr = LogisticRegression(
C=1.0,
penalty='l2',
solver='lbfgs',
max_iter=100)"] C3["lr.fit(X_train, y_train)"] C4["predictions = lr.predict(X_test)"] C5["probabilities = lr.predict_proba(X_test)"] C6["coefficients = lr.coef_
intercept = lr.intercept_"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 C3 --> C6 style C4 fill:#90EE90 style C5 fill:#ADD8E6 style C6 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: When to Use Logistic Regression
steps.explanation: Logistic regression works well for linearly separable data, when interpretability matters, as a baseline model, and when you need probability outputs. It struggles with non-linear boundaries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good For"
    G1["Linear decision boundaries"]
    G2["Interpretable coefficients"]
    G3["Probability estimates"]
    G4["Baseline model"]
    G5["High-dimensional sparse data"]
    end
    subgraph "Not Good For"
    B1["Complex non-linear patterns"]
    B2["Feature interactions (need to add manually)"]
    B3["When more accuracy needed"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#90EE90
    style G2 fill:#90EE90
    style B1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style B3 fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: Feature Branch Workflow
fields.description: The standard Git workflow for team development: create branch, develop, open PR, merge

steps.title: The Feature Branch Workflow
steps.explanation: The most common Git workflow for teams: **main** branch is always deployable, all work happens on **feature branches**, changes are merged via **Pull Requests** after code review.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Feature Branch Workflow"
    A["1. Create branch"] --> B["2. Make commits"]
    B --> C["3. Push branch"]
    C --> D["4. Open PR"]
    D --> E["5. Code review"]
    E --> F["6. Merge to main"]
    end
    style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: Start from Updated Main
steps.explanation: Always start from the latest **main** branch. This ensures your feature branch has all recent changes and minimizes conflicts later.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before Starting"
    A["git checkout main"]
    B["git pull origin main"]
    C["Now you have latest code"]
    end
    A --> B --> C
    subgraph "Main Branch"
    M1["A"] --> M2["B"] --> M3["C"]
    M3 --> HEAD["HEAD"]
    end
    style C fill:#90EE90
    style HEAD fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Step 2: Create Feature Branch
steps.explanation: Create a descriptive branch name. Common conventions: **feature/**, **fix/**, **chore/**. The branch starts at the same commit as main.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "C" tag: "main"
    branch feature/user-auth
    checkout feature/user-auth
    commit id: "" tag: "HEAD"

steps.title: Step 3: Develop on Feature Branch
steps.explanation: Make commits on your feature branch. Commit often with clear messages. Your work is isolated - it doesn't affect main or other developers until merged.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "C" tag: "main"
    branch feature/user-auth
    checkout feature/user-auth
    commit id: "Add login form"
    commit id: "Add validation"
    commit id: "Add tests" tag: "HEAD"

steps.title: Meanwhile: Main May Move Ahead
steps.explanation: While you work, other developers may merge their PRs to main. This is normal! We'll handle it before merging. Your branch and main have **diverged**.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "C"
    branch feature/user-auth
    checkout feature/user-auth
    commit id: "D (your work)"
    commit id: "E (your work)"
    checkout main
    commit id: "F (teammate)" tag: "main"
    commit id: "G (teammate)"

steps.title: Step 4: Push Branch to Remote
steps.explanation: Push your feature branch to the remote repository. Use **-u** flag the first time to set up tracking. This backs up your work and enables PR creation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Local" L["feature/user-auth
commits D, E"] end subgraph "Remote (GitHub)" R["origin/feature/user-auth
commits D, E"] end L -->|"git push -u origin feature/user-auth"| R style R fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 5: Open Pull Request
steps.explanation: Create a **Pull Request** (PR) on GitHub/GitLab. Add a description of changes, link related issues, and request reviewers. The PR shows the diff between your branch and main.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pull Request" TITLE["Title: Add user authentication"] DESC["Description:
- Added login form
- Added validation
- Added tests"] DIFF["Files changed: 5
+200 -50 lines"] REV["Reviewers: @alice, @bob"] end subgraph "PR Status" S1["CI checks running..."] S2["Awaiting review"] end style TITLE fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Step 6: Code Review
steps.explanation: Teammates review your code, leave comments, and may request changes. Address feedback with new commits. The PR updates automatically when you push.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Review Process" R1["Reviewer comments:
'Consider using async here'"] R2["You make changes"] R3["Push new commit"] R4["Reviewer approves ✓"] end R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 subgraph "PR Updated" P["Original commits + fix commit"] end R3 --> P style R4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 7: Update Branch if Needed
steps.explanation: If main has new commits, update your branch before merging. Either **merge main into feature** or **rebase feature onto main**. This ensures clean integration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{"Main has new commits?"} Q -->|Yes| UPDATE["Update your branch"] Q -->|No| READY["Ready to merge"] UPDATE --> OPT1["Option 1: git merge main
(creates merge commit)"] UPDATE --> OPT2["Option 2: git rebase main
(linear history)"] OPT1 --> PUSH["git push"] OPT2 --> PUSHF["git push --force-with-lease"] PUSH --> READY PUSHF --> READY style READY fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 8: Merge the PR
steps.explanation: Once approved and CI passes, merge the PR. Common strategies: **Merge commit** (preserves history), **Squash and merge** (clean history), **Rebase and merge** (linear history).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Merge Strategies" M1["Merge commit
Preserves all commits
Shows branch history"] M2["Squash and merge
All commits → one commit
Clean main history"] M3["Rebase and merge
Replay commits on main
Linear history"] end M2 --> REC["Most common for
feature branches"] style M2 fill:#90EE90 style REC fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: After Merge: Clean Up
steps.explanation: After merging, **delete the feature branch** (GitHub can do this automatically). Update your local main and delete the local feature branch.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB A["PR merged!"] --> B["Delete remote branch
(GitHub button or CLI)"] B --> C["git checkout main"] C --> D["git pull origin main"] D --> E["git branch -d feature/user-auth"] E --> F["Ready for next feature!"] style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Final Result
steps.explanation: Your feature is now part of main! If you used squash merge, it appears as a single commit. The feature branch is gone, and main has moved forward.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "C"
    commit id: "F (teammate)"
    commit id: "G (teammate)"
    commit id: "Add user auth" tag: "main" type: HIGHLIGHT

fields.title: Keeping Your Feature Branch Updated
fields.description: How to update your feature branch when main has moved ahead: merge vs rebase approaches

steps.title: The Situation: Diverged Branches
steps.explanation: You're working on a feature branch while teammates merge changes to main. Your branch has **diverged** from main. Before merging your PR, you need to incorporate those main changes.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C (yours)"
    commit id: "D (yours)"
    checkout main
    commit id: "E (teammate)"
    commit id: "F (teammate)" tag: "main"

steps.title: Why Update Your Branch?
steps.explanation: Updating ensures: (1) Your code works with latest main, (2) Conflicts are resolved NOW, not during merge, (3) CI tests against current main state, (4) Reviewers see the final integrated code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Problems if you don't update"
    P1["Conflicts discovered at merge time"]
    P2["Your code may break with new main"]
    P3["CI passes but merge fails"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefits of updating"
    B1["Resolve conflicts early"]
    B2["Test against real main"]
    B3["Smooth merge"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style B3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Two Approaches: Merge vs Rebase
steps.explanation: You have two options: **Merge main into feature** (easier, preserves history) or **Rebase feature onto main** (cleaner history, rewrites commits). Both achieve the same result.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB DIVERGED["Diverged branches"] DIVERGED --> MERGE["Option 1: Merge"] DIVERGED --> REBASE["Option 2: Rebase"] MERGE --> M_RES["Creates merge commit
Preserves exact history
Easier for beginners"] REBASE --> R_RES["Rewrites your commits
Linear history
Cleaner but advanced"] style MERGE fill:#ADD8E6 style REBASE fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Option 1: Merge Main into Feature
steps.explanation: Fetch latest main, then merge it INTO your feature branch. This creates a **merge commit** that combines both histories. Your original commits stay unchanged.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Commands"
    C1["git checkout feature"]
    C2["git fetch origin"]
    C3["git merge origin/main"]
    C4["# Resolve conflicts if any"]
    C5["git push"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    style C3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Merge Result
steps.explanation: After merging main into feature, you have a **merge commit** (M) that combines both branches. Your commits C, D are unchanged. The feature branch now includes main's changes.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    branch feature
    checkout feature
    commit id: "C (yours)"
    commit id: "D (yours)"
    checkout main
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F" tag: "main"
    checkout feature
    merge main id: "M" tag: "feature"

steps.title: Option 2: Rebase Feature onto Main
steps.explanation: Fetch latest main, then rebase your feature branch ONTO main. This **replays** your commits on top of main, creating new commit hashes. Results in linear history.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Commands"
    C1["git checkout feature"]
    C2["git fetch origin"]
    C3["git rebase origin/main"]
    C4["# Resolve conflicts if any"]
    C5["git push --force-with-lease"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    style C3 fill:#90EE90
    style C5 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Rebase Result
steps.explanation: After rebasing, your commits C, D are **replayed** as C', D' on top of main. They have new hashes but same changes. The history is now linear - no merge commit needed.
steps.diagram_data:
gitGraph
    commit id: "A"
    commit id: "B"
    commit id: "E"
    commit id: "F" tag: "main"
    commit id: "C' (yours)" type: HIGHLIGHT
    commit id: "D' (yours)" tag: "feature" type: HIGHLIGHT

steps.title: Why --force-with-lease?
steps.explanation: After rebasing, your local commits have NEW hashes. You need **force push** to update the remote. Use **--force-with-lease** instead of --force - it's safer and fails if someone else pushed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Force Push Safety" F1["--force
Overwrites remote blindly
May lose teammate's commits"] F2["--force-with-lease
Checks remote first
Fails if remote changed"] end F1 --> DANGER["Dangerous!"] F2 --> SAFE["Safe choice"] style DANGER fill:#FFB6C1 style SAFE fill:#90EE90 style F2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Handling Conflicts
steps.explanation: Both merge and rebase may encounter conflicts if you and teammates edited the same code. You'll need to manually resolve these conflicts before completing the operation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "During Merge"
    M1["CONFLICT in file.py"]
    M2["Fix conflicts in editor"]
    M3["git add file.py"]
    M4["git commit"]
    end
    subgraph "During Rebase"
    R1["CONFLICT in file.py"]
    R2["Fix conflicts in editor"]
    R3["git add file.py"]
    R4["git rebase --continue"]
    end
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    style M4 fill:#90EE90
    style R4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Which Should You Use?
steps.explanation: Both are valid! **Merge** is simpler and preserves history. **Rebase** creates cleaner history but rewrites commits. Many teams have conventions - follow your team's practice.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q{"Which to use?"}
    Q -->|"Beginner / shared branch"| MERGE["Use Merge"]
    Q -->|"Clean history / solo branch"| REBASE["Use Rebase"]
    Q -->|"Team has convention"| FOLLOW["Follow team practice"]
    subgraph "Common Conventions"
    CONV1["Rebase before opening PR"]
    CONV2["Merge for long-running branches"]
    CONV3["Squash merge into main"]
    end
    style FOLLOW fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of commands for both approaches. Remember: after rebase, you must force push. After merge, regular push works.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Merge Approach" M["git fetch origin
git checkout feature
git merge origin/main
# resolve conflicts
git push"] end subgraph "Rebase Approach" R["git fetch origin
git checkout feature
git rebase origin/main
# resolve conflicts
git push --force-with-lease"] end style M fill:#ADD8E6 style R fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts
fields.description: Step-by-step guide to understanding and resolving Git merge conflicts

steps.title: What Causes Merge Conflicts?
steps.explanation: A **merge conflict** occurs when Git can't automatically merge changes because both branches modified the **same lines** in the same file, or one branch deleted a file the other modified.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Main Branch" M["Line 5: print('Hello')"] end subgraph "Feature Branch" F["Line 5: print('Hi there')"] end M --> CONFLICT["CONFLICT!
Same line, different changes
Git can't choose"] F --> CONFLICT style CONFLICT fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: When Conflicts DON'T Happen
steps.explanation: Git auto-merges when changes are in **different files** or **different parts of the same file**. Only overlapping changes cause conflicts. Most merges are automatic!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Auto-merge: Different files"
    A1["You: edit file_a.py"]
    A2["Teammate: edit file_b.py"]
    A3["No conflict!"]
    end
    subgraph "Auto-merge: Different lines"
    B1["You: edit lines 1-10"]
    B2["Teammate: edit lines 50-60"]
    B3["No conflict!"]
    end
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    style A3 fill:#90EE90
    style B3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Conflict Markers
steps.explanation: When a conflict occurs, Git marks the conflicting section in the file with special markers. You'll see **your changes** and **their changes** separated by markers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Conflict Markers in File"
    C1["<<<<<<< HEAD"]
    C2["print('Hello')  # Your version"]
    C3["======="]
    C4["print('Hi there')  # Their version"]
    C5[">>>>>>> feature-branch"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    style C1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style C3 fill:#FFFACD
    style C5 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Understanding the Markers
steps.explanation: **<<<<<<< HEAD**: Start of YOUR current branch's version. **=======**: Separator between versions. **>>>>>>> branch**: End of INCOMING branch's version. You need to resolve and remove all markers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Marker Meanings" M1["<<<<<<< HEAD
Start of YOUR changes
(current branch)"] M2["=======
Divider between
the two versions"] M3[">>>>>>> feature
End of THEIR changes
(incoming branch)"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style M2 fill:#FFFACD style M3 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 1: Identify Conflicted Files
steps.explanation: After a failed merge/rebase, use **git status** to see which files have conflicts. They'll be marked as "both modified" or "unmerged".
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "git status output"
    S1["Unmerged paths:"]
    S2["both modified: src/app.py"]
    S3["both modified: src/utils.py"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    subgraph "What to do"
    D1["Open each conflicted file"]
    D2["Find conflict markers"]
    D3["Resolve each conflict"]
    end
    S3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    style S2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S3 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 2: Open File and Find Conflicts
steps.explanation: Open each conflicted file in your editor. Search for **<<<<<<<** to find all conflicts. Most editors highlight conflicts with colors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "In Your Editor"
    E1["Line 10: def calculate():"]
    E2["Line 11: <<<<<<< HEAD"]
    E3["Line 12:     return x + y"]
    E4["Line 13: ======="]
    E5["Line 14:     return x * y"]
    E6["Line 15: >>>>>>> feature"]
    E7["Line 16: "]
    end
    E2 --> CONFLICT["Conflict here!"]
    style E2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style CONFLICT fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 3: Decide What to Keep
steps.explanation: You have four choices: (1) Keep YOUR version, (2) Keep THEIR version, (3) Keep BOTH, (4) Write something completely NEW. The right choice depends on what the code should do.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CONFLICT["Conflict: x+y vs x*y"] CONFLICT --> YOURS["Keep yours:
return x + y"] CONFLICT --> THEIRS["Keep theirs:
return x * y"] CONFLICT --> BOTH["Keep both:
return x + y, x * y"] CONFLICT --> NEW["Write new:
return x + y + z"] style YOURS fill:#ADD8E6 style THEIRS fill:#90EE90 style BOTH fill:#FFFACD style NEW fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Step 4: Edit the File
steps.explanation: Manually edit the file: remove ALL conflict markers and keep/write the correct code. The result should be valid, working code with NO markers remaining.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Before (with markers)"
    B1["<<<<<<< HEAD"]
    B2["return x + y"]
    B3["======="]
    B4["return x * y"]
    B5[">>>>>>> feature"]
    end
    subgraph "After (resolved)"
    A1["return x + y  # Kept your version"]
    end
    B1 --> A1
    style A1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 5: Stage the Resolved File
steps.explanation: After fixing ALL conflicts in a file, stage it with **git add**. This tells Git you've resolved the conflicts. Repeat for all conflicted files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A["Fix conflicts in src/app.py"]
    B["git add src/app.py"]
    C["Fix conflicts in src/utils.py"]
    D["git add src/utils.py"]
    E["All conflicts resolved!"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    style E fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 6: Complete the Merge/Rebase
steps.explanation: For **merge**: run **git commit** (Git provides a default merge message). For **rebase**: run **git rebase --continue**. The operation completes successfully.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "After Merge"
    M1["All files staged"]
    M2["git commit"]
    M3["Merge complete!"]
    end
    subgraph "After Rebase"
    R1["All files staged"]
    R2["git rebase --continue"]
    R3["May hit more conflicts..."]
    R4["Eventually: Rebase complete!"]
    end
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    style M3 fill:#90EE90
    style R4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Aborting: When to Give Up
steps.explanation: If conflicts are too complex or you made a mistake, you can **abort** and return to the state before the merge/rebase. Nothing is lost!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Abort Commands" A1["git merge --abort
Cancel merge, go back"] A2["git rebase --abort
Cancel rebase, go back"] end subgraph "When to Abort" W1["Conflicts too complex"] W2["Need to discuss with teammate"] W3["Made mistakes during resolution"] W4["Want to try different approach"] end W1 --> A1 W1 --> A2 style A1 fill:#FFFACD style A2 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Pro Tips: Preventing Conflicts
steps.explanation: Reduce conflicts by: (1) Pulling/rebasing frequently, (2) Keeping PRs small, (3) Communicating with teammates about file changes, (4) Using feature flags for big changes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Prevention Strategies" P1["Update branch daily
git pull --rebase origin main"] P2["Small, focused PRs
Fewer files = fewer conflicts"] P3["Communicate
'I'm refactoring auth.py'"] P4["Feature flags
Merge often, enable later"] end style P1 fill:#90EE90 style P2 fill:#90EE90 style P3 fill:#ADD8E6 style P4 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Quick Reference: Conflict Resolution
steps.explanation: Summary of the complete conflict resolution workflow for both merge and rebase scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Merge Conflict Resolution"
    M1["git merge feature"]
    M2["CONFLICT!"]
    M3["Edit files, remove markers"]
    M4["git add <files>"]
    M5["git commit"]
    M6["Done!"]
    end
    subgraph "Rebase Conflict Resolution"
    R1["git rebase main"]
    R2["CONFLICT!"]
    R3["Edit files, remove markers"]
    R4["git add <files>"]
    R5["git rebase --continue"]
    R6["Repeat if more conflicts..."]
    R7["Done!"]
    end
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7
    style M6 fill:#90EE90
    style R7 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Lasso vs Ridge Regression
fields.description: Understand L1 and L2 regularization, when to use each, and how they prevent overfitting through geometric and intuitive explanations.

steps.title: The Overfitting Problem
steps.explanation: When a model has too many features or high-degree polynomials, it can **overfit** the training data. The model learns noise instead of the true pattern, leading to poor generalization. **Regularization** adds a penalty term to prevent coefficients from growing too large.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Without Regularization" A["Training Data"] --> B["Model learns
every detail"] B --> C["❌ Overfits noise"] end subgraph "With Regularization" D["Training Data"] --> E["Model constrained
by penalty"] E --> F["✅ Generalizes well"] end style C fill:#FFB6C1 style F fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Linear Regression Loss Function
steps.explanation: Standard linear regression minimizes the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** between predictions and actual values. The goal is to find coefficients β that minimize this loss. However, without constraints, coefficients can become very large.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Loss["Loss = Σ(yᵢ - ŷᵢ)²"]
    Loss --> MSE["Mean Squared Error"]
    MSE --> Goal["Minimize prediction error"]
    Coef["Coefficients β₁, β₂, ... βₙ"]
    Coef --> Problem["Can grow unbounded!"]
    style Problem fill:#FFB6C1
    style Goal fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Ridge Regression (L2 Regularization)
steps.explanation: **Ridge regression** adds an L2 penalty: the sum of squared coefficients multiplied by λ (alpha). This shrinks coefficients toward zero but never exactly to zero. Good when you believe all features contribute somewhat.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Ridge["Ridge Loss = MSE + λ Σβᵢ²"] Ridge --> L2["L2 Penalty:
Sum of squared coefficients"] L2 --> Effect["Effect: Coefficients
shrink toward 0"] L2 --> Keep["But never exactly 0"] Lambda["λ (alpha) controls
penalty strength"] Lambda --> High["High λ → More shrinkage"] Lambda --> Low["Low λ → Less shrinkage"] style Ridge fill:#ADD8E6 style Effect fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Lasso Regression (L1 Regularization)
steps.explanation: **Lasso regression** adds an L1 penalty: the sum of absolute values of coefficients multiplied by λ. This can shrink coefficients exactly to zero, performing **automatic feature selection**. Useful when you suspect only some features matter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Lasso["Lasso Loss = MSE + λ Σ|βᵢ|"] Lasso --> L1["L1 Penalty:
Sum of absolute values"] L1 --> Effect["Effect: Some coefficients
become exactly 0"] L1 --> Select["Automatic feature selection!"] Lambda["λ (alpha) controls
penalty strength"] Lambda --> High["High λ → More zeros"] Lambda --> Low["Low λ → Fewer zeros"] style Lasso fill:#E6E6FA style Select fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Geometric Interpretation
steps.explanation: The constraint regions explain why L1 creates zeros. **Ridge** has a circular constraint (coefficients can trade off smoothly). **Lasso** has a diamond constraint with corners at the axes, making it likely the optimal solution hits a corner where one coefficient is exactly zero.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Ridge (L2)" R1["Circular
constraint region"] R2["Smooth trade-off
between β₁ and β₂"] R1 --> R2 end subgraph "Lasso (L1)" L1["Diamond
constraint region"] L2["Corners on axes
where βᵢ = 0"] L1 --> L2 end R2 --> RR["Both coefficients
usually non-zero"] L2 --> LR["Often hits corner
→ sparse solution"] style RR fill:#ADD8E6 style LR fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Choosing Alpha (λ)
steps.explanation: The regularization strength **alpha** (λ) is a hyperparameter. Too small → overfitting. Too large → underfitting. Use **cross-validation** to find the optimal value. Common approaches: GridSearchCV or LassoCV/RidgeCV which have built-in CV.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Alpha["Alpha (λ)"] Alpha --> Small["α ≈ 0
No regularization
→ Overfitting"] Alpha --> Medium["α optimal
Best generalization"] Alpha --> Large["α very large
All coefficients → 0
→ Underfitting"] CV["Cross-Validation"] --> Find["Find optimal α"] Find --> Medium style Small fill:#FFB6C1 style Medium fill:#90EE90 style Large fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: When to Use Which?
steps.explanation: **Use Ridge** when you believe all features are relevant and want to reduce their impact proportionally. **Use Lasso** when you suspect many features are irrelevant and want automatic selection. **Use Elastic Net** (combines both) when features are correlated.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Question["Which regularization?"] Question --> Q1{"Many irrelevant
features?"} Q1 --> |"Yes"| Lasso["Lasso (L1)
Feature selection"] Q1 --> |"No"| Q2{"Correlated
features?"} Q2 --> |"Yes"| Elastic["Elastic Net
L1 + L2"] Q2 --> |"No"| Ridge["Ridge (L2)
Shrink all"] style Lasso fill:#E6E6FA style Ridge fill:#ADD8E6 style Elastic fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: In **scikit-learn**, use `Ridge` and `Lasso` from `sklearn.linear_model`. The `alpha` parameter controls regularization strength. Use `RidgeCV` and `LassoCV` for automatic cross-validated alpha selection.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Ridge" R1["from sklearn.linear_model
import Ridge, RidgeCV"] R2["model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)"] R3["model.fit(X, y)"] R1 --> R2 --> R3 end subgraph "Lasso" L1["from sklearn.linear_model
import Lasso, LassoCV"] L2["model = Lasso(alpha=0.1)"] L3["model.fit(X, y)"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 end style R2 fill:#ADD8E6 style L2 fill:#E6E6FA

fields.title: K-Means Clustering
fields.description: Learn the K-means clustering algorithm step-by-step: initialization, assignment, update, and convergence. Includes how to choose K and when to use alternatives.

steps.title: What is Clustering?
steps.explanation: **Clustering** is an unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points together without labeled examples. **K-means** is one of the most popular algorithms: it partitions data into K clusters, where each point belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean (centroid).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Unsupervised Learning" Data["Unlabeled Data"] --> Algo["Clustering
Algorithm"] Algo --> Groups["Discovered Groups"] end Groups --> C1["Cluster 1"] Groups --> C2["Cluster 2"] Groups --> C3["Cluster 3"] style Data fill:#FFFACD style Groups fill:#90EE90

steps.title: K-Means Overview
steps.explanation: K-means works by iteratively: (1) assigning points to the nearest centroid, and (2) updating centroids to be the mean of assigned points. It repeats until centroids stop moving. You must specify **K** (number of clusters) in advance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Start["Start: Choose K"] --> Init["Initialize K centroids"] Init --> Assign["Assign each point
to nearest centroid"] Assign --> Update["Update centroids
= mean of points"] Update --> Check{"Centroids
moved?"} Check --> |"Yes"| Assign Check --> |"No"| Done["Done!"] style Start fill:#ADD8E6 style Done fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 1: Initialize Centroids
steps.explanation: Place K initial centroids. Common methods: **Random** (pick K random points), **K-means++** (smart initialization that spreads centroids apart). K-means++ usually converges faster and finds better solutions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Random Init" R1["Pick K points
randomly"] R2["May cluster
poorly"] R1 --> R2 end subgraph "K-means++ Init" K1["Pick 1st centroid
randomly"] K2["Pick next centroid
far from existing"] K3["Repeat until K"] K1 --> K2 --> K3 end R2 --> Bad["❌ Can converge
to local minimum"] K3 --> Good["✅ Better spread
faster convergence"] style Bad fill:#FFB6C1 style Good fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 2: Assign Points to Clusters
steps.explanation: For each data point, calculate the **distance** to every centroid (usually Euclidean distance). Assign the point to the cluster with the **nearest centroid**. This creates a Voronoi partition of the space.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Point["Data Point (x, y)"] Point --> D1["Distance to C₁"] Point --> D2["Distance to C₂"] Point --> D3["Distance to C₃"] D1 --> Compare["Find minimum"] D2 --> Compare D3 --> Compare Compare --> Assign["Assign to
nearest cluster"] Dist["Distance = √((x₁-x₂)² + (y₁-y₂)²)"] style Assign fill:#90EE90 style Dist fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 3: Update Centroids
steps.explanation: After all points are assigned, **recalculate each centroid** as the mean position of all points in that cluster. The centroid moves to the center of its assigned points.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Cluster 1" P1["Point A (1,2)"] P2["Point B (3,4)"] P3["Point C (2,3)"] end P1 --> Mean["New Centroid =
mean(A, B, C)"] P2 --> Mean P3 --> Mean Mean --> New["C₁ = (2, 3)"] Formula["Centroid = (Σxᵢ/n, Σyᵢ/n)"] style New fill:#ADD8E6 style Formula fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 4: Iterate Until Convergence
steps.explanation: Repeat assignment and update steps until **convergence**: when centroids no longer move (or move less than a threshold), or maximum iterations reached. Each iteration reduces the total within-cluster variance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR I1["Iteration 1
Centroids move a lot"] I2["Iteration 2
Centroids move less"] I3["Iteration 3
Small adjustments"] I4["Iteration N
No movement"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 I4 --> Conv["Converged!"] Metric["Inertia decreases
each iteration"] style I1 fill:#FFB6C1 style I4 fill:#90EE90 style Conv fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing K: Elbow Method
steps.explanation: How do you choose K? The **Elbow Method** plots inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) vs K. Look for the 'elbow' where adding more clusters gives diminishing returns. The **Silhouette Score** measures how similar points are to their own cluster vs others.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Elbow Method" E1["Plot Inertia vs K"] E2["Find the 'elbow'
where curve bends"] E3["That K is optimal"] E1 --> E2 --> E3 end subgraph "Silhouette Score" S1["Score from -1 to 1"] S2["Higher = better
cluster separation"] S3["Try multiple K,
pick highest score"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end style E3 fill:#90EE90 style S3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: K-Means Limitations
steps.explanation: K-means assumes **spherical clusters** of similar size. It struggles with elongated, irregular, or overlapping clusters. It's sensitive to **outliers** and **initialization**. For non-spherical clusters, consider DBSCAN or Gaussian Mixture Models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Limit["K-Means Limitations"] Limit --> L1["Assumes spherical
clusters"] Limit --> L2["Sensitive to
outliers"] Limit --> L3["Must specify K
in advance"] Limit --> L4["Can find local
minimum"] Alt["Alternatives"] Alt --> A1["DBSCAN
Any shape, auto K"] Alt --> A2["GMM
Elliptical clusters"] Alt --> A3["Hierarchical
Dendrogram"] style Limit fill:#FFB6C1 style Alt fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: In **scikit-learn**, use `KMeans` from `sklearn.cluster`. Key parameters: `n_clusters` (K), `init` ('k-means++' recommended), `n_init` (run multiple times, pick best). Access results via `labels_` and `cluster_centers_`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" I1["from sklearn.cluster
import KMeans"] I2["kmeans = KMeans(
n_clusters=3,
init='k-means++')"] I3["kmeans.fit(X)"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 end subgraph "Results" R1["kmeans.labels_
Cluster assignments"] R2["kmeans.cluster_centers_
Centroid positions"] R3["kmeans.inertia_
Within-cluster variance"] end I3 --> R1 I3 --> R2 I3 --> R3 style I2 fill:#ADD8E6 style R1 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: DBSCAN Clustering
fields.description: Master density-based clustering with DBSCAN: understand core/border/noise points, the algorithm steps, parameter tuning, and when to choose DBSCAN over K-means.

steps.title: What is DBSCAN?
steps.explanation: **DBSCAN** (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) groups points that are closely packed together. Unlike K-means, it can find **arbitrarily shaped clusters** and automatically identifies **outliers** as noise. No need to specify K!
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "K-Means"
        K1["Must specify K"]
        K2["Spherical clusters only"]
        K3["No outlier detection"]
    end
    subgraph "DBSCAN"
        D1["Finds K automatically"]
        D2["Any cluster shape"]
        D3["Labels outliers as noise"]
    end
    K1 --> |"vs"| D1
    K2 --> |"vs"| D2
    K3 --> |"vs"| D3
    style D1 fill:#90EE90
    style D2 fill:#90EE90
    style D3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Key Parameters: ε and MinPts
steps.explanation: DBSCAN has two parameters: **ε (epsilon)** defines the radius of a neighborhood around each point. **MinPts** is the minimum number of points required within ε to form a dense region. These define what 'density' means.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Params["DBSCAN Parameters"] Params --> Eps["ε (epsilon)
Neighborhood radius"] Params --> Min["MinPts
Minimum neighbors"] Eps --> EpsEx["Points within distance ε
are 'neighbors'"] Min --> MinEx["Need ≥ MinPts neighbors
to be 'dense'"] Example["Example: ε=0.5, MinPts=5
Need 5 points within 0.5 distance"] style Eps fill:#ADD8E6 style Min fill:#E6E6FA style Example fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Three Types of Points
steps.explanation: DBSCAN classifies every point as: **Core Point** (has ≥ MinPts neighbors within ε), **Border Point** (within ε of a core point but has < MinPts neighbors), or **Noise Point** (neither core nor border - an outlier).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Point["Each Point"] Point --> Check{"≥ MinPts
within ε?"} Check --> |"Yes"| Core["Core Point
Forms cluster center"] Check --> |"No"| Check2{"Within ε of
a core point?"} Check2 --> |"Yes"| Border["Border Point
Edge of cluster"] Check2 --> |"No"| Noise["Noise Point
Outlier"] style Core fill:#90EE90 style Border fill:#ADD8E6 style Noise fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Step 1: Find Core Points
steps.explanation: For each point, count how many other points are within distance ε. If count ≥ MinPts, mark it as a **core point**. Core points are the foundation of clusters.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Checking Point P" P["Point P"] P --> Count["Count neighbors
within ε radius"] Count --> C1["Found 7 neighbors"] C1 --> Check{"7 ≥ MinPts(5)?"} Check --> |"Yes"| Core["P is Core Point ✓"] end subgraph "Checking Point Q" Q["Point Q"] Q --> Count2["Count neighbors
within ε radius"] Count2 --> C2["Found 2 neighbors"] C2 --> Check2{"2 ≥ MinPts(5)?"} Check2 --> |"No"| NotCore["Q is NOT core"] end style Core fill:#90EE90 style NotCore fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Step 2: Connect Core Points
steps.explanation: Two core points that are within ε of each other belong to the **same cluster**. This creates chains of connected core points. The algorithm uses a graph traversal (like BFS/DFS) to find all connected components.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    C1["Core 1"] --> |"within ε"| C2["Core 2"]
    C2 --> |"within ε"| C3["Core 3"]
    C3 --> |"within ε"| C4["Core 4"]
    C1 --> Same["Same Cluster!"]
    C2 --> Same
    C3 --> Same
    C4 --> Same
    C5["Core 5"] --> |"far from others"| Diff["Different Cluster"]
    style Same fill:#90EE90
    style Diff fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Step 3: Assign Border & Noise
steps.explanation: Non-core points within ε of a core point become **border points** and join that cluster. Points not near any core point are labeled as **noise** (outliers). Border points can be near multiple clusters - assigned to the first found.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB NonCore["Non-Core Point"] NonCore --> Check{"Near a
core point?"} Check --> |"Yes, within ε"| Border["Border Point
Join that cluster"] Check --> |"No"| Noise["Noise Point
Label as -1"] Note["Border points belong
to cluster but don't
expand it"] style Border fill:#ADD8E6 style Noise fill:#FFB6C1 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The Complete Algorithm
steps.explanation: DBSCAN visits each point once. For unvisited core points, it starts a new cluster and recursively adds all density-reachable points. Time complexity is O(n²) naive, or O(n log n) with spatial indexing (like KD-tree).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Start["For each unvisited point P"] Start --> Mark["Mark P as visited"] Mark --> Find["Find neighbors within ε"] Find --> Check{"≥ MinPts?"} Check --> |"No"| Noise["Mark as Noise
(may change later)"] Check --> |"Yes"| New["Start new cluster"] New --> Expand["Add P to cluster"] Expand --> Process["Process each neighbor:
If core, add its neighbors too"] Process --> Expand style New fill:#90EE90 style Noise fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Choosing ε and MinPts
steps.explanation: **MinPts**: Rule of thumb is MinPts ≥ dimensions + 1 (often MinPts = 4 for 2D). **ε**: Use a **k-distance plot** - plot distance to k-th nearest neighbor (k=MinPts), sorted. Look for the 'elbow' where distances jump.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Choosing MinPts" M1["MinPts ≥ dim + 1"] M2["2D data → MinPts = 4"] M3["Higher MinPts
= smoother clusters"] M1 --> M2 --> M3 end subgraph "Choosing ε (k-distance plot)" E1["Compute distance to
k-th nearest neighbor"] E2["Sort distances,
plot ascending"] E3["Find 'elbow'
= good ε value"] E1 --> E2 --> E3 end style M2 fill:#ADD8E6 style E3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: DBSCAN Strengths & Weaknesses
steps.explanation: **Strengths**: Finds arbitrary shapes, handles outliers, no need to specify K. **Weaknesses**: Struggles with varying densities, sensitive to ε and MinPts, doesn't work well in high dimensions (curse of dimensionality).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Strengths ✓" S1["Any cluster shape"] S2["Automatic outlier detection"] S3["No need to specify K"] S4["Single pass, deterministic
(except border points)"] end subgraph "Weaknesses ✗" W1["Varying density clusters
need different ε"] W2["High-dimensional data
distances less meaningful"] W3["Parameter tuning
can be tricky"] end style S1 fill:#90EE90 style S2 fill:#90EE90 style S3 fill:#90EE90 style W1 fill:#FFB6C1 style W2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: In **scikit-learn**, use `DBSCAN` from `sklearn.cluster`. Key parameters: `eps` and `min_samples`. Noise points get label `-1`. For better performance on large datasets, use `algorithm='ball_tree'` or `'kd_tree'`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" I1["from sklearn.cluster
import DBSCAN"] I2["dbscan = DBSCAN(
eps=0.5,
min_samples=5)"] I3["labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X)"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 end subgraph "Results" R1["labels array
-1 = noise"] R2["n_clusters =
len(set(labels)) - 1"] R3["Core samples:
dbscan.core_sample_indices_"] end I3 --> R1 I3 --> R2 I3 --> R3 style I2 fill:#ADD8E6 style R1 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Gradient Descent
fields.description: Master the core optimization algorithm behind machine learning: understand the update rule, learning rates, batch vs stochastic vs mini-batch, momentum, and Adam optimizer.

steps.title: The Optimization Problem
steps.explanation: Machine learning models have parameters (weights) that need to be tuned. **Gradient Descent** is an optimization algorithm that finds the parameter values that minimize a **loss function**. Think of it as finding the lowest point in a valley.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Goal["Goal: Find parameters
that minimize loss"] Goal --> Loss["Loss Function L(θ)"] Loss --> Measure["Measures how wrong
predictions are"] Measure --> Find["Find θ where
L(θ) is minimum"] Example["Example: MSE, Cross-Entropy"] style Goal fill:#ADD8E6 style Find fill:#90EE90 style Example fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The Gradient: Which Way is Down?
steps.explanation: The **gradient** (∇L) is a vector of partial derivatives - it points in the direction of steepest *increase*. To minimize, we go the **opposite direction** (negative gradient). The gradient tells us both direction and steepness.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Grad["Gradient ∇L"] Grad --> Partial["Vector of partial
derivatives"] Partial --> Dir["Points toward
steepest INCREASE"] Dir --> Neg["Negative gradient
→ steepest DECREASE"] Neg --> Move["Move parameters
in this direction"] style Neg fill:#90EE90 style Move fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Update Rule
steps.explanation: Each iteration: compute gradient, then update parameters by subtracting the gradient times a **learning rate** (α). The learning rate controls step size. Formula: **θ_new = θ_old - α · ∇L(θ_old)**
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Rule["θ_new = θ_old - α · ∇L"] Rule --> Theta["θ = parameters
(weights)"] Rule --> Alpha["α = learning rate
(step size)"] Rule --> Gradient["∇L = gradient
(direction)"] Loop["Repeat until
convergence"] Rule --> Loop style Rule fill:#ADD8E6 style Alpha fill:#FFFACD style Loop fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Learning Rate: Too Big vs Too Small
steps.explanation: **Learning rate** is crucial. Too small → very slow convergence, may get stuck. Too large → overshoots minimum, may diverge! Finding the right balance is key. Common values: 0.001 to 0.1.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    LR["Learning Rate α"]
    LR --> Small["Too Small (0.0001)"]
    LR --> Right["Just Right (0.01)"]
    LR --> Big["Too Large (1.0)"]
    Small --> S1["✗ Very slow"]
    Small --> S2["✗ May never converge"]
    Right --> R1["✓ Steady progress"]
    Right --> R2["✓ Finds minimum"]
    Big --> B1["✗ Overshoots"]
    Big --> B2["✗ May diverge!"]
    style Small fill:#FFB6C1
    style Right fill:#90EE90
    style Big fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Batch Gradient Descent
steps.explanation: **Batch GD** computes the gradient using the **entire dataset** each iteration. Pros: stable, guaranteed convergence. Cons: slow for large datasets, requires all data in memory.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Batch Gradient Descent" All["All N samples"] All --> Compute["Compute gradient
over full dataset"] Compute --> Update["One update
per epoch"] end Pros["✓ Stable gradient
✓ Smooth convergence"] Cons["✗ Slow per update
✗ Memory intensive"] Update --> Pros Update --> Cons style All fill:#ADD8E6 style Cons fill:#FFB6C1 style Pros fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
steps.explanation: **SGD** computes gradient on a **single random sample** each iteration. Much faster updates, but noisy gradient. The noise can help escape local minima but causes erratic convergence.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Stochastic GD" One["1 random sample"] One --> Compute["Compute gradient
on single point"] Compute --> Update["Update immediately"] Update --> Next["Next sample..."] end Pros["✓ Fast updates
✓ Can escape local minima
✓ Online learning"] Cons["✗ Noisy gradient
✗ Erratic path"] Update --> Pros Update --> Cons style One fill:#E6E6FA style Pros fill:#90EE90 style Cons fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent
steps.explanation: **Mini-batch GD** is the practical sweet spot: compute gradient on a **small batch** (typically 32-256 samples). Balances speed and stability. This is what most deep learning uses.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mini-Batch GD" Batch["Batch of 32-256
samples"] Batch --> Compute["Compute gradient
on mini-batch"] Compute --> Update["Update weights"] Update --> Next["Next batch..."] end Best["✓ Fast (vectorized)
✓ Stable enough
✓ GPU efficient
✓ Most commonly used!"] Next --> Best style Batch fill:#90EE90 style Best fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Momentum: Build Up Speed
steps.explanation: **Momentum** adds a fraction of the previous update to the current one. Like a ball rolling downhill - it builds speed and can roll past small bumps (local minima). Typical momentum value: 0.9.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Mom["Momentum Update"] Mom --> V["v = β·v_prev + ∇L"] V --> Update["θ = θ - α·v"] Beta["β = momentum (0.9)
Remembers past direction"] V --> Beta Benefits["✓ Faster convergence
✓ Dampens oscillations
