steps.explanation: Use custom pyfunc when you need: **preprocessing** bundled with the model, **multiple models** in an ensemble, **business logic** in predictions, or **non-ML artifacts** (lookup tables, configs). The model becomes self-contained.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Custom["Use Custom PyFunc For"] Custom --> Pre["Preprocessing
Scaling, encoding bundled"] Custom --> Ensemble["Ensembles
Multiple models combined"] Custom --> Logic["Business Logic
Rules, thresholds"] Custom --> Artifacts["Extra Artifacts
Lookup tables, configs"] Result["Result: Self-contained
deployable unit"] Pre --> Result Ensemble --> Result Logic --> Result Artifacts --> Result style Custom fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Flavor Selection Summary
steps.explanation: **Decision guide**: Use built-in flavor for standard models. Use pyfunc loading for deployment. Use custom pyfunc for complex pipelines. Always check if a built-in flavor exists before writing custom code!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Q1{"Standard framework
model?"} Q1 --> |Yes| Builtin["Use built-in flavor
mlflow.sklearn, etc."] Q1 --> |No| Q2{"Need custom
predict logic?"} Q2 --> |Yes| CustomPF["Custom PyFunc
Subclass PythonModel"] Q2 --> |No| Generic["Generic PyFunc
mlflow.pyfunc.log_model"] Deploy{"For deployment?"} Builtin --> Deploy Deploy --> |Always| PyFuncLoad["Load as pyfunc"] style Builtin fill:#90EE90 style CustomPF fill:#ADD8E6 style PyFuncLoad fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: MLflow Model Signatures & Input Examples
fields.description: Master MLflow model signatures and input examples: schema definition, automatic inference, validation at serving time, and debugging deployment issues.

steps.title: Why Signatures Matter
steps.explanation: A **Model Signature** defines the expected input and output schema. Without it, you might deploy a model that crashes on unexpected data. Signatures enable **validation**, **documentation**, and **UI generation** in serving tools.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR NoSig["Without Signature"] NoSig --> Problem1["Wrong input shape?
Runtime crash"] NoSig --> Problem2["Wrong dtype?
Silent errors"] NoSig --> Problem3["Missing columns?
Confusion"] WithSig["With Signature"] WithSig --> Solution1["Validated before predict"] WithSig --> Solution2["Clear error messages"] WithSig --> Solution3["Self-documenting"] style NoSig fill:#FFB6C1 style WithSig fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Signature Components
steps.explanation: A signature has two parts: **inputs** (what the model expects) and **outputs** (what it returns). Each defines column names and data types. For tensor models, it specifies shape and dtype instead of columns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Sig["Model Signature"] Sig --> Inputs["Inputs Schema"] Sig --> Outputs["Outputs Schema"] Inputs --> Col1["Column: 'age'
Type: long"] Inputs --> Col2["Column: 'income'
Type: double"] Inputs --> Col3["Column: 'category'
Type: string"] Outputs --> Out1["Column: 'prediction'
Type: long"] Outputs --> Out2["Column: 'probability'
Type: double"] style Sig fill:#ADD8E6 style Inputs fill:#90EE90 style Outputs fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Automatic Signature Inference
steps.explanation: MLflow can **infer signatures automatically** from training data. Pass `signature=True` or use `mlflow.models.infer_signature(X, y_pred)`. This captures column names and types from your actual data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Option 1: Auto with autolog" A1["mlflow.autolog(
log_model_signatures=True)"] end subgraph "Option 2: Explicit inference" B1["from mlflow.models import infer_signature"] B2["sig = infer_signature(X_train, model.predict(X_train))"] B3["mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', signature=sig)"] B1 --> B2 --> B3 end Both["Both produce same result"] style A1 fill:#90EE90 style B2 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Manual Signature Definition
steps.explanation: For precise control, define signatures manually using `Schema` and `ColSpec`. Useful when inference doesn't capture everything (e.g., categorical constraints, custom types). Also needed for complex input/output structures.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Manual Definition" M1["from mlflow.models import ModelSignature"] M2["from mlflow.types.schema import Schema, ColSpec"] M3["input_schema = Schema([
ColSpec('double', 'age'),
ColSpec('string', 'city')
])"] M4["output_schema = Schema([
ColSpec('long', 'class')
])"] M5["sig = ModelSignature(inputs=input_schema,
outputs=output_schema)"] end M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 style M3 fill:#ADD8E6 style M5 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Supported Data Types
steps.explanation: MLflow signatures support standard types: **boolean**, **integer**, **long**, **float**, **double**, **string**, **binary**, **datetime**. For tensor models: numpy dtypes with shape specification. Arrays and nested types also supported.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Types["MLflow Data Types"]
    Types --> Scalar["Scalar Types"]
    Types --> Tensor["Tensor Types"]
    Scalar --> S1["boolean"]
    Scalar --> S2["integer / long"]
    Scalar --> S3["float / double"]
    Scalar --> S4["string"]
    Scalar --> S5["datetime"]
    Tensor --> T1["numpy dtypes"]
    Tensor --> T2["shape: (-1, 224, 224, 3)"]
    Note["-1 means variable batch size"]
    style Types fill:#ADD8E6
    style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Input Examples
steps.explanation: An **Input Example** is sample data saved with the model. It serves as documentation and enables UI tools to show example requests. Pass `input_example=X_sample` when logging. Use a small representative sample.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Log["mlflow.sklearn.log_model(
model,
'model',
input_example=X_train[:5]
)"] Log --> Saved["Saved in artifacts/
input_example.json"] Saved --> UI["MLflow UI shows
example request"] Saved --> Docs["Self-documenting
API contract"] Saved --> Test["Easy testing
of served model"] style Log fill:#ADD8E6 style UI fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Signature Validation at Serving
steps.explanation: When serving, MLflow **validates inputs** against the signature. Wrong types or missing columns raise clear errors BEFORE calling predict. This catches issues early and provides actionable error messages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Request["Incoming Request"] Request --> Validate{"Matches signature?"} Validate --> |Yes| Predict["Call model.predict()"] Validate --> |No| Error["400 Bad Request
'Column age: expected double,
got string'"] Predict --> Response["200 OK + predictions"] Benefits["Benefits:
• Early failure
• Clear messages
• No silent bugs"] style Validate fill:#ADD8E6 style Error fill:#FFB6C1 style Predict fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Common Signature Issues
steps.explanation: **Common pitfalls**: (1) Pandas index included accidentally, (2) Column order mismatch, (3) Nullable types not specified, (4) Forgetting to save signature. Always test locally before deploying!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Issues["Common Issues"] Issues --> I1["Pandas index in data
Fix: .reset_index(drop=True)"] Issues --> I2["Column order matters
Fix: Use DataFrame, not array"] Issues --> I3["Null values unexpected
Fix: Handle in preprocessing"] Issues --> I4["No signature saved
Fix: Always pass signature=True"] Test["Always test locally:
loaded_model.predict(sample_data)"] style Issues fill:#FFB6C1 style Test fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Best Practices Summary
steps.explanation: **Best practices**: (1) Always include signatures, (2) Use infer_signature with real data, (3) Include input_example, (4) Test locally before deploying, (5) Version your schemas. Signatures are cheap insurance!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB BP["Signature Best Practices"] BP --> B1["Always include signatures
No exceptions!"] BP --> B2["Infer from real data
Not synthetic examples"] BP --> B3["Include input_example
5-10 rows is enough"] BP --> B4["Test locally first
load_model + predict"] BP --> B5["Version your schemas
Document changes"] Result["Result: Robust,
debuggable deployments"] style BP fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

fields.title: MLflow Projects: Reproducibility
fields.description: Master MLflow Projects for reproducible ML: MLproject file structure, entry points, environment specification, and running projects locally or remotely.

steps.title: What Are MLflow Projects?
steps.explanation: **MLflow Projects** package ML code for reproducible execution. A project is a directory with an **MLproject** file defining how to run it. Anyone can reproduce your experiment with `mlflow run` - same code, same environment, same results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Proj["MLflow Project"] Proj --> Code["Your ML Code
train.py, etc."] Proj --> MLproj["MLproject file
Defines how to run"] Proj --> Env["Environment spec
conda.yaml / Dockerfile"] Run["mlflow run .
or mlflow run git://..."] Run --> Reproduce["Reproducible execution
anywhere!"] style Proj fill:#ADD8E6 style Reproduce fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Project Directory Structure
steps.explanation: A minimal project has: (1) **MLproject** file (YAML config), (2) **conda.yaml** or **Dockerfile** (environment), (3) Your code files. The MLproject file is the entry point that ties everything together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "my_project/"
        MLproj["MLproject"]
        Conda["conda.yaml"]
        Train["train.py"]
        Utils["utils.py"]
        Data["data/"]
    end
    MLproj --> |defines| Entry["Entry points"]
    MLproj --> |references| Conda
    Entry --> |runs| Train
    style MLproj fill:#90EE90
    style Conda fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: The MLproject File
steps.explanation: The **MLproject** file (YAML) defines: **name**, **environment** (conda/docker), and **entry_points** (commands to run). Each entry point can have parameters with types and defaults.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "MLproject file"
        L1["name: my_ml_project"]
        L2["conda_env: conda.yaml"]
        L3["entry_points:"]
        L4["  main:"]
        L5["    parameters:"]
        L6["      learning_rate: {type: float, default: 0.01}"]
        L7["      epochs: {type: int, default: 10}"]
        L8["    command: python train.py --lr {learning_rate}"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L8
    style L3 fill:#90EE90
    style L8 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Entry Points Explained
steps.explanation: **Entry points** are named commands in your project. The **main** entry point is default. Each has **parameters** (typed, with defaults) and a **command** (shell command with parameter substitution). Parameters enable flexible runs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Entry Points" Main["main
Default training"] Val["validate
Run validation"] Serve["serve
Start server"] end Run1["mlflow run . -e main"] Run2["mlflow run . -e validate"] Run1 --> Main Run2 --> Val Params["Pass params:
mlflow run . -P lr=0.001 -P epochs=50"] style Main fill:#90EE90 style Params fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Environment Specification
steps.explanation: Projects specify their environment with **conda.yaml** (Conda) or **Dockerfile** (Docker). MLflow creates an isolated environment before running. This guarantees dependency versions match exactly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Env["Environment Options"] Env --> Conda["conda_env: conda.yaml"] Env --> Docker["docker_env:
image: my-image:v1"] Env --> System["(no env specified)
Uses current env"] subgraph "conda.yaml example" C1["name: my_env"] C2["channels: [conda-forge]"] C3["dependencies:"] C4[" - python=3.9"] C5[" - scikit-learn=1.2"] C6[" - pip:"] C7[" - mlflow"] end Conda --> C1 style Conda fill:#90EE90 style Docker fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Running Projects Locally
steps.explanation: Run a local project with `mlflow run .` or `mlflow run /path/to/project`. Use `-P param=value` to override parameters. MLflow creates the environment, then executes the entry point. Results are tracked automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Cmd["mlflow run . -P learning_rate=0.001"] Cmd --> Step1["1. Read MLproject"] Step1 --> Step2["2. Create conda env
(if not exists)"] Step2 --> Step3["3. Activate environment"] Step3 --> Step4["4. Run command
python train.py --lr 0.001"] Step4 --> Step5["5. Log to MLflow
params, metrics, artifacts"] style Cmd fill:#ADD8E6 style Step5 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Running Remote Projects
steps.explanation: Run projects directly from **Git URLs** - no need to clone first! MLflow clones, creates env, and runs. Use `#branch` or `@commit` to pin versions. Great for sharing reproducible experiments.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Remote Run Options" Git["mlflow run git://github.com/user/repo"] Branch["...repo#branch-name"] Tag["...repo@v1.0.0"] Commit["...repo@abc123f"] end Git --> Clone["MLflow clones repo"] Clone --> Run["Creates env & runs"] Share["Share experiments:
'Run this exact version'"] style Git fill:#ADD8E6 style Share fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Projects with Docker
steps.explanation: For full reproducibility, use **Docker environments**. Specify `docker_env` in MLproject with an image name. MLflow runs your code inside the container. Includes OS-level dependencies, not just Python packages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "MLproject with Docker"
        D1["name: my_project"]
        D2["docker_env:"]
        D3["  image: myregistry/myimage:v1"]
        D4["  volumes: ['/data:/data']"]
        D5["  environment: [['AWS_KEY', 'xxx']]"]
    end
    Benefits["Docker Benefits"]
    Benefits --> B1["OS-level deps"]
    Benefits --> B2["GPU drivers"]
    Benefits --> B3["System libraries"]
    Benefits --> B4["100% reproducible"]
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    style D3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style B4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Best Practices & Use Cases
steps.explanation: **Best practices**: Pin all versions, use entry points for different tasks, keep projects self-contained. **Use cases**: Team collaboration, CI/CD pipelines, paper reproducibility, model retraining jobs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB BP["Best Practices"] BP --> B1["Pin all versions
python=3.9.5, not 3.9"] BP --> B2["Multiple entry points
train, evaluate, serve"] BP --> B3["Self-contained
No external dependencies"] BP --> B4["Document parameters
Types and defaults"] UC["Use Cases"] UC --> U1["Team collaboration"] UC --> U2["CI/CD pipelines"] UC --> U3["Paper reproducibility"] UC --> U4["Scheduled retraining"] style BP fill:#ADD8E6 style UC fill:#90EE90

fields.title: XGBoost: Gradient Boosting Internals
fields.description: Understand how XGBoost builds trees sequentially, uses gradient boosting, applies regularization, and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Essential for ML interviews.

steps.title: What is XGBoost?
steps.explanation: **XGBoost** (eXtreme Gradient Boosting) is an optimized gradient boosting algorithm. It builds trees **sequentially**, where each tree corrects errors from previous trees. Known for speed, performance, and winning Kaggle competitions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR XGB["XGBoost"] XGB --> Boost["Gradient Boosting
Sequential trees"] XGB --> Reg["Regularization
Prevents overfitting"] XGB --> Speed["Optimizations
Parallel, cache-aware"] XGB --> Win["State-of-the-art
Tabular data"] style XGB fill:#ADD8E6 style Win fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Boosting vs Bagging
steps.explanation: **Bagging** (Random Forest) builds trees in parallel, each on random subsets. **Boosting** (XGBoost) builds trees sequentially - each tree learns from previous mistakes. Boosting reduces bias; bagging reduces variance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bagging (Random Forest)" B1["Tree 1"] B2["Tree 2"] B3["Tree 3"] B1 --> Vote["Average votes"] B2 --> Vote B3 --> Vote end subgraph "Boosting (XGBoost)" X1["Tree 1"] --> E1["Errors"] E1 --> X2["Tree 2"] --> E2["Errors"] E2 --> X3["Tree 3"] end Note["Sequential learning
from mistakes"] style X1 fill:#ADD8E6 style X2 fill:#ADD8E6 style X3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Gradient Boosting Idea
steps.explanation: Start with a simple prediction (mean). Calculate **residuals** (errors). Train a tree to predict residuals. Add tree's predictions (scaled by learning rate) to improve. Repeat. Each tree fixes what previous trees got wrong.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Init["Initial: F₀ = mean(y)"]
    Init --> R1["Residuals = y - F₀"]
    R1 --> T1["Tree 1 predicts residuals"]
    T1 --> F1["F₁ = F₀ + η·Tree1"]
    F1 --> R2["New residuals = y - F₁"]
    R2 --> T2["Tree 2 predicts residuals"]
    T2 --> F2["F₂ = F₁ + η·Tree2"]
    F2 --> Dots["... repeat ..."]
    Note["η = learning rate (0.1 typical)"]
    style Init fill:#E6E6FA
    style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: XGBoost's Objective Function
steps.explanation: XGBoost optimizes: **Loss + Regularization**. Loss measures prediction error. Regularization penalizes complex trees (many leaves, large weights). This balance prevents overfitting while maintaining accuracy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Obj["Objective = Loss + Ω(tree)"] Obj --> Loss["Loss Function
MSE, LogLoss, etc."] Obj --> Reg["Regularization Ω"] Reg --> Gamma["γ · (num_leaves)
Penalize many leaves"] Reg --> Lambda["λ · Σ(weights²)
Penalize large weights"] Balance["Balance: Accuracy vs Complexity"] style Obj fill:#ADD8E6 style Balance fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Finding the Best Split
steps.explanation: For each split, XGBoost calculates **Gain** = improvement in objective. Gain considers both error reduction AND regularization cost. Only split if Gain > γ (minimum gain threshold). This builds pruned trees directly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Split["Evaluate Split"]
    Split --> Gain["Gain = Loss_reduction - γ"]
    Gain --> Check{"Gain > 0?"}
    Check --> |Yes| DoSplit["Make the split"]
    Check --> |No| Stop["Don't split (leaf)"]
    Formula["Gain = ½[G²_L/(H_L+λ) + G²_R/(H_R+λ) - G²/(H+λ)] - γ"]
    Note["G=gradient sum, H=hessian sum"]
    style Gain fill:#ADD8E6
    style Formula fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Key Hyperparameters
steps.explanation: **max_depth**: Tree depth (3-10 typical). **learning_rate (η)**: Step size (0.01-0.3). **n_estimators**: Number of trees. **min_child_weight**: Minimum samples in leaf. **subsample/colsample_bytree**: Randomness like RF.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Params["Key Hyperparameters"] Params --> Depth["max_depth: 3-10
Controls tree complexity"] Params --> LR["learning_rate: 0.01-0.3
Lower = more trees needed"] Params --> Trees["n_estimators: 100-1000
More if low LR"] Params --> Reg["gamma, lambda, alpha
Regularization strength"] Params --> Sample["subsample: 0.8
colsample_bytree: 0.8"] style Params fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: XGBoost Optimizations
steps.explanation: XGBoost is fast due to: **Histogram binning** (like LightGBM), **parallel split finding**, **cache-aware access**, **out-of-core computing** for large data. Also supports **GPU training** for massive speedups.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Opt["XGBoost Optimizations"] Opt --> Hist["Histogram binning
Bucket continuous features"] Opt --> Para["Parallel processing
Split finding parallelized"] Opt --> Cache["Cache-aware
Optimized memory access"] Opt --> OOC["Out-of-core
Handles big data"] Opt --> GPU["GPU support
10x+ speedup"] style Opt fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Importance
steps.explanation: XGBoost provides feature importance via: **Gain** (total improvement from feature), **Cover** (samples affected), **Frequency** (times used). Gain is usually most informative. Use SHAP values for detailed explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB FI["Feature Importance"] FI --> Gain["Gain (weight='gain')
Total error reduction"] FI --> Cover["Cover (weight='cover')
Samples in splits"] FI --> Freq["Frequency (weight='weight')
Times feature used"] SHAP["SHAP Values
More detailed,
per-prediction"] Gain --> Best["Usually most
informative"] style FI fill:#ADD8E6 style Best fill:#90EE90

steps.title: XGBoost vs LightGBM vs CatBoost
steps.explanation: **XGBoost**: Most mature, great defaults. **LightGBM**: Faster training, leaf-wise growth. **CatBoost**: Best for categoricals, less tuning needed. All three are excellent - pick based on your data and constraints.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "XGBoost"
        X1["Most mature"]
        X2["Level-wise growth"]
        X3["Great documentation"]
    end
    subgraph "LightGBM"
        L1["Fastest training"]
        L2["Leaf-wise growth"]
        L3["Lower memory"]
    end
    subgraph "CatBoost"
        C1["Best for categoricals"]
        C2["Less tuning needed"]
        C3["Ordered boosting"]
    end
    All["All excellent for tabular data!"]
    style All fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `xgboost.XGBClassifier` or `XGBRegressor`. Scikit-learn compatible API. Key: use **early_stopping** to find optimal n_estimators. Monitor eval metric on validation set to prevent overfitting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" I1["import xgboost as xgb"] I2["model = xgb.XGBClassifier(
n_estimators=100,
learning_rate=0.1,
max_depth=5)"] I3["model.fit(X_train, y_train,
eval_set=[(X_val, y_val)],
early_stopping_rounds=10)"] end I1 --> I2 --> I3 Tip["Always use early_stopping!"] style I2 fill:#ADD8E6 style Tip fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Precision, Recall, F1 Score Explained
fields.description: Master classification metrics: precision, recall, F1 score, and when to optimize each. Includes the precision-recall tradeoff and real-world examples.

steps.title: Why Accuracy Isn't Enough
steps.explanation: **Accuracy** = correct predictions / total predictions. But with imbalanced data (99% negative), predicting all negative gives 99% accuracy while missing all positives! We need metrics that focus on the **positive class**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Data["1000 samples
990 negative, 10 positive"] Data --> Pred["Model predicts ALL negative"] Pred --> Acc["Accuracy = 990/1000 = 99%"] Pred --> Miss["But missed ALL 10 positives!"] Problem["Accuracy misleading
for imbalanced data"] style Acc fill:#FFB6C1 style Miss fill:#FFB6C1 style Problem fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The Confusion Matrix
steps.explanation: The **confusion matrix** shows: **True Positives (TP)** - correctly predicted positive, **False Positives (FP)** - incorrectly predicted positive, **True Negatives (TN)** - correctly predicted negative, **False Negatives (FN)** - missed positives.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Confusion Matrix" subgraph "Predicted Positive" TP["TP
True Positive
Correct!"] FP["FP
False Positive
Type I Error"] end subgraph "Predicted Negative" FN["FN
False Negative
Type II Error"] TN["TN
True Negative
Correct!"] end end Labels["Rows: Actual | Cols: Predicted"] style TP fill:#90EE90 style TN fill:#90EE90 style FP fill:#FFB6C1 style FN fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Precision: How Trustworthy?
steps.explanation: **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP). Of all **predicted positives**, how many are actually positive? High precision = few false alarms. Important when **false positives are costly** (spam filter, fraud alerts).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Pred["Predicted Positive
TP + FP"] Pred --> Prec["Precision = TP / (TP + FP)"] Prec --> Meaning["'When I say positive,
how often am I right?'"] Example["Spam filter: Precision = 95%
5% of 'spam' was actually legitimate"] When["Optimize when FP costly:
Spam, fraud alerts, legal"] style Prec fill:#ADD8E6 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Recall: How Complete?
steps.explanation: **Recall** (Sensitivity) = TP / (TP + FN). Of all **actual positives**, how many did we catch? High recall = few missed cases. Important when **false negatives are costly** (cancer detection, fraud).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Actual["Actual Positive
TP + FN"] Actual --> Rec["Recall = TP / (TP + FN)"] Rec --> Meaning["'Of all positives,
how many did I find?'"] Example["Cancer screening: Recall = 98%
Missed 2% of actual cancers"] When["Optimize when FN costly:
Disease, fraud, security"] style Rec fill:#90EE90 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The Precision-Recall Tradeoff
steps.explanation: Increasing precision often decreases recall (and vice versa). Raising the **threshold** makes model more selective: higher precision, lower recall. Lowering threshold catches more positives but more false alarms.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Thresh["Classification Threshold"] Thresh --> High["High threshold (0.9)"] Thresh --> Low["Low threshold (0.3)"] High --> HP["High Precision
Few false positives"] High --> LR["Low Recall
Miss many positives"] Low --> LP["Low Precision
Many false positives"] Low --> HR["High Recall
Catch most positives"] Tradeoff["Can't maximize both!"] style Tradeoff fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: F1 Score: The Balance
steps.explanation: **F1 Score** = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall). The **harmonic mean** of precision and recall. Balances both metrics. F1 is high only if BOTH precision AND recall are high. Range: 0 to 1.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB F1["F1 = 2 × (P × R) / (P + R)"] F1 --> HM["Harmonic mean
Punishes extremes"] subgraph "Examples" E1["P=0.9, R=0.9 → F1=0.90"] E2["P=0.9, R=0.1 → F1=0.18"] E3["P=0.6, R=0.6 → F1=0.60"] end Note["F1 only high when
BOTH P and R are good"] style F1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: When to Use Each Metric
steps.explanation: **Precision-focused**: Spam filtering, recommendation systems, legal/medical positive predictions. **Recall-focused**: Disease screening, fraud detection, search engines. **F1-focused**: Balanced importance, information retrieval.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Optimize Precision" P1["Spam filter
Don't lose real email"] P2["Recommendations
Relevance matters"] P3["Legal accusations
FP very costly"] end subgraph "Optimize Recall" R1["Cancer screening
Don't miss cases"] R2["Fraud detection
Catch all fraud"] R3["Security threats
Miss nothing"] end subgraph "Optimize F1" F1["Balanced needs"] F2["Info retrieval"] F3["NER, tagging"] end style P1 fill:#ADD8E6 style R1 fill:#90EE90 style F1 fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Micro vs Macro Averaging
steps.explanation: For **multi-class**: **Micro** averages across all instances (global TP, FP, FN). **Macro** averages metrics per class equally. Use micro for imbalanced classes; macro when all classes matter equally.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Multi["Multi-class Metrics"] Multi --> Micro["Micro Average
Global TP/(TP+FP)"] Multi --> Macro["Macro Average
Mean of per-class"] Micro --> M1["Dominated by
majority class"] Macro --> M2["Equal weight
to all classes"] When1["Micro: Imbalanced,
overall performance"] When2["Macro: All classes
equally important"] style Micro fill:#ADD8E6 style Macro fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `sklearn.metrics` for all metrics. `classification_report` gives a complete summary. Always specify `pos_label` for binary or use `average` parameter for multi-class (micro, macro, weighted).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "sklearn.metrics" I1["from sklearn.metrics import
precision_score, recall_score,
f1_score, classification_report"] I2["precision_score(y_true, y_pred)"] I3["recall_score(y_true, y_pred)"] I4["f1_score(y_true, y_pred)"] I5["classification_report(y_true, y_pred)"] end I5 --> Report["Complete summary
per class + averages"] style I5 fill:#90EE90 style Report fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: ROC Curve & AUC Explained
fields.description: Understand ROC curves, AUC scores, threshold selection, and when to use ROC vs Precision-Recall curves. Essential for model evaluation interviews.

steps.title: What is an ROC Curve?
steps.explanation: **ROC** (Receiver Operating Characteristic) plots **True Positive Rate** vs **False Positive Rate** at various thresholds. It shows the tradeoff between catching positives (TPR) and false alarms (FPR) as you vary the decision threshold.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR ROC["ROC Curve"] ROC --> X["X-axis: FPR
False Positive Rate"] ROC --> Y["Y-axis: TPR
True Positive Rate"] ROC --> Thresh["Each point =
different threshold"] Goal["Goal: High TPR,
Low FPR (upper-left)"] style ROC fill:#ADD8E6 style Goal fill:#90EE90

steps.title: TPR and FPR Formulas
steps.explanation: **TPR** (Recall/Sensitivity) = TP / (TP + FN) - of actual positives, how many caught? **FPR** = FP / (FP + TN) - of actual negatives, how many falsely flagged? ROC shows how these change together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "True Positive Rate (TPR)" TPR["TPR = TP / (TP + FN)"] TPR --> TPR_M["Also called Recall
or Sensitivity"] end subgraph "False Positive Rate (FPR)" FPR["FPR = FP / (FP + TN)"] FPR --> FPR_M["Also: 1 - Specificity"] end Relation["As threshold ↓:
TPR ↑, FPR ↑"] style TPR fill:#90EE90 style FPR fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: How the Curve is Built
steps.explanation: Start with threshold = 1 (predict all negative): TPR=0, FPR=0. Lower threshold gradually. More predictions become positive: TPR increases (catching more), FPR increases (more false alarms). Plot each point.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB T1["Threshold = 1.0
All negative
TPR=0, FPR=0"] T2["Threshold = 0.8
Some positive
TPR↑, FPR↑"] T3["Threshold = 0.5
More positive
TPR↑↑, FPR↑↑"] T4["Threshold = 0.0
All positive
TPR=1, FPR=1"] T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 Curve["Connect points
= ROC Curve"] style T1 fill:#E6E6FA style T4 fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: What is AUC?
steps.explanation: **AUC** (Area Under the Curve) summarizes ROC in one number. Range: 0 to 1. AUC = 0.5 means random guessing (diagonal line). AUC = 1.0 means perfect separation. Higher AUC = better model at distinguishing classes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB AUC["AUC Score"] AUC --> A5["AUC = 0.5
Random guessing"] AUC --> A7["AUC = 0.7-0.8
Fair model"] AUC --> A8["AUC = 0.8-0.9
Good model"] AUC --> A9["AUC = 0.9+
Excellent model"] AUC --> A10["AUC = 1.0
Perfect separation"] Interp["AUC = P(random positive
ranked higher than
random negative)"] style A5 fill:#FFB6C1 style A9 fill:#90EE90 style Interp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Interpreting the Curve Shape
steps.explanation: **Good model**: Curve hugs upper-left corner (high TPR at low FPR). **Poor model**: Curve near diagonal. **Perfect model**: Goes straight up then right (TPR=1 before any FPR). Steeper initial rise = better.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "ROC Curve Shapes" Perfect["Perfect: L-shaped
AUC = 1.0"] Good["Good: Hugs upper-left
AUC = 0.85"] Fair["Fair: Moderate curve
AUC = 0.70"] Random["Random: Diagonal
AUC = 0.50"] end Tip["Look for steep rise
in left portion"] style Perfect fill:#90EE90 style Random fill:#FFB6C1 style Tip fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Choosing a Threshold
steps.explanation: ROC helps pick optimal threshold. Common approaches: **Youden's J** = max(TPR - FPR), **Cost-based** if FP/FN have different costs, **Target TPR** (e.g., 95% recall), **Target FPR** (e.g., max 5% false alarms).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Choose["Threshold Selection"] Choose --> Youden["Youden's J
max(TPR - FPR)
Balanced choice"] Choose --> Cost["Cost-based
Minimize expected cost"] Choose --> TPR_T["Target TPR
'Need 95% recall'"] Choose --> FPR_T["Target FPR
'Max 5% false alarm'"] Note["Find threshold where
curve meets requirement"] style Youden fill:#ADD8E6 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: ROC vs Precision-Recall Curves
steps.explanation: **ROC** works well for balanced data. **Precision-Recall** better for imbalanced data (rare positives). ROC can look good even when precision is poor. When positive class is rare, prefer PR curves.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use ROC When" R1["Balanced classes"] R2["Care about negatives"] R3["Comparing models"] end subgraph "Use PR Curve When" P1["Imbalanced data"] P2["Positive class rare"] P3["Focus on precision"] end Rule["Rule: If positive < 10%,
prefer PR curve"] style R1 fill:#ADD8E6 style P1 fill:#90EE90 style Rule fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Comparing Multiple Models
steps.explanation: Plot multiple ROC curves on same axes. Higher curve = better model. If curves cross, one model may be better at low FPR and another at high FPR. AUC provides single-number comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Compare["Comparing Models"] Compare --> Plot["Plot all curves
on same axes"] Compare --> Higher["Higher curve = better"] Compare --> Cross["Curves may cross
= different strengths"] Compare --> AUC_C["AUC for quick
single-number compare"] Caution["Same AUC ≠ same curve
Always visualize!"] style Compare fill:#ADD8E6 style Caution fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `sklearn.metrics.roc_curve` for the curve and `roc_auc_score` for AUC. Pass **probabilities** (not predictions) to `roc_auc_score`. Use `RocCurveDisplay` for easy plotting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "sklearn.metrics" I1["from sklearn.metrics import
roc_curve, roc_auc_score"] I2["fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(
y_true, y_proba)"] I3["auc = roc_auc_score(
y_true, y_proba)"] I4["RocCurveDisplay.from_predictions(
y_true, y_proba)"] end Note["Use probabilities,
not class predictions!"] style I4 fill:#90EE90 style Note fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Cross-Validation Strategies
fields.description: Master cross-validation techniques: K-Fold, Stratified, Leave-One-Out, Time Series CV, and when to use each. Essential for reliable model evaluation.

steps.title: Why Cross-Validation?
steps.explanation: A single train/test split can be **lucky or unlucky** - results depend on which data points end up where. **Cross-validation** uses multiple splits to get a more reliable performance estimate and reduce variance in evaluation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Problem["Single Split Problem"] Problem --> Lucky["Lucky split:
Score = 95%"] Problem --> Unlucky["Unlucky split:
Score = 80%"] Problem --> Real["Real performance?
Unknown!"] CV["Cross-Validation"] CV --> Multiple["Multiple splits
Average results"] CV --> Reliable["More reliable
estimate"] style Problem fill:#FFB6C1 style CV fill:#90EE90

steps.title: K-Fold Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: Split data into **K equal folds** (typically 5 or 10). Train on K-1 folds, test on remaining fold. Repeat K times, each fold serving as test once. Average scores across all folds. Every sample gets tested exactly once.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "5-Fold CV"
        F1["Fold 1: TEST | Train | Train | Train | Train"]
        F2["Fold 2: Train | TEST | Train | Train | Train"]
        F3["Fold 3: Train | Train | TEST | Train | Train"]
        F4["Fold 4: Train | Train | Train | TEST | Train"]
        F5["Fold 5: Train | Train | Train | Train | TEST"]
    end
    F1 --> Avg["Average 5 scores"]
    F2 --> Avg
    F3 --> Avg
    F4 --> Avg
    F5 --> Avg
    style Avg fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Stratified K-Fold
steps.explanation: **Stratified** K-Fold preserves the **class distribution** in each fold. If 20% of data is positive, each fold has ~20% positive. Essential for **imbalanced classification**. Default in sklearn for classifiers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Data["Data: 80% negative, 20% positive"]
    subgraph "Regular K-Fold"
        R1["Fold 1: 85% neg, 15% pos"]
        R2["Fold 2: 70% neg, 30% pos"]
        R3["Fold 3: 90% neg, 10% pos"]
    end
    subgraph "Stratified K-Fold"
        S1["Fold 1: 80% neg, 20% pos"]
        S2["Fold 2: 80% neg, 20% pos"]
        S3["Fold 3: 80% neg, 20% pos"]
    end
    Data --> R1
    Data --> S1
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S2 fill:#90EE90
    style S3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Leave-One-Out (LOO)
steps.explanation: **LOO** is K-Fold where K = n (number of samples). Train on n-1 samples, test on 1. Repeat n times. Maximum use of data for training. But: computationally expensive, high variance in estimates. Use for small datasets only.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB LOO["Leave-One-Out CV"] LOO --> Train["Train on n-1 samples"] LOO --> Test["Test on 1 sample"] LOO --> Repeat["Repeat n times"] Pros["Pros: Max training data"] Cons["Cons: Slow (n models),
high variance"] When["Use when: n < 100"] style LOO fill:#ADD8E6 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Time Series Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: For **time series data**, you can't use random splits - that's **data leakage** (using future to predict past). Use **expanding window** or **sliding window**: train on past, test on future, move forward.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Time Series CV (Expanding)" T1["Split 1: [Train] | Test | - | - | -"] T2["Split 2: [Train | Train] | Test | - | -"] T3["Split 3: [Train | Train | Train] | Test | -"] T4["Split 4: [Train | Train | Train | Train] | Test"] end Rule["Rule: Always train on PAST,
test on FUTURE"] Wrong["Wrong: Random split
= data leakage!"] style Rule fill:#90EE90 style Wrong fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Group K-Fold
steps.explanation: When data has **groups** (e.g., multiple samples per patient), ensure entire groups stay together. **GroupKFold** prevents same group appearing in both train and test, avoiding data leakage from correlated samples.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Data["Data: Samples from 10 patients"]
    subgraph "Wrong: Regular K-Fold"
        W1["Patient A in train AND test"]
        W2["Leakage: Memorize patient!"]
    end
    subgraph "Correct: GroupKFold"
        C1["Patient A only in train"]
        C2["Patient B only in test"]
        C3["No patient in both"]
    end
    style W1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style C1 fill:#90EE90
    style C2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Nested Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: For **hyperparameter tuning + evaluation**: use **nested CV**. Outer loop evaluates model. Inner loop tunes hyperparameters. This gives unbiased estimate of tuned model's performance. Avoids overfitting to test set.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Outer["Outer CV (5-fold)
Model evaluation"] Outer --> Inner["Inner CV (3-fold)
Hyperparameter tuning"] Inner --> Best["Best params for
this outer fold"] Best --> Train["Train with best params"] Train --> Test["Test on outer fold"] Final["Final: Average outer scores"] Note["Unbiased performance
of tuning process"] style Outer fill:#ADD8E6 style Inner fill:#E6E6FA style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Choosing K and Strategy
steps.explanation: **K=5 or 10** is standard. More K = less bias, more variance, slower. **Stratified**: classification with imbalance. **Group**: grouped data. **Time Series**: temporal data. **LOO**: tiny datasets only.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Choose["How to Choose"]
    Choose --> K["K value"]
    Choose --> Strategy["Strategy"]
    K --> K5["K=5: Fast, slight bias"]
    K --> K10["K=10: Standard choice"]
    K --> KLOO["K=n: LOO, small data"]
    Strategy --> Strat["Stratified: Imbalanced"]
    Strategy --> Group["Group: Grouped samples"]
    Strategy --> TS["TimeSeries: Temporal"]
    style K10 fill:#90EE90
    style Strat fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `cross_val_score` for quick evaluation. For more control, use `KFold`, `StratifiedKFold`, etc. with manual loops or `cross_validate`. Always set `shuffle=True` and `random_state` for reproducibility.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Quick: cross_val_score"
        Q1["from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score"]
        Q2["scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5)"]
        Q3["print(scores.mean(), scores.std())"]
    end
    subgraph "Detailed: KFold"
        D1["from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold"]
        D2["cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True)"]
        D3["for train_idx, test_idx in cv.split(X, y):"]
    end
    style Q2 fill:#90EE90
    style D2 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Handling Imbalanced Datasets
fields.description: Learn techniques for imbalanced classification: SMOTE, undersampling, class weights, threshold tuning, and choosing the right evaluation metrics.

steps.title: The Imbalanced Data Problem
steps.explanation: **Imbalanced data** has unequal class distribution (e.g., 99% negative, 1% positive). Models tend to predict the majority class always, achieving high accuracy but missing minorities. Common in fraud, disease, churn detection.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Data["Imbalanced Dataset"] Data --> Maj["Majority: 9900 samples"] Data --> Min["Minority: 100 samples"] Model["Naive Model"] Model --> Pred["Predicts ALL majority"] Pred --> Acc["Accuracy = 99%"] Pred --> Recall["Recall = 0%"] Problem["High accuracy,
useless model!"] style Min fill:#FFB6C1 style Problem fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Strategy Overview
steps.explanation: Four main approaches: (1) **Resampling** - change data distribution, (2) **Cost-sensitive** - penalize minority errors more, (3) **Algorithmic** - use algorithms robust to imbalance, (4) **Threshold** - adjust decision boundary.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Strategies["Handling Imbalance"] Strategies --> Resample["Resampling
Change data"] Strategies --> Cost["Cost-sensitive
Class weights"] Strategies --> Algo["Algorithmic
Robust models"] Strategies --> Thresh["Threshold tuning
Adjust cutoff"] Resample --> Over["Oversample minority"] Resample --> Under["Undersample majority"] style Strategies fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Undersampling
steps.explanation: **Undersampling** removes samples from majority class to balance. Simple but **loses information**. Works when you have lots of majority data. Variants: Random, Tomek Links (remove borderline), NearMiss (keep informative).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Before["Before
9900 neg, 100 pos"] Under["Random Undersampling"] After["After
100 neg, 100 pos"] Before --> Under --> After Pros["Pros: Fast, simple"] Cons["Cons: Loses data!
May lose important info"] When["Use when: Lots of
majority data available"] style Cons fill:#FFB6C1 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Oversampling & SMOTE
steps.explanation: **Oversampling** duplicates minority samples. **SMOTE** (Synthetic Minority Oversampling) creates NEW synthetic samples by interpolating between existing minorities. Better than duplication - adds variety, reduces overfitting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Random Oversampling" R1["Duplicate minority samples"] R2["Risk: Overfitting to
exact same points"] end subgraph "SMOTE" S1["Find k nearest minority neighbors"] S2["Create synthetic point
between them"] S3["New diverse samples!"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 end Better["SMOTE usually better
than duplication"] style S3 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Class Weights
steps.explanation: **Class weights** tell the model that minority errors are more costly. Set `class_weight='balanced'` in sklearn to auto-calculate weights inversely proportional to class frequency. No data manipulation needed!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Weights["Class Weights"] Weights --> Balanced["class_weight='balanced'
Auto-calculate weights"] Weights --> Manual["class_weight={0: 1, 1: 10}
Manually set weights"] Formula["Weight = n_samples /
(n_classes × n_class_samples)"] Effect["Effect: Minority errors
penalized more in loss"] Pros["Pros: No data change,
works with any model"] style Balanced fill:#90EE90 style Pros fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Threshold Tuning
steps.explanation: Default threshold is 0.5. For imbalanced data, **lower the threshold** to predict more positives. Use Precision-Recall curve to find optimal threshold. Threshold tuning adjusts decision boundary without changing the model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Thresh["Threshold Tuning"] Thresh --> Default["Default: 0.5
May miss minorities"] Thresh --> Lower["Lower: 0.3
Catch more positives"] Thresh --> Higher["Higher: 0.7
Fewer false positives"] Find["Finding Optimal"] Find --> PR["Use PR curve"] Find --> F1["Maximize F1"] Find --> Business["Business requirements"] style Lower fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Evaluation Metrics for Imbalance
steps.explanation: **Don't use accuracy!** Use: **Precision/Recall/F1** for positive class, **ROC-AUC** for ranking ability, **PR-AUC** (better for severe imbalance), **Balanced Accuracy** = (TPR + TNR) / 2.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Metrics["Metrics for Imbalanced Data"] Metrics --> Bad["BAD: Accuracy
Misleading!"] Metrics --> Good["GOOD Metrics"] Good --> F1["F1 Score
Balance P & R"] Good --> PR["PR-AUC
Severe imbalance"] Good --> ROC["ROC-AUC
Ranking ability"] Good --> BA["Balanced Accuracy
(TPR + TNR) / 2"] style Bad fill:#FFB6C1 style Good fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Combining Techniques
steps.explanation: Best results often combine multiple techniques: **SMOTE + Undersampling** (SMOTETomek, SMOTEENN), **SMOTE + Class Weights**, **Threshold tuning on weighted model**. Experiment to find what works for your data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Combine["Combining Techniques"] Combine --> ST["SMOTE + Tomek Links
Clean borderline cases"] Combine --> SE["SMOTE + ENN
Remove noisy samples"] Combine --> SW["SMOTE + Class Weights
Double reinforcement"] Combine --> TT["Any method + Threshold
Fine-tune decision"] Experiment["Always experiment!
No universal best"] style Combine fill:#ADD8E6 style Experiment fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `imbalanced-learn` library for SMOTE and resampling. Use sklearn's `class_weight='balanced'`. Remember: **only resample training data**, never test data. Use pipelines to avoid data leakage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "imbalanced-learn" I1["from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE"] I2["from imblearn.combine import SMOTETomek"] I3["sm = SMOTE(random_state=42)"] I4["X_res, y_res = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)"] end subgraph "Class Weights" W1["model = RandomForestClassifier(
class_weight='balanced')"] end Warn["Only resample TRAIN,
never test data!"] style I4 fill:#90EE90 style Warn fill:#FFB6C1

fields.title: PCA: Dimensionality Reduction
fields.description: Understand Principal Component Analysis: variance, eigenvectors, choosing components, and when to use PCA. Essential for feature reduction and visualization.

steps.title: Why Dimensionality Reduction?
steps.explanation: High-dimensional data causes problems: **curse of dimensionality**, slow training, overfitting, hard to visualize. **Dimensionality reduction** projects data to fewer dimensions while preserving important information.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Problems["High Dimensions Problems"] Problems --> Curse["Curse of dimensionality
Data becomes sparse"] Problems --> Slow["Slow training
More computation"] Problems --> Overfit["Overfitting
Too many features"] Problems --> Viz["No visualization
Can't plot 100D"] Solution["Solution: Reduce dimensions
Keep important info"] style Problems fill:#FFB6C1 style Solution fill:#90EE90

steps.title: What is PCA?
steps.explanation: **PCA** (Principal Component Analysis) finds new axes (principal components) that capture maximum **variance** in data. PC1 captures most variance, PC2 second most (orthogonal to PC1), etc. Project data onto top K components.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Original["Original Data
100 features"] PCA["PCA"] Reduced["Reduced Data
10 components"] Original --> PCA --> Reduced PC["Principal Components"] PC --> PC1["PC1: Most variance"] PC --> PC2["PC2: Second most
(orthogonal to PC1)"] PC --> PCK["PC_k: k-th most"] style PCA fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Variance = Information
steps.explanation: PCA assumes **variance = information**. Features with high variance carry more signal. PCA rotates axes to align with directions of maximum variance, then keeps the top components. Low-variance directions are noise.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Idea["Core Idea"] Idea --> HighVar["High variance direction
= Important pattern"] Idea --> LowVar["Low variance direction
= Noise, discard"] Example["Example: Data spread
mostly along one axis"] Example --> Keep["Keep that axis (PC1)"] Example --> Drop["Drop perpendicular axis"] style HighVar fill:#90EE90 style LowVar fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: How PCA Works
steps.explanation: Steps: (1) **Center** data (subtract mean), (2) Compute **covariance matrix**, (3) Find **eigenvectors** (directions) and **eigenvalues** (variance), (4) Sort by eigenvalue, (5) Project onto top K eigenvectors.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB S1["1. Center data
X - mean(X)"] S2["2. Covariance matrix
C = XᵀX / (n-1)"] S3["3. Eigendecomposition
C = VΛVᵀ"] S4["4. Sort eigenvectors
by eigenvalue (desc)"] S5["5. Project: X_new = X · V_k"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 Note["Eigenvector = direction
Eigenvalue = variance"] style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Choosing Number of Components
steps.explanation: How many components to keep? Use **explained variance ratio**. Common rules: keep enough for **95% variance**, use **elbow method** (plot variance vs components), or set based on downstream task performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Choose["Choosing K"] Choose --> Var95["95% Variance Rule
Keep until 95% explained"] Choose --> Elbow["Elbow Method
Plot cumulative variance"] Choose --> Task["Task-based
Cross-validate with K"] Var["Explained Variance Ratio"] Var --> V1["PC1: 60%"] Var --> V2["PC1+PC2: 80%"] Var --> V3["PC1+PC2+PC3: 92%"] style Var95 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use PCA
steps.explanation: Use PCA for: **visualization** (reduce to 2-3D), **noise reduction**, **speeding up training**, **decorrelating features**, **compression**. Don't use when: features have different scales (standardize first!), interpretability needed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Good Use Cases" G1["Visualization
Project to 2D/3D"] G2["Preprocessing
Before ML model"] G3["Noise reduction
Remove low-var dims"] G4["Compression
Store fewer features"] end subgraph "Cautions" C1["Scale features first!
PCA sensitive to scale"] C2["Loses interpretability
PCs are combinations"] C3["Linear only
Use kernel PCA if nonlinear"] end style G1 fill:#90EE90 style C1 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: PCA vs Other Methods
steps.explanation: **PCA**: Linear, unsupervised, preserves global variance. **t-SNE/UMAP**: Nonlinear, for visualization only. **LDA**: Supervised, maximizes class separation. **Autoencoders**: Nonlinear, learned compression.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Compare["Dimensionality Reduction Methods"] Compare --> PCA["PCA
Linear, fast, global"] Compare --> TSNE["t-SNE/UMAP
Nonlinear, visualization"] Compare --> LDA["LDA
Supervised, class separation"] Compare --> AE["Autoencoders
Nonlinear, neural"] When1["PCA: Preprocessing, speed"] When2["t-SNE: 2D visualization"] style PCA fill:#ADD8E6 style TSNE fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Interpreting Components
steps.explanation: Each PC is a **linear combination** of original features. **Loadings** show feature contributions to each PC. High absolute loading = feature important for that component. Use to understand what each PC represents.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB PC1["PC1 = 0.8×height + 0.6×weight"] Interpret["Interpretation:
'Body size' component"] PC1 --> Interpret Loadings["Loadings Matrix"] Loadings --> L1["pca.components_
Shows all loadings"] Loadings --> L2["High |loading| =
Important feature"] Viz["Visualize with heatmap"] style PC1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Interpret fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Python Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `sklearn.decomposition.PCA`. **Always standardize first** with `StandardScaler`. Check `explained_variance_ratio_` to choose components. Use `fit_transform` on train, only `transform` on test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard PCA Pipeline" I1["from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler"] I2["from sklearn.decomposition import PCA"] I3["scaler = StandardScaler()"] I4["X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)"] I5["pca = PCA(n_components=0.95) # 95% variance"] I6["X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)"] end Check["pca.explained_variance_ratio_
pca.n_components_"] style I4 fill:#FFB6C1 style I5 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Multi-Head Attention
fields.description: Understand how multi-head attention allows transformers to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces, capturing diverse patterns in the data.

steps.title: Why Multiple Heads?
steps.explanation: A single attention head can only focus on one type of relationship at a time. **Multi-head attention** runs multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning different patterns: syntax, semantics, coreference, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Head Limitation" S1["'The cat sat on the mat'"] S1 --> A1["One Attention Head"] A1 --> R1["Can only capture
ONE relationship type"] end subgraph "Multi-Head Solution" S2["'The cat sat on the mat'"] S2 --> H1["Head 1: Syntax"] S2 --> H2["Head 2: Semantics"] S2 --> H3["Head 3: Position"] H1 & H2 & H3 --> R2["Rich, multi-faceted
understanding"] end style H1 fill:#FFB6C1 style H2 fill:#98FB98 style H3 fill:#87CEEB

steps.title: The Architecture Overview
steps.explanation: Multi-head attention takes Q, K, V inputs and projects them into **h** different subspaces (h=8 in original Transformer). Each head performs attention independently, then results are concatenated and projected back.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    Q["Q"] --> Split1["Split"]
    K["K"] --> Split2["Split"]
    V["V"] --> Split3["Split"]
    Split1 --> H1["Head 1"]
    Split1 --> H2["Head 2"]
    Split1 --> Hdot["..."]
    Split1 --> Hh["Head h"]
    Split2 --> H1
    Split2 --> H2
    Split2 --> Hdot
    Split2 --> Hh
    Split3 --> H1
    Split3 --> H2
    Split3 --> Hdot
    Split3 --> Hh
    H1 & H2 & Hdot & Hh --> Concat["Concat"]
    Concat --> WO["W_O"]
    WO --> Out["Output"]
    style H1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style H2 fill:#98FB98
    style Hh fill:#87CEEB
    style Concat fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Dimension Split
steps.explanation: If model dimension d_model=512 and h=8 heads, each head works with d_k = d_model/h = 64 dimensions. This keeps computation cost similar to single-head attention with full dimensions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Original Dimensions"
        D1["d_model = 512"]
    end
    D1 --> Split["Split into h=8 heads"]
    subgraph "Per-Head Dimensions"
        DK["d_k = 512/8 = 64"]
        DV["d_v = 512/8 = 64"]
    end
    Split --> DK
    Split --> DV
    subgraph "Key Insight"
        I1["8 heads × 64 dims = 512"]
        I2["Same total computation!"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E6E6FA
    style DK fill:#98FB98
    style DV fill:#98FB98

steps.title: Linear Projections
steps.explanation: Each head has its own learned projection matrices: **W_Q^i**, **W_K^i**, **W_V^i**. These transform the input into head-specific query, key, and value representations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR X["Input X
(seq_len × d_model)"] --> WQ1["W_Q^1"] X --> WK1["W_K^1"] X --> WV1["W_V^1"] WQ1 --> Q1["Q_1
(seq_len × d_k)"] WK1 --> K1["K_1
(seq_len × d_k)"] WV1 --> V1["V_1
(seq_len × d_v)"] Q1 & K1 & V1 --> Attn1["Attention
Head 1"] style WQ1 fill:#FFB6C1 style WK1 fill:#98FB98 style WV1 fill:#87CEEB style Attn1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Parallel Attention Computation
steps.explanation: Each head computes scaled dot-product attention independently: **Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V**. Heads run in parallel on GPU for efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Head 1"
        Q1["Q_1"] --> MM1["Q_1 × K_1^T"]
        K1["K_1"] --> MM1
        MM1 --> Scale1["÷ √d_k"]
        Scale1 --> SM1["Softmax"]
        SM1 --> MV1["× V_1"]
        V1["V_1"] --> MV1
        MV1 --> O1["head_1"]
    end
    subgraph "Head 2"
        Q2["Q_2"] --> MM2["Q_2 × K_2^T"]
        K2["K_2"] --> MM2
        MM2 --> Scale2["÷ √d_k"]
        Scale2 --> SM2["Softmax"]
        SM2 --> MV2["× V_2"]
        V2["V_2"] --> MV2
        MV2 --> O2["head_2"]
    end
    style O1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style O2 fill:#98FB98

steps.title: Concatenation
steps.explanation: After all heads compute their outputs, we **concatenate** them along the feature dimension. If each head outputs (seq_len × d_v), concatenation gives (seq_len × h*d_v).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR H1["head_1
(seq × 64)"] --> Concat H2["head_2
(seq × 64)"] --> Concat H3["head_3
(seq × 64)"] --> Concat Hdot["..."] --> Concat H8["head_8
(seq × 64)"] --> Concat{"Concat"} Concat --> Out["Concatenated
(seq × 512)"] style H1 fill:#FFB6C1 style H2 fill:#98FB98 style H3 fill:#87CEEB style H8 fill:#DDA0DD style Out fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Final Linear Projection
steps.explanation: The concatenated output is projected through **W_O** (output projection) to produce the final multi-head attention output. This allows the model to mix information from all heads.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR Concat["Concat(head_1,...,head_h)
(seq × 512)"] --> WO["W_O
(512 × 512)"] WO --> Out["MultiHead Output
(seq × 512)"] subgraph "W_O learns to combine heads" Mix["Mixes patterns from
all attention heads"] end style Concat fill:#FFFACD style WO fill:#E6E6FA style Out fill:#98FB98

steps.title: What Different Heads Learn
steps.explanation: Research shows different heads specialize in different linguistic patterns. Some focus on adjacent words, others on syntactic relationships, and some on long-range dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    S["'The cat that I saw yesterday ran away'"]
    S --> H1["Head 1: Adjacent"]
    S --> H2["Head 2: Subject-Verb"]
    S --> H3["Head 3: Relative Clause"]
    H1 --> P1["'The→cat', 'ran→away'"]
    H2 --> P2["'cat→ran', 'I→saw'"]
    H3 --> P3["'cat→that→saw'"]
    style H1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style H2 fill:#98FB98
    style H3 fill:#87CEEB
    style P1 fill:#FFE4E1
    style P2 fill:#F0FFF0
    style P3 fill:#F0F8FF

steps.title: The Complete Formula
steps.explanation: **MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head_1,...,head_h)W_O** where **head_i = Attention(QW_Q^i, KW_K^i, VW_V^i)**. Total parameters: 4 × d_model² (for W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Multi-Head Attention Formula"
        F1["MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head_1,...,head_h) × W_O"]
        F2["head_i = Attention(Q×W_Q^i, K×W_K^i, V×W_V^i)"]
        F3["Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k) × V"]
    end
    subgraph "Parameters (d=512, h=8)"
        P1["W_Q: 512×512"]
        P2["W_K: 512×512"]
        P3["W_V: 512×512"]
        P4["W_O: 512×512"]
        PT["Total: 4 × 512² = 1M params"]
    end
    style F1 fill:#FFFACD
    style F2 fill:#E6E6FA
    style F3 fill:#E6E6FA
    style PT fill:#98FB98

steps.title: Interview Key Points
steps.explanation: **Key takeaways**: (1) Multi-head enables diverse attention patterns, (2) Dimension split keeps computation efficient, (3) Each head has separate W_Q, W_K, W_V, (4) Final W_O combines all heads, (5) Typical: 8-16 heads in practice.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Common Interview Questions"
        Q1["Why multiple heads?"]
        A1["Capture different relationship types"]
        Q2["How is it efficient?"]
        A2["Split d_model across heads"]
        Q3["How many heads typically?"]
        A3["8 heads (base), 16 heads (large)"]
        Q4["Can heads be pruned?"]
        A4["Yes! Many heads are redundant"]
    end
    Q1 --> A1
    Q2 --> A2
    Q3 --> A3
    Q4 --> A4
    style Q1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Q2 fill:#98FB98
    style Q3 fill:#87CEEB
    style Q4 fill:#DDA0DD

fields.title: Positional Encoding
fields.description: Understand how transformers encode sequence position information using sinusoidal functions, enabling the model to understand word order without recurrence.

steps.title: The Position Problem
steps.explanation: Unlike RNNs that process tokens sequentially, transformers process all tokens in parallel. This means they have no inherent notion of word order.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Without_Position_Info
        A[The] --> ATT[Attention]
        B[cat] --> ATT
        C[sat] --> ATT
        ATT --> OUT[Same output regardless of order]
    end
    style ATT fill:#ff6b6b
    style OUT fill:#ffa8a8

steps.title: Positional Encoding Solution
steps.explanation: We add a unique positional encoding vector to each token embedding. This vector encodes the absolute position of each token in the sequence.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    subgraph Position_0
        E0[Token Embedding] --> ADD0((+))
        P0[Pos Encoding 0] --> ADD0
        ADD0 --> OUT0[Final Embedding]
    end
    subgraph Position_1
        E1[Token Embedding] --> ADD1((+))
        P1[Pos Encoding 1] --> ADD1
        ADD1 --> OUT1[Final Embedding]
    end
    style P0 fill:#74c0fc
    style P1 fill:#74c0fc
    style ADD0 fill:#69db7c
    style ADD1 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Sinusoidal Functions
steps.explanation: PE uses sine and cosine at different frequencies. PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)), PE(pos,2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)). Even dimensions use sine, odd use cosine.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    POS[Position pos] --> CALC[Calculate frequency]
    DIM[Dimension i] --> CALC
    CALC --> FREQ[freq = 10000^2i/d_model]
    FREQ --> SIN[sin pos/freq]
    FREQ --> COS[cos pos/freq]
    SIN --> EVEN[Even dims: 0 2 4]
    COS --> ODD[Odd dims: 1 3 5]
    style SIN fill:#ffd43b
    style COS fill:#63e6be

steps.title: Frequency Spectrum
steps.explanation: Different dimensions capture position at different scales. Low dimensions have high frequency for local position. High dimensions have low frequency for global position.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    subgraph Low_Dimension
        L0[pos 0] --> LV0[0.00]
        L1[pos 1] --> LV1[0.84]
        L2[pos 2] --> LV2[0.91]
    end
    subgraph High_Dimension
        H0[pos 0] --> HV0[0.00]
        H1[pos 1] --> HV1[0.0001]
        H2[pos 2] --> HV2[0.0002]
    end
    style L0 fill:#ff8787
    style L1 fill:#ff8787
    style L2 fill:#ff8787
    style H0 fill:#74c0fc
    style H1 fill:#74c0fc
    style H2 fill:#74c0fc

steps.title: Why Sinusoidal Functions
steps.explanation: Key insight: PE(pos+k) can be expressed as a linear function of PE(pos). This enables the model to learn relative position patterns easily.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    PE1[PE pos] --> LINEAR[Linear Transform]
    LINEAR --> PE2[PE pos+k]
    A[Token at pos] --> ATT[Attention]
    B[Token at pos+k] --> ATT
    ATT --> REL[Learns relative distance k]
    style LINEAR fill:#b197fc
    style REL fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Position Encoding Matrix
steps.explanation: For sequence length L and model dimension d_model, we precompute an L x d_model matrix. Each row is the positional encoding for that position.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph PE_Matrix
        R0[Row 0: sin cos sin cos]
        R1[Row 1: sin cos sin cos]
        R2[Row 2: sin cos sin cos]
    end
    subgraph Frequencies
        F0[Cols 0-1: High freq]
        F1[Cols 2-3: Med freq]
        F2[Cols 4-5: Low freq]
    end
    style F0 fill:#ff8787
    style F1 fill:#ffd43b
    style F2 fill:#74c0fc

steps.title: Adding to Embeddings
steps.explanation: Positional encodings are added element-wise to token embeddings before any transformer layers. Both vectors have dimension d_model.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    TOK[Input Tokens] --> EMB[Embedding Layer]
    EMB --> TOKEMB[Token Embeddings]
    SEQ[Sequence Length] --> PE[Positional Encoding]
    PE --> POSEMB[Position Embeddings]
    TOKEMB --> ADD((+))
    POSEMB --> ADD
    ADD --> FINAL[Final Input to Transformer]
    style ADD fill:#69db7c
    style FINAL fill:#b197fc

steps.title: Learned vs Fixed Encodings
steps.explanation: Alternative: learn position embeddings as parameters. BERT and GPT use learned embeddings. Trade-off: learned cannot extrapolate beyond training length.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Sinusoidal_Fixed
        S1[Deterministic formula]
        S2[Extrapolates to any length]
        S3[No added parameters]
    end
    subgraph Learned
        L1[Trained with model]
        L2[Limited to max position]
        L3[Adds parameters]
    end
    style S1 fill:#69db7c
    style S2 fill:#69db7c
    style L2 fill:#ffa8a8

steps.title: Relative Position Encodings
steps.explanation: Modern variants like RoPE and ALiBi encode relative rather than absolute positions. Used in LLaMA and GPT-NeoX. Focus on distance between tokens.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Absolute
        A1[Token at pos 5] --> AE1[PE 5]
        A2[Token at pos 8] --> AE2[PE 8]
    end
    subgraph Relative
        R1[Token at pos 5] --> RE[Relative distance 3]
        R2[Token at pos 8] --> RE
        RE --> ATT[Attention modified by distance]
    end
    style RE fill:#b197fc
    style ATT fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Complete Picture
steps.explanation: Positional encoding is crucial for transformers to understand sequence order. Sinusoidal approach is parameter-free and generalizes well with unique encoding per position.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    IN[Input: The cat sat] --> TOK[Tokenize]
    TOK --> EMB[Token Embeddings]
    POS[Positions: 0 1 2] --> PE[Sinusoidal PE]
    EMB --> ADD((+))
    PE --> ADD
    ADD --> TRANS[Transformer Layers]
    TRANS --> OUT[Context-aware Output]
    style PE fill:#74c0fc
    style ADD fill:#69db7c
    style TRANS fill:#b197fc
    style OUT fill:#ffd43b

fields.title: Layer Normalization and Residual Connections
fields.description: Learn how layer normalization and residual connections work together to enable stable training of deep transformer networks.

steps.title: The Deep Network Challenge
steps.explanation: Deep networks face two major training challenges: vanishing/exploding gradients and internal covariate shift. Transformers use residual connections and layer normalization to address both problems.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Deep_Network_Problems
        P1[Vanishing Gradients]
        P2[Exploding Gradients]
        P3[Internal Covariate Shift]
    end
    subgraph Solutions
        S1[Residual Connections]
        S2[Layer Normalization]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S1
    P3 --> S2
    style P1 fill:#ffa8a8
    style P2 fill:#ffa8a8
    style P3 fill:#ffa8a8
    style S1 fill:#69db7c
    style S2 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Residual Connections
steps.explanation: Also called skip connections. Instead of learning F(x), we learn F(x) + x. The identity mapping x flows directly through, making it easier to learn small refinements. If the layer learns nothing useful, gradients still flow through the skip.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    X[Input x] --> LAYER[Sub-Layer]
    LAYER --> F[F of x]
    X --> SKIP[Skip Connection]
    F --> ADD((+))
    SKIP --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[x + F of x]
    style SKIP fill:#74c0fc
    style ADD fill:#69db7c
    style OUT fill:#b197fc

steps.title: Why Residuals Work
steps.explanation: During backpropagation, gradients can flow directly through the skip connection. This creates a highway for gradients, preventing vanishing gradient problems even in very deep networks.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Forward
        X1[x] --> L1[Layer]
        L1 --> A1((+))
        X1 --> A1
        A1 --> Y1[Output]
    end
    subgraph Backward
        G1[Gradient] --> SUM[Sum]
        G2[Gradient through skip] --> SUM
        G3[Gradient through layer] --> SUM
    end
    style G2 fill:#69db7c
    style SUM fill:#b197fc

steps.title: Layer Normalization
steps.explanation: Normalizes activations across the feature dimension. For each token, compute mean and variance across all d_model features, then normalize. Unlike batch norm, works independently per token.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Single_Token
        X[Token embedding d_model dims] --> MEAN[Compute mean mu]
        X --> VAR[Compute variance sigma^2]
        X --> NORM[Normalize]
        MEAN --> NORM
        VAR --> NORM
        NORM --> SCALE[Scale gamma and shift beta]
        SCALE --> OUT[Normalized output]
    end
    style NORM fill:#74c0fc
    style SCALE fill:#69db7c

steps.title: LayerNorm Formula
steps.explanation: LN(x) = gamma * (x - mu) / sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon) + beta. Mu and sigma computed across features. Gamma and beta are learned parameters that allow the model to undo normalization if needed.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    X[Input x] --> MU[mu = mean of x]
    X --> SIG[sigma^2 = var of x]
    X --> SUB[x - mu]
    MU --> SUB
    SUB --> DIV[divide by sqrt sigma^2 + eps]
    SIG --> DIV
    DIV --> SCALE[multiply by gamma]
    SCALE --> SHIFT[add beta]
    SHIFT --> OUT[LN output]
    style MU fill:#ffd43b
    style SIG fill:#ffd43b
    style SCALE fill:#74c0fc
    style SHIFT fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm
steps.explanation: Batch norm normalizes across the batch dimension - problematic for sequences of varying lengths. Layer norm normalizes across features, making it ideal for NLP where sequences vary in length.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Batch_Norm
        BN1[Normalize across batch]
        BN2[Same position, different samples]
        BN3[Requires batch statistics]
    end
    subgraph Layer_Norm
        LN1[Normalize across features]
        LN2[Same sample, different features]
        LN3[Works with batch size 1]
    end
    style BN1 fill:#ffa8a8
    style LN1 fill:#69db7c
    style LN3 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Transformer Block Structure
steps.explanation: Each transformer block has two sub-layers: multi-head attention and feed-forward network. Each sub-layer has its own residual connection and layer normalization.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    X[Input] --> ATT[Multi-Head Attention]
    ATT --> ADD1((+))
    X --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> LN1[Layer Norm]
    LN1 --> FFN[Feed Forward]
    FFN --> ADD2((+))
    LN1 --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> LN2[Layer Norm]
    LN2 --> OUT[Output]
    style ADD1 fill:#69db7c
    style ADD2 fill:#69db7c
    style LN1 fill:#74c0fc
    style LN2 fill:#74c0fc

steps.title: Pre-LN vs Post-LN
steps.explanation: Original transformer uses Post-LN: LN after residual add. Many modern models use Pre-LN: LN before sub-layer. Pre-LN is more stable for training very deep models.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Post_LN_Original
        X1[x] --> SUB1[Sub-Layer]
        SUB1 --> A1((+))
        X1 --> A1
        A1 --> LN1[LayerNorm]
    end
    subgraph Pre_LN_Modern
        X2[x] --> LN2[LayerNorm]
        LN2 --> SUB2[Sub-Layer]
        SUB2 --> A2((+))
        X2 --> A2
    end
    style LN1 fill:#ffd43b
    style LN2 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: RMSNorm Alternative
steps.explanation: RMSNorm removes the mean centering, using only the root mean square. Faster to compute with similar effectiveness. Used in LLaMA and other efficient models.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph LayerNorm
        LN1[Subtract mean]
        LN2[Divide by std]
        LN3[Scale and shift]
    end
    subgraph RMSNorm
        RMS1[Compute RMS]
        RMS2[Divide by RMS]
        RMS3[Scale only]
    end
    LN1 --> LN2 --> LN3
    RMS1 --> RMS2 --> RMS3
    style LN1 fill:#ffd43b
    style RMS1 fill:#69db7c
    style RMS3 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Complete Sub-Layer Flow
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: input goes through normalization, then the sub-layer, then adds back to input. This pattern repeats for attention and FFN in each transformer layer.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    IN[Input from previous layer] --> LN[Layer Norm]
    LN --> SUB[Sub-Layer: Attention or FFN]
    SUB --> DROP[Dropout]
    DROP --> ADD((+))
    IN --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[To next sub-layer]
    style LN fill:#74c0fc
    style ADD fill:#69db7c
    style OUT fill:#b197fc

fields.title: Tokenization Pipeline
fields.description: Understand how text is converted to tokens using BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece algorithms - the critical first step in all transformer models.

steps.title: Why Tokenization Matters
steps.explanation: Transformers cannot process raw text. Text must be converted to numerical tokens. The choice of tokenization algorithm affects vocabulary size, handling of unknown words, and model performance.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    TEXT[Raw Text: Hello world] --> TOK[Tokenizer]
    TOK --> IDS[Token IDs: 15496 995]
    IDS --> EMB[Embedding Layer]
    EMB --> TRANS[Transformer]
    style TOK fill:#74c0fc
    style IDS fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Word-Level Tokenization
steps.explanation: Simplest approach: split on whitespace and punctuation. Problem: huge vocabulary size, cannot handle out-of-vocabulary words, and morphological variants are treated as unrelated.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Word_Level
        W1[running] --> ID1[Token 5234]
        W2[runs] --> ID2[Token 8912]
        W3[runner] --> ID3[Token 12045]
        W4[ran] --> ID4[Token 3421]
    end
    PROB[Problem: All treated as unrelated words]
    style PROB fill:#ffa8a8

steps.title: Character-Level Tokenization
steps.explanation: Tokenize each character individually. Very small vocabulary but very long sequences. Loses word-level semantics and requires the model to learn spelling.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    TEXT[hello] --> C1[h]
    TEXT --> C2[e]
    TEXT --> C3[l]
    TEXT --> C4[l]
    TEXT --> C5[o]
    C1 --> V[Vocabulary: 26 letters + special]
    style V fill:#ffd43b

steps.title: Subword Tokenization
steps.explanation: Best of both worlds: common words stay whole, rare words split into subwords. Handles unknown words by composition. BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece are all subword methods.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Subword
        W1[unhappiness] --> S1[un]
        W1 --> S2[happiness]
        W2[running] --> S3[run]
        W2 --> S4[##ning]
        W3[cat] --> S5[cat]
    end
    style S1 fill:#74c0fc
    style S2 fill:#74c0fc
    style S3 fill:#69db7c
    style S4 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Byte Pair Encoding BPE
steps.explanation: Start with characters, iteratively merge most frequent adjacent pairs. GPT-2, GPT-3, and many models use BPE. Greedy algorithm that builds vocabulary bottom-up.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    START[Start: character vocabulary]
    I1[Iteration 1: Merge most frequent pair]
    I2[Iteration 2: Merge next frequent pair]
    I3[Continue until vocab size reached]
    START --> I1 --> I2 --> I3
    subgraph Example
        E1[l o w --> low]
        E2[e r --> er]
        E3[low er --> lower]
    end
    style START fill:#ffd43b
    style I3 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: BPE Training Example
steps.explanation: Given corpus with words low, lower, newest, widest. Count pairs, merge most frequent, repeat. Final vocabulary includes full words and useful subwords.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Initial
        V1[l o w: 5]
        V2[l o w e r: 2]
        V3[n e w e s t: 6]
        V4[w i d e s t: 3]
    end
    subgraph After_Merges
        M1[es -> es]
        M2[est -> est]
        M3[lo -> lo]
        M4[low -> low]
    end
    V3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    V1 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    style M4 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: WordPiece
steps.explanation: Similar to BPE but uses likelihood-based selection. Merges pairs that maximize likelihood of training data. Used by BERT. Marks continuation tokens with ## prefix.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    INPUT[unaffable] --> TOK[WordPiece]
    TOK --> T1[un]
    TOK --> T2[##aff]
    TOK --> T3[##able]
    NOTE[## marks word continuation]
    style T1 fill:#74c0fc
    style T2 fill:#69db7c
    style T3 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: SentencePiece
steps.explanation: Language-agnostic tokenizer that treats text as raw bytes. Does not require pre-tokenization. Uses either BPE or Unigram algorithm. Used by T5, LLaMA, and multilingual models.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    subgraph Traditional
        T1[Whitespace split first]
        T2[Then subword tokenize]
    end
    subgraph SentencePiece
        S1[Treat space as special char]
        S2[Tokenize raw text directly]
        S3[Language agnostic]
    end
    style S1 fill:#69db7c
    style S2 fill:#69db7c
    style S3 fill:#69db7c

steps.title: Special Tokens
steps.explanation: Models use special tokens for structure. CLS for classification, SEP for separation, PAD for padding, UNK for unknown, MASK for masked language modeling.
steps.diagram:
graph LR
    subgraph BERT_Example
        CLS[[CLS]] --> T1[Hello]
        T1 --> T2[world]
        T2 --> SEP[[SEP]]
        SEP --> T3[How]
        T3 --> T4[are]
        T4 --> T5[you]
        T5 --> SEP2[[SEP]]
    end
    style CLS fill:#b197fc
    style SEP fill:#b197fc
    style SEP2 fill:#b197fc

steps.title: Complete Tokenization Pipeline
steps.explanation: Full pipeline: normalize text, pre-tokenize, apply subword algorithm, add special tokens, convert to IDs. Each model has its own trained tokenizer with specific vocabulary.
steps.diagram:
graph TD
    RAW[Raw Text] --> NORM[Normalize: lowercase, unicode]
    NORM --> PRE[Pre-tokenize: split on whitespace]
    PRE --> SUB[Subword: BPE or WordPiece]
    SUB --> SPEC[Add special tokens]
    SPEC --> IDS[Convert to token IDs]
    IDS --> ATTN[Create attention mask]
    IDS --> EMB[To embedding layer]
    style SUB fill:#74c0fc
    style IDS fill:#69db7c
    style EMB fill:#b197fc

fields.title: NVIDIA GPU Generations: Volta to Blackwell
fields.description: Explore the evolution of NVIDIA GPUs for ML/AI, from Volta's introduction of Tensor Cores to Blackwell's latest innovations

steps.title: GPU Generation Timeline
steps.explanation: NVIDIA has released several GPU architectures optimized for AI/ML workloads. Each generation brings significant improvements in Tensor Core performance, memory bandwidth, and specialized features for deep learning.
steps.diagram_data:
timeline
    title NVIDIA GPU Architectures for AI/ML
    2017 : Volta (V100)
         : 1st Gen Tensor Cores
    2018 : Turing (T4, RTX 20)
         : 2nd Gen Tensor Cores
         : RT Cores introduced
    2020 : Ampere (A100, RTX 30)
         : 3rd Gen Tensor Cores
         : TF32 format
    2022 : Ada Lovelace (L40, RTX 40)
         : 4th Gen Tensor Cores
         : FP8 support
    2022 : Hopper (H100)
         : Transformer Engine
         : HBM3 memory
    2024 : Blackwell (B100/B200)
         : 5th Gen Tensor Cores
         : 2nd Gen Transformer Engine

steps.title: Volta (2017) - The Tensor Core Revolution
steps.explanation:
**Volta** introduced **Tensor Cores** - specialized matrix multiply units that revolutionized deep learning training. The V100 became the workhorse of AI research, offering mixed-precision (FP16/FP32) training with massive speedups.

**Key Specs (V100):**
- 5,120 CUDA Cores
- 640 Tensor Cores (1st Gen)
- 16GB/32GB HBM2 (900 GB/s)
- 125 TFLOPS Tensor (FP16)
- NVLink 2.0 (300 GB/s)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Volta V100 Architecture" SM["80 Streaming Multiprocessors"] TC["640 Tensor Cores
(8 per SM)"] CUDA["5,120 CUDA Cores"] HBM["HBM2 Memory
16/32GB @ 900 GB/s"] NVL["NVLink 2.0
300 GB/s"] end subgraph "Tensor Core Operation" Matrix["D = A × B + C
(4×4 matrices)"] FP16["FP16 inputs"] FP32["FP32 accumulate"] end SM --> TC SM --> CUDA TC --> Matrix FP16 --> Matrix Matrix --> FP32 style TC fill:#90EE90 style Matrix fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Turing (2018) - RT Cores & INT8 Inference
steps.explanation:
**Turing** brought 2nd Gen Tensor Cores with **INT8 inference** support, enabling faster and more efficient model deployment. Also introduced RT Cores for ray tracing (less relevant for ML).

**Key Specs (T4):**
- 2,560 CUDA Cores
- 320 Tensor Cores (2nd Gen)
- 16GB GDDR6 (320 GB/s)
- 65 TFLOPS (FP16), 130 TOPS (INT8)
- Popular for inference workloads
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Turing Improvements" TC2["2nd Gen Tensor Cores"] INT8["INT8 Precision
2x INT8 throughput"] RT["RT Cores
(Ray Tracing)"] INF["Inference Optimized"] end subgraph "T4 Use Cases" Cloud["Cloud Inference
(AWS, GCP)"] Edge["Edge Deployment"] Video["Video Processing"] end TC2 --> INT8 INT8 --> INF INF --> Cloud INF --> Edge INF --> Video style TC2 fill:#90EE90 style INT8 fill:#FFFACD style INF fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Ampere (2020) - TF32 & Sparsity
steps.explanation:
**Ampere** introduced **TF32** - a new format that provides FP32-level accuracy with Tensor Core speeds (no code changes needed!). Also added **structural sparsity** support for 2x speedups on sparse models.

**Key Specs (A100):**
- 6,912 CUDA Cores
- 432 Tensor Cores (3rd Gen)
- 40GB/80GB HBM2e (2 TB/s)
- 312 TFLOPS (TF32), 624 TFLOPS (FP16)
- Multi-Instance GPU (MIG)
- NVLink 3.0 (600 GB/s)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Ampere A100 Features" TC3["3rd Gen Tensor Cores"] TF32["TF32 Format
FP32 range, FP16 speed"] BF16["BF16 Support"] Sparse["Structural Sparsity
2:4 pattern = 2x speedup"] MIG["Multi-Instance GPU
