Up to 7 instances"] end subgraph "Precision Formats" direction LR F32["FP32: 19 TFLOPS"] T32["TF32: 156 TFLOPS"] F16["FP16: 312 TFLOPS"] I8["INT8: 624 TOPS"] end TC3 --> TF32 TC3 --> BF16 TC3 --> Sparse style TC3 fill:#90EE90 style TF32 fill:#FFD700 style Sparse fill:#DDA0DD style MIG fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Ada Lovelace (2022) - FP8 & Efficiency
steps.explanation:
**Ada Lovelace** brought 4th Gen Tensor Cores with **FP8 precision** for even faster inference. Focused on efficiency improvements and consumer/professional markets (RTX 40 series, L40).

**Key Specs (L40):**
- 18,176 CUDA Cores
- 568 Tensor Cores (4th Gen)
- 48GB GDDR6 (864 GB/s)
- 181 TFLOPS (FP16), 362 TFLOPS (FP8)
- DLSS 3 / AI-accelerated features
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Ada Lovelace Features" TC4["4th Gen Tensor Cores"] FP8["FP8 Precision
E4M3 & E5M2 formats"] DLSS["DLSS 3
AI Frame Generation"] EFF["Power Efficiency
2x perf/watt vs Ampere"] end subgraph "FP8 Benefits" Train["Faster Training
(with loss scaling)"] Infer["2x Inference Speed"] Mem["Lower Memory Usage"] end TC4 --> FP8 FP8 --> Train FP8 --> Infer FP8 --> Mem style TC4 fill:#90EE90 style FP8 fill:#FFD700 style EFF fill:#98FB98

steps.title: Hopper (2022) - Transformer Engine
steps.explanation:
**Hopper** was designed specifically for large language models with the **Transformer Engine** - hardware that automatically manages FP8/FP16 precision per-layer. Features HBM3 memory and massive improvements for LLM training.

**Key Specs (H100 SXM):**
- 16,896 CUDA Cores
- 528 Tensor Cores (4th Gen)
- 80GB HBM3 (3.35 TB/s)
- 989 TFLOPS (FP16), 1,979 TFLOPS (FP8)
- Transformer Engine
- NVLink 4.0 (900 GB/s)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hopper H100 Features" TE["Transformer Engine
Auto FP8-FP16 per layer"] HBM3["HBM3 Memory
3.35 TB/s bandwidth"] NVL4["NVLink 4.0
900 GB/s"] DPX["DPX Instructions
Dynamic Programming"] end subgraph "Transformer Engine Detail" Layer1["Layer 1: FP8
(stable gradients)"] Layer2["Layer 2: FP16
(sensitive layer)"] Layer3["Layer 3: FP8
(stable gradients)"] Auto["Automatic Selection
Per-layer optimization"] end TE --> Layer1 TE --> Layer2 TE --> Layer3 TE --> Auto style TE fill:#FFD700 style HBM3 fill:#ADD8E6 style Auto fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Blackwell (2024) - The Next Frontier
steps.explanation:
**Blackwell** represents NVIDIA's latest architecture with 5th Gen Tensor Cores, 2nd Gen Transformer Engine, and unprecedented scale for training frontier models.

**Key Specs (B200):**
- 208B transistors (2 dies)
- 5th Gen Tensor Cores
- 192GB HBM3e (8 TB/s)
- 4,500 TFLOPS (FP8), 9,000 TFLOPS (FP4)
- 2nd Gen Transformer Engine
- NVLink 5.0 (1.8 TB/s)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Blackwell B200 Features" TC5["5th Gen Tensor Cores"] TE2["2nd Gen Transformer Engine"] FP4["FP4 Precision
New lowest precision"] MCM["Multi-Chip Module
2 dies, 208B transistors"] HBM3E["HBM3e Memory
192GB @ 8 TB/s"] end subgraph "Performance Leap" Train["4x Training Speed
vs H100"] Infer["30x Inference Speed
for LLMs"] Power["25x Energy Efficiency
vs H100"] end TC5 --> Train TE2 --> Infer FP4 --> Power style TC5 fill:#90EE90 style TE2 fill:#FFD700 style FP4 fill:#DDA0DD style MCM fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Tensor Core Evolution
steps.explanation: Tensor Cores have evolved dramatically across generations, with each version adding new precision formats and capabilities. Understanding these helps you choose the right GPU and optimize your code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph Gen1["1st Gen - Volta"]
        V1["FP16 to FP32"]
    end

    subgraph Gen2["2nd Gen - Turing"]
        V2["FP16 to FP32"]
        V2b["INT8, INT4, INT1"]
    end

    subgraph Gen3["3rd Gen - Ampere"]
        V3["TF32, BF16"]
        V3b["FP64 Tensor Cores"]
        V3c["Sparsity Support"]
    end

    subgraph Gen4["4th Gen - Ada/Hopper"]
        V4["FP8 E4M3, E5M2"]
        V4b["Transformer Engine"]
    end

    subgraph Gen5["5th Gen - Blackwell"]
        V5["FP4"]
        V5b["2nd Gen TE"]
        V5c["9 PFLOPS peak"]
    end

    Gen1 --> Gen2 --> Gen3 --> Gen4 --> Gen5

    style Gen1 fill:#E6E6FA
    style Gen2 fill:#FFFACD
    style Gen3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style Gen4 fill:#90EE90
    style Gen5 fill:#FFD700

steps.title: Memory Bandwidth Progression
steps.explanation: Memory bandwidth is often the bottleneck in ML workloads. Each generation has significantly increased bandwidth through faster memory technologies (HBM2 -> HBM2e -> HBM3 -> HBM3e).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Memory Evolution" V["V100
HBM2
900 GB/s"] A["A100
HBM2e
2,039 GB/s"] H["H100
HBM3
3,350 GB/s"] B["B200
HBM3e
8,000 GB/s"] end V -->|"2.3x"| A A -->|"1.6x"| H H -->|"2.4x"| B subgraph "Capacity Growth" VC["16-32 GB"] AC["40-80 GB"] HC["80 GB"] BC["192 GB"] end style V fill:#E6E6FA style A fill:#ADD8E6 style H fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#FFD700

steps.title: Choosing the Right GPU
steps.explanation: Different GPUs serve different purposes. Consider your workload (training vs inference), model size, and budget when selecting hardware.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Start{What is your use case?} Start -->|"Training
Large Models"| Train Start -->|"Inference
Production"| Infer Start -->|"Development
Experimentation"| Dev subgraph Train["Training Recommendations"] H100T["H100/H200
LLMs, Foundation Models"] A100T["A100 80GB
Large CV/NLP Models"] A10T["A10G
Medium Models"] end subgraph Infer["Inference Recommendations"] L40["L40/L40S
Balanced perf/cost"] T4["T4
Cost-effective"] H100I["H100
Latency-critical LLMs"] end subgraph Dev["Development Recommendations"] RTX["RTX 4090
Best consumer GPU"] A10D["A10
Cloud dev instance"] T4D["T4
Budget option"] end style H100T fill:#FFD700 style A100T fill:#90EE90 style L40 fill:#ADD8E6 style RTX fill:#DDA0DD

steps.title: PyTorch GPU Optimization Tips
steps.explanation: To leverage these GPU features in PyTorch, use automatic mixed precision (AMP), enable TF32 on Ampere+, and consider torch.compile for additional optimizations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Enable Mixed Precision" AMP["torch.autocast
torch.amp.GradScaler"] end subgraph "Enable TF32 - Ampere+" TF32["torch.backends.cuda
.matmul.allow_tf32 = True"] end subgraph "Compile - PyTorch 2.0+" Compile["model = torch.compile
mode='max-autotune'"] end subgraph "Results" Speed["2-3x Training Speed"] Mem["Lower Memory Usage"] Easy["No Code Changes
(for TF32)"] end AMP --> Speed TF32 --> Easy Compile --> Speed AMP --> Mem style AMP fill:#90EE90 style TF32 fill:#FFD700 style Compile fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: GPU Architecture: Components Deep Dive
fields.description: Understand the key components of a modern GPU - from Streaming Multiprocessors to memory hierarchy

steps.title: GPU vs CPU: High-Level View
steps.explanation: A **GPU** is designed for massive parallelism with thousands of simple cores, while a **CPU** has fewer but more powerful cores optimized for sequential tasks. GPUs excel at tasks that can be broken into many independent operations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph CPU["CPU - Few Powerful Cores"] direction LR C1["Core 1
Complex ALU"] C2["Core 2
Complex ALU"] C3["Core 3
Complex ALU"] C4["Core 4
Complex ALU"] CC["Large Cache"] end subgraph GPU["GPU - Many Simple Cores"] direction TB SM1["SM"] SM2["SM"] SM3["SM"] SM4["SM"] SM5["..."] SM6["SM"] SMN["80+ SMs"] end CPU -->|"Best for"| Seq["Sequential Tasks
Complex Logic
Branch-Heavy Code"] GPU -->|"Best for"| Par["Parallel Tasks
Matrix Operations
Data-Parallel Workloads"] style CPU fill:#ADD8E6 style GPU fill:#90EE90 style Par fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: GPU High-Level Architecture
steps.explanation: A modern NVIDIA GPU consists of multiple **Graphics Processing Clusters (GPCs)**, each containing multiple **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**. The GPU also has L2 cache, memory controllers, and high-bandwidth memory (HBM or GDDR).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph GPU["NVIDIA GPU - e.g. H100"] subgraph GPC1["GPC 1"] SM1["SM"] SM2["SM"] SM3["SM"] end subgraph GPC2["GPC 2"] SM4["SM"] SM5["SM"] SM6["SM"] end subgraph GPC3["GPC ..."] SM7["SM"] SM8["SM"] SM9["SM"] end L2["L2 Cache - 50+ MB"] MC["Memory Controllers"] end HBM["HBM3 Memory

steps.title: Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)
steps.explanation: The **SM** is the fundamental building block of a GPU. Each SM contains CUDA cores for general computation, Tensor Cores for matrix operations, shared memory/L1 cache, and warp schedulers that manage thread execution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph SM["Streaming Multiprocessor"] subgraph Compute["Compute Units"] CUDA["128 CUDA Cores
FP32/INT32"] TC["4 Tensor Cores
Matrix Ops"] SFU["Special Function Units
sin, cos, sqrt"] LD["Load/Store Units"] end subgraph Sched["Scheduling"] WS1["Warp Scheduler 1"] WS2["Warp Scheduler 2"] WS3["Warp Scheduler 3"] WS4["Warp Scheduler 4"] end subgraph Mem["Memory"] RF["Register File
256 KB"] SMEM["Shared Memory
+ L1 Cache
128-228 KB"] end end Sched --> Compute Compute --> Mem style TC fill:#90EE90 style CUDA fill:#ADD8E6 style SMEM fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: CUDA Cores
steps.explanation: **CUDA Cores** are the basic processing units that execute arithmetic operations. Each CUDA core can perform one floating-point or integer operation per clock cycle. Modern GPUs have thousands of CUDA cores distributed across all SMs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CUDA Core" FPU["FP32 Unit
Floating Point"] ALU["INT32 Unit
Integer"] Dispatch["Dispatch Port"] Result["Result Bus"] end subgraph "Operations" Add["Addition"] Mul["Multiplication"] FMA["Fused Multiply-Add
a * b + c"] Logic["Bitwise Logic"] end Dispatch --> FPU Dispatch --> ALU FPU --> Result ALU --> Result FPU --> Add FPU --> Mul FPU --> FMA subgraph "Scale" Count["H100: 16,896 CUDA Cores
A100: 6,912 CUDA Cores
RTX 4090: 16,384 CUDA Cores"] end style FMA fill:#90EE90 style Count fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Tensor Cores
steps.explanation: **Tensor Cores** are specialized units designed for matrix multiply-accumulate operations (D = A x B + C). They operate on small matrices (e.g., 4x4 or 8x8) and provide massive speedups for deep learning workloads.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Tensor Core Operation" A["Matrix A
4x4 FP16"] B["Matrix B
4x4 FP16"] C["Matrix C
4x4 FP32"] D["Matrix D
4x4 FP32"] end A --> MMA["Matrix Multiply
Accumulate"] B --> MMA C --> MMA MMA --> D subgraph "Supported Precisions" FP16["FP16/BF16"] TF32["TF32"] FP8["FP8"] INT8["INT8"] FP64["FP64 - A100+"] end subgraph "Performance - H100" TFLOP["FP16: 989 TFLOPS
FP8: 1,979 TFLOPS
vs CUDA: ~60 TFLOPS"] end style MMA fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#FFFACD style TFLOP fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Warp Schedulers and Execution
steps.explanation: Threads on a GPU are organized into **warps** of 32 threads that execute in lockstep (SIMT - Single Instruction, Multiple Threads). **Warp schedulers** select ready warps and issue instructions to the execution units.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Thread Hierarchy" Grid["Grid
All Threads"] Block["Thread Block
Up to 1024 threads"] Warp["Warp
32 threads"] Thread["Thread"] end Grid --> Block Block --> Warp Warp --> Thread subgraph "Warp Execution" WS["Warp Scheduler"] Ready["Ready Warps"] Stalled["Stalled Warps
waiting for memory"] Exec["Execute 1 instruction
across all 32 threads"] end WS --> Ready Ready --> Exec Stalled -.->|"data arrives"| Ready subgraph "Key Concept" SIMT["SIMT: All 32 threads
execute SAME instruction
on DIFFERENT data"] end style Warp fill:#90EE90 style SIMT fill:#FFFACD style WS fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Memory Hierarchy Overview
steps.explanation: GPUs have a complex memory hierarchy optimized for throughput. Understanding it is crucial for writing efficient GPU code. Memory closer to the cores is faster but smaller.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Memory Hierarchy - Fastest to Slowest" R["Registers
~256 KB per SM
~0 cycles latency"] SM["Shared Memory / L1
~128-228 KB per SM
~20-30 cycles"] L2["L2 Cache
~50 MB total
~200 cycles"] HBM["HBM / Global Memory
80 GB
~400-800 cycles"] end R -->|"Fastest"| SM SM --> L2 L2 -->|"Slowest"| HBM subgraph "Bandwidth" BW_R["Registers: ~20 TB/s"] BW_SM["Shared: ~10 TB/s"] BW_L2["L2: ~5 TB/s"] BW_HBM["HBM3: ~3 TB/s"] end style R fill:#90EE90 style SM fill:#FFFACD style L2 fill:#ADD8E6 style HBM fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Registers
steps.explanation: **Registers** are the fastest memory, private to each thread. The compiler allocates registers automatically. Using too many registers per thread reduces **occupancy** (number of concurrent warps).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Register Allocation" Thread1["Thread 1
32 registers"] Thread2["Thread 2
32 registers"] ThreadN["Thread N
32 registers"] Pool["SM Register File
65,536 registers"] end Pool --> Thread1 Pool --> Thread2 Pool --> ThreadN subgraph "Trade-off" More["More Registers/Thread"] Fewer["Fewer Active Warps"] Less["Less Latency Hiding"] end More --> Fewer --> Less subgraph "Best Practice" Tip["Limit register usage
Use --maxrregcount
Monitor with nvcc -Xptxas -v"] end style Pool fill:#90EE90 style Tip fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Shared Memory
steps.explanation: **Shared Memory** is fast, programmer-managed memory shared by all threads in a thread block. It's ideal for data reuse within a block and reducing global memory access.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Thread Block" T1["Thread 1"] T2["Thread 2"] T3["Thread 3"] TN["Thread N"] SMEM["Shared Memory
Up to 228 KB"] end T1 <--> SMEM T2 <--> SMEM T3 <--> SMEM TN <--> SMEM subgraph "Use Cases" Tile["Matrix Tiling"] Reduce["Reductions"] Comm["Thread Communication"] Cache["Manual Caching"] end SMEM --> Tile SMEM --> Reduce SMEM --> Comm subgraph "Code Example" Code["__shared__ float data[256]
data[threadIdx.x] = ...
__syncthreads()
// All threads can read"] end style SMEM fill:#FFFACD style Code fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: L2 Cache and Global Memory
steps.explanation: **L2 Cache** is shared across all SMs and automatically caches global memory accesses. **Global Memory (HBM)** is the largest but slowest memory, accessible by all threads and the CPU.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "All SMs" SM1["SM 1"] SM2["SM 2"] SMN["SM N"] end L2["L2 Cache
50+ MB
Automatic Caching"] SM1 <--> L2 SM2 <--> L2 SMN <--> L2 subgraph "Memory Controllers" MC1["MC 1"] MC2["MC 2"] MC3["MC 3"] end L2 <--> MC1 L2 <--> MC2 L2 <--> MC3 HBM["HBM3 Global Memory
80 GB @ 3+ TB/s"] MC1 <--> HBM MC2 <--> HBM MC3 <--> HBM style L2 fill:#ADD8E6 style HBM fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Memory Coalescing
steps.explanation: **Memory coalescing** is critical for performance. When threads in a warp access consecutive memory addresses, the GPU combines them into fewer memory transactions. Uncoalesced access wastes bandwidth.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coalesced Access - GOOD" W1["Warp: 32 Threads"] CA["Thread 0 -> addr 0
Thread 1 -> addr 4
Thread 2 -> addr 8
...
Thread 31 -> addr 124"] One["1 Memory Transaction
128 bytes"] end W1 --> CA --> One subgraph "Uncoalesced Access - BAD" W2["Warp: 32 Threads"] UA["Thread 0 -> addr 0
Thread 1 -> addr 512
Thread 2 -> addr 1024
...
Random pattern"] Many["32 Memory Transactions
Massive slowdown"] end W2 --> UA --> Many style One fill:#90EE90 style Many fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: NVLink and PCIe
steps.explanation: **NVLink** provides high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU communication (up to 900 GB/s on H100), enabling multi-GPU training. **PCIe** connects the GPU to the CPU/host memory (much slower, ~64 GB/s for Gen5).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Multi-GPU System" GPU1["GPU 1"] GPU2["GPU 2"] GPU3["GPU 3"] GPU4["GPU 4"] end GPU1 <-->|"NVLink
900 GB/s"| GPU2 GPU2 <-->|"NVLink"| GPU3 GPU3 <-->|"NVLink"| GPU4 GPU4 <-->|"NVLink"| GPU1 CPU["CPU / Host"] RAM["System RAM"] GPU1 <-->|"PCIe Gen5
64 GB/s"| CPU CPU <--> RAM subgraph "Use Cases" NVL_Use["NVLink: Tensor Parallelism
All-Reduce, P2P Transfer"] PCIe_Use["PCIe: Data Loading
Model Transfer"] end style GPU1 fill:#90EE90 style GPU2 fill:#90EE90 style GPU3 fill:#90EE90 style GPU4 fill:#90EE90 style NVL_Use fill:#ADD8E6

steps.explanation: When you launch a CUDA kernel, the GPU distributes thread blocks across SMs, warps get scheduled, threads access memory through the hierarchy, and results flow back. Understanding this flow helps optimize performance.

Issue Instructions"] Cores["CUDA/Tensor Cores
Compute"] Mem["Memory Access
Reg->Shared->L2->HBM"] end SM1 --> WS --> Cores --> Mem subgraph "Optimization Goals" Occ["High Occupancy
many active warps"] Coal["Coalesced Memory"] Reuse["Data Reuse

fields.title: Mixed Precision Training: Complete Guide
fields.description: Master FP32, FP16, BF16, TF32, and FP8 - when to use each precision format and how automatic mixed precision works

steps.title: Why Mixed Precision?
steps.explanation:
**Mixed precision training** uses lower-precision formats (FP16, BF16) for most operations while keeping critical computations in FP32. Benefits:

- **2-3x faster training** via Tensor Cores
- **~50% memory reduction** (smaller activations/gradients)
- **Same model accuracy** when done correctly

The key insight: most neural network operations don't need full FP32 precision.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Traditional FP32 Training" F32["All operations in FP32
32 bits per number"] F32_Mem["Memory: 100%"] F32_Speed["Speed: 1x
CUDA cores only"] end subgraph "Mixed Precision Training" Mixed["Most ops in FP16/BF16
Critical ops in FP32"] Mix_Mem["Memory: ~50%"] Mix_Speed["Speed: 2-3x
Tensor Cores"] end F32 --> |"Same accuracy"| Mixed style Mixed fill:#90EE90 style Mix_Speed fill:#90EE90 style Mix_Mem fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Precision Formats Overview
steps.explanation: Different floating-point formats trade off **range** (exponent bits) vs **precision** (mantissa bits). Understanding these helps you choose the right format for your workload.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Format Breakdown" FP32["FP32: 1 sign + 8 exp + 23 mantissa
Range: ~1e-38 to 1e38
Precision: ~7 decimal digits"] FP16["FP16: 1 sign + 5 exp + 10 mantissa
Range: ~6e-5 to 65504
Precision: ~3 decimal digits"] BF16["BF16: 1 sign + 8 exp + 7 mantissa
Range: same as FP32
Precision: ~2 decimal digits"] TF32["TF32: 1 sign + 8 exp + 10 mantissa
Range: same as FP32
Precision: ~3 decimal digits"] FP8["FP8: 1 sign + 4/5 exp + 2/3 mantissa
Very limited range/precision
Inference focused"] end FP32 --> FP16 FP32 --> BF16 FP32 --> TF32 FP16 --> FP8 style FP32 fill:#E6E6FA style BF16 fill:#90EE90 style TF32 fill:#FFD700

steps.title: FP16 vs BF16: The Key Difference
steps.explanation:
**FP16** has more precision but limited range (max ~65504). **BF16** has the same range as FP32 but less precision. For deep learning, **range matters more than precision** - gradients can be very small or large.

**BF16 is generally preferred** for training because:
- No overflow issues (same range as FP32)
- No loss scaling needed
- Supported on Ampere+ GPUs
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "FP16 Limitations" FP16_Over["Max value: 65504
Large gradients OVERFLOW"] FP16_Under["Min value: ~6e-5
Small gradients UNDERFLOW"] FP16_Fix["Requires LOSS SCALING
to prevent underflow"] end subgraph "BF16 Advantages" BF16_Range["Same range as FP32
No overflow issues"] BF16_Simple["No loss scaling needed
Drop-in replacement"] BF16_Req["Requires Ampere+ GPU
A100, RTX 30xx+"] end FP16_Fix -->|"or use"| BF16_Simple style FP16_Over fill:#FFB6C1 style FP16_Under fill:#FFB6C1 style BF16_Range fill:#90EE90 style BF16_Simple fill:#90EE90

steps.title: TF32: Automatic Speedup on Ampere+
steps.explanation:
**TF32 (TensorFloat-32)** is NVIDIA's format for Ampere+ GPUs. It provides FP32-level accuracy with Tensor Core speedups - **with zero code changes**.

TF32 uses FP32 range but only 10 mantissa bits internally during matrix operations. Results are stored as FP32.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "TF32 Operation" Input["FP32 Input"] Round["Round to 10-bit mantissa
internally"] TC["Tensor Core
Matrix Multiply"] Output["FP32 Output"] end Input --> Round --> TC --> Output subgraph "Enable in PyTorch" Code["torch.backends.cuda.matmul
.allow_tf32 = True

torch.backends.cudnn
.allow_tf32 = True"] end subgraph "Performance" Perf["8x faster than FP32 CUDA
Same accuracy for most models
Default ON in PyTorch 1.12+"] end style TC fill:#FFD700 style Perf fill:#90EE90 style Code fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Loss Scaling for FP16
steps.explanation: When using FP16, small gradients can **underflow to zero**. **Loss scaling** multiplies the loss by a large factor before backward pass, then divides gradients after. This keeps gradients in FP16's representable range.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Loss Scaling"
        Loss1["Loss: 0.001"]
        Grad1["Gradient: 1e-6"]
        FP16_1["FP16: UNDERFLOWS to 0"]
        Update1["No weight update!"]
    end

    Loss1 --> Grad1 --> FP16_1 --> Update1

    subgraph "With Loss Scaling"
        Loss2["Loss: 0.001"]
        Scale["Scale: x 1024"]
        Scaled["Scaled Loss: 1.024"]
        Grad2["Gradient: 0.001"]
        FP16_2["FP16: Representable"]
        Unscale["Unscale: / 1024"]
        Update2["Correct weight update"]
    end

    Loss2 --> Scale --> Scaled --> Grad2 --> FP16_2 --> Unscale --> Update2

    style FP16_1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Update1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style FP16_2 fill:#90EE90
    style Update2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Dynamic Loss Scaling
steps.explanation:
**Dynamic loss scaling** automatically adjusts the scale factor during training. If gradients overflow (become inf/nan), it reduces the scale. If no overflows occur for N steps, it increases the scale.

PyTorch's `GradScaler` handles this automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dynamic Loss Scaling Algorithm" Start["Initial scale: 65536"] Forward["Forward pass in FP16"] ScaleLoss["Multiply loss by scale"] Backward["Backward pass"] Check{{"Check for inf/nan
in gradients"}} Overflow["Skip update
Scale = Scale / 2"] NoOverflow["Unscale gradients
Optimizer step"] Count{{"N steps without
overflow?"}} Increase["Scale = Scale * 2"] Next["Next iteration"] end Start --> Forward --> ScaleLoss --> Backward --> Check Check -->|"inf/nan found"| Overflow --> Next Check -->|"gradients OK"| NoOverflow --> Count Count -->|"Yes"| Increase --> Next Count -->|"No"| Next Next --> Forward style Overflow fill:#FFB6C1 style NoOverflow fill:#90EE90 style Increase fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: PyTorch AMP: Automatic Mixed Precision
steps.explanation: PyTorch's **torch.amp** module provides easy mixed precision training with `autocast` and `GradScaler`. It automatically selects which ops run in FP16 vs FP32.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "AMP Training Loop" Code["scaler = GradScaler()

for data, target in loader:
optimizer.zero_grad()

with autocast(dtype=torch.float16):
output = model(data)
loss = criterion(output, target)

scaler.scale(loss).backward()
scaler.step(optimizer)
scaler.update()"] end subgraph "What autocast does" Auto1["Matmul, Conv -> FP16"] Auto2["Softmax, LayerNorm -> FP32"] Auto3["Loss functions -> FP32"] end subgraph "What GradScaler does" GS1["Scales loss before backward"] GS2["Unscales gradients"] GS3["Skips steps on overflow"] GS4["Dynamically adjusts scale"] end style Code fill:#E6E6FA style Auto1 fill:#90EE90 style Auto2 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Which Ops Use Which Precision?
steps.explanation: PyTorch's autocast automatically selects precision based on numerical stability. Some ops are always FP32 for safety, while compute-heavy ops use FP16 for speed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "FP16 - Fast Path" FP16_Ops["Linear / MatMul
Conv1d, Conv2d, Conv3d
BMM, GEMM
RNN cells (LSTM, GRU)"] FP16_Why["Tensor Core accelerated
Largest speedup"] end subgraph "FP32 - Safe Path" FP32_Ops["Softmax
LayerNorm, BatchNorm
Loss functions
Log, Exp, Pow
Sum, Mean reductions"] FP32_Why["Numerically sensitive
Need full precision"] end subgraph "Varies by Input" Vary_Ops["Pointwise ops
Activation functions
Dropout"] Vary_Why["Uses input dtype"] end style FP16_Ops fill:#90EE90 style FP32_Ops fill:#FFFACD style Vary_Ops fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: BF16 Training (Simpler)
steps.explanation: **BF16 training is simpler** than FP16 because it doesn't need loss scaling. On Ampere+ GPUs, BF16 is often the best choice for training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BF16 Training Loop" Code["# No GradScaler needed!

for data, target in loader:
optimizer.zero_grad()

with autocast(dtype=torch.bfloat16):
output = model(data)
loss = criterion(output, target)

loss.backward()
optimizer.step()"] end subgraph "BF16 Advantages" Adv1["No loss scaling"] Adv2["No GradScaler"] Adv3["Same range as FP32"] Adv4["Simpler code"] end subgraph "BF16 Requirements" Req1["Ampere+ GPU
A100, RTX 30xx, H100"] Req2["PyTorch 1.10+"] end style Code fill:#E6E6FA style Adv1 fill:#90EE90 style Adv4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: FP8 Training (Emerging)
steps.explanation: **FP8** is the newest format, available on Hopper (H100) GPUs. It offers 2x throughput over FP16 but requires careful handling. The **Transformer Engine** automates FP8 for transformer models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "FP8 Formats" E4M3["E4M3: 4 exp + 3 mantissa
Better precision
Forward pass"] E5M2["E5M2: 5 exp + 2 mantissa
Better range
Backward pass"] end subgraph "Transformer Engine" TE1["Automatic FP8 quantization"] TE2["Per-tensor scaling"] TE3["Delayed scaling"] TE4["Automatic format selection"] end subgraph "Usage" Code["import transformer_engine as te

model = te.Linear(in, out)
# Automatic FP8 in forward/backward"] end subgraph "Performance" Perf["2x throughput vs FP16
Requires H100 GPU
Best for transformers"] end style E4M3 fill:#90EE90 style E5M2 fill:#ADD8E6 style Perf fill:#FFD700

steps.title: Decision Flowchart: Which Precision?
steps.explanation: Use this flowchart to decide which precision format to use for your training workload.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Start{{"What GPU do you have?"}} Start -->|"Hopper H100"| Hopper Start -->|"Ampere A100/RTX30"| Ampere Start -->|"Turing/Volta"| Older subgraph Hopper["H100 Options"] H_Q{{"Training transformers?"}} H_Q -->|"Yes"| H_FP8["Use FP8 + Transformer Engine

steps.title: Common Pitfalls and Solutions
steps.explanation: Mixed precision training can fail in subtle ways. Here are the most common issues and how to fix them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Loss goes to NaN" NaN_Cause["Gradient overflow in FP16"] NaN_Fix["1. Use BF16 instead
2. Reduce learning rate
3. Add gradient clipping"] end subgraph "Training diverges" Div_Cause["Precision loss in accumulation"] Div_Fix["1. Keep optimizer states in FP32
2. Use FP32 for loss computation
3. Check BatchNorm is FP32"] end subgraph "Slow training" Slow_Cause["Ops not using Tensor Cores"] Slow_Fix["1. Ensure dims divisible by 8
2. Use channels_last format
3. Profile with torch.profiler"] end subgraph "Memory not reduced" Mem_Cause["FP32 copies kept"] Mem_Fix["1. Use model.half() for inference
2. Check optimizer states
3. Use gradient checkpointing"] end NaN_Cause --> NaN_Fix Div_Cause --> Div_Fix Slow_Cause --> Slow_Fix Mem_Cause --> Mem_Fix style NaN_Fix fill:#90EE90 style Div_Fix fill:#90EE90 style Slow_Fix fill:#90EE90 style Mem_Fix fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Best Practices Summary
steps.explanation: Follow these best practices for successful mixed precision training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" Do1["Use BF16 on Ampere+ GPUs"] Do2["Keep optimizer states in FP32"] Do3["Keep loss computation in FP32"] Do4["Use dimensions divisible by 8"] Do5["Profile to verify Tensor Core usage"] Do6["Start with default autocast settings"] end subgraph "Avoid" Dont1["Dont disable autocast for softmax/norms"] Dont2["Dont use FP16 weights for inference
without testing accuracy"] Dont3["Dont ignore inf/nan warnings"] Dont4["Dont skip loss scaling with FP16"] end subgraph "Performance Checklist" Check1["Batch size divisible by 8"] Check2["Hidden dims divisible by 8"] Check3["Sequence length divisible by 8"] Check4["Using channels_last for CNNs"] end style Do1 fill:#90EE90 style Do2 fill:#90EE90 style Dont1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Dont4 fill:#FFB6C1 style Check1 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: CUDA Programming Model: Grids, Blocks, and Threads
fields.description: Understand the CUDA execution model - how work is organized into grids, blocks, threads, and warps

steps.title: The Big Picture: Kernels
steps.explanation: A **CUDA kernel** is a function that runs on the GPU. When you launch a kernel, you specify how many **threads** should execute it. Each thread runs the same code but operates on different data - this is **SIMT** (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CPU (Host)" CPU_Code["Python/C++ Code"] Launch["kernel<<>>(args)"] end subgraph "GPU (Device)" Kernel["CUDA Kernel Function"] T1["Thread 0"] T2["Thread 1"] T3["Thread 2"] TN["Thread N-1"] end CPU_Code --> Launch Launch --> Kernel Kernel --> T1 Kernel --> T2 Kernel --> T3 Kernel --> TN subgraph "Key Concept" SIMT["Every thread executes
the SAME kernel code
on DIFFERENT data"] end style Launch fill:#ADD8E6 style SIMT fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Thread Hierarchy: Grid, Block, Thread
steps.explanation:
CUDA organizes threads in a **3-level hierarchy**:

1. **Grid** - All threads for one kernel launch
2. **Block** - A group of threads that can cooperate (share memory, synchronize)
3. **Thread** - Individual execution unit

This hierarchy maps to GPU hardware for efficient execution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph Grid["Grid (All Threads)"]
        subgraph Block0["Block 0"]
            T00["Thread 0"]
            T01["Thread 1"]
            T02["Thread ..."]
        end
        subgraph Block1["Block 1"]
            T10["Thread 0"]
            T11["Thread 1"]
            T12["Thread ..."]
        end
        subgraph Block2["Block 2"]
            T20["Thread 0"]
            T21["Thread 1"]
            T22["Thread ..."]
        end
        subgraph BlockN["Block N-1"]
            TN0["Thread 0"]
            TN1["Thread 1"]
            TN2["Thread ..."]
        end
    end

    subgraph "Hardware Mapping"
        HW1["Grid -> Entire GPU"]
        HW2["Block -> 1 SM"]
        HW3["Thread -> 1 CUDA Core"]
    end

    style Grid fill:#E6E6FA
    style Block0 fill:#ADD8E6
    style Block1 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Thread Indexing
steps.explanation:
Every thread knows its position via built-in variables:

- `threadIdx.x/y/z` - Position within block
- `blockIdx.x/y/z` - Block position within grid
- `blockDim.x/y/z` - Block dimensions
- `gridDim.x/y/z` - Grid dimensions

Global thread ID = `blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x`
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "1D Example: 256 elements, 64 threads per block" Grid1D["Grid: 4 blocks"] B0["Block 0
blockIdx.x = 0
threads 0-63"] B1["Block 1
blockIdx.x = 1
threads 64-127"] B2["Block 2
blockIdx.x = 2
threads 128-191"] B3["Block 3
blockIdx.x = 3
threads 192-255"] end Grid1D --> B0 Grid1D --> B1 Grid1D --> B2 Grid1D --> B3 subgraph "Global ID Formula" Formula["global_id = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x

Example: Block 2, Thread 5
= 2 * 64 + 5 = 133"] end style Formula fill:#FFFACD style B0 fill:#90EE90 style B2 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: 2D and 3D Grids
steps.explanation: Grids and blocks can be **1D, 2D, or 3D**. Use 2D for images/matrices, 3D for volumes. This is just syntactic sugar - hardware sees it as 1D.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "2D Grid for Image Processing" direction TB Row0["Block(0,0) | Block(1,0) | Block(2,0) | Block(3,0)"] Row1["Block(0,1) | Block(1,1) | Block(2,1) | Block(3,1)"] Row2["Block(0,2) | Block(1,2) | Block(2,2) | Block(3,2)"] end subgraph "2D Block for 16x16 Tile" Tile["Each block = 16x16 = 256 threads
threadIdx.x: 0-15
threadIdx.y: 0-15"] end subgraph "Pixel Position" Pos["pixel_x = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
pixel_y = blockIdx.y * blockDim.y + threadIdx.y"] end style Row1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Tile fill:#90EE90 style Pos fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Warps: The True Execution Unit
steps.explanation: The GPU executes threads in groups of **32 called warps**. All 32 threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** at the same time. This is critical for performance - divergent branches hurt!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Block of 128 Threads" Warp0["Warp 0
Threads 0-31"] Warp1["Warp 1
Threads 32-63"] Warp2["Warp 2
Threads 64-95"] Warp3["Warp 3
Threads 96-127"] end subgraph "Warp Execution" Exec["All 32 threads execute
SAME instruction
at SAME time"] end subgraph "Performance Impact" Good["All threads take same path
= Full efficiency"] Bad["Threads take different paths
= Serialized execution"] end Warp0 --> Exec Exec --> Good Exec --> Bad style Exec fill:#FFFACD style Good fill:#90EE90 style Bad fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Warp Divergence
steps.explanation: When threads in a warp take **different branches**, the warp must execute **both paths sequentially**, with inactive threads masked. This is called **warp divergence** and kills performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Divergent Code" Code["if (threadIdx.x < 16):
do_A() # Threads 0-15
else:
do_B() # Threads 16-31"] end subgraph "Execution (SLOW)" Step1["Step 1: Execute do_A()
Threads 0-15 active
Threads 16-31 MASKED"] Step2["Step 2: Execute do_B()
Threads 0-15 MASKED
Threads 16-31 active"] Step3["Step 3: Converge
All threads continue"] end Code --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 subgraph "Impact" Impact["2x slower!
Both paths executed sequentially"] end style Step1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Step2 fill:#FFB6C1 style Impact fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Block Limits and Occupancy
steps.explanation: Each block has limits on resources. **Occupancy** is the ratio of active warps to maximum possible warps on an SM. Higher occupancy helps hide memory latency.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Block Limits" Threads["Max 1024 threads per block"] Regs["Limited registers per SM
~65K registers"] Smem["Limited shared memory
48-228 KB per SM"] Blocks["Max ~16-32 blocks per SM"] end subgraph "Occupancy Calculation" Occ["Occupancy = Active Warps / Max Warps

Example SM with max 64 warps:
- 2 blocks x 256 threads = 16 warps
- Occupancy = 16/64 = 25%"] end subgraph "Trade-offs" Trade1["More threads per block
-> Fewer blocks fit"] Trade2["More registers per thread
-> Fewer warps fit"] Trade3["More shared memory per block
-> Fewer blocks fit"] end style Occ fill:#FFFACD style Threads fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Kernel Launch Syntax
steps.explanation: In CUDA C++, kernels are launched with the `<<<grid, block>>>` syntax. In PyTorch/Triton, this is abstracted but the concepts remain.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CUDA C++ Launch" CUDA["// Define kernel
__global__ void add(float* a, float* b, float* c) {
int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;
c[i] = a[i] + b[i];
}

// Launch kernel
add<<>>(a, b, c);"] end subgraph "PyTorch Custom CUDA" PyTorch["# Uses torch.cuda.launch_kernel
# or custom CUDA extension"] end subgraph "Triton (Recommended)" Triton["@triton.jit
def add_kernel(a_ptr, b_ptr, c_ptr, n):
pid = tl.program_id(0) # ~blockIdx
offsets = pid * BLOCK + tl.arange(0, BLOCK)
tl.store(c_ptr + offsets,
tl.load(a_ptr + offsets) +
tl.load(b_ptr + offsets))"] end style CUDA fill:#E6E6FA style Triton fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing Block Size
steps.explanation:
Block size affects performance significantly. Guidelines:

- **Minimum 128 threads** (4 warps) for latency hiding
- **Usually 256 or 512** for good occupancy
- **Multiple of 32** (warp size)
- **Power of 2** often works well
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TD Start{{"Choosing Block Size"}} Start --> Q1{{"Shared memory needed?"}} Q1 -->|"Heavy use"| Small["128-256 threads
More shared mem per thread"] Q1 -->|"Light/none"| Large["256-512 threads
Better occupancy"] Start --> Q2{{"Register pressure?"}} Q2 -->|"Many registers"| Smaller["Smaller blocks
Fit more warps"] Q2 -->|"Few registers"| Larger["Larger blocks OK"] subgraph "Common Choices" C1["1D: 256 or 512"] C2["2D: 16x16 = 256"] C3["3D: 8x8x8 = 512"] end subgraph "Always" Rule1["Multiple of 32"] Rule2["At most 1024"] Rule3["Profile to verify!"] end style Small fill:#ADD8E6 style Large fill:#90EE90 style Rule3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Synchronization Within Blocks
steps.explanation: Threads within a block can **synchronize** using `__syncthreads()`. This creates a barrier - all threads must reach it before any can proceed. **Cannot sync across blocks!**
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Within Block - OK" T1_1["Thread 0: Load data"] T2_1["Thread 1: Load data"] TN_1["Thread N: Load data"] Sync["__syncthreads()"] T1_2["Thread 0: Use shared data"] T2_2["Thread 1: Use shared data"] TN_2["Thread N: Use shared data"] end T1_1 --> Sync T2_1 --> Sync TN_1 --> Sync Sync --> T1_2 Sync --> T2_2 Sync --> TN_2 subgraph "Across Blocks - NOT POSSIBLE" Block1["Block 0"] Block2["Block 1"] NoSync["Cannot sync!
Blocks may run
in any order"] end Block1 -.- NoSync Block2 -.- NoSync style Sync fill:#90EE90 style NoSync fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Hardware Mapping Summary
steps.explanation: Understanding how the software hierarchy maps to hardware helps you write efficient kernels.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Software" Grid["Grid"] Block["Block"] Warp["Warp
32 threads"] Thread["Thread"] end subgraph "Hardware" GPU["Entire GPU"] SM["Streaming Multiprocessor"] WarpSched["Warp Scheduler"] Core["CUDA Core"] end Grid --> GPU Block --> SM Warp --> WarpSched Thread --> Core subgraph "Key Points" K1["Multiple blocks can run on 1 SM"] K2["Blocks cannot migrate between SMs"] K3["Warps are scheduled independently"] K4["All 32 threads execute together"] end style Grid fill:#E6E6FA style Block fill:#ADD8E6 style Warp fill:#90EE90 style Thread fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Complete Example: Vector Add
steps.explanation: Let's trace through a complete vector addition example to see all concepts in action.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" Prob["Add two arrays of 10,000 elements
C[i] = A[i] + B[i]"] end subgraph "Configuration" Config["threads_per_block = 256
num_blocks = ceil(10000/256) = 40
Total threads = 40 * 256 = 10,240"] end subgraph "Kernel Code" Code["__global__ void add(float* A, float* B, float* C, int N) {

if (i < N) { // Bounds check!
C[i] = A[i] + B[i];
}
}"] end subgraph "Execution" B0["Block 0: threads 0-255
Process elements 0-255"] B1["Block 1: threads 256-511
Process elements 256-511"] B39["Block 39: threads 9984-10239
Process elements 9984-9999
Threads 10000-10239 exit early"] end Prob --> Config --> Code --> B0 Code --> B1 Code --> B39 style Config fill:#ADD8E6 style Code fill:#E6E6FA style B39 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: These patterns appear frequently in CUDA kernels. Mastering them helps you write efficient GPU code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Grid-Stride Loop" GSL["Process more elements than threads:

for (int i = idx; i < N; i += gridDim.x * blockDim.x)
process(i);"] GSL_Why["Handles any array size
Reuses threads efficiently"] end subgraph "Tiled Processing" Tile["Load tile to shared memory
__syncthreads()
Process tile
__syncthreads()
Move to next tile"] Tile_Why["Reduces global memory access
Essential for matrix multiply"] end subgraph "Reduction" Red["Parallel sum/max/min:
Each thread loads one element
Tree-based reduction in shared mem
Block result -> global memory"] Red_Why["Log(N) steps instead of N"] end style GSL fill:#ADD8E6 style Tile fill:#90EE90 style Red fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Pandas Merge/Join Operations
fields.description: Master the different types of joins in Pandas - inner, outer, left, and right joins with clear visual examples

steps.title: Our Example DataFrames
steps.explanation: Let's work with two DataFrames that share a common column (`id`). The **left** DataFrame has employees, and the **right** DataFrame has departments. Notice that not all IDs exist in both tables - this is where join types matter!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Left DataFrame: employees" L["| id | name | salary |
|----|---------|--------|
| 1 | Alice | 50000 |
| 2 | Bob | 60000 |
| 3 | Charlie | 55000 |
| 4 | Diana | 70000 |"] end subgraph "Right DataFrame: departments" R["| id | dept |
|----|------------|
| 1 | Engineering|
| 2 | Marketing |
| 5 | Sales |
| 6 | HR |"] end subgraph "Matching Keys" Match["IDs 1, 2: exist in BOTH
IDs 3, 4: only in LEFT
IDs 5, 6: only in RIGHT"] end L --> Match R --> Match style L fill:#ADD8E6 style R fill:#90EE90 style Match fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Inner Join - Intersection Only
steps.explanation:
**Inner join** keeps only rows where the key exists in **both** DataFrames. This is the most restrictive join type - you lose rows that don't have matches.

```python
pd.merge(employees, departments, on='id', how='inner')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Left: employees" L1["1 - Alice"] L2["2 - Bob"] L3["3 - Charlie"] L4["4 - Diana"] end subgraph "Right: departments" R1["1 - Engineering"] R2["2 - Marketing"] R5["5 - Sales"] R6["6 - HR"] end subgraph "Inner Join Result" Result["| id | name | salary | dept |
|----|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Alice | 50000 | Engineering |
| 2 | Bob | 60000 | Marketing |"] end L1 -->|"match"| R1 L2 -->|"match"| R2 L3 -.->|"no match"| X1["dropped"] L4 -.->|"no match"| X2["dropped"] R5 -.->|"no match"| X3["dropped"] R6 -.->|"no match"| X4["dropped"] R1 --> Result R2 --> Result style Result fill:#90EE90 style X1 fill:#FFB6C1 style X2 fill:#FFB6C1 style X3 fill:#FFB6C1 style X4 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Left Join - Keep All Left Rows
steps.explanation:
**Left join** keeps all rows from the left DataFrame. If there's no match in the right DataFrame, you get `NaN` values. Use this when you want to preserve all your original data.

```python
pd.merge(employees, departments, on='id', how='left')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Left: employees - ALL KEPT" L1["1 - Alice ✓"] L2["2 - Bob ✓"] L3["3 - Charlie ✓"] L4["4 - Diana ✓"] end subgraph "Right: departments" R1["1 - Engineering"] R2["2 - Marketing"] R5["5 - Sales"] R6["6 - HR"] end subgraph "Left Join Result" Result["| id | name | salary | dept |
|----|---------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Alice | 50000 | Engineering |
| 2 | Bob | 60000 | Marketing |
| 3 | Charlie | 55000 | NaN |
| 4 | Diana | 70000 | NaN |"] end L1 -->|"match"| R1 L2 -->|"match"| R2 L3 -->|"no match"| NaN1["NaN filled"] L4 -->|"no match"| NaN2["NaN filled"] R5 -.->|"not used"| X1["dropped"] R6 -.->|"not used"| X2["dropped"] style L1 fill:#90EE90 style L2 fill:#90EE90 style L3 fill:#90EE90 style L4 fill:#90EE90 style NaN1 fill:#FFFACD style NaN2 fill:#FFFACD style X1 fill:#FFB6C1 style X2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Right Join - Keep All Right Rows
steps.explanation:
**Right join** keeps all rows from the right DataFrame. If there's no match in the left DataFrame, you get `NaN` values. This is the mirror of left join.

```python
pd.merge(employees, departments, on='id', how='right')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Left: employees" L1["1 - Alice"] L2["2 - Bob"] L3["3 - Charlie"] L4["4 - Diana"] end subgraph "Right: departments - ALL KEPT" R1["1 - Engineering ✓"] R2["2 - Marketing ✓"] R5["5 - Sales ✓"] R6["6 - HR ✓"] end subgraph "Right Join Result" Result["| id | name | salary | dept |
|----|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Alice | 50000 | Engineering |
| 2 | Bob | 60000 | Marketing |
| 5 | NaN | NaN | Sales |
| 6 | NaN | NaN | HR |"] end L1 -->|"match"| R1 L2 -->|"match"| R2 L3 -.->|"not used"| X1["dropped"] L4 -.->|"not used"| X2["dropped"] R5 -->|"no match"| NaN1["NaN filled"] R6 -->|"no match"| NaN2["NaN filled"] style R1 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R5 fill:#90EE90 style R6 fill:#90EE90 style NaN1 fill:#FFFACD style NaN2 fill:#FFFACD style X1 fill:#FFB6C1 style X2 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Outer Join - Keep Everything
steps.explanation:
**Outer join** (full outer join) keeps all rows from **both** DataFrames. Missing values are filled with `NaN`. This is the most inclusive join type.

```python
pd.merge(employees, departments, on='id', how='outer')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Left: employees - ALL KEPT" L1["1 - Alice ✓"] L2["2 - Bob ✓"] L3["3 - Charlie ✓"] L4["4 - Diana ✓"] end subgraph "Right: departments - ALL KEPT" R1["1 - Engineering ✓"] R2["2 - Marketing ✓"] R5["5 - Sales ✓"] R6["6 - HR ✓"] end subgraph "Outer Join Result" Result["| id | name | salary | dept |
|----|---------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Alice | 50000 | Engineering |
| 2 | Bob | 60000 | Marketing |
| 3 | Charlie | 55000 | NaN |
| 4 | Diana | 70000 | NaN |
| 5 | NaN | NaN | Sales |
| 6 | NaN | NaN | HR |"] end L1 --> Result L2 --> Result L3 --> Result L4 --> Result R5 --> Result R6 --> Result style L1 fill:#90EE90 style L2 fill:#90EE90 style L3 fill:#90EE90 style L4 fill:#90EE90 style R1 fill:#90EE90 style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R5 fill:#90EE90 style R6 fill:#90EE90 style Result fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Visual Summary of Join Types
steps.explanation: Here's a Venn diagram view of all four join types. Think of the circles as the sets of keys in each DataFrame.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Inner Join" I_L["Left"] I_Int["Only
Intersection"] I_R["Right"] I_L -.-> I_Int I_Int <-.- I_R end subgraph "Left Join" LJ_L["Left
ALL"] LJ_Int["Intersection"] LJ_R["Right"] LJ_L --> LJ_Int LJ_Int <-.- LJ_R end subgraph "Right Join" RJ_L["Left"] RJ_Int["Intersection"] RJ_R["Right
ALL"] RJ_L -.-> RJ_Int RJ_Int <-- RJ_R end subgraph "Outer Join" O_L["Left
ALL"] O_Int["Intersection"] O_R["Right
ALL"] O_L --> O_Int O_Int <-- O_R end style I_Int fill:#90EE90 style LJ_L fill:#ADD8E6 style LJ_Int fill:#90EE90 style RJ_R fill:#ADD8E6 style RJ_Int fill:#90EE90 style O_L fill:#ADD8E6 style O_Int fill:#90EE90 style O_R fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Merging on Different Column Names
steps.explanation:
What if the key columns have different names in each DataFrame? Use `left_on` and `right_on` parameters.

```python
# employees has 'emp_id', departments has 'dept_id'
pd.merge(employees, departments, 
         left_on='emp_id', right_on='dept_id')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "employees" E["| emp_id | name |
|--------|-------|
| 1 | Alice |
| 2 | Bob |"] end subgraph "departments" D["| dept_id | dept |
|---------|------------|
| 1 | Engineering|
| 2 | Marketing |"] end subgraph "Merge Code" Code["pd.merge(employees, departments,
left_on='emp_id',
right_on='dept_id')"] end subgraph "Result" R["| emp_id | name | dept_id | dept |
|--------|-------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Alice | 1 | Engineering |
| 2 | Bob | 2 | Marketing |"] end E --> Code D --> Code Code --> R subgraph "Note" Note["Both emp_id and dept_id columns
are kept in the result!
Drop one if redundant."] end style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multi-Column Joins
steps.explanation:
You can join on multiple columns when a single column isn't unique enough to identify rows.

```python
# Join on both year and month
pd.merge(sales, targets, on=['year', 'month'])
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "sales" S["| year | month | revenue |
|------|-------|---------|
| 2024 | Jan | 10000 |
| 2024 | Feb | 12000 |
| 2024 | Jan | 8000 |"] end subgraph "targets" T["| year | month | target |
|------|-------|--------|
| 2024 | Jan | 9000 |
| 2024 | Feb | 11000 |"] end subgraph "Multi-Column Join" Join["pd.merge(sales, targets,
on=['year', 'month'])"] end subgraph "Result" R["| year | month | revenue | target |
|------|-------|---------|--------|
| 2024 | Jan | 10000 | 9000 |
| 2024 | Feb | 12000 | 11000 |
| 2024 | Jan | 8000 | 9000 |"] end S --> Join T --> Join Join --> R style Join fill:#90EE90

steps.title: join() vs merge()
steps.explanation:
Pandas has two ways to combine DataFrames:

- **merge()**: SQL-style joins, works on columns
- **join()**: Joins on index by default, more convenient for index-based operations
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "merge() - Column-based" M1["pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key')"] M2["• Joins on column values
• More flexible
• Explicit join column"] end subgraph "join() - Index-based" J1["df1.join(df2)"] J2["• Joins on index by default
• Cleaner syntax
• Good for time series"] end subgraph "Equivalents" Eq1["df1.join(df2)"] Eq2["pd.merge(df1, df2,
left_index=True,
right_index=True)"] Eq1 <-->|"same as"| Eq2 end subgraph "When to Use" Use1["merge(): Most data operations"] Use2["join(): Index-aligned data,
time series"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style J1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Handling Duplicate Column Names
steps.explanation:
When both DataFrames have columns with the same name (besides the join key), Pandas adds suffixes to distinguish them.

```python
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id', suffixes=('_left', '_right'))
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "df1" D1["| id | value |
|----|-------|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 200 |"] end subgraph "df2" D2["| id | value |
|----|-------|
| 1 | 'A' |
| 2 | 'B' |"] end subgraph "Default Behavior" Def["pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id')"] DefR["| id | value_x | value_y |
|----|---------|---------|
| 1 | 100 | 'A' |
| 2 | 200 | 'B' |"] end subgraph "Custom Suffixes" Cust["pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id',
suffixes=('_num', '_str'))"] CustR["| id | value_num | value_str |
|----|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 100 | 'A' |
| 2 | 200 | 'B' |"] end D1 --> Def D2 --> Def Def --> DefR D1 --> Cust D2 --> Cust Cust --> CustR style Def fill:#FFFACD style Cust fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Indicator Column
steps.explanation:
The `indicator` parameter adds a column showing where each row came from. Useful for debugging joins and finding unmatched rows.

```python
pd.merge(df1, df2, how='outer', indicator=True)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "df1" D1["| id | val1 |
|----|------|
| 1 | A |
| 2 | B |"] end subgraph "df2" D2["| id | val2 |
|----|------|
| 2 | X |
| 3 | Y |"] end subgraph "Outer Join with Indicator" Code["pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id',
how='outer',
indicator=True)"] end subgraph "Result" R["| id | val1 | val2 | _merge |
|----|------|------|------------|
| 1 | A | NaN | left_only |
| 2 | B | X | both |
| 3 | NaN | Y | right_only |"] end D1 --> Code D2 --> Code Code --> R subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Find unmatched: df[df['_merge'] == 'left_only']"] U2["Find matched: df[df['_merge'] == 'both']"] end style Code fill:#ADD8E6 style U1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: One-to-Many and Many-to-Many
steps.explanation: Joins can create more rows than either input DataFrame when there are duplicate keys. Understanding this prevents unexpected row multiplication.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "One-to-One" O1["Each key appears once in both"] O1R["Result: same row count"] end subgraph "One-to-Many" OM["| id | name | | id | item |
|----|------| |----|------|
| 1 | Alice| | 1 | Apple|
| 2 | Bob | | 1 | Banana|"] OMR["Result:
| 1 | Alice | Apple |
| 1 | Alice | Banana |
| 2 | Bob | NaN |"] end subgraph "Many-to-Many ⚠️" MM["| id | A | | id | B |
|----|---| |----|---|
| 1 | x | | 1 | p |
| 1 | y | | 1 | q |"] MMR["Result: 2 × 2 = 4 rows!
| 1 | x | p |
| 1 | x | q |
| 1 | y | p |
| 1 | y | q |"] end subgraph "Validate Parameter" Val["pd.merge(..., validate='one_to_one')
Raises error if assumption violated!"] end O1 --> O1R OM --> OMR MM --> MMR style MMR fill:#FFB6C1 style Val fill:#90EE90

steps.title: concat() vs merge()
steps.explanation: **concat()** stacks DataFrames (rows or columns), while **merge()** combines based on key values. They serve different purposes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "concat() - Stacking" C1["pd.concat([df1, df2])"] C2["Stacks rows vertically
(or columns horizontally with axis=1)"] C3["| A | B |
| 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 4 |
| 5 | 6 |
| 7 | 8 |"] end subgraph "merge() - Joining" M1["pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key')"] M2["Combines based on
matching key values"] M3["| key | A | B |
| x | 1 | 5 |
| y | 2 | 6 |"] end subgraph "When to Use" When1["concat(): Same columns,
different rows (append data)"] When2["merge(): Different columns,
shared keys (combine info)"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 M1 --> M2 --> M3 style C1 fill:#ADD8E6 style M1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Here's a summary of the most common merge operations you'll use.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Most Common Operations" Op1["Inner Join
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key')"] Op2["Left Join
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key', how='left')"] Op3["Multi-key Join
pd.merge(df1, df2, on=['k1', 'k2'])"] Op4["Different Key Names

fields.title: Pandas Pivot and Melt: Reshaping Data
fields.description: Learn to reshape DataFrames between wide and long formats using pivot, pivot_table, melt, and stack/unstack

steps.title: Wide vs Long Format
steps.explanation:
Data can be stored in two main formats:

- **Wide format**: Each variable gets its own column
- **Long format**: Variables are stacked in rows with a 'variable' and 'value' column

**Pivot** transforms long → wide.
**Melt** transforms wide → long.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Wide Format" W["| name | math | science |
|-------|------|---------|
| Alice | 90 | 85 |
| Bob | 80 | 95 |"] end subgraph "Long Format" L["| name | subject | score |
|-------|---------|-------|
| Alice | math | 90 |
| Alice | science | 85 |
| Bob | math | 80 |
| Bob | science | 95 |"] end W -->|"melt()"| L L -->|"pivot()"| W style W fill:#ADD8E6 style L fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use Each Format
steps.explanation:
Different formats are better for different operations:

- **Wide**: Easier for humans to read, good for correlation analysis
- **Long**: Better for plotting (seaborn/plotly), groupby operations, and tidy data principles
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wide Format Best For" W1["Human readability"] W2["Correlation between columns"] W3["Matrix operations"] W4["Spreadsheet-like display"] end subgraph "Long Format Best For" L1["Seaborn/plotly plotting"] L2["GroupBy operations"] L3["Tidy data principles"] L4["Database storage"] end subgraph "Example: Plotting" Plot["# Long format makes this easy:
sns.barplot(data=long_df,
x='subject',
y='score',
hue='name')"] end style W1 fill:#ADD8E6 style L1 fill:#90EE90 style Plot fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: melt() - Wide to Long
steps.explanation:
**melt()** unpivots a DataFrame from wide to long format. Key parameters:

- `id_vars`: Columns to keep as identifiers
- `value_vars`: Columns to unpivot (default: all others)
- `var_name`: Name for the variable column
- `value_name`: Name for the value column
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wide DataFrame" Wide["| name | math | science |
|-------|------|---------|
| Alice | 90 | 85 |
| Bob | 80 | 95 |"] end subgraph "melt() Code" Code["df.melt(
id_vars=['name'],
value_vars=['math', 'science'],
var_name='subject',
value_name='score'
)"] end subgraph "Long Result" Long["| name | subject | score |
|-------|---------|-------|
| Alice | math | 90 |
| Bob | math | 80 |
| Alice | science | 85 |
| Bob | science | 95 |"] end subgraph "What Happened" Exp["• 'name' stayed as identifier
• 'math' & 'science' columns melted
• Column names → 'subject'
• Values → 'score'"] end Wide --> Code --> Long style Code fill:#90EE90 style Exp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: melt() Visual Breakdown
steps.explanation: Let's visualize exactly how melt transforms each cell in the wide DataFrame to rows in the long DataFrame.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Original Wide DataFrame"
        H1["name"]
        H2["math"]
        H3["science"]
        R1C1["Alice"]
        R1C2["90"]
        R1C3["85"]
        R2C1["Bob"]
        R2C2["80"]
        R2C3["95"]
    end

    subgraph "After melt()"
        LH1["name"]
        LH2["subject"]
        LH3["score"]
        LR1["Alice | math    | 90"]
        LR2["Alice | science | 85"]
        LR3["Bob   | math    | 80"]
        LR4["Bob   | science | 95"]
    end

    R1C1 --> LR1
    R1C1 --> LR2
    R2C1 --> LR3
    R2C1 --> LR4

    H2 -.->|"becomes"| LR1
    H3 -.->|"becomes"| LR2

    R1C2 -->|"value"| LR1
    R1C3 -->|"value"| LR2
    R2C2 -->|"value"| LR3
    R2C3 -->|"value"| LR4

    style R1C1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style R2C1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style H2 fill:#90EE90
    style H3 fill:#90EE90
    style R1C2 fill:#FFFACD
    style R1C3 fill:#FFFACD
    style R2C2 fill:#FFFACD
    style R2C3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: pivot() - Long to Wide
steps.explanation:
**pivot()** transforms long format back to wide format. Key parameters:

- `index`: Column(s) to use as row index
- `columns`: Column whose unique values become columns
- `values`: Column containing the values

⚠️ **pivot() fails if there are duplicate entries** for the same index/column combination.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Long DataFrame" Long["| name | subject | score |
|-------|---------|-------|
| Alice | math | 90 |
| Alice | science | 85 |
| Bob | math | 80 |
| Bob | science | 95 |"] end subgraph "pivot() Code" Code["df.pivot(
index='name',
columns='subject',
values='score'
)"] end subgraph "Wide Result" Wide["subject| math | science |
name |------|---------|
Alice | 90 | 85 |
Bob | 80 | 95 |"] end subgraph "What Happened" Exp["• 'name' → row index
• 'subject' values → columns
• 'score' → cell values"] end Long --> Code --> Wide style Code fill:#90EE90 style Exp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: pivot_table() - Pivot with Aggregation
steps.explanation:
**pivot_table()** is like pivot(), but handles duplicates by aggregating them. This is the safer choice for most use cases.

```python
df.pivot_table(index='name', columns='subject', 
               values='score', aggfunc='mean')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data with Duplicates" Dup["| name | subject | score |
|-------|---------|-------|
| Alice | math | 90 |
| Alice | math | 85 |
| Bob | math | 80 |"] end subgraph "pivot() - FAILS!" Fail["df.pivot(index='name',
columns='subject',
values='score')"] Err["ValueError: Index contains
duplicate entries!"] end subgraph "pivot_table() - WORKS!" PT["df.pivot_table(
index='name',
columns='subject',
values='score',
aggfunc='mean'
)"] Result["| subject | math |
| name |------|
| Alice | 87.5 |
| Bob | 80 |"] end Dup --> Fail --> Err Dup --> PT --> Result style Err fill:#FFB6C1 style PT fill:#90EE90 style Result fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: pivot_table() Aggregation Functions
steps.explanation: **pivot_table()** supports multiple aggregation functions. You can use built-in functions or custom ones.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Common aggfunc Values" A1["'mean' - Average (default)"] A2["'sum' - Total"] A3["'count' - Number of values"] A4["'min' / 'max' - Extremes"] A5["'first' / 'last' - First/last value"] end subgraph "Multiple Aggregations" Multi["df.pivot_table(
index='name',
columns='subject',
values='score',
aggfunc=['mean', 'count']
)"] MultiR["Returns MultiIndex columns:
| mean | count |
| math | sci | math | sci |"] end subgraph "Custom Function" Custom["df.pivot_table(
...,
aggfunc=lambda x: x.max() - x.min()
)"] end A1 --> Multi Multi --> MultiR style A1 fill:#90EE90 style Multi fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Margins in pivot_table()
steps.explanation: The `margins=True` parameter adds row and column totals (or other aggregations) to the pivot table - similar to subtotals in Excel.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "pivot_table with margins" Code["df.pivot_table(
index='name',
columns='subject',
values='score',
aggfunc='sum',
margins=True,
margins_name='Total'
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["subject| math | sci | Total |
name |------|-----|-------|
Alice | 90 | 85 | 175 |
Bob | 80 | 95 | 175 |
Total | 170 | 180 | 350 |"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Summary reports"] U2["Data validation"] U3["Excel-like views"] end Code --> R style Code fill:#90EE90 style R fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: stack() and unstack()
steps.explanation:
**stack()** and **unstack()** work with the index:

- **stack()**: Pivots columns → index levels (makes data longer)
- **unstack()**: Pivots index levels → columns (makes data wider)

These are useful when working with MultiIndex DataFrames.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wide DataFrame" W[" | math | science |
name |------|---------|
Alice | 90 | 85 |
Bob | 80 | 95 |"] end subgraph "stack()" Stack["df.stack()"] Stacked["name subject
Alice math 90
science 85
Bob math 80
science 95

→ Returns a Series with
MultiIndex"] end subgraph "unstack()" Unstack["series.unstack()"] Unstacked["Back to original
wide format"] end W --> Stack --> Stacked Stacked --> Unstack --> Unstacked style Stack fill:#90EE90 style Unstack fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: stack() vs melt()
steps.explanation:
Both transform wide to long, but they differ:

- **melt()**: Works on column values, creates DataFrame
- **stack()**: Works on column labels → index, creates Series (or DataFrame with MultiIndex)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "melt()" M1["• Works on DataFrames"] M2["• Returns DataFrame"] M3["• Column names → new column"] M4["• More intuitive for reshaping"] end subgraph "stack()" S1["• Works on columns → index"] S2["• Returns Series (usually)"] S3["• Creates MultiIndex"] S4["• Better for hierarchical data"] end subgraph "When to Use" When1["melt(): General reshaping
Long format for plotting"] When2["stack(): Working with
MultiIndex DataFrames"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style S1 fill:#90EE90 style When1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Real-World Example: Sales Data
steps.explanation: Let's see a complete example transforming sales data between formats.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original: Wide Format" Wide["| store | Q1_sales | Q2_sales | Q3_sales |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|
| NYC | 100 | 120 | 150 |
| LA | 90 | 110 | 140 |"] end subgraph "Step 1: melt()" Melt["df.melt(
id_vars=['store'],
var_name='quarter',
value_name='sales'
)"] end subgraph "Long Format" Long["| store | quarter | sales |
|-------|----------|-------|
| NYC | Q1_sales | 100 |
| NYC | Q2_sales | 120 |
| NYC | Q3_sales | 150 |
| LA | Q1_sales | 90 |
| ... | ... | ... |"] end subgraph "Now Easy to Plot!" Plot["sns.lineplot(
data=long_df,
x='quarter',
y='sales',
hue='store'
)"] end Wide --> Melt --> Long --> Plot style Melt fill:#90EE90 style Plot fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Cleaning After melt()
steps.explanation: After melting, you often need to clean the variable column. Here's a common pattern.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "After melt" After["| store | quarter | sales |
|-------|----------|-------|
| NYC | Q1_sales | 100 |
| NYC | Q2_sales | 120 |"] end subgraph "Clean the quarter column" Clean["# Remove '_sales' suffix
df['quarter'] = df['quarter'].str.replace('_sales', '')

# Or extract just Q1, Q2, etc.
df['quarter'] = df['quarter'].str.extract(r'(Q\d)')"] end subgraph "Result" Result["| store | quarter | sales |
|-------|---------|-------|
| NYC | Q1 | 100 |
| NYC | Q2 | 120 |"] end After --> Clean --> Result style Clean fill:#FFFACD style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Here's a summary of the key reshaping operations in Pandas.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wide → Long" WL1["melt()
General purpose"] WL2["stack()
Columns to MultiIndex"] end subgraph "Long → Wide" LW1["pivot()
No duplicates allowed"] LW2["pivot_table()
Aggregates duplicates"] LW3["unstack()
MultiIndex to columns"] end subgraph "Key Parameters" P1["melt: id_vars, value_vars,
var_name, value_name"] P2["pivot: index, columns, values"] P3["pivot_table: + aggfunc, margins"] end subgraph "Common Pattern" CP["Wide data → melt() → plot
Long data → pivot_table() → report"] end style WL1 fill:#90EE90 style LW2 fill:#ADD8E6 style CP fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Pandas loc vs iloc: Indexing Deep Dive
fields.description: Master label-based (loc) and position-based (iloc) indexing in Pandas with clear visual examples

steps.title: The Two Types of Indexing
steps.explanation:
Pandas provides two main indexing methods:

- **loc**: Uses **labels** (row/column names)
- **iloc**: Uses **integer positions** (0, 1, 2, ...)

The 'i' in iloc stands for 'integer' - that's the key difference!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc - Label Based" Loc["df.loc['Alice', 'age']
Uses actual names/labels"] LocEx["✓ df.loc['Alice']
✓ df.loc['A':'C']
✓ df.loc[True, False, True]"] end subgraph "iloc - Integer Position" Iloc["df.iloc[0, 1]
Uses 0-based positions"] IlocEx["✓ df.iloc[0]
✓ df.iloc[0:3]
✗ df.iloc['Alice']"] end subgraph "Key Difference" Diff["loc: endpoints INCLUDED
iloc: endpoints EXCLUDED

loc['A':'C'] → A, B, C
iloc[0:3] → 0, 1, 2"] end style Loc fill:#ADD8E6 style Iloc fill:#90EE90 style Diff fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Our Example DataFrame
steps.explanation: We'll use this DataFrame throughout. Notice the index uses names (strings), not the default 0, 1, 2 integers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataFrame: df" DF[" | age | city | salary |
----------|-----|----------|--------|
Alice | 25 | New York | 70000 |
Bob | 30 | Chicago | 80000 |
Charlie | 35 | Boston | 90000 |
Diana | 28 | Seattle | 75000 |"] end subgraph "Index & Columns" Idx["Index (row labels):
['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Diana']"] Cols["Columns:
['age', 'city', 'salary']"] end subgraph "Positions" Pos["Position 0 → Alice
Position 1 → Bob
Position 2 → Charlie
Position 3 → Diana"] end DF --> Idx DF --> Cols DF --> Pos style DF fill:#E6E6FA style Idx fill:#ADD8E6 style Pos fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Selecting a Single Row
steps.explanation: Both loc and iloc can select a single row, but use different identifiers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Using loc - by label" LocCode["df.loc['Bob']"] LocResult["age 30
city Chicago
salary 80000
Name: Bob, dtype: object"] end subgraph "Using iloc - by position" IlocCode["df.iloc[1]"] IlocResult["age 30
city Chicago
salary 80000
Name: Bob, dtype: object"] end subgraph "Same Result!" Same["Both return the row for Bob
(position 1, label 'Bob')"] end LocCode --> LocResult IlocCode --> IlocResult style LocCode fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocCode fill:#90EE90 style Same fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Selecting a Single Cell
steps.explanation: Access a specific cell by specifying both row and column.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc: [row_label, column_label]" LocCode["df.loc['Charlie', 'salary']"] LocR["90000"] end subgraph "iloc: [row_position, column_position]" IlocCode["df.iloc[2, 2]"] IlocR["90000"] end subgraph "Visual" Grid[" | age | city | salary |
| [0] | [1] | [2] |
----------|-----|--------|--------|
Alice [0] | 25 | NY | 70000 |
Bob [1] | 30 | CHI | 80000 |
Charlie[2]| 35 | BOS |→90000←|
Diana [3] | 28 | SEA | 75000 |"] end LocCode --> LocR IlocCode --> IlocR style LocCode fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocCode fill:#90EE90 style Grid fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Slicing Rows - The Big Difference!
steps.explanation:
⚠️ **Critical difference**: loc includes the end point, iloc excludes it (like Python lists).

```python
df.loc['Alice':'Charlie']  # Alice, Bob, Charlie (3 rows)
df.iloc[0:3]               # positions 0, 1, 2 (3 rows)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc slice - INCLUSIVE" LocCode["df.loc['Alice':'Charlie']"] LocR["Alice ✓
Bob ✓
Charlie ✓
Diana ✗"] LocNote["'Charlie' is INCLUDED"] end subgraph "iloc slice - EXCLUSIVE" IlocCode["df.iloc[0:3]"] IlocR["Alice ✓ (pos 0)
Bob ✓ (pos 1)
Charlie ✓ (pos 2)
Diana ✗ (pos 3)"] IlocNote["Position 3 is EXCLUDED"] end subgraph "Both return same rows, different syntax!" Same["loc: start:end → includes end
iloc: start:end → excludes end"] end LocCode --> LocR IlocCode --> IlocR style LocNote fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocNote fill:#90EE90 style Same fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Selecting Multiple Rows
steps.explanation: Use a list to select specific (non-contiguous) rows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc with list of labels" LocCode["df.loc[['Alice', 'Diana']]"] LocR[" | age | city | salary |
----------|-----|----------|--------|
Alice | 25 | New York | 70000 |
Diana | 28 | Seattle | 75000 |"] end subgraph "iloc with list of positions" IlocCode["df.iloc[[0, 3]]"] IlocR["Same result!
Positions 0 and 3"] end subgraph "Note" Note["[[double brackets]] = list of indices
Returns a DataFrame

vs

['single'] = one index
Returns a Series (for rows)"] end LocCode --> LocR IlocCode --> IlocR style LocCode fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocCode fill:#90EE90 style Note fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Selecting Columns
steps.explanation:
Select specific columns using the second position in loc/iloc.

```python
df.loc[:, 'age']           # All rows, 'age' column
df.iloc[:, 0]              # All rows, first column
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc: select columns by name" LocCode1["df.loc[:, 'age']"] LocCode2["df.loc[:, ['age', 'salary']]"] LocR["The : means 'all rows'"] end subgraph "iloc: select columns by position" IlocCode1["df.iloc[:, 0]"] IlocCode2["df.iloc[:, [0, 2]]"] IlocR["Column positions:
0=age, 1=city, 2=salary"] end subgraph "Visual" Grid[" | age | city | salary |
| ↓ | | ↓ |
----------|-----|--------|--------|
Alice | 25 | ... | 70000 |
Bob | 30 | ... | 80000 |
Charlie | 35 | ... | 90000 |
Diana | 28 | ... | 75000 |

df.loc[:, ['age', 'salary']]"] end style LocCode1 fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocCode1 fill:#90EE90 style Grid fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: Row and Column Selection Together
steps.explanation: Select a subset of both rows and columns in one operation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc: rows and columns by label" LocCode["df.loc['Bob':'Diana', 'age':'city']"] LocR[" | age | city |
-------|-----|---------|
Bob | 30 | Chicago |
Charlie| 35 | Boston |
Diana | 28 | Seattle |"] end subgraph "iloc: rows and columns by position" IlocCode["df.iloc[1:4, 0:2]"] IlocR["Same result!
Rows 1-3, Cols 0-1"] end subgraph "Pattern" Pattern["df.loc[row_selection, col_selection]
df.iloc[row_selection, col_selection]"] end LocCode --> LocR IlocCode --> IlocR style LocCode fill:#ADD8E6 style IlocCode fill:#90EE90 style Pattern fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Boolean Indexing with loc
steps.explanation:
**loc** supports boolean arrays/Series for filtering. This is one of the most powerful features!

```python
df.loc[df['age'] > 28]      # All rows where age > 28
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Boolean condition" Cond["df['age'] > 28"] Bool["Alice False
Bob True
Charlie True
Diana False"] end subgraph "Using with loc" LocCode["df.loc[df['age'] > 28]"] LocR[" | age | city | salary |
-------|-----|---------|--------|
Bob | 30 | Chicago | 80000 |
Charlie| 35 | Boston | 90000 |"] end subgraph "Combine with column selection" Combo["df.loc[df['age'] > 28, 'salary']"] ComboR["Bob 80000
Charlie 90000"] end Cond --> Bool --> LocCode --> LocR style Cond fill:#ADD8E6 style Bool fill:#FFFACD style Combo fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Setting Values
steps.explanation: Both loc and iloc can be used to modify values in place. This is safer than chained indexing!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setting a single value" Set1["df.loc['Alice', 'age'] = 26"] Set2["df.iloc[0, 0] = 26"] end subgraph "Setting multiple values" SetM["# Set all salaries for age > 30
df.loc[df['age'] > 30, 'salary'] = 100000"] end subgraph "⚠️ Avoid Chained Indexing" Bad["# DON'T DO THIS
df[df['age'] > 30]['salary'] = 100000

This might not work!
SettingWithCopyWarning"] Good["# DO THIS
df.loc[df['age'] > 30, 'salary'] = 100000

Always works correctly"] end style Bad fill:#FFB6C1 style Good fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When Index is Integers
steps.explanation:
⚠️ **Confusion alert**: When your index is integers, loc and iloc behave differently!

```python
df.loc[1]   # Row with LABEL 1
df.iloc[1]  # Row at POSITION 1
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Integer Index DataFrame" DF[" | age | city |
----|-----|---------|
10 | 25 | NYC |
20 | 30 | Chicago |
30 | 35 | Boston |"] end subgraph "loc uses labels" Loc["df.loc[20]"] LocR["Row with label 20
age=30, city=Chicago"] end subgraph "iloc uses positions" Iloc["df.iloc[1]"] IlocR["Row at position 1
(same row, but different syntax)"] end subgraph "⚠️ The Trap" Trap["df.loc[1] → KeyError!
(no label '1')

df.iloc[10] → IndexError!
(only 3 positions)"] end DF --> Loc --> LocR DF --> Iloc --> IlocR style Trap fill:#FFB6C1 style Loc fill:#ADD8E6 style Iloc fill:#90EE90

steps.title: at and iat - Fast Scalar Access
steps.explanation:
For accessing a **single value**, `at` and `iat` are faster than loc/iloc.

- **at**: Like loc, uses labels
- **iat**: Like iloc, uses integer positions
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "at - label based" At["df.at['Alice', 'age']"] AtR["25"] end subgraph "iat - position based" Iat["df.iat[0, 0]"] IatR["25"] end subgraph "When to Use" When["at/iat: Single value (faster)
loc/iloc: Rows, columns, slices"] end subgraph "Speed Comparison" Speed["df.at['Alice', 'age'] → ~1μs
df.loc['Alice', 'age'] → ~5μs

Matters in tight loops!"] end style At fill:#ADD8E6 style Iat fill:#90EE90 style Speed fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Here are the most common indexing patterns you'll use.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Select Rows"
        R1["Single: df.loc['Alice']"]
        R2["Multiple: df.loc[['A', 'B']]"]
        R3["Slice: df.loc['A':'C']"]
        R4["Boolean: df.loc[df['x'] > 5]"]
    end

    subgraph "Select Columns"
        C1["Single: df['col'] or df.loc[:,'col']"]
        C2["Multiple: df[['col1','col2']]"]
    end

    subgraph "Select Both"
        B1["df.loc[rows, cols]"]
        B2["df.loc[df['age']>25, ['name','age']]"]
    end

    subgraph "Modify"
        M1["df.loc[condition, 'col'] = value"]
    end

    style R4 fill:#90EE90
    style B2 fill:#ADD8E6
    style M1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Here's the complete loc vs iloc reference.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "loc" L1["Label-based selection"] L2["Slices are INCLUSIVE"] L3["Can use booleans"] L4["df.loc[label]
df.loc[label1:label2]
df.loc[[label1, label2]]
df.loc[bool_array]"] end subgraph "iloc" I1["Integer position-based"] I2["Slices are EXCLUSIVE"] I3["Only integers/slices"] I4["df.iloc[n]
df.iloc[n:m]
df.iloc[[n, m]]"] end subgraph "Rules of Thumb" Rule1["Know your data → loc"] Rule2["Generic position → iloc"] Rule3["Filtering → loc + boolean"] Rule4["Setting values → always loc"] end style L1 fill:#ADD8E6 style I1 fill:#90EE90 style Rule4 fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Pandas DataFrame Serialization Formats
fields.description: Compare CSV, Parquet, Feather, Pickle, JSON, and other formats for saving and loading DataFrames

steps.title: Why Serialization Matters
steps.explanation:
**Serialization** converts DataFrames to bytes for storage or transmission. Different formats optimize for different goals:

- **Speed**: How fast can you read/write?
- **Size**: How much disk space?
- **Compatibility**: Can other tools read it?
- **Type preservation**: Are dtypes maintained?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataFrame in Memory" DF["df = pd.DataFrame(...)"] end subgraph "Serialization Formats" CSV["CSV
Human readable"] Parquet["Parquet
Columnar, compressed"] Feather["Feather
Fast read/write"] Pickle["Pickle
Python native"] JSON["JSON
Web compatible"] Excel["Excel
Business users"] end DF --> CSV DF --> Parquet DF --> Feather DF --> Pickle DF --> JSON DF --> Excel subgraph "Key Trade-offs" T1["Speed vs Compatibility"] T2["Size vs Readability"] T3["Features vs Portability"] end style Parquet fill:#90EE90 style CSV fill:#ADD8E6 style Pickle fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Format Comparison Overview
steps.explanation: Here's a quick comparison of the most common formats. **Parquet** is generally the best choice for data science workflows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Format Comparison"
        Header["Format | Speed | Size | Types | Portable"]
        CSV["CSV     | Slow  | Large | ✗    | ✓✓✓"]
        Parquet["Parquet | Fast  | Small | ✓    | ✓✓"]
        Feather["Feather | V.Fast| Medium| ✓    | ✓"]
        Pickle["Pickle  | Fast  | Medium| ✓    | ✗"]
        JSON["JSON    | Slow  | Large | ✗    | ✓✓✓"]
        HDF5["HDF5    | Fast  | Small | ✓    | ✓"]
    end

    subgraph "Recommendations"
        R1["Data Science: Parquet"]
        R2["Sharing with others: CSV"]
        R3["Temporary/cache: Feather"]
        R4["Python-only: Pickle"]
    end

    style Parquet fill:#90EE90
    style R1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: CSV - The Universal Format
steps.explanation:
**CSV** (Comma-Separated Values) is the most widely supported format. Every tool can read it, but it has significant limitations.

```python
df.to_csv('data.csv', index=False)
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CSV Pros" P1["✓ Universal compatibility"] P2["✓ Human readable/editable"] P3["✓ Works with Excel, SQL, etc."] P4["✓ Simple to debug"] end subgraph "CSV Cons" C1["✗ No type preservation
(dates become strings)"] C2["✗ Slow for large files"] C3["✗ Large file sizes"] C4["✗ No compression built-in"] C5["✗ Encoding issues (UTF-8)"] end subgraph "Common Parameters" Params["df.to_csv(
'file.csv',
index=False, # Skip index
compression='gzip', # Compress
encoding='utf-8'
)

pd.read_csv(
'file.csv',
dtype={'col': 'int32'},
parse_dates=['date_col']
)"] end style P1 fill:#90EE90 style C1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Params fill:#E6E6FA

steps.title: CSV Type Loss Problem
steps.explanation: The biggest CSV pitfall: **type information is lost**. Dates become strings, categories become objects, and you must manually specify types when reading.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original DataFrame" Orig["| date | category | value |
|------------|----------|-------|
| 2024-01-01 | 'A' | 1.5 |

dtypes:
date: datetime64
category: category
value: float64"] end subgraph "Save to CSV" Save["df.to_csv('data.csv')"] File["2024-01-01,A,1.5"] end subgraph "Read from CSV" Read["pd.read_csv('data.csv')"] Result["dtypes:
date: object (string!)
category: object (string!)
value: float64

Types lost!"] end subgraph "Fix: Specify types" Fix["pd.read_csv('data.csv',
dtype={'category': 'category'},
parse_dates=['date']
)"] end Orig --> Save --> File --> Read --> Result Result -.->|"manual fix"| Fix style Result fill:#FFB6C1 style Fix fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Parquet - The Modern Standard
steps.explanation:
**Parquet** is a columnar format designed for analytics. It's the recommended format for most data science work.

```python
df.to_parquet('data.parquet')
df = pd.read_parquet('data.parquet')
```
