fields.title: Tensor Core Operations: Matrix Multiply Deep Dive
fields.description: Understand how Tensor Cores accelerate matrix operations with warp-level MMA instructions

steps.title: What Are Tensor Cores?
steps.explanation:
**Tensor Cores** are specialized hardware units that perform matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) operations in a single clock cycle. They're the key to fast deep learning training and inference.

- Introduced in Volta (2017)
- Perform D = A × B + C in one operation
- 4-16x faster than CUDA cores for matrix ops
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CUDA Core - Sequential" CC1["Multiply a × b"] CC2["Multiply a × b"] CC3["...many cycles..."] CC4["Add all products"] CC1 --> CC2 --> CC3 --> CC4 end subgraph "Tensor Core - Parallel" TC["D = A × B + C
Entire matrix operation
in ONE cycle"] end subgraph "Speed Comparison" Speed["CUDA Cores: ~1 TFLOPS (FP32)
Tensor Cores: ~300+ TFLOPS (FP16)

300x faster for matrix ops!"] end style TC fill:#90EE90 style Speed fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Matrix Multiply-Accumulate (MMA)
steps.explanation:
The fundamental Tensor Core operation is **D = A × B + C** where:
- A, B are input matrices (in lower precision)
- C is the accumulator (can be higher precision)
- D is the output

This maps perfectly to neural network operations: `output = weights × input + bias`
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "MMA Operation" A["Matrix A
M × K"] B["Matrix B
K × N"] C["Matrix C
M × N"] D["Matrix D
M × N"] end A -->|"×"| Mult["A × B"] B --> Mult Mult -->|"+"| Add["+ C"] C --> Add Add --> D subgraph "Neural Network Mapping" NN["output = weights × activations + bias

A = weights
B = activations
C = bias
D = output"] end style D fill:#90EE90 style NN fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Tensor Core Shapes
steps.explanation: Tensor Cores operate on fixed matrix tile sizes. The basic unit is a small matrix fragment that the hardware can process efficiently.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Volta/Turing (First Gen)" V1["4×4×4 FP16
A[4×4] × B[4×4] = C[4×4]"] end subgraph "Ampere (2nd Gen)" A1["8×8×4 for various types
16×8×8 for INT8
More flexible shapes"] end subgraph "Hopper (3rd Gen)" H1["Up to 64×64×16
Larger tiles = more efficiency
New FP8 support"] end subgraph "Warp-Level Operation" Warp["32 threads cooperate to:
• Load matrix fragments
• Execute MMA
• Store results

All 32 threads MUST participate"] end V1 --> A1 --> H1 style H1 fill:#90EE90 style Warp fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Precision Modes
steps.explanation: Tensor Cores support multiple precision combinations. The accumulator is typically higher precision to maintain accuracy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Input Precision → Accumulator" FP16["FP16 × FP16 → FP16/FP32"] BF16["BF16 × BF16 → FP32"] TF32["TF32 × TF32 → FP32"] FP8["FP8 × FP8 → FP16/FP32"] INT8["INT8 × INT8 → INT32"] INT4["INT4 × INT4 → INT32"] end subgraph "Use Cases" UC1["FP16/BF16: Training"] UC2["TF32: Drop-in FP32 replacement"] UC3["FP8: Fast training (Hopper+)"] UC4["INT8/INT4: Inference"] end subgraph "Throughput (H100)" TP["FP16: 989 TFLOPS
TF32: 495 TFLOPS
FP8: 1,979 TFLOPS
INT8: 1,979 TOPS"] end style BF16 fill:#90EE90 style FP8 fill:#ADD8E6 style TP fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: WMMA API (Warp Matrix Multiply-Accumulate)
steps.explanation: CUDA provides the **WMMA API** for programming Tensor Cores. Operations are performed at the warp level (32 threads).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "WMMA Workflow" Step1["1. Declare fragments
wmma::fragment<...> a, b, c"] Step2["2. Load from memory
wmma::load_matrix_sync(a, ptr, stride)"] Step3["3. Execute MMA
wmma::mma_sync(c, a, b, c)"] Step4["4. Store results
wmma::store_matrix_sync(ptr, c, stride)"] end Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 subgraph "Fragment Types" Frag["matrix_a: M×K (row major)
matrix_b: K×N (col major)
accumulator: M×N"] end subgraph "Key Points" KP1["All 32 threads must call together"] KP2["_sync suffix = warp synchronization"] KP3["Fragments distributed across threads"] end style Step3 fill:#90EE90 style KP1 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Fragment Distribution
steps.explanation: Matrix fragments are distributed across the 32 threads of a warp. Each thread holds a portion of the data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "16×16 Matrix Fragment" Mat["256 elements total
Distributed across 32 threads
8 elements per thread"] end subgraph "Thread Distribution" T0["Thread 0: elements 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7"] T1["Thread 1: elements 8,9,10,11,12,13,14,15"] TN["...
Thread 31: last 8 elements"] end subgraph "Why This Matters" Why["• Can't access individual elements
• Must use load/store APIs
• Hardware manages layout
• Enables parallel execution"] end Mat --> T0 Mat --> T1 Mat --> TN style Mat fill:#ADD8E6 style Why fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Memory Requirements
steps.explanation: Tensor Cores have strict alignment and layout requirements for optimal performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Alignment Requirements" A1["Matrix A: 16-byte aligned"] A2["Matrix B: 16-byte aligned"] A3["Leading dimension: multiple of 8"] end subgraph "Layout Requirements" L1["A: Row-major (default) or Col-major"] L2["B: Col-major (default) or Row-major"] L3["Must match fragment declaration"] end subgraph "Performance Tips" P1["Use shared memory for fragments"] P2["Coalesce global memory loads"] P3["Pad matrices to alignment"] P4["Avoid bank conflicts"] end subgraph "Common Pitfall" Pit["Misaligned data:
• Silent wrong results
• Or major slowdown
• Always verify alignment!"] end style Pit fill:#FFB6C1 style P1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Tiled Matrix Multiplication
steps.explanation: Large matrices are processed in tiles. Each tile uses Tensor Core operations, and tiles are accumulated.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Large Matrix Multiply" BigA["Matrix A
1024 × 1024"] BigB["Matrix B
1024 × 1024"] BigC["Result C
1024 × 1024"] end subgraph "Tiling Strategy" Tile["Split into 16×16 tiles
64 × 64 = 4096 tiles

For each output tile:
C_tile = Σ A_tile × B_tile"] end subgraph "Outer Loop" OL["for tile_m in range(M/16):
for tile_n in range(N/16):
c_frag = 0
for tile_k in range(K/16):
a_frag = load(A, tile_m, tile_k)
b_frag = load(B, tile_k, tile_n)
c_frag = mma(a_frag, b_frag, c_frag)
store(C, tile_m, tile_n, c_frag)"] end BigA --> Tile BigB --> Tile Tile --> OL --> BigC style Tile fill:#ADD8E6 style OL fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Shared Memory Staging
steps.explanation: For best performance, load tiles into shared memory before feeding Tensor Cores. This reduces global memory traffic.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Shared Memory" Bad["Global Mem → Tensor Core
High latency, low bandwidth"] end subgraph "With Shared Memory" Good["Global Mem → Shared Mem → Tensor Core
Load once, reuse many times"] end subgraph "Pipeline Pattern" Pipe["1. Load tile A to shared mem
2. Load tile B to shared mem
3. __syncthreads()
4. WMMA from shared mem
5. Repeat for next tiles"] end subgraph "Double Buffering" DB["While computing tile N:
Prefetch tile N+1

Hides memory latency"] end Bad -.->|"slow"| X["✗"] Good -->|"fast"| Check["✓"] style Good fill:#90EE90 style DB fill:#ADD8E6 style X fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: cuBLAS and cuDNN
steps.explanation: You rarely program Tensor Cores directly. Libraries like **cuBLAS** and **cuDNN** use them automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Library Stack" PyTorch["PyTorch: torch.matmul()"] cuBLAS["cuBLAS: cublasGemmEx()"] TC["Tensor Cores"] end PyTorch --> cuBLAS --> TC subgraph "Automatic Usage" Auto["cuBLAS auto-selects Tensor Cores when:
• Dimensions are multiples of 8
• Data types are FP16/BF16/TF32
• Alignment requirements met"] end subgraph "Enabling in PyTorch" PT["# Use TF32 (default on Ampere+)
torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True

# Use FP16
with torch.autocast('cuda'):
output = model(input)"] end style cuBLAS fill:#90EE90 style PT fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Dimension Padding
steps.explanation: Tensor Cores work best when matrix dimensions are multiples of 8 (or 16). Padding odd-sized matrices improves performance significantly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" Prob["Matrix: 127 × 127
Not a multiple of 8
→ Falls back to CUDA cores
→ Much slower!"] end subgraph "Solution: Padding" Sol["Pad to 128 × 128
Multiple of 8 ✓
→ Uses Tensor Cores
→ Fast!"] end subgraph "PyTorch Tip" Tip["# Pad embedding dims, hidden sizes
hidden_size = 768 # ✓ divisible by 8
hidden_size = 770 # ✗ not divisible

# Pad batch size if needed
batch_size = 32 # ✓"] end subgraph "Performance Impact" Impact["127×127: ~50 TFLOPS
128×128: ~300 TFLOPS

6x speedup from 1 extra row/col!"] end Prob --> Sol style Prob fill:#FFB6C1 style Sol fill:#90EE90 style Impact fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Hopper Innovations
steps.explanation: NVIDIA Hopper (H100) introduced significant Tensor Core improvements with the **Transformer Engine** and new instructions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hopper New Features" H1["FP8 Support
2x throughput vs FP16"] H2["Larger Tiles
Up to 64×64"] H3["Transformer Engine
Automatic FP8 scaling"] H4["TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator)
Async bulk data movement"] end subgraph "Transformer Engine" TE["Per-tensor dynamic scaling
• Tracks activation ranges
• Adjusts scale factors
• Maintains accuracy in FP8"] end subgraph "FP8 Throughput (H100)" FP8["FP8: 1,979 TFLOPS
FP16: 989 TFLOPS
TF32: 495 TFLOPS

FP8 = 2x FP16 speed!"] end style H1 fill:#90EE90 style H3 fill:#ADD8E6 style FP8 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: When Are Tensor Cores Used?
steps.explanation: Here's how to ensure your operations use Tensor Cores rather than falling back to slower CUDA cores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Requirements for Tensor Cores" R1["✓ Matrix multiply (GEMM) operation"] R2["✓ FP16, BF16, TF32, or INT8 data"] R3["✓ Dimensions multiple of 8"] R4["✓ Aligned memory access"] R5["✓ Using cuBLAS/cuDNN"] end subgraph "Common Failures" F1["✗ Small matrices (< 256 elements)"] F2["✗ Odd dimensions (not mult of 8)"] F3["✗ FP32 without TF32 enabled"] F4["✗ Element-wise ops (not matmul)"] end subgraph "Verification" Ver["# Profile with Nsight
ncu --set full python train.py

Look for:
• sm__pipe_tensor_cycles
• tensor_active > 0"] end style R1 fill:#90EE90 style F1 fill:#FFB6C1 style Ver fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of Tensor Core essentials for ML practitioners.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Key Takeaways" K1["Tensor Cores = fast matrix multiply"] K2["D = A × B + C in one cycle"] K3["Use FP16/BF16/TF32 for training"] K4["Dimensions should be multiples of 8"] end subgraph "In PyTorch" PT1["# Enable TF32 (usually default)
torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True"] PT2["# Use automatic mixed precision
with torch.autocast('cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
output = model(input)"] end subgraph "Best Practices" BP1["Pad dimensions to multiples of 8"] BP2["Use cuBLAS/cuDNN (automatic)"] BP3["Enable TF32 or use AMP"] BP4["Profile to verify TC usage"] end style K1 fill:#90EE90 style PT2 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Multi-GPU Training Strategies
fields.description: Master data parallelism, model parallelism, tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism, and ZeRO

steps.title: Why Multi-GPU Training?
steps.explanation:
Single GPUs have memory and compute limits. Modern models require multiple GPUs to:

- **Fit larger models** (LLMs exceed single GPU memory)
- **Train faster** (parallelize computation)
- **Use larger batch sizes** (better gradient estimates)
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single GPU Limits" S1["Memory: 80GB (H100)"] S2["LLaMA 70B: ~140GB needed"] S3["→ Doesn't fit!"] end subgraph "Multi-GPU Solutions" M1["Split model across GPUs"] M2["Split data across GPUs"] M3["Or both!"] end subgraph "Parallelism Types" P1["Data Parallelism
Same model, different data"] P2["Model Parallelism
Split model layers"] P3["Tensor Parallelism
Split individual layers"] P4["Pipeline Parallelism
Split stages"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 --> M1 S3 --> M2 style S3 fill:#FFB6C1 style M1 fill:#90EE90 style M2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Data Parallelism (DP)
steps.explanation:
**Data Parallelism** is the simplest approach: replicate the entire model on each GPU, split the batch across GPUs, then synchronize gradients.

Each GPU processes a different mini-batch of data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data Parallel Setup" Data["Batch: 32 samples"] GPU1["GPU 0
Full Model
Samples 0-7"] GPU2["GPU 1
Full Model
Samples 8-15"] GPU3["GPU 2
Full Model
Samples 16-23"] GPU4["GPU 3
Full Model
Samples 24-31"] end Data --> GPU1 Data --> GPU2 Data --> GPU3 Data --> GPU4 subgraph "Gradient Sync" Sync["All-Reduce gradients
Average across all GPUs
Then update weights"] end GPU1 --> Sync GPU2 --> Sync GPU3 --> Sync GPU4 --> Sync subgraph "Result" R["All GPUs have identical weights
Effective batch = 32
4x faster than single GPU"] end Sync --> R style Sync fill:#ADD8E6 style R fill:#90EE90

steps.title: DP vs DDP
steps.explanation:
PyTorch has two data parallel implementations:

- **DataParallel (DP)**: Single-process, simple but slow
- **DistributedDataParallel (DDP)**: Multi-process, fast and scalable

**Always use DDP** for serious training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataParallel (DP) - Avoid" DP1["Single Python process"] DP2["GIL bottleneck"] DP3["Data copied through GPU 0"] DP4["Uneven GPU memory usage"] end subgraph "DistributedDataParallel (DDP) - Use This" DDP1["One process per GPU"] DDP2["No GIL issues"] DDP3["Direct GPU-to-GPU (NCCL)"] DDP4["Even memory distribution"] end subgraph "Code" Code["# DDP setup
torch.distributed.init_process_group('nccl')
model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank])

# Run with torchrun
torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py"] end style DP1 fill:#FFB6C1 style DDP1 fill:#90EE90 style Code fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: All-Reduce Communication
steps.explanation: **All-Reduce** is the key operation in data parallelism. It sums gradients across all GPUs and distributes the result back to all GPUs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Before All-Reduce" G0["GPU 0: grad = [1, 2]"] G1["GPU 1: grad = [3, 4]"] G2["GPU 2: grad = [5, 6]"] G3["GPU 3: grad = [7, 8]"] end subgraph "All-Reduce Operation" Op["Sum all gradients:
[1+3+5+7, 2+4+6+8]
= [16, 20]"] end subgraph "After All-Reduce" R0["GPU 0: grad = [16, 20]"] R1["GPU 1: grad = [16, 20]"] R2["GPU 2: grad = [16, 20]"] R3["GPU 3: grad = [16, 20]"] end G0 --> Op G1 --> Op G2 --> Op G3 --> Op Op --> R0 Op --> R1 Op --> R2 Op --> R3 subgraph "Implementations" Impl["Ring All-Reduce: O(N) bandwidth-optimal
Tree All-Reduce: O(log N) latency-optimal
NCCL chooses automatically"] end style Op fill:#90EE90 style Impl fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Data Parallelism Limitations
steps.explanation: Data parallelism requires the **full model** to fit on each GPU. For large models like GPT-3 (175B parameters), this isn't possible.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Memory Per GPU (Data Parallel)" Mem["Model weights: W
Gradients: W
Optimizer states: 2W (Adam)
Activations: varies

Total: ~4W + activations"] end subgraph "Example: 7B Parameter Model" Ex["7B params × 4 bytes = 28 GB
+ Gradients: 28 GB
+ Adam states: 56 GB
+ Activations: ~20 GB

Total: ~132 GB per GPU!"] end subgraph "Problem" Prob["H100 has 80 GB
→ Model doesn't fit
→ Need model parallelism"] end Mem --> Ex --> Prob style Prob fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Model Parallelism (Layer-wise)
steps.explanation: **Model Parallelism** splits the model across GPUs. Each GPU holds different layers and passes activations to the next GPU.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "GPU 0" L1["Layers 1-8"] end subgraph "GPU 1" L2["Layers 9-16"] end subgraph "GPU 2" L3["Layers 17-24"] end subgraph "GPU 3" L4["Layers 25-32"] end Input["Input"] --> L1 L1 -->|"activations"| L2 L2 -->|"activations"| L3 L3 -->|"activations"| L4 L4 --> Output["Output"] subgraph "Problem: Low Utilization" Prob["GPU 0 works → GPUs 1,2,3 idle
GPU 1 works → GPUs 0,2,3 idle
...
Only 25% utilization!"] end style Prob fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Pipeline Parallelism
steps.explanation: **Pipeline Parallelism** solves the idle GPU problem by processing multiple micro-batches simultaneously. While GPU 1 processes batch 1, GPU 0 starts batch 2.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Time →" T1["t=1"] T2["t=2"] T3["t=3"] T4["t=4"] T5["t=5"] end subgraph "GPU 0 (Layers 1-8)" G0T1["B1"] G0T2["B2"] G0T3["B3"] G0T4["B4"] G0T5["-"] end subgraph "GPU 1 (Layers 9-16)" G1T1["-"] G1T2["B1"] G1T3["B2"] G1T4["B3"] G1T5["B4"] end subgraph "GPU 2 (Layers 17-24)" G2T1["-"] G2T2["-"] G2T3["B1"] G2T4["B2"] G2T5["B3"] end subgraph "Key Concepts" KC["Micro-batches: B1, B2, B3, B4
Pipeline bubble: startup/shutdown idle time
More micro-batches = less bubble overhead"] end style G0T1 fill:#90EE90 style G1T2 fill:#90EE90 style G2T3 fill:#90EE90 style KC fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Tensor Parallelism
steps.explanation: **Tensor Parallelism** splits individual layers across GPUs. For transformers, this means splitting attention heads and MLP columns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single GPU" Single["Linear layer: [4096 × 16384]
All on one GPU"] end subgraph "Tensor Parallel (4 GPUs)" TP0["GPU 0: [4096 × 4096]"] TP1["GPU 1: [4096 × 4096]"] TP2["GPU 2: [4096 × 4096]"] TP3["GPU 3: [4096 × 4096]"] end subgraph "Attention Heads Split" AH["32 attention heads
→ 8 heads per GPU
Each GPU computes partial attention
All-reduce to combine"] end subgraph "Advantages" Adv["• Reduces memory per GPU
• Minimal pipeline bubbles
• Good for wide layers"] end subgraph "Disadvantage" Dis["Requires fast GPU interconnect
(NVLink preferred)"] end Single --> TP0 Single --> TP1 Single --> TP2 Single --> TP3 style TP0 fill:#90EE90 style AH fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: ZeRO: Zero Redundancy Optimizer
steps.explanation: **ZeRO** (from DeepSpeed) eliminates memory redundancy in data parallelism by partitioning optimizer states, gradients, and parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Data Parallel" SDP["Each GPU stores:
• Full weights (W)
• Full gradients (W)
• Full optimizer (2W)
Total: 4W per GPU"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 1" Z1["Partition optimizer states
Each GPU: W + W + 2W/N
Saves optimizer memory"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 2" Z2["+ Partition gradients
Each GPU: W + W/N + 2W/N
Saves gradient memory"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 3 (FSDP)" Z3["+ Partition weights
Each GPU: W/N + W/N + 2W/N
= 4W/N total
Near-linear scaling!"] end SDP --> Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 subgraph "Memory Comparison (8 GPUs)" Comp["Standard: 4W per GPU
ZeRO-3: 0.5W per GPU
8x memory reduction!"] end style Z3 fill:#90EE90 style Comp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: ZeRO Stage 3 / FSDP
steps.explanation: **ZeRO-3** (DeepSpeed) and **FSDP** (PyTorch) fully shard all model states. During forward/backward, parameters are gathered just-in-time.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sharded State" Shard["GPU 0: params[0:N/4]
GPU 1: params[N/4:N/2]
GPU 2: params[N/2:3N/4]
GPU 3: params[3N/4:N]"] end subgraph "Forward Pass" FW1["1. All-gather layer params"] FW2["2. Compute forward"] FW3["3. Discard non-owned params"] FW4["4. Repeat for next layer"] end subgraph "Backward Pass" BW1["1. All-gather layer params"] BW2["2. Compute gradients"] BW3["3. Reduce-scatter gradients"] BW4["4. Each GPU keeps its shard"] end Shard --> FW1 --> FW2 --> FW3 --> FW4 subgraph "Trade-off" Trade["More communication
but fits larger models
in less total memory"] end style Shard fill:#ADD8E6 style Trade fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Combining Parallelism Strategies
steps.explanation: Large-scale training often combines multiple parallelism strategies. This is called **3D Parallelism** or **hybrid parallelism**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "3D Parallelism Example" Config["64 GPUs total
Data Parallel: 4-way
Tensor Parallel: 4-way
Pipeline Parallel: 4-way
4 × 4 × 4 = 64"] end subgraph "Hierarchy" DP["Data Parallel Groups
(across nodes)"] TP["Tensor Parallel Groups
(within node, NVLink)"] PP["Pipeline Parallel Groups
(across nodes)"] end subgraph "Why This Works" Why["• TP needs fast interconnect → within node
• PP tolerates latency → across nodes
• DP scales batch size → across nodes"] end subgraph "Real Example: GPT-3 175B" GPT3["• 8-way tensor parallel
• 8-way pipeline parallel
• 8-way data parallel
• Total: 512 GPUs"] end style Config fill:#90EE90 style GPT3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Memory Comparison
steps.explanation: Here's how different strategies affect memory usage for a 7B parameter model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "7B Model Memory Analysis" Base["Base memory per GPU:
Weights: 28 GB (FP32)
Gradients: 28 GB
Adam: 56 GB
Total: 112 GB"] end subgraph "Data Parallel" DP["Each GPU: 112 GB
→ Doesn't fit in 80GB!"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 1" Z1["Each GPU: ~70 GB
Partitioned optimizer"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 2" Z2["Each GPU: ~42 GB
+ Partitioned grads"] end subgraph "ZeRO Stage 3 / FSDP" Z3["Each GPU: ~14 GB (8 GPUs)
Fully sharded
Fits easily!"] end Base --> DP Base --> Z1 Base --> Z2 Base --> Z3 style DP fill:#FFB6C1 style Z3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing a Strategy
steps.explanation: Use this decision tree to choose the right parallelism strategy for your use case.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Start["Does model fit on 1 GPU?"] Start -->|"Yes"| Q1{"Need faster training?"} Start -->|"No"| Q2{"How much larger?"} Q1 -->|"Yes"| DDP["Use DDP
Data Parallel"] Q1 -->|"No"| Single["Single GPU is fine"] Q2 -->|"2-4x"| FSDP["Use FSDP/ZeRO-3
Shard everything"] Q2 -->|"10x+"| Q3{"Have NVLink?"} Q3 -->|"Yes"| TP["Tensor Parallel
+ FSDP"] Q3 -->|"No"| PP["Pipeline Parallel
+ FSDP"] subgraph "Quick Guide" QG["DDP: Simple, model fits, need speed
FSDP: Model barely fits, easy setup
TP: Large model, fast interconnect
PP: Very large model, any interconnect"] end style DDP fill:#90EE90 style FSDP fill:#ADD8E6 style QG fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: PyTorch Code Examples
steps.explanation: Here's how to set up each parallelism strategy in PyTorch.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DDP (Data Parallel)" DDP_Code["# Launch with torchrun
model = DDP(model)"] end subgraph "FSDP (Fully Sharded)" FSDP_Code["from torch.distributed.fsdp import FSDP
model = FSDP(
model,
sharding_strategy=FULL_SHARD,
mixed_precision=MixedPrecision(...)
)"] end subgraph "DeepSpeed ZeRO" DS_Code["import deepspeed
model, optimizer, _, _ = deepspeed.initialize(
model=model,
config='ds_config.json' # ZeRO stage 3
)"] end subgraph "Tensor Parallel (Megatron)" TP_Code["# Requires model changes
from megatron.core import tensor_parallel
linear = tensor_parallel.ColumnParallelLinear(...)"] end style DDP_Code fill:#90EE90 style FSDP_Code fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of multi-GPU training strategies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Strategy Summary" S1["DDP: Replicate model, split data
FSDP/ZeRO-3: Shard everything
TP: Split layers horizontally
PP: Split layers vertically"] end subgraph "Communication Patterns" C1["DDP: All-reduce gradients
FSDP: All-gather + reduce-scatter
TP: All-reduce activations
PP: Point-to-point activations"] end subgraph "Best Practices" BP1["Start with DDP if model fits"] BP2["Use FSDP for larger models"] BP3["Combine strategies for huge models"] BP4["Profile to find bottlenecks"] end subgraph "Tools" Tools["PyTorch: DDP, FSDP
DeepSpeed: ZeRO
Megatron-LM: TP + PP
FairScale: FSDP predecessor"] end style S1 fill:#ADD8E6 style BP1 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: GPU Memory Optimization Techniques
fields.description: Master memory coalescing, bank conflicts, tiling strategies, and memory-bound vs compute-bound optimization

steps.title: GPU Memory Hierarchy Review
steps.explanation: Understanding the memory hierarchy is essential for optimization. Each level has different speed, size, and access patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Memory Hierarchy (H100)" Reg["Registers
Fastest, per-thread
~256 KB per SM"] Shared["Shared Memory
Per-block, ~19 TB/s
228 KB per SM"] L1["L1 Cache
Per-SM, automatic
256 KB per SM"] L2["L2 Cache
Shared across SMs
50 MB total"] HBM["HBM3 Global Memory
80 GB, ~3 TB/s
Slowest"] end Reg --> Shared --> L1 --> L2 --> HBM subgraph "Access Latency" Lat["Registers: 1 cycle
Shared: ~20 cycles
L1: ~30 cycles
L2: ~200 cycles
HBM: ~400 cycles"] end subgraph "Key Insight" Key["Minimize HBM access!
Reuse data in shared memory
and registers"] end style Reg fill:#90EE90 style HBM fill:#FFB6C1 style Key fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Memory Coalescing
steps.explanation: **Memory coalescing** combines multiple thread memory accesses into fewer transactions. When threads access consecutive addresses, the GPU can load a single cache line instead of many separate loads.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coalesced Access (GOOD)" C1["Thread 0 → addr 0"] C2["Thread 1 → addr 4"] C3["Thread 2 → addr 8"] C4["Thread 31 → addr 124"] CResult["→ 1 transaction (128 bytes)"] end subgraph "Strided Access (BAD)" S1["Thread 0 → addr 0"] S2["Thread 1 → addr 512"] S3["Thread 2 → addr 1024"] S4["Thread 31 → addr 15872"] SResult["→ 32 transactions!"] end subgraph "Impact" Impact["Coalesced: Full bandwidth
Strided: 1/32 bandwidth
32x slowdown!"] end C1 --> CResult S1 --> SResult style CResult fill:#90EE90 style SResult fill:#FFB6C1 style Impact fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Coalescing in Practice
steps.explanation: Array access patterns determine coalescing. Adjacent threads should access adjacent memory.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "2D Array: Row-major in C" RM["Memory: [row0][row1][row2]...

data[i][j]: row-major layout"] end subgraph "Good: Access along rows" Good["// tid = thread index
data[row][tid] // Adjacent threads → adjacent addresses
→ COALESCED"] end subgraph "Bad: Access along columns" Bad["data[tid][col] // Adjacent threads → strided addresses
→ NOT COALESCED"] end subgraph "Fix: Transpose or change access" Fix["Option 1: Transpose data
Option 2: Load to shared memory
in coalesced pattern,
then access strided from shared"] end RM --> Good RM --> Bad Bad --> Fix style Good fill:#90EE90 style Bad fill:#FFB6C1 style Fix fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Shared Memory Basics
steps.explanation: **Shared memory** is fast, programmer-managed memory shared by all threads in a block. It's organized into **banks** for parallel access.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Shared Memory Properties" P1["~100x faster than global memory"] P2["Limited size (~48-228 KB per SM)"] P3["Shared within thread block"] P4["Programmer managed"] end subgraph "Declaration" Code["__shared__ float tile[16][16];

// All threads in block can
// read/write 'tile'"] end subgraph "Use Cases" UC1["Cache global memory reads"] UC2["Inter-thread communication"] UC3["Avoid redundant loads"] UC4["Enable coalesced patterns"] end subgraph "Synchronization" Sync["__syncthreads();
Barrier for all threads in block
REQUIRED after shared mem writes
before other threads read"] end style P1 fill:#90EE90 style Sync fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Shared Memory Banks
steps.explanation: Shared memory is divided into 32 **banks** (like lanes on a highway). Each bank can serve one address per cycle. Conflicts occur when multiple threads access the same bank.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "32 Banks" Banks["Bank 0: addr 0, 32, 64...
Bank 1: addr 1, 33, 65...
Bank 2: addr 2, 34, 66...
...
Bank 31: addr 31, 63, 95..."] end subgraph "No Conflict" NC["Thread 0 → Bank 0
Thread 1 → Bank 1
Thread 2 → Bank 2
...
All different banks → parallel access"] end subgraph "Bank Conflict" BC["Thread 0 → Bank 0
Thread 1 → Bank 0 (conflict!)
Thread 2 → Bank 0 (conflict!)
...
Same bank → serialized access"] end subgraph "32-way Conflict" Worst["All threads access Bank 0
→ 32x slower!"] end Banks --> NC Banks --> BC BC --> Worst style NC fill:#90EE90 style Worst fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Avoiding Bank Conflicts
steps.explanation: Common patterns that cause bank conflicts and how to fix them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Conflict: Column Access" Col["__shared__ float data[32][32];

// Each thread accesses same column
data[tid][0]; // All hit bank 0!"] end subgraph "Fix 1: Padding" Pad["__shared__ float data[32][33]; // Add 1 column

// Now columns are staggered across banks
data[tid][0]; // Different banks!"] end subgraph "Fix 2: Transpose access" Trans["// Access rows instead of columns
data[0][tid]; // Different banks"] end subgraph "Conflict: Stride-32 access" Stride["data[tid * 32]; // All same bank!"] end subgraph "Fix: Change stride" FixS["data[tid * 33]; // Different banks"] end Col --> Pad Col --> Trans Stride --> FixS style Col fill:#FFB6C1 style Pad fill:#90EE90 style Stride fill:#FFB6C1 style FixS fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Tiling Strategy
steps.explanation: **Tiling** loads data into shared memory in chunks (tiles), processes the tile, then moves to the next. This maximizes data reuse.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Tiling" NoTile["Each element loaded from global memory
No reuse
Memory bound"] end subgraph "With Tiling" Tile["1. Load TILE_SIZE × TILE_SIZE to shared
2. Process entire tile
3. Move to next tile
4. Repeat"] end subgraph "Matrix Multiply Example" MM["C[i,j] = Σ A[i,k] × B[k,j]

Each A[i,k] used N times (all j)
Each B[k,j] used M times (all i)

Load once to shared, use many times!"] end subgraph "Reuse Factor" Reuse["Tile size: 16×16
Each element used 16 times
→ 16x fewer global loads!"] end NoTile --> Tile --> MM style Tile fill:#90EE90 style Reuse fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Tiled Matrix Multiply
steps.explanation: Step-by-step tiled matrix multiplication, the canonical example of GPU memory optimization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Algorithm" Step1["1. Load A_tile[TILE×TILE] to shared"] Step2["2. Load B_tile[TILE×TILE] to shared"] Step3["3. __syncthreads()"] Step4["4. Each thread: partial_sum += A_tile × B_tile"] Step5["5. __syncthreads()"] Step6["6. Move to next tile, repeat"] Step7["7. Write final sum to C"] end Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Step5 --> Step6 --> Step7 subgraph "Memory Access Pattern" Pattern["Global reads: 2 × N × (N/TILE)²
vs without tiling: 2 × N³

With TILE=16, N=1024:
8M reads vs 2B reads!"] end subgraph "Key Insight" Key["Each global load is REUSED
TILE times from shared memory"] end style Step4 fill:#90EE90 style Key fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Memory Bound vs Compute Bound
steps.explanation: Operations are limited by either memory bandwidth or compute throughput. Different optimizations apply to each.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Compute Bound" CB["Arithmetic intensity HIGH
Many ops per byte loaded

Examples:
• Matrix multiply
• Convolutions

Optimize: More compute per thread"] end subgraph "Memory Bound" MB["Arithmetic intensity LOW
Few ops per byte loaded

Examples:
• Element-wise ops
• Reductions
• Softmax

Optimize: Reduce memory traffic"] end subgraph "Arithmetic Intensity" AI["AI = FLOPs / Bytes loaded

Example:
Vector add: 1 FLOP / 12 bytes = 0.08
MatMul NxN: N FLOPs / 4 bytes = N/4"] end subgraph "Roofline Model" Roof["If AI < HW_ratio: memory bound
If AI > HW_ratio: compute bound

H100: ~10 FLOPs/byte threshold"] end style CB fill:#90EE90 style MB fill:#ADD8E6 style AI fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Kernel Fusion
steps.explanation: **Kernel fusion** combines multiple operations into a single kernel, reducing memory traffic between operations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Fusion" WF1["Kernel 1: y = x + 1
Read x from HBM
Write y to HBM"] WF2["Kernel 2: z = y * 2
Read y from HBM
Write z to HBM"] WF3["4 HBM accesses"] end subgraph "With Fusion" Fused["Single Kernel: z = (x + 1) * 2
Read x from HBM
y in registers only
Write z to HBM"] FResult["2 HBM accesses
2x faster!"] end WF1 --> WF2 --> WF3 Fused --> FResult subgraph "Tools for Fusion" Tools["• torch.compile (TorchInductor)
• Triton
• Custom CUDA kernels
• XLA"] end style WF3 fill:#FFB6C1 style FResult fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Register Pressure
steps.explanation: Each thread has limited registers. Using too many **spills** to slow local memory. Using too few reduces parallelism.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Register Limits" Lim["Max 255 registers per thread
65536 registers per SM (H100)

More registers per thread =
fewer concurrent threads"] end subgraph "Occupancy Trade-off" Occ["High register usage:
• Less parallelism
• But more work per thread

Low register usage:
• More parallelism
• But may spill to memory"] end subgraph "Spilling" Spill["When registers exhausted:
Compiler spills to 'local memory'
Actually in global memory!
Very slow!"] end subgraph "Optimization" Opt["1. Reduce variables in loop
2. Recompute vs store
3. Use __launch_bounds__
4. Check nvcc --ptxas-options=-v"] end Lim --> Occ Occ --> Spill style Spill fill:#FFB6C1 style Opt fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Memory Access Patterns
steps.explanation: Common access patterns and their performance characteristics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sequential (Best)" Seq["data[tid]
Coalesced, full bandwidth"] end subgraph "Strided (Varies)" Str["data[tid * stride]
Small stride: OK
Large stride: Bad"] end subgraph "Random (Worst)" Rand["data[random_idx]
Cache misses, low bandwidth"] end subgraph "Broadcast (OK)" Broad["data[0] // All threads same addr
L1 cache broadcast
No conflict, but serialized"] end subgraph "Optimization Strategies" Strat["1. Reorganize data for sequential access
2. Use shared memory to reorder
3. Sort indices before access
4. Use texture cache for random"] end Seq --> Str --> Rand style Seq fill:#90EE90 style Rand fill:#FFB6C1 style Strat fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Prefetching and Async Copies
steps.explanation: Modern GPUs support asynchronous memory copies to overlap data movement with computation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Synchronous (Old Way)" Sync1["Load tile N"] Sync2["Compute tile N"] Sync3["Load tile N+1"] Sync4["Compute tile N+1"] Sync1 --> Sync2 --> Sync3 --> Sync4 end subgraph "Async/Prefetch (Modern)" Async1["Load tile N"] Async2["Compute N-1 | Load N+1"] Async3["Compute N | Load N+2"] Async1 --> Async2 --> Async3 end subgraph "CUDA API" API["// Async copy (Ampere+)
cuda::memcpy_async(dst, src, size, barrier);

// Wait for completion
barrier.arrive_and_wait();"] end subgraph "Benefit" Ben["Overlaps memory latency
with computation
Can hide ~100% of load time"] end style Async2 fill:#90EE90 style Ben fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Profiling Memory Performance
steps.explanation: Use NVIDIA profiling tools to identify memory bottlenecks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Key Metrics" M1["Global Memory Throughput
Should be close to peak (~3 TB/s)"] M2["L2 Hit Rate
Higher = better cache usage"] M3["Shared Memory Efficiency
Bank conflicts reduce this"] M4["Achieved Occupancy
Thread utilization"] end subgraph "Tools" T1["nsight-compute (ncu)
Detailed kernel analysis"] T2["nsight-systems (nsys)
Timeline view"] T3["torch.profiler
PyTorch integration"] end subgraph "Example Command" Cmd["ncu --set full \
--target-processes all \
python train.py"] end subgraph "Common Issues" Issues["Low throughput → coalescing
High L2 miss → working set too large
Low SM efficiency → bank conflicts"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Issues fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of GPU memory optimization techniques.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Essential Techniques"
        E1["Coalescing: Adjacent threads → adjacent memory"]
        E2["Tiling: Load to shared, reuse many times"]
        E3["Bank conflicts: Pad arrays, avoid stride-32"]
        E4["Fusion: Combine kernels, reduce HBM access"]
    end

    subgraph "Checklist"
        C1["✓ Access memory sequentially"]
        C2["✓ Use shared memory for reuse"]
        C3["✓ Pad shared arrays to avoid conflicts"]
        C4["✓ Fuse element-wise operations"]
        C5["✓ Profile with ncu to verify"]
    end

    subgraph "Rules of Thumb"
        R1["Coalesced > cached > random"]
        R2["Shared memory > global memory"]
        R3["Compute more, load less"]
        R4["Profile before optimizing"]
    end

    style E1 fill:#90EE90
    style C1 fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: FlashAttention: Memory-Efficient Attention
fields.description: Understand how FlashAttention reduces memory from O(n²) to O(n) using tiling and kernel fusion

steps.title: The Attention Memory Problem
steps.explanation: Standard attention computes **Q × K^T** creating an N×N attention matrix. For long sequences, this matrix becomes huge and causes out-of-memory errors.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Attention Memory" QKV["Q, K, V: N × d
Each: N × d × 4 bytes"] Attn["Attention scores: N × N
N² × 4 bytes"] Soft["After softmax: N × N
N² × 4 bytes"] end subgraph "Memory Scaling" Ex1["N = 1K: 4 MB"] Ex2["N = 4K: 64 MB"] Ex3["N = 16K: 1 GB"] Ex4["N = 64K: 16 GB"] Ex5["N = 256K: 256 GB!"] end subgraph "Problem" Prob["Long sequences = OOM
Even if compute is fast,
can't fit attention matrix!"] end QKV --> Attn --> Soft Ex1 --> Ex2 --> Ex3 --> Ex4 --> Ex5 style Ex4 fill:#FFB6C1 style Ex5 fill:#FFB6C1 style Prob fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: FlashAttention Key Insight
steps.explanation: FlashAttention's key insight: **never materialize the full N×N attention matrix**. Instead, compute attention in tiles and accumulate results directly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Attention" S1["1. S = Q × K^T (N×N matrix)"] S2["2. P = softmax(S) (N×N matrix)"] S3["3. O = P × V"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 SMem["Memory: O(N²)"] end subgraph "FlashAttention" F1["Process in tiles"] F2["Never store full N×N"] F3["Accumulate output directly"] F1 --> F2 --> F3 FMem["Memory: O(N)"] end subgraph "The Challenge" Chal["Softmax needs ALL values
to compute denominator...
How to do in tiles?"] end Chal --> F1 style SMem fill:#FFB6C1 style FMem fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Tiled Computation
steps.explanation: FlashAttention processes Q, K, V in small tiles that fit in fast **SRAM** (shared memory). Each tile computes a partial result.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Tile Setup" Q["Q matrix: split into Q₁, Q₂, ..."] K["K matrix: split into K₁, K₂, ..."] V["V matrix: split into V₁, V₂, ..."] end subgraph "Processing" Loop["For each Q tile:
For each K,V tile:
Load Qᵢ, Kⱼ, Vⱼ to SRAM
Compute partial attention
Update running output"] end subgraph "Memory" Mem["Only store in SRAM:
• Current Q tile: Bq × d
• Current K tile: Bk × d
• Current V tile: Bk × d
• Running output: Bq × d

Independent of N!"] end Q --> Loop K --> Loop V --> Loop Loop --> Mem style Mem fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Online Softmax
steps.explanation: The clever trick: compute softmax **incrementally** using the online softmax algorithm. Track running max and sum to update correctly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Normal Softmax" NS["softmax(x)ᵢ = exp(xᵢ) / Σⱼ exp(xⱼ)

Need ALL values to compute
the denominator!"] end subgraph "Online Softmax" OS1["Track running: m (max), l (sum)"] OS2["For new values x:
m_new = max(m, max(x))
l_new = l × exp(m - m_new) + Σ exp(x - m_new)"] OS3["Rescale previous results
by exp(m_old - m_new)"] end subgraph "Key Equations" Eq["m = max(m, max(Sᵢⱼ))
l = l × exp(m_prev - m) + Σ exp(Sᵢⱼ - m)
O = O × exp(m_prev - m) + softmax_tile × Vⱼ"] end NS --> OS1 --> OS2 --> OS3 style OS1 fill:#90EE90 style Eq fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: FlashAttention Algorithm
steps.explanation: The complete FlashAttention algorithm, processing tiles in a nested loop.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Algorithm"
        Init["Initialize O = 0, l = 0, m = -∞"]
        Loop1["For j = 1 to N/Bk:"]
        Load["  Load Kⱼ, Vⱼ to SRAM"]
        Comp["  Sᵢⱼ = Qᵢ × Kⱼᵀ"]
        Max["  m_new = max(m, rowmax(Sᵢⱼ))"]
        Soft["  P̃ᵢⱼ = exp(Sᵢⱼ - m_new)"]
        Sum["  l_new = l × exp(m - m_new) + rowsum(P̃ᵢⱼ)"]
        Update["  O = O × exp(m - m_new) + P̃ᵢⱼ × Vⱼ"]
        Save["  m = m_new, l = l_new"]
        Final["O = O / l"]
    end

    Init --> Loop1 --> Load --> Comp --> Max --> Soft --> Sum --> Update --> Save
    Save -->|"next j"| Loop1
    Loop1 -->|"done"| Final

    style Comp fill:#ADD8E6
    style Update fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Memory Comparison
steps.explanation: FlashAttention dramatically reduces memory usage, enabling much longer sequences.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Attention Memory" Std["Store N×N attention matrix
Memory: O(N²)"] end subgraph "FlashAttention Memory" FA["Store only tiles in SRAM
Output accumulator: O(N)
Memory: O(N)"] end subgraph "Concrete Example: N=8192, d=64" StdEx["Standard:
Attention matrix: 8192² × 4 = 256 MB
+ Q, K, V: 6 MB
= 262 MB"] FAEx["FlashAttention:
Tiles in SRAM: ~1 MB
Output: 2 MB
= ~3 MB"] Ratio["~87x memory reduction!"] end Std --> StdEx FA --> FAEx StdEx --> Ratio FAEx --> Ratio style StdEx fill:#FFB6C1 style FAEx fill:#90EE90 style Ratio fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Why Is It Also Faster?
steps.explanation: FlashAttention isn't just memory-efficient—it's **faster** than standard attention despite doing more FLOPs!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Attention I/O" IO1["1. Read Q, K from HBM"] IO2["2. Write S (N×N) to HBM"] IO3["3. Read S from HBM"] IO4["4. Write P to HBM"] IO5["5. Read P, V from HBM"] IO6["6. Write O to HBM"] IOT["Many HBM round-trips!"] end subgraph "FlashAttention I/O" FA1["1. Read Q, K, V from HBM"] FA2["2. All computation in SRAM"] FA3["3. Write O to HBM"] FAT["Minimal HBM access!"] end subgraph "The Key" Key["Modern GPUs are memory-bound
HBM bandwidth: 3 TB/s
Compute: 1000+ TFLOPS

Reducing I/O > reducing FLOPs"] end IO1 --> IO2 --> IO3 --> IO4 --> IO5 --> IO6 --> IOT FA1 --> FA2 --> FA3 --> FAT style IOT fill:#FFB6C1 style FAT fill:#90EE90 style Key fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Kernel Fusion
steps.explanation: FlashAttention fuses multiple operations into a single GPU kernel, eliminating intermediate memory writes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard: Multiple Kernels" K1["Kernel 1: S = Q @ K.T"] K2["Write S to HBM"] K3["Kernel 2: S = S / sqrt(d)"] K4["Write S to HBM"] K5["Kernel 3: P = softmax(S)"] K6["Write P to HBM"] K7["Kernel 4: O = P @ V"] end subgraph "FlashAttention: One Kernel" FK["Single fused kernel:
• Matmul Q @ K.T
• Scale by sqrt(d)
• Online softmax
• Matmul with V
• Accumulate output

All in SRAM!"] end K1 --> K2 --> K3 --> K4 --> K5 --> K6 --> K7 K1 -.->|"fuse"| FK style K2 fill:#FFB6C1 style K4 fill:#FFB6C1 style K6 fill:#FFB6C1 style FK fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Backward Pass
steps.explanation: FlashAttention also optimizes the backward pass using **recomputation** instead of storing the attention matrix.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Backward" StdB["Need attention matrix P
to compute gradients

Either store P (O(N²) memory)
Or recompute naively (slow)"] end subgraph "FlashAttention Backward" FAB1["Store only: Q, K, V, O, logsumexp"] FAB2["Recompute P in tiles during backward"] FAB3["Same tiling strategy as forward"] FAB4["Still O(N) memory!"] end subgraph "Checkpointing" Ckpt["Standard gradient checkpointing:
Recompute forward to get P

FlashAttention:
Efficient tiled recomputation
built into backward kernel"] end StdB --> FAB1 --> FAB2 --> FAB3 --> FAB4 style StdB fill:#FFB6C1 style FAB4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: FlashAttention-2 Improvements
steps.explanation: FlashAttention-2 introduced additional optimizations for even better performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "FlashAttention-1" FA1_1["Good parallelism across batch, heads"] FA1_2["Sequential over sequence length"] end subgraph "FlashAttention-2" FA2_1["Parallelize over sequence length too"] FA2_2["Better work partitioning"] FA2_3["Reduced non-matmul FLOPs"] FA2_4["~2x faster than FA-1"] end subgraph "Key Changes" KC1["Inner loop over K,V (not Q)"] KC2["Better thread block sizing"] KC3["Warp specialization"] end subgraph "Performance" Perf["FA-2 achieves 50-73% of
theoretical max FLOPS
(vs ~25-40% for standard)"] end FA1_1 --> FA2_1 FA1_2 --> FA2_1 FA2_1 --> FA2_4 style FA2_4 fill:#90EE90 style Perf fill:#FFFACD

Up to 2x faster than FA-2
FP8: 1.2 PFLOPS attention!"] end H1 --> I1 H2 --> I1 H3 --> I1 H4 --> I3 I1 --> Perf style Perf fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use FlashAttention
steps.explanation: FlashAttention is beneficial in most scenarios but has some considerations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use FlashAttention When" Use1["✓ Training transformers"] Use2["✓ Long sequence lengths"] Use3["✓ Memory is a bottleneck"] Use4["✓ Using PyTorch 2.0+"] end subgraph "Considerations" C1["Short sequences: Less benefit

FlashAttention-2: Faster
FlashAttention-3: Hopper optimized

from torch.nn.functional import scaled_dot_product_attention

out = scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)

# Enable Flash specifically
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(
enable_flash=True,

enable_mem_efficient=False
):
out = scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)"] end subgraph "flash-attn Package" FA["pip install flash-attn

from flash_attn import flash_attn_func
out = flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=True)"] end subgraph "HuggingFace" HF["model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(
'model_name',
attn_implementation='flash_attention_2'
)"] end style PT fill:#90EE90 style HF fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of FlashAttention concepts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Key Ideas" K1["Tile computation: process in SRAM blocks"] K2["Online softmax: incremental computation"] K3["Kernel fusion: minimize HBM access"] K4["Recomputation: backward without storing P"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Memory: O(N²) → O(N)"] B2["Speed: 2-4x faster"] B3["Longer sequences: 16K+ tokens"] end subgraph "Usage" U1["PyTorch 2.0+: scaled_dot_product_attention"] U2["HuggingFace: attn_implementation='flash_attention_2'"] U3["Direct: flash-attn package"] end subgraph "Remember" R["FlashAttention is I/O-aware
Not just memory efficient—
it's faster because modern GPUs

fields.title: Gradient Checkpointing: Trading Compute for Memory
fields.description: Understand activation recomputation to train larger models with limited GPU memory

steps.title: The Memory Problem
steps.explanation: During training, we must store **activations** from the forward pass to compute gradients in the backward pass. For deep networks, this uses enormous memory.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Forward Pass" X["Input x"] A1["a₁ = f₁(x)"] A2["a₂ = f₂(a₁)"] A3["a₃ = f₃(a₂)"] AN["aₙ = fₙ(aₙ₋₁)"] L["Loss L"] end X --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 -->|"..."| AN --> L subgraph "Must Store for Backward" Store["x, a₁, a₂, a₃, ..., aₙ₋₁

All intermediate activations
needed to compute gradients!"] end subgraph "Memory Problem" Prob["GPT-3 (175B):
~100 GB just for activations
per batch!"] end A1 --> Store A2 --> Store A3 --> Store style Store fill:#FFB6C1 style Prob fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Why Activations Are Needed
steps.explanation: The chain rule requires activations from the forward pass to compute gradients.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Chain Rule" CR["∂L/∂W₂ = ∂L/∂a₂ × ∂a₂/∂W₂

∂a₂/∂W₂ requires a₁!
(the input to layer 2)"] end subgraph "Concrete Example" Ex["a₂ = W₂ × a₁ + b₂

∂a₂/∂W₂ = a₁

Must have stored a₁
from forward pass!"] end subgraph "Memory Per Layer" Mem["Each layer stores:
• Input activation: batch × hidden_dim
• Sometimes also: masks, norms, etc.

100 layers × batch 8 × hidden 4096
= several GB"] end style CR fill:#ADD8E6 style Ex fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The Checkpointing Idea
steps.explanation: **Gradient checkpointing** trades compute for memory: don't store all activations, **recompute** them during the backward pass.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Training" Std1["Forward: compute & STORE all aᵢ"] Std2["Backward: use stored aᵢ"] StdMem["Memory: O(n) for n layers"] end subgraph "With Checkpointing" Ck1["Forward: compute all, STORE only some"] Ck2["Backward: RECOMPUTE missing aᵢ"] CkMem["Memory: O(√n) possible!"] CkComp["Compute: ~33% more (recompute once)"] end Std1 --> Std2 --> StdMem Ck1 --> Ck2 --> CkMem Ck2 --> CkComp subgraph "Trade-off" Trade["Less memory
↓
More compute
↓
Fits larger models!"] end style StdMem fill:#FFB6C1 style CkMem fill:#90EE90 style Trade fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: How It Works
steps.explanation: Checkpointing marks certain layers as **checkpoint boundaries**. Activations are only stored at boundaries; intermediate activations are recomputed during backward.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "8 Layers, Checkpoint Every 4" F1["Layer 1"] F2["Layer 2"] F3["Layer 3"] F4["Layer 4 ✓"] F5["Layer 5"] F6["Layer 6"] F7["Layer 7"] F8["Layer 8 ✓"] end subgraph "Forward Pass" FP["Compute all layers
Store: input, a₄, a₈
Discard: a₁, a₂, a₃, a₅, a₆, a₇"] end subgraph "Backward Pass (Layer 5-8)" BP1["Need a₅, a₆, a₇ for gradients"] BP2["Recompute: forward from a₄ to a₇"] BP3["Compute gradients for layers 5-8"] BP4["Discard a₅, a₆, a₇"] end F4 --> FP F8 --> FP FP --> BP1 --> BP2 --> BP3 --> BP4 style F4 fill:#90EE90 style F8 fill:#90EE90 style BP2 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Memory vs Compute Trade-off
steps.explanation: Different checkpoint frequencies trade off memory and compute differently.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "No Checkpointing" NC["Memory: O(n)
Compute: 1x forward, 1x backward
Total: 2x"] end subgraph "Checkpoint Every Layer" CE["Memory: O(1) - just input/output
Compute: n forward passes!
Total: (n+1)x
Very slow!"] end subgraph "Checkpoint Every √n Layers" SQ["Memory: O(√n)
Compute: 1x + 1x recompute
Total: ~3x
Good balance!"] end subgraph "Comparison (32 layers)" Comp["No checkpoint: 32 activations, 2x compute
Every layer: 1 activation, 33x compute
Every 6 layers: 6 activations, 3x compute"] end style NC fill:#FFB6C1 style CE fill:#FFB6C1 style SQ fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Optimal Checkpointing
steps.explanation: For n layers, checkpointing every **√n** layers gives optimal memory-compute trade-off: O(√n) memory with ~1.5x compute overhead.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Math" Math["n layers, checkpoint every k layers

Memory: O(n/k + k)
Recompute: O(n/k × k) = O(n)

Minimize n/k + k:
Optimal k = √n"] end subgraph "Example: 36 Layers" Ex["√36 = 6
Checkpoint every 6 layers

Store: 6 checkpoints
Max segment: 6 layers
Memory: 6 + 6 = 12 units
vs 36 units without"] end subgraph "Memory Reduction" Red["36 layers: 3x reduction
100 layers: 10x reduction
1000 layers: 30x reduction"] end Math --> Ex --> Red style Red fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Selective Checkpointing
steps.explanation: Not all layers need checkpointing equally. **Attention layers** typically use more memory than FFN layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Transformer Block Memory" Attn["Self-Attention:
• Q, K, V projections
• Attention scores (N²)
• High memory!"] FFN["Feed-Forward:
• Linear projections
• Lower memory"] end subgraph "Smart Checkpointing" Smart["Checkpoint attention layers
Keep FFN activations

Or: Use FlashAttention
(built-in recomputation)"] end subgraph "HuggingFace Strategy" HF["Full: checkpoint everything
Selective: checkpoint attention only
None: no checkpointing"] end Attn --> Smart FFN --> Smart style Attn fill:#FFB6C1 style Smart fill:#90EE90

steps.title: PyTorch Implementation
steps.explanation: PyTorch provides `checkpoint` and `checkpoint_sequential` for easy gradient checkpointing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic API" Basic["from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint

def forward(self, x):
# Checkpoint a function
x = checkpoint(self.layer1, x)
x = checkpoint(self.layer2, x)
return x"] end subgraph "Sequential API" Seq["from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint_sequential

layers = nn.Sequential(layer1, layer2, ..., layerN)

# Checkpoint every 'segments' layers
output = checkpoint_sequential(layers, segments=4, input)"] end subgraph "Important" Imp["Use use_reentrant=False (PyTorch 2.0+)
Avoids subtle bugs with autograd"] end style Basic fill:#ADD8E6 style Imp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: HuggingFace Integration
steps.explanation: HuggingFace Transformers has built-in gradient checkpointing support.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Training Arguments" Args["training_args = TrainingArguments(
gradient_checkpointing=True,
...
)"] end subgraph "Model Config" Config["model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
'model_name'
)
model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()"] end subgraph "Manual Toggle" Toggle["# Enable
model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()

# Disable
model.gradient_checkpointing_disable()

# Check
model.is_gradient_checkpointing"] end style Args fill:#90EE90 style Config fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Combining with Other Techniques
steps.explanation: Gradient checkpointing works well with other memory optimization techniques.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Memory Reduction Stack" GC["Gradient Checkpointing
~3x memory reduction"] MP["Mixed Precision (FP16/BF16)
~2x memory reduction"] FA["FlashAttention
O(N) vs O(N²) attention"] ZeRO["ZeRO/FSDP
Shard optimizer states"] end subgraph "Combined Effect" Comb["All combined:
10-20x memory reduction!

Train 70B model on 8×A100
instead of 64×A100"] end subgraph "Order of Application" Order["1. Mixed Precision (free speedup)
2. FlashAttention (faster + less memory)
3. Gradient Checkpointing (if needed)
4. FSDP/ZeRO (multi-GPU)"] end GC --> Comb MP --> Comb FA --> Comb ZeRO --> Comb style Comb fill:#90EE90

steps.title: When to Use Checkpointing
steps.explanation: Gradient checkpointing has trade-offs. Use it when memory is the bottleneck.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" Use1["✓ Running out of GPU memory"] Use2["✓ Want larger batch sizes"] Use3["✓ Training very deep models"] Use4["✓ Can tolerate slower training"] end subgraph "Avoid When" Avoid1["✗ Memory is not the bottleneck"] Avoid2["✗ Training speed is critical"] Avoid3["✗ Can use more GPUs instead"] end subgraph "Typical Slowdown" Slow["~20-35% slower training
But enables training
that otherwise wouldn't fit!"] end subgraph "Decision" Dec["OOM with batch=1?
→ Must use checkpointing

OOM with batch=8?
→ Checkpoint or reduce batch"] end style Use1 fill:#90EE90 style Slow fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Debugging Tips
steps.explanation: Common issues and solutions when using gradient checkpointing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Issue: NaN Gradients" NaN["Sometimes see NaN with checkpointing

Fix: Use use_reentrant=False
checkpoint(fn, x, use_reentrant=False)"] end subgraph "Issue: Different Results" Diff["Results differ with/without checkpointing

Cause: Dropout uses different random state
Fix: deterministic=True or seed carefully"] end subgraph "Issue: Slow Performance" Slow["Checkpointing slower than expected

Check: Are you recomputing expensive ops?
Try: Selective checkpointing"] end subgraph "Best Practice" BP["# PyTorch 2.0+ recommended
checkpoint(fn, x, use_reentrant=False)"] end style NaN fill:#FFB6C1 style BP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Advanced: CPU Offloading
steps.explanation: Extreme memory savings: offload activations to CPU RAM, reload during backward.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Activation Offloading" Off["Store checkpointed activations on CPU
Move back to GPU for backward

Even more memory savings
But much slower (PCIe bandwidth)"] end subgraph "When Useful" When["• Single GPU, must fit huge model
• Very long sequences
• Willing to trade speed for capability"] end subgraph "DeepSpeed" DS["DeepSpeed activation checkpointing:

ds_config = {
'activation_checkpointing': {
'partition_activations': True,
'cpu_checkpointing': True,
'contiguous_memory_optimization': True
}
}"] end Off --> When When --> DS style Off fill:#ADD8E6 style DS fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of gradient checkpointing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Key Concept" KC["Don't store all activations
Recompute during backward
Trade compute for memory"] end subgraph "Memory Savings" MS["Checkpoint every √n layers:
O(n) → O(√n) memory
~30% compute overhead"] end subgraph "PyTorch Code" Code["from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint
output = checkpoint(layer, input, use_reentrant=False)"] end subgraph "When to Use" When["OOM errors
Want larger batch
Training deep models"] end subgraph "Combine With" Comb["Mixed precision
FlashAttention
FSDP/ZeRO"] end style KC fill:#ADD8E6 style MS fill:#90EE90 style Code fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Gradient Descent Variants: SGD to Adam
fields.description: Understand batch, mini-batch, SGD, momentum, RMSprop, and Adam optimizers

steps.title: What is Gradient Descent?
steps.explanation:
**Gradient descent** finds the minimum of a function by iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent (negative gradient).

`θ = θ - α × ∇L(θ)`

where α is the learning rate and ∇L is the gradient of the loss.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Intuition" Ball["Imagine a ball rolling downhill
It follows the steepest path
Eventually settles at the bottom"] end subgraph "Update Rule" Rule["θ_new = θ_old - learning_rate × gradient

• Gradient points uphill
• Negative gradient points downhill
• Learning rate controls step size"] end subgraph "Goal" Goal["Find θ that minimizes Loss(θ)"] end style Rule fill:#ADD8E6 style Goal fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Batch Gradient Descent
steps.explanation: **Batch GD** computes the gradient using the **entire dataset** before each update. Accurate but slow for large datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Batch Gradient Descent" Data["Full Dataset
N samples"] Grad["Compute gradient over ALL N samples
∇L = (1/N) Σ ∇L_i"] Update["Single weight update"] end Data --> Grad --> Update subgraph "Pros" P1["✓ Stable convergence"] P2["✓ Guaranteed to find minimum (convex)"] end subgraph "Cons" C1["✗ Very slow for large datasets"] C2["✗ Requires all data in memory"] C3["✗ Can't escape local minima"] end style Grad fill:#ADD8E6 style C1 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
steps.explanation: **SGD** updates weights after each **single sample**. Fast but noisy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Stochastic GD" Sample["One sample"] Grad["Compute gradient for 1 sample
∇L = ∇L_i"] Update["Weight update"] Loop["Repeat for each sample"] end Sample --> Grad --> Update --> Loop --> Sample subgraph "Pros" P1["✓ Fast updates"] P2["✓ Can escape local minima (noise helps)"] P3["✓ Online learning possible"] end subgraph "Cons" C1["✗ Very noisy gradients"] C2["✗ May not converge smoothly"] C3["✗ Can't leverage vectorization"] end style Grad fill:#90EE90 style C1 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent
steps.explanation: **Mini-batch GD** is the practical middle ground: compute gradient on a **small batch** (32-512 samples). Best of both worlds!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mini-Batch GD" Batch["Mini-batch
32-512 samples"] Grad["Gradient over batch
∇L = (1/B) Σ ∇L_i"] Update["Weight update"] end Batch --> Grad --> Update subgraph "Why It Works" W1["Batch size 32-512 typical"] W2["Leverages GPU parallelism"] W3["Noise helps generalization"] W4["Stable enough to converge"] end subgraph "This is what everyone uses!" Use["When people say 'SGD' in deep learning
they usually mean mini-batch GD"] end style Use fill:#90EE90 style Grad fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: The Problem with Vanilla SGD
steps.explanation:
Vanilla SGD struggles with:
- Ravines (different curvature in different directions)
- Saddle points
- Choosing the right learning rate
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Ravine Problem" Rav["Loss surface is steep in one direction
flat in another

SGD oscillates across ravine
while making slow progress along it"] end subgraph "Learning Rate Dilemma" LR["Too high: Overshoots, diverges
Too low: Very slow convergence

Different params may need different LRs!"] end subgraph "Solutions" Sol["1. Momentum: Smooth out oscillations
2. Adaptive LR: Per-parameter rates
3. Both: Adam!"] end Rav --> Sol LR --> Sol style Rav fill:#FFB6C1 style Sol fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Momentum
steps.explanation: **Momentum** adds a velocity term that accumulates past gradients. Like a ball rolling downhill, it builds up speed in consistent directions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Momentum Update" V["v = β × v + ∇L(θ)

θ = θ - α × v"] Explain["v: velocity (accumulated gradient)
β: momentum coefficient (typically 0.9)
α: learning rate"] end subgraph "Effect" E1["Accelerates in consistent direction"] E2["Dampens oscillations"] E3["Helps escape local minima"] end subgraph "Visualization" Vis["Without momentum: zig-zag path
With momentum: smoother, faster path"] end V --> Explain style V fill:#90EE90 style Vis fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: RMSprop
steps.explanation: **RMSprop** adapts the learning rate for each parameter based on the magnitude of recent gradients.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "RMSprop Update" S["s = β × s + (1-β) × (∇L)²

θ = θ - α × ∇L / √(s + ε)"] Explain["s: running average of squared gradients
β: decay rate (typically 0.9)
ε: small constant for stability"] end subgraph "Effect" E1["Large gradients → smaller effective LR"] E2["Small gradients → larger effective LR"] E3["Adapts to each parameter's scale"] end subgraph "Key Insight" Key["Dividing by √s normalizes the update
Parameters with large gradients
get smaller updates (and vice versa)"] end style S fill:#90EE90 style Key fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Adam: The Default Choice
steps.explanation: **Adam** (Adaptive Moment Estimation) combines momentum AND adaptive learning rates. It's the most popular optimizer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Adam = Momentum + RMSprop" M["m = β₁ × m + (1-β₁) × ∇L
(momentum term)"] V["v = β₂ × v + (1-β₂) × (∇L)²
(RMSprop term)"] Update["θ = θ - α × m̂ / √(v̂ + ε)"] end subgraph "Bias Correction" Bias["m̂ = m / (1 - β₁ᵗ)
v̂ = v / (1 - β₂ᵗ)

Corrects initialization bias"] end subgraph "Default Hyperparameters" HP["β₁ = 0.9
β₂ = 0.999
ε = 1e-8
α = 0.001 (or 3e-4 for transformers)"] end M --> Update V --> Update style Update fill:#90EE90 style HP fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: AdamW: Adam with Decoupled Weight Decay
steps.explanation: **AdamW** fixes a subtle bug in Adam's weight decay. It's now the standard for training transformers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" Prob["Adam + L2 regularization
doesn't work as intended

L2 penalty gets scaled by
adaptive learning rate"] end subgraph "AdamW Solution" Sol["Decouple weight decay from gradient

θ = θ - α × (m̂/√(v̂+ε) + λ×θ)

Weight decay applied directly,
not through gradient"] end subgraph "In Practice" Prac["Use AdamW for transformers
Default in HuggingFace, PyTorch"] end Prob --> Sol --> Prac style Sol fill:#90EE90 style Prac fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Learning Rate Selection
steps.explanation: The learning rate is the most important hyperparameter. Too high = divergence, too low = slow training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Learning Rate Effects" High["Too High (0.1+)
Loss explodes, NaN"] Good["Just Right
Smooth decrease"] Low["Too Low (1e-6)
Barely moves"] end subgraph "Finding Good LR" LRF["LR Range Test:
1. Start with tiny LR
2. Increase exponentially
3. Plot loss vs LR
4. Pick LR where loss drops fastest"] end subgraph "Typical Values" Typ["SGD: 0.01 - 0.1
Adam: 0.001 - 0.0001
Fine-tuning: 1e-5 - 5e-5"] end style Good fill:#90EE90 style High fill:#FFB6C1 style Low fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Optimizer Comparison
steps.explanation: When to use which optimizer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "SGD + Momentum" SGD["✓ Often better final accuracy
✓ Better generalization
✗ Requires careful LR tuning
✗ Slower convergence

Use for: CNNs, final fine-tuning"] end subgraph "Adam/AdamW" Adam["✓ Fast convergence
✓ Works with default HPs
✓ Handles sparse gradients
✗ May generalize worse

Use for: Transformers, RNNs, prototyping"] end subgraph "Quick Guide" Guide["Starting out? → Adam
Transformers? → AdamW
CNNs, want best accuracy? → SGD+Momentum
Unsure? → Adam, then try SGD"] end style Adam fill:#90EE90 style Guide fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Cross-Validation: Reliable Model Evaluation
fields.description: Master K-fold, stratified, time series CV, and train/val/test splits

steps.title: Why Cross-Validation?
steps.explanation: A single train/test split can give misleading results. **Cross-validation** gives a more reliable estimate of model performance by testing on multiple different splits.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "The Problem"
        P1["Single split might be lucky/unlucky"]
        P2["Small test set = high variance"]
        P3["Wastes data for training"]
    end
    subgraph "The Solution"
        S1["Multiple train/test splits"]
        S2["Average performance across splits"]
        S3["More reliable estimate"]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style S2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Train/Validation/Test Split
steps.explanation: The fundamental data split for ML: **Train** to learn, **Validation** to tune hyperparameters, **Test** for final evaluation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Data Split" Data["Full Dataset"] Train["Training Set
60-80%"] Val["Validation Set
10-20%"] Test["Test Set
10-20%"] end Data --> Train Data --> Val Data --> Test subgraph "Purpose" TrainP["Train: Fit model parameters"] ValP["Val: Tune hyperparameters"] TestP["Test: Final unbiased evaluation
Only use ONCE!"] end style Train fill:#90EE90 style Val fill:#ADD8E6 style Test fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: K-Fold Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: **K-Fold CV** splits data into K parts. Each part takes a turn as the validation set while the rest are used for training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "5-Fold CV" F1["Fold 1: [VAL][Train][Train][Train][Train]"] F2["Fold 2: [Train][VAL][Train][Train][Train]"] F3["Fold 3: [Train][Train][VAL][Train][Train]"] F4["Fold 4: [Train][Train][Train][VAL][Train]"] F5["Fold 5: [Train][Train][Train][Train][VAL]"] end subgraph "Result" R["5 scores → Average ± Std
e.g., Accuracy: 0.85 ± 0.02"] end F1 --> R F2 --> R F3 --> R F4 --> R F5 --> R style R fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing K
steps.explanation: Common choices for K and their tradeoffs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "K = 5" K5["Most common default
Good balance
5 model trainings"] end subgraph "K = 10" K10["Lower variance
More compute
Good for smaller datasets"] end subgraph "K = N (Leave-One-Out)" LOO["N model trainings
Lowest bias, highest variance
Very expensive"] end subgraph "Recommendation" Rec["K=5: Large datasets, expensive models
K=10: Medium datasets
LOO: Very small datasets only"] end style K5 fill:#90EE90 style Rec fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Stratified K-Fold
steps.explanation: **Stratified K-Fold** maintains the class distribution in each fold. Essential for imbalanced datasets!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Imbalanced Data" Data["90% Class A, 10% Class B"] end subgraph "Regular K-Fold Problem" Prob["Some folds might have 0% Class B!
Bad validation estimate"] end subgraph "Stratified K-Fold" Strat["Each fold has ~90% A, ~10% B
Preserves class proportions"] end Data --> Prob Data --> Strat subgraph "Code" Code["from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5)
for train_idx, val_idx in skf.split(X, y):"] end style Prob fill:#FFB6C1 style Strat fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Time Series CV
steps.explanation: For time series data, you can't randomly shuffle! Use **expanding window** or **sliding window** CV.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Why Different?" Why["Can't use future data to predict past!
Random splits would leak information"] end subgraph "Expanding Window" E1["[Train ] [Val]"] E2["[Train ] [Val]"] E3["[Train ] [Val]"] end subgraph "Sliding Window" S1["[Train] [Val]"] S2[" [Train] [Val]"] S3[" [Train] [Val]"] end subgraph "Code" Code["from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)"] end style Why fill:#FFB6C1 style E3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Nested Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: **Nested CV** uses an outer loop for evaluation and inner loop for hyperparameter tuning. Gives unbiased performance estimate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Outer Loop (Evaluation)" O1["Fold 1: Train(2,3,4,5) → Test(1)"] O2["Fold 2: Train(1,3,4,5) → Test(2)"] O3["..."] end subgraph "Inner Loop (HP Tuning)" I1["For each outer train set:
Run K-fold CV to find best HPs"] end subgraph "Process" P1["1. Outer split"] P2["2. Inner CV to tune HPs"] P3["3. Train with best HPs"] P4["4. Evaluate on outer test"] P5["5. Repeat for each outer fold"] end O1 --> I1 P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 style I1 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Group K-Fold
steps.explanation: When samples belong to groups (e.g., multiple samples per patient), ensure all samples from a group stay together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem: Data Leakage" Prob["Patient 1 in train AND test
Model memorizes patient-specific patterns
Overly optimistic performance"] end subgraph "Solution: GroupKFold" Sol["All samples from Patient 1
go to same fold
No leakage between train/test"] end subgraph "Code" Code["from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold
gkf = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)
for train_idx, val_idx in gkf.split(X, y, groups=patient_ids):"] end Prob --> Sol style Prob fill:#FFB6C1 style Sol fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Common Mistakes
steps.explanation: Avoid these cross-validation pitfalls.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mistake 1: Data Leakage" M1["Preprocessing before splitting
e.g., StandardScaler on full data"] F1["Fix: Fit scaler on train only"] end subgraph "Mistake 2: Ignoring Groups" M2["Related samples in train and test"] F2["Fix: Use GroupKFold"] end subgraph "Mistake 3: Wrong CV for Time Series" M3["Random shuffle with temporal data"] F3["Fix: Use TimeSeriesSplit"] end subgraph "Mistake 4: HP Tuning on Test" M4["Using test set to select hyperparameters"] F4["Fix: Use validation set or nested CV"] end style M1 fill:#FFB6C1 style M2 fill:#FFB6C1 style F1 fill:#90EE90 style F2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of cross-validation strategies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Choose Your CV" C1["Classification → StratifiedKFold"] C2["Regression → KFold"] C3["Time Series → TimeSeriesSplit"] C4["Grouped Data → GroupKFold"] C5["HP Tuning + Eval → Nested CV"] end subgraph "sklearn Code" Code["from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5)
print(f'{scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}')"] end subgraph "Best Practices" BP["Always use CV for model selection
Report mean ± std
Keep test set completely separate"] end style C1 fill:#90EE90 style Code fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Regularization: Preventing Overfitting
fields.description: Master L1, L2, Elastic Net, dropout, early stopping, and weight decay

steps.title: What is Overfitting?
steps.explanation: **Overfitting** occurs when a model learns the training data too well, including noise, and fails to generalize to new data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Underfitting" U["Too simple
High bias
Poor train & test"] end subgraph "Good Fit" G["Just right
Captures pattern
Good generalization"] end subgraph "Overfitting" O["Too complex
High variance
Great train, poor test"] end U --> G --> O subgraph "Signs of Overfitting" S1["Train accuracy: 99%"] S2["Test accuracy: 70%"] S3["Large gap = overfitting"] end style G fill:#90EE90 style O fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: How Regularization Helps
steps.explanation: **Regularization** adds a penalty term to the loss function that discourages complex models, pushing towards simpler solutions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original Loss" Orig["Loss = Prediction Error"] end subgraph "Regularized Loss" Reg["Loss = Prediction Error + λ × Penalty(weights)"] end subgraph "Effect" E1["Penalty increases with model complexity"] E2["Model trades some training accuracy"] E3["For better generalization"] end Orig --> Reg Reg --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 subgraph "λ (Lambda)" L["Controls regularization strength
Higher λ = more regularization"] end style Reg fill:#90EE90

steps.title: L2 Regularization (Ridge)
steps.explanation: **L2** adds the sum of squared weights to the loss. It shrinks weights toward zero but rarely makes them exactly zero.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "L2 Penalty" L2["Penalty = λ × Σ wᵢ²

Loss = MSE + λ × Σ wᵢ²"] end subgraph "Effect on Weights" E["Large weights → large penalty
Shrinks all weights toward zero
But keeps all features"] end subgraph "When to Use" Use["• Many correlated features
• Want to keep all features
• Default choice for neural nets"] end subgraph "Code" Code["sklearn: Ridge(alpha=1.0)
PyTorch: weight_decay in optimizer"] end style L2 fill:#ADD8E6 style Use fill:#90EE90

steps.title: L1 Regularization (Lasso)
steps.explanation: **L1** adds the sum of absolute weights. It can shrink weights to exactly zero, performing feature selection!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "L1 Penalty" L1["Penalty = λ × Σ |wᵢ|

Loss = MSE + λ × Σ |wᵢ|"] end subgraph "Effect on Weights" E["Pushes small weights to ZERO
Sparse solutions
Automatic feature selection"] end subgraph "When to Use" Use["• Want feature selection
• Expect many irrelevant features
• Need interpretable model"] end subgraph "L1 vs L2" Comp["L1: Sparse, feature selection
L2: Dense, all features kept"] end style L1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Comp fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Elastic Net
steps.explanation: **Elastic Net** combines L1 and L2 regularization. Best of both worlds!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Elastic Net" EN["Penalty = α×λ×Σ|wᵢ| + (1-α)×λ×Σwᵢ²

α: Mix ratio (0=Ridge, 1=Lasso)"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["Handles correlated features (like L2)"] A2["Can select features (like L1)"] A3["More stable than pure L1"] end subgraph "When to Use" Use["• Many features, some correlated
• Want some feature selection
• Lasso is unstable"] end subgraph "Code" Code["from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNet
model = ElasticNet(alpha=1.0, l1_ratio=0.5)"] end style EN fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Dropout
steps.explanation: **Dropout** randomly sets neurons to zero during training. It prevents co-adaptation and acts like training an ensemble.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "During Training" Train["Randomly drop p% of neurons
Each forward pass uses different subset
Typical p = 0.1 to 0.5"] end subgraph "During Inference" Inf["Use ALL neurons
Scale outputs by (1-p)
Or scale during training"] end subgraph "Why It Works" Why["Prevents co-adaptation
Each neuron must be useful alone
Like training many networks"] end subgraph "Code" Code["nn.Dropout(p=0.5)

model.train() # dropout active
model.eval() # dropout disabled"] end style Train fill:#ADD8E6 style Code fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Early Stopping
steps.explanation: **Early stopping** monitors validation loss and stops training when it starts increasing. Simple and effective!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Training Curves" TC["Train loss: keeps decreasing
Val loss: decreases, then INCREASES

Stop when val loss increases!"] end subgraph "Implementation" Impl["Track best val loss
Count epochs without improvement
Stop after patience epochs"] end subgraph "Parameters" Params["patience: epochs to wait (5-10)
min_delta: minimum improvement
restore_best_weights: True"] end subgraph "Code" Code["callback = EarlyStopping(
monitor=val_loss,
patience=5,
restore_best_weights=True
)"] end style TC fill:#FFFACD style Code fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Data Augmentation
steps.explanation: **Data augmentation** creates new training samples by transforming existing ones. More data = less overfitting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Image Augmentation" Img["Flip, rotate, crop, zoom
Color jitter, blur
Cutout, mixup"] end subgraph "Text Augmentation" Txt["Synonym replacement
Back translation
Random insertion/deletion"] end subgraph "Why It Works" Why["More training data
Model sees more variations
Better generalization"] end subgraph "Code" Code["transforms.Compose([
transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
transforms.RandomRotation(10),
transforms.ColorJitter()
])"] end style Img fill:#ADD8E6 style Why fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Batch Normalization as Regularization
steps.explanation: **Batch Normalization** normalizes layer inputs. It has a slight regularization effect due to noise in batch statistics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BatchNorm" BN["Normalize: x̂ = (x - μ_batch) / σ_batch
Scale & shift: y = γx̂ + β"] end subgraph "Regularization Effect" Reg["Batch statistics add noise
Similar effect to dropout
Can often reduce/remove dropout"] end subgraph "Note" Note["Primary purpose: faster training
Regularization is a side effect
Use with small batch sizes carefully"] end style BN fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Comparison & When to Use
steps.explanation: Choose regularization based on your model and problem.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Linear Models" Lin["L2 (Ridge): Default
L1 (Lasso): Feature selection
Elastic Net: Correlated features"] end subgraph "Neural Networks" NN["Weight decay (L2): Always
Dropout: MLPs, attention
Early stopping: Always
Data augmentation: Images, NLP"] end subgraph "Trees" Tree["Max depth, min samples
Pruning
Ensemble methods"] end subgraph "Quick Guide" Guide["Start with L2 + Early stopping
Add dropout for deep nets
Use augmentation for images"] end style Guide fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Classification Metrics: Beyond Accuracy
fields.description: Master precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, confusion matrix, and PR curves

steps.title: Why Accuracy Isn't Enough
steps.explanation: **Accuracy** can be misleading, especially with imbalanced classes. A model predicting 'no fraud' 99% of the time has 99% accuracy but catches 0 fraudsters!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Imbalanced Data" Data["1000 samples
990 negative, 10 positive"] end subgraph "Naive Model" Model["Always predict NEGATIVE
Accuracy = 99%!"] end subgraph "Reality" Real["Catches 0 positives
Useless for fraud detection"] end Data --> Model --> Real style Model fill:#FFB6C1 style Real fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Confusion Matrix
steps.explanation: The **confusion matrix** shows all prediction outcomes: true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Confusion Matrix" CM[" Predicted
Neg Pos
Actual Neg [ TN | FP ]
Actual Pos [ FN | TP ]"] end subgraph "Definitions" TP["TP: Correctly predicted positive"] TN["TN: Correctly predicted negative"] FP["FP: Incorrectly predicted positive (Type I)"] FN["FN: Incorrectly predicted negative (Type II)"] end style TP fill:#90EE90 style TN fill:#90EE90 style FP fill:#FFB6C1 style FN fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Precision
steps.explanation:
**Precision** answers: Of all positive predictions, how many were correct?

`Precision = TP / (TP + FP)`
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Precision" P["Precision = TP / (TP + FP)

Of things I called positive,
how many actually were?"] end subgraph "Example" Ex["Model predicts 100 spam emails
80 were actually spam
Precision = 80/100 = 80%"] end subgraph "When It Matters" When["High cost of false positives
• Spam filter (don't lose real emails)
• Recommender (don't annoy users)"] end style P fill:#ADD8E6 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Recall (Sensitivity)
steps.explanation:
**Recall** answers: Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?

`Recall = TP / (TP + FN)`
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Recall" R["Recall = TP / (TP + FN)

Of all actual positives,
how many did I find?"] end subgraph "Example" Ex["100 actual fraud cases
Model caught 70
Recall = 70/100 = 70%"] end subgraph "When It Matters" When["High cost of false negatives
• Fraud detection (catch all fraud)
• Disease screening (don't miss cases)"] end style R fill:#ADD8E6 style When fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Precision vs Recall Trade-off
steps.explanation: There's an inherent trade-off: increasing precision typically decreases recall and vice versa.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "High Threshold" HT["Only predict positive when very confident
High precision, low recall
Few false positives, many false negatives"] end subgraph "Low Threshold" LT["Predict positive more liberally
Low precision, high recall
