Many false positives, few false negatives"] end subgraph "Trade-off" Trade["Can't maximize both!
Choose based on business needs"] end HT --> Trade LT --> Trade style Trade fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: F1 Score
steps.explanation: **F1 Score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. Use when you need a single balanced metric.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "F1 Formula" F1["F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall)

Harmonic mean penalizes extremes"] end subgraph "Example" Ex["Precision = 80%, Recall = 60%
F1 = 2 × 0.8 × 0.6 / (0.8 + 0.6)
F1 = 0.685 or 68.5%"] end subgraph "When to Use" When["• Imbalanced classes
• Care about both precision and recall
• Need single number for comparison"] end style F1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: ROC Curve
steps.explanation: The **ROC curve** plots True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at different thresholds.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Axes" Y["Y-axis: TPR = TP/(TP+FN) = Recall"] X["X-axis: FPR = FP/(FP+TN)"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I1["Top-left corner: Perfect classifier"] I2["Diagonal: Random guessing"] I3["Curve above diagonal: Better than random"] end subgraph "AUC" AUC["Area Under Curve (AUC)
1.0 = Perfect
0.5 = Random
< 0.5 = Worse than random"] end style AUC fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: PR Curve
steps.explanation: The **Precision-Recall curve** is better than ROC for imbalanced datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "PR Curve" PR["Y-axis: Precision
X-axis: Recall
Plots P vs R at different thresholds"] end subgraph "When to Use" When["Imbalanced data where
negative class is much larger

ROC can be misleading
PR focuses on positive class"] end subgraph "Interpretation" Int["Top-right corner: Perfect
Higher curve = better model
AP (Average Precision) = AUC of PR curve"] end style When fill:#FFFACD style Int fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Multi-class Metrics
steps.explanation: For multi-class problems, metrics are computed per-class then averaged.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Averaging Methods" Macro["Macro: Average per-class metrics
Treats all classes equally"] Micro["Micro: Aggregate TP/FP/FN, then compute
Weights by class frequency"] Weight["Weighted: Macro weighted by support
Accounts for class imbalance"] end subgraph "When to Use" M_Use["Macro: All classes equally important"] Mi_Use["Micro: Overall performance matters"] W_Use["Weighted: Imbalanced classes"] end subgraph "Code" Code["f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='macro')
f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='weighted')"] end style Weight fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Choosing the Right Metric
steps.explanation: Choose metrics based on your problem and business needs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Balanced Classes" Bal["Accuracy often fine
F1 for safety"] end subgraph "Imbalanced Classes" Imb["ROC-AUC or PR-AUC
F1, Precision, Recall"] end subgraph "Business Priority" FP_Cost["FP costly → Precision
(spam: don't lose real mail)"] FN_Cost["FN costly → Recall
(fraud: catch all fraud)"] Both["Both matter → F1"] end subgraph "Quick Guide" Guide["Balanced → Accuracy or F1
Imbalanced → PR-AUC or F1
Ranking → ROC-AUC
Always: Look at confusion matrix"] end style Guide fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Ensemble Methods: Bagging, Boosting, Stacking
fields.description: Combine multiple models for better predictions than any single model

steps.title: Why Ensemble?
steps.explanation: **Ensemble methods** combine multiple models to get better predictions. The wisdom of crowds often beats any individual expert!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Model" SM["One model
One prediction
One set of errors"] end subgraph "Ensemble" E1["Model 1"] E2["Model 2"] E3["Model 3"] Combine["Combine predictions
Errors cancel out"] end E1 --> Combine E2 --> Combine E3 --> Combine subgraph "Benefits" B1["Lower variance"] B2["Better generalization"] B3["More robust"] end style Combine fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Three Main Approaches
steps.explanation: Ensembles differ in how they train models and combine predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bagging" Bag["Train models in PARALLEL
On different data samples
Average predictions"] end subgraph "Boosting" Boost["Train models SEQUENTIALLY
Each fixes previous errors
Weighted combination"] end subgraph "Stacking" Stack["Train diverse models
Use another model to combine
Learn optimal weights"] end subgraph "Key Difference" Diff["Bagging: Reduce variance
Boosting: Reduce bias
Stacking: Best of both"] end style Bag fill:#ADD8E6 style Boost fill:#90EE90 style Stack fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)
steps.explanation: **Bagging** trains each model on a bootstrap sample (random sample with replacement) of the training data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Training Data" Data["N samples"] end subgraph "Bootstrap Samples" B1["Sample 1: N samples
(with replacement)"] B2["Sample 2: N samples"] B3["Sample 3: N samples"] end Data --> B1 Data --> B2 Data --> B3 subgraph "Train Models" M1["Model 1"] M2["Model 2"] M3["Model 3"] end B1 --> M1 B2 --> M2 B3 --> M3 subgraph "Combine" Avg["Classification: Vote
Regression: Average"] end M1 --> Avg M2 --> Avg M3 --> Avg style Avg fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Random Forest
steps.explanation: **Random Forest** = Bagging + Random feature selection. Each tree sees different samples AND different features.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Random Forest" RF["1. Bootstrap sample of rows
2. Random subset of features
3. Build decision tree
4. Repeat for many trees
5. Average/vote predictions"] end subgraph "Why Random Features?" Why["Decorrelates trees
Different trees make different errors
Better ensemble effect"] end subgraph "Hyperparameters" HP["n_estimators: 100-1000 trees
max_features: sqrt(n) for clf
max_depth: None or limit"] end subgraph "Strengths" Str["Robust, hard to overfit
Handles missing values
Feature importance built-in"] end style RF fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Boosting Overview
steps.explanation: **Boosting** trains models sequentially, each focusing on mistakes of previous models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Boosting Process" M1["Model 1: Train on data"] E1["Find errors"] W1["Increase weight of misclassified"] M2["Model 2: Focus on errors"] E2["Find new errors"] M3["Model 3: Focus on remaining errors"] end M1 --> E1 --> W1 --> M2 --> E2 --> M3 subgraph "Final Prediction" Final["Weighted sum of all models
Better models get higher weight"] end M1 --> Final M2 --> Final M3 --> Final style Final fill:#90EE90

steps.title: AdaBoost
steps.explanation: **AdaBoost** adjusts sample weights to focus on hard examples.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "AdaBoost Algorithm" S1["1. Initialize equal sample weights"] S2["2. Train weak learner (e.g., stump)"] S3["3. Calculate learner weight (α)"] S4["4. Update sample weights
Increase for misclassified"] S5["5. Repeat T times"] S6["6. Final: Σ αₜ × hₜ(x)"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 subgraph "Key Idea" Key["Hard examples get more attention
Each learner complements previous"] end style S4 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Gradient Boosting
steps.explanation: **Gradient Boosting** fits each new model to the residual errors (gradients) of the previous ensemble.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Gradient Boosting" S1["1. Start with simple prediction (mean)"] S2["2. Compute residuals: y - ŷ"] S3["3. Fit tree to residuals"] S4["4. Update: ŷ = ŷ + lr × tree"] S5["5. Repeat"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 subgraph "Implementations" Impl["XGBoost: Fast, regularized
LightGBM: Faster, leaf-wise
CatBoost: Handles categoricals"] end subgraph "Why Gradient?" Why["Residuals = negative gradient of MSE
Works for any loss function"] end style Impl fill:#90EE90

steps.title: XGBoost/LightGBM
steps.explanation: **XGBoost** and **LightGBM** are highly optimized gradient boosting libraries that dominate tabular data competitions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "XGBoost Features" X1["L1/L2 regularization"] X2["Parallel tree construction"] X3["Built-in cross-validation"] X4["Handles missing values"] end subgraph "LightGBM Features" L1["Faster training (leaf-wise)"] L2["Lower memory usage"] L3["Native categorical support"] L4["GOSS: Gradient-based sampling"] end subgraph "Key Hyperparameters" HP["learning_rate: 0.01-0.1
n_estimators: 100-1000
max_depth: 3-10
min_child_weight/samples"] end style X1 fill:#ADD8E6 style L1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Stacking
steps.explanation: **Stacking** trains a meta-model to optimally combine base model predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Level 0: Base Models" B1["Random Forest"] B2["XGBoost"] B3["Neural Net"] B4["SVM"] end subgraph "Generate Meta-Features" Meta["Use cross-validation predictions
as features for meta-model"] end B1 --> Meta B2 --> Meta B3 --> Meta B4 --> Meta subgraph "Level 1: Meta-Model" M1["Logistic Regression
or another model"] Final["Final Prediction"] end Meta --> M1 --> Final style M1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Bagging vs Boosting
steps.explanation: When to use which ensemble approach.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bagging (Random Forest)" BagWhen["• High variance models (trees)
• Overfitting is a concern
• Parallel training needed
• Robust, less tuning"] end subgraph "Boosting (XGBoost)" BoostWhen["• High bias models
• Need best performance
• Careful tuning available
• Tabular data competitions"] end subgraph "Quick Guide" Guide["Start: Random Forest (robust)
Optimize: XGBoost/LightGBM
Kaggle: Stack everything"] end style BagWhen fill:#ADD8E6 style BoostWhen fill:#90EE90 style Guide fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Dimensionality Reduction: PCA, t-SNE, UMAP
fields.description: Reduce features while preserving important information for visualization and modeling

steps.title: Why Reduce Dimensions?
steps.explanation: High-dimensional data is hard to visualize, slow to process, and prone to the **curse of dimensionality**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Problems with High Dimensions"
        P1["Can't visualize > 3D"]
        P2["Slow training, high memory"]
        P3["Sparse data in high-D space"]
        P4["Many features are redundant"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Visualization: 1000D → 2D"]
        S2["Faster ML: 1000D → 50D"]
        S3["Noise reduction"]
        S4["Feature compression"]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    style S1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Two Types of Reduction
steps.explanation: Dimensionality reduction techniques fall into two categories: **linear** (PCA) and **non-linear** (t-SNE, UMAP).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Linear Methods" Lin["PCA, LDA, SVD
Project onto hyperplanes
Fast, interpretable
Good for preprocessing"] end subgraph "Non-Linear Methods" NonLin["t-SNE, UMAP, Isomap
Preserve local structure
Better visualizations
Slower, less interpretable"] end subgraph "When to Use" Use["Preprocessing/ML: PCA
Visualization: UMAP or t-SNE"] end style Lin fill:#ADD8E6 style NonLin fill:#90EE90

steps.title: PCA: Principal Component Analysis
steps.explanation: **PCA** finds directions (principal components) of maximum variance. It projects data onto these directions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "PCA Idea" Idea["1. Find direction of max variance (PC1)
2. Find perpendicular direction (PC2)
3. Continue for more PCs
4. Keep top K components"] end subgraph "Properties" P1["Linear transformation"] P2["PCs are orthogonal"] P3["Ordered by variance explained"] P4["First few PCs capture most info"] end subgraph "Example" Ex["100 features → 10 PCs
explains 95% of variance"] end style Idea fill:#ADD8E6 style Ex fill:#90EE90

steps.title: PCA: How Many Components?
steps.explanation: Choose the number of components based on **explained variance ratio**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scree Plot" Scree["Plot variance explained by each PC
Look for 'elbow' point"] end subgraph "Cumulative Variance" Cum["Sum variance until threshold
Typically keep 90-99%"] end subgraph "Code" Code["pca = PCA(n_components=0.95) # keep 95%
pca = PCA(n_components=50) # keep 50

print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)"] end subgraph "Guidelines" Guide["Visualization: 2-3
ML: keep 90-99% variance
Compression: depends on needs"] end style Cum fill:#90EE90 style Code fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: t-SNE
steps.explanation: **t-SNE** (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) preserves local neighborhoods. Great for visualization!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "t-SNE Idea" Idea["1. Compute similarities in high-D
2. Initialize random low-D points
3. Move points to match similarities
4. Optimize with gradient descent"] end subgraph "Key Parameter: Perplexity" Perp["Perplexity ≈ number of neighbors
Typical: 5-50
Try different values!"] end subgraph "Limitations" Lim["Slow for large datasets
Random initialization
Distances not meaningful
Use for visualization only"] end style Idea fill:#ADD8E6 style Lim fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: UMAP
steps.explanation: **UMAP** (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is faster than t-SNE with similar quality.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "UMAP Advantages" A1["Much faster than t-SNE"] A2["Preserves global structure better"] A3["Works for larger datasets"] A4["Can embed new points"] end subgraph "Key Parameters" Params["n_neighbors: 5-50 (local vs global)
min_dist: 0-1 (tightness of clusters)"] end subgraph "Code" Code["import umap
reducer = umap.UMAP(n_components=2)
embedding = reducer.fit_transform(X)"] end subgraph "Recommendation" Rec["Generally prefer UMAP over t-SNE
Faster, better global structure"] end style A1 fill:#90EE90 style Rec fill:#90EE90

steps.title: PCA vs t-SNE vs UMAP
steps.explanation: Comparison of the three main techniques.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "PCA" PCA["Linear
Fast
Interpretable
Global structure
Good for ML"] end subgraph "t-SNE" TSNE["Non-linear
Slow
Local structure
Great clusters
Visualization only"] end subgraph "UMAP" UMAP["Non-linear
Fast
Local + global
Great clusters
Can embed new data"] end subgraph "Choose" Choose["ML preprocessing: PCA
Visualization: UMAP
Small data viz: t-SNE or UMAP"] end style PCA fill:#ADD8E6 style UMAP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Interpreting Visualizations
steps.explanation: What you can and cannot infer from 2D projections.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Can Interpret" Can["Cluster separation
Relative cluster sizes
Local neighborhoods
Outliers"] end subgraph "Cannot Interpret (t-SNE/UMAP)" Cannot["Distances between clusters
Cluster densities
Global positions
Exact shapes"] end subgraph "Tips" Tips["Color by known labels
Try multiple runs
Vary perplexity/n_neighbors
Don't over-interpret"] end style Cannot fill:#FFB6C1 style Tips fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary of dimensionality reduction methods.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Method Selection" M1["ML preprocessing → PCA"] M2["Visualization → UMAP"] M3["Small data viz → t-SNE or UMAP"] M4["Interpretability → PCA"] end subgraph "Code Snippets" Code["# PCA
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=50)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X)

# UMAP
import umap
X_umap = umap.UMAP().fit_transform(X)"] end subgraph "Key Parameters" Params["PCA: n_components
t-SNE: perplexity (5-50)
UMAP: n_neighbors, min_dist"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style M2 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Transfer Learning: Fine-tuning Pre-trained Models
fields.description: Leverage pre-trained models for your tasks - when to freeze layers, how to fine-tune

steps.title: What is Transfer Learning?
steps.explanation: **Transfer learning** uses knowledge from one task to help with another. Instead of training from scratch, start with a pre-trained model!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Transfer Learning"
        W1["Train from random weights"]
        W2["Need millions of samples"]
        W3["Days/weeks of training"]
        W4["Expensive GPUs"]
    end
    subgraph "With Transfer Learning"
        T1["Start with pretrained model"]
        T2["Need thousands of samples"]
        T3["Hours of training"]
        T4["Works on single GPU"]
    end
    W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    style W4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style T4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Why Does It Work?
steps.explanation: Neural networks learn hierarchical features. Early layers learn general features that transfer across tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "CNN Feature Hierarchy"
        L1["Layer 1: Edges, colors"]
        L2["Layer 2: Textures, patterns"]
        L3["Layer 3: Parts (eyes, wheels)"]
        L4["Layer 4: Objects (faces, cars)"]
        L5["Layer 5: Task-specific"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    subgraph "Transferability"
        T1["Early layers: Very transferable"]
        T2["Middle layers: Somewhat transferable"]
        T3["Late layers: Task-specific"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#90EE90
    style L2 fill:#90EE90
    style L5 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Two Main Approaches
steps.explanation: **Feature extraction** (freeze backbone) vs **Fine-tuning** (update all weights).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Feature Extraction"
        FE1["Freeze pretrained layers"]
        FE2["Only train new classifier"]
        FE3["Fast, works with small data"]
    end
    subgraph "Fine-tuning"
        FT1["Unfreeze some/all layers"]
        FT2["Train entire network"]
        FT3["Better accuracy, needs more data"]
    end
    subgraph "When to Use"
        W1["Small data → Feature extraction"]
        W2["More data → Fine-tune last layers"]
        W3["Lots of data → Fine-tune all"]
    end
    FE1 --> FE2 --> FE3
    FT1 --> FT2 --> FT3
    style FE3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style FT3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Feature Extraction
steps.explanation: Use pretrained model as a fixed feature extractor. Only train a new classification head.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Pretrained Model" Backbone["ResNet/ViT backbone
FROZEN (no gradient updates)"] end subgraph "New Head" Head["New classifier layers
TRAINED on your data"] end subgraph "Code" Code["# Freeze backbone
for param in model.backbone.parameters():
param.requires_grad = False

# Replace classifier
model.fc = nn.Linear(2048, num_classes)"] end Backbone --> Head subgraph "Benefits" Ben["Fast training
Works with 100s of samples
No overfitting risk"] end style Backbone fill:#ADD8E6 style Head fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Fine-tuning Strategy
steps.explanation: Gradually unfreeze layers from top to bottom. Use lower learning rates for pretrained layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Progressive Unfreezing" S1["1. Train only new head (1-2 epochs)"] S2["2. Unfreeze last block (1-2 epochs)"] S3["3. Unfreeze more blocks"] S4["4. Optional: Unfreeze all"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 subgraph "Learning Rate Strategy" LR["New layers: 1e-3 to 1e-4
Pretrained: 1e-5 to 1e-6
10-100x smaller for pretrained!"] end subgraph "Why Lower LR?" Why["Pretrained weights are good!
Don't destroy useful features
Small adjustments only"] end style LR fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Discriminative Learning Rates
steps.explanation: Use different learning rates for different layers. Lower rates for earlier (more general) layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Layer Groups" G1["Early layers: lr × 0.01"] G2["Middle layers: lr × 0.1"] G3["Late layers: lr × 1.0"] G4["New head: lr × 1.0"] end subgraph "Implementation" Code["optimizer = Adam([
{'params': model.layer1.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-6},
{'params': model.layer4.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-5},
{'params': model.fc.parameters(), 'lr': 1e-3},
])"] end G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 style G1 fill:#ADD8E6 style G4 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Popular Pretrained Models
steps.explanation: Common pretrained models for different domains.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Computer Vision" CV["ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT
Pretrained on ImageNet
torchvision.models"] end subgraph "NLP" NLP["BERT, RoBERTa, GPT
Pretrained on large text corpora
HuggingFace transformers"] end subgraph "Audio" Audio["Wav2Vec, Whisper
Speech recognition
HuggingFace"] end subgraph "Multimodal" Multi["CLIP, LLaVA
Image + text
OpenAI, HuggingFace"] end style CV fill:#ADD8E6 style NLP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Domain Similarity
steps.explanation: Transfer works best when source and target domains are similar.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "High Transfer" H1["ImageNet → Medical images
(both natural images)"] H2["English BERT → Spanish
(similar languages)"] end subgraph "Medium Transfer" M1["ImageNet → Satellite images
(different viewpoint)"] M2["BERT → Legal text
(specialized domain)"] end subgraph "Low Transfer" L1["ImageNet → Spectrograms
(different modality)"] L2["English BERT → Code
(different structure)"] end subgraph "Rule" Rule["Similar domain → More layers transfer
Different domain → Only early layers"] end style H1 fill:#90EE90 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: Common Mistakes
steps.explanation: Avoid these transfer learning pitfalls.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mistake 1" M1["Using same high LR for all layers
Destroys pretrained features"] F1["Fix: Lower LR for pretrained layers"] end subgraph "Mistake 2" M2["Fine-tuning with tiny dataset
Overfits immediately"] F2["Fix: Use feature extraction instead"] end subgraph "Mistake 3" M3["Wrong input preprocessing
Model expects specific normalization"] F3["Fix: Use same transforms as pretraining"] end subgraph "Mistake 4" M4["Forgetting to set model.eval()
BatchNorm/Dropout behave wrong"] F4["Fix: Always eval() for inference"] end style M1 fill:#FFB6C1 style F1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Transfer learning decision guide.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data Size Guide" D1["< 1K samples: Feature extraction"] D2["1K-10K: Fine-tune top layers"] D3["10K+: Fine-tune most/all layers"] end subgraph "Checklist" C1["1. Match input preprocessing"] C2["2. Replace final layer"] C3["3. Freeze initially"] C4["4. Use lower LR for pretrained"] C5["5. Gradually unfreeze"] end subgraph "Code Pattern" Code["model = torchvision.models.resnet50(pretrained=True)
model.fc = nn.Linear(2048, num_classes)
for param in model.parameters():
param.requires_grad = False
model.fc.requires_grad = True"] end style D1 fill:#ADD8E6 style D3 fill:#90EE90

fields.title: Spark Cluster Architecture
fields.description: Understand how Driver, Executors, and Cluster Manager work together in a Spark cluster

steps.title: The Big Picture
steps.explanation: A Spark cluster consists of three main components: the **Driver Program** (your application), the **Cluster Manager** (resource allocator), and **Executors** (workers that run tasks). Understanding this architecture is essential for debugging, tuning, and scaling Spark applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Spark Cluster" Driver["Driver Program
(Your Application)"] CM["Cluster Manager
(YARN/Mesos/K8s/Standalone)"] subgraph "Worker Nodes" E1["Executor 1"] E2["Executor 2"] E3["Executor 3"] end end Driver <--> CM CM --> E1 CM --> E2 CM --> E3 Driver <--> E1 Driver <--> E2 Driver <--> E3 style Driver fill:#4CAF50,color:white style CM fill:#2196F3,color:white style E1 fill:#FF9800 style E2 fill:#FF9800 style E3 fill:#FF9800

steps.title: The Driver Program
steps.explanation: The **Driver** is the heart of your Spark application. It runs your `main()` function, creates the **SparkContext/SparkSession**, defines transformations and actions on RDDs/DataFrames, and schedules tasks on executors. The driver maintains all the metadata about your application.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Driver Program" Main["main() function"] SC["SparkSession/
SparkContext"] DAG["DAG Scheduler
(Builds execution plan)"] TS["Task Scheduler
(Assigns tasks to executors)"] BC["Block Manager Master
(Tracks data location)"] end Main --> SC SC --> DAG DAG --> TS TS --> BC User["Your Code:
df.filter().groupBy().count()"] --> Main style Main fill:#4CAF50,color:white style SC fill:#81C784 style DAG fill:#A5D6A7 style TS fill:#C8E6C9 style User fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Cluster Manager
steps.explanation: The **Cluster Manager** is responsible for allocating resources across applications. Spark supports multiple cluster managers: **YARN** (Hadoop), **Mesos**, **Kubernetes**, and **Standalone**. It negotiates resources and launches executors on worker nodes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cluster Manager Options" YARN["YARN
Hadoop integration
Resource sharing
Enterprise-ready"] K8s["Kubernetes
Container native
Cloud friendly
Auto-scaling"] Mesos["Mesos
Fine-grained sharing
Multi-framework"] SA["Standalone
Simple setup
Built-in
Good for testing"] end Driver["Driver"] --> |"Request Resources"| CM["Cluster Manager"] CM --> |"Allocate"| YARN CM --> |"Allocate"| K8s CM --> |"Allocate"| Mesos CM --> |"Allocate"| SA style YARN fill:#2196F3,color:white style K8s fill:#326CE5,color:white style Mesos fill:#9C27B0,color:white style SA fill:#FF9800,color:white style CM fill:#607D8B,color:white

steps.title: Executors - The Workers
steps.explanation: **Executors** are JVM processes launched on worker nodes. Each executor runs **tasks** (units of work) and stores data for caching. Executors have a fixed number of **cores** (slots for parallel tasks) and **memory** for processing and storage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Executor JVM Process" subgraph "Task Slots - Cores" T1["Task 1
Processing partition 0"] T2["Task 2
Processing partition 1"] T3["Task 3
Processing partition 2"] T4["Task 4
Idle"] end subgraph "Memory" EM["Execution Memory
(Shuffles, Joins, Sorts)"] SM["Storage Memory
(Cached RDDs/DataFrames)"] end BM["Block Manager
(Data storage)"] end Driver["Driver"] --> |"Send Tasks"| T1 Driver --> |"Send Tasks"| T2 Driver --> |"Send Tasks"| T3 T1 --> EM T2 --> EM SM --> BM style T1 fill:#4CAF50 style T2 fill:#4CAF50 style T3 fill:#4CAF50 style T4 fill:#E0E0E0 style EM fill:#FF9800 style SM fill:#2196F3

steps.title: Communication Flow
steps.explanation: Here's how a typical Spark job flows: **1)** Driver submits app to Cluster Manager, **2)** CM allocates executors on workers, **3)** Driver sends tasks to executors, **4)** Executors run tasks and return results, **5)** Executors can communicate for shuffles.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant CM as Cluster Manager
    participant W1 as Worker 1
    participant W2 as Worker 2
    participant E1 as Executor 1
    participant E2 as Executor 2

    D->>CM: 1. Submit Application
    CM->>W1: 2. Launch Executor
    CM->>W2: 2. Launch Executor
    W1->>E1: Start JVM
    W2->>E2: Start JVM
    E1->>D: 3. Register
    E2->>D: 3. Register
    D->>E1: 4. Send Tasks
    D->>E2: 4. Send Tasks
    E1->>E2: 5. Shuffle Data
    E1->>D: 6. Return Results
    E2->>D: 6. Return Results

steps.title: Key Configuration Parameters
steps.explanation: Understanding these config parameters is crucial for tuning Spark applications. The Driver needs enough memory for collecting results; Executors need balanced cores and memory for efficient task execution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Driver Config" DM["spark.driver.memory
Default: 1g
Increase for large collects"] DC["spark.driver.cores
Default: 1
Usually 1 is enough"] end subgraph "Executor Config" EM["spark.executor.memory
Default: 1g
4-8g typical"] EC["spark.executor.cores
Default: 1
4-5 cores optimal"] EN["spark.executor.instances
Number of executors"] end subgraph "Memory Breakdown" Total["Executor Memory"] --> |"60%"| Exec["Execution + Storage"] Total --> |"40%"| Over["Reserved + Overhead"] Exec --> |"Unified"| UM["Shared pool for
shuffle and cache"] end style DM fill:#4CAF50,color:white style EM fill:#FF9800 style EC fill:#FF9800 style EN fill:#FF9800 style UM fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Cluster vs Client Mode
steps.explanation: Spark can run in **cluster mode** (driver runs on cluster) or **client mode** (driver runs on your machine). Use cluster mode for production and client mode for interactive development.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Client Mode" direction TB YM1["Your Machine"] D1["Driver runs here
Good for spark-shell, notebooks"] CL1["Cluster"] E1["Executors"] YM1 --> D1 D1 <--> CL1 CL1 --> E1 end subgraph "Cluster Mode" direction TB YM2["Your Machine"] Sub2["Submits job"] CL2["Cluster"] D2["Driver runs on cluster
Good for production"] E2["Executors"] YM2 --> Sub2 Sub2 --> CL2 CL2 --> D2 D2 --> E2 end style D1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style D2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style YM1 fill:#E3F2FD style YM2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Fault Tolerance
steps.explanation: Spark handles failures gracefully. If an **executor fails**, its tasks are rescheduled on other executors. If the **driver fails** in cluster mode, the entire application fails (use checkpointing for recovery). RDD lineage enables recomputation of lost partitions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Executor Failure" EF["Executor 2 dies"] TS["Task Scheduler detects failure"] RE["Reschedule tasks
on Executor 1 and 3"] RD["Recompute lost partitions
using RDD lineage"] end EF --> TS --> RE --> RD subgraph "Driver Failure" DF["Driver dies"] AF["Application fails"] CP["Recovery options:
Checkpointing
Spark Streaming recovery"] end DF --> AF --> CP style EF fill:#FFCDD2 style DF fill:#FFCDD2 style RE fill:#C8E6C9 style CP fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Job to Stage to Task Breakdown
fields.description: Learn how Spark decomposes your code into Jobs, Stages, and Tasks for distributed execution

steps.title: The Execution Hierarchy
steps.explanation: When you run Spark code, it gets broken down into a hierarchy: **Application** to **Jobs** to **Stages** to **Tasks**. Understanding this hierarchy is key to debugging and optimizing Spark applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB App["Application
(SparkSession)"] App --> J1["Job 1"] App --> J2["Job 2"] J1 --> S1["Stage 1"] J1 --> S2["Stage 2"] J2 --> S3["Stage 3"] S1 --> T1["Task 1.1"] S1 --> T2["Task 1.2"] S1 --> T3["Task 1.3"] S2 --> T4["Task 2.1"] S2 --> T5["Task 2.2"] style App fill:#4CAF50,color:white style J1 fill:#2196F3,color:white style J2 fill:#2196F3,color:white style S1 fill:#FF9800 style S2 fill:#FF9800 style S3 fill:#FF9800 style T1 fill:#E1BEE7 style T2 fill:#E1BEE7 style T3 fill:#E1BEE7 style T4 fill:#E1BEE7 style T5 fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: What Triggers a Job?
steps.explanation: A **Job** is triggered by an **action** - an operation that returns a value to the driver or writes to external storage. Common actions include: `count()`, `collect()`, `save()`, `show()`, `take()`. Each action creates one job.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Actions = Jobs"
        A1["df.count()"] --> J1["Job 1"]
        A2["df.collect()"] --> J2["Job 2"]
        A3["df.write.parquet()"] --> J3["Job 3"]
        A4["df.show()"] --> J4["Job 4"]
    end
    subgraph "NOT Actions - Lazy"
        T1["df.filter()"] --> X1["No job"]
        T2["df.select()"] --> X2["No job"]
        T3["df.groupBy()"] --> X3["No job"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style A2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style A3 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style A4 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style T1 fill:#E0E0E0
    style T2 fill:#E0E0E0
    style T3 fill:#E0E0E0

steps.title: Stages: Divided by Shuffles
steps.explanation: Within a job, **stages** are divided by **shuffle boundaries**. A shuffle occurs when data needs to be redistributed across partitions (e.g., `groupBy`, `join`, `repartition`). Operations that don't require shuffles stay in the same stage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Stage 1 - No Shuffle" Read["Read Data"] --> Filter["filter()"] Filter --> Select["select()"] Select --> Map["map()"] end Shuffle1["SHUFFLE
groupByKey()"] subgraph "Stage 2" Agg["aggregate()"] end Shuffle2["SHUFFLE
join()"] subgraph "Stage 3" Join["join result"] Join --> Write["write()"] end Map --> Shuffle1 Shuffle1 --> Agg Agg --> Shuffle2 Shuffle2 --> Join style Shuffle1 fill:#FF5722,color:white style Shuffle2 fill:#FF5722,color:white style Read fill:#4CAF50 style Write fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Tasks: One Per Partition
steps.explanation: A **task** is the smallest unit of work. Each stage runs one task per partition. If your DataFrame has 100 partitions, each stage will have 100 tasks. Tasks run in parallel across executor cores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Stage 1: 4 Partitions = 4 Tasks" P1["Partition 0"] --> T1["Task 0"] P2["Partition 1"] --> T2["Task 1"] P3["Partition 2"] --> T3["Task 2"] P4["Partition 3"] --> T4["Task 3"] end subgraph "Parallel Execution" T1 --> E1["Executor 1
Core 1"] T2 --> E1C2["Executor 1
Core 2"] T3 --> E2["Executor 2
Core 1"] T4 --> E2C2["Executor 2
Core 2"] end style P1 fill:#E3F2FD style P2 fill:#E3F2FD style P3 fill:#E3F2FD style P4 fill:#E3F2FD style T1 fill:#4CAF50 style T2 fill:#4CAF50 style T3 fill:#4CAF50 style T4 fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Example: Word Count Job
steps.explanation: Let's trace a word count job through the hierarchy. The code `textFile.flatMap().map().reduceByKey().collect()` creates one job (collect is the action), two stages (reduceByKey causes a shuffle), and tasks equal to partition count.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" Code["words = sc.textFile file
.flatMap lambda x: x.split
.map lambda x: x, 1
.reduceByKey lambda a,b: a+b
.collect"] end subgraph "Job 1 triggered by collect" subgraph "Stage 1: Read and Map" S1T["textFile - flatMap - map"] S1P["4 partitions = 4 tasks"] end SH["SHUFFLE
reduceByKey"] subgraph "Stage 2: Reduce" S2T["reduceByKey"] S2P["4 partitions = 4 tasks"] end end Code --> S1T S1T --> SH SH --> S2T S2T --> |"collect()"| Driver["Results to Driver"] style Code fill:#FFF9C4 style SH fill:#FF5722,color:white style Driver fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: The DAG Scheduler
steps.explanation: The **DAG Scheduler** builds a Directed Acyclic Graph of stages. It identifies shuffle boundaries, creates stages, and determines the optimal execution order. Stages with no dependencies can run in parallel.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TD subgraph "DAG Scheduler Process" A["Action called
(e.g., collect)"] B["Build RDD/DataFrame lineage"] C["Identify shuffle boundaries"] D["Create stages"] E["Determine dependencies"] F["Submit to Task Scheduler"] end A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F subgraph "Example DAG" S1["Stage 1
Read + Filter"] S2["Stage 2
Read + Map"] S3["Stage 3
Join"] S4["Stage 4
Aggregate + Write"] S1 --> S3 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style S1 fill:#2196F3 style S2 fill:#2196F3 style S3 fill:#FF9800 style S4 fill:#E91E63,color:white

steps.title: The Task Scheduler
steps.explanation: The **Task Scheduler** assigns tasks to executors. It considers **data locality** (running tasks where data lives) and **speculative execution** (re-launching slow tasks). Tasks are assigned to executor cores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Task Scheduler" TQ["Task Queue"] LS["Locality Scheduling
1. PROCESS_LOCAL same JVM
2. NODE_LOCAL same node
3. RACK_LOCAL same rack
4. ANY anywhere"] SE["Speculative Execution
Re-run slow tasks"] end TQ --> LS LS --> E1["Executor 1"] LS --> E2["Executor 2"] LS --> E3["Executor 3"] SE -.-> |"Slow task?"| E3 subgraph "Best Case" Data["Data on Node 1"] Task["Task runs on Node 1"] Data --> Task Fast["Fast! No network transfer"] end style LS fill:#4CAF50,color:white style SE fill:#FFC107 style Fast fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Viewing in Spark UI
steps.explanation: The Spark UI (port 4040) shows all jobs, stages, and tasks. Use it to identify bottlenecks, shuffle sizes, and stragglers. The **DAG Visualization** shows stage dependencies graphically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Spark UI - Jobs Tab" J["Jobs
Status: Succeeded/Failed/Running
Duration, Stages per job"] end subgraph "Spark UI - Stages Tab" S["Stages
Input/Output size
Shuffle Read/Write
Tasks: Succeeded/Failed"] end subgraph "Spark UI - Tasks" T["Task Details
Duration per task
GC time
Shuffle spill
Data locality"] end J --> S --> T subgraph "What to Look For" L1["Stragglers: One task much slower"] L2["Shuffle spill: Data does not fit in memory"] L3["Skew: Uneven partition sizes"] L4["Data locality: Most tasks PROCESS_LOCAL"] end style L1 fill:#FFCDD2 style L2 fill:#FFCDD2 style L3 fill:#FFCDD2 style L4 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Lazy Evaluation in Action
fields.description: Understand how Spark's lazy evaluation optimizes execution by waiting for actions

steps.title: What is Lazy Evaluation?
steps.explanation: In Spark, **transformations** (like `filter`, `map`, `select`) don't execute immediately. Instead, Spark builds a **logical plan** and waits until an **action** (like `count`, `collect`, `show`) is called. This enables powerful optimizations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Eager Execution - Not Spark"
        E1["filter()"] --> |"Execute now"| E2["Data filtered"]
        E2 --> E3["map()"]
        E3 --> |"Execute now"| E4["Data mapped"]
    end
    subgraph "Lazy Execution - Spark"
        L1["filter()"] --> |"Add to plan"| L2["Plan: filter"]
        L2 --> L3["map()"]
        L3 --> |"Add to plan"| L4["Plan: filter then map"]
        L4 --> L5["count()"]
        L5 --> |"Execute ALL"| L6["Results!"]
    end
    style E2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style E4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style L5 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style L6 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Transformations vs Actions
steps.explanation: **Transformations** return a new RDD/DataFrame and are lazy. **Actions** trigger execution and return values or write data. Knowing the difference is crucial for understanding when Spark actually does work.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Transformations - Lazy"
        T1["filter - narrow"]
        T2["map - narrow"]
        T3["select - narrow"]
        T4["withColumn - narrow"]
        T5["groupBy - wide"]
        T6["join - wide"]
        T7["repartition - wide"]
    end
    subgraph "Actions - Trigger Execution"
        A1["count returns number"]
        A2["collect returns array"]
        A3["take n returns array"]
        A4["show prints"]
        A5["write.parquet creates files"]
        A6["foreach side effects"]
    end
    T1 --> |"Add to plan"| Plan["Logical Plan"]
    T5 --> |"Add to plan"| Plan
    Plan --> |"Action triggers"| A1
    style T1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style T5 fill:#BBDEFB
    style A1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style Plan fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Building the Execution Plan
steps.explanation: As you chain transformations, Spark builds a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** of operations. Nothing runs yet! The DAG represents what *will* happen, not what *has* happened.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Your Code" C1["df = spark.read.parquet data"] C2["df2 = df.filter col age > 21"] C3["df3 = df2.select name, age"] C4["df4 = df3.withColumn senior, col age > 65"] end subgraph "What Spark Has - Logical Plan" P1["Scan parquet"] P2["Filter: age > 21"] P3["Project: name, age"] P4["Add column: senior"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 end subgraph "What Has Run" Nothing["Nothing!
No data read
No computation done"] end C1 --> P1 C2 --> P2 C3 --> P3 C4 --> P4 style Nothing fill:#FFCDD2 style P4 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Action Triggers Execution
steps.explanation: When you call an action like `count()`, Spark's **Catalyst optimizer** takes the logical plan, optimizes it, creates a physical plan, and finally executes. All the chained transformations run together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Before Action" LP["Logical Plan
Your transformations"] end Action["df4.count()"] subgraph "Optimization" Cat["Catalyst Optimizer"] O1["Predicate pushdown"] O2["Column pruning"] O3["Join reordering"] end subgraph "Execution" PP["Physical Plan"] Tasks["Generate Tasks"] Run["Run on Executors"] Result["Return Result: 42"] end LP --> Action Action --> Cat Cat --> O1 Cat --> O2 Cat --> O3 O1 --> PP O2 --> PP O3 --> PP PP --> Tasks --> Run --> Result style Action fill:#4CAF50,color:white style Cat fill:#2196F3,color:white style Result fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Optimization: Predicate Pushdown
steps.explanation: Because Spark sees the whole plan before executing, it can optimize. **Predicate pushdown** moves filters earlier to read less data. If you filter after joining, Spark may push the filter before the join!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "What You Wrote" W1["Read all columns"] W2["Join"] W3["Filter: status = active"] end W1 --> W2 --> W3 subgraph "What Spark Does - Optimized" O1["Read + Filter: status = active
Pushed down to scan"] O2["Join
Less data!"] end O1 --> O2 style W3 fill:#FFCDD2 style O1 fill:#C8E6C9 style O2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Optimization: Column Pruning
steps.explanation: **Column pruning** eliminates columns you don't need. If you only select 3 columns but the source has 100, Spark reads only those 3 (especially effective with columnar formats like Parquet).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "What You Wrote" W1["df = spark.read.parquet wide_table
100 columns"] W2["df.select name, email, age"] W3["df.show()"] end subgraph "What Spark Does" O1["Read ONLY 3 columns from Parquet
name, email, age"] O2["Show results"] end subgraph "Savings" S1["97 columns never read"] S2["97 percent less I/O"] S3["97 percent less memory"] end style O1 fill:#C8E6C9 style S1 fill:#FFCDD2 style S2 fill:#C8E6C9 style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Actions = Multiple Executions
steps.explanation: Each action triggers a new execution of the DAG. If you call `count()` then `show()`, Spark runs everything twice! Use `cache()` or `persist()` to avoid recomputation when you need multiple actions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Cache"
        R1["read + transform"] --> A1["count()"]
        R2["read + transform AGAIN"] --> A2["show()"]
        R3["read + transform AGAIN"] --> A3["write()"]
        Waste["3x computation!"]
    end
    subgraph "With Cache"
        R4["read + transform"] --> C["cache()"]
        C --> |"Stored in memory"| A4["count() - fast!"]
        C --> A5["show() - fast!"]
        C --> A6["write() - fast!"]
        Win["1x computation"]
    end
    style Waste fill:#FFCDD2
    style Win fill:#C8E6C9
    style C fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Key Takeaways
steps.explanation: Lazy evaluation is a superpower, not a limitation. It enables whole-program optimization that's impossible with eager execution. Master this concept to write efficient Spark code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Benefits of Lazy Evaluation" B1["Optimization
Catalyst optimizes entire plan"] B2["Efficiency
Only read/compute what is needed"] B3["Flexibility
Build complex pipelines"] B4["Fault Tolerance
Lineage for recomputation"] end subgraph "Tips" T1["1. Chain transformations freely"] T2["2. Use cache for reused data"] T3["3. Call explain to see the plan"] T4["4. Actions: count, collect, show, write"] end subgraph "Debug" D["df.explain True
Shows all optimization stages"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B2 fill:#C8E6C9 style B3 fill:#C8E6C9 style B4 fill:#C8E6C9 style D fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Partitioning Strategies
fields.description: Learn how data is distributed across partitions using Hash and Range partitioning

steps.title: What is Partitioning?
steps.explanation: **Partitioning** is how Spark divides data across multiple nodes for parallel processing. Each partition is processed by one task. Good partitioning = even distribution = efficient parallel execution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataFrame: 12 Million Rows" Data["Full Dataset"] end Data --> |"Partitioned"| P1["Partition 0
3M rows"] Data --> P2["Partition 1
3M rows"] Data --> P3["Partition 2
3M rows"] Data --> P4["Partition 3
3M rows"] P1 --> T1["Task 0
Executor 1"] P2 --> T2["Task 1
Executor 1"] P3 --> T3["Task 2
Executor 2"] P4 --> T4["Task 3
Executor 2"] style Data fill:#E3F2FD style P1 fill:#4CAF50 style P2 fill:#4CAF50 style P3 fill:#4CAF50 style P4 fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Default Partitioning
steps.explanation: When you read data, the number of partitions depends on the source. HDFS files: 1 partition per block (128MB default). Local files: based on `spark.default.parallelism`. You can control this with `repartition()` or `coalesce()`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Reading from HDFS" HDFS["HDFS File: 512MB"] HDFS --> |"128MB blocks"| B1["Block 1"] HDFS --> B2["Block 2"] HDFS --> B3["Block 3"] HDFS --> B4["Block 4"] B1 --> P1["Partition 1"] B2 --> P2["Partition 2"] B3 --> P3["Partition 3"] B4 --> P4["Partition 4"] end subgraph "Configuration" C1["spark.default.parallelism
Default partitions for shuffles"] C2["spark.sql.shuffle.partitions
Default: 200 often too high!"] end style HDFS fill:#2196F3,color:white style P1 fill:#4CAF50 style P2 fill:#4CAF50 style P3 fill:#4CAF50 style P4 fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Hash Partitioning
steps.explanation: **Hash partitioning** uses a hash function on a key to determine which partition a record goes to: `partition = hash(key) % numPartitions`. This ensures all records with the same key end up in the same partition.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hash Partitioning - 4 partitions" K1["user_id: 101"] --> |"hash 101 mod 4 = 1"| P1["Partition 1"] K2["user_id: 102"] --> |"hash 102 mod 4 = 2"| P2["Partition 2"] K3["user_id: 103"] --> |"hash 103 mod 4 = 3"| P3["Partition 3"] K4["user_id: 104"] --> |"hash 104 mod 4 = 0"| P0["Partition 0"] K5["user_id: 201"] --> |"hash 201 mod 4 = 1"| P1 K6["user_id: 205"] --> |"hash 205 mod 4 = 1"| P1 end subgraph "Result" R["Same key = Same partition
Good for: groupBy, join"] end style P0 fill:#4CAF50 style P1 fill:#4CAF50 style P2 fill:#4CAF50 style P3 fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Range Partitioning
steps.explanation: **Range partitioning** divides data based on key ranges. Records with keys in the same range go to the same partition. This is used for sorted data and is essential for efficient range queries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Range Partitioning by Age" D["All Users"] D --> |"age 0-25"| P0["Partition 0
Young users"] D --> |"age 26-50"| P1["Partition 1
Adults"] D --> |"age 51-75"| P2["Partition 2
Middle-aged"] D --> |"age 76-100"| P3["Partition 3
Seniors"] end subgraph "Boundaries" B["Boundaries: 25, 50, 75
Determined by sampling data"] end subgraph "Good For" G1["Range queries: WHERE age BETWEEN 30 AND 40"] G2["Sorted output: ORDER BY age"] G3["Merge joins on sorted data"] end style P0 fill:#4CAF50 style P1 fill:#4CAF50 style P2 fill:#4CAF50 style P3 fill:#4CAF50

steps.title: Hash vs Range Comparison
steps.explanation: Choose the right partitioning strategy based on your query patterns. **Hash** is better for equality joins and groupBy. **Range** is better for range queries and sorted output.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hash Partitioning" H1["Good: Equality operations
groupBy, join on key"] H2["Good: Uniform distribution
usually"] H3["Bad: Range queries scan all partitions"] H4["Bad: Sorted output requires shuffle"] end subgraph "Range Partitioning" R1["Good: Range queries
Only scan relevant partitions"] R2["Good: Sorted output
No extra shuffle needed"] R3["Bad: Can be skewed
If data is not uniform"] R4["Bad: Requires sampling to find boundaries"] end style H1 fill:#C8E6C9 style H2 fill:#C8E6C9 style H3 fill:#FFCDD2 style H4 fill:#FFCDD2 style R1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R2 fill:#C8E6C9 style R3 fill:#FFCDD2 style R4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: repartition vs coalesce
steps.explanation: `repartition(n)` reshuffles data to create exactly n partitions (expensive, full shuffle). `coalesce(n)` reduces partitions without a full shuffle (efficient, only combines). Use `coalesce` when reducing partitions, `repartition` when increasing or rebalancing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "repartition 4 - Full Shuffle" R1["8 Partitions"] R1 --> |"Full shuffle"| R2["4 Partitions
Evenly distributed"] R3["Can increase OR decrease"] R4["Expensive: moves all data"] end subgraph "coalesce 4 - No Shuffle" C1["8 Partitions"] C1 --> |"Combine locally"| C2["4 Partitions
May be uneven"] C3["Can only DECREASE"] C4["Cheap: no data movement"] end subgraph "When to Use" W1["repartition: After filter that shrinks data"] W2["coalesce: Before write to reduce files"] W3["repartition col: Partition by column for joins"] end style R4 fill:#FFCDD2 style C4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Partition Pruning
steps.explanation: When data is partitioned on disk (e.g., Parquet files partitioned by date), Spark can skip entire partitions that don't match filter predicates. This is called **partition pruning** and can dramatically speed up queries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data Partitioned by Date" Root["sales/"] Root --> D1["date=2024-01-01/"] Root --> D2["date=2024-01-02/"] Root --> D3["date=2024-01-03/"] Root --> D4["..."] Root --> D5["date=2024-12-31/"] end subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT * FROM sales
WHERE date = 2024-01-02"] end subgraph "Partition Pruning" D1 --> |"Skipped"| Skip1["Not read"] D2 --> |"Read"| Read["Only this partition!"] D3 --> |"Skipped"| Skip2["Not read"] D5 --> |"Skipped"| Skip3["Not read"] end Q --> D2 style D2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style D1 fill:#E0E0E0 style D3 fill:#E0E0E0 style D5 fill:#E0E0E0

steps.title: Choosing the Right Number of Partitions
steps.explanation: The optimal partition count balances parallelism and overhead. Too few = underutilized cores. Too many = scheduling overhead. Rule of thumb: **2-4x your total cores**, with **~128MB per partition**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Too Few Partitions" TF1["4 partitions, 100 cores"] TF2["96 cores idle!"] TF3["Tasks take forever"] end subgraph "Too Many Partitions" TM1["10,000 partitions"] TM2["Scheduling overhead"] TM3["Too many small tasks"] TM4["Driver overwhelmed"] end subgraph "Just Right" JR1["Partitions = 2-4x cores"] JR2["~128MB per partition"] JR3["Adjust based on data size"] end subgraph "Formula" F["Recommended partitions =
max total_cores x 2,
data_size_gb / 0.128"] end style TF2 fill:#FFCDD2 style TM2 fill:#FFCDD2 style JR1 fill:#C8E6C9 style JR2 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Shuffle Operation Deep Dive
fields.description: Understand how shuffle operations redistribute data between partitions during groupBy and join

steps.title: What is a Shuffle?
steps.explanation: A **shuffle** is Spark's mechanism for redistributing data across partitions. It's triggered when data needs to be reorganized by key (groupBy, join, reduceByKey). Shuffles are expensive because they require disk I/O and network transfer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Before Shuffle" P1["Partition 1
A:1, B:2, C:3"] P2["Partition 2
A:4, B:5, D:6"] P3["Partition 3
B:7, C:8, D:9"] end subgraph "Shuffle Required" SH["groupByKey
Must group all As together,
all Bs together, etc."] end subgraph "After Shuffle" R1["Partition 1
A:1, A:4"] R2["Partition 2
B:2, B:5, B:7"] R3["Partition 3
C:3, C:8, D:6, D:9"] end P1 --> SH P2 --> SH P3 --> SH SH --> R1 SH --> R2 SH --> R3 style SH fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Why Shuffles are Expensive
steps.explanation: Shuffles are the most expensive operation in Spark because they involve: **1)** Writing data to disk, **2)** Transferring data over network, **3)** Reading and merging on the target partition. This is why minimizing shuffles is key to Spark performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Shuffle Cost" C1["1. Serialize data"] C2["2. Write to local disk
Shuffle files"] C3["3. Transfer over network
Often the bottleneck!"] C4["4. Read on target executor"] C5["5. Deserialize data"] C6["6. Merge and sort"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 subgraph "Impact" I1["Slow: Disk + Network I/O"] I2["Disk space for shuffle files"] I3["GC pressure from serialization"] I4["Can cause OOM if data does not fit"] end style C3 fill:#FF5722,color:white style I1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: The Shuffle Process - Step by Step
steps.explanation: Let's trace a shuffle through `reduceByKey`. The **map side** writes shuffle files partitioned by target partition. The **reduce side** fetches and combines these files.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M1 as Map Task 1
    participant M2 as Map Task 2
    participant SM as Shuffle Manager
    participant R1 as Reduce Task 1
    participant R2 as Reduce Task 2

    Note over M1,M2: Map Side - Write
    M1->>SM: Write partition 1 data to file_1_1
    M1->>SM: Write partition 2 data to file_1_2
    M2->>SM: Write partition 1 data to file_2_1
    M2->>SM: Write partition 2 data to file_2_2

    Note over R1,R2: Reduce Side - Read
    R1->>SM: Fetch file_1_1, file_2_1
    SM->>R1: Send data
    R1->>R1: Merge and reduce
    R2->>SM: Fetch file_1_2, file_2_2
    SM->>R2: Send data
    R2->>R2: Merge and reduce

steps.title: Shuffle in groupByKey
steps.explanation: `groupByKey()` shuffles ALL values for each key to a single partition. This can be memory-intensive because all values must fit in memory before processing. Prefer `reduceByKey` which pre-aggregates on the map side.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "groupByKey - All data shuffled"
        GB1["Partition 1: A,1 B,2"]
        GB2["Partition 2: A,3 A,4"]
        GB3["Partition 3: B,5 B,6"]
        GBS["SHUFFLE"]
        GBR1["Partition 1: A, 1,3,4"]
        GBR2["Partition 2: B, 2,5,6"]
    end
    GB1 --> GBS
    GB2 --> GBS
    GB3 --> GBS
    GBS --> GBR1
    GBS --> GBR2
    subgraph "reduceByKey - Pre-aggregate first"
        RB1["Partition 1: A,1 B,2"]
        RB2["Partition 2: A,7"]
        RB3["Partition 3: B,11"]
        Pre["Local combine first!"]
        RBS["Smaller shuffle"]
        RBR1["Partition 1: A, 8"]
        RBR2["Partition 2: B, 13"]
    end
    style GBS fill:#FF5722,color:white
    style RBS fill:#FFC107
    style Pre fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Shuffle in Joins
steps.explanation: When joining two DataFrames, both must be shuffled so matching keys end up on the same partition. This is a **shuffle join** (also called sort-merge join). Both sides are partitioned by the join key.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "DataFrame A"
        A1["P1: 1,a 2,b"]
        A2["P2: 3,c 4,d"]
    end
    subgraph "DataFrame B"
        B1["P1: 1,x 3,y"]
        B2["P2: 2,z 4,w"]
    end
    subgraph "Shuffle Both Sides"
        SH["Both shuffled by join key"]
    end
    subgraph "After Shuffle - Colocated"
        R1["P1: A 1,a B 1,x"]
        R2["P2: A 2,b B 2,z"]
        R3["P3: A 3,c B 3,y"]
        R4["P4: A 4,d B 4,w"]
    end
    A1 --> SH
    A2 --> SH
    B1 --> SH
    B2 --> SH
    SH --> R1
    SH --> R2
    SH --> R3
    SH --> R4
    style SH fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Avoiding Shuffles: Broadcast Join
steps.explanation: If one DataFrame is small enough to fit in memory, use a **broadcast join**. The small table is sent to all executors, eliminating the shuffle of the large table entirely!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Shuffle Join - Both Shuffled" SJ1["Large Table A
1TB - Shuffled"] SJ2["Small Table B
100MB - Shuffled"] SJS["Both shuffle"] end subgraph "Broadcast Join - No Shuffle!" BJ1["Large Table A
1TB - NOT shuffled"] BJ2["Small Table B
100MB - Broadcast"] BJB["Sent to all executors"] BJR["Join happens locally
on each partition"] end BJ2 --> BJB --> BJR BJ1 --> BJR subgraph "Code" Code["from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
df_large.join broadcast df_small, key"] end style SJS fill:#FF5722,color:white style BJB fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Shuffle Configuration
steps.explanation: Key configurations to tune shuffle performance. `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` controls output partitions (default 200 is often wrong!). Shuffle compression and buffer sizes affect I/O efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Key Configurations" C1["spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 200
Default for SQL shuffles
Often too high or low!"] C2["spark.shuffle.compress = true
Compress shuffle data
Reduces network I/O"] C3["spark.shuffle.spill.compress = true
Compress spilled data
Saves disk space"] C4["spark.reducer.maxSizeInFlight = 48m
Buffer size for fetching
Increase for fast networks"] end subgraph "Tuning Tips" T1["Partitions = 2-3x cores"] T2["Target ~128MB per partition"] T3["Monitor shuffle spill in UI"] T4["Consider repartition before wide ops"] end style C1 fill:#FFF9C4 style T1 fill:#C8E6C9 style T2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Monitoring Shuffles in Spark UI
steps.explanation: The Spark UI shows shuffle metrics for each stage. Watch **Shuffle Read/Write** sizes, **Shuffle Spill** (data that didn't fit in memory), and **Task Duration** distribution to identify shuffle bottlenecks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Spark UI - Stage Details" M1["Shuffle Write: 50GB
Data written by map tasks"] M2["Shuffle Read: 50GB
Data read by reduce tasks"] M3["Shuffle Spill Memory: 10GB
Data spilled to disk"] M4["Shuffle Spill Disk: 8GB
Compressed on disk"] end subgraph "Warning Signs" W1["High spill: Need more memory"] W2["Uneven task times: Data skew"] W3["Slow tasks: Network bottleneck"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Increase executor memory"] S2["Use salting for skewed keys"] S3["Reduce partition count"] S4["Use broadcast joins"] end style M3 fill:#FFCDD2 style W1 fill:#FFCDD2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9 style S4 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Wide vs Narrow Transformations
fields.description: Learn which operations cause shuffles and how to minimize them for better performance

steps.title: Two Types of Transformations
steps.explanation: Spark transformations are either **narrow** (no shuffle, partition-local) or **wide** (require shuffle, data moves between partitions). Understanding this distinction is crucial for performance optimization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Narrow Transformations" N1["Each output partition
depends on ONE input partition"] NP1["Partition 1"] --> NT1["Transform"] NP2["Partition 2"] --> NT2["Transform"] NP3["Partition 3"] --> NT3["Transform"] NT1 --> NR1["Output 1"] NT2 --> NR2["Output 2"] NT3 --> NR3["Output 3"] end subgraph "Wide Transformations" W1["Each output partition
depends on MULTIPLE input partitions"] WP1["Partition 1"] --> WS["Shuffle"] WP2["Partition 2"] --> WS WP3["Partition 3"] --> WS WS --> WR1["Output 1"] WS --> WR2["Output 2"] end style NR1 fill:#C8E6C9 style NR2 fill:#C8E6C9 style NR3 fill:#C8E6C9 style WS fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Narrow Transformations - The Fast Ones
steps.explanation: **Narrow transformations** operate on individual partitions without data movement. They're fast because each task works independently on its partition. No network I/O, no waiting for other partitions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Narrow Transformations" M["map / flatMap"] F["filter"] S["select"] W["withColumn"] U["union"] C["coalesce"] MP["mapPartitions"] end subgraph "Data Flow - No Shuffle" P1["Partition 1
1,2,3"] --> |"filter x > 1"| R1["2,3"] P2["Partition 2
4,5,6"] --> |"filter x > 1"| R2["4,5,6"] P3["Partition 3
0,1,7"] --> |"filter x > 1"| R3["7"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["No network transfer"] B2["No disk I/O"] B3["Pipeline within same task"] B4["Can run in same stage"] end style M fill:#C8E6C9 style F fill:#C8E6C9 style S fill:#C8E6C9 style W fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Wide Transformations - The Expensive Ones
steps.explanation: **Wide transformations** require data from multiple input partitions to compute each output partition. This means data must be shuffled across the network, creating a stage boundary.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Wide Transformations"
        G["groupByKey / groupBy"]
        R["reduceByKey"]
        A["aggregateByKey"]
        J["join / cogroup"]
        RP["repartition"]
        S["sortByKey / sort"]
        D["distinct"]
    end
    subgraph "Data Flow - Shuffle Required"
        P1["P1: A,1 B,2"]
        P2["P2: A,3 B,4"]
        P3["P3: A,5 C,6"]
        SH["SHUFFLE"]
        R1["P1: A,1,3,5"]
        R2["P2: B,2,4"]
        R3["P3: C,6"]
    end
    P1 --> SH
    P2 --> SH
    P3 --> SH
    SH --> R1
    SH --> R2
    SH --> R3
    style G fill:#FFCDD2
    style R fill:#FFCDD2
    style J fill:#FFCDD2
    style SH fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Stage Boundaries
steps.explanation: Wide transformations create **stage boundaries**. All narrow transformations before a wide one run in Stage 1, then shuffle happens, then Stage 2 begins. This is visible in Spark UI.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Stage 1" A["read"] --> B["filter"] B --> C["select"] C --> D["withColumn"] end E["groupBy
Wide Transform"] subgraph "Stage 2" F["agg"] --> G["filter"] G --> H["select"] end I["sort
Wide Transform"] subgraph "Stage 3" J["write"] end D --> E --> F H --> I --> J style E fill:#FF5722,color:white style I fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Pipelining Within Stages
steps.explanation: Within a stage, narrow transformations are **pipelined** - they execute in a single pass without materializing intermediate results. This is extremely efficient! Wide transforms break this pipeline.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Pipelined - One Stage - Fast!" Read["Read row"] --> F["Filter"] F --> S["Select"] S --> M["Map"] M --> Write["Write to shuffle"] Note1["All in memory,
single pass per row!"] end subgraph "NOT Pipelined - Multiple Stages" S1["Stage 1:
Read - Filter - Select"] SH["Shuffle to disk and network"] S2["Stage 2:
Aggregate"] SH2["Shuffle again"] S3["Stage 3:
Sort - Write"] S1 --> SH --> S2 --> SH2 --> S3 end style Note1 fill:#C8E6C9 style SH fill:#FF5722,color:white style SH2 fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: Strategies to Minimize Shuffles
steps.explanation: Reducing shuffles is one of the best ways to speed up Spark jobs. Here are key strategies: pre-aggregation, broadcast joins, and partition alignment.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "1. Pre-aggregate" PA1["reduceByKey vs groupByKey"] PA2["Combine locally first"] PA3["Less data shuffled!"] end subgraph "2. Broadcast small tables" BC1["broadcast df_small"] BC2["Small table to all executors"] BC3["No shuffle for big table!"] end subgraph "3. Partition alignment" AL1["repartition col both DFs
before join"] AL2["Same partitioning = no shuffle"] end subgraph "4. Filter early" FE1["Filter before join/group"] FE2["Less data to shuffle"] end style PA3 fill:#C8E6C9 style BC3 fill:#C8E6C9 style AL2 fill:#C8E6C9 style FE2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Quick Reference Table
steps.explanation: Here's a handy reference for common operations. Remember: narrow = fast (same stage), wide = shuffle (new stage).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Narrow - No Shuffle"
        N1["map, flatMap, mapPartitions"]
        N2["filter, where"]
        N3["select, withColumn, drop"]
        N4["union, sample"]
        N5["coalesce reduce only"]
    end
    subgraph "Wide - Causes Shuffle"
        W1["groupByKey, reduceByKey"]
        W2["groupBy.agg"]
        W3["join, leftOuterJoin"]
        W4["distinct, dropDuplicates"]
        W5["sort, orderBy"]
        W6["repartition"]
    end
    subgraph "Tips"
        T1["Chain narrow transforms freely"]
        T2["Think twice before wide transforms"]
        T3["Check UI for shuffle sizes"]
    end
    style N1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style W2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style W3 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Data Skew: The Silent Performance Killer
fields.description: Understand how uneven data distribution causes bottlenecks and learn strategies to fix it

steps.title: What is Data Skew?
steps.explanation: **Data skew** occurs when data is unevenly distributed across partitions. One partition might have millions of rows while others have thousands. This creates a bottleneck because Spark must wait for the slowest task.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Ideal Distribution" I1["Partition 1
1M rows"] I2["Partition 2
1M rows"] I3["Partition 3
1M rows"] I4["Partition 4
1M rows"] IT["All finish same time"] end subgraph "Skewed Distribution" S1["Partition 1
100K rows"] S2["Partition 2
3.7M rows!"] S3["Partition 3
100K rows"] S4["Partition 4
100K rows"] ST["Waiting for P2..."] end style S2 fill:#FF5722,color:white style IT fill:#C8E6C9 style ST fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Common Causes of Skew
steps.explanation: Skew typically happens with **hot keys** - values that appear much more frequently than others. Examples: null values, default categories like 'unknown', popular users in social data, or 'US' in country fields.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Example: User Activity Data"
        U1["User A: 50 actions"]
        U2["User B: 100 actions"]
        U3["Bot Account: 5,000,000 actions"]
        U4["User D: 75 actions"]
    end
    subgraph "After groupBy user_id"
        P1["Partition for A,B,D: 225 rows"]
        P2["Partition for Bot: 5,000,000 rows"]
    end
    subgraph "Real-World Hot Keys"
        H1["NULL values"]
        H2["unknown / other categories"]
        H3["Top 0.1 percent users - power users"]
        H4["Popular products"]
        H5["Default values"]
    end
    style U3 fill:#FF5722,color:white
    style P2 fill:#FF5722,color:white

steps.title: How to Detect Skew
steps.explanation: Look for these warning signs in Spark UI: one task takes much longer than others, shuffle read/write sizes are uneven, or you see 'stragglers'. Also check data distribution directly with `groupBy().count()`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Spark UI Warning Signs" W1["Task Duration"] W2["Task 1: 5 seconds"] W3["Task 2: 5 seconds"] W4["Task 3: 300 seconds"] W5["Task 4: 5 seconds"] end subgraph "Check Data Distribution" Code["df.groupBy key
.count
.orderBy desc count
.show"] Result["key | count
------|-------
NULL | 5000000
US | 1000000
UK | 50000
..."] end subgraph "Metrics to Watch" M1["Shuffle Read Size per task"] M2["Input Records per task"] M3["GC Time - high = OOM risk"] end style W4 fill:#FF5722,color:white style Result fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Solution 1: Salting
steps.explanation: **Salting** artificially increases key cardinality by appending a random number. This distributes hot keys across multiple partitions. After aggregation, remove the salt and combine results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Before Salting" B1["All US rows go to 1 partition"] B2["Partition 1: US, US, US, US...
1 million rows"] end subgraph "With Salting - salt 0-9" S1["Add salt: US becomes US_0, US_1, ... US_9"] S2["P1: US_0 100K"] S3["P2: US_1 100K"] S4["P3: US_2 100K"] S5["... distributed across 10 partitions"] end subgraph "Code Pattern" Code["# Add salt
df = df.withColumn salt, rand * 10 cast int
df = df.withColumn salted_key, concat col key, lit _, col salt

# Aggregate with salted key
result = df.groupBy salted_key .agg ...

# Remove salt and combine
result = result.withColumn key, split salted_key, _ index 0
final = result.groupBy key .agg sum ..."] end style B2 fill:#FF5722,color:white style S2 fill:#C8E6C9 style S3 fill:#C8E6C9 style S4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Solution 2: Broadcast Join for Skewed Keys
steps.explanation: For skewed joins, separate the hot keys and handle them differently. Broadcast join the hot keys (no shuffle!) and regular join the rest, then union the results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Split Approach" Big["Large Table"] Small["Small Table"] Big --> |"filter hot keys"| Hot["Hot Keys - broadcast join"] Big --> |"filter normal keys"| Normal["Normal Keys - shuffle join"] Hot --> BC["Broadcast Join
No shuffle"] Normal --> SH["Shuffle Join"] Small --> BC Small --> SH BC --> Union["UNION"] SH --> Union end subgraph "Code Pattern" Code["hot_keys = US, NULL, unknown

# Split
df_hot = df_big.filter col key .isin hot_keys
df_normal = df_big.filter ~col key .isin hot_keys

# Join separately
result_hot = df_hot.join broadcast df_small, key
result_normal = df_normal.join df_small, key

# Combine
result = result_hot.union result_normal"] end style BC fill:#C8E6C9 style SH fill:#FF9800

steps.title: Solution 3: Adaptive Query Execution - AQE
steps.explanation: Spark 3.0+ includes **Adaptive Query Execution** which automatically handles skew! Enable it with `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`. AQE detects skewed partitions and splits them automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "AQE Skew Handling" D["Detect skewed partition
during execution"] S["Split into smaller partitions"] P["Process in parallel"] end D --> S --> P subgraph "Configuration" C1["spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = true"] C2["spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true"] C3["spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor = 5
partition is skewed if 5x median"] C4["spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes = 256MB"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Automatic - no code changes"] B2["Dynamic based on actual data"] B3["Also optimizes partition count"] end style C1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style C2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Solution 4: Handle Nulls Separately
steps.explanation: NULL values are a common source of skew. Handle them separately by filtering out nulls, processing them without a shuffle, and unioning the results back.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem: NULL Skew" P1["df.groupBy category .agg ..."] P2["90 percent of rows have NULL category"] P3["One partition gets 90 percent of data"] end subgraph "Solution: Separate NULL Handling" S1["Split data"] S2["Non-null: Regular groupBy"] S3["Nulls: Process separately
no groupBy needed!"] S4["Union results"] end subgraph "Code Pattern" Code["# Split
df_with_cat = df.filter col category .isNotNull
df_null_cat = df.filter col category .isNull

# Process separately
result1 = df_with_cat.groupBy category .agg ...
result2 = df_null_cat.agg ...
.withColumn category, lit None

# Combine
result = result1.union result2"] end style P3 fill:#FF5722,color:white style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Summary: Skew Fighting Toolkit
steps.explanation: Data skew is a common performance problem with multiple solutions. Start with AQE (easiest), then apply manual techniques for specific cases. Always verify improvement in Spark UI.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Detection" D1["1. Check Spark UI for stragglers"] D2["2. groupBy count to find hot keys"] D3["3. Monitor shuffle read sizes"] end subgraph "Solutions - Try in Order" S1["1. Enable AQE - Spark 3+
Automatic, no code changes"] S2["2. Filter out NULL/default values
Handle separately"] S3["3. Broadcast join small tables
Avoid shuffle entirely"] S4["4. Salting for aggregations
Distribute hot keys"] S5["5. Split + broadcast for skewed joins
Handle hot keys differently"] end subgraph "Verify" V1["Check task times are even"] V2["Shuffle sizes balanced"] V3["Overall job faster"] end style S1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style S2 fill:#C8E6C9 style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Gradient Boosting Fundamentals
fields.description: Understand how gradient boosting builds an ensemble by sequentially fitting trees to residuals

steps.title: The Core Idea
steps.explanation: **Gradient Boosting** builds an ensemble of weak learners (usually decision trees) sequentially. Each new tree learns to correct the mistakes of the previous ensemble. The final prediction is the sum of all trees' predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Gradient Boosting Ensemble" T1["Tree 1"] --> |"+"| Sum1["Sum"] T2["Tree 2"] --> |"+"| Sum1 T3["Tree 3"] --> |"+"| Sum1 T4["..."] --> |"+"| Sum1 TN["Tree N"] --> |"+"| Sum1 Sum1 --> Pred["Final Prediction"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["Each tree corrects errors
of previous trees"] end style T1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style T2 fill:#81C784 style T3 fill:#A5D6A7 style Pred fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Step 1: Initial Prediction
steps.explanation: Start with a simple initial prediction, typically the **mean of target values** for regression or **log-odds** for classification. This is F₀(x) - our baseline before any trees.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Training Data" D["Samples with targets:
y = [3, 7, 5, 9, 6]"] end subgraph "Initial Prediction F0" F0["F₀(x) = mean(y) = 6
Same prediction for all samples"] end subgraph "Initial Errors" E["Errors = y - F₀(x)
= [3-6, 7-6, 5-6, 9-6, 6-6]
= [-3, 1, -1, 3, 0]"] end D --> F0 --> E style F0 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style E fill:#FF9800

steps.title: Step 2: Compute Residuals (Pseudo-Residuals)
steps.explanation: Calculate the **residuals** (errors) between actual values and current predictions. For regression with MSE loss, residuals are simply: `residual = y - current_prediction`. These residuals become the **target for the next tree**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Current State" CP["Current Prediction: F(x) = 6 for all"] AV["Actual Values: [3, 7, 5, 9, 6]"] end subgraph "Compute Residuals" R1["Sample 1: 3 - 6 = -3"] R2["Sample 2: 7 - 6 = +1"] R3["Sample 3: 5 - 6 = -1"] R4["Sample 4: 9 - 6 = +3"] R5["Sample 5: 6 - 6 = 0"] end subgraph "Why Residuals?" W["Residuals point toward
the direction of improvement!
Negative = prediction too high
Positive = prediction too low"] end CP --> R1 CP --> R2 CP --> R3 CP --> R4 CP --> R5 style R1 fill:#FFCDD2 style R2 fill:#C8E6C9 style R3 fill:#FFCDD2 style R4 fill:#C8E6C9 style R5 fill:#E0E0E0

steps.title: Step 3: Fit a Tree to Residuals
steps.explanation: Train a new decision tree to **predict the residuals**, not the original target. This tree learns to predict how much to adjust the current prediction for each sample.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Tree 1 Training" Input["Features X"] Target["Target: Residuals [-3, 1, -1, 3, 0]"] Tree["Decision Tree"] Output["Learns to predict:
Which samples need +/-
adjustments"] end Input --> Tree Target --> Tree Tree --> Output subgraph "Tree Structure Example" Root["feature1 < 5?"] L["Predict: -2
(samples 1,3)"] R["feature2 < 3?"] RL["Predict: +0.5
(samples 2,5)"] RR["Predict: +3
(sample 4)"] Root --> |"Yes"| L Root --> |"No"| R R --> |"Yes"| RL R --> |"No"| RR end style Tree fill:#4CAF50,color:white style Target fill:#FF9800

steps.title: Step 4: Update Predictions with Learning Rate
steps.explanation: Add the tree's predictions to the ensemble, but **scaled by the learning rate** (η). A smaller learning rate means slower learning but better generalization. `F₁(x) = F₀(x) + η × Tree₁(x)`
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Update Formula" F["F₁(x) = F₀(x) + η × Tree₁(x)"] end subgraph "Example with η = 0.1" S1["Sample 1: 6 + 0.1 × (-2) = 5.8"] S2["Sample 2: 6 + 0.1 × (+0.5) = 6.05"] S3["Sample 3: 6 + 0.1 × (-2) = 5.8"] S4["Sample 4: 6 + 0.1 × (+3) = 6.3"] S5["Sample 5: 6 + 0.1 × (+0.5) = 6.05"] end subgraph "Why Learning Rate?" LR["Small steps prevent overfitting
Typical values: 0.01 - 0.3
Smaller η = more trees needed"] end F --> S1 F --> S2 F --> S3 F --> S4 F --> S5 style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style LR fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Step 5: Repeat Until Done
steps.explanation: Repeat steps 2-4: compute new residuals from updated predictions, fit another tree, update predictions. Stop when you reach `n_estimators` trees or early stopping triggers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Iteration Loop" Start["Current Ensemble F_m"] Res["Compute Residuals
r = y - F_m(x)"] Fit["Fit Tree_{m+1} to residuals"] Update["F_{m+1} = F_m + η × Tree_{m+1}"] Check{"Done?"} End["Final Model"] end Start --> Res --> Fit --> Update --> Check Check --> |"No"| Start Check --> |"Yes"| End subgraph "Stopping Criteria" SC1["n_estimators reached"] SC2["Early stopping: validation
score stopped improving"] SC3["Min improvement threshold"] end style Start fill:#4CAF50,color:white style End fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: The Additive Model
steps.explanation: The final model is an **additive ensemble**: the sum of the initial prediction plus all trees scaled by learning rate. Each tree contributes a small correction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Final Prediction Formula" Formula["F(x) = F₀(x) + η×T₁(x) + η×T₂(x) + ... + η×Tₙ(x)"] end subgraph "Visual Breakdown" F0["F₀ = 6.0
(baseline)"] T1["+ 0.1 × T₁
= +0.2"] T2["+ 0.1 × T₂
= +0.15"] T3["+ 0.1 × T₃
= +0.1"] TN["+ ... more trees"] Final["= 6.45 + ..."] end F0 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> TN --> Final subgraph "Key Properties" K1["Each tree is weak individually"] K2["Combined they are strong"] K3["Later trees have smaller impact"] end style Formula fill:#4CAF50,color:white style Final fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Why 'Gradient' Boosting?
steps.explanation: The residuals we fit are actually the **negative gradient of the loss function**. For MSE loss, gradient = prediction - actual, so negative gradient = actual - prediction = residual. This connects to gradient descent optimization!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Connection to Gradient Descent" GD["Gradient Descent:
θ = θ - η × ∇Loss"] GB["Gradient Boosting:
F = F + η × Tree(−∇Loss)"] end subgraph "For MSE Loss" MSE["Loss = ½(y - F)²"] Grad["∇Loss = -(y - F) = F - y"] NegGrad["-∇Loss = y - F = residual!"] end MSE --> Grad --> NegGrad subgraph "Generalization" G["Works with any differentiable loss:
• MSE for regression
• Log-loss for classification
• Custom losses"] end style GD fill:#E3F2FD style GB fill:#E3F2FD style NegGrad fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Let's trace through a complete mini-example showing how predictions improve over 3 iterations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data: y = [10, 20, 15]" I0["Iter 0: F₀ = 15 for all
Errors: [-5, 5, 0]"] I1["Iter 1: Tree predicts [-4, 4, 0]
F₁ = 15 + 0.5×[-4,4,0] = [13, 17, 15]
Errors: [-3, 3, 0]"] I2["Iter 2: Tree predicts [-2.5, 2.5, 0]
F₂ = [13,17,15] + 0.5×[-2.5,2.5,0]
= [11.75, 18.25, 15]
Errors: [-1.75, 1.75, 0]"] I3["Iter 3: Continue...
Errors shrink each iteration!"] end I0 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 subgraph "Error Reduction" E0["Iter 0: MSE = 16.67"] E1["Iter 1: MSE = 6.00"] E2["Iter 2: MSE = 2.04"] E3["Converging to 0!"] end style I0 fill:#FFCDD2 style I1 fill:#FFE0B2 style I2 fill:#FFF9C4 style I3 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Leaf-wise vs Level-wise Tree Growth
fields.description: Compare LightGBM's leaf-wise strategy with XGBoost's level-wise approach

steps.title: Two Strategies for Growing Trees
steps.explanation: When building a decision tree, we must decide which leaf to split next. **Level-wise** (XGBoost default) splits all leaves at the current depth. **Leaf-wise** (LightGBM) splits the leaf with the highest gain, regardless of depth.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["Given a tree with multiple leaves,
which leaf should we split next?"] end subgraph "Level-wise - XGBoost" LW["Split ALL leaves at current depth
Then move to next level"] end subgraph "Leaf-wise - LightGBM" LF["Split the ONE leaf with
highest potential gain"] end Q --> LW Q --> LF style LW fill:#FF9800,color:white style LF fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Level-wise Growth (XGBoost)
steps.explanation: **Level-wise** grows the tree horizontally, one level at a time. All nodes at depth d are split before any node at depth d+1. This creates balanced trees but may split nodes with little gain.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Level-wise Growth Steps" subgraph "Step 1: Depth 0" R1["Root
Split!"] end subgraph "Step 2: Depth 1" R2["Root"] R2 --> L2["Left
Split!"] R2 --> R2R["Right
Split!"] end subgraph "Step 3: Depth 2" R3["Root"] R3 --> L3["L"] R3 --> R3R["R"] L3 --> LL["LL
Split!"] L3 --> LR["LR
Split!"] R3R --> RL["RL
Split!"] R3R --> RR["RR
Split!"] end end style R1 fill:#FF9800 style L2 fill:#FF9800 style R2R fill:#FF9800 style LL fill:#FF9800 style LR fill:#FF9800 style RL fill:#FF9800 style RR fill:#FF9800

steps.title: Leaf-wise Growth (LightGBM)
steps.explanation: **Leaf-wise** always splits the leaf with the **maximum gain reduction**, creating asymmetric trees. This is more efficient - why split a leaf with gain=0.1 when another has gain=10?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Leaf-wise Growth Steps" subgraph "Step 1" S1R["Root
gain=10"] S1R --> S1L["Left
gain=8"] S1R --> S1RR["Right
gain=2"] S1Note["Split Left - highest gain!"] end subgraph "Step 2" S2R["Root"] S2R --> S2L["L"] S2R --> S2RR["Right
gain=2"] S2L --> S2LL["LL
