mlflow.log_params(params)"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["Log ALL hyperparameters"] B2["Include data version/path"] B3["Log random seeds"] B4["Use consistent naming"] end style M fill:#E3F2FD style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Logging Metrics
steps.explanation: **Metrics** are output measurements (accuracy, loss, etc.). Log them during and after training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Metric" S["mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', 0.95)
mlflow.log_metric('f1_score', 0.92)"] end subgraph "Multiple Metrics" M["mlflow.log_metrics({
'accuracy': 0.95,
'precision': 0.93,
'recall': 0.91
})"] end subgraph "Metrics Over Time" T["for epoch in range(100):
loss = train_epoch()
mlflow.log_metric('loss', loss, step=epoch)

# Creates a time series!"] end subgraph "View in UI" V["MLflow UI shows charts
for step-based metrics"] end T --> V style T fill:#E3F2FD style V fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Logging Artifacts
steps.explanation: **Artifacts** are output files: models, plots, data files, anything you want to save with the run.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Log Single File" F["mlflow.log_artifact('model.pkl')
mlflow.log_artifact('confusion_matrix.png')"] end subgraph "Log Directory" D["mlflow.log_artifacts('output_dir/')
# Logs all files in directory"] end subgraph "Log with Subdirectory" S["mlflow.log_artifact(
'plot.png',
artifact_path='figures'
)
# Creates figures/plot.png"] end subgraph "Common Artifacts" A1["Trained models"] A2["Plots and visualizations"] A3["Predictions CSV"] A4["Feature importance"] A5["Config files"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Using Tags
steps.explanation: **Tags** add searchable metadata to runs. Use them for filtering and organization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setting Tags" S["mlflow.set_tag('model_type', 'xgboost')
mlflow.set_tag('dataset', 'v2.1')
mlflow.set_tag('team', 'fraud-detection')"] end subgraph "Multiple Tags" M["mlflow.set_tags({
'model_type': 'xgboost',
'feature_set': 'v3',
'priority': 'high'
})"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Filter runs in UI"] U2["Group by team/project"] U3["Mark experimental vs production"] U4["Track data versions"] end subgraph "Search by Tag" SR["mlflow.search_runs(
filter_string="tags.team='fraud-detection'"
)"] end style M fill:#E3F2FD style SR fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Complete Tracking Example
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: a complete training script with MLflow tracking.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Full Example" C["import mlflow
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

mlflow.set_experiment('customer-churn')

with mlflow.start_run(run_name='rf-baseline'):
# Log parameters
params = {'n_estimators': 100, 'max_depth': 10}
mlflow.log_params(params)
mlflow.set_tag('model_type', 'random_forest')

# Train
model = RandomForestClassifier(**params)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Log metrics
acc = model.score(X_test, y_test)
mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', acc)

# Log model
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')"] end subgraph "Result" R["Run with params, metrics, model
all linked and versioned"] end C --> R style C fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Querying Runs
steps.explanation: Search and compare runs programmatically using the MLflow API.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Search Runs" S["# Get all runs from experiment
runs = mlflow.search_runs(
experiment_names=['customer-churn']
)

# Filter by metric
runs = mlflow.search_runs(
filter_string='metrics.accuracy > 0.9'
)

# Filter by parameter
runs = mlflow.search_runs(
filter_string="params.model_type = 'xgboost'"
)"] end subgraph "Get Best Run" B["best = mlflow.search_runs(
order_by=['metrics.accuracy DESC'],
max_results=1
)"] end subgraph "Load Run Data" L["run = mlflow.get_run(run_id)
params = run.data.params
metrics = run.data.metrics"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: MLflow Run Lifecycle
fields.description: Understand run states, nested runs, and parent-child relationships

steps.title: Run States
steps.explanation: An MLflow run goes through different states during its lifecycle. Understanding these helps with debugging and monitoring.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Run States" R["RUNNING
Active execution"] F["FINISHED
Completed successfully"] FL["FAILED
Error occurred"] K["KILLED
Manually terminated"] end R --> |"Success"| F R --> |"Error"| FL R --> |"Terminated"| K style R fill:#2196F3,color:white style F fill:#4CAF50,color:white style FL fill:#F44336,color:white style K fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: Run Lifecycle Flow
steps.explanation: A run starts when you call `start_run()` and ends when the context exits or you call `end_run()`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Lifecycle" S["mlflow.start_run()"] R["RUNNING
- Log params
- Log metrics
- Log artifacts"] E["mlflow.end_run()"] D["FINISHED / FAILED"] end S --> R R --> E E --> D subgraph "With Context Manager" CM["with mlflow.start_run():
# RUNNING
do_stuff()
# Auto end_run() here"] end subgraph "Without Context Manager" NC["run = mlflow.start_run()
# RUNNING
do_stuff()
mlflow.end_run() # Don't forget!"] end style CM fill:#C8E6C9 style NC fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: The Active Run
steps.explanation: MLflow tracks the **active run** - the run that receives all logging calls. Only one run can be active at a time (per thread).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Active Run Concept" A["mlflow.active_run()
Returns current active run or None"] end subgraph "Example" E["with mlflow.start_run():
run = mlflow.active_run()
print(run.info.run_id) # abc123

mlflow.log_metric('x', 1) # Goes to abc123

mlflow.active_run() # None - no active run"] end subgraph "Important" I["All mlflow.log_* calls go to
the currently active run"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Nested Runs
steps.explanation: **Nested runs** create parent-child relationships. Great for hyperparameter tuning where each config is a child of the main experiment.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Nested Run Structure" P["Parent Run
'hyperparameter-search'"] C1["Child Run 1
lr=0.01"] C2["Child Run 2
lr=0.001"] C3["Child Run 3
lr=0.1"] end P --> C1 P --> C2 P --> C3 subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Hyperparameter tuning"] U2["Cross-validation folds"] U3["Ensemble model training"] U4["Multi-stage pipelines"] end style P fill:#4CAF50,color:white style C1 fill:#81C784 style C2 fill:#81C784 style C3 fill:#81C784

steps.title: Creating Nested Runs
steps.explanation: Use `nested=True` to create child runs within a parent run context.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" C["with mlflow.start_run(run_name='hp-search') as parent:
mlflow.log_param('search_space', 'grid')

for lr in [0.01, 0.001, 0.1]:
with mlflow.start_run(
run_name=f'lr-{lr}',
nested=True # KEY!
) as child:
mlflow.log_param('lr', lr)
acc = train_model(lr)
mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', acc)"] end subgraph "Result in UI" R["Parent: hp-search
├── Child: lr-0.01 (acc=0.92)
├── Child: lr-0.001 (acc=0.89)
└── Child: lr-0.1 (acc=0.88)"] end C --> R style C fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Resuming Runs
steps.explanation: You can resume an existing run by passing its run_id. Useful for long-running experiments or adding data later.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Resume a Run" R["# Get run_id from previous run
run_id = 'abc123def456'

# Resume it
with mlflow.start_run(run_id=run_id):
# Continue logging to same run
mlflow.log_metric('final_accuracy', 0.97)
mlflow.log_artifact('final_model.pkl')"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Add final metrics after validation"] U2["Log artifacts computed later"] U3["Fix missing parameters"] U4["Resume interrupted training"] end subgraph "Note" N["Run must be in FINISHED state
to resume (not RUNNING)"] end style R fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Run Information
steps.explanation: Each run has metadata accessible via `run.info` and data via `run.data`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "run.info" I1["run_id: Unique identifier"] I2["experiment_id: Parent experiment"] I3["status: RUNNING/FINISHED/etc"] I4["start_time: When started"] I5["end_time: When ended"] I6["artifact_uri: Where artifacts stored"] end subgraph "run.data" D1["params: Dict of parameters"] D2["metrics: Dict of latest metrics"] D3["tags: Dict of tags"] end subgraph "Access Example" E["run = mlflow.get_run(run_id)
print(run.info.status)
print(run.data.params['lr'])
print(run.data.metrics['accuracy'])"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Run Context Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for managing run lifecycle effectively.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Always use context managers"] D2["Name your runs meaningfully"] D3["Use nested runs for HP tuning"] D4["Log early and often"] D5["Set tags for organization"] end subgraph "Don't" N1["Don't forget end_run()"] N2["Don't create runs in loops
without nested=True"] N3["Don't resume RUNNING runs"] end subgraph "Error Handling" E["with mlflow.start_run():
try:
train()
except Exception as e:
mlflow.set_tag('error', str(e))
raise # Run ends as FAILED"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style N1 fill:#FFCDD2 style E fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: MLflow Artifact Organization
fields.description: Learn artifact storage patterns, paths, and retrieval strategies

steps.title: What are Artifacts?
steps.explanation: **Artifacts** are any files produced by your ML run: trained models, plots, data files, configs, etc. MLflow stores them alongside run metadata.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Common Artifacts"
        A1["Trained models (.pkl, .h5, .pt)"]
        A2["Visualizations (.png, .html)"]
        A3["Data samples (.csv, .parquet)"]
        A4["Configuration files (.yaml, .json)"]
        A5["Evaluation reports (.txt, .md)"]
        A6["Feature importance plots"]
    end
    subgraph "Why Use Artifacts?"
        W1["Version control outputs"]
        W2["Reproduce results"]
        W3["Share with team"]
        W4["Deploy models"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#4CAF50
    style A6 fill:#2196F3

steps.title: Artifact Storage Locations
steps.explanation: Artifacts are stored based on your tracking server configuration. Can be local filesystem or cloud storage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Local Storage" L["./mlruns///artifacts/

Default when using local tracking"] end subgraph "Remote Storage" R1["s3://bucket/mlflow/artifacts/..."] R2["gs://bucket/mlflow/artifacts/..."] R3["azure://container/mlflow/..."] R4["dbfs:/databricks/mlflow/..."] end subgraph "Configuration" C["# Set artifact location per experiment
mlflow.create_experiment(
'my-exp',
artifact_location='s3://my-bucket/exp1'
)

# Or set default for tracking server
mlflow server --default-artifact-root s3://..."] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Logging Artifacts
steps.explanation: Use `log_artifact()` for single files and `log_artifacts()` for directories.

graph TB subgraph "Single File" S["# Log a file

# File goes to: artifacts/model.pkl"] end subgraph "Directory" D["# Log all files in a directory
mlflow.log_artifacts('output/')

# Contents go to: artifacts/
# (preserves directory structure)"] end subgraph "With Subdirectory" SD["# Organize into subdirectories
mlflow.log_artifact(
'plot.png',
artifact_path='figures'
)

# Goes to: artifacts/figures/plot.png"] end subgraph "Result" R["artifacts/
├── model.pkl
├── figures/
│ └── plot.png
└── data/
└── predictions.csv"] end style SD fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#E3F2FD

# Data

├── model/

steps.explanation: MLflow has special methods for logging models that include metadata and enable easy deployment.

Just saves the file

)

steps.title: Artifact Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for effective artifact management.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use artifact_path for organization"] D2["Log models with mlflow..log_model"] D3["Log config files for reproducibility"] D4["Use meaningful file names"] D5["Log sample predictions"] end subgraph "Don't" N1["Don't log huge datasets"] N2["Don't log temporary files"] N3["Don't log sensitive data"] N4["Don't skip model signature"] end subgraph "Storage Tips" T["Large artifacts → Use cloud storage

fields.title: MLflow Model Registry Lifecycle
fields.description: Master model versioning, staging, and production transitions

steps.title: What is the Model Registry?
steps.explanation: The **Model Registry** is a centralized store for managing ML models: versioning, staging, and deployment tracking.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P1["Which model version is in production?"] P2["Who approved this model?"] P3["What's the history of changes?"] P4["Which version should I roll back to?"] end subgraph "Solution: Model Registry" R["Centralized model hub
- Version control
- Stage management
- Transition history
- Annotations"] end P1 --> R P2 --> R P3 --> R P4 --> R style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Registry Concepts
steps.explanation: The registry organizes models by **Registered Model** (the name) containing multiple **Versions**, each in a **Stage**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hierarchy" RM["Registered Model
'FraudDetector'"] V1["Version 1"] V2["Version 2"] V3["Version 3"] end RM --> V1 RM --> V2 RM --> V3 subgraph "Stages" S1["None - Just registered"] S2["Staging - Testing"] S3["Production - Live"] S4["Archived - Retired"] end V1 --> S4 V2 --> S3 V3 --> S2 style RM fill:#2196F3,color:white style V2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style V3 fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: Registering a Model
steps.explanation: Register models from a run's artifacts or directly during training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Method 1: During Training" M1["with mlflow.start_run():
# Train model...
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(
model,
'model',
registered_model_name='FraudDetector'
)"] end subgraph "Method 2: After Training" M2["# Register from existing run
result = mlflow.register_model(
'runs:/abc123/model',
'FraudDetector'
)

print(result.version) # 1, 2, 3..."] end subgraph "Result" R["Creates:
- Registered Model (if new name)
- New Version (always)
- Stage: None"] end M1 --> R M2 --> R style M1 fill:#E3F2FD style M2 fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Model Stages Explained
steps.explanation: Stages indicate where a model version is in the deployment lifecycle.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Stage Progression" N["None
Just registered"] S["Staging
Testing/validation"] P["Production
Serving live traffic"] A["Archived
Retired"] end N --> |"Promote"| S S --> |"Approve"| P P --> |"Replace"| A S --> |"Reject"| A subgraph "Rules" R1["Multiple versions can be in Staging"] R2["Only ONE version in Production"] R3["Archived = soft delete"] end style N fill:#E0E0E0 style S fill:#FF9800,color:white style P fill:#4CAF50,color:white style A fill:#9E9E9E,color:white

steps.title: Transitioning Stages
steps.explanation: Move models between stages using the API or UI.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Python API" C["from mlflow import MlflowClient
client = MlflowClient()

# Promote to Staging
client.transition_model_version_stage(
name='FraudDetector',
version=3,
stage='Staging'
)

# Promote to Production
client.transition_model_version_stage(
name='FraudDetector',
version=3,
stage='Production'
)"] end subgraph "Archive Old Version" A["# When promoting new version to Production
# old Production version auto-archived?
# No! You must do it manually
client.transition_model_version_stage(
name='FraudDetector',
version=2,
stage='Archived'
)"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Loading Registered Models
steps.explanation: Load models from the registry by name and version or stage.

steps.explanation: End-to-end workflow from training to production deployment.

fields.title: MLflow Tracking Server Architecture
fields.description: Understand backend store, artifact store, and deployment scenarios

steps.title: Tracking Server Components
steps.explanation: The MLflow Tracking Server has two storage components: **Backend Store** for metadata and **Artifact Store** for files.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Tracking Server" TS["MLflow Tracking Server"] end subgraph "Backend Store" BS["Stores metadata:
- Experiments
- Runs
- Parameters
- Metrics
- Tags"] end subgraph "Artifact Store" AS["Stores files:
- Models
- Plots
- Data files
- Any artifacts"] end TS --> BS TS --> AS subgraph "Options" O1["Backend: File, SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL"] O2["Artifacts: Local, S3, GCS, Azure, DBFS"] end style TS fill:#4CAF50,color:white style BS fill:#2196F3,color:white style AS fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: Scenario 1: Local Mode (No Server)
steps.explanation: Simplest setup: no server, everything stored locally. Good for individual experimentation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Your Code" C["import mlflow
mlflow.log_param('lr', 0.01)
mlflow.log_metric('acc', 0.95)"] end subgraph "Local Storage" L["./mlruns/
├── 0/ (default experiment)
│ └── /
│ ├── meta.yaml
│ ├── params/
│ ├── metrics/
│ └── artifacts/"] end subgraph "Local UI" U["mlflow ui
→ Reads from ./mlruns"] end C --> L L --> U subgraph "Pros/Cons" P["✓ No setup required
✓ Good for learning
✗ Not shareable
✗ No central server"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Scenario 2: Tracking Server with SQLite
steps.explanation: Run a tracking server with SQLite backend. Shareable but still single-file database.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Start Server" S["mlflow server \
--backend-store-uri sqlite:///mlflow.db \
--default-artifact-root ./mlartifacts \
--host 0.0.0.0"] end subgraph "Client Code" C["import mlflow
mlflow.set_tracking_uri('http://server:5000')

with mlflow.start_run():
mlflow.log_metric('acc', 0.95)"] end subgraph "Storage" DB["mlflow.db
(SQLite)"] AR["./mlartifacts/
(local files)"] end C --> S S --> DB S --> AR subgraph "Pros/Cons" P["✓ Shareable via network
✓ UI at server:5000
✗ SQLite doesn't scale
✗ Artifacts still local"] end style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Scenario 3: Production Setup
steps.explanation: Production-grade: PostgreSQL backend + S3 artifacts. Scales for teams.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Start Server" S["mlflow server \
--backend-store-uri postgresql://user:pass@host/mlflow \
--default-artifact-root s3://my-bucket/mlartifacts \
--host 0.0.0.0"] end subgraph "Clients" C1["Data Scientist 1"] C2["Data Scientist 2"] C3["Training Pipeline"] end subgraph "Backend" PG["PostgreSQL
Experiments, runs, metrics"] end subgraph "Artifacts" S3["S3 Bucket
Models, plots, files"] end C1 --> S C2 --> S C3 --> S S --> PG S --> S3 subgraph "Pros" P["✓ Scales to large teams
✓ Reliable database
✓ Durable artifact storage
✓ Load balanceable"] end style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white style PG fill:#2196F3,color:white style S3 fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: Scenario 4: Proxied Artifact Access
steps.explanation: Server proxies artifact requests. Clients don't need direct cloud access.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Proxy" WO["Client needs S3 credentials
Uploads directly to S3
Security concern!"] end subgraph "With Proxy" S["mlflow server \
--serve-artifacts \
--artifacts-destination s3://bucket"] C["Client"] P["Server proxies

steps.title: What is Auto-logging?

mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')

Lots of manual logging!"] end subgraph "With Autolog" A["mlflow.autolog() # That's it!
model.fit(X, y)

Params, metrics, model logged automatically!"] end W --> |"vs"| A style W fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Pattern: Nested Runs for HP Search
steps.explanation: Use **nested runs** to organize HP tuning: parent run for the search, child runs for each trial.

'grid-search-rf'

steps.title: Manual HP Tuning with MLflow
steps.explanation: Basic pattern for logging grid/random search manually.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code Pattern" C["import mlflow
from sklearn.model_selection import ParameterGrid

param_grid = {'n_estimators': [50, 100], 'max_depth': [5, 10]}

with mlflow.start_run(run_name='grid-search') as parent:
mlflow.log_param('search_type', 'grid')
best_acc, best_params = 0, None

for params in ParameterGrid(param_grid):
with mlflow.start_run(nested=True) as child:
mlflow.log_params(params)
model = RandomForestClassifier(**params)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
acc = model.score(X_test, y_test)
mlflow.log_metric('accuracy', acc)
if acc > best_acc:
best_acc, best_params = acc, params

mlflow.log_params({f'best_{k}': v for k, v in best_params.items()})
mlflow.log_metric('best_accuracy', best_acc)"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Integration with Optuna
steps.explanation: Optuna has built-in MLflow integration via `MLflowCallback`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Optuna + MLflow" C["import mlflow
import optuna
from optuna.integration.mlflow import MLflowCallback

def objective(trial):
lr = trial.suggest_float('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)
depth = trial.suggest_int('max_depth', 3, 10)

model = XGBClassifier(learning_rate=lr, max_depth=depth)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
return model.score(X_test, y_test)

mlflow_callback = MLflowCallback(
tracking_uri='http://localhost:5000',
metric_name='accuracy'
)

study = optuna.create_study(direction='maximize')
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=50, callbacks=[mlflow_callback])"] end subgraph "What Gets Logged" W["Each trial becomes a run with:
- All suggested parameters
- Objective value as metric
- Trial number tag"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style W fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Integration with Ray Tune
steps.explanation: Ray Tune supports MLflow via `MLflowLoggerCallback`.

from ray.tune.integration.mlflow import MLflowLoggerCallback

def trainable(config):
model = XGBClassifier(**config)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
acc = model.score(X_test, y_test)
tune.report(accuracy=acc)

analysis = tune.run(
trainable,
config={
'learning_rate': tune.loguniform(1e-4, 1e-1),
'max_depth': tune.randint(3, 10)
},
num_samples=50,
callbacks=[MLflowLoggerCallback(
tracking_uri='http://localhost:5000',
experiment_name='ray-tune-xgb'
)]
)"] end subgraph "Features" F["- Each trial = one MLflow run
- Intermediate metrics logged
- Ray trial info as tags"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style F fill:#C8E6C9

)"] end subgraph "Or Use UI" U["MLflow UI → Parent run → Child runs
→ Sort by accuracy → Register best"] end F --> R style F fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: End-to-end HP tuning with MLflow tracking.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Full Code" C["import mlflow
import optuna

mlflow.set_experiment('xgb-hp-tuning')

def objective(trial):
with mlflow.start_run(nested=True):
params = {
'learning_rate': trial.suggest_float('lr', 1e-3, 0.3, log=True),
'max_depth': trial.suggest_int('depth', 3, 10),
'n_estimators': trial.suggest_int('n_est', 50, 500)
}
mlflow.log_params(params)

model = XGBClassifier(**params)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
acc = model.score(X_val, y_val)

mlflow.log_metric('val_accuracy', acc)
mlflow.xgboost.log_model(model, 'model')
return acc

with mlflow.start_run(run_name='optuna-search'):
study = optuna.create_study(direction='maximize')
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)

mlflow.log_params({f'best_{k}': v for k, v in study.best_params.items()})
mlflow.log_metric('best_val_accuracy', study.best_value)"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Central Limit Theorem
fields.description: Why sample means become normal, regardless of population distribution

steps.title: The Magic of the CLT
steps.explanation: The **Central Limit Theorem** states that the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, *regardless* of the population's original distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Population (Any Shape!)" P1["Uniform"] P2["Exponential"] P3["Bimodal"] P4["Skewed"] end subgraph "Take Many Samples" S["Draw samples of size n
Calculate mean of each"] end subgraph "Distribution of Sample Means" N["Always approaches
NORMAL distribution!"] end P1 --> S P2 --> S P3 --> S P4 --> S S --> N style N fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Why Does This Matter?
steps.explanation: The CLT is the foundation of statistical inference. It allows us to make probabilistic statements about population parameters using sample statistics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" PR["We want to know population mean μ
But we can only observe samples"] end subgraph "CLT Solution" S1["Sample means are normally distributed"] S2["Centered at true population mean μ"] S3["Standard error = σ/√n"] end subgraph "This Enables" E1["Confidence intervals"] E2["Hypothesis testing"] E3["Statistical inference"] end PR --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 --> E1 S3 --> E2 S3 --> E3 style S1 fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: The CLT Formula
steps.explanation: As n increases, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches N(μ, σ²/n).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Population" POP["Mean: μ
Std Dev: σ
Any distribution"] end subgraph "Sampling Distribution of x̄" SD["Mean: μ (same as population)
Std Dev: σ/√n (Standard Error)
Shape: Normal (for large n)"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["As n increases:
• SE decreases (more precise)
• Shape becomes more normal"] end POP --> SD SD --> K style SD fill:#E3F2FD style K fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: How Large is 'Large Enough'?
steps.explanation: The rule of thumb is n ≥ 30, but it depends on how non-normal the population is.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sample Size Guidelines" G1["Normal population: n = 1 is enough"] G2["Symmetric population: n ≥ 15"] G3["Moderately skewed: n ≥ 30"] G4["Highly skewed: n ≥ 50+"] end subgraph "Visual" V["n=5: Still looks like population
n=10: Starting to normalize
n=30: Approximately normal
n=100: Very normal"] end style G3 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style V fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Standard Error Explained
steps.explanation: The **Standard Error (SE)** measures how much sample means vary from the true mean. It decreases with larger samples.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Standard Error Formula" F["SE = σ / √n"] end subgraph "Example: σ = 10" E1["n = 4: SE = 10/2 = 5"] E2["n = 25: SE = 10/5 = 2"] E3["n = 100: SE = 10/10 = 1"] E4["n = 400: SE = 10/20 = 0.5"] end subgraph "Diminishing Returns" D["To halve SE, must quadruple n
n: 25 → 100 → 400 → 1600"] end F --> E1 E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 E4 --> D style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style D fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: CLT in Action: Simulation
steps.explanation: Watch how the distribution of sample means becomes normal as we take more samples.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Step 1: Population" P["Exponential distribution
(highly right-skewed)"] end subgraph "Step 2: Draw Samples" S["Draw 1000 samples
Each of size n=30
Calculate mean of each"] end subgraph "Step 3: Plot Means" M["1000 sample means
Form a histogram"] end subgraph "Result" R["Histogram is bell-shaped!
Centered at population mean
Width = SE = σ/√30"] end P --> S --> M --> R style P fill:#FF9800 style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Practical Applications
steps.explanation: The CLT enables most of statistical inference in practice.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Confidence Intervals" CI["x̄ ± z*(σ/√n)
Works because x̄ is normal"] end subgraph "Hypothesis Testing" HT["z = (x̄ - μ₀)/(σ/√n)
Compare to normal distribution"] end subgraph "Quality Control" QC["Monitor process means
Control charts assume normality"] end subgraph "Polling" PO["Survey results ± margin of error
Based on CLT"] end style CI fill:#2196F3,color:white style HT fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: CLT Assumptions & Limitations
steps.explanation: The CLT requires certain conditions to hold.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Requirements" R1["Random sampling"] R2["Independent observations"] R3["Finite variance (σ² < ∞)"] R4["Large enough n"] end subgraph "Does NOT Apply When" N1["Heavy-tailed distributions
(infinite variance)"] N2["Dependent observations
(time series)"] N3["Small samples from
highly skewed populations"] end subgraph "Alternatives" A["Bootstrap methods
Non-parametric tests"] end style R1 fill:#C8E6C9 style N1 fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Probability Distributions Overview
fields.description: Normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential - when to use each

steps.title: Two Types of Distributions
steps.explanation: Probability distributions describe how likely different outcomes are. They come in two main types based on the variable type.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Discrete" D["Countable outcomes
0, 1, 2, 3, ..."] D1["Binomial"] D2["Poisson"] D3["Geometric"] end subgraph "Continuous" C["Any value in range
1.5, 2.718, π, ..."] C1["Normal"] C2["Exponential"] C3["Uniform"] end D --> D1 D --> D2 D --> D3 C --> C1 C --> C2 C --> C3 style D fill:#2196F3,color:white style C fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
steps.explanation: The bell curve. Most common in nature due to CLT. Defined by mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Normal Distribution" N["N(μ, σ²)
Symmetric bell curve
68-95-99.7 rule"] end subgraph "When to Use" W1["Heights, weights, IQ scores"] W2["Measurement errors"] W3["Sum of many small effects"] W4["Sample means (via CLT)"] end subgraph "Key Properties" K1["Mean = Median = Mode"] K2["Tails extend to ±∞"] K3["~68% within 1σ of mean"] K4["~95% within 2σ of mean"] end N --> W1 N --> W2 N --> W3 N --> W4 style N fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Binomial Distribution
steps.explanation: Count of successes in n independent yes/no trials, each with probability p.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Binomial Distribution" B["B(n, p)
n trials, p = P(success)"] end subgraph "Requirements" R1["Fixed number of trials (n)"] R2["Each trial: success or failure"] R3["Constant probability (p)"] R4["Independent trials"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Coin flips: heads in 10 flips"] E2["Defects: faulty items in batch of 100"] E3["Clicks: conversions from 1000 views"] end subgraph "Parameters" P["Mean = np
Variance = np(1-p)"] end B --> R1 R4 --> E1 style B fill:#2196F3,color:white style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Poisson Distribution
steps.explanation: Count of events in a fixed interval when events occur at a constant average rate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Poisson Distribution" P["Pois(λ)
λ = average rate"] end subgraph "Requirements" R1["Events occur independently"] R2["Average rate is constant"] R3["Two events can't happen
at exact same instant"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Calls to call center per hour"] E2["Typos per page"] E3["Customers arriving per minute"] E4["Accidents per month"] end subgraph "Properties" PR["Mean = λ
Variance = λ
(Mean equals Variance!)"] end P --> R1 R3 --> E1 style P fill:#FF9800,color:white style PR fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Exponential Distribution
steps.explanation: Time between events in a Poisson process. The continuous counterpart to Poisson.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Exponential Distribution" E["Exp(λ)
λ = rate parameter"] end subgraph "What It Models" W1["Time until next event"] W2["Lifetime of components"] W3["Wait time in queue"] end subgraph "Key Property: Memoryless" M["P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t)

'Past doesn't affect future'
If you've waited 5 min, expected
additional wait is still the same"] end subgraph "Parameters" P["Mean = 1/λ
Variance = 1/λ²"] end E --> W1 E --> W2 E --> W3 style E fill:#9C27B0,color:white style M fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Choosing the Right Distribution
steps.explanation: A decision tree for selecting the appropriate distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Q1["What type of variable?"]
    Q1 --> |"Discrete"| Q2["Counting what?"]
    Q1 --> |"Continuous"| Q3["What phenomenon?"]

    Q2 --> |"Successes in n trials"| B["Binomial"]
    Q2 --> |"Events in interval"| P["Poisson"]
    Q2 --> |"Trials until success"| G["Geometric"]

    Q3 --> |"Natural measurements"| N["Normal"]
    Q3 --> |"Time between events"| E["Exponential"]
    Q3 --> |"Equal likelihood"| U["Uniform"]

    style B fill:#2196F3,color:white
    style P fill:#FF9800,color:white
    style N fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style E fill:#9C27B0,color:white

steps.title: Relationships Between Distributions
steps.explanation: Many distributions are related through limits and special cases.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Approximations" A1["Binomial → Normal
when n large, p not extreme"] A2["Binomial → Poisson
when n large, p small, np moderate"] A3["Poisson → Normal
when λ large"] end subgraph "Connections" C1["Exponential: time between Poisson events"] C2["Gamma: sum of Exponentials"] C3["Chi-square: sum of squared Normals"] end subgraph "Rule of Thumb" R["Binomial ≈ Normal if np ≥ 10 and n(1-p) ≥ 10
Binomial ≈ Poisson if n ≥ 20 and p ≤ 0.05
Poisson ≈ Normal if λ ≥ 10"] end style A1 fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Quick Reference Card
steps.explanation: Summary of key distributions and their use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Distribution | Use When | Parameters" T["Normal | Continuous, symmetric data | μ, σ
Binomial | Count successes in n trials | n, p
Poisson | Count events in fixed interval | λ
Exponential| Time until next event | λ
Uniform | All outcomes equally likely | a, b
Geometric | Trials until first success | p"] end subgraph "ML Applications" M1["Normal: Linear regression errors"] M2["Binomial: Classification outcomes"] M3["Poisson: Count predictions"] M4["Exponential: Survival analysis"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Hypothesis Testing Framework
fields.description: Null/alternative hypotheses, p-values, Type I/II errors

steps.title: The Hypothesis Testing Question
steps.explanation: Hypothesis testing answers: 'Is this observed effect real, or could it be due to random chance?'
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Scenario" S["You observe something in your data
e.g., Treatment group improved by 5%"] end subgraph "The Question" Q["Is this real improvement?
Or just random variation?"] end subgraph "Hypothesis Testing" H["A formal framework to decide
using probability theory"] end S --> Q --> H style Q fill:#FFF9C4 style H fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: The Two Hypotheses
steps.explanation: We always test two competing claims: the null hypothesis (H₀) and alternative hypothesis (H₁ or Hₐ).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Null Hypothesis H₀" N["The 'nothing special' hypothesis
No effect, no difference
Status quo is true"] end subgraph "Alternative Hypothesis H₁" A["What you want to prove
There IS an effect
Something interesting happened"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["H₀: Drug has no effect (μ = 0)"] E2["H₁: Drug has an effect (μ ≠ 0)"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["We assume H₀ is true
Then ask: How likely is our data?"] end style N fill:#E0E0E0 style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style K fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: The P-Value Explained
steps.explanation: The **p-value** is the probability of observing data as extreme as ours, *assuming H₀ is true*.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "P-Value Definition"
        D["P(observing this data | H₀ is true)"]
    end
    subgraph "Interpretation"
        I1["Small p-value (e.g., 0.01)"]
        I2["Data is unlikely under H₀"]
        I3["Evidence against H₀"]
    end
    subgraph "NOT What P-Value Means"
        N1["NOT: P(H₀ is true)"]
        N2["NOT: P(results are due to chance)"]
        N3["NOT: Effect size or importance"]
    end
    D --> I1 --> I2 --> I3
    style D fill:#2196F3,color:white
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style N2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style N3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Significance Level (α)
steps.explanation: We set a threshold α (commonly 0.05) before the test. If p < α, we reject H₀.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Decision Rule" R["If p-value < α: Reject H₀
If p-value ≥ α: Fail to reject H₀"] end subgraph "Common α Values" A1["α = 0.05 (5%) - Standard"] A2["α = 0.01 (1%) - Stringent"] A3["α = 0.10 (10%) - Lenient"] end subgraph "Example" E["α = 0.05, p-value = 0.03
0.03 < 0.05 → Reject H₀
'Statistically significant'"] end subgraph "Warning" W["α must be set BEFORE
looking at the data!"] end style A1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style E fill:#C8E6C9 style W fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Type I and Type II Errors
steps.explanation: Two types of mistakes we can make in hypothesis testing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Reality vs Decision" T[" | H₀ True | H₀ False
----------------|------------|------------
Reject H₀ | Type I (α) | Correct ✓
Fail to Reject | Correct ✓ | Type II (β)"] end subgraph "Type I Error (α)" T1["False Positive
Reject H₀ when it's true
See effect that doesn't exist"] end subgraph "Type II Error (β)" T2["False Negative
Fail to reject H₀ when it's false
Miss real effect"] end subgraph "Power = 1 - β" P["Probability of correctly
detecting a real effect"] end style T1 fill:#FFCDD2 style T2 fill:#FFE0B2 style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: The Testing Procedure
steps.explanation: Step-by-step process for conducting a hypothesis test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB S1["1. State hypotheses
H₀ and H₁"] S2["2. Choose α
(usually 0.05)"] S3["3. Collect data
Calculate test statistic"] S4["4. Find p-value
From test statistic"] S5["5. Make decision
Compare p to α"] S6["6. State conclusion
In context"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 style S1 fill:#E3F2FD style S5 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: One-Tailed vs Two-Tailed Tests
steps.explanation: The direction of your alternative hypothesis determines the type of test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Two-Tailed" TT["H₁: μ ≠ μ₀
Effect could be + or -
Reject if too high OR too low"] end subgraph "One-Tailed (Right)" RT["H₁: μ > μ₀
Only interested in increase
Reject if too high"] end subgraph "One-Tailed (Left)" LT["H₁: μ < μ₀
Only interested in decrease
Reject if too low"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["Two-tailed: Default choice
One-tailed: Only if direction
specified BEFORE seeing data"] end style TT fill:#4CAF50,color:white style W fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Common Misconceptions
steps.explanation: Avoiding the most frequent misunderstandings about hypothesis testing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Wrong"
        W1["p = 0.03 means 3% chance H₀ is true"]
        W2["Not significant = H₀ is true"]
        W3["Significant = Important effect"]
        W4["p = 0.049 vs p = 0.051 are very different"]
    end
    subgraph "Right"
        R1["p = 0.03 means 3% chance of this data if H₀ true"]
        R2["Not significant = Insufficient evidence against H₀"]
        R3["Significance ≠ practical importance"]
        R4["p-values near α are borderline; report actual values"]
    end
    W1 --> R1
    W2 --> R2
    W3 --> R3
    W4 --> R4
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Confidence Intervals
fields.description: What they actually mean, how width relates to sample size

steps.title: Point vs Interval Estimates
steps.explanation: A **confidence interval** gives a range of plausible values for a parameter, not just a single point estimate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Point Estimate" P["Sample mean x̄ = 52.3
Single best guess
But how precise?"] end subgraph "Interval Estimate" I["95% CI: [48.1, 56.5]
Range of plausible values
Quantifies uncertainty"] end subgraph "Why Intervals?" W["Point estimates are never exactly right
Intervals show the uncertainty"] end P --> I style P fill:#FFF9C4 style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: What 95% Confidence Means
steps.explanation: The confidence level describes the procedure, not any single interval.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Procedure" P["If we repeated sampling many times
And computed a 95% CI each time
95% of those CIs would contain μ"] end subgraph "NOT What It Means" N["95% probability μ is in THIS interval
(μ is fixed, it's either in or not)"] end subgraph "Analogy" A["Like a net that catches fish 95% of the time
Once you cast it, the fish is either caught or not
But the net is reliable 95% of the time"] end style P fill:#C8E6C9 style N fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: CI Formula for Means
steps.explanation: The confidence interval for a population mean combines the sample mean, margin of error, and critical value.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Formula" F["CI = x̄ ± (critical value) × (standard error)
CI = x̄ ± z* × (σ/√n)"] end subgraph "Components" C1["x̄ = sample mean (center)"] C2["z* = critical value (1.96 for 95%)"] C3["σ/√n = standard error"] C4["z* × σ/√n = margin of error"] end subgraph "Common Critical Values" CV["90% CI: z* = 1.645
95% CI: z* = 1.960
99% CI: z* = 2.576"] end F --> C1 F --> C2 F --> C3 style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style CV fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: What Affects CI Width?
steps.explanation: Three factors determine how wide your confidence interval will be.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wider CI" W1["Higher confidence level (99% > 95%)"] W2["Larger standard deviation (σ)"] W3["Smaller sample size (n)"] end subgraph "Narrower CI" N1["Lower confidence level (90% < 95%)"] N2["Smaller standard deviation (σ)"] N3["Larger sample size (n)"] end subgraph "Key Trade-off" T["Higher confidence → Wider interval
More certain but less precise"] end style W1 fill:#FFE0B2 style N3 fill:#C8E6C9 style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Sample Size Effect
steps.explanation: Larger samples give narrower (more precise) confidence intervals.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Example: σ = 10, x̄ = 50, 95% CI" E1["n = 25: 50 ± 1.96×(10/5) = [46.1, 53.9]"] E2["n = 100: 50 ± 1.96×(10/10) = [48.0, 52.0]"] E3["n = 400: 50 ± 1.96×(10/20) = [49.0, 51.0]"] end subgraph "The Pattern" P["Width ∝ 1/√n
To halve the width:
Quadruple the sample size"] end subgraph "Diminishing Returns" D["n: 25 → 100 → 400 → 1600
Width: 7.8 → 3.9 → 2.0 → 1.0"] end style E3 fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: CI vs Hypothesis Testing
steps.explanation: Confidence intervals and hypothesis tests are two sides of the same coin.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Connection" C["95% CI excludes μ₀ ↔ p < 0.05 for H₀: μ = μ₀"] end subgraph "Example" E["H₀: μ = 100
95% CI: [102, 108]
100 not in CI → Reject H₀ at α = 0.05"] end subgraph "CI Advantages" A1["Shows effect SIZE, not just significance"] A2["Shows direction of effect"] A3["Shows precision of estimate"] A4["More informative than p-value alone"] end C --> E style C fill:#2196F3,color:white style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Interpreting CIs Correctly
steps.explanation: Best practices for reporting and understanding confidence intervals.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Good Interpretation" G1["We are 95% confident that the
true mean is between 48.1 and 56.5"] G2["The procedure generates intervals
that capture μ 95% of the time"] end subgraph "Bad Interpretation" B1["There's 95% probability μ
is in this specific interval"] B2["95% of the data falls
in this interval"] end subgraph "Reporting" R["Mean = 52.3, 95% CI [48.1, 56.5]
or: 52.3 (95% CI: 48.1-56.5)"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: CIs for Different Parameters
steps.explanation: Confidence intervals exist for many different statistics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Common CIs" C1["Mean: x̄ ± t × (s/√n)"] C2["Proportion: p̂ ± z × √(p̂(1-p̂)/n)"] C3["Difference in means: (x̄₁-x̄₂) ± t × SE"] C4["Regression coefficients: β̂ ± t × SE(β̂)"] end subgraph "Note" N["Use t-distribution when σ unknown
(almost always in practice)"] end subgraph "For Proportions" P["Rule of thumb: Need np ≥ 10
and n(1-p) ≥ 10"] end style C1 fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: t-Test Family
fields.description: One-sample, two-sample, paired - when to use which

steps.title: What is a t-Test?
steps.explanation: The **t-test** compares means when the population standard deviation is unknown (almost always). It uses the t-distribution instead of the normal distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use t-Test" W1["Comparing means"] W2["σ (population SD) unknown"] W3["Sample size not huge"] end subgraph "Why t, not z?" T["t-distribution accounts for
extra uncertainty from
estimating σ with s"] end subgraph "t vs Normal" N["t: heavier tails, wider
As n→∞, t→Normal"] end W1 --> T W2 --> T T --> N style T fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Three Types of t-Tests
steps.explanation: Choose based on your research question and data structure.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "One-Sample t-Test" O["Compare sample mean to
a known/hypothesized value
H₀: μ = μ₀"] end subgraph "Two-Sample t-Test" T["Compare means of two
independent groups
H₀: μ₁ = μ₂"] end subgraph "Paired t-Test" P["Compare means of two
related measurements
H₀: μ_diff = 0"] end subgraph "Decision" D["How many groups?
Are observations paired?"] end D --> O D --> T D --> P style O fill:#4CAF50,color:white style T fill:#2196F3,color:white style P fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: One-Sample t-Test
steps.explanation: Test whether a sample mean differs from a known or hypothesized value.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["One group of measurements
Testing against known standard"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Is average IQ of students ≠ 100?"] E2["Does machine produce 500g on average?"] E3["Is mean response time < 2 seconds?"] end subgraph "Formula" F["t = (x̄ - μ₀) / (s/√n)
df = n - 1"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy import stats
stats.ttest_1samp(data, popmean=100)"] end U --> E1 style F fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Two-Sample (Independent) t-Test
steps.explanation: Compare means of two separate, unrelated groups.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["Two independent groups
Different subjects in each"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Treatment vs Control group"] E2["Male vs Female scores"] E3["Method A vs Method B"] end subgraph "Assumptions" A1["Independent samples"] A2["Normal distribution (or large n)"] A3["Equal variances? (see Welch's)"] end subgraph "Python" P["# Equal variances
stats.ttest_ind(group1, group2)

# Unequal variances (Welch's)
stats.ttest_ind(group1, group2, equal_var=False)"] end style A3 fill:#FFF9C4 style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Paired t-Test
steps.explanation: Compare two related measurements on the same subjects.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["Same subjects measured twice
Natural pairing exists"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Before vs After treatment"] E2["Left eye vs Right eye"] E3["Monday vs Friday performance"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["Actually a one-sample t-test
on the DIFFERENCES
H₀: μ_diff = 0"] end subgraph "Python" P["stats.ttest_rel(before, after)

# Or equivalently:
diffs = after - before
stats.ttest_1samp(diffs, 0)"] end style K fill:#FFF9C4 style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Choosing the Right t-Test
steps.explanation: Decision tree for selecting your t-test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Q1["How many groups?"] Q1 --> |"One"| ONE["One-Sample t-Test
Compare to known value"] Q1 --> |"Two"| Q2["Are measurements paired?"] Q2 --> |"Yes"| PAIR["Paired t-Test
Same subjects, two times"] Q2 --> |"No"| Q3["Equal variances?"] Q3 --> |"Yes"| IND["Student's t-Test
Two independent groups"] Q3 --> |"No/Unsure"| WELCH["Welch's t-Test
Safer choice"] style ONE fill:#4CAF50,color:white style PAIR fill:#FF9800,color:white style IND fill:#2196F3,color:white style WELCH fill:#9C27B0,color:white

steps.title: Assumptions & Violations
steps.explanation: What can go wrong and how to handle it.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Assumptions" A1["1. Independence"] A2["2. Normality (or large n)"] A3["3. Equal variances (two-sample)"] end subgraph "If Violated" V1["Non-independence: Need different test"] V2["Non-normal: Use Mann-Whitney or larger n"] V3["Unequal variances: Use Welch's t-test"] end subgraph "Robustness" R["t-test is robust to mild
normality violations
especially with n > 30"] end A1 --> V1 A2 --> V2 A3 --> V3 style V3 fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Effect Size: Cohen's d
steps.explanation: Statistical significance doesn't tell you the practical importance. Effect size does.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cohen's d" F["d = (x̄₁ - x̄₂) / pooled_SD

Standardized difference"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I1["d = 0.2: Small effect"] I2["d = 0.5: Medium effect"] I3["d = 0.8: Large effect"] end subgraph "Why Report Both?" W["p = 0.001 with d = 0.1
Statistically significant but
practically meaningless

Always report effect size!"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD style W fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: ANOVA: Comparing Multiple Groups
fields.description: One-way, two-way, post-hoc tests

steps.title: Why Not Multiple t-Tests?
steps.explanation: When comparing 3+ groups, running multiple t-tests inflates Type I error rate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["3 groups → 3 pairwise comparisons
4 groups → 6 comparisons
5 groups → 10 comparisons"] end subgraph "Error Inflation" E["Each test: α = 0.05
3 tests: P(at least one Type I) ≈ 0.14
10 tests: P(at least one Type I) ≈ 0.40"] end subgraph "Solution: ANOVA" A["Single test: Are ANY means different?
Controls overall Type I error at α"] end P --> E --> A style E fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: ANOVA Logic
steps.explanation: ANOVA compares variation BETWEEN groups to variation WITHIN groups.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["Are group means different enough
relative to within-group variation?"] end subgraph "Two Sources of Variance" B["Between-group variance
Differences in group means"] W["Within-group variance
Individual differences within groups"] end subgraph "F-Statistic" F["F = Between-group variance / Within-group variance

Large F → Groups likely different"] end Q --> B Q --> W B --> F W --> F style F fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: One-Way ANOVA
steps.explanation: Compare means across groups defined by ONE factor.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["One independent variable (factor)
with 3+ levels (groups)"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Drug dosage: Low, Medium, High"] E2["Teaching method: A, B, C, D"] E3["Region: North, South, East, West"] end subgraph "Hypotheses" H["H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ = μ₃ = ... = μₖ
H₁: At least one mean differs"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy import stats
f_stat, p_val = stats.f_oneway(
group1, group2, group3
)"] end style H fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: The ANOVA Table
steps.explanation: ANOVA results are presented in a standard table format.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "ANOVA Table" T["Source | SS | df | MS | F
----------|---------|-------|-----------|-------
Between | SS_B | k-1 | SS_B/(k-1)| MS_B/MS_W
Within | SS_W | N-k | SS_W/(N-k)|
Total | SS_T | N-1 | |"] end subgraph "Terms" T1["SS = Sum of Squares"] T2["df = degrees of freedom"] T3["MS = Mean Square = SS/df"] T4["k = number of groups"] T5["N = total sample size"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Two-Way ANOVA
steps.explanation: Analyze effects of TWO factors and their interaction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["Two independent variables
Each with 2+ levels"] end subgraph "Example" E["Factor A: Drug (Placebo, Drug1, Drug2)
Factor B: Gender (Male, Female)
Outcome: Blood pressure"] end subgraph "Effects Tested" E1["Main effect of A"] E2["Main effect of B"] E3["Interaction A×B"] end subgraph "Interaction" I["Does the effect of A
depend on level of B?"] end E1 --> I E2 --> I style E3 fill:#FF9800,color:white style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Post-Hoc Tests
steps.explanation: If ANOVA is significant, which specific groups differ?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["ANOVA says: 'Something differs'
But which pairs?"] end subgraph "Common Post-Hoc Tests" T1["Tukey's HSD
Most common, controls familywise α"] T2["Bonferroni
Conservative, simple adjustment"] T3["Scheffé
Most conservative"] T4["Dunnett
Compare all to control group"] end subgraph "Python" PY["from statsmodels.stats.multicomp import pairwise_tukeyhsd
tukey = pairwise_tukeyhsd(data, groups)
print(tukey)"] end style T1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style PY fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: ANOVA Assumptions
steps.explanation: Check these before trusting your results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Assumptions" A1["1. Independence
Observations are independent"] A2["2. Normality
Data in each group is normal"] A3["3. Homogeneity of Variance
Equal variances across groups"] end subgraph "How to Check" C1["Study design ensures independence"] C2["Shapiro-Wilk test, Q-Q plots"] C3["Levene's test, Bartlett's test"] end subgraph "If Violated" V["Non-normal → Kruskal-Wallis
Unequal variance → Welch's ANOVA"] end A1 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> C3 style V fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Effect Size: η² (Eta-squared)
steps.explanation: Measure the proportion of variance explained by group membership.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Formula" F["η² = SS_between / SS_total

Proportion of variance explained"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I1["η² = 0.01: Small effect"] I2["η² = 0.06: Medium effect"] I3["η² = 0.14: Large effect"] end subgraph "Example" E["η² = 0.25
25% of outcome variance
is explained by group membership"] end subgraph "Note" N["ω² (omega-squared) is less biased
but η² is more common"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD style E fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Chi-Square Tests
fields.description: Goodness of fit vs independence tests

steps.title: Chi-Square Tests Overview
steps.explanation: Chi-square (χ²) tests are used for **categorical data**. They compare observed frequencies to expected frequencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Two Main Types" G["Goodness of Fit
Does data match a distribution?"] I["Test of Independence
Are two variables related?"] end subgraph "Data Type" D["Both work with COUNTS
(categorical data)"] end subgraph "Common Uses" U1["Survey responses"] U2["Classification outcomes"] U3["A/B test conversions"] end G --> D I --> D style G fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Chi-Square Statistic
steps.explanation: The χ² statistic measures how much observed counts deviate from expected counts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Formula" F["χ² = Σ (Observed - Expected)² / Expected

Sum over all categories"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I1["χ² = 0: Perfect match to expected"] I2["χ² small: Close to expected"] I3["χ² large: Far from expected"] end subgraph "Decision" D["Compare to χ² distribution
with appropriate df
Get p-value"] end F --> I1 I3 --> D style F fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Goodness of Fit Test
steps.explanation: Test whether observed frequencies match an expected distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Question" Q["Does my data follow a
hypothesized distribution?"] end subgraph "Example: Fair Die" E["Roll die 60 times
Expected: 10 each face
Observed: 8,12,9,11,10,10
Is it fair?"] end subgraph "Hypotheses" H["H₀: Distribution matches expected
H₁: Distribution doesn't match"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy.stats import chisquare
observed = [8, 12, 9, 11, 10, 10]
stat, pval = chisquare(observed)
# assumes equal expected by default"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Test of Independence
steps.explanation: Test whether two categorical variables are related or independent.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Question" Q["Are these two categorical
variables related?"] end subgraph "Contingency Table" T[" | Outcome A | Outcome B
--------------|-----------|----------
Group 1 | 30 | 20
Group 2 | 25 | 25"] end subgraph "Hypotheses" H["H₀: Variables are independent
H₁: Variables are related"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency
table = [[30, 20], [25, 25]]
chi2, pval, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(table)"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Expected Frequencies
steps.explanation: For independence test, expected counts are calculated assuming no relationship.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Formula" F["Expected = (Row Total × Column Total) / Grand Total"] end subgraph "Example" E[" | Click | No Click | Total
--------------|-------|----------|------
Version A | 40 | 60 | 100
Version B | 30 | 70 | 100
Total | 70 | 130 | 200

Expected(A,Click) = 100×70/200 = 35"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I["If independent, expect 35 clicks for A
Observed: 40
Is 40 vs 35 significant?"] end style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Degrees of Freedom
steps.explanation: The df determines which χ² distribution to use.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Goodness of Fit" G["df = k - 1
k = number of categories"] end subgraph "Independence Test" I["df = (r - 1) × (c - 1)
r = rows, c = columns"] end subgraph "Example" E1["3 categories: df = 2"] E2["2×3 table: df = 1×2 = 2"] E3["4×5 table: df = 3×4 = 12"] end style G fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Assumptions & Rules
steps.explanation: Chi-square tests have important requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Requirements" R1["Random sample"] R2["Independent observations"] R3["Mutually exclusive categories"] R4["Expected counts ≥ 5 (usually)"] end subgraph "If Expected < 5" V1["Combine categories"] V2["Use Fisher's Exact Test (2×2)"] V3["Use simulation-based methods"] end subgraph "Warning" W["Chi-square uses COUNTS
Never use percentages!"] end R4 --> V1 R4 --> V2 style R4 fill:#FFF9C4 style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Effect Size: Cramér's V
steps.explanation: Measure the strength of association between categorical variables.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Formula" F["V = √(χ²/(n × min(r-1, c-1)))"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I1["V = 0: No association"] I2["V = 0.1: Small"] I3["V = 0.3: Medium"] I4["V = 0.5: Large"] I5["V = 1: Perfect association"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["Always report with χ² test
Especially for significant results"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD style I4 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Non-Parametric Tests
fields.description: Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis - when parametric assumptions fail

steps.title: Parametric vs Non-Parametric
steps.explanation: Non-parametric tests make fewer assumptions about the data distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parametric Tests" P1["Assume specific distribution
(usually normal)"] P2["t-test, ANOVA, etc."] P3["More powerful IF assumptions met"] end subgraph "Non-Parametric Tests" N1["Distribution-free
(fewer assumptions)"] N2["Work with ranks, not values"] N3["Robust to outliers"] end subgraph "When to Use Non-Parametric" W1["Non-normal data"] W2["Ordinal data (rankings)"] W3["Small samples"] W4["Outliers present"] end style P3 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style N3 fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: The Rank-Based Approach
steps.explanation: Most non-parametric tests convert data to ranks, reducing impact of extreme values.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original Data" D["Group A: 2, 5, 100
Group B: 3, 4, 6"] end subgraph "Ranked Data" R["Combined & Ranked:
2→1, 3→2, 4→3, 5→4, 6→5, 100→6

Group A ranks: 1, 4, 6
Group B ranks: 2, 3, 5"] end subgraph "Benefit" B["Outlier (100) becomes just '6'
No longer dominates the analysis"] end D --> R --> B style R fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Mann-Whitney U Test
steps.explanation: Non-parametric alternative to independent two-sample t-test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["Two independent groups
Can't assume normality
Ordinal or continuous data"] end subgraph "Tests" T["H₀: Distributions are equal
H₁: One tends to have larger values"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy.stats import mannwhitneyu
stat, pval = mannwhitneyu(group1, group2)"] end subgraph "Also Known As" A["Wilcoxon rank-sum test
(different from signed-rank!)"] end style U fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test
steps.explanation: Non-parametric alternative to paired t-test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["Paired/matched samples
Before-after measurements
Can't assume normal differences"] end subgraph "How It Works" H["1. Calculate differences
2. Rank absolute differences
3. Sum ranks of + and - separately
4. Compare sums"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy.stats import wilcoxon
stat, pval = wilcoxon(before, after)"] end subgraph "Related" R["Sign test: Even simpler
Just counts + vs - signs"] end style H fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Kruskal-Wallis Test
steps.explanation: Non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use When" U["3+ independent groups
Can't assume normality
Comparing distributions"] end subgraph "Tests" T["H₀: All groups have same distribution
H₁: At least one differs"] end subgraph "Python" P["from scipy.stats import kruskal
stat, pval = kruskal(g1, g2, g3)"] end subgraph "Post-Hoc" PH["If significant, use:
Dunn's test for pairwise comparisons"] end style U fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Quick Reference: Which Test?
steps.explanation: Choosing between parametric and non-parametric alternatives.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parametric → Non-Parametric" T1["One-sample t-test → Wilcoxon signed-rank"] T2["Two-sample t-test → Mann-Whitney U"] T3["Paired t-test → Wilcoxon signed-rank"] T4["One-way ANOVA → Kruskal-Wallis"] T5["Repeated measures → Friedman test"] end subgraph "Decision Rule" D["Check normality (Shapiro-Wilk)
If p < 0.05, use non-parametric
Or if n < 30 and visibly skewed"] end style D fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Trade-offs
steps.explanation: Understanding the pros and cons of non-parametric tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Advantages" A1["No normality assumption"] A2["Robust to outliers"] A3["Work with ordinal data"] A4["Work with small samples"] end subgraph "Disadvantages" D1["Less statistical power
(need larger n for same power)"] D2["Less informative
(compare medians, not means)"] D3["Harder to construct CIs"] end subgraph "Rule of Thumb" R["If normality is reasonable:
Use parametric (more power)

If clearly violated:
Use non-parametric (valid results)"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style D1 fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Effect Sizes for Non-Parametric Tests
steps.explanation: Reporting effect sizes with non-parametric tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mann-Whitney" M["r = Z / √N
or rank-biserial correlation"] end subgraph "Wilcoxon" W["r = Z / √N"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I["r = 0.1: Small
r = 0.3: Medium
r = 0.5: Large"] end subgraph "Always Report" A["Test statistic (U, W, or H)
p-value
Effect size
Sample sizes"] end style I fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
fields.description: How MLE works, connection to loss functions

steps.title: The MLE Question
steps.explanation: **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** finds parameter values that make the observed data most probable.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Setup" S["We have data: x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ
We assume a distribution with parameter θ"] end subgraph "The Question" Q["What value of θ makes this
data most likely to occur?"] end subgraph "MLE Answer" A["θ̂_MLE = argmax P(data | θ)
Find θ that maximizes likelihood"] end S --> Q --> A style Q fill:#FFF9C4 style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Likelihood vs Probability
steps.explanation: Likelihood and probability use the same formula but ask different questions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Probability" P["P(data | θ)
θ is FIXED
What data might we see?"] end subgraph "Likelihood" L["L(θ | data)
Data is FIXED
Which θ explains it best?"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["Same formula, different perspective!
L(θ | data) = P(data | θ)
but we vary θ, not data"] end P --> K L --> K style K fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The Likelihood Function
steps.explanation: For independent observations, the likelihood is the product of individual probabilities.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Likelihood" L["L(θ) = P(x₁|θ) × P(x₂|θ) × ... × P(xₙ|θ)
= ∏ P(xᵢ|θ)"] end subgraph "Log-Likelihood" LL["ℓ(θ) = log L(θ) = Σ log P(xᵢ|θ)

Products → Sums (easier!)"] end subgraph "Why Log?" W1["Products of small numbers → underflow"] W2["Sums are numerically stable"] W3["Same maximum (log is monotonic)"] end L --> LL style LL fill:#4CAF50,color:white style W3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: MLE Example: Coin Flip
steps.explanation: Estimate the probability of heads from observed flips.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data" D["10 flips: H H T H H H T H H H
7 heads, 3 tails"] end subgraph "Model" M["Binomial: P(k heads | p) = C(n,k) pᵏ (1-p)ⁿ⁻ᵏ"] end subgraph "Likelihood" L["L(p) = p⁷ × (1-p)³"] end subgraph "Maximize" MAX["Take derivative, set to 0:
d/dp [7 log p + 3 log(1-p)] = 0
p̂ = 7/10 = 0.7"] end D --> M --> L --> MAX style MAX fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: MLE for Normal Distribution
steps.explanation: The MLE for normal parameters gives familiar formulas.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Model" M["X ~ N(μ, σ²)"] end subgraph "Log-Likelihood" L["ℓ(μ,σ²) = -n/2 log(2πσ²) - 1/(2σ²) Σ(xᵢ-μ)²"] end subgraph "MLE Solutions" S1["μ̂ = x̄ = (1/n) Σ xᵢ"] S2["σ̂² = (1/n) Σ (xᵢ-x̄)²"] end subgraph "Note" N["MLE for σ² is biased!
Unbiased version divides by (n-1)"] end M --> L --> S1 L --> S2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9 style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: MLE and Loss Functions
steps.explanation: Many ML loss functions are derived from MLE via negative log-likelihood.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Connection" C["Maximize likelihood
= Minimize negative log-likelihood
= Minimize a loss function"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Normal → MSE Loss
-log N(y|ŷ,σ²) ∝ (y-ŷ)²"] E2["Bernoulli → Binary Cross-Entropy
-log Bernoulli(y|p) = BCE"] E3["Categorical → Cross-Entropy
-log Categorical(y|p) = CE"] end subgraph "Insight" I["Training neural nets with BCE
IS doing MLE!"] end C --> E1 C --> E2 C --> E3 E2 --> I style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Properties of MLE
steps.explanation: Why MLE is so widely used - it has excellent theoretical properties.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Asymptotic Properties" A1["Consistent: θ̂ → θ as n → ∞"] A2["Asymptotically normal"] A3["Asymptotically efficient
(lowest variance among consistent estimators)"] end subgraph "Practical Benefits" B1["Usually has closed-form solution"] B2["If not, gradient descent works"] B3["Standard errors available"] end subgraph "Limitations" L1["Can overfit with small n"] L2["Point estimate (no uncertainty)"] L3["Sensitive to model specification"] end style A3 fill:#C8E6C9 style L1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: MLE in Practice
steps.explanation: How MLE is computed in real applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Closed Form" CF["Some distributions have
analytical MLE solutions
(Normal, Exponential, Binomial)"] end subgraph "Numerical Optimization" NO["Most cases: gradient descent
on negative log-likelihood

scipy.optimize.minimize
or torch/tensorflow"] end subgraph "Example: Logistic Regression" LR["No closed form!
Use iterative optimization
sklearn uses L-BFGS or SAG"] end subgraph "Tips" T["Use log-likelihood (numerical stability)
Check convergence
Try multiple starting points"] end style NO fill:#2196F3,color:white style T fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Bayesian vs Frequentist
fields.description: Prior, posterior, likelihood - the Bayesian workflow

steps.title: Two Philosophies of Probability
steps.explanation: Frequentist and Bayesian statistics have fundamentally different interpretations of probability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Frequentist"
        F1["Probability = Long-run frequency"]
        F2["Parameters are FIXED but unknown"]
        F3["Data is random"]
        F4["No prior beliefs allowed"]
    end
    subgraph "Bayesian"
        B1["Probability = Degree of belief"]
        B2["Parameters have distributions"]
        B3["Data is fixed (once observed)"]
        B4["Prior beliefs are explicit"]
    end
    style F2 fill:#2196F3,color:white
    style B2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Bayes' Theorem
steps.explanation: The foundation of Bayesian inference: update beliefs with data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bayes' Theorem" B["P(θ|data) = P(data|θ) × P(θ) / P(data)"] end subgraph "Components" C1["P(θ|data) = Posterior
What we believe AFTER seeing data"] C2["P(data|θ) = Likelihood
How likely is data given θ"] C3["P(θ) = Prior
What we believed BEFORE data"] C4["P(data) = Evidence
Normalizing constant"] end subgraph "Intuition" I["Posterior ∝ Likelihood × Prior"] end B --> C1 B --> C2 B --> C3 B --> C4 style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: The Bayesian Workflow
steps.explanation: How Bayesian analysis proceeds from prior to posterior.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR P["Prior P(θ)
Initial belief"] D["Data
Observations"] L["Likelihood P(data|θ)
Data generating process"] POST["Posterior P(θ|data)
Updated belief"] P --> POST D --> L --> POST subgraph "Then" T1["Make predictions"] T2["Summarize posterior
(mean, credible interval)"] T3["Use as prior for more data"] end POST --> T1 POST --> T2 POST --> T3 style P fill:#FF9800,color:white style POST fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Priors: Types and Choices
steps.explanation: The prior encodes your beliefs before seeing data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Informative Prior" I["Based on domain knowledge
Previous studies
Expert opinion"] end subgraph "Weakly Informative" W["Regularizing effect
Rules out implausible values
e.g., Normal(0, 10)"] end subgraph "Non-Informative / Flat" N["Uniform over range
'Let the data speak'
Can cause problems"] end subgraph "Conjugate Prior" C["Mathematically convenient
Posterior same family as prior
e.g., Beta-Binomial"] end style W fill:#4CAF50,color:white style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Credible vs Confidence Intervals
steps.explanation: Bayesian credible intervals have the interpretation people think confidence intervals have!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Confidence Interval (Frequentist)" CI["95% of such intervals
would contain true θ

Can't say: '95% prob θ in interval'"] end subgraph "Credible Interval (Bayesian)" CR["95% posterior probability
that θ is in interval

CAN say: '95% prob θ in interval'"] end subgraph "Why Different?" W["Frequentist: θ is fixed
Bayesian: θ has distribution"] end CI --> W CR --> W style CI fill:#2196F3,color:white style CR fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Example: Coin Flipping
steps.explanation: Compare frequentist MLE vs Bayesian posterior for estimating coin bias.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Data" D["7 heads in 10 flips"] end subgraph "Frequentist (MLE)" F["p̂ = 7/10 = 0.7
Point estimate
95% CI: [0.35, 0.93]"] end subgraph "Bayesian" B["Prior: Beta(1,1) = Uniform
Posterior: Beta(8, 4)
Mean: 0.67
95% Credible: [0.38, 0.89]"] end subgraph "With Strong Prior" S["Prior: Beta(10,10) (believe fair)
Posterior: Beta(17, 13)
Mean: 0.57
(pulled toward 0.5)"] end D --> F D --> B D --> S style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: When to Use Which?
steps.explanation: Practical guidance on choosing between approaches.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use Frequentist When" F1["Large samples"] F2["No prior information"] F3["Need reproducibility / objectivity"] F4["Regulatory requirements"] end subgraph "Use Bayesian When" B1["Small samples + prior knowledge"] B2["Want probability statements"] B3["Incorporating expert knowledge"] B4["Complex hierarchical models"] B5["Sequential updating"] end subgraph "In Practice" P["Many modern methods blend both
e.g., regularization ≈ priors"] end style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Bayesian in ML
steps.explanation: How Bayesian thinking appears in machine learning.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Direct Applications" D1["Bayesian Neural Networks"] D2["Gaussian Processes"] D3["Bayesian Optimization"] D4["Probabilistic Programming (PyMC, Stan)"] end subgraph "Implicit Bayesian Ideas" I1["L2 regularization = Gaussian prior"] I2["L1 regularization = Laplace prior"] I3["Dropout ≈ Bayesian approximation"] I4["Ensemble methods ≈ posterior sampling"] end subgraph "Key Benefit" K["Uncertainty quantification!
Know when model is uncertain"] end style K fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I1 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Correlation vs Causation
fields.description: Simpson's paradox, confounding variables

steps.title: The Classic Warning
steps.explanation: Correlation does not imply causation - but what does this actually mean?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Correlation" C["X and Y move together
When X increases, Y tends to increase
(or decrease)"] end subgraph "Causation" CA["X actually CAUSES Y
Changing X would change Y"] end subgraph "The Gap" G["Correlation: We observe a relationship
Causation: We know the mechanism

Correlation is necessary but
NOT sufficient for causation"] end C --> G CA --> G style G fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Why Correlation ≠ Causation
steps.explanation: There are multiple reasons two variables can be correlated without one causing the other.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Possible Explanations" E1["X causes Y
(what we hope)"] E2["Y causes X
(reverse causation)"] E3["Z causes both X and Y
(confounding)"] E4["Pure coincidence
(spurious correlation)"] end subgraph "Example: Ice cream & Drowning" EX["Ice cream sales ↑ → Drownings ↑

Confounder: Hot weather
causes both!"] end E3 --> EX style E3 fill:#FF9800,color:white style EX fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Confounding Variables
steps.explanation: A **confounder** is a third variable that influences both X and Y, creating a spurious association.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Confounded" Z["Confounder Z"] X["X (apparent cause)"] Y["Y (outcome)"] Z --> X Z --> Y X -.->|"Spurious"| Y end subgraph "Examples" E1["Coffee → Heart disease?
Confounder: Smoking"] E2["Education → Income?
Confounder: Family wealth"] E3["Hospital → Death?
Confounder: Severity of illness"] end style Z fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: Simpson's Paradox
steps.explanation: A trend in aggregated data can reverse when data is separated into groups!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Aggregated Data" A["Treatment A: 80/100 survive (80%)
Treatment B: 75/100 survive (75%)

A looks better!"] end subgraph "Separated by Severity" S1["Mild cases:
A: 70/80 (87.5%)
B: 5/5 (100%)"] S2["Severe cases:
A: 10/20 (50%)
B: 70/95 (74%)"] end subgraph "Truth" T["B is better for BOTH groups!
But A got more mild cases
(selection bias)"] end A --> S1 A --> S2 S1 --> T S2 --> T style A fill:#FFCDD2 style T fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Establishing Causation
steps.explanation: How can we actually determine if X causes Y?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Gold Standard: RCT" R["Randomized Controlled Trial
Random assignment breaks confounding"] end subgraph "Observational Methods" O1["Control for confounders
(regression, matching)"] O2["Instrumental variables"] O3["Regression discontinuity"] O4["Difference-in-differences"] end subgraph "Bradford Hill Criteria" B["Strength, Consistency, Specificity
Temporality, Biological gradient
Plausibility, Coherence, Experiment"] end style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white style O1 fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: Controlling for Confounders
steps.explanation: Statistical techniques to account for confounding variables.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Stratification" S["Analyze within subgroups
e.g., compare within age groups"] end subgraph "Matching" M["Match treated/control on confounders
e.g., propensity score matching"] end subgraph "Regression" R["Include confounders as covariates
Estimates 'adjusted' effect"] end subgraph "Warning" W["Can only control for
MEASURED confounders
Unmeasured confounding remains!"] end style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Causal Diagrams (DAGs)
steps.explanation: Directed Acyclic Graphs help visualize causal relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Simple Causation" X1["X"] --> Y1["Y"] end subgraph "Confounding" Z2["Z"] --> X2["X"] Z2 --> Y2["Y"] end subgraph "Mediation" X3["X"] --> M3["M"] --> Y3["Y"] end subgraph "Collider" X4["X"] --> C4["C"] Y4["Y"] --> C4 end subgraph "Rules" R["Control for confounders
Don't control for colliders
Don't control for mediators
(if want total effect)"] end style Z2 fill:#FF9800 style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: In ML: Correlation is Often Fine
steps.explanation: For prediction, correlation suffices. For intervention, causation matters.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Prediction Tasks" P["Correlation is enough!
Don't need to know WHY
Just predict accurately"] end subgraph "Intervention Tasks" I["Need causal knowledge
What happens if we DO X?
Correlation can mislead"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Predict churn: use correlates ✓"] E2["Reduce churn: need causes
Don't just correlate!"] end subgraph "Modern ML" M["Causal ML is growing field
DoWhy, CausalML libraries"] end style P fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I fill:#2196F3,color:white

fields.title: A/B Testing Deep Dive
fields.description: Sample size calculation, statistical power, early stopping

steps.title: A/B Testing Basics
steps.explanation: A/B testing is a randomized experiment comparing two versions to measure which performs better.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" U["Users arrive"] A["Version A (Control)"] B["Version B (Treatment)"] U --> |"Random 50%"| A U --> |"Random 50%"| B end subgraph "Measure" M["Compare conversion rates
or other metrics"] end subgraph "Question" Q["Is the difference
statistically significant?"] end A --> M B --> M M --> Q style A fill:#2196F3,color:white style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: The Hypothesis Test
steps.explanation: A/B testing is fundamentally a two-sample hypothesis test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hypotheses" H0["H₀: pₐ = pᵦ
No difference"] H1["H₁: pₐ ≠ pᵦ
There IS a difference"] end subgraph "Test Statistic" Z["z = (p̂ₐ - p̂ᵦ) / SE

SE = √(p̂(1-p̂)(1/nₐ + 1/nᵦ))"] end subgraph "Decision" D["If |z| > 1.96: Reject H₀
(for α = 0.05, two-tailed)"] end H0 --> Z --> D style Z fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Sample Size Calculation
steps.explanation: Before running a test, calculate how many users you need.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Inputs" I1["Baseline rate (p₁): e.g., 10%"] I2["Minimum detectable effect (MDE):
e.g., 1% absolute (10% → 11%)"] I3["Significance level α: typically 0.05"] I4["Power (1-β): typically 0.80"] end subgraph "Formula" F["n ≈ 16 × p(1-p) / MDE²
(per group, for 80% power, α=0.05)"] end subgraph "Example" E["p = 0.10, MDE = 0.01
n = 16 × 0.10 × 0.90 / 0.01²
n = 14,400 per group!"] end I1 --> F I2 --> F F --> E style E fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Statistical Power
steps.explanation: **Power** is the probability of detecting a real effect when it exists.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Definition" D["Power = 1 - β
= P(Reject H₀ | H₁ is true)
= P(detect real effect)"] end subgraph "Factors Affecting Power" F1["↑ Sample size → ↑ Power"] F2["↑ Effect size → ↑ Power"] F3["↑ α (significance level) → ↑ Power"] F4["↓ Variance → ↑ Power"] end subgraph "Typical Target" T["Power = 80% is standard
90% for important decisions"] end style D fill:#2196F3,color:white style T fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: The Peeking Problem
steps.explanation: Looking at results multiple times inflates your false positive rate!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Temptation" T["Check results daily
Stop when significant"] end subgraph "The Problem" P["Each peek is a test
Multiple testing increases
Type I error dramatically"] end subgraph "Example" E["5 peeks at α=0.05 each
Actual α ≈ 0.14!
(not 0.05)"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Pre-set sample size, don't peek"] S2["Sequential testing methods"] S3["Use proper stopping rules"] end T --> P --> E style P fill:#FFCDD2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Testing Correction
steps.explanation: Testing multiple variants or metrics requires adjusting significance levels.
steps.diagram_data:
