graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["Test 20 metrics at α=0.05
Expected false positives: 1
Even with no real effects!"] end subgraph "Bonferroni Correction" B["α_adjusted = α / n_tests
0.05 / 20 = 0.0025
Conservative"] end subgraph "FDR Control" F["Benjamini-Hochberg procedure
Controls false discovery rate
Less conservative"] end subgraph "Best Practice" BP["Define PRIMARY metric
Others are exploratory"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style BP fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Common Pitfalls
steps.explanation: Mistakes that invalidate A/B test results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Randomization Issues"
        R1["Non-random assignment"]
        R2["Sample ratio mismatch"]
        R3["Cookie/device conflicts"]
    end
    subgraph "Analysis Issues"
        A1["Peeking at results"]
        A2["Stopping early"]
        A3["Post-hoc metric selection"]
        A4["Simpson's paradox in segments"]
    end
    subgraph "Design Issues"
        D1["Insufficient sample size"]
        D2["Contamination between groups"]
        D3["Novelty/learning effects"]
    end
    style A2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Beyond Basic A/B
steps.explanation: Advanced experimentation techniques.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Multi-Armed Bandits" M["Dynamically allocate traffic
to better variants
Balances explore/exploit"] end subgraph "Bayesian A/B Testing" B["P(B > A | data)
Direct probability statements
Intuitive stopping rules"] end subgraph "Sequential Testing" S["Continuously monitor
with valid stopping rules
e.g., SPRT, GST"] end subgraph "Factorial Designs" F["Test multiple factors
simultaneously
Detect interactions"] end style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white style M fill:#2196F3,color:white

fields.title: Logging Hierarchy & Flow
fields.description: Loggers, handlers, formatters, filters - how they connect

steps.title: The Four Components
steps.explanation: Python's logging module has four main components that work together to process log messages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Components" L["Logger
Entry point for logging"] H["Handler
Where to send logs"] F["Formatter
How to format logs"] FI["Filter
Which logs to process"] end subgraph "Relationships" L --> H H --> F L -.-> FI H -.-> FI end style L fill:#4CAF50,color:white style H fill:#2196F3,color:white style F fill:#FF9800,color:white style FI fill:#9C27B0,color:white

steps.title: Log Message Flow
steps.explanation: When you call `logger.info()`, the message flows through multiple stages before output.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "1. Create" C["logger.info('message')"] end subgraph "2. Logger Level Check" LC["Is INFO >= logger.level?"] end subgraph "3. Logger Filter" LF["Pass logger filters?"] end subgraph "4. Handler Level Check" HC["Is INFO >= handler.level?"] end subgraph "5. Handler Filter" HF["Pass handler filters?"] end subgraph "6. Format & Emit" FE["Format message
Write to destination"] end C --> LC LC -->|"Yes"| LF LC -->|"No"| X1["Discard"] LF -->|"Yes"| HC LF -->|"No"| X2["Discard"] HC -->|"Yes"| HF HF -->|"Yes"| FE style FE fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Loggers
steps.explanation: **Loggers** are the entry point. You get them by name, and they form a hierarchy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Getting a Logger" G["logger = logging.getLogger('myapp.module')

Same name = same logger (singleton)"] end subgraph "Logger Methods" M1["logger.debug('...')"] M2["logger.info('...')"] M3["logger.warning('...')"] M4["logger.error('...')"] M5["logger.critical('...')"] M6["logger.exception('...') # includes traceback"] end subgraph "Logger Properties" P1["level: minimum level to process"] P2["handlers: list of handlers"] P3["propagate: pass to parent?"] end style G fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Handlers
steps.explanation: **Handlers** determine WHERE log messages go. A logger can have multiple handlers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "One Logger, Multiple Handlers" L["Logger"] H1["StreamHandler
(console)"] H2["FileHandler
(app.log)"] H3["SMTPHandler
(email errors)"] end L --> H1 L --> H2 L --> H3 subgraph "Each Handler Can Have" P1["Its own level"] P2["Its own formatter"] P3["Its own filters"] end subgraph "Example" E["Console: DEBUG and above
File: INFO and above
Email: ERROR and above"] end style L fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Formatters
steps.explanation: **Formatters** control HOW log messages appear. Each handler has one formatter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Format String" F["formatter = logging.Formatter(
'%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s'
)"] end subgraph "Common Attributes" A1["%(asctime)s - Timestamp"] A2["%(name)s - Logger name"] A3["%(levelname)s - Level (INFO, ERROR)"] A4["%(message)s - The log message"] A5["%(filename)s - Source file"] A6["%(lineno)d - Line number"] A7["%(funcName)s - Function name"] end subgraph "Output" O["2024-01-15 10:30:45 - myapp.db - ERROR - Connection failed"] end F --> O style F fill:#E3F2FD style O fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Filters
steps.explanation: **Filters** provide fine-grained control over which records are processed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Filter Attachment" L["Logger"] -.-> F1["Filter 1"] H["Handler"] -.-> F2["Filter 2"] end subgraph "Custom Filter" CF["class MyFilter(logging.Filter):
def filter(self, record):
# Return True to process
# Return False to drop
return 'secret' not in record.msg"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Filter by logger name"] U2["Filter by message content"] U3["Add extra fields to record"] U4["Rate limiting"] end style CF fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Putting It Together
steps.explanation: Complete example setting up logging with all components.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup Code" C["import logging

# Create logger
logger = logging.getLogger('myapp')
logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

# Create handler
handler = logging.StreamHandler()
handler.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# Create formatter
formatter = logging.Formatter('%(name)s - %(message)s')
handler.setFormatter(formatter)

# Add handler to logger
logger.addHandler(handler)"] end subgraph "Usage" U["logger.info('Hello') # Output: myapp - Hello
logger.debug('Hidden') # No output (handler level)"] end C --> U style C fill:#E3F2FD style U fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Flow Diagram
steps.explanation: The full picture of how a log message is processed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Your Code"
        LOG["logger.error('Failed')"]
    end
    subgraph "Logger Processing"
        L1["Logger level check"]
        L2["Logger filters"]
        L3["Propagate to parent?"]
    end
    subgraph "Handler Processing"
        H1["Handler level check"]
        H2["Handler filters"]
        H3["Format with Formatter"]
        H4["Emit to destination"]
    end
    LOG --> L1 --> L2 --> H1
    L2 --> L3
    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4
    subgraph "Destinations"
        D1["Console"]
        D2["File"]
        D3["Network"]
    end
    H4 --> D1
    H4 --> D2
    H4 --> D3
    style LOG fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style H4 fill:#2196F3,color:white

fields.title: Log Levels Explained
fields.description: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL - when to use each

steps.title: The Five Standard Levels
steps.explanation: Python logging has five built-in levels, each with a numeric value. Lower values are more verbose.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Levels (Low to High)" D["DEBUG (10)
Detailed diagnostic info"] I["INFO (20)
Confirmation things work"] W["WARNING (30)
Something unexpected"] E["ERROR (40)
Serious problem"] C["CRITICAL (50)
Program may crash"] end D --> I --> W --> E --> C style D fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#C8E6C9 style W fill:#FFF9C4 style E fill:#FFCDD2 style C fill:#F44336,color:white

steps.title: DEBUG Level
steps.explanation: **DEBUG** is for detailed information useful during development and debugging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use DEBUG" U1["Variable values during execution"] U2["Function entry/exit"] U3["Loop iterations"] U4["SQL queries being executed"] U5["API request/response details"] end subgraph "Examples" E["logger.debug(f'Processing user {user_id}')
logger.debug(f'Query: {sql}')
logger.debug(f'Response: {response.json()}')"] end subgraph "Note" N["Usually disabled in production
Can be very verbose"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: INFO Level
steps.explanation: **INFO** confirms that things are working as expected. Good for tracking normal flow.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use INFO" U1["Service started/stopped"] U2["Configuration loaded"] U3["User logged in"] U4["Task completed successfully"] U5["Batch job progress"] end subgraph "Examples" E["logger.info('Server started on port 8080')
logger.info(f'Loaded {len(records)} records')
logger.info('Daily backup completed')"] end subgraph "Rule of Thumb" R["If you'd want to see it in
production logs, use INFO"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: WARNING Level
steps.explanation: **WARNING** indicates something unexpected happened, but the program can continue.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use WARNING" U1["Deprecated API usage"] U2["Fallback to default value"] U3["Retry after failure"] U4["Resource running low"] U5["Configuration issue (non-fatal)"] end subgraph "Examples" E["logger.warning('Config not found, using defaults')
logger.warning(f'Disk space low: {percent}%')
logger.warning('API rate limit approaching')"] end subgraph "Key Point" K["Something to investigate
but not immediately critical"] end style E fill:#FFF9C4 style K fill:#FF9800,color:white

steps.title: ERROR Level
steps.explanation: **ERROR** means a serious problem occurred. Some functionality is not working.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use ERROR" U1["Failed to complete operation"] U2["Database connection lost"] U3["API call failed (after retries)"] U4["File not found (when expected)"] U5["Permission denied"] end subgraph "Examples" E["logger.error('Failed to save user data')
logger.error(f'API returned {status_code}')
logger.exception('Database query failed') # with traceback"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Use logger.exception() in except blocks
It auto-includes the traceback"] end style E fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: CRITICAL Level
steps.explanation: **CRITICAL** means the program itself may be unable to continue running.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use CRITICAL" U1["Application cannot start"] U2["Essential service unavailable"] U3["Data corruption detected"] U4["Security breach detected"] U5["Unrecoverable state"] end subgraph "Examples" E["logger.critical('Cannot connect to database')
logger.critical('Out of memory')
logger.critical('License expired')"] end subgraph "Note" N["Rarely used
Often followed by sys.exit()"] end style E fill:#F44336,color:white style N fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Level Filtering
steps.explanation: Setting a level means: process this level AND all higher severity levels.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Logger Level = WARNING" A["DEBUG ❌ (10 < 30)"] B["INFO ❌ (20 < 30)"] C["WARNING ✓ (30 >= 30)"] D["ERROR ✓ (40 >= 30)"] E["CRITICAL ✓ (50 >= 30)"] end subgraph "Common Configurations" C1["Development: DEBUG"] C2["Staging: DEBUG or INFO"] C3["Production: INFO or WARNING"] end subgraph "Code" CD["logger.setLevel(logging.WARNING)
# Only WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL logged"] end style C fill:#4CAF50 style D fill:#4CAF50 style E fill:#4CAF50 style A fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Decision guide for choosing the right level.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Level Selection Guide" T["Level | When to Use
---------|------------------------------------------
DEBUG | Detailed info for debugging
INFO | Normal operation confirmation
WARNING | Unexpected but handled situation
ERROR | Operation failed, needs attention
CRITICAL | System failure, may crash"] end subgraph "Questions to Ask" Q1["Will devs need this for debugging? → DEBUG"] Q2["Is this normal operation? → INFO"] Q3["Is something wrong but handled? → WARNING"] Q4["Did an operation fail? → ERROR"] Q5["Can the system continue? → ERROR vs CRITICAL"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Logger Inheritance
fields.description: Parent-child logger relationships, propagation

steps.title: Logger Hierarchy
steps.explanation: Loggers form a tree hierarchy based on their names, separated by dots.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Logger Tree"
        R["root (unnamed)"]
        A["myapp"]
        B["myapp.db"]
        C["myapp.api"]
        D["myapp.api.users"]
        E["myapp.api.orders"]
    end
    R --> A
    A --> B
    A --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    subgraph "Name Pattern"
        N["parent.child.grandchild"]
    end
    style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white
    style A fill:#81C784

steps.title: The Root Logger
steps.explanation: The **root logger** is at the top of the hierarchy. It's what `logging.info()` uses directly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Getting Root Logger" G1["logging.getLogger() # No name = root"] G2["logging.getLogger('') # Empty string = root"] end subgraph "Using Root Directly" U["logging.info('message') # Uses root logger
logging.warning('warning') # Uses root logger"] end subgraph "vs Named Logger" N["logger = logging.getLogger('myapp')
logger.info('message') # Uses 'myapp' logger"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Use named loggers in libraries/modules
Root logger is for simple scripts only"] end style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Propagation: How It Works
steps.explanation: By default, log messages **propagate** up to parent loggers for handling.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Propagation Flow" LOG["myapp.api.users logs ERROR"] L1["myapp.api.users
(no handlers)"] L2["myapp.api
(no handlers)"] L3["myapp
(has FileHandler)"] L4["root
(has StreamHandler)"] end LOG --> L1 L1 -->|"propagate"| L2 L2 -->|"propagate"| L3 L3 -->|"propagate"| L4 subgraph "Result" R["Message handled by BOTH:
- myapp's FileHandler
- root's StreamHandler"] end style L3 fill:#2196F3,color:white style L4 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Effective Level
steps.explanation: A logger without an explicit level inherits its **effective level** from ancestors.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Level Inheritance" R["root
level=WARNING"] A["myapp
level=DEBUG"] B["myapp.db
level=NOTSET"] C["myapp.api
level=NOTSET"] end R --> A --> B A --> C subgraph "Effective Levels" E1["root: WARNING"] E2["myapp: DEBUG (explicit)"] E3["myapp.db: DEBUG (inherited from myapp)"] E4["myapp.api: DEBUG (inherited from myapp)"] end subgraph "NOTSET" N["level=NOTSET (0) means:
'Look at my parent's level'"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Controlling Propagation
steps.explanation: Set `propagate=False` to prevent messages from bubbling up.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Default (propagate=True)" D["logger.propagate = True
Messages go to parent handlers too"] end subgraph "Disabled (propagate=False)" P["logger.propagate = False
Messages only handled locally"] end subgraph "Use Cases for False" U1["Prevent duplicate logs"] U2["Isolate library logging"] U3["Separate log destinations"] end subgraph "Example" E["logger = logging.getLogger('myapp.api')
logger.propagate = False
logger.addHandler(my_handler)
# Messages won't go to root"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Common Pattern: Module Loggers
steps.explanation: Best practice is to create a logger per module using `__name__`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "In Each Module" M["# myapp/api/users.py
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
# __name__ = 'myapp.api.users'"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Automatic hierarchy from package structure"] B2["Easy to filter by module"] B3["Clear log message origin"] end subgraph "Configure at Top Level" C["# In main.py or config
logging.getLogger('myapp').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
logging.getLogger('myapp.api').setLevel(logging.INFO)"] end style M fill:#E3F2FD style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Avoiding Duplicate Logs
steps.explanation: A common issue: seeing the same message twice due to multiple handlers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["Message logged twice!
2024-01-15 ERROR myapp - Failed
2024-01-15 ERROR myapp - Failed"] end subgraph "Cause" C["Both 'myapp' and root have handlers
Message propagates from myapp → root
Both handlers emit it"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Option 1: propagate=False
logger.propagate = False"] S2["Option 2: Only configure root
Don't add handlers to child loggers"] S3["Option 3: Don't use basicConfig
if configuring loggers manually"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Library Logging Best Practice
steps.explanation: When writing a library, follow these guidelines.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Library Code" L["# mylibrary/__init__.py
import logging
logging.getLogger(__name__).addHandler(
logging.NullHandler()
)"] end subgraph "Why NullHandler?" W1["Prevents 'No handler found' warning"] W2["Library doesn't configure logging"] W3["Application decides how to log"] end subgraph "Application Configures" A["# In the application
logging.getLogger('mylibrary').setLevel(logging.WARNING)"] end subgraph "Rule" R["Libraries: Create loggers, add NullHandler
Applications: Configure handlers"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white

fields.title: Configuration Methods
fields.description: basicConfig, dictConfig, fileConfig - when to use each

steps.title: Configuration Options
steps.explanation: Python logging offers three main ways to configure logging, each suited for different scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Configuration Methods" BC["basicConfig()
Simple, quick setup"] DC["dictConfig()
Dictionary-based, flexible"] FC["fileConfig()
INI file-based"] end subgraph "Complexity" S["Simple Scripts"] --> BC M["Medium Projects"] --> DC L["Legacy/External Config"] --> FC end style DC fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: basicConfig() - Quick Start
steps.explanation: **basicConfig()** is the simplest way to set up logging. Perfect for scripts and quick debugging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" B1["import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logging.info('Hello!')"] end subgraph "Common Parameters" P1["level=logging.DEBUG"] P2["format='%(levelname)s - %(message)s'"] P3["filename='app.log'"] P4["datefmt='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'"] P5["handlers=[handler1, handler2]"] end subgraph "Important Note" N["Only works ONCE!
Must call before any logging
Subsequent calls are ignored"] end style B1 fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: basicConfig() Examples
steps.explanation: Common basicConfig patterns for different use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Console + File" CF["logging.basicConfig(
level=logging.DEBUG,
format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s',
handlers=[
logging.FileHandler('app.log'),
logging.StreamHandler()
]
)"] end subgraph "File Only" FO["logging.basicConfig(
filename='app.log',
level=logging.INFO,
format='%(asctime)s - %(message)s'
)"] end subgraph "Limitations" L1["Can't configure per-logger settings"] L2["Can't modify after first logging call"] L3["No filter support"] end style CF fill:#E3F2FD style L1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: dictConfig() - Recommended
steps.explanation: **dictConfig()** uses a dictionary to define the complete logging configuration. This is the recommended approach for most projects.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Structure" D["LOGGING_CONFIG = {
'version': 1,
'disable_existing_loggers': False,
'formatters': {...},
'handlers': {...},
'loggers': {...},
'root': {...}
}"] end subgraph "Usage" U["from logging.config import dictConfig
dictConfig(LOGGING_CONFIG)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Full control over all components"] B2["Easy to store as JSON/YAML"] B3["Can reconfigure at runtime"] B4["Supports incremental config"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: dictConfig() Full Example
steps.explanation: A complete dictConfig example with multiple handlers and loggers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Complete Config" C["LOGGING = {
'version': 1,
'formatters': {
'detailed': {'format': '%(asctime)s %(name)s %(levelname)s %(message)s'},
'simple': {'format': '%(levelname)s %(message)s'}
},
'handlers': {
'console': {'class': 'logging.StreamHandler', 'formatter': 'simple'},
'file': {'class': 'logging.FileHandler', 'filename': 'app.log', 'formatter': 'detailed'}
},
'loggers': {
'myapp': {'level': 'DEBUG', 'handlers': ['console', 'file']}
},
'root': {'level': 'WARNING', 'handlers': ['console']}
}"] end subgraph "Key Fields" K1["version: Always 1 (schema version)"] K2["disable_existing_loggers: Usually False"] K3["formatters: Define output formats"] K4["handlers: Define destinations"] K5["loggers: Configure named loggers"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Django Logging Example
steps.explanation: Django uses dictConfig internally. Here's a typical Django logging setup.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "settings.py" S["LOGGING = {
'version': 1,
'disable_existing_loggers': False,
'handlers': {
'console': {'class': 'logging.StreamHandler'},
'file': {'class': 'logging.FileHandler', 'filename': 'debug.log'}
},
'loggers': {
'django': {'handlers': ['console'], 'level': 'INFO'},
'django.db.backends': {'handlers': ['file'], 'level': 'DEBUG'},
'myapp': {'handlers': ['console', 'file'], 'level': 'DEBUG'}
}
}"] end subgraph "Common Django Loggers" D1["django - General Django"] D2["django.request - HTTP requests"] D3["django.db.backends - SQL queries"] D4["django.security - Security warnings"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: fileConfig() - INI Files
steps.explanation: **fileConfig()** reads configuration from an INI-style file. Less common but useful for external configuration.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "logging.conf" F["[loggers]
keys=root,myapp

[handlers]
keys=consoleHandler

[formatters]
keys=simpleFormatter

[logger_myapp]
level=DEBUG
handlers=consoleHandler
qualname=myapp

[handler_consoleHandler]
class=StreamHandler
formatter=simpleFormatter

[formatter_simpleFormatter]
format=%(levelname)s - %(message)s"] end subgraph "Usage" U["from logging.config import fileConfig
fileConfig('logging.conf')"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["External config management
Non-Python config systems
Legacy compatibility"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: YAML Configuration
steps.explanation: A popular approach: store dictConfig in YAML for readability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "logging.yaml" Y["version: 1
formatters:
simple:
format: '%(levelname)s - %(message)s'
handlers:
console:
class: logging.StreamHandler
formatter: simple
loggers:
myapp:
level: DEBUG
handlers: [console]"] end subgraph "Load and Apply" L["import yaml
from logging.config import dictConfig

with open('logging.yaml') as f:
config = yaml.safe_load(f)
dictConfig(config)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Human-readable"] B2["Easy to version control"] B3["Comments supported"] end style Y fill:#E3F2FD style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Configuration Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Choose the right configuration method for your needs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Quick Script?" Q["basicConfig()"] end subgraph "Real Application?" R["dictConfig()"] end subgraph "External Config File?" E["YAML + dictConfig()"] end subgraph "Legacy System?" L["fileConfig()"] end subgraph "Summary" S["Scripts → basicConfig
Applications → dictConfig
Config files → YAML/JSON + dictConfig
Legacy → fileConfig"] end style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white style S fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Handler Types
fields.description: StreamHandler, FileHandler, RotatingFileHandler, and more

steps.title: What Are Handlers?
steps.explanation: Handlers determine **where** log messages go. Each handler sends logs to a different destination.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Logger" L["Logger receives message"] end subgraph "Handlers" H1["StreamHandler
(console)"] H2["FileHandler
(file)"] H3["SMTPHandler
(email)"] H4["HTTPHandler
(web server)"] end L --> H1 L --> H2 L --> H3 L --> H4 subgraph "One Logger, Many Handlers" N["A logger can have
multiple handlers"] end style L fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: StreamHandler
steps.explanation: **StreamHandler** writes to a stream - usually stdout or stderr (console).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "StreamHandler" S["logging.StreamHandler()"] end subgraph "Default" D["Writes to sys.stderr"] end subgraph "Custom Stream" C["import sys
handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Development/debugging"] U2["Docker containers (stdout)"] U3["Real-time monitoring"] end style S fill:#2196F3,color:white style U2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: FileHandler
steps.explanation: **FileHandler** writes logs to a file. Simple but watch out for large files!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "FileHandler" F["logging.FileHandler('app.log')"] end subgraph "Parameters" P1["filename - Path to log file"] P2["mode - 'a' (append) or 'w' (write)"] P3["encoding - Usually 'utf-8'"] P4["delay - True = don't open until first log"] end subgraph "Example" E["handler = logging.FileHandler(
'logs/app.log',
mode='a',
encoding='utf-8'
)"] end subgraph "Warning" W["Files can grow infinitely!
Use RotatingFileHandler instead"] end style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: RotatingFileHandler
steps.explanation: **RotatingFileHandler** rotates logs when they reach a certain size. Essential for production!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "RotatingFileHandler" R["from logging.handlers import RotatingFileHandler

handler = RotatingFileHandler(
'app.log',
maxBytes=5*1024*1024, # 5MB
backupCount=5
)"] end subgraph "File Rotation" F1["app.log (current)"] F2["app.log.1 (previous)"] F3["app.log.2 (older)"] F4["app.log.3 (older)"] F5["app.log.4 (oldest, then deleted)"] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 subgraph "Benefits" B["Prevents disk full
Keeps recent history
Old logs auto-deleted"] end style R fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: TimedRotatingFileHandler
steps.explanation: **TimedRotatingFileHandler** rotates logs based on time - daily, hourly, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "TimedRotatingFileHandler" T["from logging.handlers import TimedRotatingFileHandler

handler = TimedRotatingFileHandler(
'app.log',
when='midnight',
interval=1,
backupCount=30
)"] end subgraph "When Options" W1["'S' - Seconds"] W2["'M' - Minutes"] W3["'H' - Hours"] W4["'D' - Days"] W5["'midnight' - Roll at midnight"] W6["'W0'-'W6' - Weekday (Mon=0)"] end subgraph "Result" R["app.log
app.log.2024-01-15
app.log.2024-01-14
app.log.2024-01-13"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: WatchedFileHandler
steps.explanation: **WatchedFileHandler** detects when the log file is rotated by external tools (like logrotate).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["External tool (logrotate) moves app.log
FileHandler keeps writing to old file descriptor"] end subgraph "Solution" S["WatchedFileHandler checks inode
Reopens file if it was rotated"] end subgraph "Usage" U["from logging.handlers import WatchedFileHandler

handler = WatchedFileHandler('app.log')"] end subgraph "Use Case" UC["Unix systems with logrotate
Containerized apps with sidecar log rotation"] end style S fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: SocketHandler & HTTPHandler

'logserver.example.com',

)"] end subgraph "HTTPHandler" H["logging.handlers.HTTPHandler(

'/log/endpoint',
method='POST'
)"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Centralized logging"] U2["Log aggregation systems"] U3["Distributed applications"] end subgraph "Production" P["Often use specialized libraries:
python-logstash, watchtower (AWS)"] end style U1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: QueueHandler (Thread-safe)
steps.explanation: **QueueHandler** enables non-blocking logging - critical for high-performance applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["Logging to slow handler
(file, network) blocks your code"] end subgraph "Solution: QueueHandler" Q["import queue
from logging.handlers import QueueHandler, QueueListener

log_queue = queue.Queue()
queue_handler = QueueHandler(log_queue)

# Listener processes queue in background
listener = QueueListener(log_queue, file_handler)
listener.start()"] end subgraph "Flow" F1["Logger"] --> F2["QueueHandler"] F2 --> F3["Queue"] F3 --> F4["QueueListener"] F4 --> F5["FileHandler"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Non-blocking logging
Better performance
Batch processing"] end style Q fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

--------------------|---------------------------

fields.title: Formatters Deep Dive

steps.title: What Are Formatters?
steps.explanation: Formatters control **how** log messages look. They convert LogRecord objects into text strings.

graph LR subgraph "Flow" LR["LogRecord
(name, level, msg, ...)"] F["Formatter"] T["Formatted Text
'2024-01-15 ERROR myapp - Failed'"] end LR --> F --> T subgraph "Formatter's Job" J["Convert LogRecord attributes
into readable text output"] end style F fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Basic Format String
steps.explanation: Format strings use `%(attribute)s` syntax to include LogRecord attributes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Simple Format" S["'%(levelname)s - %(message)s'
→ 'ERROR - Connection failed'"] end subgraph "Detailed Format" D["'%(asctime)s %(name)s %(levelname)s %(message)s'
→ '2024-01-15 10:30:45 myapp ERROR Connection failed'"] end subgraph "With Module Info" M["'%(asctime)s [%(filename)s:%(lineno)d] %(message)s'
→ '2024-01-15 10:30:45 [api.py:42] Connection failed'"] end subgraph "Usage" U["formatter = logging.Formatter(
'%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s'
)"] end style U fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LogRecord Attributes
steps.explanation: Every LogRecord has these attributes available for formatting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Core Attributes"
        C1["%(name)s - Logger name"]
        C2["%(levelname)s - Level (DEBUG, INFO...)"]
        C3["%(levelno)d - Level number (10, 20...)"]
        C4["%(message)s - The log message"]
    end
    subgraph "Time Attributes"
        T1["%(asctime)s - Timestamp string"]
        T2["%(created)f - Unix timestamp"]
        T3["%(relativeCreated)d - ms since logging loaded"]
    end
    subgraph "Location Attributes"
        L1["%(filename)s - Source filename"]
        L2["%(funcName)s - Function name"]
        L3["%(lineno)d - Line number"]
        L4["%(module)s - Module name"]
        L5["%(pathname)s - Full file path"]
    end
    subgraph "Process/Thread"
        P1["%(process)d - Process ID"]
        P2["%(processName)s - Process name"]
        P3["%(thread)d - Thread ID"]
        P4["%(threadName)s - Thread name"]
    end

steps.title: Date/Time Formatting

(includes milliseconds)"] end subgraph "Custom datefmt" C["formatter = logging.Formatter(
'%(asctime)s %(message)s',
datefmt='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'
)
→ '2024-01-15 10:30:45 Message'"] end subgraph "Common Patterns" P1["%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S - ISO-ish"] P2["%d/%b/%Y %H:%M:%S - Apache style"] P3["%H:%M:%S - Time only"] P4["%Y%m%d_%H%M%S - Sortable"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Style Parameter (Python 3.2+)
steps.explanation: Python supports three format string styles: `%`, `{`, and `$`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Percent Style (Default)" P["'%(levelname)s - %(message)s'
style='%'"] end subgraph "Brace Style" B["'{levelname} - {message}'
style='{'"] end subgraph "Dollar Style" D["'$levelname - $message'
style='$'"] end subgraph "Example" E["logging.Formatter(
'{asctime} {levelname} {message}',
style='{'
)"] end subgraph "Note" N["Brace style is cleaner but
% style is more compatible"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Custom Formatters
steps.explanation: Subclass `logging.Formatter` for advanced customization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Custom Formatter Class" C["class ColorFormatter(logging.Formatter):
COLORS = {
'DEBUG': '\033[36m', # cyan
'INFO': '\033[32m', # green
'WARNING': '\033[33m', # yellow
'ERROR': '\033[31m', # red
}

def format(self, record):
color = self.COLORS.get(record.levelname, '')
record.levelname = f'{color}{record.levelname}\033[0m'
return super().format(record)"] end subgraph "Methods to Override" M1["format(record) - Main formatting"] M2["formatTime(record, datefmt)"] M3["formatException(exc_info)"] M4["formatStack(stack_info)"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: JSON Formatter Example
steps.explanation: Create a JSON formatter for structured logging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "JSON Formatter" J["import json
from datetime import datetime

class JSONFormatter(logging.Formatter):
def format(self, record):
log_obj = {
'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
'level': record.levelname,
'logger': record.name,
'message': record.getMessage(),
'module': record.module,
'line': record.lineno
}
if record.exc_info:
log_obj['exception'] = self.formatException(record.exc_info)
return json.dumps(log_obj)"] end subgraph "Output" O["{"timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:45",
"level": "ERROR",
"logger": "myapp",
"message": "Connection failed"}"] end style J fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Formatter Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for effective log formatting.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Development" D["Include: timestamp, level, file:line, message
'%(asctime)s %(levelname)s [%(filename)s:%(lineno)d] %(message)s'"] end subgraph "Production" P["Include: timestamp, level, logger, request_id, message
Consider JSON for log aggregation"] end subgraph "Tips" T1["Always include timestamp"] T2["Include source location for debugging"] T3["Use consistent format across handlers"] T4["Add context (request_id, user_id)"] end subgraph "Anti-patterns" A1["Too much info → noise"] A2["No timestamp → can't correlate"] A3["No level → can't filter"] end style T1 fill:#C8E6C9 style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Structured Logging
fields.description: JSON logging, extra fields, contextvars for request tracking

steps.title: Why Structured Logging?
steps.explanation: Traditional text logs are hard to parse and analyze. Structured logging outputs machine-readable formats (usually JSON).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Traditional Log" T["2024-01-15 10:30:45 ERROR User 123 failed to login from 192.168.1.1"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Hard to parse programmatically"] P2["Inconsistent formats"] P3["Can't query fields easily"] end subgraph "Structured Log" S["{
"timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:45Z",
"level": "ERROR",
"event": "login_failed",
"user_id": 123,
"ip": "192.168.1.1"
}"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Easy to parse"] B2["Queryable fields"] B3["Works with log aggregators"] end style T fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: The 'extra' Parameter
steps.explanation: Add custom fields to log records using the `extra` parameter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Using extra" E["logger.info(
'User logged in',
extra={
'user_id': 123,
'ip_address': '192.168.1.1',
'browser': 'Chrome'
}
)"] end subgraph "Accessing in Formatter" F["'%(user_id)s from %(ip_address)s: %(message)s'
→ '123 from 192.168.1.1: User logged in'"] end subgraph "Gotcha" G["extra keys become LogRecord attributes
They MUST be in format string
or you'll get KeyError"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD style G fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: LoggerAdapter for Context
steps.explanation: **LoggerAdapter** automatically adds context to all log calls.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "LoggerAdapter" L["class RequestAdapter(logging.LoggerAdapter):
def process(self, msg, kwargs):
# Add request_id to every log
extra = kwargs.setdefault('extra', {})
extra['request_id'] = self.extra['request_id']
return msg, kwargs"] end subgraph "Usage" U["logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
adapter = RequestAdapter(logger, {'request_id': 'abc-123'})

adapter.info('Processing started')
adapter.error('Validation failed')
# All logs include request_id='abc-123'"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Don't need to pass extra every time
Context travels with the adapter"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: contextvars for Request Tracking
steps.explanation: Use `contextvars` to track request context across async code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Context Variable" C["import contextvars

request_id_var = contextvars.ContextVar('request_id', default=None)"] end subgraph "Set in Middleware" M["async def middleware(request, call_next):
request_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
request_id_var.set(request_id)
response = await call_next(request)
return response"] end subgraph "Access in Filter" F["class ContextFilter(logging.Filter):
def filter(self, record):
record.request_id = request_id_var.get()
return True"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Works across async boundaries
No need to pass context manually
Thread-safe"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: structlog Library
steps.explanation: **structlog** is the most popular library for structured logging in Python.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Install" I["pip install structlog"] end subgraph "Basic Usage" B["import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()
logger.info('user_logged_in', user_id=123, ip='192.168.1.1')"] end subgraph "Output (JSON)" O["{"event": "user_logged_in",
"user_id": 123,
"ip": "192.168.1.1",
"timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:45Z"}"] end subgraph "Features" F1["Automatic JSON output"] F2["Processors pipeline"] F3["Bound loggers (context)"] F4["stdlib integration"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style F1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: structlog Configuration
steps.explanation: Configure structlog with processors for your needs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Configuration" C["import structlog

structlog.configure(
processors=[
structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt='iso'),
structlog.processors.StackInfoRenderer(),
structlog.processors.format_exc_info,
structlog.processors.JSONRenderer()
],
wrapper_class=structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger,
context_class=dict,
logger_factory=structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory(),
)"] end subgraph "Processors" P1["Add timestamps"] P2["Add log level"] P3["Format exceptions"] P4["Render to JSON"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Bound Loggers in structlog
steps.explanation: Bind context that persists across multiple log calls.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Binding Context" B["logger = structlog.get_logger()

# Bind context for this request
log = logger.bind(request_id='abc-123', user_id=42)

log.info('processing_started')
log.info('validation_passed')
log.info('processing_complete')
# All logs have request_id and user_id"] end subgraph "New Context" N["# Add more context
log = log.bind(order_id=999)
log.info('order_created')
# Has request_id, user_id, AND order_id"] end subgraph "Unbind" U["log = log.unbind('order_id')
# Back to just request_id and user_id"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: python-json-logger
steps.explanation: A simpler alternative: **python-json-logger** adds JSON output to stdlib logging.

graph TB subgraph "Install" I["pip install python-json-logger"] end subgraph "Usage" U["from pythonjsonlogger import jsonlogger

logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.info('Hello', extra={'user_id': 123})"] end subgraph "Output" O["{"timestamp": "2024-01-15 10:30:45",
"level": "INFO",
"message": "Hello",
"user_id": 123}"] end style U fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for effective structured logging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use consistent field names"] D2["Include correlation IDs (request_id)"] D3["Log events, not sentences"] D4["Use appropriate types (int, not '123')"] D5["Include timestamp in UTC"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Log sensitive data (passwords, tokens)"] DN2["Log huge objects"] DN3["Use inconsistent naming"] end subgraph "Field Naming" F["user_id not userId (consistency)

mlflow.log_artifact('plots/loss_curve.png')
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Common Anti-Patterns
fields.description: Common logging mistakes and how to avoid them

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Using print()
steps.explanation: The most common mistake: using `print()` instead of logging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: print()" B["print('Debug: user_id =', user_id)
print('Error occurred!')
print(f'Processing {item}')"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Can't disable in production"] P2["No log levels"] P3["No timestamps"] P4["Goes to stdout, hard to capture"] end subgraph "GOOD: logging" G["logger.debug('user_id=%s', user_id)
logger.error('Error occurred!')
logger.info('Processing %s', item)"] end subgraph "Benefits" BN["Configurable levels
Structured output
Multiple destinations"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: String Formatting in Log Calls
steps.explanation: Format strings are evaluated even if the log level is disabled!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Eager Formatting" B["logger.debug(f'User data: {expensive_call()}')
logger.debug('Data: ' + str(large_object))
logger.debug('User: %s' % user.to_dict())"] end subgraph "Problem" P["Even if DEBUG is disabled,
the formatting/function call
STILL HAPPENS"] end subgraph "GOOD: Lazy Formatting" G["logger.debug('User data: %s', expensive_call)
logger.debug('Data: %s', large_object)
logger.debug('User: %s', user)"] end subgraph "Why It Works" W["Logger only formats if
the level is enabled"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Catching and Silencing Exceptions
steps.explanation: Logging an error but hiding important information.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Lost Information" B1["try:
process()
except Exception:
logger.error('Something went wrong')"] B2["try:
process()
except Exception as e:
logger.error(str(e)) # Loses traceback"] end subgraph "GOOD: Full Context" G["try:
process()
except Exception:
logger.exception('Failed to process')
# Automatically includes traceback!"] end subgraph "Alternative" A["logger.error('Failed', exc_info=True)"] end style B1 fill:#FFCDD2 style B2 fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Root Logger Abuse
steps.explanation: Using the root logger directly instead of named loggers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Root Logger" B["import logging
logging.info('Processing...') # Uses root
logging.error('Failed!') # Uses root"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Can't filter by module"] P2["Can't set different levels"] P3["Hard to identify log source"] end subgraph "GOOD: Named Logger" G["import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info('Processing...')
logger.error('Failed!')"] end subgraph "Benefits" BN["logger.name = 'myapp.module'
Filter: myapp.* = DEBUG
Third-party libs = WARNING"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Logging Sensitive Data
steps.explanation: Accidentally logging passwords, tokens, or personal data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Sensitive Data" B["logger.info(f'Login: {username}:{password}')
logger.debug(f'Request: {request.headers}')
logger.info(f'User profile: {user.__dict__}')"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Security breach"] P2["Compliance violations (GDPR)"] P3["Log files become sensitive"] end subgraph "GOOD: Sanitize" G["logger.info(f'Login attempt: {username}')
logger.debug(f'Request to: {request.path}')
logger.info(f'User: id={user.id}, role={user.role}')"] end subgraph "Tools" T["Create sanitize_log() helper
Mask patterns: credit cards, SSN
Audit log statements"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9 style P1 fill:#F44336,color:white

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Too Much or Too Little
steps.explanation: Extremes in logging: either flooding logs or not logging enough.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Too Much" TM["for item in million_items:
logger.debug(f'Processing {item}')

Problems:
- Huge log files
- Performance impact
- Hard to find real issues"] end subgraph "Too Little" TL["def complex_process():
# 500 lines of code
# No logging
return result

Problems:
- Can't debug production
- No visibility"] end subgraph "Just Right" JR["logger.info('Starting batch of %d items', len(items))
for i, item in enumerate(items):
if i % 1000 == 0:
logger.debug('Progress: %d/%d', i, len(items))
logger.info('Batch complete')"] end style TM fill:#FFCDD2 style TL fill:#FFCDD2 style JR fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Not Using Appropriate Levels
steps.explanation: Using wrong log levels makes filtering useless.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "BAD Examples"
        B1["logger.error('Starting server')  # Not an error!"]
        B2["logger.debug('CRITICAL: Database down!')  # Wrong level"]
        B3["logger.info('x=5, y=10, z=15')  # Debug noise"]
    end
    subgraph "Level Guidelines"
        G1["DEBUG: Developer needs to see"]
        G2["INFO: Operations should know"]
        G3["WARNING: Something unexpected"]
        G4["ERROR: Something failed"]
        G5["CRITICAL: System is failing"]
    end
    subgraph "GOOD Examples"
        GE1["logger.info('Starting server on port %d', port)"]
        GE2["logger.critical('Database connection failed!')"]
        GE3["logger.debug('Variables: x=%d, y=%d, z=%d', x, y, z)"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style GE1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Duplicate Log Handlers
steps.explanation: Accidentally adding multiple handlers leads to duplicate logs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Bug" B["def setup_logging():
handler = logging.StreamHandler()
logging.getLogger().addHandler(handler)

# Called multiple times!
setup_logging() # 1 handler
setup_logging() # 2 handlers
setup_logging() # 3 handlers
# Same message logged 3 times!"] end subgraph "Fix 1: Check First" F1["if not logger.handlers:
logger.addHandler(handler)"] end subgraph "Fix 2: Clear First" F2["logger.handlers.clear()
logger.addHandler(handler)"] end subgraph "Fix 3: Use dictConfig" F3["dictConfig() replaces config
Not additive like addHandler()"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style F3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern Checklist
steps.explanation: Quick reference to avoid common mistakes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Checklist" C["✗ Using print() instead of logging
✗ Eager string formatting in log calls
✗ Swallowing exceptions without traceback
✗ Using root logger in libraries
✗ Logging sensitive data
✗ Logging too much (every iteration)
✗ Wrong log levels
✗ Duplicate handlers
✗ Not testing logging configuration"] end subgraph "Best Practices" BP["✓ Use named loggers (__name__)
✓ Lazy formatting (logger.info('%s', x))
✓ logger.exception() in except blocks
✓ Use dictConfig for configuration
✓ Sanitize sensitive data
✓ Log at appropriate levels
✓ Test your logging in dev"] end style C fill:#FFCDD2 style BP fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Performance Considerations
fields.description: Optimizing logging for high-throughput applications

steps.title: Logging Has Cost
steps.explanation: Every log call has overhead. In high-throughput apps, this matters.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Log Call Overhead" O1["1. Check if level enabled"] O2["2. Create LogRecord object"] O3["3. Format the message"] O4["4. Write to handler (I/O!)"] end subgraph "I/O is Slowest" IO["File write: ~1ms
Network: ~10-100ms
Console: ~0.1ms"] end subgraph "Impact" I["1M log calls with file I/O
= ~1000 seconds of waiting!"] end style IO fill:#FFF9C4 style I fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Lazy Evaluation is Critical
steps.explanation: Never format strings before passing to logger.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "SLOW: Eager" S["# Always formats, even if DEBUG disabled
logger.debug(f'Data: {expensive_serialize(obj)}')
logger.debug('User: ' + user.to_json())"] end subgraph "FAST: Lazy" F["# Only formats if DEBUG enabled
logger.debug('Data: %s', obj)
logger.debug('User: %r', user)"] end subgraph "Why?" W["Level check happens FIRST
If disabled, formatting SKIPPED"] end subgraph "Benchmark" B["Disabled DEBUG with eager: 1.5μs/call
Disabled DEBUG with lazy: 0.1μs/call
15x faster!"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#C8E6C9 style B fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: isEnabledFor() Check
steps.explanation: For expensive operations, check if level is enabled first.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use" W["When log message requires
expensive computation"] end subgraph "Pattern" P["if logger.isEnabledFor(logging.DEBUG):
# Only runs if DEBUG enabled
stats = compute_expensive_stats()
logger.debug('Stats: %s', stats)"] end subgraph "Alternative" A["# For simple cases, lazy eval is enough
logger.debug('Value: %s', obj) # obj.__str__ only if needed"] end subgraph "Note" N["Don't overuse - adds complexity
Only for truly expensive operations"] end style P fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Async Logging with QueueHandler
steps.explanation: Move I/O off the main thread with asynchronous logging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["Synchronous file write
blocks your application"] end subgraph "Solution: QueueHandler" S["import queue
from logging.handlers import QueueHandler, QueueListener

log_queue = queue.Queue(-1) # Unlimited

# Main thread logs to queue (fast)
queue_handler = QueueHandler(log_queue)
logger.addHandler(queue_handler)

# Background thread writes to file (slow)
file_handler = logging.FileHandler('app.log')
listener = QueueListener(log_queue, file_handler)
listener.start()"] end subgraph "Result" R["Log call: <1μs (queue put)
File I/O: background thread
No blocking!"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Buffered Logging
steps.explanation: Batch multiple log records before writing to reduce I/O.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "MemoryHandler" M["from logging.handlers import MemoryHandler

# Buffer up to 100 records
memory_handler = MemoryHandler(
capacity=100,
flushLevel=logging.ERROR, # Flush on ERROR
target=file_handler
)"] end subgraph "How It Works" H1["Buffers log records in memory"] H2["Flushes when capacity reached"] H3["Flushes immediately on ERROR+"] H4["Reduces I/O operations"] end subgraph "Trade-off" T["+ Fewer I/O operations
- May lose logs on crash
- Slightly delayed output"] end style M fill:#E3F2FD style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Sampling and Rate Limiting
steps.explanation: Don't log every event in high-volume scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sampling" S["import random

def sampled_log(logger, level, msg, sample_rate=0.01):
if random.random() < sample_rate:
logger.log(level, msg + ' (sampled 1%)')"] end subgraph "Rate Limiting" R["from functools import lru_cache
import time

@lru_cache(maxsize=1000)
def should_log(key, interval=60):
return time.time() # Returns same value for interval

def rate_limited_log(logger, key, msg):
if should_log(key): # Once per key per interval
logger.warning(msg)"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U["High-volume request logging
Repeated error suppression
Metric collection"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Reduce LogRecord Creation
steps.explanation: LogRecord creation has overhead. Minimize unnecessary records.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "LogRecord Overhead" O["Each log call creates:
- LogRecord object
- Captures stack frame
- Gets thread/process info
~1-2μs per call"] end subgraph "Optimization 1" O1["# Batch log instead of per-item

# BAD
for item in items:
logger.debug('Processing %s', item)

# GOOD
logger.debug('Processing %d items', len(items))"] end subgraph "Optimization 2" O2["# Aggregate metrics

# Instead of logging each request time
metrics.append(response_time)

# Log summary periodically
logger.info('Avg response: %dms', avg(metrics))"] end style O fill:#FFF9C4 style O1 fill:#C8E6C9

Handler: StreamHandler
No sampling"] end subgraph "Production" P["Level: INFO or WARNING

Consider sampling"] end subgraph "Config Example" C["LOGGING = {
'loggers': {
'myapp': {'level': 'INFO'},
'myapp.requests': {'level': 'WARNING'}, # High volume
},

'async_file': {
'class': 'logging.handlers.QueueHandler',

}
}
}"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Performance Checklist
steps.explanation: Quick reference for high-performance logging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Always Do" A1["✓ Use lazy formatting (%s, not f-string)"] A2["✓ Set appropriate levels per logger"] A3["✓ Use QueueHandler for I/O-bound handlers"] A4["✓ Rotate logs to manage file size"] end subgraph "Consider" C1["Use isEnabledFor() for expensive operations"] C2["Sample high-volume logs"] C3["Buffer with MemoryHandler"] C4["Aggregate instead of per-event logging"] end subgraph "Benchmark" B["Measure your specific workload

fields.title: Event Loop Fundamentals
fields.description: How the event loop schedules and runs coroutines

steps.title: What is an Event Loop?
steps.explanation: The event loop is the **heart** of asyncio. It's a single-threaded loop that manages and runs asynchronous tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Event Loop" EL["Event Loop
(Single Thread)"] end subgraph "Tasks Queue" T1["Task 1"] T2["Task 2"] T3["Task 3"] end subgraph "I/O Waiting" IO1["Network Read"] IO2["File Write"] IO3["Timer"] end EL --> T1 EL --> T2 EL --> T3 IO1 -.-> EL IO2 -.-> EL IO3 -.-> EL subgraph "Key Insight" K["One thread juggles many tasks
by switching when tasks wait for I/O"] end style EL fill:#4CAF50,color:white style K fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The Event Loop Cycle
steps.explanation: The event loop continuously cycles through: check for ready tasks, run them until they await, then check for completed I/O.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Loop Cycle" A["1. Check ready tasks"] --> B["2. Run task until await"] B --> C["3. Task yields control"] C --> D["4. Check I/O completions"] D --> E["5. Wake up waiting tasks"] E --> A end subgraph "Example" EX["Task A runs → hits await fetch()
Loop switches to Task B
Task B runs → hits await sleep()
Network response arrives
Loop wakes Task A to continue"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style EX fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Getting the Event Loop
steps.explanation: Different ways to access and run the event loop.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Modern Way (Python 3.7+)" M["asyncio.run(main())
# Creates loop, runs, closes"] end subgraph "Get Current Loop" G["loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop = asyncio.get_running_loop() # Inside async"] end subgraph "Manual Control (Advanced)" A["loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
try:
loop.run_until_complete(main())
finally:
loop.close()"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Use asyncio.run() for entry point
Use get_running_loop() inside coroutines"] end style M fill:#4CAF50,color:white style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Event Loop Internals
steps.explanation: Under the hood, the event loop uses OS-level I/O multiplexing (select, epoll, kqueue).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Event Loop Components" S["Selector
(epoll/kqueue/select)"] RQ["Ready Queue
(tasks to run)"] SC["Scheduled Callbacks
(timers, delays)"] end subgraph "OS Integration" OS["Operating System"] FD["File Descriptors
(sockets, pipes)"] end S --> OS OS --> FD FD -.->|"ready"| S S -->|"wake up"| RQ subgraph "How It Works" H["1. Register sockets with selector
2. selector.select() blocks efficiently
3. OS notifies when I/O ready
4. Loop wakes relevant tasks"] end style S fill:#2196F3,color:white style H fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Running Tasks
steps.explanation: Tasks are the unit of work in asyncio. The event loop schedules and runs them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Creating Tasks" C1["task = asyncio.create_task(coro())
# Schedules coroutine to run"] C2["task = loop.create_task(coro())
# Same, explicit loop"] end subgraph "Task States" S1["PENDING"] --> S2["RUNNING"] S2 --> S3["DONE"] S2 --> S4["CANCELLED"] end subgraph "Example" E["async def main():
task1 = asyncio.create_task(fetch_data())
task2 = asyncio.create_task(process_data())
# Both run concurrently!
result1 = await task1
result2 = await task2"] end style C1 fill:#E3F2FD style S2 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Scheduling Callbacks
steps.explanation: The event loop can schedule regular functions (not coroutines) to run.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Immediate Callback" I["loop.call_soon(callback, arg1, arg2)
# Run in next iteration"] end subgraph "Delayed Callback" D["loop.call_later(delay, callback, arg)
# Run after delay seconds"] end subgraph "Scheduled Callback" S["loop.call_at(when, callback, arg)
# Run at specific loop time"] end subgraph "Thread-Safe" T["loop.call_soon_threadsafe(callback)
# Call from another thread"] end subgraph "Note" N["Callbacks are regular functions
For coroutines, use create_task()"] end style I fill:#E3F2FD style N fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Debug Mode
steps.explanation: Enable debug mode to catch common async mistakes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Enable Debug Mode" E1["asyncio.run(main(), debug=True)"] E2["PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1 python app.py"] E3["loop.set_debug(True)"] end subgraph "What It Catches" C1["Coroutines not awaited"] C2["Callbacks taking too long"] C3["Resource warnings"] C4["Slow I/O operations"] end subgraph "Slow Callback Warning" W["WARNING: Executing took 0.200s
# Anything > 0.1s is flagged"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Always develop with debug=True
Disable in production"] end style E1 fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Event Loop Lifecycle
steps.explanation: Complete lifecycle from creation to cleanup.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Lifecycle" A["Create Loop"] --> B["Run Tasks"] B --> C["Handle Signals"] C --> D["Shutdown"] D --> E["Close Loop"] end subgraph "Proper Shutdown" S["async def shutdown(loop):
tasks = asyncio.all_tasks(loop)
for task in tasks:
task.cancel()
await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
loop.stop()"] end subgraph "asyncio.run() Does This" AR["1. Creates new event loop
2. Runs the coroutine
3. Cancels remaining tasks
4. Closes the loop
# All handled automatically!"] end style AR fill:#4CAF50,color:white

fields.title: Coroutines vs Threads vs Processes
fields.description: Comparing Python's concurrency models

steps.title: Three Concurrency Models
steps.explanation: Python offers three main ways to run code concurrently: asyncio (coroutines), threading, and multiprocessing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "asyncio" A["Single thread
Cooperative multitasking
Best for I/O-bound"] end subgraph "threading" T["Multiple threads
Preemptive multitasking
Limited by GIL"] end subgraph "multiprocessing" M["Multiple processes
True parallelism
Best for CPU-bound"] end subgraph "Key Question" Q["Is your code waiting (I/O)
or computing (CPU)?"] end Q -->|"I/O-bound"| A Q -->|"Legacy/blocking"| T Q -->|"CPU-bound"| M style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style Q fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The GIL Problem
steps.explanation: Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) prevents true parallel execution of Python bytecode in threads.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "GIL (Global Interpreter Lock)" G["Only ONE thread can execute
Python bytecode at a time"] end subgraph "Threads with GIL" T1["Thread 1: Run"] --> T2["Thread 1: Wait for GIL"] T3["Thread 2: Wait"] --> T4["Thread 2: Run"] end subgraph "Consequences" C1["Threads don't speed up CPU work"] C2["Threads still help with I/O"] C3["Multiprocessing bypasses GIL"] end subgraph "GIL Releases" R["GIL released during:
- I/O operations
- time.sleep()
- C extensions"] end style G fill:#F44336,color:white style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Memory & Overhead Comparison
steps.explanation: Each model has different resource requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coroutines (asyncio)" A["Memory: ~1KB per coroutine
Creation: ~1μs
Context switch: ~100ns
Can have 100,000+ concurrent"] end subgraph "Threads" T["Memory: ~1MB per thread (stack)
Creation: ~1ms
Context switch: ~10μs
Practical limit: 1000s"] end subgraph "Processes" P["Memory: Full process copy
Creation: ~100ms
Context switch: ~1ms
Limited by CPU cores"] end subgraph "Scale" S["10K connections?
asyncio: Easy
threading: Difficult
multiprocessing: Impossible"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: I/O-Bound: asyncio Wins
steps.explanation: For I/O-bound tasks (network, files), asyncio is most efficient.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scenario: 1000 HTTP Requests" SC["Each request: 100ms network wait"] end subgraph "Threading Approach" T["1000 threads = 1GB+ memory
OS scheduler overhead
GIL contention"] end subgraph "asyncio Approach" A["1000 coroutines = ~1MB
Single thread
No lock contention
Clean cooperative switching"] end subgraph "Code Comparison" C1["# Threading
with ThreadPoolExecutor(1000) as e:
results = e.map(fetch, urls)"] C2["# asyncio
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:
results = await asyncio.gather(
*[fetch(s, url) for url in urls]
)"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: CPU-Bound: Multiprocessing Wins
steps.explanation: For CPU-intensive tasks, only multiprocessing provides true parallelism.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scenario: Image Processing" SC["Process 100 images
Each takes 1 second CPU time"] end subgraph "asyncio" A["Still single thread
100 seconds total
❌ No speedup"] end subgraph "threading" T["GIL blocks parallel execution
~100 seconds total
❌ No speedup"] end subgraph "multiprocessing" M["4 cores = 4 parallel workers
~25 seconds total
✓ Real speedup!"] end subgraph "Code" C["from multiprocessing import Pool

with Pool(4) as p:
results = p.map(process_image, images)"] end style M fill:#4CAF50,color:white style A fill:#FFCDD2 style T fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Hybrid Approach
steps.explanation: Combine models for complex workloads: asyncio for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Common Pattern" EL["Event Loop
(asyncio)"] PP["Process Pool
(CPU work)"] end EL -->|"offload CPU work"| PP PP -->|"return results"| EL subgraph "Code" C["async def main():
loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
with ProcessPoolExecutor() as pool:
# I/O work in event loop
data = await fetch_data()

# CPU work in process pool
result = await loop.run_in_executor(
pool, cpu_intensive, data
)"] end subgraph "Best of Both" B["asyncio handles 10K connections
Process pool handles CPU bursts"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: async/await Under the Hood
fields.description: What happens when you await

steps.title: Coroutines Are Generators
steps.explanation: Under the hood, `async def` creates a coroutine object, which is built on Python's generator machinery.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Generator (Old Style)" G["def gen():
yield 1
yield 2"] end subgraph "Coroutine (Async)" C["async def coro():
await something()
return result"] end subgraph "Key Insight" K["Both can pause and resume
yield pauses generators
await pauses coroutines"] end subgraph "Evolution" E["Python 3.4: yield from
Python 3.5: async/await
Coroutines = special generators"] end style K fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: What async def Creates
steps.explanation: Calling an async function doesn't run it - it creates a coroutine object.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Regular Function" RF["def regular():
return 42

result = regular() # Runs immediately
# result = 42"] end subgraph "Async Function" AF["async def async_func():
return 42

coro = async_func() # Creates coroutine
# coro = "] end subgraph "To Actually Run It" R["result = await coro # Inside async context
# OR
result = asyncio.run(coro) # Entry point"] end subgraph "Common Mistake" M["async_func() # Creates coroutine but never runs!
# RuntimeWarning: coroutine never awaited"] end style AF fill:#E3F2FD style M fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: The Awaitable Protocol
steps.explanation: An object is awaitable if it has `__await__` method. This includes coroutines, Tasks, and Futures.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Awaitable Types" C["Coroutine
(from async def)"] T["Task
(scheduled coroutine)"] F["Future
(placeholder for result)"] end subgraph "__await__ Method" A["class Awaitable:
def __await__(self):
yield # Pause point
return self.result"] end subgraph "What await Does" W["1. Calls obj.__await__()
2. Gets iterator
3. Yields control to event loop
4. Resumes when ready"] end C --> A T --> A F --> A style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Await Execution Flow
steps.explanation: Step-by-step: what happens when `await` is executed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" CODE["async def fetch():
result = await http_get(url)
return result"] end subgraph "Step 1" S1["await encounters http_get coroutine"] end subgraph "Step 2" S2["Coroutine yields to event loop
'I need to wait for network I/O'"] end subgraph "Step 3" S3["Event loop runs other tasks
Registers socket for read"] end subgraph "Step 4" S4["Network data arrives
Event loop resumes coroutine"] end subgraph "Step 5" S5["await returns the result
Coroutine continues"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 style S2 fill:#FFF9C4 style S4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Coroutine State Machine
steps.explanation: A coroutine is a state machine that remembers where it paused.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coroutine States" S1["CORO_CREATED
(just created)"] S2["CORO_RUNNING
(executing)"] S3["CORO_SUSPENDED
(at await)"] S4["CORO_CLOSED
(finished)"] end S1 -->|"first send()"| S2 S2 -->|"hits await"| S3 S3 -->|"resumed"| S2 S2 -->|"return/exception"| S4 subgraph "Inspect State" I["import inspect
inspect.getcoroutinestate(coro)
# Returns: 'CORO_SUSPENDED'"] end style S3 fill:#FFF9C4 style S4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Futures and Promises
steps.explanation: A Future is a container for a result that doesn't exist yet.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Future" F["asyncio.Future()
Placeholder for eventual result"] end subgraph "States" S1["Pending
(waiting for result)"] S2["Done
(has result or exception)"] S3["Cancelled"] end S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 subgraph "Methods" M1["future.result() - Get result"] M2["future.set_result(val) - Set result"] M3["future.done() - Check if done"] M4["future.add_done_callback(fn)"] end subgraph "Await on Future" A["result = await future
# Suspends until future is done
# Then returns the result"] end style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Task Wraps Coroutine
steps.explanation: A Task is a Future that wraps a coroutine and schedules it to run.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coroutine Alone" C["coro = fetch_data()
# Does nothing until awaited"] end subgraph "Task" T["task = asyncio.create_task(fetch_data())
# Scheduled to run immediately!"] end subgraph "Task = Future + Coroutine" TF["Task inherits from Future
+ Drives coroutine execution
+ Catches exceptions
+ Handles cancellation"] end subgraph "Key Difference" K["Coroutine: Lazy, needs await
Task: Eager, starts running now"] end C --> T style T fill:#4CAF50,color:white style K fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Bytecode View
steps.explanation: How Python compiles async/await to bytecode.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Source" S["async def example():
x = await fetch()
return x + 1"] end subgraph "Simplified Bytecode" B["GET_AWAITABLE
LOAD_CONST None
YIELD_FROM # Pause here
STORE_FAST x
..."] end subgraph "Key Instructions" K1["GET_AWAITABLE: Get __await__ iterator"] K2["YIELD_FROM: Delegate to sub-iterator"] K3["SEND: Resume with value"] end subgraph "Insight" I["await is syntactic sugar for
yield from coroutine.__await__()"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Custom Awaitable
steps.explanation: Create your own awaitable object.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Custom Awaitable Class" C["class AsyncTimer:
def __init__(self, delay):
self.delay = delay

def __await__(self):
yield from asyncio.sleep(self.delay).__await__()
return 'Done!'

# Usage
result = await AsyncTimer(1.0)"] end subgraph "Simpler: async def" S["async def async_timer(delay):
await asyncio.sleep(delay)
return 'Done!'

# Usually prefer this!"] end subgraph "When Custom Awaitable?" W["Need __await__ for special cases
Library integration
Advanced control flow"] end style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white style C fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Task Lifecycle
fields.description: Creating, running, cancelling asyncio tasks

steps.title: Task States
steps.explanation: A Task moves through several states during its lifecycle.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Task Lifecycle" A["Created
(Pending)"] --> B["Running"] B --> C["Suspended
(await)"] C --> B B --> D["Done"] A --> E["Cancelled"] B --> E C --> E end subgraph "Done States" D1["Success
(has result)"] D2["Failed
(has exception)"] end D --> D1 D --> D2 style A fill:#FFF9C4 style D fill:#4CAF50,color:white style E fill:#F44336,color:white

steps.title: Creating Tasks
steps.explanation: Different ways to create and schedule tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Recommended (Python 3.7+)" R["task = asyncio.create_task(coro())
# Name for debugging (3.8+)
