# Works with futures too
# Less explicit than create_task"] end subgraph "Direct Task Constructor" D["loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
task = loop.create_task(coro())"] end subgraph "Key Point" K["Task starts running immediately
after creation (in next loop iteration)"] end style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white style K fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Task vs Coroutine
steps.explanation: Understanding when tasks run vs when coroutines run.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coroutine (Lazy)" C["coro = fetch() # Nothing happens
await coro # Now it runs"] end subgraph "Task (Eager)" T["task = asyncio.create_task(fetch())
# Already scheduled!
result = await task # Wait for completion"] end subgraph "Concurrency Difference" CD1["# Sequential (one at a time)
a = await fetch_a()
b = await fetch_b()"] CD2["# Concurrent (both running)
task_a = asyncio.create_task(fetch_a())
task_b = asyncio.create_task(fetch_b())
a = await task_a
b = await task_b"] end style T fill:#4CAF50,color:white style CD2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Getting Task Results
steps.explanation: How to retrieve the result or exception from a completed task.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Await the Task" A["result = await task
# Waits until done, returns result"] end subgraph "Check Without Waiting" C["if task.done():
result = task.result() # May raise
exc = task.exception() # Or get exception"] end subgraph "Callback on Completion" CB["def handle_result(task):
if task.exception():
print(f'Failed: {task.exception()}')
else:
print(f'Result: {task.result()}')

task.add_done_callback(handle_result)"] end subgraph "Warning" W["task.result() raises InvalidStateError
if task is not done yet"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Cancelling Tasks
steps.explanation: How to cancel a running task and handle cancellation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cancel a Task" C["task.cancel()
# Requests cancellation
# Doesn't immediately stop!"] end subgraph "What Happens" W1["1. CancelledError raised at await point"] W2["2. Task can catch and ignore/cleanup"] W3["3. Task.cancelled() returns True"] end subgraph "Handle Cancellation" H["async def my_task():
try:
await long_operation()
except asyncio.CancelledError:
print('Cleaning up...')
raise # Re-raise to confirm cancel"] end subgraph "Check Status" S["task.cancelled() # Was it cancelled?
task.done() # Is it finished?"] end style C fill:#F44336,color:white style H fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Cancellation Best Practices
steps.explanation: Properly handling cancellation in your code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Swallowing Cancel" B["async def bad_task():
try:
await operation()
except asyncio.CancelledError:
pass # Silently ignores!
# Task appears to complete normally"] end subgraph "GOOD: Cleanup and Re-raise" G["async def good_task():
try:
await operation()
except asyncio.CancelledError:
await cleanup() # Clean up
raise # Propagate cancellation"] end subgraph "BETTER: Finally Block" BT["async def better_task():
try:
await operation()
finally:
await cleanup() # Always runs"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9 style BT fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Task Groups (Python 3.11+)
steps.explanation: TaskGroup provides structured concurrency - automatic cleanup on failure.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "TaskGroup" TG["async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
task1 = tg.create_task(fetch_a())
task2 = tg.create_task(fetch_b())
task3 = tg.create_task(fetch_c())
# All tasks complete or all cancelled"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Automatic cancellation on error"] B2["No orphaned tasks"] B3["ExceptionGroup for multiple errors"] B4["Clean structured concurrency"] end subgraph "If One Fails" F["Task 2 raises exception →
Task 1, 3 automatically cancelled →
TaskGroup raises ExceptionGroup"] end style TG fill:#4CAF50,color:white style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Getting All Tasks
steps.explanation: Inspecting and managing all running tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "List All Tasks" L["tasks = asyncio.all_tasks()
# Returns set of all tasks

current = asyncio.current_task()
# The currently running task"] end subgraph "Cancel All Tasks" C["for task in asyncio.all_tasks():
if task is not asyncio.current_task():
task.cancel()"] end subgraph "Wait for All" W["tasks = asyncio.all_tasks()
await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)"] end subgraph "Debugging" D["for task in asyncio.all_tasks():
print(f'{task.get_name()}: {task.get_coro()}')"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Task Patterns
steps.explanation: Common patterns for working with tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Fire and Forget" FF["task = asyncio.create_task(background_job())
# Add callback to log errors
task.add_done_callback(log_if_exception)"] end subgraph "Timeout" TO["try:
result = await asyncio.wait_for(task, timeout=5.0)
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
print('Task timed out')"] end subgraph "Shield from Cancellation" S["result = await asyncio.shield(important_task())
# Task continues even if outer is cancelled"] end subgraph "Wait for First" WF["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks, return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED
)"] end style FF fill:#E3F2FD style TO fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: asyncio.gather vs asyncio.wait
fields.description: Running multiple coroutines concurrently

steps.title: Two Ways to Wait
steps.explanation: asyncio provides two main functions for running multiple coroutines: `gather` and `wait`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "asyncio.gather" G["Simple, returns results in order
Best for 'run all, get all results'"] end subgraph "asyncio.wait" W["Flexible, returns task sets
Best for 'wait for first/any/timeout'"] end subgraph "Quick Guide" Q["Need results in order? → gather
Need to handle completion? → wait
Need timeout? → wait_for or wait"] end style G fill:#4CAF50,color:white style W fill:#2196F3,color:white

steps.title: asyncio.gather Basics
steps.explanation: gather runs multiple awaitables and returns their results as a list.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" B["results = await asyncio.gather(
fetch_user(1),
fetch_user(2),
fetch_user(3),
)
# results = [user1, user2, user3]"] end subgraph "Key Features" K1["Results in same order as inputs"] K2["All run concurrently"] K3["Returns when ALL complete"] end subgraph "Unpacking" U["user1, user2, user3 = await asyncio.gather(
fetch_user(1),
fetch_user(2),
fetch_user(3),
)"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style K1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: gather: Exception Handling
steps.explanation: By default, gather fails fast. Use `return_exceptions=True` to collect all results.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Default (Fail Fast)" D["try:
results = await asyncio.gather(a(), b(), c())
except Exception as e:
# First exception stops everything
# Other tasks still running!"] end subgraph "return_exceptions=True" R["results = await asyncio.gather(
a(), b(), c(),
return_exceptions=True
)
# results = [result_a, ValueError(...), result_c]
# Exceptions are returned, not raised"] end subgraph "Check Results" C["for result in results:
if isinstance(result, Exception):
handle_error(result)
else:
process(result)"] end style R fill:#4CAF50,color:white style D fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: asyncio.wait Basics
steps.explanation: wait returns two sets: done tasks and pending tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" B["tasks = [asyncio.create_task(c) for c in coros]

done, pending = await asyncio.wait(tasks)"] end subgraph "Returns" R1["done: Set of completed tasks"] R2["pending: Set of still-running tasks"] end subgraph "Get Results" G["for task in done:
try:
result = task.result()
except Exception as e:
handle_error(e)"] end subgraph "Important" I["wait() requires Task objects
Not bare coroutines!"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: wait: return_when Options
steps.explanation: Control when wait() returns with the `return_when` parameter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "ALL_COMPLETED (default)" AC["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks,
return_when=asyncio.ALL_COMPLETED
)
# Wait until ALL tasks done"] end subgraph "FIRST_COMPLETED" FC["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks,
return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED
)
# Return when ANY task completes"] end subgraph "FIRST_EXCEPTION" FE["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks,
return_when=asyncio.FIRST_EXCEPTION
)
# Return on first exception (or all done)"] end style FC fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: wait with Timeout
steps.explanation: Set a timeout for wait() - incomplete tasks go to pending.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Timeout Example" T["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks,
timeout=5.0
)

# After 5 seconds:
# done = tasks that completed
# pending = tasks still running"] end subgraph "Handle Pending" H["# Cancel tasks that didn't finish
for task in pending:
task.cancel()

# Or let them continue in background"] end subgraph "Pattern: Race with Timeout" P["done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks,
timeout=10.0,
return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED
)"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: asyncio.wait_for
steps.explanation: For single coroutine with timeout, use wait_for.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "wait_for" W["try:
result = await asyncio.wait_for(
slow_operation(),
timeout=5.0
)
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
print('Operation timed out')"] end subgraph "Key Difference" K["wait_for: Single awaitable + timeout
wait: Multiple tasks + flexible"] end subgraph "Timeout Behavior" T["On timeout:
- Task is CANCELLED
- TimeoutError raised
- Task doesn't continue"] end subgraph "Shield from Cancel" S["# Don't cancel on timeout
await asyncio.wait_for(
asyncio.shield(important()),
timeout=5.0
)"] end style W fill:#E3F2FD style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Practical patterns using gather and wait.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parallel API Calls" P["users = await asyncio.gather(*[
fetch_user(id) for id in user_ids
])"] end subgraph "Race: First Result" R["tasks = [get_from_cache(), get_from_db()]
done, pending = await asyncio.wait(
tasks, return_when=FIRST_COMPLETED
)
result = done.pop().result()
for t in pending: t.cancel()"] end subgraph "Batch with Progress" B["tasks = [asyncio.create_task(work(i)) for i in items]
while tasks:
done, tasks = await asyncio.wait(
tasks, return_when=FIRST_COMPLETED
)
for t in done:
print(f'Completed: {t.result()}')"] end style P fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Comparison Summary
steps.explanation: When to use each function.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use gather When" G1["Need results in order"] G2["All tasks must complete"] G3["Simple 'run all' pattern"] G4["Don't need to handle tasks individually"] end subgraph "Use wait When" W1["Need flexible completion conditions"] W2["Want to process as they complete"] W3["Need timeout with pending handling"] W4["Building complex coordination"] end subgraph "Use wait_for When" WF["Single operation with timeout"] end subgraph "Use TaskGroup When" TG["Python 3.11+
Automatic cancellation on error
Structured concurrency"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style W1 fill:#C8E6C9 style TG fill:#4CAF50,color:white

fields.title: Semaphores & Rate Limiting
fields.description: Controlling concurrency with asyncio primitives

steps.title: Why Limit Concurrency?
steps.explanation: Sometimes you need to limit how many tasks run simultaneously.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["1000 URLs to fetch
Launch all at once?
→ Server overwhelmed
→ Connection limits
→ Memory exhaustion"] end subgraph "Solution" S["Limit to 10 concurrent
→ Server happy
→ Connections available
→ Memory stable"] end subgraph "Tools" T1["Semaphore - Limit concurrent access"] T2["BoundedSemaphore - Stricter limits"] T3["Rate limiters - X per second"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Semaphore Basics
steps.explanation: A semaphore limits how many tasks can hold it at once.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Semaphore" SE["sem = asyncio.Semaphore(3)
# Only 3 tasks can hold at once"] end subgraph "Usage" U["async with sem:
# Only 3 here at a time
await do_work()"] end subgraph "Visualization" V["Task 1: [holds sem] working...
Task 2: [holds sem] working...
Task 3: [holds sem] working...
Task 4: [waiting...]
Task 5: [waiting...]"] end subgraph "When Task Finishes" W["Task 1 exits → sem released
Task 4 acquires → starts working"] end style SE fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Semaphore Pattern
steps.explanation: Common pattern: limit concurrent HTTP requests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Pattern" P["sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10) # Max 10 concurrent

async def fetch_limited(url):
async with sem: # Wait for slot
return await fetch(url)

# All 1000 scheduled, but only 10 run at once
results = await asyncio.gather(*[
fetch_limited(url) for url in urls
])"] end subgraph "Flow" F1["1000 tasks created"] F2["10 acquire semaphore"] F3["990 wait"] F4["As each finishes, next acquires"] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: BoundedSemaphore
steps.explanation: BoundedSemaphore prevents releasing more than acquired.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Regular Semaphore" R["sem = Semaphore(3)
sem.release() # Now it's 4!
sem.release() # Now it's 5!
# No error, limit exceeded"] end subgraph "BoundedSemaphore" B["sem = BoundedSemaphore(3)
sem.release() # ValueError!
# Can't release without acquire"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["BoundedSemaphore: Strict resource pools
Semaphore: When extra releases OK"] end subgraph "Best Practice" BP["Use 'async with' - auto releases
No manual release() needed"] end style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white style BP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Rate Limiter: Token Bucket
steps.explanation: Semaphore limits concurrent tasks. Rate limiter limits tasks per time period.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Difference" D["Semaphore: Max 10 at once
Rate Limiter: Max 100 per second"] end subgraph "Token Bucket Algorithm" TB["class RateLimiter:
def __init__(self, rate, per):
self.rate = rate # tokens
self.per = per # per X seconds
self.tokens = rate
self.updated_at = time.monotonic()

async def acquire(self):
while self.tokens < 1:
self.add_tokens()
await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
self.tokens -= 1"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD style TB fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Simple Rate Limiter
steps.explanation: A practical rate limiter implementation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Implementation" I["class RateLimiter:
def __init__(self, calls_per_second):
self.period = 1.0 / calls_per_second
self.last_call = 0
self.lock = asyncio.Lock()

async def __aenter__(self):
async with self.lock:
now = time.monotonic()
wait = self.last_call + self.period - now
if wait > 0:
await asyncio.sleep(wait)
self.last_call = time.monotonic()

async def __aexit__(self, *args): pass"] end subgraph "Usage" U["limiter = RateLimiter(10) # 10/second

async def fetch_rate_limited(url):
async with limiter:
return await fetch(url)"] end style I fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Combining Semaphore + Rate Limit
steps.explanation: Often you need both: limit concurrent AND limit rate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Both Limits" B["sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10) # Max 10 concurrent
limiter = RateLimiter(100) # Max 100/second

async def fetch_controlled(url):
async with sem: # Wait for concurrency slot
async with limiter: # Wait for rate slot
return await fetch(url)"] end subgraph "Why Both?" W["Semaphore: Don't overwhelm connections
Rate Limit: Don't overwhelm server"] end subgraph "Example" E["API allows 100 req/sec but only 10 connections
→ Need both limits"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style E fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Lock vs Semaphore
steps.explanation: Lock is a special case of Semaphore with count=1.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Lock" L["lock = asyncio.Lock()
# Only ONE task at a time
async with lock:
# Exclusive access"] end subgraph "Semaphore(1)" S["sem = asyncio.Semaphore(1)
# Same as Lock, but allows
# release without acquire"] end subgraph "Use Lock For" UL["Protecting shared state
Exclusive resource access
Critical sections"] end subgraph "Use Semaphore For" US["Connection pools
Throttling
Resource limits"] end style L fill:#2196F3,color:white style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for effective concurrency control.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use 'async with' for auto-release"] D2["Create semaphore ONCE, reuse"] D3["Set limits based on server capacity"] D4["Monitor for bottlenecks"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Create new semaphore per request"] DN2["Set limits too high (defeats purpose)"] DN3["Forget to handle timeout"] end subgraph "Timeout Pattern" T["try:
async with asyncio.timeout(5):
async with sem:
await work()
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
print('Could not acquire in time')"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Producer-Consumer Pattern
fields.description: Using asyncio.Queue for work distribution

steps.title: Producer-Consumer Pattern
steps.explanation: Producers create work items. Consumers process them. Queue connects them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Producers" P1["Producer 1"] P2["Producer 2"] end subgraph "Queue" Q["asyncio.Queue"] end subgraph "Consumers" C1["Consumer 1"] C2["Consumer 2"] C3["Consumer 3"] end P1 --> Q P2 --> Q Q --> C1 Q --> C2 Q --> C3 subgraph "Benefits" B["Decouple production from consumption
Handle rate differences
Distribute work to multiple workers"] end style Q fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: asyncio.Queue Basics
steps.explanation: asyncio.Queue is an async-safe FIFO queue.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Create Queue" C["queue = asyncio.Queue() # Unlimited
queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=100) # Bounded"] end subgraph "Producer" P["await queue.put(item) # Add item
# Blocks if queue full (bounded)"] end subgraph "Consumer" CO["item = await queue.get() # Get item
# Blocks if queue empty
queue.task_done() # Mark processed"] end subgraph "Methods" M["queue.qsize() # Current size
queue.empty() # Is empty?
queue.full() # Is full?"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Basic Producer-Consumer
steps.explanation: Simple example with one producer and one consumer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Producer" P["async def producer(queue):
for i in range(10):
await queue.put(f'item-{i}')
print(f'Produced item-{i}')
await queue.put(None) # Signal done"] end subgraph "Consumer" C["async def consumer(queue):
while True:
item = await queue.get()
if item is None:
break
print(f'Consumed {item}')
queue.task_done()"] end subgraph "Main" M["async def main():
queue = asyncio.Queue()
await asyncio.gather(
producer(queue),
consumer(queue)
)"] end style P fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Multiple Workers
steps.explanation: Scale by adding more consumer workers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Worker Pool" W["async def worker(name, queue):
while True:
item = await queue.get()
try:
await process(item)
finally:
queue.task_done()"] end subgraph "Create Workers" C["async def main():
queue = asyncio.Queue()

# Create 5 workers
workers = [
asyncio.create_task(worker(f'w-{i}', queue))
for i in range(5)
]

# Add work
for item in items:
await queue.put(item)

# Wait for completion
await queue.join() # Wait until all done

# Cancel workers
for w in workers:
w.cancel()"] end style W fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: queue.join() Pattern
steps.explanation: join() waits until all items are processed (task_done called).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Pattern" P["# Producer adds items
for item in items:
await queue.put(item)

# Wait for ALL items processed
await queue.join()

# Now we know everything is done"] end subgraph "How It Works" H1["put() increments internal counter"] H2["task_done() decrements counter"] H3["join() waits for counter = 0"] end subgraph "Critical" C["MUST call task_done() for every get()
Or join() hangs forever!"] end H1 --> H2 --> H3 style C fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Bounded Queue Backpressure
steps.explanation: Bounded queues provide backpressure - slow producers when consumers can't keep up.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Unbounded Queue Problem" U["queue = asyncio.Queue()
# Fast producer + slow consumer
# = Memory grows forever!"] end subgraph "Bounded Queue Solution" B["queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=100)
# put() blocks when full
# Producer slows down automatically"] end subgraph "Non-blocking Put" N["try:
queue.put_nowait(item)
except asyncio.QueueFull:
# Queue full, handle it
drop_or_wait()"] end style U fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Priority Queue
steps.explanation: Process high-priority items first with PriorityQueue.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "PriorityQueue" P["queue = asyncio.PriorityQueue()

# Items sorted by first element
await queue.put((1, 'urgent'))
await queue.put((10, 'low priority'))
await queue.put((5, 'medium'))

item = await queue.get() # (1, 'urgent')"] end subgraph "With Dataclass" D["@dataclass(order=True)
class Task:
priority: int
name: str = field(compare=False)

await queue.put(Task(1, 'urgent'))"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U["Job schedulers
Request prioritization
Event handling"] end style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LIFO Queue (Stack)
steps.explanation: Last-in-first-out processing with LifoQueue.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "LifoQueue" L["queue = asyncio.LifoQueue()

await queue.put('first')
await queue.put('second')
await queue.put('third')

await queue.get() # 'third'
await queue.get() # 'second'
await queue.get() # 'first'"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U["Depth-first traversal
Undo stacks
Most-recent processing"] end subgraph "Queue Types" T["Queue - FIFO (default)
PriorityQueue - By priority
LifoQueue - LIFO/Stack"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Real-World Pattern
steps.explanation: Complete example: URL crawler with worker pool.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Crawler" C["async def crawl(start_url, max_workers=10):
queue = asyncio.Queue()
seen = set()
results = []

async def worker():
while True:
url = await queue.get()
try:
links = await fetch_and_parse(url)
for link in links:
if link not in seen:
seen.add(link)
await queue.put(link)
finally:
queue.task_done()

workers = [asyncio.create_task(worker()) for _ in range(max_workers)]
await queue.put(start_url)
await queue.join()
for w in workers: w.cancel()"] end subgraph "Features" F["Bounded concurrency
Automatic work distribution
Graceful shutdown"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Timeouts & Cancellation
fields.description: Handling async operations safely with timeouts

steps.title: Why Timeouts Matter
steps.explanation: Without timeouts, async code can hang forever waiting for I/O.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["result = await fetch(url)
# What if server never responds?
# Your program hangs forever!"] end subgraph "Solution" S["result = await asyncio.wait_for(
fetch(url),
timeout=5.0
)
# Raises TimeoutError after 5s"] end subgraph "Timeout Scenarios" T1["Slow server"] T2["Network issues"] T3["Deadlocks"] T4["Infinite loops"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: asyncio.wait_for
steps.explanation: The primary way to add timeout to a single operation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Usage" B["try:
result = await asyncio.wait_for(
operation(),
timeout=10.0
)
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
print('Operation timed out')"] end subgraph "What Happens on Timeout" W1["1. TimeoutError raised"] W2["2. Inner task is CANCELLED"] W3["3. CancelledError propagates inside"] end subgraph "Important" I["The operation is STOPPED
not just ignored!"] end W1 --> W2 --> W3 style B fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: asyncio.timeout (Python 3.11+)
steps.explanation: Context manager for scoped timeouts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "timeout Context Manager" T["async with asyncio.timeout(5.0):
await step1()
await step2()
await step3()
# Total must complete in 5s"] end subgraph "vs wait_for" V["wait_for: Single operation
timeout: Multiple operations"] end subgraph "Handling" H["try:
async with asyncio.timeout(5.0):
await work()
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
print('Timed out')"] end subgraph "Check Time Remaining" C["async with asyncio.timeout(10) as cm:
await step1()
remaining = cm.when() - asyncio.get_event_loop().time()"] end style T fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: asyncio.timeout_at
steps.explanation: Set absolute deadline instead of relative timeout.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Absolute Deadline" A["loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
deadline = loop.time() + 30 # 30s from now

async with asyncio.timeout_at(deadline):
await operation1()
await operation2()
# Both must finish by deadline"] end subgraph "Use Case" U["Request has SLA: respond in 30s
Multiple steps share the budget"] end subgraph "Reschedule" R["async with asyncio.timeout(10) as cm:
await step1()
cm.reschedule(cm.when() + 5) # Add 5s"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Shield from Cancellation
steps.explanation: Protect important operations from cancellation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["On timeout, operation is cancelled
What if we need to finish it?"] end subgraph "asyncio.shield" S["try:
result = await asyncio.wait_for(
asyncio.shield(important_db_write()),
timeout=5.0
)
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
# TimeoutError raised
# BUT db_write continues in background!"] end subgraph "Caution" C["Shielded task keeps running
You may need to track it
Results might be lost"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Task Cancellation
steps.explanation: How to cancel running tasks programmatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cancel a Task" C["task = asyncio.create_task(work())

# Later...
task.cancel() # Request cancellation

try:
await task
except asyncio.CancelledError:
print('Task was cancelled')"] end subgraph "Cancel with Message (3.9+)" M["task.cancel('User requested stop')"] end subgraph "Check State" S["task.cancelled() # Was cancelled?
task.done() # Is finished?"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Handling CancelledError
steps.explanation: Properly handle cancellation in your async functions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Swallow Cancel" B["async def bad():
try:
await work()
except asyncio.CancelledError:
pass # Silently ignores!"] end subgraph "GOOD: Cleanup and Re-raise" G["async def good():
try:
await work()
except asyncio.CancelledError:
await cleanup()
raise # Re-raise!"] end subgraph "BEST: Finally" BE["async def best():
try:
await work()
finally:
await cleanup() # Always runs"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9 style BE fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Graceful Shutdown
steps.explanation: Cancel all tasks cleanly when shutting down.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Shutdown Pattern" S["async def shutdown():
# Get all running tasks
tasks = [t for t in asyncio.all_tasks()
if t is not asyncio.current_task()]

# Cancel all
for task in tasks:
task.cancel()

# Wait for cancellation
await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

# Now safe to close loop"] end subgraph "Signal Handling" SH["loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
for sig in (SIGTERM, SIGINT):
loop.add_signal_handler(
sig,
lambda: asyncio.create_task(shutdown())
)"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Timeout Patterns Summary
steps.explanation: Quick reference for different timeout scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Operation" S["await asyncio.wait_for(op(), timeout=5)"] end subgraph "Multiple Operations" M["async with asyncio.timeout(10):
await op1()
await op2()"] end subgraph "Absolute Deadline" A["async with asyncio.timeout_at(deadline):
await work()"] end subgraph "Protect from Cancel" P["await asyncio.shield(critical())"] end subgraph "Best Practices" BP["Always use timeouts for I/O
Handle TimeoutError explicitly
Clean up in finally blocks
Re-raise CancelledError after cleanup"] end style BP fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Async HTTP Requests
fields.description: aiohttp, httpx patterns for async HTTP clients

steps.title: Why Async HTTP?
steps.explanation: requests library blocks. For concurrent HTTP calls, use async libraries.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sync (requests)" S["import requests
for url in urls:
r = requests.get(url) # Blocks!
# Wait... wait... wait...
# 100 URLs × 100ms = 10 seconds"] end subgraph "Async (aiohttp/httpx)" A["async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:
tasks = [s.get(url) for url in urls]
responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
# 100 URLs concurrent = ~100ms"] end subgraph "Speedup" SP["100x faster for I/O-bound work!"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#C8E6C9 style SP fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Library Comparison
steps.explanation: Two main async HTTP libraries: aiohttp and httpx.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "aiohttp" A1["Mature, battle-tested"] A2["Async-only"] A3["Fast, lower memory"] A4["Server + client"] end subgraph "httpx" H1["Modern, requests-like API"] H2["Sync AND async"] H3["HTTP/2 support"] H4["Easy migration from requests"] end subgraph "Choose" C["New project: httpx (easier API)
High-performance: aiohttp
HTTP/2 needed: httpx"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: aiohttp Basics
steps.explanation: Using aiohttp for async HTTP requests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Session Pattern" S["async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
# GET
async with session.get(url) as resp:
data = await resp.json()

# POST
async with session.post(url, json=payload) as resp:
result = await resp.text()"] end subgraph "Important" I["ALWAYS use ClientSession context manager
Reuse session for multiple requests
Session manages connection pool"] end subgraph "Single Request" SR["async with aiohttp.request('GET', url) as r:
data = await r.json()"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: httpx Basics
steps.explanation: Using httpx for async HTTP - familiar requests-like API.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Async Client" A["async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
# GET
r = await client.get(url)
data = r.json()

# POST
r = await client.post(url, json=payload)
result = r.text"] end subgraph "Sync Client (Fallback)" S["# Can also use synchronously
r = httpx.get(url)
data = r.json()"] end subgraph "Migration from requests" M["# requests
r = requests.get(url)

# httpx (async)
r = await client.get(url)
# Same .json(), .text, .status_code"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style M fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Concurrent Requests
steps.explanation: The main benefit: making many requests at once.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "aiohttp Pattern" A["async def fetch_all(urls):
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async def fetch(url):
async with session.get(url) as r:
return await r.json()

return await asyncio.gather(*[
fetch(url) for url in urls
])"] end subgraph "httpx Pattern" H["async def fetch_all(urls):
async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
tasks = [client.get(url) for url in urls]
responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
return [r.json() for r in responses]"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style H fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Rate Limiting Requests
steps.explanation: Don't overwhelm the server - use semaphores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Limited Concurrency" L["async def fetch_all(urls, max_concurrent=10):
sem = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

async def fetch(session, url):
async with sem:
async with session.get(url) as r:
return await r.json()

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
return await asyncio.gather(*[
fetch(session, url) for url in urls
])"] end subgraph "Why?" W["Server rate limits
Connection pool limits
Memory management"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Error Handling & Retries
steps.explanation: Handle network errors and implement retry logic.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Error Handling" E["async def safe_fetch(client, url):
try:
r = await client.get(url, timeout=10)
r.raise_for_status()
return r.json()
except httpx.TimeoutException:
return {'error': 'timeout'}
except httpx.HTTPStatusError as e:
return {'error': str(e.response.status_code)}"] end subgraph "Retry Pattern" R["async def fetch_with_retry(client, url, retries=3):
for i in range(retries):
try:
r = await client.get(url)
r.raise_for_status()
return r.json()
except (httpx.HTTPError, httpx.TimeoutException):
if i == retries - 1:
raise
await asyncio.sleep(2 ** i) # Backoff"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Connection Pooling
steps.explanation: Sessions maintain connection pools for efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Connection Pool" CP["async with aiohttp.ClientSession(
connector=aiohttp.TCPConnector(
limit=100, # Total connections
limit_per_host=10, # Per host
ttl_dns_cache=300, # DNS cache TTL
)
) as session:
# All requests reuse connections"] end subgraph "httpx Limits" H["async with httpx.AsyncClient(
limits=httpx.Limits(
max_connections=100,
max_keepalive_connections=20
)
) as client:
pass"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["TCP handshake reuse
TLS session reuse
Lower latency"] end style CP fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for async HTTP clients.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Reuse session/client across requests"] D2["Use connection limits"] D3["Set timeouts"] D4["Handle errors gracefully"] D5["Use context managers"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Create new session per request"] DN2["Ignore rate limits"] DN3["Forget to close sessions"] DN4["Block the event loop"] end subgraph "Pattern" P["# At app startup
app.state.client = httpx.AsyncClient()

# In handlers
r = await app.state.client.get(url)

# At shutdown
await app.state.client.aclose()"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Async Database Access

steps.title: Why Async Database?
steps.explanation: Database I/O can block. Async drivers let other tasks run while waiting.

# Thread waits 10ms for DB
# Can't do anything else"] end subgraph "Async Database" A["result = await db.execute(query) # Yields
# Event loop runs other tasks
# Returns when DB responds"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Handle more requests per server
Better resource utilization
Required for async web frameworks"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#C8E6C9

)

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for async database access.

steps.title: HTTP vs WebSocket

Live dashboards
Gaming
Real-time updates"] end style W1 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

async def handler(websocket):
async for message in websocket:

async def main():

finally:

await ws.send(message)
except websockets.ConnectionClosed:

steps.title: Structured Messages

{'type': 'join', 'room': 'general'}

async for raw in websocket:

steps.title: Pub/Sub Pattern
steps.explanation: Implement channels/rooms for targeted messaging.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Room Manager" R["rooms: dict[str, set[WebSocket]] = defaultdict(set)

async def join_room(ws, room):
rooms[room].add(ws)

async def leave_room(ws, room):
rooms[room].discard(ws)

async def broadcast_to_room(room, message):
for ws in rooms[room]:
await ws.send(message)"] end subgraph "Redis Pub/Sub" RD["# For multi-server deployment

async def subscribe():

steps.explanation: Guidelines for WebSocket applications.

Multiple servers: Redis pub/sub
Load balancer: sticky sessions"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Common Async Anti-Patterns
fields.description: Blocking the event loop, forgotten awaits, and other mistakes

steps.title: The Cardinal Sin: Blocking
steps.explanation: Never call blocking functions in async code - it freezes the entire event loop.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BLOCKING (Bad)" B["async def bad():
time.sleep(5) # Blocks everything!
requests.get(url) # Blocks!
open('file').read() # Blocks!"] end subgraph "What Happens" W["All other tasks STOP
Event loop frozen
Server unresponsive"] end subgraph "NON-BLOCKING (Good)" G["async def good():
await asyncio.sleep(5)
await httpx.get(url)
await aiofiles.open('file')"] end style B fill:#F44336,color:white style G fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Forgotten await
steps.explanation: Calling async function without await creates coroutine but doesn't run it.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bug" B["async def process():
fetch_data() # Missing await!
# RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'fetch_data' never awaited
# Data NOT fetched!"] end subgraph "Fix" F["async def process():
await fetch_data() # Correct!
data = await fetch_data() # Even better"] end subgraph "Debugging" D["# Enable warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('error', category=RuntimeWarning)

# Or use debug mode
asyncio.run(main(), debug=True)"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Creating Tasks But Never Awaiting
steps.explanation: Created tasks need to be awaited or they may be garbage collected.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bug" B["async def bad():
asyncio.create_task(background_job())
# Task might be garbage collected!
# Or exception goes unhandled"] end subgraph "Fix 1: Keep Reference" F1["tasks = set()

async def good():
task = asyncio.create_task(background_job())
tasks.add(task)
task.add_done_callback(tasks.discard)"] end subgraph "Fix 2: Await Later" F2["async def good():
task = asyncio.create_task(job())
# ... do other work ...
await task # Wait for completion"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style F1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Sequential Instead of Concurrent
steps.explanation: Awaiting in sequence when tasks could run concurrently.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sequential (Slow)" S["async def slow():
a = await fetch_a() # 100ms
b = await fetch_b() # 100ms
c = await fetch_c() # 100ms
# Total: 300ms"] end subgraph "Concurrent (Fast)" C["async def fast():
a, b, c = await asyncio.gather(
fetch_a(),
fetch_b(),
fetch_c()
)
# Total: 100ms"] end subgraph "When Sequential is OK" W["When b depends on a
When order matters
When rate-limiting"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style C fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Sync Code in Async Functions
steps.explanation: Using sync libraries in async code blocks the loop.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Common Mistakes" M1["requests.get(url) # Use httpx/aiohttp"] M2["psycopg2.connect() # Use asyncpg"] M3["time.sleep(5) # Use asyncio.sleep"] M4["open().read() # Use aiofiles"] M5["subprocess.run() # Use asyncio.subprocess"] end subgraph "If You Must Use Sync" S["# Run in thread pool
loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
result = await loop.run_in_executor(
None, # Default ThreadPoolExecutor
sync_function,
arg1, arg2
)"] end style M1 fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Improper Exception Handling
steps.explanation: Exceptions in async code can be tricky to handle.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Lost Exception" L["task = asyncio.create_task(buggy())
# Exception happens but nobody sees it!"] end subgraph "gather Hides Errors" G["results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
# One exception = all fail, others cancelled"] end subgraph "Better" B["# Option 1: return_exceptions
results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
for r in results:
if isinstance(r, Exception):
handle_error(r)

# Option 2: Done callback
task.add_done_callback(log_exception)"] end style L fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Resource Leaks
steps.explanation: Not closing async resources properly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Leak" L["session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
# ... use session ...
# Never closed! Connections leak"] end subgraph "Fix: Context Manager" F["async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
# ... use session ...
# Automatically closed"] end subgraph "Common Resources" R["ClientSession (aiohttp/httpx)
Database connections
File handles
WebSocket connections"] end style L fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Mixing asyncio.run()
steps.explanation: Don't call asyncio.run() from inside async code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bug" B["async def handler():
asyncio.run(other_coro()) # ERROR!
# RuntimeError: cannot run nested event loops"] end subgraph "Fix" F["async def handler():
await other_coro() # Just await it!"] end subgraph "Rules" R["asyncio.run(): Only at top level
await: Inside async functions
create_task(): Schedule without waiting"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Anti-Pattern Checklist
steps.explanation: Quick reference to avoid common mistakes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Avoid" A1["✗ time.sleep() → asyncio.sleep()"] A2["✗ requests → httpx/aiohttp"] A3["✗ Forgotten await"] A4["✗ Sequential when concurrent possible"] A5["✗ Blocking file I/O → aiofiles"] A6["✗ Unhandled task exceptions"] A7["✗ Resource leaks"] A8["✗ Nested asyncio.run()"] end subgraph "Debugging Tools" D["asyncio.run(main(), debug=True)
PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1
warnings.filterwarnings('error')"] end style A1 fill:#FFCDD2 style D fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: SHAP Values Explained
fields.description: Shapley values, force plots, summary plots

steps.title: What is SHAP?
steps.explanation: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) uses game theory to explain model predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["How much did each feature
contribute to this prediction?"] end subgraph "SHAP Answer" S["Prediction = Base Value + Σ SHAP values

Example: House Price = $200k
Base (avg): $180k
+$30k (size)
-$15k (age)
+$5k (location)"] end subgraph "Key Properties" P1["Local accuracy: Sums to prediction"] P2["Consistency: Same contribution = same value"] P3["Missingness: Missing features = 0"] end style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Shapley Values from Game Theory
steps.explanation: SHAP is based on Shapley values - how to fairly distribute a payout among players.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Game Theory Analogy" G["Players = Features
Payout = Prediction
Coalition = Feature subset"] end subgraph "The Idea" I["What's the average contribution of a feature
across ALL possible feature orderings?"] end subgraph "Calculation" C["For feature i:
SHAP(i) = Average of:
f({features before i} ∪ {i}) - f({features before i})
across all orderings"] end subgraph "Complexity" X["2^n subsets to consider
Exact: O(2^n) - expensive!
Approximations needed"] end style I fill:#E3F2FD style X fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: SHAP Explainer Types
steps.explanation: Different explainers for different model types.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "TreeExplainer" T["For tree models
XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest
Fast, exact computation"] end subgraph "DeepExplainer" D["For deep learning
Neural networks
Uses DeepLIFT algorithm"] end subgraph "KernelExplainer" K["Model-agnostic
Works with ANY model
Slower, approximate"] end subgraph "LinearExplainer" L["For linear models
Exact, very fast"] end subgraph "Choose" CH["Tree model? → TreeExplainer
Neural net? → DeepExplainer
Other? → KernelExplainer"] end style T fill:#4CAF50,color:white style CH fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Computing SHAP Values
steps.explanation: Basic usage of the SHAP library.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["import shap

# Train your model
model = xgb.XGBClassifier()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)"] end subgraph "Create Explainer" E["# For tree models
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)

# For any model
explainer = shap.KernelExplainer(model.predict, X_train)"] end subgraph "Compute Values" C["# For one sample
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test[0:1])

# For multiple samples
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)"] end subgraph "Output Shape" O["shap_values.shape = (n_samples, n_features)
Each value = feature's contribution"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Force Plot: Single Prediction
steps.explanation: Visualize how features push prediction from base value.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Force Plot" F["shap.force_plot(
explainer.expected_value,
shap_values[0],
X_test.iloc[0]
)"] end subgraph "Reading the Plot" R["Base value (average) in center
Red features push prediction UP
Blue features push prediction DOWN
Width = magnitude of effect"] end subgraph "Example" E["Base: 0.5 (50% probability)
age=65 → +0.15 (red)
income=high → +0.10 (red)
history=bad → -0.20 (blue)
Final: 0.55 (55%)"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Summary Plot: Global View
steps.explanation: See feature importance across all predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Summary Plot" S["shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test)"] end subgraph "Beeswarm Plot" B["Each dot = one prediction
Y-axis = features (sorted by importance)
X-axis = SHAP value
Color = feature value (high/low)"] end subgraph "What to Look For" W1["Wide spread = high importance"] W2["Color pattern = relationship"] W3["Red right = high value → high pred"] W4["Blue right = low value → high pred"] end subgraph "Bar Version" BR["shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, plot_type='bar')
# Shows mean |SHAP| per feature"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Dependence Plot
steps.explanation: How one feature affects predictions, with interactions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dependence Plot" D["shap.dependence_plot(
'feature_name',
shap_values,
X_test
)"] end subgraph "Reading It" R["X-axis = Feature value
Y-axis = SHAP value
Color = Interacting feature"] end subgraph "Example Insight" E["Age dependence plot shows:
- SHAP increases with age
- But colored by income:
High income (red) = steeper increase"] end subgraph "Interaction Effects" I["shap.dependence_plot('age', shap_values, X_test,
interaction_index='income')"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Waterfall Plot
steps.explanation: Step-by-step breakdown for one prediction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Waterfall Plot" W["shap.plots.waterfall(shap_values[0])"] end subgraph "Shows" S["E[f(x)] = 0.35 (base)
────────────────
age = +0.12
income = +0.08
history = -0.05
... other features ...
────────────────
f(x) = 0.55 (final)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Clear feature-by-feature breakdown
Easy to explain to stakeholders
Shows cumulative effect"] end style W fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: SHAP Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for using SHAP effectively.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use TreeExplainer for tree models (fast)"] D2["Sample background data for KernelExplainer"] D3["Check that SHAP values sum to prediction"] D4["Use multiple plot types for insights"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Compare SHAP values across models"] DN2["Assume causation from SHAP"] DN3["Use on untrained/bad models"] end subgraph "Performance Tips" P["# For large datasets
background = shap.sample(X_train, 100)
explainer = shap.KernelExplainer(model, background)"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN2 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: LIME: Local Interpretable Explanations
fields.description: How LIME approximates complex models locally

steps.title: What is LIME?
steps.explanation: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) explains predictions by approximating the model locally.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Idea" I["Complex model = Black box globally
But near one point, can be approximated
by a simple, interpretable model"] end subgraph "LIME Approach" L["1. Pick a prediction to explain
2. Generate nearby samples
3. Get model predictions for them
4. Fit simple model (linear) locally
5. Use simple model to explain"] end subgraph "Result" R["'For THIS prediction,
feature A increased probability by 20%'"] end style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: How LIME Works
steps.explanation: Step-by-step process of generating a LIME explanation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Step 1: Sample Point" S1["x = instance to explain
f(x) = model's prediction"] end subgraph "Step 2: Perturb" S2["Generate Z = perturbed samples
around x (add noise, mask features)"] end subgraph "Step 3: Predict" S3["Get f(z) for each z in Z
These are the 'labels'"] end subgraph "Step 4: Weight" S4["Weight samples by distance to x
Closer = higher weight"] end subgraph "Step 5: Fit" S5["Fit interpretable model g
(linear, decision tree)
to weighted (Z, f(Z))"] end subgraph "Step 6: Explain" S6["Use coefficients of g
as feature importances"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 style S5 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: LIME for Tabular Data
steps.explanation: Using LIME on structured data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["from lime.lime_tabular import LimeTabularExplainer

explainer = LimeTabularExplainer(
X_train.values,
feature_names=X_train.columns,
class_names=['No', 'Yes'],
mode='classification'
)"] end subgraph "Explain Instance" E["exp = explainer.explain_instance(
X_test.iloc[0].values,
model.predict_proba,
num_features=10
)

exp.show_in_notebook()"] end subgraph "Output" O["Feature contributions:
age > 50: +0.25
income < 30k: -0.15
employed = True: +0.10"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LIME for Text
steps.explanation: Explaining text classification predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["from lime.lime_text import LimeTextExplainer

explainer = LimeTextExplainer(
class_names=['negative', 'positive']
)"] end subgraph "Explain" E["exp = explainer.explain_instance(
'This movie was absolutely terrible',
classifier.predict_proba,
num_features=6
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["Words highlighted:
'terrible' → negative (strong)
'absolutely' → negative (moderate)
'movie' → neutral"] end subgraph "How It Works" H["Randomly removes words
Checks prediction change
Words that flip prediction = important"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LIME for Images
steps.explanation: Explaining image classification predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Setup" S["from lime import lime_image

explainer = lime_image.LimeImageExplainer()"] end subgraph "Explain" E["exp = explainer.explain_instance(
image,
classifier.predict,
top_labels=3,
hide_color=0,
num_samples=1000
)"] end subgraph "Visualization" V["temp, mask = exp.get_image_and_mask(
label=predicted_class,
positive_only=True
)
# Shows superpixels that support prediction"] end subgraph "How It Works" H["Segments image into superpixels
Randomly masks superpixels
Checks prediction change"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LIME Parameters
steps.explanation: Key parameters that affect LIME explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "num_samples" NS["Number of perturbed samples
More = more stable, slower
Default: 5000"] end subgraph "num_features" NF["Features in explanation
Top K most important
Default: 10"] end subgraph "kernel_width" KW["How local is 'local'?
Smaller = more local
Affects sample weights"] end subgraph "discretize_continuous" DC["Bin continuous features?
True: 'age > 50' style
False: 'age = 0.23' coefficient"] end style NS fill:#E3F2FD style KW fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: LIME vs SHAP
steps.explanation: When to use LIME vs SHAP.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "LIME" L1["✓ Faster for single explanations"] L2["✓ Works with any model"] L3["✓ Good for text/images"] L4["✗ Less consistent (random sampling)"] L5["✗ Sensitive to parameters"] end subgraph "SHAP" S1["✓ Theoretically grounded"] S2["✓ Consistent across runs"] S3["✓ Great global summaries"] S4["✗ Slower for some models"] S5["✗ Harder for text/images"] end subgraph "Choose" C["Quick local explanation? → LIME
Tree model? → SHAP (TreeExplainer)
Need consistency? → SHAP
Text/image? → LIME"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: LIME Limitations
steps.explanation: Understanding LIME's weaknesses.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Stability Issues" S["Run LIME twice on same instance
May get different explanations!
Due to random sampling"] end subgraph "Locality Definition" L["What is 'local'?
kernel_width affects this
Wrong setting = bad explanations"] end subgraph "Linear Assumption" LA["Fits linear model locally
If true boundary is curved
Explanation may be misleading"] end subgraph "Mitigations" M["Increase num_samples
Run multiple times, average
Validate with domain knowledge"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style M fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: LIME Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for reliable LIME explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use sufficient num_samples (>1000)"] D2["Test stability (run multiple times)"] D3["Validate against domain knowledge"] D4["Use discretize for interpretability"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Trust single run blindly"] DN2["Use tiny kernel_width"] DN3["Explain bad models"] end subgraph "Production Pattern" P["# Run 5 times, check consistency
explanations = [explain(x) for _ in range(5)]
if consistent(explanations):
return average(explanations)
else:
flag_for_review()"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Feature Importance Methods
fields.description: Permutation, impurity-based, drop-column comparison

steps.title: Types of Feature Importance
steps.explanation: Different methods to measure which features matter most.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Three Main Approaches" I["Impurity-Based
(tree models only)"] P["Permutation Importance
(model-agnostic)"] D["Drop-Column
(most expensive)"] end subgraph "Key Difference" K["Impurity: How features split data
Permutation: How shuffling hurts accuracy
Drop: How removing feature hurts model"] end I --> K P --> K D --> K style P fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Impurity-Based Importance
steps.explanation: Built into tree models - measures how much each feature reduces impurity.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "How It Works" H["For each tree node split:
- Calculate impurity reduction
- Attribute to splitting feature
- Sum across all nodes & trees"] end subgraph "Code" C["# Built into sklearn
model = RandomForestClassifier().fit(X, y)
importances = model.feature_importances_

# Plot
pd.Series(importances, index=X.columns)
.sort_values().plot(kind='barh')"] end subgraph "Advantages" A["Free (computed during training)
Fast
Works with any tree model"] end subgraph "Disadvantages" D["Biased toward high-cardinality
Doesn't work on test set
Can be misleading with correlated features"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style D fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Permutation Importance
steps.explanation: Shuffle a feature's values and measure performance drop.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Algorithm" A1["1. Get baseline score on test set"] A2["2. For each feature:"] A3[" - Shuffle feature values"] A4[" - Measure new score"] A5[" - Importance = baseline - new"] A6["3. Repeat for stability"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 subgraph "Intuition" I["If feature matters:
Shuffling breaks relationship
Score drops significantly"] end style A5 fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Permutation Importance Code
steps.explanation: Using sklearn's permutation importance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "sklearn Implementation" S["from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

result = permutation_importance(
model, X_test, y_test,
n_repeats=10,
random_state=42,
scoring='accuracy'
)

importances = result.importances_mean"] end subgraph "Plot" P["sorted_idx = importances.argsort()
plt.barh(X.columns[sorted_idx], importances[sorted_idx])
plt.xlabel('Permutation Importance')"] end subgraph "With Confidence" C["# result.importances has shape (n_features, n_repeats)
plt.boxplot(result.importances.T)"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Drop-Column Importance
steps.explanation: Remove feature entirely and retrain - most accurate but expensive.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Algorithm" A["1. Train model on all features, get score
2. For each feature:
- Remove it from data
- Retrain model
- Get new score
3. Importance = original - new score"] end subgraph "Code" C["baseline = cross_val_score(model, X, y).mean()

importances = {}
for col in X.columns:
X_drop = X.drop(columns=[col])
score = cross_val_score(model, X_drop, y).mean()
importances[col] = baseline - score"] end subgraph "Trade-off" T["Most accurate importance
But: n_features × training time
Often impractical"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Correlated Features Problem
steps.explanation: Feature importance can be misleading with correlated features.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["Features A and B are correlated (r=0.95)
Both equally important in reality
But importance may show:
A = 0.8, B = 0.1"] end subgraph "Why?" W["Impurity: One feature does the work
Permutation: Shuffling A, B compensates
Drop: Removing A, B fills in"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Remove correlated features first"] S2["Use grouped permutation"] S3["Use SHAP (handles it better)"] S4["Domain knowledge"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Comparison Summary
steps.explanation: When to use which method.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Impurity-Based" I["Speed: ⚡ Instant
Model: Trees only
Correlated: ❌ Misleading
Use: Quick exploration"] end subgraph "Permutation" P["Speed: 🔄 Moderate
Model: Any
Correlated: ⚠️ Can mislead
Use: General purpose"] end subgraph "Drop-Column" D["Speed: 🐢 Slow
Model: Any
Correlated: ✓ Most accurate
Use: Critical decisions"] end subgraph "Recommendation" R["Start: Permutation importance
Verify: SHAP for detailed view
Production: Monitor with permutation"] end style P fill:#4CAF50,color:white style R fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for reliable feature importance analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use test set for permutation importance"] D2["Run multiple repeats for stability"] D3["Check for correlated features first"] D4["Combine with SHAP for full picture"] D5["Validate with domain experts"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Trust impurity importance blindly"] DN2["Ignore negative importance values"] DN3["Use on overfit models"] end subgraph "Negative Importance?" N["Feature hurts model!
Adding noise helps generalization
Sign of overfitting or bad feature"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)
fields.description: Marginal effect of features on predictions

steps.title: What is PDP?
steps.explanation: Partial Dependence Plots show the **marginal effect** of a feature on predictions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["How does changing feature X
affect the average prediction?"] end subgraph "PDP Answer" A["For each value of X:
- Set all samples to that value
- Get predictions
- Average them
Plot average vs X values"] end subgraph "Example" E["PDP for 'age' shows:
As age increases 20→60,
average prediction increases 0.2→0.7"] end style A fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: How PDP Works
steps.explanation: Step-by-step calculation of partial dependence.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Algorithm" A1["1. Choose feature and grid of values"] A2["2. For each grid value v:"] A3[" a. Set feature = v for ALL samples"] A4[" b. Get model predictions"] A5[" c. Average predictions"] A6["3. Plot (v, avg_prediction)"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 subgraph "Example" E["Grid: age = [20, 30, 40, 50, 60]
For age=40:
Set all 1000 samples age=40
Get predictions
Average = 0.45"] end style A5 fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Creating PDPs in Python
steps.explanation: Using sklearn's PartialDependenceDisplay.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "sklearn Method" S["from sklearn.inspection import PartialDependenceDisplay

# Single feature
PartialDependenceDisplay.from_estimator(
model, X_train, features=['age']
)
plt.show()"] end subgraph "Multiple Features" M["PartialDependenceDisplay.from_estimator(
model, X_train,
features=['age', 'income', 'years_employed']
)"] end subgraph "2D PDP" D["# Interaction between two features
PartialDependenceDisplay.from_estimator(
model, X_train,
features=[('age', 'income')]
)"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: ICE Plots
steps.explanation: Individual Conditional Expectation - PDP for each sample.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "ICE vs PDP" I["ICE: One line per sample
PDP: Average of all ICE lines"] end subgraph "Code" C["PartialDependenceDisplay.from_estimator(
model, X_train, features=['age'],
kind='both' # ICE + PDP
)"] end subgraph "Why ICE?" W["PDP averages can hide heterogeneity
ICE shows if effect varies by sample"] end subgraph "Example" E["PDP: flat line (no avg effect)
ICE: half going up, half going down
→ Interaction with another feature!"] end style W fill:#FFF9C4 style I fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Reading PDP/ICE Plots
steps.explanation: How to interpret the visualizations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Positive Slope" P["↗ As feature increases
prediction increases"] end subgraph "Negative Slope" N["↘ As feature increases
prediction decreases"] end subgraph "Flat Line" F["─ Feature has no effect
on average prediction"] end subgraph "Non-linear" NL["∿ Threshold effects
Saturation
Complex relationships"] end subgraph "Spread ICE Lines" S["Wide spread = heterogeneous effect
Tight = consistent across samples"] end style NL fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: 2D Partial Dependence
steps.explanation: Visualize interaction between two features.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "2D PDP" D["Contour plot or heatmap
X-axis: Feature 1 values
Y-axis: Feature 2 values
Color: Average prediction"] end subgraph "Code" C["PartialDependenceDisplay.from_estimator(
model, X_train,
features=[('age', 'income')],
kind='average'
)"] end subgraph "Interpretation" I["Parallel contours = no interaction
Non-parallel = interaction exists
'Effect of age depends on income'"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: PDP Limitations
steps.explanation: When PDPs can be misleading.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Assumes Independence" A["PDP assumes features are independent
Sets feature to values that may be
impossible in reality"] end subgraph "Example" E["Age=25, Retired=True
Doesn't exist in real data
But PDP evaluates it"] end subgraph "Heterogeneity Hidden" H["Averaging hides different effects
for different subgroups
→ Use ICE plots"] end subgraph "Solutions" S["Check feature correlations
Use ICE for heterogeneity
ALE plots (handle correlations)"] end style A fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Accumulated Local Effects (ALE)

PDP: Sets features globally"] end subgraph "ALE Algorithm" AL["For each interval of feature X:
- Use only samples in that range
- Compute local effect
- Accumulate effects"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Handles correlated features
More realistic scenarios
Faster computation"] end subgraph "Library" L["# alibi library
from alibi.explainers import ALE
ale = ALE(model.predict)
ale.explain(X_train)"] end style AL fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for effective PDP analysis.

fields.title: Tree-Based Explainability

steps.title: Why Trees Are Interpretable

Age ≤ 50 AND Student = Yes'

Clear, human-readable rules!"] end style EX fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Decision Path Extraction
steps.explanation: Get the exact path through the tree for a prediction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" C["from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

model = DecisionTreeClassifier()
model.fit(X, y)

# Get decision path for sample
path = model.decision_path(X_test[0:1])

# path is sparse matrix
# Non-zero entries = nodes visited"] end subgraph "Extract Rules" E["feature = model.tree_.feature
threshold = model.tree_.threshold

node_indices = path.indices
for node in node_indices:
if feature[node] != -2: # Not leaf
print(f'{feature_names[feature[node]]}
{'<=' if going_left else '>'}
{threshold[node]}')"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Visualizing Decision Trees

plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10))

)
plt.show()"] end subgraph "export_graphviz" G["from sklearn.tree import export_graphviz
import graphviz

dot = export_graphviz(model, ...)
graphviz.Source(dot)"] end subgraph "dtreeviz" D["from dtreeviz.trees import dtreeviz

viz = dtreeviz(model, X, y, ...)
viz.view() # Beautiful viz!"] end style D fill:#4CAF50,color:white

Computes exact Shapley values

RandomForest, GradientBoosting
Any sklearn tree ensemble"] end subgraph "Speed" SP["1000x faster than KernelSHAP
Exact, not approximate"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD style SP fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Attention Visualization

steps.title: Attention as Explanation
steps.explanation: Attention weights show which parts of input the model focuses on.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Idea" I["Attention = soft alignment
High weight = 'model looked here'"] end subgraph "Example" E["Input: 'The movie was terrible'
Sentiment: Negative

Attention on 'terrible': 0.7
Attention on 'movie': 0.2
Attention on 'The', 'was': 0.1"] end subgraph "Caution" C["Attention ≠ Explanation
High attention doesn't mean
causal importance"] end style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Extracting Attention Weights
steps.explanation: Getting attention weights from transformer models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "HuggingFace" H["from transformers import AutoModel

model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
outputs = model(**inputs, output_attentions=True)

attentions = outputs.attentions
# Tuple of (batch, heads, seq, seq) per layer"] end subgraph "Shape" S["len(attentions) = num_layers (12)
attentions[0].shape = (1, 12, seq_len, seq_len)

Each layer has 12 attention heads
Each head has seq×seq attention matrix"] end style H fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Visualizing Attention
steps.explanation: Tools for attention visualization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BertViz" B["from bertviz import head_view, model_view

# Interactive attention visualization
head_view(attention, tokens)
model_view(attention, tokens)"] end subgraph "Manual Heatmap" M["import seaborn as sns

attention = attentions[layer][0, head].detach()
sns.heatmap(attention, xticklabels=tokens,
yticklabels=tokens)"] end subgraph "Aggregate Heads" A["# Average across heads
avg_attention = attentions[layer].mean(dim=1)

# Or max
max_attention = attentions[layer].max(dim=1)"] end style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Attention Rollout
steps.explanation: Propagate attention through layers for better explanation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Problem" P["Raw attention is layer-specific
Doesn't show full picture
Information flows through layers"] end subgraph "Attention Rollout" AR["For each layer:
attention = attention + residual
attention = attention @ previous_attention

Accumulated attention through network"] end subgraph "Code" C["def attention_rollout(attentions):
result = torch.eye(attentions[0].shape[-1])
for attention in attentions:
attention = attention.mean(dim=1) # avg heads
attention = attention + torch.eye(attention.shape[-1])
attention = attention / attention.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
result = attention @ result
return result"] end style AR fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Attention for Text Classification
steps.explanation: Which tokens influenced the classification?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CLS Token Attention" C["# For classification, look at [CLS] attention
cls_attention = attentions[-1][0, :, 0, :]
# Shape: (n_heads, seq_len)

# Which tokens does [CLS] attend to?
token_importance = cls_attention.mean(dim=0)"] end subgraph "Visualization" V["for token, score in zip(tokens, token_importance):
color = score_to_color(score)
print(f'{token}', color=color)"] end subgraph "Example Output" E["The [0.1] movie [0.2] was [0.1] terrible [0.6]
^^^^^^^^
Most attended"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Attention in Vision Transformers
steps.explanation: Visualizing what ViT attends to in images.

Attention shows patch relationships"] end subgraph "Code" C["from transformers import ViTModel

outputs = model(images, output_attentions=True)
attentions = outputs.attentions[-1] # Last layer

# CLS token attention to patches
cls_attn = attentions[0, :, 0, 1:] # Skip CLS itself
cls_attn = cls_attn.mean(0) # Average heads
cls_attn = cls_attn.reshape(14, 14) # Patch grid"] end subgraph "Overlay on Image" O["# Resize attention to image size
attn_map = resize(cls_attn, image_size)
plt.imshow(image)
plt.imshow(attn_map, alpha=0.5, cmap='hot')"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.explanation: Why attention isn't always a good explanation.

correlate with feature importance

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for attention-based explanations.

Captum - Integrated gradients
ecco - LM analysis

fields.title: Gradient-Based Methods
fields.description: Saliency maps, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM

steps.title: Gradient-Based Explanations
steps.explanation: Use gradients to understand what inputs affect the output.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Idea" I["Gradient ∂y/∂x tells us:
How much does output y change
if we change input x?"] end subgraph "High Gradient" H["= Small change in pixel/word
causes big change in prediction
= Important!"] end subgraph "Methods" M1["Vanilla Gradients (Saliency)"] M2["Integrated Gradients"] M3["GradCAM"] M4["SmoothGrad"] end style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Vanilla Gradients (Saliency Maps)
steps.explanation: Simplest method: compute gradient of output w.r.t. input.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Algorithm" A["1. Forward pass: y = f(x)
2. Backward pass: ∂y/∂x
3. Visualize gradient magnitude"] end subgraph "Code" C["x.requires_grad_(True)
output = model(x)
output[0, target_class].backward()

saliency = x.grad.abs().max(dim=1)[0]
plt.imshow(saliency, cmap='hot')"] end subgraph "Problems" P["Noisy gradients
Saturated neurons = zero gradient
Doesn't satisfy axioms"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Integrated Gradients
steps.explanation: Accumulate gradients along path from baseline to input.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["Gradient at one point isn't enough
Need to understand full path"] end subgraph "Integrated Gradients" IG["IG(x) = (x - x') × ∫ ∂f(x' + α(x-x'))/∂x dα

Where:
x' = baseline (black image, zero text)
α = interpolation from 0 to 1"] end subgraph "Properties" PR1["Sensitivity: Non-zero if feature matters"] PR2["Implementation Invariance"] PR3["Completeness: Attributions sum to output - baseline"] end style IG fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Integrated Gradients Code
steps.explanation: Implementing Integrated Gradients with Captum.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Captum Library" C["from captum.attr import IntegratedGradients

ig = IntegratedGradients(model)
attributions = ig.attribute(
input,
target=predicted_class,
baselines=baseline, # e.g., black image
n_steps=50
)"] end subgraph "Visualize" V["from captum.attr import visualization as viz

viz.visualize_image_attr(
attributions.squeeze().permute(1,2,0).numpy(),
original_image,
method='blended_heat_map'
)"] end subgraph "For Text" T["# Attribute to embeddings
ig = LayerIntegratedGradients(model, model.embedding)
attributions = ig.attribute(input_ids, target=label)"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: GradCAM
steps.explanation: Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping for CNNs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "How GradCAM Works" G["1. Get activations from conv layer
2. Compute gradients of output w.r.t activations
3. Global average pool gradients → weights
4. Weighted sum of activations
5. ReLU → keep positive"] end subgraph "Formula" F["CAM = ReLU(Σ αk × Ak)
where αk = global_avg_pool(∂y/∂Ak)"] end subgraph "Result" R["Coarse heatmap showing
which image regions matter
for the predicted class"] end style G fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: GradCAM Code
steps.explanation: Implementing GradCAM for image models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Using Captum" C["from captum.attr import LayerGradCam

gradcam = LayerGradCam(model, model.layer4[-1])
attributions = gradcam.attribute(
input,
target=predicted_class
)

# Upsample to input size
upsampled = F.interpolate(attributions, input.shape[2:])"] end subgraph "Overlay" O["heatmap = upsampled.squeeze().cpu().numpy()
heatmap = (heatmap - heatmap.min()) / (heatmap.max() - heatmap.min())

plt.imshow(original_image)
plt.imshow(heatmap, alpha=0.5, cmap='jet')"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: SmoothGrad
steps.explanation: Reduce noise by averaging gradients over noisy inputs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Problem" P["Vanilla gradients are noisy
Small input changes → big gradient changes"] end subgraph "SmoothGrad" S["1. Add Gaussian noise to input N times
2. Compute gradient for each
3. Average the gradients

SmoothGrad = (1/n) Σ ∂f(x + noise)/∂x"] end subgraph "Code" C["from captum.attr import NoiseTunnel, Saliency

saliency = Saliency(model)
nt = NoiseTunnel(saliency)

smoothgrad = nt.attribute(
input, target=class,
nt_type='smoothgrad',
nt_samples=50,
stdevs=0.2
)"] end style S fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Comparison of Methods
steps.explanation: When to use which gradient method.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Vanilla Gradient" V["+ Fast, simple
- Noisy, misleading"] end subgraph "Integrated Gradients" IG["+ Theoretically sound
+ Works for any model
- Needs baseline choice
- Slower (n_steps)"] end subgraph "GradCAM" GC["+ Visual, interpretable
+ Class-specific
- CNNs only
- Coarse resolution"] end subgraph "SmoothGrad" SG["+ Cleaner than vanilla
- Even slower
- Parameter choice"] end subgraph "Recommendation" R["Images: GradCAM + IG
Text: IG + attention
Any model: IG"] end style R fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for gradient-based explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Use Integrated Gradients over vanilla"] D2["Choose meaningful baselines"] D3["Combine with other methods"] D4["Validate explanations make sense"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Trust vanilla gradients alone"] DN2["Ignore baseline sensitivity"] DN3["Over-interpret noise"] end subgraph "Baseline Choices" B["Images: Black image, blurred image
Text: Padding token, mask token
Tabular: Mean values, zeros"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Global vs Local Explanations

Local: Trust this decision"] end style G fill:#2196F3,color:white style L fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.explanation: Guidelines for comprehensive explainability.

fields.title: Counterfactual Explanations
fields.description: 'What if' scenarios and minimal changes for different outcomes

steps.title: What Are Counterfactuals?
steps.explanation: Counterfactuals answer: 'What minimal change would give a different prediction?'
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" O["Loan denied
Income: $40k
Credit: 650
Debt: $20k"] end subgraph "Counterfactual" C["Loan APPROVED
Income: $40k ✓
Credit: 700 (+50)
Debt: $20k ✓"] end subgraph "Insight" I["'If your credit score was 700
instead of 650, you would
have been approved.'"] end O --> C style C fill:#4CAF50,color:white style I fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Why Counterfactuals?
steps.explanation: Counterfactuals are intuitive and actionable.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Benefits" B1["Human-friendly explanations"] B2["Actionable insights"] B3["No model internals needed"] B4["Contrastive reasoning"] end subgraph "vs Other Methods" V["SHAP: 'Credit hurt by 15%'
CF: 'Increase credit by 50 points'

CF gives ACTIONS, not weights"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U["Loan decisions
Insurance claims
Medical diagnosis
College admissions"] end style V fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Finding Counterfactuals
steps.explanation: Optimization problem: minimal change that flips prediction.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Objective" O["minimize: distance(x, x')
subject to: f(x') ≠ f(x)"] end subgraph "Constraints" C1["Minimal change (small distance)"] C2["Realistic values (in distribution)"] C3["Actionable (can't change age)"] C4["Sparse (few features changed)"] end subgraph "Methods" M1["Gradient-based search"] M2["Genetic algorithms"] M3["Mixed-integer programming"] M4["Prototype-based"] end style O fill:#E3F2FD

Each with minimal changes
e.g., +$5k income OR +50 credit"] end style G fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Diversity in Counterfactuals
steps.explanation: Multiple diverse counterfactuals are more useful than one.

User has choices
Different tradeoffs"] end subgraph "Example" E["CF1: Increase income by $10k
CF2: Increase credit by 50
CF3: Decrease debt by $5k
CF4: Small changes to all three"] end subgraph "DiCE Diversity" D["exp.generate_counterfactuals(

)"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

predict_fn,
shape=(1, n_features),
use_kdtree=True
)
cf.fit(X_train)

explanation = cf.explain(instance)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B["Uses prototypes from training data
More realistic counterfactuals
Works with autoencoders"] end subgraph "Other Methods" O["CounterfactualRL - reinforcement learning
CEM - contrastive explanations"] end style CP fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Evaluating Counterfactuals
steps.explanation: Metrics for counterfactual quality.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Validity" V["Does it actually flip the prediction?
cf_model.predict(cf) ≠ original_pred"] end subgraph "Proximity" P["How different from original?
L1, L2 distance
Smaller = better"] end subgraph "Sparsity" S["How many features changed?
Fewer = easier to understand"] end subgraph "Actionability" A["Are changes realistic?
In-distribution?
Mutable features only?"] end subgraph "Diversity" D["Multiple different paths?
Options for the user?"] end style V fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for effective counterfactual explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Generate multiple diverse CFs"] D2["Respect immutable features"] D3["Check CFs are in-distribution"] D4["Make CFs actionable"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Generate single CF only"] DN2["Ignore domain constraints"] DN3["Accept out-of-distribution CFs"] end subgraph "Communication" C["'To get approved, you could either:
- Increase credit score by 50, OR
- Increase income by $10k, OR
- Pay off $5k of debt'"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Real-Time vs Batch Explanations
steps.explanation: Two strategies for generating explanations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Real-Time" RT["Generate with each prediction
+ Always available
- Adds latency
- Higher compute cost"] end subgraph "Batch" B["Generate async/offline
+ No latency impact
+ Can use expensive methods
- Delayed availability"] end subgraph "Hybrid" H["Fast method real-time (importance)
Detailed method on-demand
Batch for monitoring"] end subgraph "Choose" C["User-facing? → Real-time
Debugging? → On-demand
Monitoring? → Batch"] end style H fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Fast Explanation Methods

]

steps.explanation: Guidelines for production explainability.
