fields.title: Faithfulness vs Interpretability
fields.description: Trade-offs in explanation quality

steps.title: Two Properties of Explanations
steps.explanation: Good explanations need to be both faithful AND interpretable.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Faithfulness" F["Does explanation accurately
reflect model behavior?

'Is it TRUE?'"] end subgraph "Interpretability" I["Can humans understand
the explanation?

'Is it USEFUL?'"] end subgraph "The Tension" T["Most faithful: model itself
Most interpretable: 'always sunny'
Need balance!"] end style F fill:#2196F3,color:white style I fill:#4CAF50,color:white style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Faithfulness Spectrum
steps.explanation: Different levels of faithfulness to the original model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "High Faithfulness" HF["Exact SHAP values
Integrated Gradients
Decision paths"] end subgraph "Medium Faithfulness" MF["LIME (local approximation)
Surrogate models
Attention weights"] end subgraph "Low Faithfulness" LF["Post-hoc rationalizations
Unvalidated heuristics
Human-written rules"] end subgraph "Risk" R["Low faithfulness = misleading!
User thinks X caused prediction
but actually Y did"] end style HF fill:#4CAF50,color:white style LF fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Interpretability Spectrum
steps.explanation: How easy is the explanation to understand?
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Highly Interpretable" HI["'Denied because income < $50k'
Simple rules
Counterfactuals"] end subgraph "Moderate" M["'Top factors: income (-), credit (+)'
Feature importance
Partial plots"] end subgraph "Technical" T["SHAP waterfall plots
Attention heatmaps
Saliency maps"] end subgraph "Opaque" O["Raw weights
Embedding vectors
Gradient tensors"] end style HI fill:#4CAF50,color:white style O fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Measuring Faithfulness
steps.explanation: How to evaluate if explanations are faithful.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Perturbation Tests" PT["Remove 'important' features
Prediction should change!
Remove 'unimportant' features
Prediction should stay same"] end subgraph "Fidelity Metrics" FM["For surrogate models:
How well does surrogate match
original on test data?"] end subgraph "Consistency" C["Similar inputs should get
similar explanations
Run multiple times: stable?"] end subgraph "Completeness" CO["Do attributions sum to
prediction - baseline?
(SHAP guarantees this)"] end style PT fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Evaluating Interpretability
steps.explanation: How to measure if explanations are understandable.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "User Studies" US["Show explanations to users
Can they predict model behavior?
Do they trust correctly?"] end subgraph "Proxy Metrics" PM["Explanation size (fewer = simpler)
Vocabulary (common words = clearer)
Visual complexity"] end subgraph "Task Performance" TP["Can user correct model mistakes?
Can user identify biases?
Decision quality improves?"] end subgraph "Audience Matters" A["ML expert: technical OK
Domain expert: feature names
End user: plain language"] end style US fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Trade-off Examples
steps.explanation: Concrete examples of the faithfulness-interpretability trade-off.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Faithful but Complex" FC["100-feature SHAP explanation
Accurate but overwhelming
User ignores most of it"] end subgraph "Simple but Unfaithful" SU["'Denied due to credit score'
Easy to understand
But actually 5 factors mattered"] end subgraph "Balanced" B["'Top 3 factors: credit (40%),
income (30%), debt (20%)'
Faithful enough + understandable"] end style FC fill:#FFF9C4 style SU fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#4CAF50,color:white

steps.title: Strategies for Balance
steps.explanation: Techniques to achieve both properties.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hierarchical Explanations" H["Level 1: Simple summary
Level 2: Top features
Level 3: Full SHAP"] end subgraph "Faithful Simplification" FS["Compute full SHAP
Show only top K
Aggregate similar features"] end subgraph "Validation" V["Simple explanation with
fidelity check:
'This explains 85% of decision'"] end subgraph "User Control" UC["Let user choose detail level
Progressive disclosure
Drill-down capability"] end style H fill:#E3F2FD style FS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Guidelines for balancing faithfulness and interpretability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["Start with faithful method (SHAP)"] D2["Simplify presentation, not computation"] D3["Validate simplified explanations"] D4["Match complexity to audience"] D5["Provide detail on demand"] end subgraph "Don't" DN1["Sacrifice faithfulness for simplicity"] DN2["Assume attention = importance"] DN3["Skip validation of explanations"] end subgraph "Report" R["Always report:
- Method used
- Faithfulness metric
- Limitations"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style DN1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Test Pyramid
fields.description: Understanding the layers of testing: unit, integration, and end-to-end tests with their tradeoffs

steps.title: The Testing Challenge
steps.explanation: How should we balance different types of tests? Too many slow tests = slow feedback. Too few tests = bugs in production. The **Test Pyramid** provides a proven strategy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["How many of each test type
should we write?"] end subgraph "Considerations" C1["Speed of feedback"] C2["Cost to write/maintain"] C3["Confidence in correctness"] C4["Debugging difficulty"] end Q --> C1 Q --> C2 Q --> C3 Q --> C4

steps.title: Unit Tests - The Foundation
steps.explanation: **Unit tests** form the base of the pyramid. They test individual functions/classes in isolation. They're fast (milliseconds), cheap to write, and easy to debug. You should have the MOST of these.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Unit Tests"
        U1["test_calculate_tax()"]
        U2["test_validate_email()"]
        U3["test_parse_json()"]
        U4["test_sort_users()"]
        U5["...hundreds more"]
    end
    subgraph "Characteristics"
        C1["Fast: < 10ms each"]
        C2["Isolated: no DB, no network"]
        C3["Precise: pinpoint failures"]
        C4["Cheap: easy to write"]
    end
    style U1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U4 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Integration Tests - The Middle Layer
steps.explanation: **Integration tests** verify that components work together correctly. They test real database queries, API calls between services, or module interactions. Slower but catch interface bugs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Integration Tests"
        I1["test_user_service_creates_in_db()"]
        I2["test_api_returns_valid_json()"]
        I3["test_cache_syncs_with_db()"]
    end
    subgraph "What They Test"
        W1["Service to Database"]
        W2["Service to Service"]
        W3["API to Business Logic"]
    end
    subgraph "Characteristics"
        C1["Slower: 100ms - 1s"]
        C2["Tests real connections"]
        C3["Harder to debug"]
        C4["More expensive"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style I2 fill:#FFF9C4
    style I3 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: E2E Tests - The Peak
steps.explanation: **End-to-end tests** simulate real user journeys through the entire application. They catch integration issues but are slow, flaky, and expensive. Use sparingly for critical paths only.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "E2E Tests"
        E1["test_user_can_complete_checkout()"]
        E2["test_login_to_dashboard_flow()"]
    end
    subgraph "What They Cover"
        W["Browser to Frontend to API to DB to External Services"]
    end
    subgraph "Characteristics"
        C1["Slow: 10s - minutes"]
        C2["Flaky: timing issues"]
        C3["Hard to debug"]
        C4["Very expensive"]
    end
    style E1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style E2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: The Complete Pyramid
steps.explanation: The pyramid shape shows the ideal distribution: **many unit tests** at the base, **fewer integration tests** in the middle, and **minimal E2E tests** at the top.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph pyramid [" "] E2E["E2E Tests
~5-10 percent
Critical paths only"] INT["Integration Tests
~20-30 percent
Component boundaries"] UNIT["Unit Tests
~60-70 percent
All business logic"] end E2E --> INT INT --> UNIT style E2E fill:#FFCDD2 style INT fill:#FFF9C4 style UNIT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Speed vs Confidence Tradeoff
steps.explanation: Moving up the pyramid increases confidence but decreases speed. The pyramid shape optimizes this tradeoff: fast feedback from unit tests, confidence from integration tests, and validation from E2E tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Speed"
        S1["Unit: Very Fast"]
        S2["Integration: Medium"]
        S3["E2E: Slow"]
    end
    subgraph "Confidence"
        C1["Unit: Low-Med"]
        C2["Integration: Medium"]
        C3["E2E: High"]
    end
    subgraph "Cost"
        CO1["Unit: Low"]
        CO2["Integration: Medium"]
        CO3["E2E: High"]
    end

steps.title: Anti-Pattern: Ice Cream Cone
steps.explanation: The **ice cream cone** anti-pattern inverts the pyramid: too many E2E tests, few unit tests. This leads to slow CI, flaky tests, and difficult debugging. Refactor toward the pyramid!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph bad ["Ice Cream Cone - Bad"] BE["Many E2E Tests
Slow, Flaky"] BI["Some Integration"] BU["Few Unit Tests"] end subgraph good ["Pyramid - Good"] GE["Few E2E"] GI["Some Integration"] GU["Many Unit Tests
Fast, Reliable"] end style BE fill:#FFCDD2 style BU fill:#FFCDD2 style GE fill:#C8E6C9 style GU fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Practical Guidelines
steps.explanation: Apply the pyramid to your codebase with these rules of thumb. The exact ratios vary by project, but the shape should always be a pyramid.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Guidelines" G1["Unit: Test every function
with business logic"] G2["Integration: Test every
service boundary"] G3["E2E: Test only critical
user journeys 3-5 max"] end subgraph "Red Flags" R1["CI takes more than 10 min"] R2["Tests fail randomly"] R3["Cannot find bug location"] R4["Afraid to refactor"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style G2 fill:#FFF9C4 style G3 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Test-Driven Development (TDD) Cycle
fields.description: The Red-Green-Refactor cycle for writing tests before code

steps.title: What is TDD?
steps.explanation: **Test-Driven Development** is a discipline where you write tests BEFORE writing production code. It follows a strict cycle: Red, Green, Refactor.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Traditional"
        T1["Write Code"] --> T2["Write Tests"] --> T3["Fix Bugs"]
    end
    subgraph "TDD"
        D1["Write Test"] --> D2["Write Code"] --> D3["Refactor"] --> D1
    end
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style D2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D3 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: RED - Write a Failing Test
steps.explanation: Start by writing a test for functionality that doesn't exist yet. The test MUST fail. This proves the test is actually testing something.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    W["Write test for new feature"] --> R["Run test"] --> F["Test FAILS"]
    style F fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: GREEN - Make It Pass
steps.explanation: Write the MINIMUM code needed to make the test pass. Don't optimize or add features. Just make it work.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    W["Write minimal code"] --> R["Run test"] --> P["Test PASSES"]
    style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: REFACTOR - Clean Up
steps.explanation: Now that tests pass, safely improve the code. Remove duplication, improve names, simplify logic. Tests ensure you don't break anything.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    I["Identify improvements"] --> C["Clean up code"] --> R["Run tests"] --> P["Still PASSES"]
    style P fill:#C8E6C9
    style I fill:#BBDEFB
    style C fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: The Complete Cycle
steps.explanation: TDD is a continuous cycle. After refactoring, write the next failing test and repeat. Each cycle should be minutes, not hours.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR RED["RED
Write failing test"] --> GREEN["GREEN
Make it pass"] --> REFACTOR["REFACTOR
Clean up"] --> RED style RED fill:#FFCDD2 style GREEN fill:#C8E6C9 style REFACTOR fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Benefits of TDD
steps.explanation: TDD provides multiple benefits beyond just having tests: better design, documentation, and confidence to refactor.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    B1["100% coverage by definition"]
    B2["Better design - testable = modular"]
    B3["Living documentation"]
    B4["Confidence to refactor"]
    B5["Fewer bugs in production"]
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B4 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B5 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Test Fixtures and Setup/Teardown
fields.description: Understanding when setup, test, and teardown execute in test frameworks

steps.title: What Are Test Fixtures?
steps.explanation: **Fixtures** are the setup and teardown code that prepares the test environment. They create test data, initialize connections, and clean up afterward.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Fixtures"
        T1["Test 1: create user, test, delete"]
        T2["Test 2: create user, test, delete"]
        T3["Test 3: create user, test, delete"]
    end
    subgraph "With Fixtures"
        F["Fixture: create user"]
        FT1["Test 1: test logic only"]
        FT2["Test 2: test logic only"]
        C["Cleanup: delete user"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style F fill:#C8E6C9
    style C fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Fixture Scopes
steps.explanation: Fixtures run at different scopes: **function** (per test), **class** (per class), **module** (per file), **session** (entire run). Wider scopes are faster but share state.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Session"
        SS["Session Setup"]
        subgraph "Module"
            MS["Module Setup"]
            subgraph "Function"
                FS["Setup"] --> T["Test"] --> FT["Teardown"]
            end
            MT["Module Teardown"]
        end
        ST["Session Teardown"]
    end

steps.title: Function Scope - Most Common
steps.explanation: **Function scope** fixtures run before and after each test function. This ensures complete isolation - each test starts fresh.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "test_one"
        S1["Setup"] --> T1["Test"] --> D1["Teardown"]
    end
    subgraph "test_two"
        S2["Setup"] --> T2["Test"] --> D2["Teardown"]
    end
    D1 --> S2
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style D2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Yield for Teardown
steps.explanation: Use **yield** to run teardown code after the test. Code before yield is setup, code after is teardown.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Fixture with yield"
        S["resource = create()"]
        Y["yield resource"]
        T["resource.cleanup()"]
    end
    S --> Y --> T
    style S fill:#C8E6C9
    style T fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Fixture Dependencies
steps.explanation: Fixtures can depend on other fixtures. Pytest resolves these automatically, running fixtures in the correct order.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    DB["db_connection"] --> USER["user"] --> TOKEN["auth_token"] --> CLIENT["api_client"]
    CLIENT --> TEST["test function"]
    style DB fill:#BBDEFB
    style USER fill:#C8E6C9
    style TOKEN fill:#FFF9C4
    style CLIENT fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Mock vs Stub vs Fake vs Spy
fields.description: Understanding the different types of test doubles and when to use each

steps.title: What Are Test Doubles?
steps.explanation: **Test doubles** replace real dependencies with controlled substitutes. They let you test in isolation without databases, APIs, or other external systems.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Production"
        Code --> RealDB[(Real Database)]
        Code --> RealAPI[Real API]
    end
    subgraph "Testing"
        TestCode --> FakeDB["Test Double"]
        TestCode --> MockAPI["Test Double"]
    end
    style FakeDB fill:#C8E6C9
    style MockAPI fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Stub - Returns Canned Answers
steps.explanation: A **stub** provides pre-programmed responses. It doesn't verify how it was called - it just returns what you told it to return.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Stub Example"
        S["stub.get_user(1)"]
        R["Always returns User(id=1, name='Test')"]
    end
    S --> R
    subgraph "Use When"
        U1["You need specific return values"]
        U2["You don't care HOW it's called"]
        U3["Testing happy path scenarios"]
    end
    style S fill:#FFF9C4
    style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Mock - Verifies Behavior
steps.explanation: A **mock** records how it was called and lets you assert on those calls. Use mocks when the interaction itself is what you're testing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Mock Example"
        C["code.send_email(user)"]
        M["mock_email.send.assert_called_with(user.email, subject, body)"]
    end
    C --> M
    subgraph "Use When"
        U1["Verifying side effects"]
        U2["Checking method was called correctly"]
        U3["Testing error handling paths"]
    end
    style C fill:#BBDEFB
    style M fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Fake - Working Implementation
steps.explanation: A **fake** is a working but simplified implementation. Examples: in-memory database, local file system instead of S3.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Real"
        R["PostgreSQL Database"]
    end
    subgraph "Fake"
        F["In-memory dict/SQLite"]
    end
    subgraph "Properties"
        P1["Actually works"]
        P2["Simpler/faster than real"]
        P3["Good for integration tests"]
    end
    R -.-> F
    style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Spy - Wraps Real Object
steps.explanation: A **spy** wraps a real object, recording calls while still using the real implementation. Useful when you want real behavior but need to verify calls.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Spy"
        CALL["spy.process(data)"]
        REAL["Calls REAL method"]
        RECORD["Records the call"]
        ASSERT["spy.process.assert_called_once()"]
    end
    CALL --> REAL
    CALL --> RECORD
    RECORD --> ASSERT
    style REAL fill:#C8E6C9
    style RECORD fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Comparison Table
steps.explanation: Choose the right test double based on what you need to test.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "When to Use What" STUB["STUB
Need specific return value
Don't care about calls"] MOCK["MOCK
Verify interactions
Check call arguments"] FAKE["FAKE
Need working implementation
Integration tests"] SPY["SPY
Real behavior + verification
Partial mocking"] end style STUB fill:#FFF9C4 style MOCK fill:#BBDEFB style FAKE fill:#C8E6C9 style SPY fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Python unittest.mock Example
steps.explanation: Python's unittest.mock provides Mock objects that can be stubs, mocks, or spies depending on how you use them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Mock as Stub"
        S["mock.return_value = 42"]
    end
    subgraph "Mock as Mock"
        M["mock.assert_called_with(args)"]
    end
    subgraph "Mock as Spy"
        SP["@patch wraps=real_func"]
    end
    style S fill:#FFF9C4
    style M fill:#BBDEFB
    style SP fill:#E1BEE7

fields.title: Dependency Injection for Testing
fields.description: How dependency injection makes code testable by allowing mock injection

steps.title: The Problem: Hard to Test Code
steps.explanation: When code creates its own dependencies, you can't replace them with test doubles. This makes testing difficult or impossible.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Hard to Test" UC["class UserService:
def __init__:
self.db = PostgresDB()"] P["Creates real DB connection!
Cannot inject mock"] end UC --> P style UC fill:#FFCDD2 style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Solution: Inject Dependencies
steps.explanation: **Dependency Injection** passes dependencies from outside instead of creating them inside. This lets tests inject mocks/fakes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Easy to Test" UC["class UserService:
def __init__(self, db):
self.db = db"] P["Accepts any DB implementation!
Real in prod, mock in test"] end UC --> P style UC fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Before vs After
steps.explanation: Compare tightly coupled code (hard to test) vs loosely coupled code (easy to test).
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Before: Tight Coupling"
        B1["Service"] --> B2["new Database()"]
        B3["Cannot replace DB"]
    end
    subgraph "After: Loose Coupling"
        A1["Service"] --> A2["db interface"]
        A3["RealDB"] --> A2
        A4["MockDB"] --> A2
    end
    style B1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Testing with DI
steps.explanation: In tests, inject mock objects. In production, inject real implementations. Same code, different dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Production" PS["UserService(RealDB())"] end subgraph "Testing" TS["UserService(MockDB())"] end subgraph "Same UserService Class" USC["Works with any DB
that has same interface"] end PS --> USC TS --> USC style TS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: DI Patterns
steps.explanation: Three common ways to inject dependencies: constructor, method parameter, or property setter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Constructor Injection"
        C["def __init__(self, db):"]
    end
    subgraph "Method Injection"
        M["def query(self, db):"]
    end
    subgraph "Setter Injection"
        S["service.db = mock_db"]
    end
    style C fill:#C8E6C9
    style M fill:#FFF9C4
    style S fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Mocking External APIs
fields.description: Techniques for testing code that calls external APIs without making real requests

steps.title: Why Mock External APIs?
steps.explanation: Real API calls in tests are slow, flaky, expensive, and may have rate limits. Mocking gives you fast, reliable, free tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Problems with Real APIs"
        P1["Slow: network latency"]
        P2["Flaky: network failures"]
        P3["Costly: API charges"]
        P4["Limited: rate limits"]
        P5["Unreliable: external downtime"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefits of Mocking"
        B1["Fast: instant responses"]
        B2["Reliable: no network"]
        B3["Free: no API charges"]
        B4["Controllable: test edge cases"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Where to Mock
steps.explanation: You can mock at different layers: HTTP level (intercept requests), client level (mock SDK), or service level (mock your wrapper).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Your Code"
        SVC["Your Service"]
        CLIENT["API Client/SDK"]
        HTTP["HTTP Library"]
    end
    subgraph "External"
        API["External API"]
    end
    SVC --> CLIENT --> HTTP --> API
    M1["Mock here: Service level"] -.-> SVC
    M2["Mock here: Client level"] -.-> CLIENT
    M3["Mock here: HTTP level"] -.-> HTTP
    style M3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: HTTP Level Mocking
steps.explanation: Libraries like `responses` or `httpx_mock` intercept HTTP requests before they leave your machine. This tests the full integration path.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Test" T["Test Code"] SVC["Service"] CLIENT["HTTP Client"] end subgraph "Mock Layer" MOCK["responses/httpx_mock
Intercepts request
Returns fake response"] end T --> SVC --> CLIENT --> MOCK style MOCK fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Testing Error Scenarios
steps.explanation: Mocking lets you test error cases that are hard to trigger with real APIs: timeouts, rate limits, server errors.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Scenarios to Test"
        S1["200 OK - normal response"]
        S2["404 Not Found"]
        S3["429 Rate Limited"]
        S4["500 Server Error"]
        S5["Timeout after 30s"]
        S6["Invalid JSON response"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#FFF9C4
    style S3 fill:#FFF9C4
    style S4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S5 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S6 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Recording and Playback
steps.explanation: Tools like VCR.py record real API responses once, then replay them in future tests. Best of both worlds.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "First Run"
        F1["Make real API call"]
        F2["Record response to file"]
    end
    subgraph "Future Runs"
        R1["Load recorded response"]
        R2["No network call needed"]
    end
    F1 --> F2
    R1 --> R2
    style F1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style R2 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: ML Model Testing Pipeline
fields.description: Comprehensive testing strategy for machine learning models from data to production

steps.title: ML Testing is Different
steps.explanation: Traditional software has deterministic outputs. ML models have probabilistic behavior, making testing more complex. We need multiple testing strategies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Traditional Software" T1["Input A"] --> T2["Always Output B"] end subgraph "ML Model" M1["Input A"] --> M2["Output B with 85% confidence
Could also be C or D"] end style T2 fill:#C8E6C9 style M2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Data Validation
steps.explanation: First, validate your training and inference data. Bad data leads to bad models. Test for schema, ranges, distributions, and missing values.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Data Tests"
        D1["Schema validation"]
        D2["No null in required fields"]
        D3["Values in expected range"]
        D4["Distribution matches expected"]
        D5["No data leakage"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style D5 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Unit Tests for ML Code
steps.explanation: Test your feature engineering, preprocessing, and model code like regular software. These should be deterministic.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Unit Tests"
        U1["Feature transformations"]
        U2["Preprocessing functions"]
        U3["Custom loss functions"]
        U4["Metric calculations"]
        U5["Data loaders"]
    end
    style U1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U4 fill:#C8E6C9
    style U5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Model Validation
steps.explanation: Test model performance on held-out data. Set minimum thresholds for accuracy, precision, recall, etc. Fail CI if model underperforms.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Model Tests"
        M1["Accuracy > 0.85"]
        M2["Precision > 0.80"]
        M3["Recall > 0.75"]
        M4["No class has < 70% accuracy"]
        M5["Latency < 100ms"]
    end
    M1 --> PASS["Pass/Fail CI"]
    M2 --> PASS
    M3 --> PASS
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style PASS fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Integration Tests
steps.explanation: Test the full pipeline: data ingestion, preprocessing, inference, and post-processing. Ensure components work together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    DATA["Raw Data"] --> PREP["Preprocessing"]
    PREP --> MODEL["Model Inference"]
    MODEL --> POST["Post-processing"]
    POST --> OUTPUT["Final Output"]
    style DATA fill:#BBDEFB
    style OUTPUT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: A/B Testing in Production
steps.explanation: The final test: compare new model vs current model with real users. Statistical significance required before full rollout.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Production A/B Test" TRAFFIC["User Traffic"] A["Model A: Control
50% of users"] B["Model B: Candidate
50% of users"] METRICS["Compare Metrics"] DECISION["Statistically Significant?"] end TRAFFIC --> A TRAFFIC --> B A --> METRICS B --> METRICS METRICS --> DECISION style DECISION fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Complete ML Testing Pipeline
steps.explanation: All testing stages in order. Each gate must pass before proceeding to the next.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR DV["Data
Validation"] --> UT["Unit
Tests"] UT --> MV["Model
Validation"] MV --> IT["Integration
Tests"] IT --> ST["Staging
Tests"] ST --> AB["A/B
Test"] style DV fill:#BBDEFB style UT fill:#C8E6C9 style MV fill:#FFF9C4 style IT fill:#E1BEE7 style ST fill:#FFE0B2 style AB fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Data Drift Detection
fields.description: Detecting when production data distribution differs from training data

steps.title: What is Data Drift?
steps.explanation: **Data drift** occurs when production data differs from training data. This causes model performance to degrade silently.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Training Time" TD["Training Data
Distribution A"] end subgraph "Production" PD["Production Data
Distribution B"] end TD --> MODEL["Model trained on A"] PD --> MODEL MODEL --> PROBLEM["Poor predictions!
Model expects A, gets B"] style PROBLEM fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Types of Drift
steps.explanation: There are multiple types of drift: covariate (input features change), concept (relationship changes), or label (target distribution changes).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Covariate Drift" C1["Input features change
Age: 25-40 to 18-65"] end subgraph "Concept Drift" C2["X to Y relationship changes
Buying patterns shift"] end subgraph "Label Drift" C3["Target distribution changes
More positive cases"] end style C1 fill:#BBDEFB style C2 fill:#FFF9C4 style C3 fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Statistical Tests
steps.explanation: Use statistical tests to detect drift: KS test, Chi-squared, PSI. Compare training distribution to production batches.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Detection Methods" KS["Kolmogorov-Smirnov
Continuous features"] CHI["Chi-Squared
Categorical features"] PSI["Population Stability Index
Binned comparison"] end KS --> ALERT["Alert if p-value < 0.05"] CHI --> ALERT PSI --> ALERT style ALERT fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Monitoring Pipeline
steps.explanation: Set up continuous monitoring to detect drift early. Compare each batch or window of production data against reference.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR PROD["Production
Data"] --> SAMPLE["Sample
Batch"] SAMPLE --> COMPARE["Compare to
Reference"] COMPARE --> CHECK{"Drift
Detected?"} CHECK -->|Yes| ALERT["Alert Team"] CHECK -->|No| LOG["Log Metrics"] style ALERT fill:#FFCDD2 style LOG fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Response to Drift
steps.explanation: When drift is detected: investigate cause, retrain if needed, or update monitoring thresholds if drift is expected.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB DRIFT["Drift Detected"] --> INVESTIGATE["Investigate Cause"] INVESTIGATE --> REAL{"Real
Change?"} REAL -->|Yes| RETRAIN["Retrain Model"] REAL -->|No| FIX["Fix Data Pipeline"] RETRAIN --> DEPLOY["Deploy New Model"] style DRIFT fill:#FFCDD2 style DEPLOY fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Cross-Validation Folds
fields.description: How K-fold cross-validation rotates data through train/test splits

steps.title: The Problem with Single Split
steps.explanation: A single train/test split can be lucky or unlucky. Results depend heavily on which data ends up in test set.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB DATA["Full Dataset"] --> SPLIT["Random 80/20 Split"] SPLIT --> TRAIN["Train: 80%"] SPLIT --> TEST["Test: 20%"] TEST --> Q["Got 92% accuracy...
But was test set easy or hard?"] style Q fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: K-Fold Cross-Validation
steps.explanation: Split data into K equal folds. Train on K-1 folds, test on 1 fold. Rotate K times so every fold is tested once.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB DATA["Dataset"] --> F1["Fold 1"] DATA --> F2["Fold 2"] DATA --> F3["Fold 3"] DATA --> F4["Fold 4"] DATA --> F5["Fold 5"] F1 --> R["Each fold takes turn
as test set"] style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: 5-Fold Example: Iteration 1
steps.explanation: First iteration: Fold 1 is test, Folds 2-5 are training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR F1["Fold 1
TEST"] F2["Fold 2
TRAIN"] F3["Fold 3
TRAIN"] F4["Fold 4
TRAIN"] F5["Fold 5
TRAIN"] style F1 fill:#FFCDD2 style F2 fill:#C8E6C9 style F3 fill:#C8E6C9 style F4 fill:#C8E6C9 style F5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: 5-Fold Example: Iteration 2
steps.explanation: Second iteration: Fold 2 is test, others are training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR F1["Fold 1
TRAIN"] F2["Fold 2
TEST"] F3["Fold 3
TRAIN"] F4["Fold 4
TRAIN"] F5["Fold 5
TRAIN"] style F2 fill:#FFCDD2 style F1 fill:#C8E6C9 style F3 fill:#C8E6C9 style F4 fill:#C8E6C9 style F5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Final Score
steps.explanation: Average scores across all K iterations. This gives a more robust estimate of model performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB I1["Iter 1: 91%"] I2["Iter 2: 89%"] I3["Iter 3: 93%"] I4["Iter 4: 88%"] I5["Iter 5: 90%"] AVG["Average: 90.2%
Std Dev: 1.9%"] I1 --> AVG I2 --> AVG I3 --> AVG I4 --> AVG I5 --> AVG style AVG fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Code Coverage Types
fields.description: Understanding line, branch, and path coverage with examples

steps.title: What is Code Coverage?
steps.explanation: **Code coverage** measures what percentage of your code is executed by tests. Higher coverage generally means more thorough testing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" L1["Line 1: x = input()"] L2["Line 2: if x > 0:"] L3["Line 3: return positive"] L4["Line 4: return negative"] end subgraph "Test" T["test(x=5)
Executes: L1, L2, L3"] end T --> COV["Coverage: 75%
3 of 4 lines"] style COV fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Line Coverage
steps.explanation: **Line coverage** measures which lines of code were executed. Simple but can miss edge cases in conditionals.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Code" L1["Line 1: executed"] L2["Line 2: executed"] L3["Line 3: NOT executed"] end RESULT["Line Coverage: 66%
2 of 3 lines"] style L1 fill:#C8E6C9 style L2 fill:#C8E6C9 style L3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Branch Coverage
steps.explanation: **Branch coverage** measures which branches (if/else paths) were taken. More thorough than line coverage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB IF{"if x > 0"} IF -->|True| POS["return positive"] IF -->|False| NEG["return negative"] subgraph "Test 1 only" T1["x=5: takes True branch
Branch coverage: 50%"] end subgraph "Both tests" T2["x=5: True, x=-1: False
Branch coverage: 100%"] end style T1 fill:#FFF9C4 style T2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Path Coverage
steps.explanation: **Path coverage** measures all possible execution paths through code. Most thorough but can explode combinatorially.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB IF1{"Condition A"} -->|T| IF2A{"Condition B"} IF1 -->|F| IF2B{"Condition B"} IF2A -->|T| P1["Path 1: T,T"] IF2A -->|F| P2["Path 2: T,F"] IF2B -->|T| P3["Path 3: F,T"] IF2B -->|F| P4["Path 4: F,F"] PATHS["4 paths for 2 conditions
n conditions = 2^n paths"] style PATHS fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Coverage Comparison
steps.explanation: Higher coverage types are more thorough but harder to achieve. Most teams target branch coverage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Coverage Types" LINE["Line: Which lines ran?
Easy to achieve"] BRANCH["Branch: Which paths taken?
Good balance"] PATH["Path: All combinations?
Often impractical"] end LINE --> BRANCH --> PATH style LINE fill:#C8E6C9 style BRANCH fill:#FFF9C4 style PATH fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Coverage Limitations
steps.explanation: 100% coverage does not mean bug-free. Coverage shows what ran, not whether assertions are correct.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "False Confidence"
        CODE["def add(a, b): return a * b"]
        TEST["def test: add(2, 3)  # No assert!"]
        COV["100% Line Coverage"]
        BUG["Still has a bug!"]
    end
    CODE --> TEST --> COV --> BUG
    style COV fill:#FFCDD2
    style BUG fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Mutation Testing
fields.description: How mutation testing finds weak tests by injecting bugs

steps.title: The Problem: Weak Tests
steps.explanation: Tests can have 100% coverage but still miss bugs. Mutation testing checks if your tests actually catch errors.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Question" Q["Your tests pass...
But would they catch a real bug?"] end subgraph "Mutation Testing" M["Inject fake bugs (mutants)
See if tests catch them"] end Q --> M style Q fill:#FFF9C4 style M fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: How It Works
steps.explanation: Create mutants (code with small changes), run tests against each mutant. If tests fail, mutant is 'killed'. If tests pass, mutant 'survived' (your tests are weak).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    ORIG["Original Code"] --> MUT["Create Mutants"]
    MUT --> M1["Mutant 1: + to -"]
    MUT --> M2["Mutant 2: > to >="]
    MUT --> M3["Mutant 3: True to False"]
    M1 --> RUN["Run Tests"]
    M2 --> RUN
    M3 --> RUN
    RUN --> RESULT{"Tests Fail?"}
    RESULT -->|Yes| KILLED["Mutant Killed"]
    RESULT -->|No| SURVIVED["Mutant Survived!"]
    style KILLED fill:#C8E6C9
    style SURVIVED fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Common Mutation Operators
steps.explanation: Mutants are created using operators that make small, realistic changes to code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Mutation Operators"
        OP1["Arithmetic: + to -, * to /"]
        OP2["Relational: > to >=, == to !="]
        OP3["Logical: and to or, True to False"]
        OP4["Delete: remove statement"]
        OP5["Constant: 0 to 1, empty to value"]
    end
    style OP1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style OP2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style OP3 fill:#FFF9C4
    style OP4 fill:#E1BEE7
    style OP5 fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Mutation Score
steps.explanation: Mutation score = killed mutants / total mutants. A higher score means more effective tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    TOTAL["100 Mutants Created"]
    KILLED["85 Killed by tests"]
    SURVIVED["15 Survived"]
    SCORE["Mutation Score: 85%"]
    TOTAL --> KILLED
    TOTAL --> SURVIVED
    KILLED --> SCORE
    style SCORE fill:#FFF9C4
    style SURVIVED fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Improving Tests
steps.explanation: Surviving mutants show exactly where tests are weak. Add tests to kill each survivor.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    SURV["Survived: x > 0 to x >= 0"]
    ANALYZE["Test never checks x=0!"]
    FIX["Add test: assert func(0) == expected"]
    RERUN["Re-run mutation testing"]
    KILLED["Now mutant is killed"]
    SURV --> ANALYZE --> FIX --> RERUN --> KILLED
    style FIX fill:#C8E6C9
    style KILLED fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Test Pipeline Stages
fields.description: How tests flow through a CI/CD pipeline from lint to deploy

steps.title: CI Pipeline Overview
steps.explanation: A CI pipeline runs tests automatically on every push. Tests are organized in stages, with fast tests first.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    PUSH["Git Push"] --> LINT["Lint"]
    LINT --> UNIT["Unit Tests"]
    UNIT --> INT["Integration"]
    INT --> E2E["E2E Tests"]
    E2E --> DEPLOY["Deploy"]
    style PUSH fill:#BBDEFB
    style DEPLOY fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Stage 1: Lint and Format
steps.explanation: First, check code style and formatting. Fast to run, catches obvious issues immediately. Fail fast on style violations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Lint Stage - Seconds"
        L1["ruff check ."]
        L2["ruff format --check ."]
        L3["mypy type checking"]
    end
    L1 --> PASS{"Pass?"}
    L2 --> PASS
    L3 --> PASS
    PASS -->|No| FAIL["Fail Early"]
    PASS -->|Yes| NEXT["Continue"]
    style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2
    style NEXT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Stage 2: Unit Tests
steps.explanation: Run fast, isolated unit tests. These should complete in under a minute. Catch logic errors quickly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Unit Test Stage - 1 min"
        U1["pytest tests/unit/"]
        U2["Coverage check >= 80%"]
    end
    U1 --> PASS{"All Pass?"}
    U2 --> PASS
    PASS -->|No| FAIL["Fail - Fix Logic"]
    PASS -->|Yes| NEXT["Continue"]
    style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2
    style NEXT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Stage 3: Integration Tests
steps.explanation: Test components working together. Involves databases, APIs, caches. Slower but catches integration bugs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Integration Stage - 5 min"
        I1["Start test containers"]
        I2["Run migrations"]
        I3["pytest tests/integration/"]
        I4["Stop containers"]
    end
    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4
    I3 --> PASS{"Pass?"}
    style I1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style I3 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Stage 4: E2E Tests
steps.explanation: Full end-to-end tests with browser automation. Slowest stage, run only on main branch or before deploy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "E2E Stage - 10 min"
        E1["Deploy to staging"]
        E2["Run Playwright/Selenium"]
        E3["Test critical user journeys"]
    end
    E1 --> E2 --> E3
    E3 --> PASS{"Pass?"}
    PASS -->|Yes| DEPLOY["Ready to Deploy"]
    style E1 fill:#E1BEE7
    style DEPLOY fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Pipeline Gates
steps.explanation: Each stage is a gate. Failure in any stage stops the pipeline. This prevents bad code from reaching production.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    L["Lint"] -->|Pass| U["Unit"]
    U -->|Pass| I["Integration"]
    I -->|Pass| E["E2E"]
    E -->|Pass| D["Deploy"]
    L -->|Fail| X1["Stop"]
    U -->|Fail| X2["Stop"]
    I -->|Fail| X3["Stop"]
    E -->|Fail| X4["Stop"]
    style X1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style X2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style X3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style X4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style D fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Parallel Test Execution
fields.description: Speeding up tests by running them in parallel across multiple workers

steps.title: The Problem: Slow Test Suites
steps.explanation: As test suites grow, they take longer to run. A 20-minute test suite blocks developers and slows CI.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Sequential Execution"
        T1["Test 1: 2s"]
        T2["Test 2: 3s"]
        T3["Test 3: 2s"]
        T4["Test 4: 3s"]
    end
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> TOTAL["Total: 10 seconds"]
    style TOTAL fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Solution: Run in Parallel
steps.explanation: Distribute tests across multiple workers (processes/machines). Total time approaches longest single test, not sum of all.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Worker 1"
        W1T1["Test 1: 2s"]
        W1T2["Test 3: 2s"]
    end
    subgraph "Worker 2"
        W2T1["Test 2: 3s"]
        W2T2["Test 4: 3s"]
    end
    W1T1 --> W1T2
    W2T1 --> W2T2
    W1T2 --> TOTAL["Total: 6 seconds"]
    W2T2 --> TOTAL
    style TOTAL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Test Sharding
steps.explanation: Tests are divided into shards (groups) assigned to different workers. Good sharding balances work evenly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    TESTS["100 Tests"] --> SHARD["Shard by time/count"]
    SHARD --> S1["Shard 1: 25 tests"]
    SHARD --> S2["Shard 2: 25 tests"]
    SHARD --> S3["Shard 3: 25 tests"]
    SHARD --> S4["Shard 4: 25 tests"]
    S1 --> W1["Worker 1"]
    S2 --> W2["Worker 2"]
    S3 --> W3["Worker 3"]
    S4 --> W4["Worker 4"]
    style SHARD fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: pytest-xdist
steps.explanation: pytest-xdist is the standard tool for parallel pytest execution. Use -n auto to use all CPU cores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Commands" C1["pytest -n auto
Use all CPU cores"] C2["pytest -n 4
Use 4 workers"] C3["pytest -n 8
Use 8 workers"] end subgraph "Load Balancing" LB["Tests distributed
dynamically as workers
become available"] end style C1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Test Isolation Requirements
steps.explanation: Parallel tests must be isolated. They cannot share state, files, or database rows without conflicts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Problems"
        P1["Shared database state"]
        P2["Same temp files"]
        P3["Global variables"]
        P4["Port conflicts"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Per-worker DB schemas"]
        S2["Unique file names"]
        S3["No global state"]
        S4["Dynamic port allocation"]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    P4 --> S4
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: CI Parallelization
steps.explanation: In CI, you can parallelize across multiple machines, not just cores. This scales to thousands of tests.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CI Matrix" M1["Machine 1
Tests 1-250"] M2["Machine 2
Tests 251-500"] M3["Machine 3
Tests 501-750"] M4["Machine 4
Tests 751-1000"] end M1 --> RESULTS["Aggregate Results"] M2 --> RESULTS M3 --> RESULTS M4 --> RESULTS RESULTS --> REPORT["Final Report"] style REPORT fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Container vs VM Architecture
fields.description: Comparing container and virtual machine architectures and their tradeoffs

steps.title: The Isolation Problem
steps.explanation: Applications need isolation from each other. Two main approaches: **Virtual Machines** (hardware-level) and **Containers** (OS-level).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "The Goal" G["Run multiple isolated apps
on the same hardware"] end subgraph "Approaches" VM["Virtual Machines
Hardware virtualization"] CT["Containers
OS virtualization"] end G --> VM G --> CT

steps.title: Virtual Machine Architecture
steps.explanation: VMs run on a **hypervisor** that emulates hardware. Each VM has its own full OS kernel, making them heavy but completely isolated.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Virtual Machines" subgraph "VM 1" A1["App A"] L1["Libraries"] OS1["Guest OS
Full Linux Kernel"] end subgraph "VM 2" A2["App B"] L2["Libraries"] OS2["Guest OS
Full Windows"] end HV["Hypervisor
VMware / KVM / Hyper-V"] HW["Hardware"] end A1 --> L1 --> OS1 --> HV A2 --> L2 --> OS2 --> HV HV --> HW style OS1 fill:#FFCDD2 style OS2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Container Architecture
steps.explanation: Containers share the host OS kernel. The **container runtime** (Docker) provides isolation using Linux namespaces and cgroups. Much lighter than VMs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Containers" subgraph "Container 1" CA1["App A"] CL1["Libraries"] end subgraph "Container 2" CA2["App B"] CL2["Libraries"] end DR["Container Runtime
Docker Engine"] HOS["Host OS
Shared Linux Kernel"] CHW["Hardware"] end CA1 --> CL1 --> DR CA2 --> CL2 --> DR DR --> HOS --> CHW style HOS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
steps.explanation: The key difference: VMs virtualize hardware, containers virtualize the OS. Containers share the kernel, making them faster and smaller.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "VM Stack"
        VA["App"] --> VL["Libs"] --> VOS["Guest OS"] --> VHV["Hypervisor"] --> VHW["Hardware"]
    end
    subgraph "Container Stack"
        CA["App"] --> CL["Libs"] --> CDR["Runtime"] --> COS["Host OS"] --> CHW["Hardware"]
    end
    style VOS fill:#FFCDD2
    style CDR fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Resource Comparison
steps.explanation: Containers are dramatically more efficient: faster startup, less memory, smaller disk footprint.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "VMs"
        VM1["Startup: Minutes"]
        VM2["Memory: GBs per VM"]
        VM3["Disk: GBs per image"]
        VM4["Density: 10s per host"]
    end
    subgraph "Containers"
        C1["Startup: Seconds"]
        C2["Memory: MBs per container"]
        C3["Disk: MBs per image"]
        C4["Density: 100s per host"]
    end
    style VM1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style C4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Isolation Comparison
steps.explanation: VMs have stronger isolation (separate kernels). Containers have weaker isolation but are improving with technologies like gVisor and Kata Containers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "VM Isolation" VI["Complete isolation
Separate kernel
Hardware-level security"] end subgraph "Container Isolation" CI["Process isolation
Shared kernel
Namespace boundaries"] end subgraph "Enhanced Container Isolation" ECI["gVisor: user-space kernel
Kata: lightweight VMs
Firecracker: microVMs"] end VI --> STRONG["Stronger"] CI --> GOOD["Good enough for most"] ECI --> BETTER["Better isolation"] style VI fill:#C8E6C9 style CI fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Use VMs for strong isolation, different OS kernels, or legacy apps. Use containers for microservices, CI/CD, and cloud-native apps.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Use VMs When"
        V1["Running Windows on Linux host"]
        V2["Strong security isolation needed"]
        V3["Legacy applications"]
        V4["Different kernel versions"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Containers When"
        C1["Microservices architecture"]
        C2["CI/CD pipelines"]
        C3["Cloud-native apps"]
        C4["High density needed"]
        C5["Fast scaling required"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style C5 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Docker Image Layers
fields.description: How Docker images are built from layers, caching, and why order matters

steps.title: Images are Layered
steps.explanation: A Docker image is not a single file - it's a stack of **read-only layers**. Each layer represents a change from the previous layer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Docker Image"
        L4["Layer 4: COPY app code"]
        L3["Layer 3: RUN pip install"]
        L2["Layer 2: RUN apt-get update"]
        L1["Layer 1: Base OS (Ubuntu)"]
    end
    L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
    style L1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style L4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Dockerfile Creates Layers
steps.explanation: Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new layer. The layer contains only the changes (delta) from the previous layer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Dockerfile"
        D1["FROM ubuntu:22.04"]
        D2["RUN apt-get update"]
        D3["RUN pip install flask"]
        D4["COPY . /app"]
    end
    subgraph "Layers"
        L1["Layer 1"]
        L2["Layer 2"]
        L3["Layer 3"]
        L4["Layer 4"]
    end
    D1 --> L1
    D2 --> L2
    D3 --> L3
    D4 --> L4

steps.title: Layer Sharing
steps.explanation: Multiple images can share the same base layers. This saves disk space and speeds up pulls. Only unique layers need to be downloaded.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Image A"
        A3["App A code"]
        A2["Flask"]
    end
    subgraph "Image B"
        B3["App B code"]
        B2["Flask"]
    end
    subgraph "Shared Layers"
        S2["pip install flask"]
        S1["Ubuntu base"]
    end
    A3 --> A2 --> S2
    B3 --> B2 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Build Cache
steps.explanation: Docker caches each layer. If a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached version. Cache invalidation propagates down - one changed layer invalidates all following layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "First Build"
        F1["Layer 1: Built"]
        F2["Layer 2: Built"]
        F3["Layer 3: Built"]
        F4["Layer 4: Built"]
    end
    subgraph "Second Build - Code Changed"
        S1["Layer 1: CACHED"]
        S2["Layer 2: CACHED"]
        S3["Layer 3: CACHED"]
        S4["Layer 4: REBUILT"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S4 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Order Matters!
steps.explanation: Put frequently changing instructions (like COPY code) LAST. Put stable instructions (like installing dependencies) FIRST. This maximizes cache hits.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "BAD Order"
        B1["FROM python"]
        B2["COPY . /app"]
        B3["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        BN["Code change = reinstall deps!"]
    end
    subgraph "GOOD Order"
        G1["FROM python"]
        G2["COPY requirements.txt ."]
        G3["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        G4["COPY . /app"]
        GN["Code change = only rebuild last layer"]
    end
    style B2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style BN fill:#FFCDD2
    style G4 fill:#C8E6C9
    style GN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Container Layer
steps.explanation: When a container runs, Docker adds a thin **writable layer** on top. All changes happen in this layer. The image layers remain read-only.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Running Container" WL["Writable Container Layer
All changes go here"] IL4["Image Layer 4 (read-only)"] IL3["Image Layer 3 (read-only)"] IL2["Image Layer 2 (read-only)"] IL1["Image Layer 1 (read-only)"] end WL --> IL4 --> IL3 --> IL2 --> IL1 style WL fill:#FFF9C4 style IL1 fill:#E3F2FD style IL2 fill:#E3F2FD style IL3 fill:#E3F2FD style IL4 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Inspecting Layers
steps.explanation: Use `docker history` to see layers and their sizes. Identify large layers and optimize them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "docker history myimage" H1["abc123 2MB COPY . /app"] H2["def456 150MB RUN pip install"] H3["ghi789 200MB RUN apt-get"] H4["jkl012 75MB Ubuntu base"] end TIP["Tip: Combine RUN commands
to reduce layer count"] style H2 fill:#FFCDD2 style H3 fill:#FFCDD2 style TIP fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Container Lifecycle
fields.description: Container states and transitions from creation to removal

steps.title: Container States
steps.explanation: A container goes through several states during its lifecycle: **created**, **running**, **paused**, **stopped**, and eventually **removed**.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    CREATED["Created"] --> RUNNING["Running"]
    RUNNING --> PAUSED["Paused"]
    PAUSED --> RUNNING
    RUNNING --> STOPPED["Stopped"]
    STOPPED --> RUNNING
    STOPPED --> REMOVED["Removed"]
    CREATED --> REMOVED
    style RUNNING fill:#C8E6C9
    style REMOVED fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Create a Container
steps.explanation: `docker create` creates a container from an image but doesn't start it. The container exists but no process is running.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB IMG["Image"] --> CREATE["docker create"] CREATE --> CONTAINER["Container
State: CREATED"] NOTE["Container exists
Filesystem ready
Not running yet"] CONTAINER --> NOTE style CONTAINER fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Start and Run
steps.explanation: `docker start` starts a created container. `docker run` combines create + start in one command. The container's main process is now executing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Two Ways to Run" W1["docker create + docker start"] W2["docker run (create + start)"] end W1 --> RUNNING["Container
State: RUNNING"] W2 --> RUNNING NOTE["Main process (PID 1) executing
Container is live"] RUNNING --> NOTE style RUNNING fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Pause and Unpause
steps.explanation: `docker pause` freezes all processes using cgroups. The container still exists and uses memory, but processes don't execute. `docker unpause` resumes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR RUNNING["Running"] -->|"docker pause"| PAUSED["Paused"] PAUSED -->|"docker unpause"| RUNNING NOTE["Processes frozen
Memory preserved
No CPU usage"] PAUSED --> NOTE style PAUSED fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Stop a Container
steps.explanation: `docker stop` sends SIGTERM, waits (default 10s), then SIGKILL. `docker kill` sends SIGKILL immediately. Container moves to stopped state.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB RUNNING["Running"] -->|"docker stop"| GRACEFUL["SIGTERM sent
Wait 10 seconds"] GRACEFUL --> STOPPED["Stopped"] RUNNING -->|"docker kill"| IMMEDIATE["SIGKILL sent"] IMMEDIATE --> STOPPED style STOPPED fill:#FFCDD2 style GRACEFUL fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Restart and Remove
steps.explanation: `docker restart` stops then starts a container. `docker rm` removes a stopped container. Use `docker rm -f` to force remove a running container.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    STOPPED["Stopped"] -->|"docker start"| RUNNING["Running"]
    STOPPED -->|"docker rm"| REMOVED["Removed"]
    RUNNING -->|"docker restart"| RUNNING
    RUNNING -->|"docker rm -f"| REMOVED
    style REMOVED fill:#FFCDD2
    style RUNNING fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Lifecycle
steps.explanation: The full lifecycle with all commands that trigger state transitions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    IMG["Image"] -->|create| CREATED["Created"]
    CREATED -->|start| RUNNING["Running"]
    RUNNING -->|pause| PAUSED["Paused"]
    PAUSED -->|unpause| RUNNING
    RUNNING -->|stop/kill| STOPPED["Stopped"]
    STOPPED -->|start| RUNNING
    STOPPED -->|rm| REMOVED["Removed"]
    CREATED -->|rm| REMOVED
    style IMG fill:#BBDEFB
    style RUNNING fill:#C8E6C9
    style REMOVED fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Dockerfile Build Process
fields.description: Step-by-step instruction execution, layer creation, and build context

steps.title: The Build Command
steps.explanation: `docker build` reads a Dockerfile and creates an image. The build context (files in the directory) is sent to the Docker daemon.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR CMD["docker build -t myapp ."] CTX["Build Context
All files in directory"] DF["Dockerfile"] DAEMON["Docker Daemon"] CMD --> CTX CMD --> DF CTX --> DAEMON DF --> DAEMON DAEMON --> IMG["Image: myapp"] style IMG fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Build Context
steps.explanation: The build context is the set of files available during build. Large contexts slow down builds. Use `.dockerignore` to exclude files.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Project Directory" APP["app/"] TESTS["tests/"] GIT[".git/"] NODE["node_modules/"] DF["Dockerfile"] end subgraph ".dockerignore" IGN[".git
node_modules
tests"] end subgraph "Build Context" SENT["app/
Dockerfile"] end APP --> SENT DF --> SENT style GIT fill:#FFCDD2 style NODE fill:#FFCDD2 style SENT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Instruction Execution
steps.explanation: Docker executes each instruction in order. Each instruction creates an intermediate container, runs the command, then commits as a new layer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "For Each Instruction" I1["1. Create temp container
from previous layer"] I2["2. Execute instruction
in container"] I3["3. Commit container
as new layer"] I4["4. Remove temp container"] end I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 I4 --> NEXT["Next instruction"] style I3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Instructions
steps.explanation: Key Dockerfile instructions and what layers they create.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Instructions"
        FROM["FROM: Set base image"]
        RUN["RUN: Execute command, creates layer"]
        COPY["COPY: Copy files, creates layer"]
        ENV["ENV: Set environment variable"]
        EXPOSE["EXPOSE: Document port (metadata)"]
        CMD["CMD: Default command (metadata)"]
    end
    RUN --> LAYER["Creates Layer"]
    COPY --> LAYER
    FROM --> LAYER
    ENV --> META["Metadata Only"]
    EXPOSE --> META
    CMD --> META
    style LAYER fill:#FFF9C4
    style META fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Build Example
steps.explanation: A complete build showing each step and resulting layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Dockerfile"
        D1["FROM python:3.11"]
        D2["WORKDIR /app"]
        D3["COPY requirements.txt ."]
        D4["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        D5["COPY . ."]
        D6["CMD python app.py"]
    end
    subgraph "Layers Created"
        L1["Layer 1: Python base"]
        L2["Layer 2: Set workdir"]
        L3["Layer 3: requirements.txt"]
        L4["Layer 4: Dependencies"]
        L5["Layer 5: App code"]
        L6["Metadata: CMD"]
    end
    D1 --> L1
    D4 --> L4
    D5 --> L5
    style L4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style L5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Build Cache in Action
steps.explanation: When rebuilding, Docker checks if each instruction has changed. Unchanged instructions use cached layers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Rebuild After Code Change"
        S1["FROM python:3.11 - CACHED"]
        S2["COPY requirements.txt - CACHED"]
        S3["RUN pip install - CACHED"]
        S4["COPY . . - REBUILDING"]
        S5["CMD - REBUILDING"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S4 fill:#FFF9C4
    style S5 fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Docker Network Types
fields.description: Bridge, host, overlay, and none networks - when to use each

steps.title: Docker Network Drivers
steps.explanation: Docker supports multiple network drivers for different use cases. The default is **bridge**, but **host**, **overlay**, and **none** are also available.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Network Drivers" BRIDGE["bridge
Default, isolated network"] HOST["host
Share host network"] OVERLAY["overlay
Multi-host networking"] NONE["none
No networking"] end style BRIDGE fill:#C8E6C9 style OVERLAY fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Bridge Network (Default)
steps.explanation: Containers on a bridge network get private IPs. They can communicate with each other but are isolated from the host network. Port mapping exposes services.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bridge Network (172.17.0.0/16)" C1["Container A
172.17.0.2"] C2["Container B
172.17.0.3"] BR["docker0 bridge"] end C1 --> BR C2 --> BR BR --> HOST["Host: -p 8080:80"] HOST --> EXT["External Traffic"] style BR fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: User-Defined Bridge
steps.explanation: Custom bridge networks provide automatic DNS resolution between containers. Containers can reach each other by name instead of IP.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "my-network (user-defined)" WEB["web container"] DB["db container"] DNS["Automatic DNS
web can reach 'db'
db can reach 'web'"] end WEB --> DNS DB --> DNS style DNS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Host Network
steps.explanation: Container shares the host's network namespace directly. No network isolation, but best performance. Useful for network-intensive applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Host Network Mode" C["Container
Uses host IP directly
No port mapping needed"] H["Host Network Stack"] end C --> H H --> NET["Network: eth0
IP: 192.168.1.100"] NOTE["No isolation
Best performance
Port conflicts possible"] style C fill:#FFF9C4 style NOTE fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Overlay Network
steps.explanation: Enables communication between containers across multiple Docker hosts. Used with Docker Swarm or Kubernetes for distributed applications.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Host 1" C1["Container A"] end subgraph "Host 2" C2["Container B"] end subgraph "Overlay Network" VXLAN["VXLAN Tunnel
Encrypted traffic"] end C1 --> VXLAN C2 --> VXLAN style VXLAN fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: None Network
steps.explanation: Container has no network access except loopback. Use for maximum isolation or when you'll configure networking manually.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB C["Container
--network none"] LO["Only loopback (127.0.0.1)"] NO["No external access
No container communication"] C --> LO C --> NO style NO fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Choosing a Network
steps.explanation: Quick decision guide for network driver selection.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    Q1{"Multi-host?"} -->|Yes| OVERLAY["Use overlay"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Need isolation?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"Need DNS?"}
    Q2 -->|No| HOST["Use host"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| CUSTOM["User-defined bridge"]
    Q3 -->|No| DEFAULT["Default bridge"]
    style CUSTOM fill:#C8E6C9
    style OVERLAY fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Container-to-Container Communication
fields.description: DNS resolution, network namespaces, and port mapping between containers

steps.title: Containers Need to Talk
steps.explanation: In a multi-container app, services need to communicate. Web servers talk to databases, APIs talk to caches. Docker provides several mechanisms.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    WEB["Web App"] --> API["API Server"]
    API --> DB["Database"]
    API --> CACHE["Redis Cache"]
    style WEB fill:#C8E6C9
    style DB fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Same Network = Direct Communication
steps.explanation: Containers on the same Docker network can communicate directly using container names (DNS) or IP addresses.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "my-network" WEB["web
172.18.0.2"] DB["db
172.18.0.3"] end WEB -->|"db:5432 or 172.18.0.3:5432"| DB NOTE["Same network = automatic connectivity"] style NOTE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Docker DNS
steps.explanation: User-defined networks have built-in DNS. Containers can reach each other by name. Docker's embedded DNS server resolves names to IPs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB WEB["web container"] DNS["Docker DNS Server
127.0.0.11"] DB["db container
172.18.0.3"] WEB -->|"resolve 'db'"| DNS DNS -->|"172.18.0.3"| WEB WEB -->|"connect"| DB style DNS fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Port Mapping to Host
steps.explanation: To expose a container to the host or external world, map container ports to host ports with `-p host:container`.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB EXT["External Client"] HOST["Host
Port 8080"] CONT["Container
Port 80"] EXT -->|"localhost:8080"| HOST HOST -->|"-p 8080:80"| CONT NOTE["docker run -p 8080:80 nginx"] style HOST fill:#FFF9C4 style CONT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Container Links (Legacy)
steps.explanation: The old `--link` flag is deprecated. Use user-defined networks instead for automatic DNS and better isolation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Legacy (Avoid)"
        L1["--link db:database"]
        L2["Injects env vars"]
        L3["Modifies /etc/hosts"]
    end
    subgraph "Modern (Preferred)"
        M1["--network my-network"]
        M2["Automatic DNS"]
        M3["Better isolation"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Network Aliases
steps.explanation: Containers can have multiple DNS names via network aliases. Useful for service discovery patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Container with Aliases"
        DB["postgres container"]
        A1["Alias: db"]
        A2["Alias: database"]
        A3["Alias: postgres"]
    end
    WEB["web"] -->|"any alias works"| DB
    A1 --> DB
    A2 --> DB
    A3 --> DB
    style DB fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Docker Volume Types
fields.description: Named volumes vs bind mounts vs tmpfs - persistence and use cases

steps.title: The Persistence Problem
steps.explanation: Container filesystems are ephemeral - data is lost when the container is removed. Volumes provide persistent storage outside the container lifecycle.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Volumes"
        C1["Container writes data"]
        C2["Container removed"]
        C3["Data LOST"]
    end
    subgraph "With Volumes"
        V1["Container writes to volume"]
        V2["Container removed"]
        V3["Data PERSISTS"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    V1 --> V2 --> V3
    style C3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style V3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Three Types of Mounts
steps.explanation: Docker supports three mount types: **volumes** (Docker-managed), **bind mounts** (host paths), and **tmpfs** (memory-only).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mount Types" VOL["Volumes
Docker-managed storage"] BIND["Bind Mounts
Host filesystem paths"] TMPFS["tmpfs
Memory only, not persisted"] end style VOL fill:#C8E6C9 style BIND fill:#FFF9C4 style TMPFS fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Named Volumes
steps.explanation: Volumes are stored in Docker's storage area. Docker manages them completely. Best for production data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Host"
        DS["/var/lib/docker/volumes/mydata/_data"]
    end
    subgraph "Container"
        CP["/app/data"]
    end
    DS <-->|"volume: mydata"| CP
    CMD["docker run -v mydata:/app/data"]
    CMD --> CP
    style DS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Bind Mounts
steps.explanation: Bind mounts map a specific host path into the container. Great for development - edit code on host, see changes in container.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Host"
        HP["/home/user/myapp"]
    end
    subgraph "Container"
        CP["/app"]
    end
    HP <-->|"bind mount"| CP
    CMD["docker run -v /home/user/myapp:/app"]
    CMD --> CP
    NOTE["Changes sync both ways"]
    style HP fill:#FFF9C4
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: tmpfs Mounts
steps.explanation: tmpfs stores data in memory only. Fast, but data is lost when container stops. Good for sensitive data you don't want on disk.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Container" TMP["/app/temp"] end subgraph "Host Memory" RAM["RAM (not disk)"] end RAM <-->|"tmpfs"| TMP NOTE["Fast, ephemeral
Good for secrets, temp files"] style RAM fill:#BBDEFB style NOTE fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Volume Comparison
steps.explanation: Quick comparison of when to use each type.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Use Volumes When"
        V1["Production data"]
        V2["Sharing between containers"]
        V3["Backup/restore needed"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Bind Mounts When"
        B1["Development workflow"]
        B2["Config files from host"]
        B3["Specific host path needed"]
    end
    subgraph "Use tmpfs When"
        T1["Sensitive data"]
        T2["Temporary files"]
        T3["Performance critical"]
    end
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style T1 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Volume Commands
steps.explanation: Common commands for managing Docker volumes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Commands"
        C1["docker volume create mydata"]
        C2["docker volume ls"]
        C3["docker volume inspect mydata"]
        C4["docker volume rm mydata"]
        C5["docker volume prune"]
    end
    C1 --> CREATE["Create named volume"]
    C2 --> LIST["List all volumes"]
    C5 --> CLEAN["Remove unused volumes"]
    style C5 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Docker Compose Service Graph
fields.description: How services, networks, and volumes interconnect in Compose

steps.title: What is Docker Compose?
steps.explanation: Docker Compose defines multi-container applications in a YAML file. One command starts all services with their networks and volumes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    YAML["docker-compose.yml"]
    CMD["docker compose up"]
    YAML --> CMD
    CMD --> S1["Service 1"]
    CMD --> S2["Service 2"]
    CMD --> NET["Network"]
    CMD --> VOL["Volume"]
    style CMD fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Service Definition
steps.explanation: Each service is a container configuration. Services can depend on each other, share networks, and mount volumes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "docker-compose.yml" S1["web:
image: nginx
ports: 80:80"] S2["api:
build: ./api
depends_on: db"] S3["db:
image: postgres
volumes: data:/var/lib/postgresql"] end style S1 fill:#C8E6C9 style S2 fill:#FFF9C4 style S3 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Service Dependencies
steps.explanation: `depends_on` controls startup order. The database starts before the API. Note: doesn't wait for the service to be ready, just started.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR DB["db"] -->|"starts first"| API["api"] API -->|"starts second"| WEB["web"] NOTE["depends_on controls order
Use healthchecks for readiness"] style DB fill:#BBDEFB style API fill:#FFF9C4 style WEB fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Automatic Networking
steps.explanation: Compose creates a default network for all services. Services can reach each other by service name.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "myapp_default network"
        WEB["web"]
        API["api"]
        DB["db"]
    end
    WEB -->|"http://api:3000"| API
    API -->|"postgres://db:5432"| DB
    NOTE["Service names are DNS names"]
    style NOTE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Custom Networks
steps.explanation: Define custom networks to isolate groups of services. Services only see others on shared networks.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "frontend network"
        WEB["web"]
        API["api"]
    end
    subgraph "backend network"
        API2["api"]
        DB["db"]
        CACHE["redis"]
    end
    WEB --> API
    API2 --> DB
    API2 --> CACHE
    NOTE["web cannot reach db directly"]
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Shared Volumes
steps.explanation: Named volumes can be shared between services. Useful for shared data or communication.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Services"
        APP["app service"]
        WORKER["worker service"]
    end
    subgraph "Shared Volume"
        VOL["uploads volume"]
    end
    APP -->|"write files"| VOL
    WORKER -->|"process files"| VOL
    style VOL fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: A typical web application with all components connected.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "docker-compose.yml" NGINX["nginx
:80, :443"] APP["app
:3000"] DB["postgres
:5432"] REDIS["redis
:6379"] DBVOL["db_data volume"] end NGINX -->|"proxy"| APP APP --> DB APP --> REDIS DB --> DBVOL style NGINX fill:#C8E6C9 style DBVOL fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Container Orchestration Basics
fields.description: Single host vs swarm vs Kubernetes - scaling concepts

steps.title: Why Orchestration?
steps.explanation: A single host has limits. Orchestration manages containers across multiple hosts, handles failures, and enables scaling.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Single Host Problems"
        P1["Limited resources"]
        P2["Single point of failure"]
        P3["Manual scaling"]
        P4["No self-healing"]
    end
    subgraph "Orchestration Solves"
        S1["Distribute across hosts"]
        S2["High availability"]
        S3["Auto-scaling"]
        S4["Auto-restart failed containers"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Single Host (Docker)
steps.explanation: Plain Docker runs containers on one machine. Fine for development, but limited for production.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Host" D["Docker Engine"] C1["Container 1"] C2["Container 2"] C3["Container 3"] end D --> C1 D --> C2 D --> C3 NOTE["Simple, but limited
Host fails = all containers fail"] style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Docker Swarm
steps.explanation: Swarm is Docker's built-in orchestration. Multiple Docker hosts form a cluster. Simple to set up if you're already using Docker.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Swarm Cluster" MGR["Manager Node"] W1["Worker Node 1"] W2["Worker Node 2"] W3["Worker Node 3"] end MGR -->|"schedule"| W1 MGR -->|"schedule"| W2 MGR -->|"schedule"| W3 NOTE["Built into Docker
Good for simpler deployments"] style MGR fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Kubernetes (K8s)
steps.explanation: Kubernetes is the industry standard orchestrator. More complex but more powerful. Manages containers as Pods across many nodes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Kubernetes Cluster" CP["Control Plane"] N1["Node 1"] N2["Node 2"] N3["Node 3"] end subgraph "Node 1" P1["Pod"] P2["Pod"] end CP --> N1 CP --> N2 CP --> N3 NOTE["Industry standard
Complex but powerful"] style CP fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Key Orchestration Features
steps.explanation: All orchestrators provide these core capabilities.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Core Features" F1["Service Discovery
Find containers by name"] F2["Load Balancing
Distribute traffic"] F3["Scaling
Add/remove replicas"] F4["Self-Healing
Restart failed containers"] F5["Rolling Updates
Zero-downtime deploys"] end style F1 fill:#C8E6C9 style F4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Scaling Comparison
steps.explanation: How scaling works at each level.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Docker (Manual)"
        D["docker run x3"]
    end
    subgraph "Swarm"
        S["docker service scale web=5"]
    end
    subgraph "Kubernetes"
        K["kubectl scale deployment web --replicas=5"]
    end
    D --> DM["Manual, no balancing"]
    S --> SM["Automatic distribution"]
    K --> KM["Declarative, auto-healing"]
    style KM fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Use What
steps.explanation: Choose based on your needs and team expertise.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Docker Only"
        D1["Development"]
        D2["Simple apps"]
        D3["Single server"]
    end
    subgraph "Docker Swarm"
        S1["Small clusters"]
        S2["Docker-native teams"]
        S3["Simpler operations"]
    end
    subgraph "Kubernetes"
        K1["Large scale"]
        K2["Complex apps"]
        K3["Multi-cloud"]
        K4["Industry standard"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style K1 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Health Checks and Restart Policies
fields.description: Container health monitoring and automatic recovery

steps.title: Why Health Checks?
steps.explanation: A running container isn't always healthy. The process might be running but the app could be stuck, deadlocked, or failing. Health checks verify the app is actually working.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Without Health Check"
        C1["Container Running"]
        A1["App Deadlocked"]
        S1["Docker thinks OK"]
    end
    subgraph "With Health Check"
        C2["Container Running"]
        A2["App Deadlocked"]
        H2["Health Check Fails"]
        S2["Docker knows UNHEALTHY"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Health Check Definition
steps.explanation: Define a command that tests if your application is healthy. Return 0 for healthy, 1 for unhealthy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dockerfile" HC["HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s
CMD curl -f http://localhost/ || exit 1"] end subgraph "Health States" STARTING["starting"] HEALTHY["healthy"] UNHEALTHY["unhealthy"] end HC --> STARTING -->|"first check passes"| HEALTHY HEALTHY -->|"check fails"| UNHEALTHY UNHEALTHY -->|"check passes"| HEALTHY style HEALTHY fill:#C8E6C9 style UNHEALTHY fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Health Check Options
steps.explanation: Configure how often to check, how long to wait, and how many failures before marking unhealthy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Options" INT["--interval=30s
Time between checks"] TO["--timeout=3s
Max time for check to complete"] SP["--start-period=5s
Grace period at startup"] RET["--retries=3
Failures before unhealthy"] end style INT fill:#BBDEFB style RET fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Restart Policies
steps.explanation: Restart policies tell Docker what to do when a container stops. Options: no, on-failure, always, unless-stopped.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Restart Policies" NO["no
Never restart"] FAIL["on-failure
Restart on non-zero exit"] ALWAYS["always
Always restart"] UNLESS["unless-stopped
Restart unless manually stopped"] end style NO fill:#FFCDD2 style ALWAYS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Restart Policy Behavior
steps.explanation: How each policy responds to different scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Container Exits with Error"
        E1["no: stays stopped"]
        E2["on-failure: restarts"]
        E3["always: restarts"]
        E4["unless-stopped: restarts"]
    end
    subgraph "Docker Daemon Restarts"
        D1["no: stays stopped"]
        D2["on-failure: stays stopped"]
        D3["always: restarts"]
        D4["unless-stopped: restarts if was running"]
    end
    style E2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style E3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Combining Health + Restart
steps.explanation: Health checks and restart policies work together. Orchestrators use health status to decide when to restart or replace containers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    HC["Health Check"] -->|"unhealthy"| ORCH["Orchestrator"]
    ORCH --> STOP["Stop unhealthy container"]
    STOP --> START["Start replacement"]
    RP["Restart Policy"] -->|"crash"| RESTART["Auto-restart"]
    style ORCH fill:#BBDEFB
    style START fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Image Security Layers
fields.description: Base image selection, vulnerability scanning, and minimal images

steps.title: Image Security Matters
steps.explanation: Container images can contain vulnerabilities inherited from base images, dependencies, or your code. Security must be built in at every layer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Attack Surface"
        BASE["Base OS vulnerabilities"]
        DEPS["Dependency vulnerabilities"]
        CODE["Application vulnerabilities"]
        CONFIG["Misconfigurations"]
    end
    style BASE fill:#FFCDD2
    style DEPS fill:#FFCDD2
    style CODE fill:#FFCDD2
    style CONFIG fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Base Image Selection
steps.explanation: Start with minimal, trusted base images. Smaller images have fewer vulnerabilities. Official images are regularly updated.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Base Image Options" FULL["ubuntu:22.04
~77MB, many packages"] SLIM["python:3.11-slim
~150MB, reduced"] ALPINE["python:3.11-alpine
~50MB, minimal"] DISTROLESS["distroless
