~20MB, no shell"] end FULL --> MORE["More vulnerabilities"] DISTROLESS --> LESS["Fewer vulnerabilities"] style FULL fill:#FFCDD2 style DISTROLESS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Vulnerability Scanning
steps.explanation: Scan images for known vulnerabilities (CVEs). Integrate scanning into CI/CD to catch issues before deployment.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR IMG["Docker Image"] --> SCAN["Scanner
Trivy/Snyk/Grype"] SCAN --> REPORT["Vulnerability Report"] REPORT --> CRIT{"Critical CVEs?"} CRIT -->|Yes| FAIL["Block Deploy"] CRIT -->|No| PASS["Allow Deploy"] style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2 style PASS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Reduce Attack Surface
steps.explanation: Remove unnecessary packages, files, and capabilities. Run as non-root user. Keep images minimal.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        NR["Run as non-root user"]
        RM["Remove unnecessary packages"]
        RO["Read-only filesystem"]
        NO["No secrets in image"]
        UP["Keep base images updated"]
    end
    style NR fill:#C8E6C9
    style NO fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Non-Root Containers
steps.explanation: Never run as root. Create a dedicated user in the Dockerfile.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dockerfile" D1["RUN useradd -r -u 1000 appuser"] D2["USER appuser"] D3["WORKDIR /app"] end subgraph "Result" R["Container runs as appuser
Limited permissions
Cannot modify system files"] end D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> R style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Image Signing
steps.explanation: Sign images to verify authenticity. Ensure images haven't been tampered with between build and deploy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR BUILD["Build Image"] --> SIGN["Sign with key"] SIGN --> PUSH["Push to Registry"] PUSH --> VERIFY["Verify signature"] VERIFY --> DEPLOY["Deploy"] NOTE["Docker Content Trust
Notary/cosign"] style SIGN fill:#BBDEFB style VERIFY fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Multi-Stage Builds
fields.description: Build stage vs runtime stage - reducing final image size

steps.title: The Problem
steps.explanation: Build tools and dependencies are needed to build but not to run. A single-stage build includes everything, bloating the final image.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Single Stage Image"
        BUILD["Build tools (gcc, make)"]
        DEPS["Build dependencies"]
        SRC["Source code"]
        BIN["Application binary"]
        SIZE["Total: 1.2 GB"]
    end
    style BUILD fill:#FFCDD2
    style DEPS fill:#FFCDD2
    style SIZE fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Multi-Stage Solution
steps.explanation: Use multiple FROM statements. Build in one stage, copy only artifacts to the final stage. Final image contains only what's needed to run.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Build Stage"
        B1["FROM golang AS builder"]
        B2["Install deps"]
        B3["Compile code"]
        B4["Binary: myapp"]
    end
    subgraph "Runtime Stage"
        R1["FROM alpine"]
        R2["COPY --from=builder /myapp"]
        R3["Final image: 15 MB"]
    end
    B4 -->|"copy"| R2
    style B1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style R3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Dockerfile Example
steps.explanation: A typical multi-stage Dockerfile for a Go application.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dockerfile" S1["# Build stage
FROM golang:1.21 AS builder"] S2["WORKDIR /src"] S3["COPY . ."] S4["RUN go build -o /app"] S5["# Runtime stage
FROM alpine:3.18"] S6["COPY --from=builder /app /app"] S7["CMD ['/app']"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 style S1 fill:#FFF9C4 style S5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Size Comparison
steps.explanation: Multi-stage builds dramatically reduce image size by excluding build-time dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Stage" SS["golang:1.21 base: 800MB
+ deps: 200MB
+ source: 50MB
= 1.05 GB"] end subgraph "Multi-Stage" MS["alpine base: 5MB
+ binary: 10MB
= 15 MB"] end SS --> REDUCTION["70x smaller!"] MS --> REDUCTION style SS fill:#FFCDD2 style MS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Build Stages
steps.explanation: You can have multiple build stages for complex pipelines: build, test, then deploy.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Stage: deps"
        D["Install dependencies"]
    end
    subgraph "Stage: test"
        T["Run tests"]
    end
    subgraph "Stage: build"
        B["Compile"]
    end
    subgraph "Stage: final"
        F["Runtime only"]
    end
    D --> T --> B --> F
    style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for effective multi-stage builds.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Do"
        D1["Use specific stage names"]
        D2["Copy only necessary files"]
        D3["Use minimal runtime base"]
        D4["Order stages for caching"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["Copying entire directories"]
        A2["Including test frameworks"]
        A3["Leaving debug tools"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Container Isolation
fields.description: Namespaces, cgroups, and security boundaries

steps.title: How Containers Isolate
steps.explanation: Containers use Linux kernel features for isolation: **namespaces** for visibility and **cgroups** for resource limits.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Isolation Mechanisms" NS["Namespaces
What you can SEE"] CG["Cgroups
What you can USE"] SEC["Seccomp/AppArmor
What you can DO"] end NS --> ISO["Container Isolation"] CG --> ISO SEC --> ISO style ISO fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Namespaces
steps.explanation: Namespaces isolate what a process can see. Each namespace type controls visibility of different resources.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Namespace Types" PID["PID: Process IDs"] NET["NET: Network stack"] MNT["MNT: Filesystem mounts"] UTS["UTS: Hostname"] IPC["IPC: Inter-process comm"] USER["USER: User/Group IDs"] end PID --> EFFECT["Container sees only
its own processes"] NET --> EFFECT2["Container has own
network interfaces"] style PID fill:#BBDEFB style NET fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: PID Namespace Example
steps.explanation: Inside a container, PID 1 is your app. The container can't see host processes. On the host, container processes have different PIDs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Host View" H1["PID 1: systemd"] H2["PID 1000: dockerd"] H3["PID 2000: container app"] end subgraph "Container View" C1["PID 1: app
Only sees itself"] end H3 -.-> C1 style C1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Cgroups (Control Groups)
steps.explanation: Cgroups limit and account for resource usage: CPU, memory, disk I/O, network. Prevents one container from starving others.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Resource Limits"
        CPU["CPU: --cpus=2"]
        MEM["Memory: --memory=512m"]
        IO["Disk I/O: --device-read-bps"]
        PID["PIDs: --pids-limit=100"]
    end
    CPU --> ENFORCE["Kernel enforces limits"]
    MEM --> ENFORCE
    ENFORCE --> OOM["Exceed memory = OOM killed"]
    style OOM fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Security Boundaries
steps.explanation: Additional security layers: seccomp filters syscalls, AppArmor/SELinux restrict access, capabilities limit root powers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Defense in Depth" SC["Seccomp
Block dangerous syscalls"] AA["AppArmor/SELinux
Mandatory access control"] CAP["Capabilities
Fine-grained root powers"] RO["Read-only rootfs
Prevent modifications"] end style SC fill:#C8E6C9 style CAP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Isolation vs VMs
steps.explanation: Containers share a kernel, so isolation is weaker than VMs. But for most use cases, it's sufficient.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "VM Isolation" VM["Complete kernel separation
Hardware virtualization
Strongest isolation"] end subgraph "Container Isolation" CT["Kernel shared
Namespace + cgroup
Good for most cases"] end subgraph "Enhanced Containers" GV["gVisor: user-space kernel
Kata: lightweight VM
Better isolation"] end style VM fill:#C8E6C9 style CT fill:#FFF9C4 style GV fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: CI/CD with Docker
fields.description: Build, test, push, deploy pipeline with container registries

steps.title: Docker CI/CD Overview
steps.explanation: Docker enables consistent CI/CD: build once, run anywhere. The same image tested in CI is deployed to production.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    CODE["Code Push"] --> BUILD["Build Image"]
    BUILD --> TEST["Test in Container"]
    TEST --> PUSH["Push to Registry"]
    PUSH --> DEPLOY["Deploy"]
    style BUILD fill:#FFF9C4
    style DEPLOY fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Build Stage
steps.explanation: CI builds a Docker image on every commit. Use multi-stage builds for efficient, small images. Tag with commit SHA for traceability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Build Stage"
        GIT["git commit abc123"]
        BUILD["docker build -t app:abc123"]
        TAG["docker tag app:abc123 app:latest"]
    end
    GIT --> BUILD --> TAG
    style BUILD fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Test Stage
steps.explanation: Run tests inside containers for consistency. Use docker-compose for integration tests with databases and services.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Test Stage" UNIT["docker run app:abc123 pytest"] INT["docker-compose up -d
Run integration tests"] SCAN["docker scan app:abc123
Security scan"] end UNIT --> INT --> SCAN style UNIT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Push to Registry
steps.explanation: Push tested images to a container registry. Use private registries for production images.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR IMG["app:abc123"] --> PUSH["docker push"] PUSH --> REG["Container Registry
Docker Hub / ECR / GCR"] subgraph "Registries" HUB["Docker Hub"] ECR["AWS ECR"] GCR["Google GCR"] ACR["Azure ACR"] end style REG fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Deploy Stage
steps.explanation: Deploy by pulling the image from registry. Orchestrators pull and run the new version with zero downtime.

        ROLL["Rolling update"]
        HEALTH["Health check"]
        DONE["Deployment complete"]
    end
    PULL --> ROLL --> HEALTH --> DONE
    subgraph "Rollback if needed"
        PREV["Previous version available"]
    end
    style DONE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Pipeline
steps.explanation: A complete CI/CD pipeline with Docker.

    subgraph "CI"
        C1["Checkout"]
        C2["Build"]
        C3["Test"]
        C4["Scan"]
        C5["Push"]
    end
    subgraph "CD"
        D1["Deploy Staging"]
        D2["Integration Tests"]
        D3["Deploy Prod"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    style C5 fill:#FFF9C4
    style D3 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Container Logging and Monitoring
fields.description: Container logs, metrics collection, and observability stack

steps.title: Container Observability
steps.explanation: Containers are ephemeral. Traditional monitoring doesn't work well. You need centralized logging and metrics collection.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Three Pillars" LOGS["Logs
What happened"] METRICS["Metrics
How it's performing"] TRACES["Traces
Request flow"] end LOGS --> OBS["Observability"] METRICS --> OBS TRACES --> OBS style OBS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Container Logging
steps.explanation: Containers write to stdout/stderr. Docker captures this. Use `docker logs` to view. Configure logging drivers for centralization.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR APP["App writes to stdout"] --> DOCKER["Docker Daemon"] DOCKER --> LOCAL["docker logs
Local JSON files"] DOCKER --> DRIVER["Logging Driver"] DRIVER --> ELK["Elasticsearch"] DRIVER --> SPLUNK["Splunk"] DRIVER --> CW["CloudWatch"] style LOCAL fill:#FFF9C4 style ELK fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Logging Drivers
steps.explanation: Configure where Docker sends logs. Default is json-file. Production typically uses fluentd, syslog, or cloud providers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Logging Drivers"
        JSON["json-file (default)"]
        SYSLOG["syslog"]
        FLUENTD["fluentd"]
        GELF["gelf (Graylog)"]
        AWS["awslogs"]
    end
    JSON --> DEV["Good for development"]
    FLUENTD --> PROD["Good for production"]
    style JSON fill:#FFF9C4
    style FLUENTD fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Metrics Collection
steps.explanation: Collect container metrics: CPU, memory, network, disk. Use cAdvisor, Prometheus, or cloud monitoring.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Metrics Sources" DOCKER["Docker daemon
docker stats"] CADVISOR["cAdvisor
Container metrics"] APP["App metrics
/metrics endpoint"] end subgraph "Collection" PROM["Prometheus
Scrape & store"] end subgraph "Visualization" GRAF["Grafana
Dashboards"] end DOCKER --> CADVISOR --> PROM --> GRAF APP --> PROM style GRAF fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Stack: ELK
steps.explanation: Elasticsearch, Logstash/Fluentd, Kibana - the classic logging stack for containers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Containers"
        C1["Container 1"]
        C2["Container 2"]
        C3["Container 3"]
    end
    subgraph "Collection"
        FD["Fluentd/Filebeat"]
    end
    subgraph "Storage"
        ES["Elasticsearch"]
    end
    subgraph "UI"
        KB["Kibana"]
    end
    C1 --> FD
    C2 --> FD
    C3 --> FD
    FD --> ES --> KB
    style KB fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Monitoring Stack: Prometheus + Grafana
steps.explanation: Prometheus scrapes metrics, Grafana visualizes. Standard for Kubernetes and container monitoring.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Exporters" NE["Node Exporter
Host metrics"] CE["cAdvisor
Container metrics"] AE["App Exporter
/metrics"] end PROM["Prometheus"] --> NE PROM --> CE PROM --> AE PROM --> GRAF["Grafana"] PROM --> ALERT["Alertmanager"] ALERT --> SLACK["Slack/PagerDuty"] style GRAF fill:#C8E6C9 style ALERT fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Key practices for container observability.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Logging"
        L1["Log to stdout/stderr"]
        L2["Use structured JSON logs"]
        L3["Include request IDs"]
        L4["Centralize all logs"]
    end
    subgraph "Metrics"
        M1["Export app metrics"]
        M2["Set up alerts"]
        M3["Create dashboards"]
        M4["Monitor resource usage"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style M2 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: BaseModel Anatomy
fields.description: Understanding the structure of a Pydantic model: fields, types, defaults, and metadata

steps.title: What is BaseModel?
steps.explanation: **BaseModel** is the foundation of Pydantic. It's a class that provides automatic validation, serialization, and type coercion based on Python type hints.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BaseModel Features" V["Validation
Check data matches types"] S["Serialization
Convert to dict/JSON"] C["Coercion
Convert types automatically"] D["Documentation
Generate JSON Schema"] end BM["BaseModel"] --> V BM --> S BM --> C BM --> D style BM fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Basic Model Structure
steps.explanation: A Pydantic model is a class that inherits from BaseModel. Fields are defined as class attributes with type annotations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Model Definition"
        CLS["class User(BaseModel):"]
        F1["    name: str"]
        F2["    age: int"]
        F3["    email: str"]
    end
    subgraph "Components"
        C1["Class inherits BaseModel"]
        C2["Field name"]
        C3["Type annotation"]
    end
    CLS --> C1
    F1 --> C2
    F1 --> C3
    style CLS fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Field Types
steps.explanation: Pydantic supports all Python types plus special types for validation. Fields can be required, optional, or have defaults.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Required Fields" R["name: str
No default = required"] end subgraph "Optional with Default" D["age: int = 0
Has default value"] end subgraph "Optional (None allowed)" O["nickname: str | None = None
Can be None"] end style R fill:#FFCDD2 style D fill:#C8E6C9 style O fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Field() for Metadata
steps.explanation: Use **Field()** to add constraints, descriptions, aliases, and other metadata to fields.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Field Options"
        F1["Field(default=...)"]
        F2["Field(min_length=1)"]
        F3["Field(ge=0, le=100)"]
        F4["Field(alias='userName')"]
        F5["Field(description='...')"]
    end
    F1 --> D["Default value"]
    F2 --> V["String constraints"]
    F3 --> N["Numeric constraints"]
    F4 --> A["JSON key alias"]
    F5 --> DOC["Documentation"]
    style F3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Model Instance Creation
steps.explanation: Create instances by passing data as keyword arguments or a dictionary. Pydantic validates and coerces automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Input" KW["User(name='Alice', age='25')"] DICT["User(**{'name': 'Alice'})"] end subgraph "Pydantic" VAL["Validate & Coerce"] end subgraph "Output" INST["User instance
name='Alice'
age=25 (int!)"] end KW --> VAL --> INST DICT --> VAL style VAL fill:#FFF9C4 style INST fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Accessing Data
steps.explanation: Access fields as attributes. Use model_dump() for dict, model_dump_json() for JSON string.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Access Methods" ATTR["user.name
Attribute access"] DUMP["user.model_dump()
Returns dict"] JSON["user.model_dump_json()
Returns JSON string"] FIELDS["user.model_fields
Field metadata"] end style ATTR fill:#C8E6C9 style DUMP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: A complete model with various field types and options.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Model" M["class User(BaseModel):
name: str
age: int = Field(ge=0)
email: str | None = None
tags: list[str] = []"] end subgraph "Usage" U["user = User(name='Bob', age=30)
user.model_dump()"] end subgraph "Output" O["{'name': 'Bob',
'age': 30,
'email': None,
'tags': []}"] end M --> U --> O style O fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Validation Flow
fields.description: How Pydantic processes input data through parsing, validation, and model creation

steps.title: Validation Pipeline
steps.explanation: When you create a model instance, Pydantic runs a pipeline: parse input, coerce types, validate constraints, run validators, create instance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    INPUT["Input Data"] --> PARSE["Parse"]
    PARSE --> COERCE["Coerce Types"]
    COERCE --> VALIDATE["Validate"]
    VALIDATE --> CUSTOM["Custom Validators"]
    CUSTOM --> INSTANCE["Model Instance"]
    style INPUT fill:#BBDEFB
    style INSTANCE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Step 1: Parse Input
steps.explanation: Pydantic accepts various input formats: keyword args, dicts, JSON strings, or other model instances.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Valid Inputs"
        KW["User(name='Alice', age=25)"]
        DICT["User(**data_dict)"]
        JSON["User.model_validate_json(json_str)"]
        MODEL["User.model_validate(other_user)"]
    end
    ALL --> PARSE["Internal dict representation"]
    style PARSE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Step 2: Type Coercion
steps.explanation: Pydantic tries to convert input to the expected type. String '25' becomes int 25. This is configurable with strict mode.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Coercion Examples"
        C1["'25' --> 25 (str to int)"]
        C2["25 --> '25' (int to str)"]
        C3["'true' --> True (str to bool)"]
        C4["{'a': 1} --> Model (dict to model)"]
    end
    subgraph "Strict Mode"
        S["No coercion - must match exactly"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Step 3: Constraint Validation
steps.explanation: After coercion, Pydantic checks field constraints: min/max values, string length, regex patterns, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Constraints Checked"
        N["Numeric: ge, le, gt, lt"]
        S["String: min_length, max_length, pattern"]
        L["List: min_length, max_length"]
        T["Type: must match annotation"]
    end
    CHECK{"All pass?"} -->|Yes| NEXT["Continue"]
    CHECK -->|No| ERROR["ValidationError"]
    style ERROR fill:#FFCDD2
    style NEXT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Step 4: Custom Validators
steps.explanation: After built-in validation, custom validators run. Field validators run per-field, model validators run on the whole model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Validator Order"
        BV["Before validators (pre-processing)"]
        FV["Field validators"]
        MV["Model validators"]
        AV["After validators (post-processing)"]
    end
    BV --> FV --> MV --> AV
    style BV fill:#BBDEFB
    style AV fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Success vs Failure
steps.explanation: If all validation passes, you get a model instance. If any fails, you get a ValidationError with all errors collected.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB VAL["Validation"] --> CHECK{"Valid?"} CHECK -->|Yes| SUCCESS["Model Instance
All fields populated"] CHECK -->|No| FAIL["ValidationError
List of all errors"] FAIL --> ERRORS["[
{'loc': ('age',), 'msg': '...'},
{'loc': ('email',), 'msg': '...'}
]"] style SUCCESS fill:#C8E6C9 style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Complete Flow Diagram
steps.explanation: The full validation flow from input to output.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB INPUT["User(name='Al', age='abc')"] INPUT --> PARSE["Parse to dict"] PARSE --> COERCE["Coerce 'abc' to int"] COERCE --> FAIL["FAILS - not a number"] FAIL --> ERROR["ValidationError:
age: value is not a valid integer"] style ERROR fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Type Coercion
fields.description: How Pydantic automatically converts input types to expected types

steps.title: What is Type Coercion?
steps.explanation: **Type coercion** is automatic conversion of input values to expected types. Pydantic is lenient by default - it tries to make data fit.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Input" I["age: int
value: '25' (string)"] end subgraph "Coercion" C["Convert '25' to 25"] end subgraph "Result" R["age = 25 (int)"] end I --> C --> R style C fill:#FFF9C4 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Coercions
steps.explanation: Pydantic handles many type conversions automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "String to Number"
        S1["'123' --> 123 (int)"]
        S2["'3.14' --> 3.14 (float)"]
    end
    subgraph "Number to String"
        N1["123 --> '123' (str)"]
    end
    subgraph "String to Bool"
        B1["'true', '1', 'yes' --> True"]
        B2["'false', '0', 'no' --> False"]
    end
    subgraph "Dict to Model"
        D1["{'name': 'X'} --> User(name='X')"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: List and Collection Coercion
steps.explanation: Sequences can be coerced between types. Items inside are also coerced recursively.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Collection Coercion" L1["(1, 2, 3) --> [1, 2, 3]
tuple to list"] L2["[1, 2, 3] --> {1, 2, 3}
list to set"] L3["['1', '2'] --> [1, 2]
list[int] coerces items"] end style L3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Datetime Coercion
steps.explanation: Strings in common formats are automatically parsed to datetime objects.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Datetime Parsing"
        DT1["'2024-01-15' --> date"]
        DT2["'2024-01-15T10:30:00' --> datetime"]
        DT3["1705312200 --> datetime (Unix timestamp)"]
    end
    style DT1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style DT3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Strict Mode
steps.explanation: Strict mode disables coercion - types must match exactly. Enable per-field or for the whole model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Lax Mode (default)" L["age: int = '25'
Coerced to 25"] end subgraph "Strict Mode" S["age: int = '25'
ValidationError!
Expected int, got str"] end subgraph "Enable Strict" E1["Field(strict=True)"] E2["model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)"] end style L fill:#C8E6C9 style S fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Coercion Failures
steps.explanation: When coercion fails, Pydantic raises a clear ValidationError.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cannot Coerce" F1["'hello' to int"] F2["'not-a-date' to datetime"] F3["'abc' to float"] end F1 --> ERROR["ValidationError:
Input should be a valid integer"] style ERROR fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Field Types and Constraints
fields.description: Built-in types, constrained types, and Field() constraints

steps.title: Built-in Type Support
steps.explanation: Pydantic supports all Python built-in types plus many standard library types.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Primitive Types"
        P1["str, int, float, bool"]
        P2["bytes, None"]
    end
    subgraph "Collections"
        C1["list, dict, set, tuple"]
        C2["frozenset, deque"]
    end
    subgraph "Standard Library"
        S1["datetime, date, time"]
        S2["UUID, Path, Decimal"]
        S3["Enum, Literal"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Constrained Types
steps.explanation: Pydantic provides pre-built constrained types for common validation patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "String Constraints"
        S1["constr(min_length=1, max_length=50)"]
        S2["constr(pattern=r'^[a-z]+$')"]
    end
    subgraph "Numeric Constraints"
        N1["conint(ge=0, le=100)"]
        N2["confloat(gt=0)"]
        N3["PositiveInt, NegativeFloat"]
    end
    subgraph "Collection Constraints"
        C1["conlist(str, min_length=1)"]
    end
    style N3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Field() Constraints
steps.explanation: Use Field() to add constraints inline with more flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Numeric"
        N1["Field(ge=0) # >= 0"]
        N2["Field(gt=0) # > 0"]
        N3["Field(le=100) # <= 100"]
        N4["Field(lt=100) # < 100"]
        N5["Field(multiple_of=5)"]
    end
    subgraph "String"
        S1["Field(min_length=1)"]
        S2["Field(max_length=50)"]
        S3["Field(pattern=r'regex')"]
    end
    style N1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Special Types
steps.explanation: Pydantic provides special types for common patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Email & URLs" E["EmailStr"] U["HttpUrl, AnyUrl"] end subgraph "Network" N["IPvAnyAddress"] P["Port"] end subgraph "Files" F["FilePath, DirectoryPath"] end subgraph "JSON" J["Json[MyModel]
Parse JSON string to model"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9 style J fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Union Types
steps.explanation: Use Union or | for fields that accept multiple types. Pydantic tries each in order.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Union Syntax" OLD["Union[str, int]"] NEW["str | int"] end subgraph "Validation Order" V["Tries str first, then int"] end subgraph "Optional" O["str | None
Same as Optional[str]"] end style NEW fill:#C8E6C9 style O fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Literal and Enum
steps.explanation: Restrict fields to specific values using Literal or Enum.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Literal" L["status: Literal['active', 'inactive']
Only these two values allowed"] end subgraph "Enum" E["class Status(Enum):
ACTIVE = 'active'
INACTIVE = 'inactive'

status: Status"] end style L fill:#C8E6C9 style E fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Validation Error Structure
fields.description: Understanding error location, type, message, and handling multiple errors

steps.title: ValidationError Overview
steps.explanation: When validation fails, Pydantic raises **ValidationError** containing all errors found. It doesn't stop at the first error.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB INPUT["Invalid Input"] --> VAL["Pydantic Validates"] VAL --> CHECK{"Valid?"} CHECK -->|Yes| MODEL["Model Instance"] CHECK -->|No| ERROR["ValidationError
Contains ALL errors"] ERROR --> LIST["List of error dicts"] style ERROR fill:#FFCDD2 style MODEL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Error Structure
steps.explanation: Each error is a dict with: **loc** (location), **msg** (message), **type** (error type), and **input** (the bad value).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Error Dict" LOC["loc: ('age',)
Path to the field"] MSG["msg: 'Input should be...'
Human-readable message"] TYPE["type: 'int_parsing'
Error type code"] INP["input: 'abc'
The invalid value"] end style LOC fill:#BBDEFB style MSG fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Location (loc) for Nested Errors
steps.explanation: For nested models, **loc** is a tuple showing the path to the error.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Nested Model" U["User.address.city"] end subgraph "Error Location" L["loc: ('address', 'city')
Tuple path to error"] end subgraph "List Index" LI["loc: ('items', 2, 'name')
Third item's name field"] end style L fill:#FFF9C4 style LI fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Common Error Types
steps.explanation: Error types help identify what went wrong programmatically.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Parsing Errors"
        P1["int_parsing"]
        P2["float_parsing"]
        P3["datetime_parsing"]
    end
    subgraph "Constraint Errors"
        C1["string_too_short"]
        C2["greater_than_equal"]
        C3["missing"]
    end
    subgraph "Type Errors"
        T1["string_type"]
        T2["list_type"]
        T3["model_type"]
    end
    style C3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Accessing Errors
steps.explanation: Use .errors() for list of dicts, or string representation for display.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Methods" E["e.errors()
List of error dicts"] J["e.json()
JSON string"] S["str(e)
Formatted string"] C["e.error_count()
Number of errors"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Handling Errors
steps.explanation: Catch ValidationError and handle appropriately for your use case.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Error Handling" TRY["try:
User(**data)"] CATCH["except ValidationError as e:
errors = e.errors()"] HANDLE["for err in errors:
field = err['loc'][0]
msg = err['msg']"] end TRY --> CATCH --> HANDLE subgraph "Use Cases" API["Return 422 response"] LOG["Log for debugging"] UI["Show form errors"] end style CATCH fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Validator Types
fields.description: field_validator vs model_validator, before/after modes, and when to use each

steps.title: Two Types of Validators
steps.explanation: Pydantic V2 has two validator decorators: **@field_validator** for single fields, **@model_validator** for the whole model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Validator Types" FV["@field_validator
Validates ONE field"] MV["@model_validator
Validates ENTIRE model"] end subgraph "Use Cases" FVU["Transform or validate
a single field value"] MVU["Cross-field validation
Computed fields"] end FV --> FVU MV --> MVU style FV fill:#C8E6C9 style MV fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Field Validator
steps.explanation: @field_validator runs on specific fields. It receives the field value and returns the (possibly modified) value.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Definition" D["@field_validator('name')
@classmethod
def validate_name(cls, v):
return v.strip().title()"] end subgraph "Flow" IN["Input: ' alice '"] VAL["Validator runs"] OUT["Output: 'Alice'"] end IN --> VAL --> OUT style OUT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Validator Modes: Before vs After
steps.explanation: Validators can run BEFORE type coercion (on raw input) or AFTER (on coerced value). Default is 'after'.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR INPUT["Raw Input"] --> BEFORE["mode='before'
Raw value"] BEFORE --> COERCE["Type Coercion"] COERCE --> AFTER["mode='after'
Typed value"] AFTER --> RESULT["Final Value"] style BEFORE fill:#FFF9C4 style AFTER fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Model Validator
steps.explanation: @model_validator validates the entire model. Use mode='before' for dict input, mode='after' for model instance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "mode='before'" B["Receives: dict
Before fields validated
Can modify input dict"] end subgraph "mode='after'" A["Receives: model instance
After all fields valid
Can access self"] end style B fill:#FFF9C4 style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Validation Order
steps.explanation: The complete order of validation in Pydantic V2.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    S1["1. model_validator mode='before'"]
    S2["2. field_validator mode='before' (per field)"]
    S3["3. Type coercion"]
    S4["4. Field constraints"]
    S5["5. field_validator mode='after' (per field)"]
    S6["6. model_validator mode='after'"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    style S1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style S6 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Fields
steps.explanation: One validator can handle multiple fields. Use '*' for all fields.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Multiple Specific Fields" M["@field_validator('email', 'username')
def validate_lowercase(cls, v):
return v.lower()"] end subgraph "All Fields" A["@field_validator('*')
def validate_all(cls, v):
# runs on every field"] end style M fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Custom Validators
fields.description: Writing validators with @field_validator and @model_validator

steps.title: Field Validator Anatomy
steps.explanation: A field validator is a classmethod decorated with @field_validator, specifying which field(s) to validate.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Structure"
        DEC["@field_validator('field_name')"]
        CLS["@classmethod"]
        DEF["def method(cls, value, info):"]
        RET["return processed_value"]
    end
    DEC --> CLS --> DEF --> RET
    style DEC fill:#BBDEFB
    style RET fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: ValidationInfo Object
steps.explanation: The optional **info** parameter provides context: field name, other field values, validation mode.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "ValidationInfo" FN["info.field_name
Name of current field"] DATA["info.data
Other validated fields"] MODE["info.mode
'python' or 'json'"] end USE["Access other fields:
info.data.get('other_field')"] DATA --> USE style DATA fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Raising Validation Errors
steps.explanation: Raise ValueError or AssertionError to fail validation. Pydantic converts these to ValidationError.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Raising Errors" VAL["@field_validator('age')
@classmethod
def check_age(cls, v):
if v < 0:
raise ValueError('Age must be positive')
return v"] end subgraph "Result" ERR["ValidationError:
age: Value error, Age must be positive"] end style ERR fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Model Validator Example
steps.explanation: Model validators can check relationships between fields or compute derived values.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Cross-Field Validation" V["@model_validator(mode='after')
def check_passwords(self):
if self.password != self.confirm:
raise ValueError('Passwords must match')
return self"] end style V fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Before Mode for Preprocessing
steps.explanation: Use mode='before' to preprocess raw input before Pydantic's type coercion.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Preprocessing Example" V["@field_validator('tags', mode='before')
@classmethod
def split_tags(cls, v):
if isinstance(v, str):
return v.split(',')
return v"] end subgraph "Input/Output" I["Input: 'a,b,c'"] O["Output: ['a', 'b', 'c']"] end I --> V --> O style O fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Frequently used validation patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Patterns"
        STRIP["Strip whitespace"]
        LOWER["Lowercase emails"]
        PHONE["Format phone numbers"]
        SLUG["Generate slugs"]
        DEFAULT["Set computed defaults"]
    end
    STRIP --> CODE1["return v.strip()"]
    LOWER --> CODE2["return v.lower()"]
    style STRIP fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Model Inheritance
fields.description: Extending models, overriding fields, and mixins

steps.title: Basic Inheritance
steps.explanation: Pydantic models can inherit from other models. Child models get all parent fields plus their own.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parent" P["class User(BaseModel):
name: str
email: str"] end subgraph "Child" C["class Admin(User):
role: str
permissions: list[str]"] end P --> C subgraph "Admin has" A["name, email (inherited)
role, permissions (new)"] end C --> A style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Overriding Fields
steps.explanation: Child models can override parent fields to change type, default, or constraints.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parent" P["class Base(BaseModel):
name: str
count: int = 0"] end subgraph "Child Override" C["class Child(Base):
name: str = 'default' # add default
count: int = 10 # change default"] end P --> C style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Multiple Inheritance
steps.explanation: Models can inherit from multiple parents. Fields are merged following Python's MRO.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mixins" T["class Timestamped(BaseModel):
created_at: datetime"] A["class Auditable(BaseModel):
modified_by: str"] end subgraph "Combined" C["class Document(Timestamped, Auditable):
title: str"] end T --> C A --> C style C fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Mixin Pattern
steps.explanation: Create reusable field groups as mixins. Great for common patterns like timestamps, soft delete, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Mixins Library" TS["TimestampMixin
created_at, updated_at"] SD["SoftDeleteMixin
is_deleted, deleted_at"] AU["AuditMixin
created_by, modified_by"] end subgraph "Usage" U["class User(TimestampMixin, AuditMixin):
name: str"] end TS --> U AU --> U style U fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Validators Inheritance
steps.explanation: Child models inherit validators from parents. You can override or extend them.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Parent" PV["@field_validator('email')
def validate_email(cls, v): ..."] end subgraph "Child" CV["Inherits parent validator
Can add new validators
Can override with same name"] end PV --> CV style CV fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Nested Models
fields.description: Models containing other models and recursive structures

steps.title: Basic Nesting
steps.explanation: A Pydantic model can have fields that are other Pydantic models. Nested dicts are automatically converted.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Models" A["class Address(BaseModel):
street: str
city: str"] U["class User(BaseModel):
name: str
address: Address"] end A --> U subgraph "Input" I["User(name='Bob',
address={'street': '123 Main', 'city': 'NYC'})"] end subgraph "Result" R["user.address.city == 'NYC'"] end I --> R style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Lists of Models
steps.explanation: Use list[Model] for arrays of nested models. Each dict in the list is validated as the model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Definition" D["class Order(BaseModel):
items: list[OrderItem]"] end subgraph "Input" I["Order(items=[
{'name': 'Widget', 'qty': 2},
{'name': 'Gadget', 'qty': 1}
])"] end subgraph "Access" A["order.items[0].name"] end D --> I --> A style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Optional Nested Models
steps.explanation: Nested models can be optional. Use Model | None with a default of None.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Optional Nested" D["class User(BaseModel):
name: str
address: Address | None = None"] end subgraph "Valid Inputs" V1["User(name='Alice')
address is None"] V2["User(name='Bob', address={...})
address is Address"] end style V1 fill:#C8E6C9 style V2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Recursive Models
steps.explanation: Models can reference themselves for tree/graph structures. Use quotes for forward references.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Self-Reference" D["class TreeNode(BaseModel):
value: int
children: list['TreeNode'] = []"] end subgraph "Usage" U["node = TreeNode(
value=1,
children=[
TreeNode(value=2),
TreeNode(value=3)
]
)"] end style D fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Validation Propagates
steps.explanation: Validation errors in nested models show the full path in the error location.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Invalid Input" I["User(name='X', address={'city': 123})"] end subgraph "Error" E["loc: ('address', 'city')
msg: Input should be a valid string"] end I --> E style E fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Generics and TypeVar
fields.description: Creating generic models with type parameters

steps.title: Why Generic Models?
steps.explanation: Generic models let you create reusable model patterns with type parameters. Like list[str] vs list[int].
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Generics" W1["class StringResponse(BaseModel):
data: str"] W2["class IntResponse(BaseModel):
data: int"] W3["Duplicate code!"] end subgraph "With Generics" G["class Response(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
data: T"] U["Response[str], Response[int]"] end style W3 fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Creating a Generic Model
steps.explanation: Define a TypeVar, inherit from Generic[T], and use T as a type hint.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Definition" TV["T = TypeVar('T')"] CLS["class Box(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
content: T
label: str"] end subgraph "Usage" U1["Box[str](content='hello', label='greeting')"] U2["Box[int](content=42, label='answer')"] end TV --> CLS CLS --> U1 CLS --> U2 style U1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Type Parameters
steps.explanation: Use multiple TypeVars for models with several generic fields.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Definition" D["K = TypeVar('K')
V = TypeVar('V')

class Pair(BaseModel, Generic[K, V]):
key: K
value: V"] end subgraph "Usage" U["Pair[str, int](key='age', value=25)"] end style U fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Generic with Constraints
steps.explanation: TypeVar can be constrained to specific types for bounded generics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bounded TypeVar" B["Number = TypeVar('Number', int, float)

class Stats(BaseModel, Generic[Number]):
values: list[Number]
avg: Number"] end subgraph "Allowed" A["Stats[int], Stats[float]"] end subgraph "Not Allowed" N["Stats[str] - Error!"] end style A fill:#C8E6C9 style N fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Common Use Cases
steps.explanation: Generic models are great for API responses, wrappers, and reusable patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "API Response" R["class ApiResponse(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
data: T
success: bool
errors: list[str] = []"] end subgraph "Pagination" P["class Page(BaseModel, Generic[T]):
items: list[T]
total: int
page: int"] end style R fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Serialization Modes
fields.description: model_dump() vs model_dump_json() and serialization options

steps.title: Two Serialization Methods
steps.explanation: Pydantic models have two main serialization methods: **model_dump()** returns a dict, **model_dump_json()** returns a JSON string.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Model" M["user = User(name='Alice', age=30)"] end subgraph "Serialization" D["model_dump()
Returns: dict"] J["model_dump_json()
Returns: str (JSON)"] end M --> D M --> J subgraph "Results" RD["{'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}"] RJ['{"name":"Alice","age":30}'] end D --> RD J --> RJ style RD fill:#C8E6C9 style RJ fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Include/Exclude Fields
steps.explanation: Control which fields appear in output using include, exclude, or exclude_unset parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Options" INC["include={'name', 'email'}
Only these fields"] EXC["exclude={'password'}
All except these"] UNS["exclude_unset=True
Skip fields not explicitly set"] DEF["exclude_defaults=True
Skip fields with default values"] end style INC fill:#C8E6C9 style EXC fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: By Alias
steps.explanation: Use by_alias=True to serialize using field aliases instead of field names.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Model" M["class User(BaseModel):
user_name: str = Field(alias='userName')"] end subgraph "Without Alias" W["model_dump()
{'user_name': 'alice'}"] end subgraph "With Alias" A["model_dump(by_alias=True)
{'userName': 'alice'}"] end M --> W M --> A style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Mode: Python vs JSON
steps.explanation: mode='python' keeps Python types, mode='json' converts to JSON-compatible types.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Python Mode (default)" P["datetime stays datetime
UUID stays UUID
Enum stays Enum"] end subgraph "JSON Mode" J["datetime → ISO string
UUID → string
Enum → value"] end subgraph "Usage" U["model_dump(mode='json')"] end style P fill:#BBDEFB style J fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Round-Trip Safety
steps.explanation: model_dump(mode='json') produces output that can round-trip through JSON safely.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR M["Model"] --> D["model_dump
mode='json'"] D --> JSON["json.dumps()"] JSON --> PARSE["json.loads()"] PARSE --> NEW["Model(**data)"] style NEW fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Custom Serializers
steps.explanation: Use @field_serializer to customize how specific fields are serialized.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Custom Serializer" S["@field_serializer('price')
def serialize_price(self, v):
return f'${v:.2f}'"] end subgraph "Result" R["price=19.99 → '$19.99'"] end S --> R style R fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Model Configuration
fields.description: ConfigDict options for customizing model behavior

steps.title: Model Config
steps.explanation: Use **model_config = ConfigDict(...)** to customize model behavior. This replaces the V1 inner Config class.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Pydantic V2 Syntax" V2["class User(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(
str_strip_whitespace=True
)"] end subgraph "V1 Syntax (deprecated)" V1["class User(BaseModel):
class Config:
anystr_strip_whitespace = True"] end style V2 fill:#C8E6C9 style V1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: String Handling Options
steps.explanation: Options for automatic string processing.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "String Options" STRIP["str_strip_whitespace=True
' hello ' → 'hello'"] LOWER["str_to_lower=True
'HELLO' → 'hello'"] UPPER["str_to_upper=True
'hello' → 'HELLO'"] MIN["str_min_length=1
'' raises error"] MAX["str_max_length=100
Limit string length"] end style STRIP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Strict Mode
steps.explanation: Enable strict mode to disable type coercion and require exact types.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Normal Mode" N["age: int = '25'
Works: coerced to 25"] end subgraph "Strict Mode" S["model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True)

age: int = '25'
Error: input should be int"] end style N fill:#FFF9C4 style S fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Extra Fields Handling
steps.explanation: Control behavior when extra fields are passed to the model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Extra Options" IGNORE["extra='ignore'
Silently drop extra fields
(default)"] FORBID["extra='forbid'
Raise error on extra fields"] ALLOW["extra='allow'
Store extra fields in __pydantic_extra__"] end style FORBID fill:#FFCDD2 style ALLOW fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Validation Options
steps.explanation: Control validation behavior at the model level.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Validation Config" VAL["validate_default=True
Validate default values"] ASSIGN["validate_assignment=True
Validate on attribute set"] ARB["arbitrary_types_allowed=True
Allow non-Pydantic types"] end style ASSIGN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Config Patterns
steps.explanation: Frequently used configuration patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Frozen Model" F["frozen=True
Immutable model (like dataclass)"] end subgraph "ORM Mode" O["from_attributes=True
Read from object attributes
(SQLAlchemy models)"] end subgraph "Populate by Name" P["populate_by_name=True
Accept both field name and alias"] end style O fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: JSON Schema Generation
fields.description: Generating JSON Schema from Pydantic models

steps.title: What is JSON Schema?
steps.explanation: JSON Schema is a standard for describing JSON data structures. Pydantic can automatically generate it from your models.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Pydantic Model" M["class User(BaseModel):
name: str
age: int"] end subgraph "JSON Schema" S["{'type': 'object',
'properties': {
'name': {'type': 'string'},
'age': {'type': 'integer'}}}"] end M --> S style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Generating Schema
steps.explanation: Use model_json_schema() to generate JSON Schema from your model.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Method" M["schema = User.model_json_schema()"] end subgraph "Output" O["{'properties': {...},
'required': [...],
'title': 'User',
'type': 'object'}"] end M --> O style O fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Adding Metadata
steps.explanation: Use Field() to add descriptions, examples, and other schema metadata.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Field Metadata" F["name: str = Field(
description='User full name',
examples=['Alice Smith'],
min_length=1,
max_length=100
)"] end subgraph "In Schema" S["'name': {
'type': 'string',
'description': 'User full name',
'examples': ['Alice Smith'],
'minLength': 1,
'maxLength': 100
}"] end F --> S style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Model Docstrings
steps.explanation: Class and field docstrings become schema descriptions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Model" M["class User(BaseModel):
'''A user in the system.'''
name: str"] end subgraph "Schema" S["'title': 'User',
'description': 'A user in the system.'"] end M --> S style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Use Cases
steps.explanation: JSON Schema enables API documentation, form generation, and validation across systems.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use Cases" API["OpenAPI/Swagger
API documentation"] FORM["Form Generation
Auto-build forms from schema"] VAL["Cross-Language Validation
Validate in JS, Go, etc."] DOC["Documentation
Auto-generate docs"] end style API fill:#C8E6C9 style FORM fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Schema Modes
steps.explanation: Control schema generation with mode parameter.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Schema Modes" V["mode='validation'
Schema for input validation
(default)"] S["mode='serialization'
Schema for output/response"] end subgraph "Difference" D["Validation includes required fields
Serialization shows what's returned"] end style V fill:#FFF9C4 style S fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Discriminated Unions
fields.description: Using tagged unions for polymorphic models

steps.title: The Problem with Unions
steps.explanation: Regular unions try each type until one works. This is slow and can match the wrong type.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Regular Union"
        U["Action = Cat | Dog | Bird"]
    end
    subgraph "Validation Process"
        T1["Try Cat..."]
        T2["Try Dog..."]
        T3["Try Bird..."]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3
    end
    subgraph "Problems"
        P1["Slow: tries each type"]
        P2["Wrong match possible"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style P2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Discriminated Union Solution
steps.explanation: A discriminated union uses a 'tag' field to instantly identify the correct type.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Tagged Models" C["class Cat(BaseModel):
pet_type: Literal['cat']
meow: str"] D["class Dog(BaseModel):
pet_type: Literal['dog']
bark: str"] end subgraph "Union with Discriminator" U["Pet = Annotated[
Cat | Dog,
Field(discriminator='pet_type')
]"] end C --> U D --> U style U fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: How It Works
steps.explanation: Pydantic checks the discriminator field first, then validates only the matching type.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    INPUT["{'pet_type': 'dog', 'bark': 'woof'}"]
    CHECK["Check 'pet_type'"]
    MATCH["'dog' → use Dog model"]
    VALID["Validate as Dog"]
    INPUT --> CHECK --> MATCH --> VALID
    style MATCH fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Benefits
steps.explanation: Discriminated unions are faster and produce clearer error messages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Benefits" FAST["O(1) type lookup
No trial-and-error"] ERR["Clear errors
'dog' not valid for pet_type"] DOC["Better OpenAPI schema
Shows discriminator"] end style FAST fill:#C8E6C9 style ERR fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Discriminated unions are great for event systems, API responses, and polymorphic data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Events" E["EventCreate, EventUpdate, EventDelete
type: Literal['create' | 'update' | 'delete']"] end subgraph "API Responses" A["SuccessResponse, ErrorResponse
status: Literal['success' | 'error']"] end subgraph "Shapes" S["Circle, Rectangle, Triangle
shape: Literal['circle' | 'rect' | 'tri']"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Nested Discriminators
steps.explanation: You can nest discriminated unions for complex hierarchies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Two-Level"
        L1["Vehicle (type: 'car' | 'boat')"]
        L2A["Car (fuel: 'gas' | 'electric')"]
        L2B["Boat (style: 'sail' | 'motor')"]
    end
    L1 --> L2A
    L1 --> L2B
    style L1 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: V1 to V2 Migration
fields.description: Key changes and migration guide from Pydantic V1 to V2

steps.title: Why Pydantic V2?
steps.explanation: Pydantic V2 was rewritten with a Rust core, making it 5-50x faster with better validation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "V2 Benefits" FAST["5-50x Faster
Rust core (pydantic-core)"] STRICT["Strict Mode
Better type safety"] ERR["Better Errors
More helpful messages"] FEAT["New Features
Discriminated unions, etc."] end style FAST fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Method Renames
steps.explanation: Many methods were renamed with 'model_' prefix for consistency.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "V1 → V2"
        A1[".dict()"] --> B1[".model_dump()"]
        A2[".json()"] --> B2[".model_dump_json()"]
        A3[".parse_obj()"] --> B3[".model_validate()"]
        A4[".parse_raw()"] --> B4[".model_validate_json()"]
        A5[".schema()"] --> B5[".model_json_schema()"]
        A6[".copy()"] --> B6[".model_copy()"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Validator Changes
steps.explanation: @validator → @field_validator, @root_validator → @model_validator.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "V1"
        V1A["@validator('name')"]
        V1B["@root_validator"]
    end
    subgraph "V2"
        V2A["@field_validator('name')"]
        V2B["@model_validator(mode='after')"]
    end
    V1A --> V2A
    V1B --> V2B
    style V2A fill:#C8E6C9
    style V2B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Config Changes
steps.explanation: Inner Config class replaced with model_config = ConfigDict(...).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "V1" V1["class Model(BaseModel):
class Config:
extra = 'forbid'"] end subgraph "V2" V2["class Model(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(
extra='forbid'
)"] end V1 --> V2 style V2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Field Changes
steps.explanation: Some Field() parameters were renamed or changed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "V1 → V2"
        A1["regex=..."] --> B1["pattern=..."]
        A2["min_items=..."] --> B2["min_length=..."]
        A3["max_items=..."] --> B3["max_length=..."]
        A4["const=True"] --> B4["Literal[value]"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Type Changes
steps.explanation: Some types were deprecated or changed location.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Deprecated" D1["Optional[X] still works"] D2["Union[X, None] still works"] end subgraph "Preferred in V2" P1["X | None"] P2["list[X] instead of List[X]"] end subgraph "Removed" R["pydantic.Json
Use json_loads in validator"] end style P1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Migration Tool
steps.explanation: Use bump-pydantic to automatically update your code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Migration Steps"
        S1["1. pip install bump-pydantic"]
        S2["2. bump-pydantic your_package/"]
        S3["3. Review changes"]
        S4["4. Run tests"]
        S5["5. Fix any remaining issues"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: JOIN Algorithms
fields.description: Nested Loop, Hash Join, Merge Join - how databases execute joins

steps.title: Three Join Algorithms
steps.explanation: Database engines use three main algorithms to execute JOINs. The query optimizer picks the best one based on table sizes, indexes, and statistics.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "JOIN Algorithms" NL["Nested Loop Join
O(n × m)"] HJ["Hash Join
O(n + m)"] MJ["Merge Join
O(n log n + m log m)"] end subgraph "Best For" NL_USE["Small tables
Indexed inner table"] HJ_USE["Large unsorted tables
Equality joins"] MJ_USE["Pre-sorted data
Range joins"] end NL --> NL_USE HJ --> HJ_USE MJ --> MJ_USE style NL fill:#FFCDD2 style HJ fill:#C8E6C9 style MJ fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Nested Loop Join
steps.explanation: For each row in the outer table, scan the inner table for matches. Simple but can be slow for large tables.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Outer Table (Employees)" E1["Alice"] E2["Bob"] E3["Carol"] end subgraph "Inner Table (Departments)" D1["Sales"] D2["Engineering"] D3["Marketing"] end subgraph "Process" P["For each employee:
Scan ALL departments
Find matching dept_id"] end E1 --> D1 E1 --> D2 E1 --> D3 E2 --> D1 E2 --> D2 E2 --> D3 style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Index Nested Loop
steps.explanation: With an index on the join column, we can look up matches directly instead of scanning.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Outer" E["Employee
dept_id=5"] end subgraph "Index Lookup" I["B-Tree Index
on dept_id"] end subgraph "Direct Access" D["Department 5
Engineering"] end E --> I --> D style I fill:#C8E6C9 style D fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Hash Join
steps.explanation: Build a hash table from the smaller table, then probe it with each row from the larger table.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Build Phase" S["Smaller Table"] H["Hash Table
{key: row}"] S --> |"Hash each row"| H end subgraph "Probe Phase" L["Larger Table"] P["For each row:
Hash key → lookup"] R["Matched Rows"] L --> P --> H P --> R end style H fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Merge Join
steps.explanation: Sort both tables by join key, then merge them like a zipper. Very efficient for pre-sorted data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sorted Table A" A1["1: Alice"] A2["2: Bob"] A3["3: Carol"] end subgraph "Sorted Table B" B1["1: Sales"] B2["2: Eng"] B3["3: Mkt"] end subgraph "Merge" M["Walk both in order
Match equal keys
Advance pointers"] end A1 --> M B1 --> M style M fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: When Each Is Used
steps.explanation: The optimizer chooses based on table sizes, available indexes, and sort order.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Nested Loop" NL["✓ Small outer table
✓ Index on inner table
✓ Few rows expected"] end subgraph "Hash Join" HJ["✓ Large tables
✓ No useful indexes
✓ Equality join only"] end subgraph "Merge Join" MJ["✓ Already sorted data
✓ Index scan possible
✓ Range conditions"] end style NL fill:#FFCDD2 style HJ fill:#C8E6C9 style MJ fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Index Structures
fields.description: B-Tree vs Hash indexes and how lookups work

steps.title: Why Indexes?
steps.explanation: Without an index, finding a row requires scanning every row (full table scan). Indexes provide fast lookups.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Without Index" TS["Table Scan
Check every row
O(n)"] end subgraph "With Index" IL["Index Lookup
Jump to row
O(log n)"] end Q["SELECT * FROM users
WHERE id = 500"] --> TS Q --> IL style TS fill:#FFCDD2 style IL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: B-Tree Structure
steps.explanation: B-Tree is the default index type. It's a balanced tree where each node contains sorted keys and pointers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "B-Tree Index" R["Root
[50]"] L1["[20, 35]"] L2["[65, 80]"] LL1["[10,15]→rows"] LL2["[25,30]→rows"] LL3["[40,45]→rows"] LL4["[55,60]→rows"] LL5["[70,75]→rows"] LL6["[85,90]→rows"] end R --> L1 R --> L2 L1 --> LL1 L1 --> LL2 L1 --> LL3 L2 --> LL4 L2 --> LL5 L2 --> LL6 style R fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: B-Tree Lookup
steps.explanation: Finding key=42: Start at root, compare, go left/right until leaf. O(log n) comparisons.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Find key=42" S1["Root: 42 < 50
Go LEFT"] S2["Node: 35 < 42
Go RIGHT"] S3["Leaf: [40,45]
Found between!"] S4["Follow pointer
to row"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 style S4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: B-Tree Supports
steps.explanation: B-Tree indexes support equality, range queries, and sorting because keys are ordered.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Supported Operations" EQ["= (equality)
WHERE id = 5"] RNG["<, >, BETWEEN
WHERE age > 21"] ORD["ORDER BY
Already sorted!"] PRE["LIKE 'abc%'
Prefix match"] end subgraph "NOT Supported" SUF["LIKE '%abc'
Suffix match"] FN["WHERE UPPER(name)=...
Function on column"] end style EQ fill:#C8E6C9 style RNG fill:#C8E6C9 style SUF fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Hash Index
steps.explanation: Hash indexes use a hash function. O(1) lookup but only support equality.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Hash Index" K["Key: 'alice'"] H["hash('alice')
= bucket 7"] B["Bucket 7
→ row pointer"] end K --> H --> B subgraph "Limitations" L["✗ No range queries
✗ No ORDER BY
✗ No prefix LIKE"] end style B fill:#C8E6C9 style L fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Choosing Index Type
steps.explanation: B-Tree is the default and most versatile. Hash is faster for pure equality lookups.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use B-Tree" BT["✓ Range queries needed
✓ ORDER BY needed
✓ General purpose
✓ Primary keys"] end subgraph "Use Hash" HI["✓ Only equality checks
✓ Very high cardinality
✓ Memory tables
✓ Join keys"] end style BT fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Clustered vs Non-Clustered Indexes
fields.description: How data is physically stored with different index types

steps.title: The Key Difference
steps.explanation: A **clustered index** determines the physical order of data. **Non-clustered** indexes are separate structures pointing to data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Clustered Index" CI["Data IS the index
Rows stored in index order
Only ONE per table"] end subgraph "Non-Clustered Index" NCI["Separate from data
Contains pointers to rows
Many per table"] end style CI fill:#C8E6C9 style NCI fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Clustered Index Structure
steps.explanation: The table data is stored sorted by the clustered index key. The leaf level IS the data.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Clustered Index (by ID)"
        R["Root"]
        L["Leaf = Actual Rows"]
        R1["ID:1 Name:Alice Dept:5"]
        R2["ID:2 Name:Bob Dept:3"]
        R3["ID:3 Name:Carol Dept:5"]
    end
    R --> L
    L --> R1
    L --> R2
    L --> R3
    style L fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Non-Clustered Index Structure
steps.explanation: Non-clustered index stores keys + pointers. The pointer leads to the actual row (or clustered index key).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Non-Clustered Index (by Name)" I["Index Leaf"] I1["Alice → ID:1"] I2["Bob → ID:2"] I3["Carol → ID:3"] end subgraph "Table Data" D["Actual Rows
(in clustered order)"] end I --> I1 --> D I --> I2 --> D I --> I3 --> D style I fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Lookup Comparison
steps.explanation: Clustered index: one lookup. Non-clustered: index lookup + bookmark lookup to get full row.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Clustered"
        C1["Index Seek"]
        C2["Row Found!"]
        C1 --> C2
    end
    subgraph "Non-Clustered"
        N1["Index Seek"]
        N2["Get Row Pointer"]
        N3["Bookmark Lookup"]
        N4["Row Found"]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4
    end
    style C2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N3 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Covering Index
steps.explanation: A **covering index** includes all columns needed by a query. No bookmark lookup required!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT name, email
FROM users
WHERE name = 'Alice'"] end subgraph "Covering Index" CI["CREATE INDEX ix_name_email
ON users(name) INCLUDE(email)"] R["Index has name AND email
No table lookup needed!"] end Q --> CI --> R style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Choosing Clustered Key
steps.explanation: Choose carefully - you only get one! Usually the primary key.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Good Clustered Keys" G1["Auto-increment ID
Sequential, narrow"] G2["Natural key if unique
and frequently queried"] end subgraph "Bad Clustered Keys" B1["UUID/GUID
Random, causes fragmentation"] B2["Wide composite keys
Copied to all non-clustered indexes"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Index Scan vs Index Seek
fields.description: Understanding when each is used and performance implications

steps.title: Scan vs Seek
steps.explanation: **Index Seek**: Jump directly to matching rows using the index tree. **Index Scan**: Read all/most index entries sequentially.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Index Seek" S["Navigate tree
Jump to exact location
Read matching rows only"] end subgraph "Index Scan" SC["Read index pages
sequentially
Filter as you go"] end style S fill:#C8E6C9 style SC fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: When Seek Is Used
steps.explanation: Seek happens when the query has a predicate on the leading column(s) of the index.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Index on (last_name, first_name)" IDX["B-Tree Index"] end subgraph "Seek" Q1["WHERE last_name = 'Smith'
✓ Uses leading column"] Q2["WHERE last_name = 'Smith'
AND first_name = 'John'
✓ Uses both columns"] end subgraph "Cannot Seek" Q3["WHERE first_name = 'John'
✗ Skips leading column"] end style Q1 fill:#C8E6C9 style Q3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: When Scan Is Used
steps.explanation: Scan happens when there's no usable predicate, or when reading most of the data is faster.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Causes Index Scan" C1["No WHERE clause"] C2["Predicate on non-leading column"] C3["Function on indexed column
WHERE UPPER(name) = ..."] C4["Low selectivity
WHERE active = true
(90% of rows match)"] end style C3 fill:#FFCDD2 style C4 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Table Scan (Worst)
steps.explanation: Table scan reads every row in the table. No index is used at all.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Table Scan" T["Read every page
Check every row
Most expensive"] end subgraph "Causes" C1["No suitable index exists"] C2["Optimizer thinks scan
is faster than seek"] C3["Very small table"] end C1 --> T C2 --> T style T fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Reading Execution Plans
steps.explanation: EXPLAIN shows which access method is used. Look for Seek vs Scan.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Good Signs"
        G1["Index Seek"]
        G2["Key Lookup (small)"]
        G3["Index Only Scan"]
    end
    subgraph "Warning Signs"
        W1["Table Scan"]
        W2["Index Scan (large table)"]
        W3["Key Lookup (many rows)"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Query Execution Plan
fields.description: Reading EXPLAIN output and identifying bottlenecks

steps.title: What Is an Execution Plan?
steps.explanation: The execution plan shows HOW the database will execute your query - which indexes, join methods, and order of operations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Your Query" Q["SELECT * FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id
WHERE c.country = 'USA'"] end subgraph "Execution Plan" P["1. Scan customers (filter USA)
2. Hash Join with orders
3. Return results"] end Q --> |"EXPLAIN"| P style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Reading the Plan
steps.explanation: Plans are trees. Read from innermost (first executed) to outermost (final result).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Execution Order"
        S1["1. Index Scan: customers"]
        S2["2. Index Scan: orders"]
        S3["3. Hash Join"]
        S4["4. Sort"]
        S5["5. Return"]
    end
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4 --> S5
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Key Metrics
steps.explanation: Focus on these numbers to identify slow operations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Important Metrics" ROWS["Rows: Estimated vs Actual
Large difference = bad stats"] COST["Cost: Relative expense
Higher = slower"] TIME["Time: Actual execution
(with ANALYZE)"] LOOPS["Loops: Times executed
Nested loop multiplier"] end style ROWS fill:#FFF9C4 style COST fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Common Operations
steps.explanation: Learn to recognize these operations in execution plans.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Table Access"
        TS["Seq Scan: Full table read"]
        IS["Index Scan: Use index + fetch row"]
        IO["Index Only Scan: Index has all data"]
    end
    subgraph "Joins"
        NL["Nested Loop: Small tables/indexed"]
        HJ["Hash Join: Large tables"]
        MJ["Merge Join: Sorted data"]
    end
    subgraph "Other"
        SO["Sort: ORDER BY"]
        AG["Aggregate: GROUP BY"]
    end
    style IO fill:#C8E6C9
    style TS fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Red Flags
steps.explanation: These patterns often indicate performance problems.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Warning Signs"
        W1["Seq Scan on large table"]
        W2["Nested Loop with many rows"]
        W3["Sort with high cost"]
        W4["Rows estimate way off"]
        W5["Multiple Key Lookups"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Add index"]
        S2["Rewrite query"]
        S3["Add covering index"]
        S4["Update statistics"]
    end
    W1 --> S1
    W3 --> S3
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style W2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: EXPLAIN Variants
steps.explanation: Different databases have different EXPLAIN syntax and options.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "PostgreSQL" PG["EXPLAIN ANALYZE
Shows actual times"] end subgraph "MySQL" MY["EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON
Detailed output"] end subgraph "SQL Server" SS["SET SHOWPLAN_ALL ON
or graphical plan"] end style PG fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Subquery vs JOIN vs CTE
fields.description: Performance tradeoffs and when to use each approach

steps.title: Three Approaches
steps.explanation: You can often solve the same problem with a subquery, JOIN, or CTE. Each has tradeoffs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Same Result, Different Approaches" SQ["Subquery
SELECT ... WHERE x IN (SELECT ...)"] JN["JOIN
SELECT ... FROM a JOIN b ON ..."] CT["CTE
WITH temp AS (...) SELECT ..."] end style SQ fill:#BBDEFB style JN fill:#C8E6C9 style CT fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Subquery Types
steps.explanation: Subqueries can be scalar (one value), column (one column), or table (full result set).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scalar Subquery" SC["SELECT name,
(SELECT MAX(price) FROM products)
FROM categories"] end subgraph "IN Subquery" IN["SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id IN
(SELECT id FROM vip_customers)"] end subgraph "Correlated" CO["SELECT * FROM orders o
WHERE price >
(SELECT AVG(price) FROM orders
WHERE category = o.category)"] end style CO fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Correlated Subquery Problem
steps.explanation: Correlated subqueries run once PER ROW of outer query. Can be very slow!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Correlated Subquery" O["Outer: 1000 rows"] S["Subquery runs
1000 times!"] O --> S end subgraph "JOIN Alternative" J["Join once
Much faster"] end style S fill:#FFCDD2 style J fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: JOIN Advantages
steps.explanation: JOINs are usually the most efficient and give the optimizer most flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "JOIN Benefits" B1["Optimizer can choose
best join algorithm"] B2["Can use indexes
on both tables"] B3["Single pass through data"] B4["Easy to read
explicit relationships"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: CTE Benefits
steps.explanation: CTEs (Common Table Expressions) improve readability and allow reuse within a query.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "CTE Structure" W["WITH sales_summary AS (
SELECT region, SUM(amount)
FROM sales GROUP BY region
)"] Q["SELECT * FROM sales_summary
WHERE sum > 10000"] W --> Q end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Readable: Named subqueries"] B2["Reusable: Reference multiple times"] B3["Recursive: Tree/graph queries"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Guidelines for choosing the right approach.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use JOIN" J["✓ Combining related tables
✓ Need columns from both
✓ Performance critical"] end subgraph "Use Subquery" S["✓ EXISTS checks
✓ Simple IN lists
✓ Scalar values in SELECT"] end subgraph "Use CTE" C["✓ Complex multi-step logic
✓ Need to reference result twice
✓ Recursive queries"] end style J fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Window Functions
fields.description: OVER, PARTITION BY, ROW_NUMBER, LAG/LEAD explained

steps.title: What Are Window Functions?
steps.explanation: Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row - without collapsing rows like GROUP BY.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "GROUP BY" G["Collapses rows
One row per group
Loses detail"] end subgraph "Window Function" W["Keeps all rows
Adds computed column
Preserves detail"] end style G fill:#FFF9C4 style W fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: OVER Clause Anatomy
steps.explanation: The OVER clause defines the window: which rows to consider and in what order.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Window Function Syntax"
        F["function() OVER ("]
        P["PARTITION BY col  -- groups"]
        O["ORDER BY col      -- sort"]
        FR["ROWS BETWEEN ... -- frame"]
        C[")"]
    end
    F --> P --> O --> FR --> C
    style P fill:#BBDEFB
    style O fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: PARTITION BY
steps.explanation: PARTITION BY divides rows into groups. The function resets for each partition.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Data"
        D1["Dept A: Alice 50k"]
        D2["Dept A: Bob 60k"]
        D3["Dept B: Carol 55k"]
        D4["Dept B: Dave 65k"]
    end
    subgraph "SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept)"
        R1["Alice: 110k (A total)"]
        R2["Bob: 110k (A total)"]
        R3["Carol: 120k (B total)"]
        R4["Dave: 120k (B total)"]
    end
    D1 --> R1
    D2 --> R2
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Ranking Functions
steps.explanation: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK assign numbers based on order.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Scores: 100, 100, 90, 80" RN["ROW_NUMBER:
1, 2, 3, 4"] RK["RANK:
1, 1, 3, 4"] DR["DENSE_RANK:
1, 1, 2, 3"] end subgraph "Difference" D["ROW_NUMBER: Always unique
RANK: Gaps after ties
DENSE_RANK: No gaps"] end style RN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: LAG and LEAD
steps.explanation: Access values from previous (LAG) or next (LEAD) rows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sales by Month" M1["Jan: 100"] M2["Feb: 120"] M3["Mar: 110"] end subgraph "With LAG(sales, 1)" L1["Jan: NULL (no prev)"] L2["Feb: 100 (Jan value)"] L3["Mar: 120 (Feb value)"] end subgraph "Calculate Change" C["sales - LAG(sales, 1)
Feb: 120-100 = +20"] end style C fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Practical uses of window functions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Running Total" RT["SUM(amount) OVER
(ORDER BY date)"] end subgraph "Top N per Group" TN["ROW_NUMBER() OVER
(PARTITION BY category
ORDER BY sales DESC)
WHERE rn <= 3"] end subgraph "Moving Average" MA["AVG(value) OVER
(ORDER BY date
ROWS 6 PRECEDING)"] end style RT fill:#C8E6C9 style TN fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Transaction Isolation Levels
fields.description: Read Uncommitted to Serializable, dirty reads, phantom reads

steps.title: Why Isolation Levels?
steps.explanation: When multiple transactions run concurrently, isolation levels control what they can see of each other's changes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Concurrent Transactions" T1["T1: UPDATE balance"] T2["T2: SELECT balance"] end subgraph "Question" Q["Does T2 see T1's change
before T1 commits?"] end T1 --> Q T2 --> Q style Q fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Four Isolation Levels
steps.explanation: SQL defines four standard isolation levels, from least to most strict.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Isolation Levels" RU["READ UNCOMMITTED
Least strict"] RC["READ COMMITTED
Default in most DBs"] RR["REPEATABLE READ
Stricter"] SE["SERIALIZABLE
Most strict"] end RU --> RC --> RR --> SE style RU fill:#FFCDD2 style SE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Dirty Read
steps.explanation: Reading uncommitted changes from another transaction. Only possible at READ UNCOMMITTED.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "T1" A1["UPDATE bal=50"] A2["ROLLBACK"] end subgraph "T2 (Dirty Read)" B1["SELECT bal
Returns 50"] B2["Uses invalid data!"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 style B2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Non-Repeatable Read
steps.explanation: Reading the same row twice gives different values because another transaction modified it.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "T1"
        A1["SELECT bal → 100"]
        A2["SELECT bal → 150"]
    end
    subgraph "T2"
        B1["UPDATE bal=150"]
        B2["COMMIT"]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> A2
    style A2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Phantom Read
steps.explanation: A query returns different rows on re-execution because another transaction inserted/deleted rows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "T1" A1["SELECT COUNT(*)
WHERE age>20 → 5"] A2["SELECT COUNT(*)
WHERE age>20 → 6"] end subgraph "T2" B1["INSERT new row
age=25"] B2["COMMIT"] end A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> A2 style A2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Isolation Level Comparison
steps.explanation: Which anomalies each level prevents.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Anomalies Prevented"
        H["Level | Dirty | Non-Rep | Phantom"]
        RU["READ UNCOMMITTED | ✗ | ✗ | ✗"]
        RC["READ COMMITTED | ✓ | ✗ | ✗"]
        RR["REPEATABLE READ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗"]
        SE["SERIALIZABLE | ✓ | ✓ | ✓"]
    end
    style SE fill:#C8E6C9
    style RU fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Tradeoffs
steps.explanation: Higher isolation = more correctness but less concurrency and performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Low Isolation" L["Fast
High concurrency
May see anomalies"] end subgraph "High Isolation" H["Slower
More blocking
Guaranteed consistency"] end L --> |"Tradeoff"| H style L fill:#C8E6C9 style H fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: ACID Properties
fields.description: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability explained

steps.title: What Is ACID?
steps.explanation: ACID is a set of properties that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "ACID"
        A["A - Atomicity"]
        C["C - Consistency"]
        I["I - Isolation"]
        D["D - Durability"]
    end
    style A fill:#FFCDD2
    style C fill:#FFF9C4
    style I fill:#BBDEFB
    style D fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Atomicity
steps.explanation: All operations in a transaction succeed, or none do. No partial updates.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Bank Transfer" S1["1. Debit $100 from A"] S2["2. Credit $100 to B"] end subgraph "Atomic" OK["Both succeed → COMMIT"] FAIL["Any fails → ROLLBACK
A and B unchanged"] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> OK S2 --> FAIL style OK fill:#C8E6C9 style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Consistency
steps.explanation: A transaction brings the database from one valid state to another. All rules (constraints) are satisfied.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Valid State 1" V1["Total: $1000
A: $600, B: $400"] end subgraph "Transaction" T["Transfer $100
A→B"] end subgraph "Valid State 2" V2["Total: $1000
A: $500, B: $500"] end V1 --> T --> V2 style V1 fill:#C8E6C9 style V2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Isolation
steps.explanation: Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other. Each sees a consistent view.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Isolation" W["T1 and T2 interleave
Unpredictable results"] end subgraph "With Isolation" I["T1 and T2 appear
to run sequentially
Predictable results"] end style W fill:#FFCDD2 style I fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Durability
steps.explanation: Once a transaction commits, its changes are permanent - even after power failure or crash.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Commit" C["COMMIT successful
Data written to disk"] end subgraph "After Crash" R["Database restarts
Committed data intact
Uncommitted rolled back"] end C --> |"Power failure"| R style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: How Databases Achieve ACID
steps.explanation: Mechanisms that implement ACID guarantees.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Atomicity" AT["Transaction log
Undo/redo operations"] end subgraph "Consistency" CO["Constraints
Triggers, checks"] end subgraph "Isolation" IS["Locks, MVCC
Isolation levels"] end subgraph "Durability" DU["Write-ahead log
Checkpoints"] end style AT fill:#FFCDD2 style IS fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Normalization Forms
