fields.description: Comparison of Python package managers and their use cases

steps.title: Package Manager Overview
steps.explanation: Three main Python package managers serve different needs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "pip" PIP["Python Package Installer
Default, PyPI-focused"] end subgraph "conda" CONDA["Cross-language Manager
Data science focused"] end subgraph "poetry" POETRY["Modern Python Tool
Dependency management + packaging"] end style PIP fill:#BBDEFB style CONDA fill:#C8E6C9 style POETRY fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: pip Characteristics
steps.explanation: pip is Python's default package installer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "pip Pros"
        P1["Default with Python"]
        P2["Huge PyPI ecosystem"]
        P3["Simple and fast"]
        P4["requirements.txt standard"]
    end
    subgraph "pip Cons"
        C1["Python-only packages"]
        C2["No env management"]
        C3["Weak dependency resolution"]
        C4["No lock file built-in"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Simple projects"]
        B2["Pure Python deps"]
    end
    style P2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style C3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: conda Characteristics
steps.explanation: conda manages packages AND environments across languages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "conda Pros"
        P1["Multi-language (Python, R, C++)"]
        P2["Built-in env management"]
        P3["Pre-compiled binaries"]
        P4["Great for data science"]
    end
    subgraph "conda Cons"
        C1["Slower resolution"]
        C2["Larger disk usage"]
        C3["Fewer packages than PyPI"]
        C4["Can conflict with pip"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Data science/ML"]
        B2["Complex native deps"]
    end
    style P3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style C1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: poetry Characteristics
steps.explanation: poetry is a modern all-in-one Python tool.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "poetry Pros"
        P1["Deterministic builds (lock file)"]
        P2["Strong dependency resolver"]
        P3["Built-in packaging"]
        P4["pyproject.toml standard"]
    end
    subgraph "poetry Cons"
        C1["Learning curve"]
        C2["Slower installs"]
        C3["Python-only"]
        C4["Less ecosystem support"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Libraries/packages"]
        B2["Reproducible builds"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style P2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Feature Comparison
steps.explanation: Side-by-side feature comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Feature Matrix" F1["Env Management:
pip: No | conda: Yes | poetry: Yes"] F2["Lock Files:
pip: No* | conda: Yes | poetry: Yes"] F3["Non-Python:
pip: No | conda: Yes | poetry: No"] F4["Build/Publish:
pip: No | conda: No | poetry: Yes"] F5["Speed:
pip: Fast | conda: Slow | poetry: Medium"] end style F1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Choose based on your project needs.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB START["New Project?"] START --> Q1{"Need non-Python
packages?"} Q1 --> |"Yes"| CONDA["Use conda"] Q1 --> |"No"| Q2{"Building a
library?"} Q2 --> |"Yes"| POETRY["Use poetry"] Q2 --> |"No"| Q3{"Need reproducible
builds?"} Q3 --> |"Yes"| POETRY Q3 --> |"Simple project"| PIP["Use pip + venv"] style CONDA fill:#C8E6C9 style POETRY fill:#E1BEE7 style PIP fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Virtual Environments
fields.description: venv, virtualenv, and conda environment isolation

steps.title: Why Virtual Environments?
steps.explanation: Isolate project dependencies to avoid conflicts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Virtual Envs" SYS["System Python"] P1["Project A
needs Django 3.2"] P2["Project B
needs Django 4.0"] SYS --> P1 SYS --> P2 CONFLICT["Version Conflict!"] end subgraph "With Virtual Envs" VA["Env A
Django 3.2"] VB["Env B
Django 4.0"] PA["Project A"] --> VA PB["Project B"] --> VB OK["No Conflict!"] end style CONFLICT fill:#FFCDD2 style OK fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: venv (Built-in)
steps.explanation: Python's built-in virtual environment tool.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Create" C1["python -m venv myenv"] end subgraph "Activate" A1["source myenv/bin/activate
(Linux/Mac)"] A2["myenv\Scripts\activate
(Windows)"] end subgraph "Use" U1["pip install packages"] U2["python script.py"] end subgraph "Deactivate" D1["deactivate"] end C1 --> A1 A1 --> U1 --> U2 --> D1 style C1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Environment Structure
steps.explanation: What's inside a virtual environment directory.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "myenv/" BIN["bin/ (or Scripts/)
python, pip, activate"] LIB["lib/pythonX.X/site-packages/
Installed packages"] INC["include/
C headers"] CFG["pyvenv.cfg
Configuration"] end subgraph "Key Points" K1["Symlinks to base Python"] K2["Isolated site-packages"] K3["Own pip installation"] end style LIB fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: conda Environments
steps.explanation: conda provides more powerful environment isolation.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Create"
        C1["conda create -n myenv python=3.11"]
        C2["conda create -n myenv --file env.yml"]
    end
    subgraph "Activate"
        A1["conda activate myenv"]
    end
    subgraph "Manage"
        M1["conda install numpy"]
        M2["conda list"]
        M3["conda env export > env.yml"]
    end
    subgraph "Deactivate"
        D1["conda deactivate"]
    end
    C1 --> A1 --> M1
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style M3 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: venv vs conda
steps.explanation: Key differences between venv and conda environments.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "venv" V1["Python only"] V2["Uses system Python"] V3["Lightweight"] V4["pip for packages"] end subgraph "conda" C1["Any language"] C2["Own Python copy"] C3["Heavier"] C4["conda or pip"] end subgraph "Choose venv when" CV["Pure Python project
Simple deps"] end subgraph "Choose conda when" CC["Data science
Native libraries
Non-Python deps"] end style CV fill:#BBDEFB style CC fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for managing virtual environments effectively.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Do" D1["One env per project"] D2["Add env to .gitignore"] D3["Document in README"] D4["Use requirements.txt or lock file"] end subgraph "Don't" N1["Don't commit env folder"] N2["Don't use global packages"] N3["Don't share envs between projects"] end subgraph "Naming" NM["Use .venv or venv
in project root"] end style D1 fill:#C8E6C9 style N1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Dependency Resolution
fields.description: How package managers resolve version conflicts

steps.title: The Dependency Problem
steps.explanation: Packages depend on other packages with version constraints.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB APP["Your App"] APP --> A["Package A
requires C>=1.0"] APP --> B["Package B
requires C<2.0"] A --> C1["C 1.5?"] B --> C1 subgraph "Resolution" R["Find C version that
satisfies both:
C>=1.0 AND C<2.0
Solution: C 1.5"] end style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Version Specifiers
steps.explanation: How to specify version requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Exact" E1["==1.2.3
Exactly this version"] end subgraph "Ranges" R1[">=1.0
1.0 or higher"] R2["<2.0
Below 2.0"] R3[">=1.0,<2.0
Between 1.0 and 2.0"] end subgraph "Compatible" C1["~=1.4
>=1.4, <1.5 (patch updates)"] C2["^1.4
>=1.4, <2.0 (minor updates)"] end subgraph "Wildcards" W1["1.*
Any 1.x version"] end style C1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C2 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Resolution Strategies
steps.explanation: Different approaches to finding compatible versions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "pip (Legacy)"
        P1["First-found wins"]
        P2["Install in order"]
        P3["May break later"]
    end
    subgraph "pip (New Resolver)"
        N1["Backtracking algorithm"]
        N2["Tries to satisfy all"]
        N3["Fails if impossible"]
    end
    subgraph "poetry/conda"
        C1["SAT solver"]
        C2["Optimal solution"]
        C3["Slower but complete"]
    end
    style P3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style C2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Conflict Example
steps.explanation: When dependencies cannot be satisfied together.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB APP["Your App"] APP --> A["Package A
requires numpy>=2.0"] APP --> B["Package B
requires numpy<1.20"] A --> NP1["numpy 2.0"] B --> NP2["numpy 1.19"] CONFLICT["CONFLICT!
No version satisfies both"] subgraph "Solutions" S1["Update Package B"] S2["Downgrade Package A"] S3["Find alternatives"] S4["Fork and patch"] end style CONFLICT fill:#FFCDD2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Transitive Dependencies
steps.explanation: Dependencies of dependencies add complexity.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    APP["Your App"]
    APP --> A["requests"]
    APP --> B["flask"]
    A --> C["urllib3"]
    A --> D["certifi"]
    B --> E["werkzeug"]
    B --> F["jinja2"]
    F --> G["markupsafe"]
    subgraph "Depth"
        DIRECT["Direct: 2"]
        TRANS["Transitive: 5"]
        TOTAL["Total: 7"]
    end
    style APP fill:#BBDEFB
    style G fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Debugging Resolution
steps.explanation: Tools to understand dependency issues.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Visualization" V1["pipdeptree
Show dependency tree"] V2["pip show pkg
Package details"] end subgraph "Debugging" D1["pip install --dry-run"] D2["pip check
Find broken deps"] D3["pip install -v
Verbose output"] end subgraph "Resolution" R1["pip install --upgrade-strategy eager"] R2["pip install pkg --force-reinstall"] end style V1 fill:#C8E6C9 style D2 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: requirements.txt vs pyproject.toml
fields.description: Dependency specification formats comparison

steps.title: Two Approaches
steps.explanation: Two main ways to specify Python project dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR subgraph "Traditional" REQ["requirements.txt
Simple list of packages"] end subgraph "Modern" PYP["pyproject.toml
Project metadata + deps"] end REQ --> |"pip install -r"| INST["Install"] PYP --> |"pip install ."| INST style REQ fill:#BBDEFB style PYP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: requirements.txt
steps.explanation: Simple text file listing packages and versions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Format" F1["# Comments
package==1.0.0
package>=1.0,<2.0
-r other-requirements.txt"] end subgraph "Variants" V1["requirements.txt
Production"] V2["requirements-dev.txt
Development"] V3["requirements-test.txt
Testing"] end subgraph "Generate" G1["pip freeze > requirements.txt"] end style F1 fill:#FFF9C4 style G1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: pyproject.toml
steps.explanation: Modern standard for Python project configuration.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sections" S1["[build-system]
Build tool config"] S2["[project]
Metadata + dependencies"] S3["[project.optional-dependencies]
Extras like dev, test"] S4["[tool.*]
Tool configs (black, pytest)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Single config file"] B2["Standard format (PEP 621)"] B3["Tool configuration"] end style S2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Comparison
steps.explanation: Key differences between the two formats.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "requirements.txt" R1["Just dependencies"] R2["No metadata"] R3["pip only"] R4["Good for apps"] end subgraph "pyproject.toml" P1["Deps + metadata"] P2["Name, version, authors"] P3["Multiple tools"] P4["Good for libraries"] end subgraph "Can use both!" BOTH["pyproject.toml for package
requirements.txt for pinning"] end style BOTH fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Migration Path
steps.explanation: Moving from requirements.txt to pyproject.toml.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Step 1" S1["Create pyproject.toml
Add [project] section"] end subgraph "Step 2" S2["Move dependencies to
[project.dependencies]"] end subgraph "Step 3" S3["Add optional deps to
[project.optional-dependencies]"] end subgraph "Step 4" S4["Keep requirements.txt
for CI/deployment pinning"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 style S4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Use Which
steps.explanation: Guidelines for choosing the right format.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Use requirements.txt" R1["Simple scripts"] R2["Legacy projects"] R3["Deployment pinning"] R4["Docker builds"] end subgraph "Use pyproject.toml" P1["New projects"] P2["Publishable packages"] P3["Need tool configs"] P4["Modern workflow"] end subgraph "Use Both" B1["Library development
pyproject.toml for package
requirements.txt for CI pins"] end style P4 fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Package Installation Flow
fields.description: PyPI, wheels, source distributions, and installation process

steps.title: Installation Overview
steps.explanation: What happens when you run pip install.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    CMD["pip install requests"]
    RESOLVE["Resolve dependencies"]
    FETCH["Fetch from PyPI"]
    CHOOSE["Choose distribution"]
    INSTALL["Install to site-packages"]
    CMD --> RESOLVE --> FETCH --> CHOOSE --> INSTALL
    style CMD fill:#BBDEFB
    style INSTALL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Distribution Types
steps.explanation: Packages can be distributed as wheels or source.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wheel (.whl)" W1["Pre-built binary"] W2["Fast installation"] W3["No compilation needed"] W4["Platform-specific"] end subgraph "Source Distribution (.tar.gz)" S1["Source code"] S2["Needs build step"] S3["May need compiler"] S4["Platform-independent"] end subgraph "pip Preference" PREF["Wheel > Source
Faster and reliable"] end style W2 fill:#C8E6C9 style S3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: PyPI Lookup
steps.explanation: How pip finds packages on PyPI.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB PIP["pip install numpy"] API["Query PyPI API
pypi.org/pypi/numpy/json"] VERS["Get available versions"] COMPAT["Filter compatible
(Python, OS, arch)"] BEST["Select best match"] DOWN["Download from CDN"] PIP --> API --> VERS --> COMPAT --> BEST --> DOWN style API fill:#BBDEFB style DOWN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Wheel Naming
steps.explanation: Wheel filenames encode compatibility information.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Wheel Filename" FN["numpy-1.24.0-cp311-cp311-manylinux_x86_64.whl"] end subgraph "Components" C1["numpy: package name"] C2["1.24.0: version"] C3["cp311: Python 3.11"] C4["cp311: ABI tag"] C5["manylinux_x86_64: platform"] end subgraph "Universal Wheel" U1["package-1.0-py3-none-any.whl
Pure Python, any platform"] end style FN fill:#FFF9C4 style U1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Installation Locations
steps.explanation: Where packages get installed.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "System Python" SYS["/usr/lib/python3.x/site-packages/
(needs sudo)"] end subgraph "User Install" USR["~/.local/lib/python3.x/site-packages/
(pip install --user)"] end subgraph "Virtual Environment" VENV["./venv/lib/python3.x/site-packages/
(recommended)"] end subgraph "Editable Install" EDIT["Creates .pth file
Links to source directory"] end style VENV fill:#C8E6C9 style SYS fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Installation Options
steps.explanation: Common pip install flags and sources.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Sources" S1["pip install pkg
From PyPI"] S2["pip install ./pkg
From local dir"] S3["pip install git+url
From Git"] S4["pip install pkg.whl
From file"] end subgraph "Useful Flags" F1["--no-cache-dir
Fresh download"] F2["--upgrade
Update existing"] F3["--pre
Include prereleases"] F4["--index-url
Custom PyPI"] end style S1 fill:#C8E6C9 style F2 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Conda Environment Architecture
fields.description: Base environment, named environments, and channels

steps.title: Conda Architecture
steps.explanation: Conda manages environments and packages separately from system Python.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Conda Installation" BASE["Base Environment
(conda, python, pip)"] end subgraph "User Environments" E1["env: ml-project
python=3.10, pytorch"] E2["env: web-app
python=3.11, flask"] E3["env: data-analysis
python=3.9, pandas"] end BASE --> E1 BASE --> E2 BASE --> E3 style BASE fill:#BBDEFB style E1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Base vs Named Environments
steps.explanation: The base environment is special.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Base Environment"
        B1["Always exists"]
        B2["Contains conda itself"]
        B3["Activated by default"]
        B4["Don't install project deps here!"]
    end
    subgraph "Named Environments"
        N1["Created with conda create"]
        N2["Isolated from base"]
        N3["Activatable by name"]
        N4["Can have different Python"]
    end
    style B4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style N2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Directory Structure
steps.explanation: Where conda stores environments and packages.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "~/miniconda3/ or ~/anaconda3/" BIN["bin/
conda, python, pip"] ENVS["envs/
Named environments"] PKGS["pkgs/
Package cache"] end subgraph "envs/myenv/" EBIN["bin/
Environment executables"] ELIB["lib/
Libraries"] ECONDA["conda-meta/
Package metadata"] end ENVS --> EBIN style PKGS fill:#FFF9C4 style ENVS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Channels
steps.explanation: Channels are package repositories.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Default Channels" DEF["defaults
(Anaconda's curated)"] end subgraph "Community Channels" CF["conda-forge
(Community maintained)"] BIO["bioconda
(Bioinformatics)"] NV["nvidia
(CUDA packages)"] end subgraph "Priority" P1["Channel order matters!
First match wins"] P2["conda config --add channels conda-forge"] end style CF fill:#C8E6C9 style P1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Environment Files
steps.explanation: Export and recreate environments.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Export" EX1["conda env export > env.yml
(All packages, exact versions)"] EX2["conda env export --from-history > env.yml
(Only explicitly installed)"] end subgraph "environment.yml" YML["name: myenv
channels:
- conda-forge
dependencies:
- python=3.11
- numpy
- pip:
- some-pip-pkg"] end subgraph "Create from file" CR["conda env create -f env.yml"] end EX2 --> YML --> CR style EX2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Commands
steps.explanation: Essential conda environment commands.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Create & Remove"
        C1["conda create -n myenv python=3.11"]
        C2["conda remove -n myenv --all"]
    end
    subgraph "Activate & List"
        A1["conda activate myenv"]
        A2["conda deactivate"]
        A3["conda env list"]
    end
    subgraph "Packages"
        P1["conda install numpy pandas"]
        P2["conda update --all"]
        P3["conda list"]
    end

fields.title: Conda vs Pip Interoperability
fields.description: When and how to safely mix conda and pip

steps.title: The Challenge
steps.explanation: Conda and pip can conflict because they don't share metadata.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "conda install numpy" C1["Conda records: numpy 1.24"] end subgraph "pip install numpy --upgrade" P1["Pip overwrites to 1.25
Conda doesn't know!"] end subgraph "conda update numpy" C2["Conda tries to install 1.24
Conflict!"] end C1 --> P1 --> C2 style P1 fill:#FFCDD2 style C2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Safe Mixing Rules
steps.explanation: Follow these rules to avoid problems.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Rule 1" R1["Install conda packages FIRST"] end subgraph "Rule 2" R2["Use pip only for packages
not available in conda"] end subgraph "Rule 3" R3["Never run conda after pip
in same environment"] end subgraph "Rule 4" R4["Pin versions when using pip"] end R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 style R1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Recommended Workflow
steps.explanation: The safe way to use both package managers.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    S1["Create conda environment"]
    S2["Install conda packages"]
    S3["Export conda environment"]
    S4["Install remaining pip packages"]
    S5["Freeze pip requirements"]
    S6["Done! Don't use conda install again"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style S4 fill:#BBDEFB
    style S6 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: environment.yml with pip
steps.explanation: Include pip packages in your conda environment file.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "environment.yml" YML["name: myproject
channels:
- conda-forge
dependencies:
- python=3.11
- numpy
- pandas
- pip
- pip:
- some-pip-only-package
- another-pip-package==1.2.3"] end subgraph "Key Points" K1["Include pip as conda dep"] K2["pip section comes last"] K3["Pin versions for pip pkgs"] end style YML fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Troubleshooting
steps.explanation: How to fix conda/pip conflicts.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "If Things Break" F1["Create fresh environment"] F2["Install conda deps first"] F3["Then pip deps"] end subgraph "Check Consistency" C1["conda list
(shows pip packages too)"] C2["pip check
(verify dependencies)"] end subgraph "Nuclear Option" N1["conda remove -n myenv --all"] N2["Start over with env.yml"] end style F1 fill:#C8E6C9 style N1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: When to Use Which
steps.explanation: Guidelines for choosing conda vs pip.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Use conda for"
        C1["Python itself"]
        C2["NumPy, SciPy, Pandas"]
        C3["PyTorch, TensorFlow"]
        C4["Non-Python libs (CUDA)"]
    end
    subgraph "Use pip for"
        P1["Packages not on conda"]
        P2["Latest versions needed"]
        P3["Pure Python packages"]
        P4["Your own packages"]
    end
    style C3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style P1 fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: pyproject.toml Structure
fields.description: Build system, dependencies, and metadata in pyproject.toml

steps.title: pyproject.toml Overview
steps.explanation: The modern standard for Python project configuration (PEP 517/621).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "pyproject.toml sections" BS["[build-system]
How to build"] PROJ["[project]
Metadata + deps"] OPT["[project.optional-dependencies]
Extras"] TOOL["[tool.*]
Tool configs"] end BS --> PROJ --> OPT --> TOOL style PROJ fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: [build-system]
steps.explanation: Specifies how to build the package.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Build System Options" SETUP["setuptools (traditional)"] HATCH["hatchling (modern)"] POETRY["poetry-core"] FLIT["flit-core (simple)"] end subgraph "Example" EX["[build-system]
requires = ['hatchling']
build-backend = 'hatchling.build'"] end style EX fill:#C8E6C9 style HATCH fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: [project] Metadata
steps.explanation: Core project information (PEP 621).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "[project]" NAME["name = 'my-package'"] VER["version = '1.0.0'"] DESC["description = 'A great package'"] AUTH["authors = [{name='Me', email='me@x.com'}]"] REQ["requires-python = '>=3.9'"] LIC["license = {text = 'MIT'}"] end subgraph "Dynamic" DYN["dynamic = ['version']
(read from __init__.py)"] end style NAME fill:#C8E6C9 style VER fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Dependencies
steps.explanation: Specifying package dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "[project]" DEPS["dependencies = [
'requests>=2.28',
'pandas~=2.0',
'numpy',
]"] end subgraph "[project.optional-dependencies]" DEV["dev = [
'pytest>=7.0',
'black',
'mypy',
]"] DOCS["docs = ['sphinx', 'furo']"] end subgraph "Install extras" INS["pip install .[dev]
pip install .[dev,docs]"] end style DEPS fill:#C8E6C9 style INS fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Entry Points & Scripts
steps.explanation: Define CLI commands and plugin entry points.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "[project.scripts]" CLI["my-cli = 'mypackage.cli:main'
(Creates 'my-cli' command)"] end subgraph "[project.gui-scripts]" GUI["my-app = 'mypackage.gui:run'
(GUI application)"] end subgraph "[project.entry-points.'group']" EP["plugin_name = 'mypackage:Plugin'
(Plugin system)"] end style CLI fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: [tool.*] Sections
steps.explanation: Configuration for development tools.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "[tool.black]" BLACK["line-length = 88

fields.title: Docker + Package Managers
fields.description: Layer caching, multi-stage builds, and efficient images

steps.title: Docker Layer Caching
steps.explanation: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a cached layer.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Dockerfile"
        D1["FROM python:3.11"]
        D2["COPY requirements.txt ."]
        D3["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        D4["COPY . ."]
    end
    subgraph "Layer Cache"
        L1["Layer 1: Base image (cached)"]
        L2["Layer 2: requirements.txt (cached if unchanged)"]
        L3["Layer 3: pip install (cached if L2 unchanged)"]
        L4["Layer 4: Source code (rebuilt on changes)"]
    end
    D1 --> L1
    D2 --> L2
    D3 --> L3
    D4 --> L4
    style L3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Optimized Layer Order
steps.explanation: Copy dependency files before source code.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Bad (cache invalidated often)"
        B1["COPY . ."]
        B2["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        B3["Any code change rebuilds deps!"]
    end
    subgraph "Good (deps cached)"
        G1["COPY requirements.txt ."]
        G2["RUN pip install -r requirements.txt"]
        G3["COPY . ."]
        G4["Code changes don't rebuild deps"]
    end
    style B3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style G4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Example Optimized Dockerfile
steps.explanation: A production-ready Python Dockerfile.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dockerfile" DF["FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system deps first
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
libpq-dev \
&& rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python deps
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy source last
COPY . .

CMD ['python', 'app.py']"] end style DF fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Git Object Model

steps.title: Git is a Content-Addressable Filesystem
steps.explanation: Git stores everything as objects identified by SHA-1 hashes.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Git Objects" BLOB["Blob
File contents"] TREE["Tree
Directory listing"] COMMIT["Commit
Snapshot + metadata"] TAG["Tag
Named reference"] end subgraph "Key Concept" SHA["Every object has a SHA-1 hash
e.g., a1b2c3d4e5f6..."] end style COMMIT fill:#C8E6C9 style SHA fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Blob Objects
steps.explanation: Blobs store file contents (no filename, just data).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "File: hello.py" FILE["print('hello')"] end subgraph "Blob Object" BLOB["SHA: 3b18e5...
Type: blob
Content: print('hello')"] end FILE --> |"git hash-object"| BLOB subgraph "Key Points" K1["Same content = same hash"] K2["No filename stored in blob"] K3["Compressed with zlib"] end style BLOB fill:#BBDEFB

steps.explanation: Trees represent directories, mapping names to blobs/trees.

graph TB subgraph "Tree Object (root)" TREE["SHA: 8f4a2b...

Type: tree"] SE1["100644 blob 9a8b7c... main.py"] end TREE --> E1 TREE --> E2 TREE --> E3 E3 --> STREE STREE --> SE1 style TREE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Commit Objects

steps.title: What is Git Revert?

fields.title: Pandas GroupBy & Aggregation
fields.description: Master groupby operations, aggregation functions, transform, and apply for powerful data analysis

steps.title: The Split-Apply-Combine Pattern
steps.explanation:
**GroupBy** follows a three-step pattern:

1. **Split**: Divide data into groups based on key(s)
2. **Apply**: Perform operation on each group
3. **Combine**: Merge results back together

This is the foundation of all groupby operations.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original DataFrame" DF["| dept | name | salary |
|------|-------|--------|
| Eng | Alice | 80000 |
| Eng | Bob | 75000 |
| Sales| Carol | 60000 |
| Sales| Dave | 65000 |"] end subgraph "1. SPLIT by dept" G1["Eng Group
Alice: 80000
Bob: 75000"] G2["Sales Group
Carol: 60000
Dave: 65000"] end subgraph "2. APPLY mean()" A1["mean = 77500"] A2["mean = 62500"] end subgraph "3. COMBINE" Result["| dept | salary |
|-------|--------|
| Eng | 77500 |
| Sales | 62500 |"] end DF --> G1 DF --> G2 G1 --> A1 G2 --> A2 A1 --> Result A2 --> Result style DF fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Basic GroupBy Syntax
steps.explanation:
The basic syntax is `df.groupby('column').agg_func()`. You can group by one or multiple columns.

```python
# Single column groupby
df.groupby('dept')['salary'].mean()

# Multiple columns
df.groupby(['dept', 'year'])['sales'].sum()

# GroupBy object (lazy - no computation yet)
grouped = df.groupby('dept')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "GroupBy Syntax" S1["df.groupby('col')"] S2["df.groupby(['col1', 'col2'])"] S3["df.groupby('col', as_index=False)"] end subgraph "Common Aggregations" A1["grouped.mean()"] A2["grouped.sum()"] A3["grouped.count()"] A4["grouped.min() / max()"] A5["grouped.std() / var()"] A6["grouped.first() / last()"] end subgraph "Key Options" O1["as_index=False
Keep group cols as columns"] O2["sort=False
Don't sort by group keys"] O3["dropna=False
Include NaN as group"] end S1 --> A1 S1 --> A2 style S1 fill:#ADD8E6 style O1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: The agg() Method - Multiple Aggregations
steps.explanation:
Use `agg()` to apply multiple functions at once or different functions to different columns.

```python
# Multiple functions on one column
df.groupby('dept')['salary'].agg(['mean', 'min', 'max'])

# Different functions per column
df.groupby('dept').agg({
    'salary': ['mean', 'sum'],
    'bonus': 'max',
    'name': 'count'
})
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Single Column, Multiple Funcs" Code1["df.groupby('dept')['salary'].agg(['mean', 'sum', 'count'])"] Result1["| dept | mean | sum | count |
|-------|-------|--------|-------|
| Eng | 77500 | 155000 | 2 |
| Sales | 62500 | 125000 | 2 |"] end subgraph "Multiple Columns, Custom Funcs" Code2["df.groupby('dept').agg({
'salary': ['mean', 'max'],
'years': 'sum'
})"] Result2["MultiIndex columns:
salary_mean, salary_max, years_sum"] end subgraph "Named Aggregations (Clean Output)" Code3["df.groupby('dept').agg(
avg_salary=('salary', 'mean'),
max_salary=('salary', 'max'),
headcount=('name', 'count')
)"] Result3["Clean column names!
avg_salary, max_salary, headcount"] end Code1 --> Result1 Code3 --> Result3 style Code3 fill:#90EE90 style Result3 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: transform() - Same Shape Output
steps.explanation:
**transform()** returns data with the same shape as input - perfect for adding group statistics back to original rows.

```python
# Add group mean as new column
df['dept_avg'] = df.groupby('dept')['salary'].transform('mean')

# Normalize within groups
df['salary_zscore'] = df.groupby('dept')['salary'].transform(
    lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()
)
```

graph TB subgraph "Original Data" Orig["| dept | name | salary |
|------|-------|--------|

| Eng | Bob | 75000 |
| Sales| Carol | 60000 |"] end subgraph "transform('mean')" Trans["Returns same-length Series:
[77500, 77500, 60000]

Each row gets its GROUP's mean"] end subgraph "Result with New Column" Result["| dept | name | salary | dept_avg |
|------|-------|--------|----------|
| Eng | Alice | 80000 | 77500 |
| Eng | Bob | 75000 | 77500 |
| Sales| Carol | 60000 | 60000 |"] end subgraph "Common Uses" U1["Group normalization"] U2["Fill missing with group mean"] U3["Rank within groups"] U4["Percent of group total"] end Orig --> Trans --> Result style Trans fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: transform() vs agg() Comparison
steps.explanation: The key difference: **agg()** reduces to one row per group, **transform()** keeps all rows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Input: 4 rows" Input["| dept | salary |
|------|--------|
| Eng | 80000 |
| Eng | 75000 |
| Sales| 60000 |
| Sales| 65000 |"] end subgraph "groupby('dept')['salary'].mean()" AggResult["2 rows (one per group)
| dept | salary |
| Eng | 77500 |
| Sales | 62500 |"] end subgraph "groupby('dept')['salary'].transform('mean')" TransResult["4 rows (same as input)
[77500, 77500, 62500, 62500]

Can assign back to df!"] end Input -->|"agg/mean"| AggResult Input -->|"transform"| TransResult style AggResult fill:#FFB6C1 style TransResult fill:#90EE90

steps.title: apply() - Maximum Flexibility
steps.explanation:
**apply()** lets you apply any function to each group. It's the most flexible but slowest option.

```python
# Custom function per group
def top_earners(group):
    return group.nlargest(2, 'salary')

df.groupby('dept').apply(top_earners)

# Lambda for quick operations
df.groupby('dept').apply(lambda g: g['salary'].max() - g['salary'].min())
```

return pd.Series({
'count': len(g),
'avg': g['salary'].mean(),
'range': g['salary'].max() - g['salary'].min()
})

df.groupby('dept').apply(summarize)"] end style Func fill:#ADD8E6 style Ex fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Multi-Level GroupBy
steps.explanation:
Group by multiple columns to create hierarchical groups.

```python
# Group by two columns
df.groupby(['dept', 'year'])['sales'].sum()

# Results in MultiIndex
# dept   year
# Eng    2023    150000
#        2024    180000
# Sales  2023    120000
```

steps.title: filter() - Keep/Remove Entire Groups
steps.explanation:
**filter()** keeps or removes entire groups based on a condition.

```python
# Keep only departments with avg salary > 70000
df.groupby('dept').filter(lambda g: g['salary'].mean() > 70000)

# Keep groups with at least 5 members
df.groupby('team').filter(lambda g: len(g) >= 5)
```

fields.title: Pandas Missing Data Handling
fields.description: Learn to detect, analyze, and handle missing values with fillna, dropna, interpolate, and more

steps.title: Types of Missing Data
steps.explanation:
In Pandas, missing data is represented by:

- **NaN** (Not a Number) - for floats
- **None** - Python's null object
- **NaT** (Not a Time) - for datetime
- **pd.NA** - Pandas nullable integer/string

All are detected by `isna()` / `isnull()` (they're identical).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Missing Value Types" NaN["np.nan / float('nan')
Used in: float columns"] None_["None
Used in: object columns"] NaT["pd.NaT
Used in: datetime columns"] NA["pd.NA
Used in: nullable int/string"] end subgraph "Detection (all work the same)" Detect["df.isna()
df.isnull()
pd.isna(value)"] end subgraph "Example DataFrame" DF["| name | age | date |
|-------|------|------------|
| Alice | 30.0 | 2024-01-01 |
| None | NaN | NaT |
| Bob | 25.0 | 2024-01-03 |"] end NaN --> Detect None_ --> Detect NaT --> Detect style NaN fill:#FFB6C1 style Detect fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Detecting Missing Values
steps.explanation:
Use these methods to find missing data:

```python
# Boolean mask of missing values
df.isna()          # True where missing
df.notna()         # True where NOT missing

# Count missing per column
df.isna().sum()

# Percent missing
df.isna().mean() * 100
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Detection Methods" M1["df.isna()
Returns boolean DataFrame"] M2["df.isna().sum()
Count per column"] M3["df.isna().sum().sum()
Total count"] M4["df.isna().any()
Any missing per column?"] M5["df.isna().all()
All missing per column?"] end subgraph "Visual Summary" Code["# Quick overview
print(df.isna().sum())

name 1
age 1
date 1
salary 0"] end subgraph "Find Rows with Missing" Rows["# Rows with any missing
df[df.isna().any(axis=1)]

# Rows with specific col missing
df[df['age'].isna()]"] end style M1 fill:#ADD8E6 style Code fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: dropna() - Remove Missing Values
steps.explanation:
Remove rows or columns with missing values.

```python
# Drop rows with ANY missing
df.dropna()

# Drop rows where ALL values missing
df.dropna(how='all')

# Drop rows missing in specific columns
df.dropna(subset=['name', 'age'])

# Require minimum non-null values
df.dropna(thresh=3)  # Keep if >= 3 non-null
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" Orig["| A | B | C |
|------|------|------|
| 1 | NaN | 3 |
| NaN | NaN | NaN |
| 7 | 8 | 9 |"] end subgraph "dropna() - any missing" Any["| A | B | C |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | 8 | 9 |

Only fully complete rows"] end subgraph "dropna(how='all')" All["| A | B | C |
|------|------|------|
| 1 | NaN | 3 |
| 7 | 8 | 9 |

Row 2 dropped (all NaN)"] end subgraph "dropna(thresh=2)" Thresh["| A | B | C |
|------|------|------|
| 1 | NaN | 3 |
| 7 | 8 | 9 |

Keep rows with ≥2 values"] end Orig --> Any Orig --> All Orig --> Thresh style Any fill:#FFB6C1 style All fill:#90EE90

steps.title: fillna() - Replace Missing Values
steps.explanation:
Fill missing values with a constant, computed value, or method.

```python
# Constant value
df.fillna(0)
df['age'].fillna(df['age'].mean())

# Different values per column
df.fillna({'age': 0, 'name': 'Unknown'})

# Forward/backward fill
df.fillna(method='ffill')  # Use previous value
df.fillna(method='bfill')  # Use next value
```

| 1 |
| NaN |
| 3 |

| 5 |"] end subgraph "fillna(0)" Zero["| value |
|-------|
| 1 |
| 0 |
| 3 |
| 0 |
| 5 |"] end subgraph "fillna(method='ffill')" FFill["| value |
|-------|
| 1 |
| 1 |← prev
| 3 |
| 3 |← prev
| 5 |"] end subgraph "fillna(method='bfill')" BFill["| value |
|-------|
| 1 |
| 3 |← next
| 3 |
| 5 |← next
| 5 |"] end Orig --> Zero Orig --> FFill Orig --> BFill style FFill fill:#90EE90 style BFill fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: interpolate() - Smart Filling
steps.explanation:
**interpolate()** estimates missing values using surrounding data - great for time series.

```python
# Linear interpolation (default)
df['value'].interpolate()

# Time-based interpolation
df['value'].interpolate(method='time')

# Polynomial interpolation
df['value'].interpolate(method='polynomial', order=2)
```

|-----|-------|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 2 |← estimated
| 2 | 4 |← estimated
| 3 | 6 |
| 4 | 8 |← estimated
| 5 | 10 |"] end subgraph "Visual" Graph["0 → ? → ? → 6
Linear: 0, 2, 4, 6

Fills gaps proportionally"] end subgraph "Methods Available" Methods["linear - straight line
time - time-weighted
index - index-weighted
polynomial - curve fit
spline - smooth curve"] end Orig --> Linear Linear --> Graph style Linear fill:#90EE90 style Graph fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Filling with Group Statistics
steps.explanation:
Fill missing values using group-specific statistics - more accurate than global fill.

```python
# Fill with group mean
df['salary'] = df.groupby('dept')['salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.fillna(x.mean())
)

# Fill with group median
df['age'] = df.groupby('category')['age'].transform(
    lambda x: x.fillna(x.median())
)
```

(mean of all salaries)

Ignores department context!"] end subgraph "Group Mean Fill" Group["Eng NaN → 75000
(mean of Eng)

Sales NaN → 50000
(mean of Sales)

Respects department context!"] end subgraph "Code" Code["df.groupby('dept')['salary'].transform(
lambda x: x.fillna(x.mean())
)"] end Orig --> Global Orig --> Group style Global fill:#FFB6C1 style Group fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Missing Data Decision Tree
steps.explanation: Use this flowchart to decide how to handle missing values.
steps.diagram_data:

May be informative!"] subgraph "Best Practices" BP1["Always document your strategy"] BP2["Consider creating 'was_missing' flag"] BP3["Use domain knowledge"] BP4["Test impact on model"] end style Drop fill:#FFB6C1 style FillMean fill:#90EE90 style Interpolate fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Creating Missing Indicators
steps.explanation:
Sometimes missingness itself is informative. Create indicator columns to preserve this signal.

```python
# Create indicator for missing
df['salary_missing'] = df['salary'].isna().astype(int)

# Then fill the original
df['salary'] = df['salary'].fillna(df['salary'].median())
```

|----|--------|----------------|

| 2 | 55000 | 1 |← filled
| 3 | 60000 | 0 |
| 4 | 55000 | 1 |← filled"] end subgraph "Why This Matters" Why["• Missing salary might mean:
- New hire (no history)
- Contractor (different pay)
- Data entry issue

• Indicator preserves this info
• Model can learn pattern"] end Orig --> Result style Result fill:#90EE90 style Why fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: replace() - Handle Special Values
steps.explanation:
Sometimes 'missing' is encoded as special values (0, -1, 999, 'N/A'). Use `replace()` first.

```python
# Replace special values with NaN
df.replace(-1, np.nan)
df.replace([999, -999, 'N/A', ''], np.nan)

# Then handle as normal missing data
df.fillna(df.mean())
```

steps.explanation: Summary of all missing data methods.

fields.title: Pandas Window Functions
fields.description: Master rolling, expanding, shift, diff, and resample for time series feature engineering

steps.title: What Are Window Functions?
steps.explanation:
**Window functions** compute values using a subset (window) of rows. Essential for:

- Moving averages
- Lag/lead features
- Rolling statistics
- Time series analysis

Unlike groupby, windows can overlap!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataFrame" D1["Row 1: 10"] D2["Row 2: 20"] D3["Row 3: 30"] D4["Row 4: 40"] D5["Row 5: 50"] end subgraph "Rolling Window (size=3)" W1["Window 1: [10, 20, 30]
mean = 20"] W2["Window 2: [20, 30, 40]
mean = 30"] W3["Window 3: [30, 40, 50]
mean = 40"] end D1 --> W1 D2 --> W1 D3 --> W1 D2 --> W2 D3 --> W2 D4 --> W2 D3 --> W3 D4 --> W3 D5 --> W3 style W1 fill:#ADD8E6 style W2 fill:#90EE90 style W3 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: rolling() - Moving Window
steps.explanation:
**rolling()** creates a sliding window of fixed size.

```python
# 3-day moving average
df['ma_3'] = df['price'].rolling(window=3).mean()

# Rolling with min_periods
df['ma_3'] = df['price'].rolling(3, min_periods=1).mean()
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original Data" Orig["| day | price |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 110 |
| 3 | 105 |
| 4 | 115 |
| 5 | 120 |"] end subgraph "rolling(3).mean()" Roll["| day | price | ma_3 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 1 | 100 | NaN |← not enough data
| 2 | 110 | NaN |← not enough data
| 3 | 105 | 105.0 |← (100+110+105)/3
| 4 | 115 | 110.0 |← (110+105+115)/3
| 5 | 120 | 113.3 |← (105+115+120)/3"] end subgraph "Common Parameters" Params["window - size of window
min_periods - min values needed
center - center the window
win_type - 'gaussian', 'triang'"] end Orig --> Roll style Roll fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Rolling Aggregation Functions
steps.explanation: All standard aggregations work with rolling windows.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Common Rolling Operations" R1["df['col'].rolling(n).mean()
Moving average"] R2["df['col'].rolling(n).sum()
Rolling sum"] R3["df['col'].rolling(n).std()
Rolling volatility"] R4["df['col'].rolling(n).min()
Rolling minimum"] R5["df['col'].rolling(n).max()
Rolling maximum"] R6["df['col'].rolling(n).count()
Rolling count"] end subgraph "Advanced Rolling" A1["rolling(n).apply(custom_func)
Any custom function"] A2["rolling(n).quantile(0.5)
Rolling median"] A3["rolling(n).var()
Rolling variance"] A4["rolling(n).skew()
Rolling skewness"] end subgraph "Feature Engineering Example" FE["# Price features
df['ma_7'] = df['price'].rolling(7).mean()
df['ma_30'] = df['price'].rolling(30).mean()
df['volatility'] = df['price'].rolling(7).std()
df['max_7d'] = df['price'].rolling(7).max()"] end style R1 fill:#90EE90 style FE fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: shift() - Lag and Lead
steps.explanation:
**shift()** moves data up or down - essential for creating lag features.

```python
# Previous value (lag)
df['price_yesterday'] = df['price'].shift(1)

# Future value (lead)
df['price_tomorrow'] = df['price'].shift(-1)

# Calculate change
df['daily_change'] = df['price'] - df['price'].shift(1)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" Orig["| day | price |
|-----|-------|
| Mon | 100 |
| Tue | 110 |
| Wed | 105 |
| Thu | 115 |"] end subgraph "shift(1) - Lag" Lag["| day | price | prev |
|-----|-------|-------|
| Mon | 100 | NaN |← no prev
| Tue | 110 | 100 |← Mon's price
| Wed | 105 | 110 |← Tue's price
| Thu | 115 | 105 |← Wed's price"] end subgraph "shift(-1) - Lead" Lead["| day | price | next |
|-----|-------|-------|
| Mon | 100 | 110 |← Tue's price
| Tue | 110 | 105 |← Wed's price
| Wed | 105 | 115 |← Thu's price
| Thu | 115 | NaN |← no next"] end Orig --> Lag Orig --> Lead style Lag fill:#90EE90 style Lead fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: diff() - Differences
steps.explanation:
**diff()** calculates the difference from the previous row - useful for changes and derivatives.

```python
# First difference (change from previous)
df['price_change'] = df['price'].diff()

# Second difference (change in change)
df['acceleration'] = df['price'].diff().diff()

# Percent change
df['pct_change'] = df['price'].pct_change()
```

steps.title: expanding() - Cumulative Window
steps.explanation:
**expanding()** uses all data up to current row - the window grows over time.

```python
# Cumulative mean
df['cumulative_avg'] = df['value'].expanding().mean()

# Cumulative max (running maximum)
df['running_max'] = df['value'].expanding().max()
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" Orig["| row | value |
|-----|-------|
| 1 | 10 |
| 2 | 20 |
| 3 | 15 |
| 4 | 25 |"] end subgraph "expanding().mean()" Exp["| row | value | cum_mean |
|-----|-------|----------|

| 4 | 25 | 17.5 |← rows 1-4"] end subgraph "Window Growth" Growth["Row 1: [10]
Row 2: [10, 20]
Row 3: [10, 20, 15]

df['cumsum'] = df['value'].cumsum()"] end Orig --> Exp Exp --> Growth style Exp fill:#90EE90 style Growth fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: resample() - Time-Based Grouping
steps.explanation:
**resample()** groups time series by time periods - like groupby for dates.

```python
# Daily data to monthly
df.resample('M')['sales'].sum()

# Hourly data to daily
df.resample('D')['temp'].mean()

# Upsample with fill
df.resample('H').ffill()
```

steps.title: ewm() - Exponential Weighted Moving
steps.explanation:
**ewm()** gives more weight to recent values - better for trend detection.

```python
# Exponential moving average
df['ema'] = df['price'].ewm(span=10).mean()

# Using halflife
df['ema'] = df['price'].ewm(halflife=5).mean()
```

fields.title: Pandas Concatenation & Appending
fields.description: Learn to combine DataFrames with concat, append, and understand axis and index alignment

steps.title: concat() vs merge() - When to Use Which
steps.explanation:
**concat()** stacks DataFrames (no key matching)
**merge()** joins DataFrames on keys

```python
# concat - stack vertically or horizontally
pd.concat([df1, df2])

# merge - join on common columns
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "concat() - Stacking" C1["df1"] C2["df2"] CR["df1
---
df2

Just stacks rows"] C1 --> CR C2 --> CR end subgraph "merge() - Joining" M1["df1 (has key)"] M2["df2 (has key)"] MR["Matched rows
by key value"] M1 --> MR M2 --> MR end subgraph "When to Use" Use1["concat: Same structure,
stack together"] Use2["merge: Different data,
match by key"] end style CR fill:#ADD8E6 style MR fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Vertical Concatenation (axis=0)
steps.explanation:
Stack DataFrames on top of each other - adds rows.

```python
# Default: vertical stack
pd.concat([df1, df2])
pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0)  # explicit

# Multiple DataFrames
pd.concat([df1, df2, df3, df4])
```

|---|---|
| 1 | 2 |← from df1
| 3 | 4 |← from df1
| 5 | 6 |← from df2
| 7 | 8 |← from df2"] end D1 -->|"stack"| Result D2 -->|"below"| Result style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Horizontal Concatenation (axis=1)
steps.explanation:
Stack DataFrames side by side - adds columns.

```python
# Horizontal stack
pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)
```

|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 5 | 6 |
| 3 | 4 | 7 | 8 |"] end D1 -->|"stack"| Result D2 -->|"beside"| Result style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Handling Different Columns
steps.explanation:
When DataFrames have different columns, `concat` fills missing with NaN.

```python
# Different columns - fills with NaN
pd.concat([df1, df2])  # keeps all columns

# Only keep common columns
pd.concat([df1, df2], join='inner')
```

| 1 | 2 | NaN |← df1 missing C
| NaN | 3 | 4 |← df2 missing A"] end subgraph "concat - join='inner'" Inner["| B |
|---|
| 2 |
| 3 |

Only common column B"] end D1 --> Outer D2 --> Outer D1 --> Inner D2 --> Inner style Outer fill:#FFFACD style Inner fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Index Handling with ignore_index
steps.explanation:
By default, concat preserves original indices. Use `ignore_index=True` to reset.

```python
# Keeps original indices (may duplicate!)
pd.concat([df1, df2])

# Reset to 0, 1, 2, ...
pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)
```

0 | A
1 | B
2 | C ← clean
3 | D ← clean"] end D1 --> Dup D2 --> Dup D1 --> Reset D2 --> Reset style Dup fill:#FFB6C1 style Reset fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Adding Keys for Identification
steps.explanation:
Use `keys` to identify source DataFrame - creates MultiIndex.

```python
# Add source identifier
pd.concat([df1, df2], keys=['first', 'second'])

# Access specific source
result.loc['first']
```

steps.explanation: Watch out for these common issues!

fields.title: Pandas Advanced Filtering & Selection
fields.description: Master boolean indexing, query(), isin(), between(), and complex filtering patterns

steps.title: Boolean Indexing Basics
steps.explanation:
Filter rows using boolean conditions - the most common filtering method.

```python
# Single condition
df[df['age'] > 30]

# Multiple conditions (use & | ~)
df[(df['age'] > 30) & (df['salary'] > 50000)]
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original DataFrame" DF["| name | age | salary |
|-------|-----|--------|
| Alice | 35 | 60000 |
| Bob | 28 | 45000 |
| Carol | 42 | 75000 |"] end subgraph "Boolean Mask: df['age'] > 30" Mask["[True, False, True]"] end subgraph "Result: df[df['age'] > 30]" Result["| name | age | salary |
|-------|-----|--------|
| Alice | 35 | 60000 |
| Carol | 42 | 75000 |"] end DF --> Mask --> Result style Mask fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Combining Conditions
steps.explanation:
Use **&** (and), **|** (or), **~** (not) for complex filters.

**Important**: Wrap each condition in parentheses!

```python
# AND - both conditions
df[(df['age'] > 30) & (df['dept'] == 'Eng')]

# OR - either condition
df[(df['age'] < 25) | (df['age'] > 60)]

# NOT - negate condition
df[~(df['status'] == 'inactive')]
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "AND: Both Must Be True" AND["(df['age'] > 30) & (df['salary'] > 50000)

True & True = True
True & False = False
False & True = False"] end subgraph "OR: Either Can Be True" OR["(df['dept'] == 'Eng') | (df['dept'] == 'Sales')

True | False = True
False | True = True
False | False = False"] end subgraph "NOT: Invert" NOT["~(df['status'] == 'inactive')

~True = False
~False = True"] end subgraph "Common Mistake" Wrong["df[df['age'] > 30 & df['salary'] > 50000]
← Missing parentheses!

df[(df['age'] > 30) & (df['salary'] > 50000)]
← Correct!"] end style AND fill:#90EE90 style Wrong fill:#FFB6C1

steps.title: query() - SQL-like Filtering
steps.explanation:
**query()** uses string expressions - cleaner for complex conditions.

```python
# Equivalent to boolean indexing
df.query('age > 30 and salary > 50000')

# Use @ for external variables
min_age = 30
df.query('age > @min_age')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Boolean Indexing" BI["df[(df['age'] > 30) &
(df['salary'] > 50000) &
(df['dept'] == 'Engineering')]"] end subgraph "query() Equivalent" Q["df.query('age > 30 and
salary > 50000 and
dept == "Engineering"')"] end subgraph "query() Benefits" B1["Cleaner syntax"] B2["No repeated df references"] B3["Can use and/or instead of &/|"] B4["Faster for large DataFrames"] end subgraph "External Variables" Ext["threshold = 50000
df.query('salary > @threshold')

@ prefix for Python variables"] end BI -.->|"same result"| Q style Q fill:#90EE90 style BI fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: isin() - Match Multiple Values
steps.explanation:
**isin()** checks if values are in a list - like SQL's IN clause.

```python
# Filter for specific values
df[df['dept'].isin(['Eng', 'Sales', 'Marketing'])]

# Exclude specific values
df[~df['status'].isin(['inactive', 'suspended'])]
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" DF["| name | dept |
|-------|---------|
| Alice | Eng |
| Bob | HR |
| Carol | Sales |
| Dave | Eng |"] end subgraph "df['dept'].isin(['Eng', 'Sales'])" Mask["[True, False, True, True]"] end subgraph "Result" Result["| name | dept |
|-------|-------|
| Alice | Eng |
| Carol | Sales |
| Dave | Eng |"] end subgraph "vs Multiple OR" Compare["# These are equivalent:
df[df['dept'].isin(['Eng', 'Sales'])]

df[(df['dept'] == 'Eng') |
(df['dept'] == 'Sales')]"] end DF --> Mask --> Result style Result fill:#90EE90 style Compare fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: between() - Range Filtering
steps.explanation:
**between()** checks if values fall within a range (inclusive by default).

```python
# Age between 25 and 35 (inclusive)
df[df['age'].between(25, 35)]

# Exclusive bounds
df[df['price'].between(100, 200, inclusive='neither')]
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original" DF["| name | age |
|-------|-----|
| Alice | 22 |
| Bob | 28 |
| Carol | 35 |
| Dave | 42 |"] end subgraph "df['age'].between(25, 35)" Mask["[False, True, True, False]"] end subgraph "Result" Result["| name | age |
|-------|-----|
| Bob | 28 |
| Carol | 35 |← 35 included"] end subgraph "inclusive parameter" Inc["'both' (default) - include both bounds
'left' - include only lower
'right' - include only upper
'neither' - exclude both"] end DF --> Mask --> Result style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: String Filtering with str accessor
steps.explanation:
Use `str` accessor for text column filtering.

```python
# Contains substring
df[df['name'].str.contains('john', case=False)]

# Starts/ends with
df[df['email'].str.endswith('@gmail.com')]

# Regex matching
df[df['phone'].str.match(r'^\d{3}-\d{4}$')]
```

graph TB subgraph "String Methods" M1["str.contains('text')
Substring match"] M2["str.startswith('prefix')

Full regex match"] M5["str.len() > 5
String length"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["# Names containing 'son'

df[df['email'].str.endswith('@gmail.com')]"] E3["# Phone format XXX-XXXX
df[df['phone'].str.match(r'^\d{3}-\d{4}$')]"] end subgraph "Handle NaN" NaN["# na=False to ignore NaN
df[df['col'].str.contains('x', na=False)]"] end style E1 fill:#90EE90 style NaN fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Filtering with loc and iloc
steps.explanation:
Combine filtering with column selection using `loc`.

```python
# Filter rows + select columns
df.loc[df['age'] > 30, ['name', 'salary']]

# Filter + modify
df.loc[df['status'] == 'active', 'bonus'] = 1000
```

# This modifies df in place!"] end subgraph "Common Pattern" Pattern["# Filter, select, and transform
result = df.loc[
df['status'] == 'active',
['id', 'name', 'amount']
].copy()"] end style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R3 fill:#ADD8E6

steps.explanation: Common real-world filtering patterns.

.filter(lambda x: len(x) > 5)"] end subgraph "Date Range" P3["df[df['date'].between('2024-01-01', '2024-12-31')]"] end subgraph "Null-Aware Filtering" P4["# Non-null AND condition

fields.title: Pandas Data Type Management
fields.description: Master astype(), dtype optimization, nullable types, and memory-efficient data handling

steps.title: Pandas Data Types Overview
steps.explanation: Pandas has many data types. Choosing the right one affects memory usage and performance.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Numeric Types" N1["int64 - Integer (8 bytes)"] N2["int32/int16/int8 - Smaller ints"] N3["float64 - Decimal (8 bytes)"] N4["float32/float16 - Smaller floats"] end subgraph "Text Types" T1["object - Any Python object"] T2["string - Pandas string type"] T3["category - Categorical"] end subgraph "Other Types" O1["bool - True/False"] O2["datetime64 - Dates/times"] O3["timedelta64 - Time differences"] end subgraph "Nullable Types (new)" NL["Int64 - Nullable integer
Float64 - Nullable float
boolean - Nullable bool
string - Nullable string"] end style N1 fill:#ADD8E6 style T3 fill:#90EE90 style NL fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Checking Data Types
steps.explanation:
Always inspect dtypes before processing.

```python
# Check all dtypes
df.dtypes

# Memory usage
df.info(memory_usage='deep')

# Check specific column
df['col'].dtype
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "df.dtypes" DT["name object
age int64
salary float64

active bool"] end subgraph "df.info()" Info["
5 columns, 1000 rows
Column Dtype Memory
name object 64KB
age int64 8KB
...
Total: 120KB"] end subgraph "Memory Inspection" Mem["df.memory_usage(deep=True)

Returns bytes per column
deep=True for accurate object size"] end style DT fill:#ADD8E6 style Info fill:#90EE90

steps.title: astype() - Convert Data Types
steps.explanation:
**astype()** converts column types.

```python
# Convert single column
df['age'] = df['age'].astype('int32')

# Convert multiple columns
df = df.astype({'age': 'int32', 'price': 'float32'})

# Convert with errors handling
df['num'] = pd.to_numeric(df['num'], errors='coerce')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Basic Conversion" B1["df['col'].astype('int64')"] B2["df['col'].astype('float32')"] B3["df['col'].astype('str')"] B4["df['col'].astype('category')"] end subgraph "Multiple Columns" MC["df.astype({
'age': 'int32',
'price': 'float32',
'name': 'string'
})"] end subgraph "Handling Errors" E1["# astype fails on bad values
df['num'].astype('int') # Error if 'abc'

# Use to_numeric for safety
pd.to_numeric(df['num'], errors='coerce')
→ Invalid becomes NaN"] end style MC fill:#90EE90 style E1 fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Category Type - Memory Saver
steps.explanation:
**category** type dramatically reduces memory for low-cardinality columns.

```python
# Convert to category
df['status'] = df['status'].astype('category')

# Check memory savings
df['status'].memory_usage(deep=True)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Object Type (Before)" Obj["status column: 100,000 rows
Values: 'active', 'inactive', 'pending'

Each row stores full string
Memory: ~6MB"] end subgraph "Category Type (After)" Cat["Stores integer codes + lookup table
0 → 'active'
1 → 'inactive'
2 → 'pending'

Memory: ~100KB (60x smaller!)"] end subgraph "When to Use" When["✓ Repeated values (status, country)
✓ Low cardinality (< 50% unique)
✗ High cardinality (IDs, names)
✗ Frequently changing values"] end Obj -->|"astype('category')"| Cat style Obj fill:#FFB6C1 style Cat fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Downcasting Numeric Types
steps.explanation:
Use smaller numeric types when values fit.

```python
# Downcast integers
df['age'] = pd.to_numeric(df['age'], downcast='integer')

# Downcast floats
df['price'] = pd.to_numeric(df['price'], downcast='float')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Integer Ranges" I1["int8: -128 to 127"] I2["int16: -32,768 to 32,767"] I3["int32: -2B to 2B"] I4["int64: -9Q to 9Q"] end subgraph "Memory Per Type" M["int8: 1 byte
int16: 2 bytes
int32: 4 bytes
int64: 8 bytes

Age column (0-100)?
→ int8 is enough!"] end subgraph "Auto-Downcast" AD["pd.to_numeric(df['col'], downcast='integer')

Automatically picks smallest type
that fits the data"] end subgraph "Example Savings" Ex["1M rows × age (0-100)
int64: 8MB
int8: 1MB
Savings: 87%!"] end style M fill:#ADD8E6 style Ex fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Nullable Integer Types
steps.explanation:
Traditional int64 can't hold NaN. Use nullable types (capital letter).

```python
# Nullable integer
df['id'] = df['id'].astype('Int64')  # Note capital I

# Can now have NaN in integer column
df['id'] = pd.array([1, 2, None, 4], dtype='Int64')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Traditional Problem" Prob["int64 + NaN → becomes float64!

[1, 2, NaN, 4]
→ [1.0, 2.0, NaN, 4.0]

Loses integer precision"] end subgraph "Nullable Types Solution" Sol["Int64 (capital I) can hold NaN

[1, 2, None, 4]
→ [1, 2, , 4]

Stays as integer!"] end subgraph "Nullable Types Available" Types["Int8, Int16, Int32, Int64
UInt8, UInt16, UInt32, UInt64
Float32, Float64
boolean, string"] end Prob -->|"astype('Int64')"| Sol style Prob fill:#FFB6C1 style Sol fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Memory Optimization Strategy
steps.explanation: Follow this process to optimize DataFrame memory usage.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB Start["Check df.info(memory_usage='deep')"] Start --> S1["1. Convert object → category
(for low cardinality strings)"] S1 --> S2["2. Downcast integers
(int64 → int32/16/8)"] S2 --> S3["3. Downcast floats
(float64 → float32)"] S3 --> S4["4. Parse dates
(object → datetime64)"] subgraph "Optimization Function" Func["def optimize_df(df):
for col in df.select_dtypes('object'):
if df[col].nunique() / len(df) < 0.5:
df[col] = df[col].astype('category')
for col in df.select_dtypes('integer'):
df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], downcast='integer')
return df"] end style S1 fill:#90EE90 style Func fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Pandas Duplicate Detection & Removal
fields.description: Find and handle duplicate rows with duplicated(), drop_duplicates(), and key-based uniqueness

steps.title: duplicated() - Find Duplicates
steps.explanation:
**duplicated()** returns a boolean Series marking duplicate rows.

```python
# Find duplicates (all columns)
df.duplicated()

# Find duplicates by specific columns
df.duplicated(subset=['name', 'email'])
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original DataFrame" DF["| id | name | email |
|----|-------|-----------------|

| 2 | Bob | bob@mail.com |
| 3 | Alice | alice@mail.com |← duplicate
| 4 | Carol | carol@mail.com |"] end subgraph "df.duplicated()" Dup["[False, False, True, False]

First occurrence = False
Subsequent = True"] end subgraph "Filter Duplicates" Filter["df[df.duplicated()]
→ Shows duplicate rows

df[~df.duplicated()]
→ Shows unique rows"] end DF --> Dup style Dup fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: keep Parameter
steps.explanation:
Control which duplicate to mark.

```python
# Mark all except first (default)
df.duplicated(keep='first')

# Mark all except last
df.duplicated(keep='last')

# Mark ALL duplicates (including first)
df.duplicated(keep=False)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Original Data" DF["Row 0: Alice

Row 3: Alice ← same as row 0"] end subgraph "keep='first' (default)" First["[False, False, True, True]

Row 0 kept (first Alice)"] end subgraph "keep='last'" Last["[True, False, True, False]

Row 3 kept (last Alice)"] end subgraph "keep=False" All["[True, False, True, True]

ALL Alices marked as duplicates"] end DF --> First DF --> Last DF --> All style First fill:#90EE90 style All fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: drop_duplicates() - Remove Duplicates

```python
# Keep last instead of first
df.drop_duplicates(keep='last')
```

| Alice | alice@mail.com |

fields.title: Pandas Apply & Custom Functions
fields.description: Learn apply(), map(), applymap(), and when to use vectorization instead

steps.title: The Apply Family
steps.explanation:
Pandas has several ways to apply functions:

- **apply()** - on rows/columns
- **map()** - element-wise on Series
- **applymap()** - element-wise on DataFrame
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Series.apply()" SA["Apply function to each element
df['col'].apply(func)"] end subgraph "DataFrame.apply()" DA["Apply function to each row/column
df.apply(func, axis=0) # columns
df.apply(func, axis=1) # rows"] end subgraph "Series.map()" SM["Element-wise mapping
df['col'].map(func_or_dict)"] end subgraph "DataFrame.applymap()" DAM["Element-wise on all cells
df.applymap(func)"] end style DA fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Series.apply() - Element-wise
steps.explanation:
Apply function to each element in a Series.

```python
# Lambda function
df['upper'] = df['name'].apply(lambda x: x.upper())

# Custom function
def clean_name(name):
    return name.strip().title()
df['clean'] = df['name'].apply(clean_name)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Input Series" Input["df['name']
['alice', 'bob', 'carol']"] end subgraph "apply(lambda x: x.upper())" Apply["For each element:
'alice' → 'ALICE'
'bob' → 'BOB'
'carol' → 'CAROL'"] end subgraph "Result" Result["['ALICE', 'BOB', 'CAROL']"] end Input --> Apply --> Result style Apply fill:#ADD8E6 style Result fill:#90EE90

steps.title: DataFrame.apply() - Row/Column
steps.explanation:
Apply function to entire rows or columns.

```python
# Apply to each column (axis=0)
df.apply(lambda col: col.max())

# Apply to each row (axis=1)
df.apply(lambda row: row['a'] + row['b'], axis=1)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "DataFrame" DF["| A | B | C |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 7 |
| 2 | 5 | 8 |
| 3 | 6 | 9 |"] end subgraph "axis=0 (default) - Per Column" Col["func([1,2,3]) → result for A
func([4,5,6]) → result for B
func([7,8,9]) → result for C"] end subgraph "axis=1 - Per Row" Row["func([1,4,7]) → result for row 0
func([2,5,8]) → result for row 1
func([3,6,9]) → result for row 2"] end DF -->|"axis=0"| Col DF -->|"axis=1"| Row style Col fill:#ADD8E6 style Row fill:#90EE90

steps.title: map() - Mapping Values
steps.explanation:
**map()** replaces values using a dict or function.

```python
# Dictionary mapping
df['grade'] = df['score'].map({90: 'A', 80: 'B', 70: 'C'})

# Function mapping
df['grade'] = df['score'].map(lambda x: 'Pass' if x >= 60 else 'Fail')
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Dictionary Mapping" Dict["mapping = {'NY': 'New York', 'CA': 'California'}
df['state'].map(mapping)

'NY' → 'New York'
'CA' → 'California'
'TX' → NaN (not in dict)"] end subgraph "Function Mapping" Func["df['category'].map(str.upper)

Same as apply() for Series"] end subgraph "map() vs apply()" Vs["map() - Can use dict or function
apply() - Only function

For dict lookups, use map()!"] end style Dict fill:#90EE90 style Vs fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Vectorization vs Apply
steps.explanation: **Vectorized operations are MUCH faster** than apply. Always prefer them!
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "BAD: Using Apply" Bad["# Slow - Python loop
df['total'] = df.apply(
lambda row: row['a'] + row['b'],
axis=1
)

Time: 1000ms"] end subgraph "GOOD: Vectorized" Good["# Fast - NumPy operation
df['total'] = df['a'] + df['b']

Time: 1ms (1000x faster!)"] end subgraph "When to Use Apply" When["Use apply ONLY when:
• Complex logic (multiple conditions)
• External function calls
• No vectorized equivalent

Try vectorized first!"] end style Bad fill:#FFB6C1 style Good fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Common Vectorized Alternatives
steps.explanation: Replace apply with these faster options.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Instead of apply() for conditions" C1["# Bad
df.apply(lambda r: 'A' if r['score']>90 else 'B', axis=1)

# Good - np.where
np.where(df['score'] > 90, 'A', 'B')

# Good - np.select for multiple
np.select([cond1, cond2], ['A', 'B'], default='C')"] end subgraph "Instead of apply() for math" M1["# Bad
df['x'].apply(lambda x: x**2)

# Good
df['x'] ** 2"] end subgraph "Instead of apply() for strings" S1["# Bad
df['name'].apply(str.upper)

# Good
df['name'].str.upper()"] end style C1 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Complex Apply Examples
steps.explanation: When you really need apply, do it right.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Multiple Columns Output" MC["def extract_info(row):
return pd.Series({
'first': row['name'].split()[0],
'last': row['name'].split()[-1],
'len': len(row['name'])
})

df[['first','last','len']] = df.apply(extract_info, axis=1)"] end subgraph "With Try/Except" TE["def safe_parse(val):
try:
return float(val)
except:
return np.nan

df['num'] = df['raw'].apply(safe_parse)"] end subgraph "Performance Tip" Tip["# If you must use apply,
# use raw=True for speed
df.apply(lambda x: x.sum(), axis=1, raw=True)"] end style MC fill:#ADD8E6 style Tip fill:#FFFACD

fields.title: Pandas Data Quality Patterns
fields.description: Master data validation, assertions, quality checks, and production-ready data pipelines

steps.title: Why Data Quality Matters
steps.explanation: Bad data in = bad predictions out. Data quality checks catch issues early.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Without Quality Checks" Bad1["Raw Data"] --> Bad2["Model Training"] Bad2 --> Bad3["Silent Failures
Wrong Predictions
Production Issues"] end subgraph "With Quality Checks" Good1["Raw Data"] --> Check["Quality Checks
Validation
Assertions"] Check -->|"Pass"| Good2["Model Training"] Check -->|"Fail"| Alert["Alert & Fix
Before Damage"] end style Bad3 fill:#FFB6C1 style Check fill:#90EE90 style Alert fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Basic Quality Checks
steps.explanation:
Essential checks for any DataFrame.

```python
# Check shape
assert len(df) > 0, 'Empty DataFrame!'

# Check for nulls
assert df['id'].notna().all(), 'Null IDs found!'

# Check for duplicates
assert not df['id'].duplicated().any(), 'Duplicate IDs!'
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Shape Checks"
        S1["len(df) > 0  # Not empty"]
        S2["len(df) == expected_rows"]
        S3["len(df.columns) == expected_cols"]
    end

    subgraph "Null Checks"
        N1["df.isna().sum()  # Count nulls"]
        N2["df['col'].notna().all()  # No nulls"]
        N3["df.dropna().shape == df.shape  # No nulls anywhere"]
    end

    subgraph "Duplicate Checks"
        D1["~df.duplicated().any()  # No dup rows"]
        D2["df['id'].nunique() == len(df)  # Unique IDs"]
    end

    subgraph "Data Type Checks"
        T1["df['id'].dtype == 'int64'"]
        T2["pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(df['col'])"]
    end

    style S1 fill:#90EE90
    style N2 fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Value Range Validation
steps.explanation:
Ensure values fall within expected ranges.

```python
# Numeric ranges
assert (df['age'] >= 0).all(), 'Negative ages!'
assert (df['age'] <= 120).all(), 'Unrealistic ages!'

# Categorical values
valid_statuses = {'active', 'inactive', 'pending'}
assert df['status'].isin(valid_statuses).all()
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Numeric Ranges" NR["# Age must be 0-120
assert df['age'].between(0, 120).all()

# Price must be positive
assert (df['price'] > 0).all()

# Percentage must be 0-100
assert df['pct'].between(0, 100).all()"] end subgraph "Categorical Values" CV["valid = ['A', 'B', 'C']
assert df['grade'].isin(valid).all()

# Find invalid values
invalid = df[~df['grade'].isin(valid)]"] end subgraph "String Patterns" SP["# Email format
pattern = r'^[\w.-]+@[\w.-]+\.\w+$'
assert df['email'].str.match(pattern).all()

# Phone format
assert df['phone'].str.match(r'^\d{10}$').all()"] end style NR fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Cross-Column Validation
steps.explanation:
Check relationships between columns.

```python
# Date order
assert (df['end_date'] >= df['start_date']).all()

# Sum constraint
assert np.isclose(df[['a','b','c']].sum(axis=1), 1.0).all()

# Conditional requirement
mask = df['status'] == 'shipped'
assert df.loc[mask, 'ship_date'].notna().all()
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Date Relationships" DR["# End after start
assert (df['end'] >= df['start']).all()

# Birth before hire
assert (df['hire_date'] > df['birth_date']).all()"] end subgraph "Numeric Relationships" NR["# Total equals sum
assert (df['a'] + df['b'] == df['total']).all()

# Percentages sum to 100
pct_cols = ['pct_a', 'pct_b', 'pct_c']
assert np.isclose(df[pct_cols].sum(axis=1), 100).all()"] end subgraph "Conditional Requirements" CR["# If shipped, must have ship_date
shipped = df['status'] == 'shipped'
assert df.loc[shipped, 'ship_date'].notna().all()

# If paid, amount must be positive
paid = df['status'] == 'paid'
assert (df.loc[paid, 'amount'] > 0).all()"] end style CR fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Creating a Validation Function
steps.explanation: Encapsulate checks in reusable functions.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Validation Function" VF["def validate_orders(df):
errors = []

if df.empty:
errors.append('Empty DataFrame')

if df['order_id'].duplicated().any():
errors.append('Duplicate order IDs')

if (df['amount'] < 0).any():
errors.append('Negative amounts')

if not df['status'].isin(['pending','complete']).all():
errors.append('Invalid status values')

if errors:
raise ValueError(f'Validation failed: {errors}')

return True"] end subgraph "Usage" U["try:
validate_orders(df)
process_orders(df)
except ValueError as e:
log.error(e)
send_alert(e)"] end style VF fill:#ADD8E6 style U fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Schema Validation with Pandera
steps.explanation:
Use **pandera** for declarative schema validation.

```python
import pandera as pa

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    'id': pa.Column(int, unique=True),
    'age': pa.Column(int, pa.Check.between(0, 120)),
    'email': pa.Column(str, pa.Check.str_matches(r'.+@.+')),
})

validated_df = schema.validate(df)
```
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Define Schema" Schema["schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
'id': pa.Column(int,
checks=pa.Check.greater_than(0),
unique=True),
'name': pa.Column(str,
nullable=False),
'score': pa.Column(float,
checks=pa.Check.between(0, 100)),
})"] end subgraph "Validate" Val["# Raises SchemaError if invalid
validated_df = schema.validate(df)

# Or as decorator
@pa.check_input(schema)
def process(df):
..."] end subgraph "Benefits" Ben["• Declarative & readable
• Detailed error messages
• Type checking
• Can generate fake data"] end style Schema fill:#ADD8E6 style Ben fill:#FFFACD

steps.title: Data Profiling
steps.explanation: Generate reports to understand data quality issues.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Quick Profile" QP["# Basic stats
df.describe(include='all')

# Missing value report
df.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

# Value counts
df['status'].value_counts(dropna=False)"] end subgraph "Custom Profile Function" CP["def profile(df):
return pd.DataFrame({
'dtype': df.dtypes,
'nulls': df.isna().sum(),
'null_pct': df.isna().mean() * 100,
'unique': df.nunique(),
'sample': df.iloc[0]
})"] end subgraph "Libraries" Lib["# ydata-profiling (pandas-profiling)
from ydata_profiling import ProfileReport
ProfileReport(df).to_file('report.html')

# Great Expectations
# Enterprise-grade validation"] end style CP fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Pipeline Integration
steps.explanation: Integrate quality checks into data pipelines.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Quality Gate Pattern" QG["def pipeline(raw_data):
# 1. Load
df = pd.read_csv(raw_data)

# 2. Validate raw
validate_raw_schema(df)

# 3. Transform
df = clean_data(df)
df = engineer_features(df)

# 4. Validate transformed
validate_output_schema(df)

# 5. Output
return df"] end subgraph "Monitoring" Mon["# Log quality metrics
log.info(f'Rows: {len(df)}')
log.info(f'Null rate: {df.isna().mean().mean():.2%}')
log.info(f'Duplicates: {df.duplicated().sum()}')

# Alert on anomalies
if len(df) < expected * 0.9:
send_alert('Row count too low!')"] end style QG fill:#90EE90 style Mon fill:#ADD8E6

fields.title: Path Anatomy & Components
fields.description: Understanding the parts of a Path object: anchor, parent, name, stem, suffix, and more

steps.title: A Path Has Many Parts
steps.explanation: Every `Path` object can be decomposed into logical components. Understanding these parts is essential for manipulating paths correctly.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Example Path"
        P["/home/user/project/data/report.csv.gz"]
    end
    subgraph "We'll Learn"
        C1["anchor - root of the path"]
        C2["parent/parents - directories above"]
        C3["name - final component"]
        C4["stem - name without suffix"]
        C5["suffix/suffixes - file extensions"]
        C6["parts - tuple of all components"]
    end
    P --> C1
    P --> C2
    P --> C3
    P --> C4
    P --> C5
    P --> C6
    style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The Anchor
steps.explanation: The **anchor** is the root of the path. On Unix it's `/`, on Windows it could be `C:\\`. For relative paths, the anchor is empty string.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Unix Paths"
        U1["Path('/home/user/file.txt')"]
        U1A["anchor = '/'"]
        U1 --> U1A
    end
    subgraph "Windows Paths"
        W1["Path('C:/Users/file.txt')"]
        W1A["anchor = 'C:\\'"]
        W1 --> W1A
    end
    subgraph "Relative Paths"
        R1["Path('data/file.txt')"]
        R1A["anchor = '' (empty)"]
        R1 --> R1A
    end
    style U1A fill:#C8E6C9
    style W1A fill:#C8E6C9
    style R1A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Parent and Parents
steps.explanation: `.parent` returns the immediate parent directory. `.parents` returns an immutable sequence of all ancestors, from immediate parent to the root.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Path: /home/user/project/file.txt"
        P["Path('/home/user/project/file.txt')"]
    end
    subgraph ".parent"
        PA["Path('/home/user/project')"]
    end
    subgraph ".parents (sequence)"
        P0["parents[0] = '/home/user/project'"]
        P1["parents[1] = '/home/user'"]
        P2["parents[2] = '/home'"]
        P3["parents[3] = '/'"]
    end
    P --> PA
    PA --> P0
    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    style PA fill:#C8E6C9
    style P0 fill:#BBDEFB
    style P1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style P2 fill:#BBDEFB
    style P3 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Name, Stem, and Suffix
steps.explanation: `.name` is the final component. `.stem` is the name without the last suffix. `.suffix` is the last extension (with dot). `.suffixes` returns ALL extensions as a list.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Path: /data/report.csv.gz" P["report.csv.gz"] end subgraph "Components" N[".name = 'report.csv.gz'
(full filename)"] ST[".stem = 'report.csv'
(name minus last suffix)"] SU[".suffix = '.gz'
(last extension only)"] SUS[".suffixes = ['.csv', '.gz']
(ALL extensions)"] end P --> N P --> ST P --> SU P --> SUS style N fill:#E1BEE7 style ST fill:#C8E6C9 style SU fill:#FFCDD2 style SUS fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Parts - The Complete Breakdown
steps.explanation: `.parts` returns a tuple of ALL path components, starting with the anchor. This is useful for iteration or indexed access.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Path: /home/user/project/file.txt"
        P["Path('/home/user/project/file.txt')"]
    end
    subgraph ".parts tuple"
        T["('/', 'home', 'user', 'project', 'file.txt')"]
        I0["parts[0] = '/'"]
        I1["parts[1] = 'home'"]
        I2["parts[2] = 'user'"]
        I3["parts[3] = 'project'"]
        I4["parts[4] = 'file.txt'"]
    end
    P --> T
    T --> I0
    T --> I1
    T --> I2
    T --> I3
    T --> I4
    style T fill:#C8E6C9
    style I0 fill:#FFCDD2
    style I4 fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Visual Summary
steps.explanation: Here's how all components map to a single path. Remember: **stem** + **suffix** = **name**, and the **anchor** is the root.
steps.diagram_data:
graph LR
    subgraph "Path: /home/user/data/report.csv.gz"
        A["/ "]
        B["home"]
        C["user"]
        D["data"]
        E["report"]
        F[".csv"]
        G[".gz"]
    end
    A ---|"anchor"| A
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    D ---|"parent"| D
    E ---|"stem"| E
    G ---|"suffix"| G
    F ---|"suffixes[0]"| F
    style A fill:#FFCDD2
    style D fill:#BBDEFB
    style E fill:#C8E6C9
    style F fill:#FFE0B2
    style G fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Code Example
steps.explanation: Here's Python code demonstrating all the path components in action.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Code"
        C1["from pathlib import Path"]
        C2["p = Path('/home/user/data/report.csv.gz')"]
        C3["p.anchor    # '/'"]
        C4["p.parent    # Path('/home/user/data')"]
        C5["p.name      # 'report.csv.gz'"]
        C6["p.stem      # 'report.csv'"]
        C7["p.suffix    # '.gz'"]
        C8["p.suffixes  # ['.csv', '.gz']"]
        C9["p.parts     # ('/', 'home', 'user', 'data', 'report.csv.gz')"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7 --> C8 --> C9
    style C1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style C2 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Path Resolution Flow
fields.description: How resolve() transforms relative paths to absolute, handles symlinks, and normalizes ./ and ../

steps.title: What Does resolve() Do?
steps.explanation: `resolve()` makes a path **absolute** and **canonical**. It resolves `.` (current), `..` (parent), and follows symlinks by default.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB subgraph "Input Path" I["./data/../config/settings.yaml"] end subgraph "resolve() does" R1["1. Make absolute
(prepend cwd)"] R2["2. Normalize dots
(remove ./ and ../)"] R3["3. Follow symlinks
