        S2["Beam Search: track top-k sequences"]
        S3["Sampling: random from distribution"]
    end
    L --> P
    P --> S1
    P --> S2
    P --> S3
    style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Greedy Decoding
steps.explanation: Always pick the highest probability token. Simple but can produce repetitive or suboptimal text.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Greedy"
        P["Probs: the 0.4, a 0.3, an 0.2"]
        G["Pick: the highest"]
        N["Repeat for next token"]
    end
    subgraph "Properties"
        PR1["+ Fast, deterministic"]
        PR2["- Locally optimal not globally"]
        PR3["- Often repetitive"]
    end
    P --> G --> N
    style PR3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Beam Search
steps.explanation: Track top-k sequences (beams) at each step. Explore multiple paths to find higher probability sequences.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Beam Search k=2"
        START["start"]
        B1["The 0.4"]
        B2["A 0.3"]
        B1C1["The cat 0.3"]
        B1C2["The dog 0.2"]
        B2C1["A cat 0.25"]
    end
    START --> B1
    START --> B2
    B1 --> B1C1
    B1 --> B1C2
    B2 --> B2C1
    subgraph "Keep Top-2"
        K["The cat, A cat"]
    end
    style K fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Temperature Sampling
steps.explanation: Divide logits by temperature T before softmax. T<1 sharpens (confident), T>1 flattens (diverse).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Temperature Effect"
        L["Logits: 2.0, 1.0, 0.5"]
        T0["T=0.5: 0.73, 0.20, 0.07 sharper"]
        T1["T=1.0: 0.51, 0.31, 0.18 normal"]
        T2["T=2.0: 0.39, 0.33, 0.28 flatter"]
    end
    subgraph "Usage"
        U1["Low T: More deterministic"]
        U2["High T: More creative/random"]
    end
    L --> T0
    L --> T1
    L --> T2
    style T0 fill:#BBDEFB
    style T2 fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Top-K Sampling
steps.explanation: Only sample from the top K most likely tokens. Prevents sampling rare, nonsensical tokens.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Top-K k=3"
        ALL["All tokens: 0.4, 0.3, 0.15, 0.08, 0.05, 0.02"]
        TOP["Keep top 3: 0.4, 0.3, 0.15"]
        NORM["Renormalize: 0.47, 0.35, 0.18"]
        SAMP["Sample from these 3"]
    end
    ALL --> TOP --> NORM --> SAMP
    subgraph "Effect"
        E["Excludes unlikely tokens"]
    end
    style TOP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Top-P Nucleus Sampling
steps.explanation: Sample from smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability >= p. Adapts to distribution shape.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Top-P p=0.9"
        ALL["Sorted probs: 0.5, 0.25, 0.12, 0.08, 0.03, 0.02"]
        CUM["Cumulative: 0.5, 0.75, 0.87, 0.95, ..."]
        SEL["Select until >=0.9: first 4 tokens"]
        NORM["Renormalize and sample"]
    end
    ALL --> CUM --> SEL --> NORM
    subgraph "Advantage"
        A["Adapts to peaked vs flat distributions"]
    end
    style SEL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Common Configurations
steps.explanation: Typical parameter combinations for different use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Deterministic code, factual"
        D["temperature=0 or greedy"]
        D2["Or beam_search with num_beams=4"]
    end
    subgraph "Balanced general chat"
        B["temperature=0.7"]
        B2["top_p=0.9"]
    end
    subgraph "Creative stories, brainstorm"
        C["temperature=1.0-1.2"]
        C2["top_p=0.95, top_k=50"]
    end
    style D fill:#BBDEFB
    style B fill:#C8E6C9
    style C fill:#FFE0B2

fields.title: Rotary Position Embeddings RoPE
fields.description: Understanding RoPE: rotation-based positional encoding used in LLaMA, GPT-NeoX, and modern LLMs

steps.title: Why Position Embeddings?
steps.explanation: Attention is permutation-invariant - it doesn't know token order. We must inject position information somehow.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "The Problem"
        P1["cat sat mat vs mat sat cat"]
        P2["Same tokens, different meaning"]
        P3["Attention treats them identically!"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Absolute: Add position embedding"]
        S2["Relative: Encode distance in attention"]
        S3["RoPE: Rotate queries and keys"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> S1
    P3 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Absolute vs Relative Position
steps.explanation: Absolute adds fixed embeddings per position. Relative encodes distance between tokens in attention scores.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Absolute Original Transformer"
        A1["pos_embed i added to token i"]
        A2["Fixed for each position"]
        A3["Hard to extrapolate beyond training length"]
    end
    subgraph "Relative"
        R1["Encode pos_i - pos_j in attention"]
        R2["Focus on distance, not absolute position"]
        R3["Better generalization"]
    end
    style A3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style R3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: RoPE Core Idea
steps.explanation: RoPE rotates query and key vectors based on position. The dot product then naturally encodes relative position.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Key Insight"
        I1["Rotate Q at position m by angle m*theta"]
        I2["Rotate K at position n by angle n*theta"]
        I3["Q dot K depends on m-n times theta"]
        I4["Relative position emerges automatically!"]
    end
    I1 --> I3
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> I4
    style I4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: 2D Rotation Visualization
steps.explanation: For each pair of dimensions, apply 2D rotation by angle theta*position. Different dimension pairs use different theta.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "2D Rotation"
        V["Vector x, y"]
        R["Rotate by theta"]
        VR["x*cos theta - y*sin theta, x*sin theta + y*cos theta"]
    end
    subgraph "RoPE applies this"
        P1["Position 0: no rotation"]
        P2["Position 1: rotate by theta"]
        P3["Position 2: rotate by 2*theta"]
        PN["Position n: rotate by n*theta"]
    end
    V --> R --> VR
    style VR fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Multi-Frequency Rotations
steps.explanation: Different dimension pairs rotate at different frequencies. Low dims: fast rotation (local). High dims: slow (global).
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Dimension Pairs"
        D1["Dims 0,1: theta_0 fastest"]
        D2["Dims 2,3: theta_1"]
        D3["Dims 4,5: theta_2"]
        DN["Dims d-2,d-1: theta_last slowest"]
    end
    subgraph "Frequencies"
        F["theta_i = 10000^-2i/d"]
        F1["Low i: high frequency local patterns"]
        F2["High i: low frequency global patterns"]
    end
    D1 --> F
    DN --> F
    style F fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: RoPE Advantages
steps.explanation: RoPE is efficient, naturally relative, and can extrapolate to longer sequences than seen in training.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Advantages"
        A1["+ Relative position naturally"]
        A2["+ No extra parameters to learn"]
        A3["+ Efficient element-wise"]
        A4["+ Some length extrapolation"]
    end
    subgraph "Used In"
        U1["LLaMA / LLaMA-2"]
        U2["GPT-NeoX"]
        U3["Falcon"]
        U4["Mistral"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Length Extrapolation
steps.explanation: RoPE can extend to longer sequences via position interpolation or NTK-aware scaling.
steps.diagram_data:
graph TB
    subgraph "Problem"
        P["Trained on 2K context"]
        P2["Want to use 8K context"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Position Interpolation: scale positions"]
        S2["NTK-aware: adjust base frequency"]
        S3["YaRN: combines multiple techniques"]
    end
    subgraph "Result"
        R["Extend context 4-8x beyond training"]
    end
    P --> P2 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    P2 --> S3
    S1 --> R
    S2 --> R
    S3 --> R
    style R fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Medallion Architecture: Bronze, Silver, Gold
fields.description: The lakehouse data organization pattern with progressive data refinement layers

steps.title: Raw Data Ingestion (Bronze)
steps.explanation: Bronze layer stores raw data exactly as received from source systems. No transformations - just append-only ingestion with metadata.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Sources S1["APIs"] S2["Databases"] S3["Files"] S4["Streams"] end subgraph Bronze["Bronze Layer"] B["Raw Data
- Exact copy of source
- Append-only
- Full history
- Metadata added"] end S1 --> B S2 --> B S3 --> B S4 --> B style Bronze fill:#CD7F32

steps.title: Cleaned Data (Silver)
steps.explanation: Silver layer contains cleaned, validated, and deduplicated data. Schema enforcement, data quality checks, and standardization happen here.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Bronze["Bronze Layer"] B["Raw Data"] end subgraph Silver["Silver Layer"] S["Cleaned Data
- Deduplicated
- Schema enforced
- Quality validated
- Standardized"] end subgraph Transforms["Transformations"] T1["Deduplication"] T2["Schema validation"] T3["Data type casting"] T4["Null handling"] end B --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> S style Bronze fill:#CD7F32 style Silver fill:#C0C0C0

steps.title: Business-Ready Data (Gold)
steps.explanation: Gold layer contains aggregated, business-level data optimized for analytics and reporting. Often organized by business domain.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Silver["Silver Layer"]
        S["Cleaned Data"]
    end
    subgraph Gold["Gold Layer"]
        G1["Sales Analytics"]
        G2["Customer 360"]
        G3["Financial Reports"]
        G4["ML Features"]
    end
    S --> G1
    S --> G2
    S --> G3
    S --> G4
    style Silver fill:#C0C0C0
    style Gold fill:#FFD700

steps.title: Complete Medallion Flow
steps.explanation: Data flows progressively through layers, with each layer adding more value. Delta Lake enables ACID transactions and time travel at every layer.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Sources["Data Sources"] S1["APIs"] S2["DBs"] S3["Files"] end subgraph Bronze["Bronze - Raw"] B["Append-only
Full history
Schema on read"] end subgraph Silver["Silver - Cleaned"] SV["Validated
Deduplicated
Conformed"] end subgraph Gold["Gold - Business"] G1["Aggregates"] G2["Features"] G3["Reports"] end subgraph Consumers["Consumers"] C1["BI Tools"] C2["ML Models"] C3["Apps"] end S1 & S2 & S3 --> Bronze Bronze --> Silver Silver --> Gold G1 & G2 & G3 --> Consumers style Bronze fill:#CD7F32 style Silver fill:#C0C0C0 style Gold fill:#FFD700

fields.title: Delta Lake Architecture
fields.description: How Delta Lake provides ACID transactions on top of cloud object storage

steps.title: Delta Table Structure
steps.explanation: A Delta table consists of Parquet data files plus a transaction log (_delta_log). The log tracks all changes to the table.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph DeltaTable["Delta Table"]
        subgraph Log["_delta_log/"]
            L1["00000.json"]
            L2["00001.json"]
            L3["00002.json"]
            L4["checkpoint"]
        end
        subgraph Data["Data Files"]
            D1["part-0001.parquet"]
            D2["part-0002.parquet"]
            D3["part-0003.parquet"]
        end
    end
    Log --> |"tracks"| Data
    style Log fill:#BBDEFB
    style Data fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Transaction Log (Delta Log)
steps.explanation: The _delta_log contains JSON files with actions: add file, remove file, metadata changes, etc. Checkpoints speed up log replay.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Log["_delta_log/"]
        subgraph V0["00000.json"]
            A1["ADD file1.parquet"]
            A2["ADD file2.parquet"]
        end
        subgraph V1["00001.json"]
            B1["REMOVE file1.parquet"]
            B2["ADD file3.parquet"]
        end
        subgraph V2["00002.json"]
            C1["ADD file4.parquet"]
        end
        subgraph CP["checkpoint"]
            CP1["Snapshot of state"]
        end
    end
    V0 --> V1 --> V2
    V2 -.-> CP
    style CP fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Write Operations
steps.explanation: Writes create new Parquet files and append entries to the transaction log. Optimistic concurrency handles conflicts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Write["Write Operation"]
        W1["1. Write new Parquet files"]
        W2["2. Create log entry"]
        W3["3. Atomic commit"]
    end
    subgraph Conflict["Conflict Resolution"]
        C1["Read current version"]
        C2["Check conflicts"]
        C3["Retry if needed"]
    end
    W1 --> W2 --> W3
    W3 --> |"conflict?"| Conflict
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> W1
    style Write fill:#C8E6C9
    style Conflict fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Read Operations
steps.explanation: Reads reconstruct table state by replaying the log from the latest checkpoint. Only active files are read.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Read["Read Operation"] R1["Find latest checkpoint"] R2["Replay log entries"] R3["Build file list"] R4["Read Parquet files"] end subgraph State["Table State"] S["Active Files:
file2.parquet
file3.parquet
file4.parquet"] end R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 R3 --> State style Read fill:#BBDEFB style State fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Delta Lake ACID Transactions
fields.description: How Delta Lake achieves Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability

steps.title: Atomicity
steps.explanation: All changes in a transaction either fully complete or fully fail. The commit to the log is atomic - partial writes are never visible.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Transaction["Transaction"] T1["Write file1.parquet"] T2["Write file2.parquet"] T3["Update log atomically"] end subgraph Success["Success Case"] S["Both files visible"] end subgraph Failure["Failure Case"] F["Neither file visible
Orphaned files cleaned up"] end T1 --> T2 --> T3 T3 --> |"commit succeeds"| Success T3 --> |"commit fails"| Failure style Success fill:#C8E6C9 style Failure fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Consistency
steps.explanation: Schema enforcement ensures data quality. Constraints and invariants are checked before commit.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Write["Incoming Data"]
        W["New records"]
    end
    subgraph Checks["Consistency Checks"]
        C1["Schema validation"]
        C2["NOT NULL constraints"]
        C3["CHECK constraints"]
        C4["Type enforcement"]
    end
    subgraph Result["Result"]
        R1["Valid: Commit"]
        R2["Invalid: Reject"]
    end
    W --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    C4 --> |"pass"| R1
    C4 --> |"fail"| R2
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style R2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Isolation
steps.explanation: Snapshot isolation - each reader sees a consistent snapshot. Writers use optimistic concurrency control.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Table["Delta Table v5"]
        T["Current State"]
    end
    subgraph Reader1["Reader 1"]
        R1["Reads v5 snapshot"]
    end
    subgraph Reader2["Reader 2"]
        R2["Reads v5 snapshot"]
    end
    subgraph Writer["Writer"]
        W1["Reads v5"]
        W2["Writes changes"]
        W3["Commits v6"]
    end
    Table --> R1
    Table --> R2
    Table --> W1 --> W2 --> W3
    R1 -.-> |"still sees v5"| R1
    style Reader1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style Reader2 fill:#BBDEFB
    style Writer fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Durability
steps.explanation: Once committed, data is durable in cloud storage. The transaction log ensures recovery after failures.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Commit["After Commit"]
        C1["Log entry written to cloud storage"]
        C2["Data files written to cloud storage"]
        C3["Replication by cloud provider"]
    end
    subgraph Recovery["After Failure"]
        R1["Read transaction log"]
        R2["Reconstruct table state"]
        R3["Clean orphaned files"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 --> |"system restart"| R1
    R1 --> R2 --> R3
    style Commit fill:#C8E6C9
    style Recovery fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Databricks Cluster Architecture
fields.description: How Databricks clusters work with driver nodes, workers, and executors

steps.title: Cluster Components
steps.explanation: A cluster has one Driver node (coordinates work) and multiple Worker nodes (execute tasks). Each worker runs Executor processes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cluster["Databricks Cluster"]
        subgraph Driver["Driver Node"]
            D1["SparkContext"]
            D2["DAG Scheduler"]
            D3["Task Scheduler"]
        end
        subgraph Workers["Worker Nodes"]
            subgraph W1["Worker 1"]
                E1["Executor"]
                E2["Executor"]
            end
            subgraph W2["Worker 2"]
                E3["Executor"]
                E4["Executor"]
            end
        end
    end
    Driver --> |"schedules tasks"| Workers
    style Driver fill:#BBDEFB
    style Workers fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Job Execution Flow
steps.explanation: User code runs on the Driver, which creates a DAG of stages. Stages are split into tasks distributed to Executors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph User["User Code"]
        U["df.filter().groupBy().count()"]
    end
    subgraph Driver["Driver"]
        D1["Parse to DAG"]
        D2["Optimize plan"]
        D3["Create tasks"]
    end
    subgraph Executors["Executors"]
        E1["Task 1"]
        E2["Task 2"]
        E3["Task 3"]
        E4["Task 4"]
    end
    U --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1 & E2 & E3 & E4
    style Driver fill:#BBDEFB
    style Executors fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Cluster Types
steps.explanation: All-Purpose clusters are interactive (notebooks). Job clusters are ephemeral (created per job). SQL Warehouses are for BI queries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Types["Cluster Types"]
        subgraph AP["All-Purpose"]
            AP1["Interactive notebooks"]
            AP2["Development"]
            AP3["Shared by users"]
            AP4["Long-running"]
        end
        subgraph Job["Job Clusters"]
            J1["Automated jobs"]
            J2["Created per run"]
            J3["Terminated after"]
            J4["Cost-efficient"]
        end
        subgraph SQL["SQL Warehouse"]
            S1["BI queries"]
            S2["Serverless option"]
            S3["Auto-scaling"]
            S4["SQL-only"]
        end
    end
    style AP fill:#BBDEFB
    style Job fill:#C8E6C9
    style SQL fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Autoscaling
steps.explanation: Clusters can autoscale between min and max workers based on load. Workers are added when tasks queue up, removed when idle.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Autoscale["Autoscaling Cluster"]
        subgraph Min["Min: 2 workers"]
            M1["Worker 1"]
            M2["Worker 2"]
        end
        subgraph Scale["Scale Up"]
            S1["Tasks queuing"]
            S2["Add workers"]
        end
        subgraph Max["Max: 8 workers"]
            X1["Worker 1-8"]
        end
        subgraph Down["Scale Down"]
            D1["Workers idle"]
            D2["Remove workers"]
        end
    end
    Min --> |"high load"| Scale --> Max
    Max --> |"low load"| Down --> Min
    style Min fill:#C8E6C9
    style Max fill:#FFE082

fields.title: Unity Catalog: Data Governance Hierarchy
fields.description: The three-level namespace for organizing and securing data assets

steps.title: Three-Level Namespace
steps.explanation: Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: Catalog > Schema > Object. This replaces the old hive_metastore.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph UC["Unity Catalog"]
        subgraph Cat["Catalog"]
            C["prod_catalog"]
        end
        subgraph Schema["Schema"]
            S1["sales"]
            S2["marketing"]
        end
        subgraph Objects["Objects"]
            T1["customers table"]
            T2["orders table"]
            V1["revenue_view"]
            F1["udf_function"]
        end
    end
    Cat --> Schema
    S1 --> T1 & T2
    S2 --> V1 & F1
    style Cat fill:#BBDEFB
    style Schema fill:#C8E6C9
    style Objects fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Metastore
steps.explanation: A Metastore is the top-level container that holds catalogs. It maps to a cloud storage location and is shared across workspaces.

Storage: s3://unity-metastore/"] subgraph Catalogs["Catalogs"] C1["prod"] C2["dev"] C3["sandbox"] end end subgraph Workspaces["Workspaces"] W1["Workspace A"] W2["Workspace B"] end M --> Catalogs Metastore --> W1 Metastore --> W2 style Metastore fill:#E1BEE7 style Workspaces fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Securable Objects

GRANT USE"] SCH["SCHEMA sales
inherits USE"] TBL["TABLE customers
inherits USE"] end subgraph Objects["Securable Objects"] O1["Tables"] O2["Views"] O3["Volumes"] O4["Functions"] O5["Models"] O6["Connections"] end CAT --> |"inherits"| SCH --> |"inherits"| TBL style Hierarchy fill:#C8E6C9 style Objects fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Delta Live Tables: Declarative ETL Pipelines
fields.description: How DLT simplifies ETL with declarative table definitions and automatic dependency management

steps.title: DLT Pipeline Concept
steps.explanation: DLT lets you declare WHAT you want (tables and transformations), not HOW to execute. The engine handles orchestration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Traditional["Traditional ETL"]
        T1["Write ingestion code"]
        T2["Handle dependencies"]
        T3["Manage retries"]
        T4["Track lineage"]

    subgraph DLT["Delta Live Tables"]
        D1["Declare tables"]
        D2["Define transforms"]
        D3["Engine handles rest"]
    end
    Traditional --> |"replaced by"| DLT
    style Traditional fill:#FFCDD2
    style DLT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Table Types
steps.explanation: Streaming tables process incremental data. Materialized views recompute on each refresh. Live tables are always up-to-date.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Types["DLT Table Types"]
        subgraph ST["Streaming Table"]
            ST1["@dlt.table"]
            ST2["Incremental processing"]
            ST3["Append-only sources"]
        end
        subgraph MV["Materialized View"]
            MV1["@dlt.table"]
            MV2["Full recompute"]
            MV3["Aggregations"]
        end
        subgraph LT["Live Table"]
            LT1["Always current"]
            LT2["Auto-refreshed"]
        end
    end
    style ST fill:#BBDEFB
    style MV fill:#C8E6C9
    style LT fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Data Quality with Expectations
steps.explanation: Expectations are data quality rules. You can warn, drop invalid records, or fail the pipeline based on violations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Expectations["Data Quality Expectations"]
        E1["@dlt.expect valid_id IS NOT NULL"]
        E2["@dlt.expect_or_drop positive_amount > 0"]
        E3["@dlt.expect_or_fail valid_date IS NOT NULL"]
    end
    subgraph Actions["On Violation"]
        A1["WARN: Log and continue"]
        A2["DROP: Remove bad records"]
        A3["FAIL: Stop pipeline"]
    end
    E1 --> A1
    E2 --> A2
    E3 --> A3
    style A1 fill:#FFE082
    style A2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style A3 fill:#F44336

steps.title: Complete Pipeline Example
steps.explanation: A typical pipeline: ingest raw data (Bronze), clean and validate (Silver), aggregate for analytics (Gold). Dependencies are automatic.

flowchart LR subgraph Bronze["Bronze"] B["raw_events
STREAMING TABLE
from cloud_files"] end subgraph Silver["Silver"] S["clean_events
STREAMING TABLE
with expectations"] end subgraph Gold["Gold"] G1["hourly_stats
MATERIALIZED VIEW"] G2["daily_summary
MATERIALIZED VIEW"] end Bronze --> Silver Silver --> G1 Silver --> G2 style Bronze fill:#CD7F32 style Silver fill:#C0C0C0 style Gold fill:#FFD700

fields.title: Delta Lake Time Travel & Versioning
fields.description: Access and restore previous versions of your data using Delta Lake's built-in versioning

steps.title: Version History
steps.explanation: Every change to a Delta table creates a new version. The transaction log stores the complete history of all versions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph History["Table History"] V0["v0: Initial load
1000 rows"] V1["v1: INSERT
+500 rows"] V2["v2: UPDATE
modified 50"] V3["v3: DELETE
-100 rows"] V4["v4: Current
1350 rows"] end V0 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 style V4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Query by Version
steps.explanation: Access any historical version using VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF. Great for debugging or reproducing analyses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Queries["Time Travel Queries"] Q1["SELECT * FROM table
VERSION AS OF 2"] Q2["SELECT * FROM table
TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15'"] Q3["SELECT * FROM table@v2"] end subgraph Results["Returns"] R["Data as it existed
at that point in time"] end Q1 & Q2 & Q3 --> R style Queries fill:#BBDEFB style Results fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: RESTORE Command
steps.explanation: RESTORE reverts the table to a previous version. This creates a NEW version that matches the old state - history is preserved.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Before["Before RESTORE"] B1["v0"] --> B2["v1"] --> B3["v2 - good"] B3 --> B4["v3 - bad data"] end subgraph Command["Command"] C["RESTORE TABLE t
TO VERSION AS OF 2"] end subgraph After["After RESTORE"] A1["v0"] --> A2["v1"] --> A3["v2"] A3 --> A4["v3"] --> A5["v4 = v2"] end Before --> Command --> After style A5 fill:#C8E6C9 style B4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Retention & Vacuum
steps.explanation: VACUUM removes old data files. Default retention is 7 days. Time travel only works for versions within retention period.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Retention["Data Retention"] R1["Default: 7 days"] R2["Configurable per table"] R3["Files older than retention
eligible for VACUUM"] end subgraph Vacuum["VACUUM Command"] V1["VACUUM table"] V2["Removes old files"] V3["Frees storage"] end subgraph Warning["Warning"] W["Cannot time travel
to vacuumed versions"] end R1 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 V3 --> Warning style Warning fill:#FFCDD2 style Retention fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Z-Ordering & Data Skipping
fields.description: Optimize query performance by colocating related data and enabling efficient file pruning

steps.title: Data Skipping Basics
steps.explanation: Delta Lake tracks min/max statistics for each file. Queries skip files that cannot contain matching data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Stats["File Statistics"] F1["file1.parquet
date: 2024-01-01 to 01-15
region: US"] F2["file2.parquet
date: 2024-01-16 to 01-31
region: EU"] F3["file3.parquet
date: 2024-02-01 to 02-15
region: US"] end subgraph Query["Query: date = 2024-01-20"] Q["Check file stats"] end subgraph Result["Result"] R1["Skip file1 - max < query"] R2["Read file2 - matches"] R3["Skip file3 - min > query"] end Query --> Stats F1 --> R1 F2 --> R2 F3 --> R3 style R1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R2 fill:#FFE082 style R3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: The Problem: Scattered Data
steps.explanation: Without optimization, related data is scattered across files. Queries must read many files even if few rows match.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before["Before Z-Ordering"]
        subgraph F1["File 1"]
            A1["US, 2024-01"]
            A2["EU, 2024-03"]
            A3["US, 2024-02"]
        end
        subgraph F2["File 2"]
            B1["EU, 2024-01"]
            B2["US, 2024-03"]
            B3["EU, 2024-02"]
        end
    end
    subgraph Query["Query: region=US"]
        Q["Must read ALL files"]
    end
    Before --> Query
    style Before fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Z-Ordering Solution
steps.explanation: OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER colocates similar values together. Data with same region/date ends up in the same files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Command["Command"] C["OPTIMIZE table
ZORDER BY (region, date)"] end subgraph After["After Z-Ordering"] subgraph F1["File 1"] A1["US, 2024-01"] A2["US, 2024-02"] A3["US, 2024-03"] end subgraph F2["File 2"] B1["EU, 2024-01"] B2["EU, 2024-02"] B3["EU, 2024-03"] end end subgraph Query["Query: region=US"] Q["Read only File 1"] end Command --> After After --> Query style After fill:#C8E6C9 style Query fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Z-ORDER on high-cardinality columns used in filters. Limit to 2-4 columns. Run OPTIMIZE regularly after writes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Good["Good Z-Order Candidates"]
        G1["High cardinality"]
        G2["Frequently filtered"]
        G3["date, region, customer_id"]
    end
    subgraph Bad["Poor Candidates"]
        B1["Low cardinality - boolean"]
        B2["Random distribution"]
        B3["Columns not in filters"]
    end
    subgraph Tips["Best Practices"]
        T1["2-4 columns max"]
        T2["Most filtered first"]
        T3["Run after large writes"]
        T4["Consider partitioning too"]
    end
    style Good fill:#C8E6C9
    style Bad fill:#FFCDD2
    style Tips fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Class Instantiation Flow
fields.description: Understand __new__ → __init__ → instance creation process

steps.title: The Instantiation Process
steps.explanation: When you call `MyClass()`, Python doesn't directly create an instance. Instead, it goes through a two-phase process: **allocation** (`__new__`) and **initialization** (`__init__`). Most developers only know about `__init__`, but `__new__` is called first.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    A["MyClass()"] --> B["__new__"]
    B --> C["__init__"]
    C --> D["instance"]
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style B fill:#FFF3E0
    style C fill:#E8F5E9
    style D fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Step 1: __new__ - Object Allocation
steps.explanation: `__new__` is a **static method** (though you don't need to declare it as such) that actually creates the instance. It receives the class as the first argument and must return an instance. If it doesn't return an instance of the class, `__init__` won't be called.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__new__(cls, *args, **kwargs)" A["Receives class as first arg"] B["Allocates memory for instance"] C["Returns new instance"] end A --> B --> C subgraph Code D["def __new__(cls):
instance = super().__new__(cls)
return instance"] end style C fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Step 2: __init__ - Object Initialization
steps.explanation: `__init__` receives the **already-created instance** as `self`. It initializes attributes but doesn't return anything (returns `None`). If `__new__` returned a different type, `__init__` is skipped.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)" A["Receives instance as self"] B["Sets instance attributes"] C["Returns None (implicit)"] end A --> B --> C subgraph Code D["def __init__(self, name):
self.name = name
# No return!"] end style B fill:#90EE90

steps.title: Full Flow with Arguments
steps.explanation: When you call `Person('Alice', 30)`, both `__new__` and `__init__` receive the same arguments. `__new__` gets `cls` + args, `__init__` gets `self` + same args.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant type
    participant __new__
    participant __init__
    participant Instance

    Caller->>type: Person('Alice', 30)
    type->>__new__: cls=Person, 'Alice', 30
    __new__->>type: new instance
    type->>__init__: self=instance, 'Alice', 30
    __init__->>Instance: self.name='Alice', self.age=30
    Instance-->>Caller: <Person object>

steps.title: When to Override __new__
steps.explanation: `__new__` is rarely overridden, but it's essential for: **immutable types** (str, int, tuple), **singletons**, **metaclasses**, and **factory patterns**. For mutable classes, `__init__` is usually sufficient.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Override __new__ for" A["Immutable types
(can't modify in __init__)"] B["Singleton pattern"] C["Metaclass customization"] D["Controlling instance creation"] end subgraph "Use __init__ for" E["Setting attributes"] F["Normal initialization"] G["Most classes"] end style A fill:#FFF3E0 style B fill:#FFF3E0 style C fill:#FFF3E0 style D fill:#FFF3E0 style E fill:#E8F5E9 style F fill:#E8F5E9 style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Singleton Pattern Example
steps.explanation: The singleton pattern ensures only one instance exists. Override `__new__` to check if an instance already exists and return it instead of creating a new one.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Singleton Implementation" Code["class Singleton:
_instance = None

def __new__(cls):
if cls._instance is None:
cls._instance = super().__new__(cls)
return cls._instance"] end subgraph Flow A["Singleton()"] --> B{_instance exists?} B -->|No| C["Create new instance"] B -->|Yes| D["Return existing"] C --> E["Store in _instance"] E --> F["Return instance"] D --> F end style C fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#ADD8E6

steps.title: Immutable Type Example
steps.explanation: For immutable types like custom integers, you must set the value in `__new__` because by the time `__init__` runs, the object is already immutable and can't be changed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Custom Immutable Integer" Code["class PositiveInt(int):
def __new__(cls, value):
if value < 0:
raise ValueError('Must be positive')
return super().__new__(cls, value)

x = PositiveInt(42) # Works
y = PositiveInt(-1) # ValueError"] end subgraph "Why __new__?" A["int is immutable"] B["Value must be set at creation"] C["__init__ is too late"] end A --> B --> C style A fill:#FFCDD2 style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Complete Picture
steps.explanation: The full instantiation involves the **metaclass** calling `__call__`, which orchestrates `__new__` and `__init__`. This is why overriding `__call__` in a metaclass gives you complete control over instantiation.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Metaclass.__call__
    participant Class.__new__
    participant Class.__init__
    participant Instance

    User->>Metaclass.__call__: MyClass(args)
    Metaclass.__call__->>Class.__new__: cls, args
    Class.__new__-->>Metaclass.__call__: instance
    alt instance is of type cls
        Metaclass.__call__->>Class.__init__: instance, args
        Class.__init__-->>Metaclass.__call__: None
    end
    Metaclass.__call__-->>User: instance

fields.title: Method Resolution Order (MRO)
fields.description: Understand diamond inheritance and C3 linearization

steps.title: What is MRO?
steps.explanation: **Method Resolution Order (MRO)** is the order in which Python searches for methods in a class hierarchy. With multiple inheritance, this becomes complex. Python uses the **C3 linearization algorithm** to create a consistent, predictable order.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Question" Q["class D(B, C): pass
d.method() - Which method?"] end subgraph "MRO Answers" A["D - B - C - A - object"] end Q --> A style Q fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Simple Inheritance MRO
steps.explanation: For single inheritance, MRO is straightforward: child - parent - grandparent - ... - object. You can view any class's MRO with `ClassName.__mro__` or `ClassName.mro()`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hierarchy
        A["object"]
        B["Animal"]
        C["Dog"]
    end
    A --> B --> C
    subgraph "Dog.__mro__"
        M["(Dog, Animal, object)"]
    end
    C -.-> M
    style M fill:#90EE90

steps.title: The Diamond Problem
steps.explanation: The **diamond problem** occurs when a class inherits from two classes that share a common ancestor. Without proper ordering, methods could be called multiple times or in the wrong order.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB A["A (grandparent)"] B["B(A)"] C["C(A)"] D["D(B, C)"] A --> B A --> C B --> D C --> D subgraph "The Problem" Q["If D calls super()...
Does A get called twice?
In what order?"] end style A fill:#FFE082 style D fill:#E3F2FD style Q fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: C3 Linearization Rules
steps.explanation: Python's C3 algorithm guarantees: 1) **Children before parents** 2) **Left-to-right order preserved** 3) **Each class appears once** 4) **Monotonicity** (if X precedes Y in one MRO, X precedes Y in all subclass MROs).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "C3 Rules"
        R1["1. Children before parents"]
        R2["2. Left-to-right order of bases"]
        R3["3. Each class appears once"]
        R4["4. Preserve parent MROs"]
    end
    subgraph "For class D(B, C)"
        E1["D comes first"]
        E2["B before C (left-to-right)"]
        E3["A appears once at end"]
    end
    R1 --> E1
    R2 --> E2
    R3 --> E3
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Diamond MRO Example
steps.explanation: For the diamond `D(B, C)` where both B and C inherit from A, the MRO is `D - B - C - A - object`. Notice A appears **once**, after both B and C, ensuring proper cooperative super() calls.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hierarchy
        A["A"]
        B["B(A)"]
        C["C(A)"]
        D["D(B, C)"]
    end
    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> D
    subgraph "D.__mro__"
        M1["D"] --> M2["B"] --> M3["C"] --> M4["A"] --> M5["object"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style M2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style M3 fill:#FFF3E0
    style M4 fill:#FFE082
    style M5 fill:#E0E0E0

steps.title: super() and MRO
steps.explanation: `super()` doesn't just call the parent class—it calls the **next class in MRO**. This enables **cooperative multiple inheritance** where each class in the chain gets a chance to run.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant D
    participant B
    participant C
    participant A

    Note over D,A: D(B, C) with MRO: D-B-C-A
    D->>B: super().__init__()
    Note over B: B's super() - C (not A!)
    B->>C: super().__init__()
    Note over C: C's super() - A
    C->>A: super().__init__()
    A->>A: object.__init__()

steps.title: Cooperative Inheritance Pattern
steps.explanation: For proper cooperative inheritance, every class should: 1) Call `super().__init__()` 2) Accept `**kwargs` and pass them along 3) Not assume which class comes next in MRO.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correct Pattern" Code["class B(A):
def __init__(self, x, **kwargs):
self.x = x
super().__init__(**kwargs)

class C(A):
def __init__(self, y, **kwargs):
self.y = y
super().__init__(**kwargs)

class D(B, C):
def __init__(self, **kwargs):
super().__init__(**kwargs)"] end subgraph "Key Points" K1["Use **kwargs"] K2["Always call super()"] K3["Pass kwargs along"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style K1 fill:#E3F2FD style K2 fill:#E3F2FD style K3 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: MRO Conflicts
steps.explanation: Some inheritance hierarchies have no valid MRO. Python raises `TypeError` when C3 linearization fails. This happens when the inheritance order creates contradictory requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Invalid MRO" A["class A: pass"] B["class B: pass"] C["class C(A, B): pass"] D["class D(B, A): pass"] E["class E(C, D): pass # TypeError!"] end subgraph "Why?" W["C says: A before B
D says: B before A
Contradiction!"] end E --> W style E fill:#FFCDD2 style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Viewing MRO
steps.explanation: You can inspect MRO using `__mro__` (tuple) or `mro()` (list). Understanding MRO is crucial for debugging multiple inheritance and using `super()` correctly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Inspecting MRO" Code[">>> D.__mro__
(D, B, C, A, object)

>>> D.mro()
[D, B, C, A, object]

>>> for cls in D.__mro__:
... print(cls.__name__)"] end subgraph "Pro Tips" T1["Print MRO when debugging"] T2["Check before complex inheritance"] T3["Use help(D) to see MRO"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style T1 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Descriptor Protocol
fields.description: How __get__, __set__, __delete__ work under the hood

steps.title: What Are Descriptors?
steps.explanation: **Descriptors** are objects that customize attribute access. They power `@property`, `@classmethod`, `@staticmethod`, and many ORM fields. Any object with `__get__`, `__set__`, or `__delete__` methods is a descriptor.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Descriptor Protocol Methods"
        G["__get__(self, obj, type)"]
        S["__set__(self, obj, value)"]
        D["__delete__(self, obj)"]
    end
    subgraph "Powers"
        P1["@property"]
        P2["@classmethod"]
        P3["@staticmethod"]
        P4["Django ORM fields"]
        P5["SQLAlchemy columns"]
    end
    G --> P1
    G --> P2
    G --> P3
    G --> P4
    S --> P4
    style G fill:#E8F5E9
    style S fill:#FFF3E0
    style D fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Data vs Non-Data Descriptors
steps.explanation: **Data descriptors** have `__set__` or `__delete__`. **Non-data descriptors** only have `__get__`. This distinction affects the lookup order—data descriptors have higher priority than instance attributes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Data Descriptor"
        DD["Has __set__ or __delete__"]
        DDE["Takes priority over instance dict"]
        DDP["Examples: property, Django fields"]
    end
    subgraph "Non-Data Descriptor"
        ND["Only has __get__"]
        NDE["Instance dict takes priority"]
        NDP["Examples: functions, staticmethod"]
    end
    DD --> DDE --> DDP
    ND --> NDE --> NDP
    style DD fill:#E8F5E9
    style ND fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Attribute Lookup Order
steps.explanation: When you access `obj.attr`, Python follows this order: 1) **Data descriptors** in class 2) **Instance `__dict__`** 3) **Non-data descriptors** in class 4) **Class `__dict__`** 5) `__getattr__` if defined.

steps.title: Simple Descriptor Example
steps.explanation: Here's a simple descriptor that logs attribute access. The descriptor is defined as a class attribute, and its `__get__` method is called whenever we access that attribute on an instance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Descriptor Class" Code1["class Verbose:
def __get__(self, obj, type):
print(f'Getting from {obj}')
return obj._value

def __set__(self, obj, value):
print(f'Setting to {value}')
obj._value = value"] end subgraph "Using It" Code2["class MyClass:
attr = Verbose() # Descriptor!

m = MyClass()
m.attr = 42 # Calls __set__
print(m.attr) # Calls __get__"] end Code1 --> Code2 style Code1 fill:#E8F5E9 style Code2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: __get__ Parameters
steps.explanation: `__get__(self, obj, type)` receives: **self** = descriptor instance, **obj** = instance (None if class access), **type** = owner class. This lets you behave differently for class vs instance access.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Class
    participant Descriptor

    Note over User,Descriptor: Instance access: obj.attr
    User->>Class: instance.attr
    Class->>Descriptor: __get__(descriptor, instance, MyClass)
    Descriptor-->>User: computed value

    Note over User,Descriptor: Class access: MyClass.attr
    User->>Class: MyClass.attr
    Class->>Descriptor: __get__(descriptor, None, MyClass)
    Descriptor-->>User: descriptor itself or other

steps.title: Implementing @property
steps.explanation: `@property` is just a data descriptor! It stores the getter, setter, and deleter functions and calls them when the attribute is accessed, set, or deleted.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "property Implementation" Code["class property:
def __init__(self, fget=None, fset=None, fdel=None):
self.fget = fget
self.fset = fset
self.fdel = fdel

def __get__(self, obj, type):
if obj is None:
return self
return self.fget(obj)

def __set__(self, obj, value):
self.fset(obj, value)"] end subgraph "Usage" Use["@property # Creates property descriptor
def name(self): return self._name

@name.setter # Adds fset
def name(self, value): self._name = value"] end Code --> Use style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Validated Attribute Descriptor
steps.explanation: A common use case: create reusable validation logic. This descriptor ensures values meet certain criteria whenever they're set.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Validated Descriptor" Code["class PositiveNumber:
def __set_name__(self, owner, name):
self.name = name # Auto-set attr name!

def __get__(self, obj, type):
return obj.__dict__.get(self.name)

def __set__(self, obj, value):
if value < 0:
raise ValueError('Must be positive')
obj.__dict__[self.name] = value"] end subgraph "Usage" Use["class Account:
balance = PositiveNumber()

a = Account()
a.balance = 100 # OK
a.balance = -50 # ValueError!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style Use fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: __set_name__ Hook
steps.explanation: `__set_name__(self, owner, name)` is called automatically when the descriptor is assigned to a class attribute. It tells the descriptor what name it was assigned to—no more string duplication!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Class Definition" A["class MyClass:
attr = MyDescriptor()"] end subgraph "Python Calls" B["MyDescriptor.__set_name__(
self=descriptor,
owner=MyClass,
name='attr'
)"] end A --> B subgraph "Benefit" C["Descriptor knows its name
without explicit passing!"] end B --> C style B fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Functions Are Descriptors!
steps.explanation: Functions are non-data descriptors. Their `__get__` method returns a **bound method** when accessed on an instance, or the function itself when accessed on the class. This is how methods work!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Function's __get__" A["class MyClass:
def greet(self):
print('Hello')"] B["MyClass.greet - function"] C["obj.greet - bound method"] end subgraph "How It Works" D["function.__get__(None, MyClass)"] E["- returns function"] F["function.__get__(obj, MyClass)"] G["- returns method(obj)"] end A --> B A --> C B --> D --> E C --> F --> G style E fill:#E3F2FD style G fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Property vs Attribute Access
fields.description: Getter/setter chain and @property decorator internals

steps.title: Direct Attribute vs Property
steps.explanation: Python allows direct attribute access unlike Java's mandatory getters/setters. However, `@property` lets you add logic later **without changing the interface**—clients still use `obj.attr` syntax.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Direct Attribute" A1["obj.name = 'Alice'"] A2["print(obj.name)"] end subgraph "Property (same syntax!)" B1["obj.name = 'Alice' # calls setter"] B2["print(obj.name) # calls getter"] end subgraph Benefit C["Change implementation
without changing interface"] end A1 --> C B1 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Basic @property Usage
steps.explanation: The `@property` decorator creates a descriptor that calls your getter method. The decorated method becomes the getter, and its name becomes the attribute name.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" Code["class Circle:
def __init__(self, radius):
self._radius = radius

@property
def radius(self):
'''The radius property getter'''
return self._radius"] end subgraph "What Happens" A["Circle.radius = property(getter_func)"] B["c.radius - calls getter_func(c)"] end Code --> A --> B style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Adding a Setter
steps.explanation: Use `@propertyname.setter` to add a setter. This creates a new property object with both getter and setter. The setter receives the value being assigned.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" Code["class Circle:
@property
def radius(self):
return self._radius

@radius.setter
def radius(self, value):
if value < 0:
raise ValueError('Negative!')
self._radius = value"] end subgraph "Now You Can" A["c.radius = 5 # calls setter"] B["setter validates value"] C["stores in _radius"] end Code --> A --> B --> C style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Adding a Deleter
steps.explanation: Use `@propertyname.deleter` to define behavior for `del obj.attr`. This is less common but useful for cleanup logic.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Full Property" Code["@property
def data(self):
return self._data

@data.setter
def data(self, value):
self._data = value

@data.deleter
def data(self):
print('Cleaning up...')
del self._data"] end subgraph "Operations" G["obj.data - getter"] S["obj.data = x - setter"] D["del obj.data - deleter"] end style G fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFF3E0 style D fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Property Access Flow
steps.explanation: When you access a property, Python's descriptor protocol takes over. The property's `__get__` method calls your getter function with the instance.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Python
    participant Property
    participant Getter

    User->>Python: obj.radius
    Python->>Python: Check for data descriptor
    Python->>Property: __get__(obj, Circle)
    Property->>Getter: getter(obj)
    Getter->>Getter: return self._radius
    Getter-->>Property: 5
    Property-->>User: 5

steps.title: Computed Properties
steps.explanation: Properties shine for **computed attributes**—values calculated from other attributes. No storage needed, always up-to-date, same clean syntax.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Computed Property" Code["class Circle:
def __init__(self, radius):
self.radius = radius

@property
def area(self):
return 3.14159 * self.radius ** 2

@property
def circumference(self):
return 2 * 3.14159 * self.radius"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["No storage needed"] B2["Always consistent"] B3["c.area reads naturally"] end Code --> B1 Code --> B2 Code --> B3 style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Cached Properties
steps.explanation: For expensive computations, use `@functools.cached_property`. It computes once and stores the result in the instance's `__dict__`, so subsequent accesses skip the computation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "cached_property" Code["from functools import cached_property

class Data:
@cached_property
def analysis(self):
print('Computing...') # Only once!
return expensive_computation()"] end subgraph "Flow" A["d.analysis - Computing..."] --> B["Result stored in d.__dict__"] B --> C["d.analysis - Returns stored value"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Property vs __slots__
steps.explanation: Properties work with `__slots__` but you must include the backing attribute (e.g., `_radius`). The property itself doesn't need a slot—it's a class attribute.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With __slots__" Code["class Circle:
__slots__ = ('_radius',) # Backing store

@property
def radius(self):
return self._radius

@radius.setter
def radius(self, value):
self._radius = value # Stored in slot"] end subgraph "Key Point" K["Property is class attribute
Backing store is in __slots__"] end Code --> K style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Read-Only Properties
steps.explanation: Omit the setter to create a read-only property. Attempting to set it raises `AttributeError`. This is a clean way to expose internal state without allowing modification.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Read-Only" Code["class User:
def __init__(self, id):
self._id = id

@property
def id(self):
return self._id # No setter!"] end subgraph "Result" A["u = User(123)"] B["u.id - 123 OK"] C["u.id = 456 - AttributeError"] end Code --> A --> B A --> C style B fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Dunder Method Dispatch
fields.description: Operator overloading flow (__add__, __eq__, etc.)

steps.title: What Are Dunder Methods?
steps.explanation: **Dunder (double underscore) methods** like `__add__`, `__eq__`, `__str__` let you define how operators and built-in functions work with your objects. They're Python's operator overloading mechanism.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Operator to Dunder"
        A["a + b"] --> A1["a.__add__(b)"]
        B["a == b"] --> B1["a.__eq__(b)"]
        C["len(a)"] --> C1["a.__len__()"]
        D["str(a)"] --> D1["a.__str__()"]
        E["a[i]"] --> E1["a.__getitem__(i)"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style E1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Binary Operator Dispatch
steps.explanation: For `a + b`, Python first tries `a.__add__(b)`. If that returns `NotImplemented`, it tries `b.__radd__(a)`. The 'r' prefix means 'reflected' or 'right-hand'.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB A["a + b"] --> B["Try a.__add__(b)"] B --> C{Returns
NotImplemented?} C -->|No| D["Return result"] C -->|Yes| E["Try b.__radd__(a)"] E --> F{Returns
NotImplemented?} F -->|No| G["Return result"] F -->|Yes| H["TypeError"] style D fill:#E8F5E9 style G fill:#E8F5E9 style H fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Implementing __add__ and __radd__
steps.explanation: Return `NotImplemented` (not raise!) when you can't handle the operation. This signals Python to try the reflected method on the other operand.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Vector Class" Code["class Vector:
def __init__(self, x, y):
self.x, self.y = x, y

def __add__(self, other):
if isinstance(other, Vector):
return Vector(self.x + other.x,
self.y + other.y)
return NotImplemented # Not raise!

def __radd__(self, other):
return self.__add__(other)"] end subgraph "Usage" U1["v1 + v2 - __add__"] U2["5 + v1 - int.__add__ fails"] U3[" - v1.__radd__(5)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Comparison Operators
steps.explanation: Comparison operators have reflected pairs: `__lt__`/`__gt__`, `__le__`/`__ge__`, `__eq__`/`__eq__`. Python swaps and reflects if the first method returns `NotImplemented`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Comparison Pairs" A["a < b"] --> A1["a.__lt__(b) or b.__gt__(a)"] B["a <= b"] --> B1["a.__le__(b) or b.__ge__(a)"] C["a == b"] --> C1["a.__eq__(b) or b.__eq__(a)"] D["a != b"] --> D1["a.__ne__(b) or not a.__eq__(b)"] end subgraph "Tip" T["Use @functools.total_ordering
to only implement __eq__ and one of
__lt__, __le__, __gt__, __ge__"] end style T fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Augmented Assignment
steps.explanation: `+=` uses `__iadd__` (in-place add) if defined, otherwise falls back to `__add__`. In-place methods should modify and return `self` for mutables, or return a new object for immutables.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB A["a += b"] --> B{Has __iadd__?} B -->|Yes| C["a = a.__iadd__(b)"] B -->|No| D["a = a.__add__(b)"] subgraph "Mutable (list)" M["def __iadd__(self, other):
self.extend(other)
return self # Same object"] end subgraph "Immutable (tuple)" I["No __iadd__
Uses __add__
Creates new object"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9 style D fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: __str__ vs __repr__
steps.explanation: `__str__` is for users (readable), `__repr__` is for developers (unambiguous). If `__str__` isn't defined, Python falls back to `__repr__`. Always define `__repr__`!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "When Called" A["print(obj)"] --> A1["__str__"] B["repr(obj)"] --> B1["__repr__"] C["f-string obj"] --> C1["__str__"] D["In REPL"] --> D1["__repr__"] end subgraph "Best Practice" Code["def __repr__(self):
return f'Point({self.x}, {self.y})'
# Ideally eval(repr(obj)) == obj

def __str__(self):
return f'Point at ({self.x}, {self.y})'"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Container Dunders
steps.explanation: Container operations use these dunders: `__len__` for `len()`, `__getitem__` for indexing, `__setitem__` for assignment, `__contains__` for `in` operator.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Container Protocol"
        A["len(c)"] --> A1["c.__len__()"]
        B["c[key]"] --> B1["c.__getitem__(key)"]
        C["c[key] = val"] --> C1["c.__setitem__(key, val)"]
        D["del c[key]"] --> D1["c.__delitem__(key)"]
        E["x in c"] --> E1["c.__contains__(x)"]
        F["for x in c"] --> F1["c.__iter__()"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style E1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style F1 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Callable Objects
steps.explanation: `__call__` makes instances callable like functions. This is used for function objects, decorators with state, and callable classes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__call__ Example" Code["class Counter:
def __init__(self):
self.count = 0

def __call__(self, increment=1):
self.count += increment
return self.count

counter = Counter()
counter() # 1
counter(5) # 6"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Decorators with state"] U2["Factory callables"] U3["Functor pattern"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Hash and Equality
steps.explanation: If you define `__eq__`, you make the class unhashable by default. To use instances as dict keys or in sets, also define `__hash__` consistently with `__eq__`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Rule" R["If a == b, then hash(a) == hash(b)
(but not vice versa)"] end subgraph "Implementation" Code["class Point:
def __init__(self, x, y):
self.x, self.y = x, y

def __eq__(self, other):
return (self.x, self.y) == (other.x, other.y)

def __hash__(self):
return hash((self.x, self.y))"] end subgraph "Warning" W["Mutable objects should NOT
be hashable (don't define __hash__)"] end R --> Code Code --> W style W fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Metaclass Chain
fields.description: type - metaclass - class - instance relationship

steps.title: Everything is an Object
steps.explanation: In Python, **everything is an object**, including classes. Classes are instances of **metaclasses**. The default metaclass is `type`. So `type` is a class that creates classes!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Chain" T["type (metaclass)"] C["MyClass (class)"] I["instance"] end T -->|"creates"| C C -->|"creates"| I subgraph "Check It" Check["type(instance) - MyClass
type(MyClass) - type
type(type) - type"] end style T fill:#FFE082 style C fill:#E8F5E9 style I fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Classes Are Instances of type
steps.explanation: When you write `class MyClass: pass`, Python calls `type('MyClass', bases, namespace)`. The `type` function is both a function and the default metaclass.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Equivalent" A["class MyClass:
x = 1"] B["MyClass = type(
'MyClass',
(object,),
{'x': 1}
)"] end A <-->|"Same result!"| B style A fill:#E8F5E9 style B fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Creating a Metaclass
steps.explanation: A metaclass is a class that inherits from `type`. Override `__new__` to customize class creation, `__init__` to customize class initialization, or `__call__` to customize instance creation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Metaclass Definition" Code["class MyMeta(type):
def __new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace):
print(f'Creating class {name}')
return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace)"] end subgraph "Using It" Use["class MyClass(metaclass=MyMeta):
pass

# Output: Creating class MyClass"] end Code --> Use style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Metaclass __new__ vs __init__
steps.explanation: `__new__` creates the class object (can modify name, bases, namespace). `__init__` initializes it after creation (can only work with the created class). Use `__new__` for most customizations.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram participant Python participant __new__ participant __init__ participant Class Python->>__new__: mcs, name, bases, namespace Note over __new__: Can modify namespace,
add/remove methods __new__->>Python: returns class object Python->>__init__: cls, name, bases, namespace Note over __init__: Class already exists,
can add attributes __init__->>Class: initialized class

steps.title: Metaclass Use Cases
steps.explanation: Metaclasses are powerful for: **registering classes** (ORMs, plugins), **validation** (ensure required methods), **transformation** (add logging to all methods), and **API enforcement**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Use Cases" A["Class Registration
(Django models, pytest plugins)"] B["Validation
(Abstract methods check)"] C["Transformation
(Auto-add methods)"] D["Singleton Pattern"] E["API Enforcement
(Required attributes)"] end M["Metaclass"] --> A M --> B M --> C M --> D M --> E style M fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Registry Pattern Example
steps.explanation: A common pattern: metaclass automatically registers all subclasses. This is how Django models, pytest fixtures, and plugin systems work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Registry Metaclass" Code["registry = {}

class RegisterMeta(type):
def __new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace):
cls = super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace)
if name != 'Base': # Skip base class
registry[name] = cls
return cls

class Base(metaclass=RegisterMeta):
pass

class Plugin1(Base): pass
class Plugin2(Base): pass

# registry = {'Plugin1': class, 'Plugin2': class}"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: __init_subclass__ Alternative
steps.explanation: Python 3.6+ offers `__init_subclass__` as a simpler alternative to metaclasses for many use cases. It's called on the **parent class** when a subclass is created.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Simpler Alternative" Code["class Base:
registry = {}

def __init_subclass__(cls, **kwargs):
super().__init_subclass__(**kwargs)
Base.registry[cls.__name__] = cls

class Plugin1(Base): pass
class Plugin2(Base): pass

# Base.registry = {'Plugin1': ..., 'Plugin2': ...}"] end subgraph "When to Use" W1["__init_subclass__: Simple registration, validation"] W2["Metaclass: Complex namespace manipulation"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style W1 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The Full Picture
steps.explanation: The complete instantiation chain: `type.__call__` - `metaclass.__new__` - `metaclass.__init__` creates the class. Then `metaclass.__call__` - `class.__new__` - `class.__init__` creates instances.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant type
    participant Metaclass
    participant Class
    participant Instance

    Note over User,Instance: Phase 1: Class Creation
    User->>type: class MyClass(metaclass=Meta)
    type->>Metaclass: __new__(mcs, name, bases, ns)
    Metaclass-->>type: class object
    type->>Metaclass: __init__(cls, name, bases, ns)

    Note over User,Instance: Phase 2: Instance Creation
    User->>Metaclass: MyClass(args)
    Metaclass->>Class: __new__(cls, args)
    Class-->>Metaclass: instance
    Metaclass->>Instance: __init__(self, args)

steps.title: Metaclass Inheritance
steps.explanation: Metaclasses are inherited. If a class's metaclass conflicts with its parent's metaclass, Python raises `TypeError`. The metaclass must be a subclass of all parent metaclasses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Valid" V["class Meta1(type): pass
class Meta2(Meta1): pass

class A(metaclass=Meta1): pass
class B(A, metaclass=Meta2): pass # OK!"] end subgraph "Invalid" I["class Meta1(type): pass
class Meta2(type): pass # Not related!

class A(metaclass=Meta1): pass
class B(A, metaclass=Meta2): pass # TypeError!"] end style V fill:#E8F5E9 style I fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: super() Resolution
fields.description: How super() walks the MRO

steps.title: What super() Really Does
steps.explanation: `super()` doesn't just call the parent class—it returns a **proxy object** that delegates method calls to the **next class in the MRO**. This enables cooperative multiple inheritance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Misconception" M["super() calls parent class"] end subgraph "Reality" R["super() calls next in MRO
(might not be parent!)"] end M -->|"Wrong!"| R style M fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: super() Syntax
steps.explanation: `super()` with no arguments (Python 3) automatically gets the class and instance. You can also use `super(ClassName, self)` explicitly, which is required in Python 2 and useful in some edge cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Python 3 (recommended)" A["class Child(Parent):
def method(self):
super().method()"] end subgraph "Explicit (Python 2 style)" B["class Child(Parent):
def method(self):
super(Child, self).method()"] end subgraph "What It Means" C["Start from Child in MRO
Find next class
Bind method to self"] end A --> C B --> C style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Single Inheritance Case
steps.explanation: With single inheritance, `super()` does call the parent. The MRO is linear: Child - Parent - Grandparent - object.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Child
    participant Parent
    participant Grandparent
    participant object

    Note over Child,object: MRO: Child - Parent - Grandparent - object

    Child->>Parent: super().method()
    Note over Parent: Next in MRO after Child
    Parent->>Grandparent: super().method()
    Note over Grandparent: Next in MRO after Parent
    Grandparent->>object: super().method()

steps.title: Multiple Inheritance - The Surprise
steps.explanation: With multiple inheritance, `super()` follows MRO, not the direct parent. In `D(B, C)` with MRO `D-B-C-A`, B's `super()` calls C, not A!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Diamond Hierarchy"
        A["A"]
        B["B(A)"]
        C["C(A)"]
        D["D(B, C)"]
    end
    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> D

    subgraph "MRO: D - B - C - A"
        M1["In B: super() - C"]
        M2["In C: super() - A"]
        M3["NOT: B.super() - A"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style M3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: super() Call Chain
steps.explanation: When D calls `super().__init__()`, it triggers a chain through the entire MRO. Each class's `super()` goes to the next class, ensuring every `__init__` runs exactly once.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant D
    participant B
    participant C
    participant A
    participant object

    Note over D,object: MRO: D - B - C - A - object

    D->>D: __init__()
    D->>B: super().__init__()
    B->>B: __init__()
    B->>C: super().__init__()
    Note over B,C: B's super() goes to C, not A!
    C->>C: __init__()
    C->>A: super().__init__()
    A->>A: __init__()
    A->>object: super().__init__()

steps.title: The Cooperative Pattern
steps.explanation: For `super()` to work correctly in multiple inheritance, all classes must: 1) Call `super()` 2) Accept and pass `**kwargs`. This is called **cooperative multiple inheritance**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correct Pattern" Code["class A:
def __init__(self, **kwargs):
super().__init__(**kwargs)

class B(A):
def __init__(self, x, **kwargs):
self.x = x
super().__init__(**kwargs)

class C(A):
def __init__(self, y, **kwargs):
self.y = y
super().__init__(**kwargs)

class D(B, C):
def __init__(self, **kwargs):
super().__init__(**kwargs)"] end subgraph "Key Points" K1["Always call super()"] K2["Use **kwargs"] K3["Pass kwargs along"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Breaking the Chain
steps.explanation: If any class in the MRO doesn't call `super()`, the chain breaks and later classes' methods won't be called. This is a common bug in multiple inheritance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Bug Example" Code["class B(A):
def __init__(self):
self.x = 1
# Forgot super()!

class C(A):
def __init__(self):
self.y = 2
super().__init__()

class D(B, C):
def __init__(self):
super().__init__()"] end subgraph "Result" R["D() - B.__init__() - STOP!
C.__init__() never called
A.__init__() never called"] end Code --> R style R fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: super() with Different Methods
steps.explanation: `super()` works with any method, not just `__init__`. Each method call goes to the next class in MRO that defines that method.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Method Lookup" A["super().method()"] B["Find current position in MRO"] C["Search forward for 'method'"] D["Call first match found"] end A --> B --> C --> D subgraph "Example" Code["class A:
def greet(self): print('A')

class B(A):
def greet(self):
print('B')
super().greet() # - A.greet"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: super() Without Instance
steps.explanation: `super(ClassName, ClassName)` creates an unbound super object for static/class methods. This is rare but useful for factory patterns and class method chains.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Class Method super()" Code["class Parent:
@classmethod
def create(cls):
return cls()

class Child(Parent):
@classmethod
def create(cls):
instance = super().create() # Works!
instance.setup()
return instance"] end subgraph "How It Works" W["super() in classmethod
automatically uses cls"] end Code --> W style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Slots vs __dict__
fields.description: Memory layout comparison

steps.title: Default: Instance __dict__
steps.explanation: By default, each Python instance has a `__dict__` dictionary storing its attributes. This is flexible (add any attribute) but uses more memory (dict overhead per instance).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Instance with __dict__" I["Point instance"] D["__dict__ = {
'x': 10,
'y': 20
}"] end I --> D subgraph "Characteristics" C1["Flexible - add any attr"] C2["More memory per instance"] C3["Slower attribute access"] end style D fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: __slots__: Fixed Attributes
steps.explanation: `__slots__` declares a fixed set of attributes. Python allocates space for these directly on the instance instead of using a dict. This saves memory and is slightly faster.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Instance with __slots__" Code["class Point:
__slots__ = ('x', 'y')

p = Point()
p.x = 10
p.y = 20"] end subgraph "Memory Layout" I["Point instance"] S["slot 'x': 10
slot 'y': 20"] end I --> S Code --> I subgraph "Characteristics" C1["Fixed attributes only"] C2["Less memory"] C3["Slightly faster access"] end style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Memory Savings
steps.explanation: For classes with many instances, `__slots__` can save significant memory. The dict overhead (~100-200 bytes per instance) is eliminated.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without __slots__" W["1 million Point instances
approx 200MB+ for __dict__ overhead"] end subgraph "With __slots__" S["1 million Point instances
approx 48MB (just x, y values)"] end subgraph "Savings" V["~75% memory reduction!"] end W --> V S --> V style W fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#E8F5E9 style V fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: __slots__ Restrictions
steps.explanation: With `__slots__`, you **cannot add arbitrary attributes**. Only the declared slots exist. Attempting to set an undeclared attribute raises `AttributeError`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" Code["class Point:
__slots__ = ('x', 'y')

p = Point()
p.x = 10 # OK
p.z = 30 # AttributeError!"] end subgraph "Result" R["AttributeError: 'Point' object
has no attribute 'z'"] end Code --> R style R fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Combining __slots__ and __dict__
steps.explanation: Include `'__dict__'` in `__slots__` to have both fixed slots AND a dict for dynamic attributes. This hybrid gives you fixed fast attributes plus flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Hybrid Approach" Code["class Flexible:
__slots__ = ('x', 'y', '__dict__')

f = Flexible()
f.x = 10 # Slot (fast)
f.y = 20 # Slot (fast)
f.z = 30 # Dict (flexible)"] end subgraph "Memory Layout" S["slot x: 10
slot y: 20
__dict__: {'z': 30}"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: __slots__ and Inheritance
steps.explanation: If parent has `__slots__`, child inherits them. If child doesn't define `__slots__`, it gets `__dict__` anyway. For memory savings throughout hierarchy, **all classes need __slots__**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Parent with slots" P["class Parent:
__slots__ = ('x',)"] end subgraph "Child without slots" C1["class Child(Parent):
pass # Gets __dict__!"] end subgraph "Child with slots" C2["class Child(Parent):
__slots__ = ('y',) # x + y, no dict"] end P --> C1 P --> C2 style C1 fill:#FFF3E0 style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: __slots__ and Properties
steps.explanation: `__slots__` works with properties. The slot holds the backing storage, the property provides the interface. Don't include the property name in `__slots__`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correct Pattern" Code["class Circle:
__slots__ = ('_radius',) # Backing store

@property
def radius(self):
return self._radius

@radius.setter
def radius(self, value):
if value < 0:
raise ValueError
self._radius = value"] end subgraph "Note" N["Property is class attr
_radius is in __slots__"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: __slots__ and __weakref__
steps.explanation: By default, slotted classes don't support weak references. Add `'__weakref__'` to `__slots__` if you need weak reference support.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without weakref slot" W1["class Foo:
__slots__ = ('x',)

import weakref
f = Foo()
r = weakref.ref(f) # TypeError!"] end subgraph "With weakref slot" W2["class Foo:
__slots__ = ('x', '__weakref__')

f = Foo()
r = weakref.ref(f) # Works!"] end style W1 fill:#FFCDD2 style W2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use __slots__
steps.explanation: Use `__slots__` when: creating many instances, fixed attribute set known ahead of time, memory or speed matters. Don't use when: need dynamic attributes, subclassing dict/list, debugging.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use __slots__"
        Y1["Many instances (10k+)"]
        Y2["Fixed attributes"]
        Y3["Memory-sensitive"]
        Y4["Data classes"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't Use"
        N1["Need dynamic attrs"]
        N2["Subclass builtins"]
        N3["Development/debugging"]
        N4["Few instances"]
    end
    style Y1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Y2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Y3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Y4 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style N2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style N3 fill:#FFF3E0
    style N4 fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Abstract Base Classes
fields.description: ABC registration and virtual subclasses

steps.title: What Are ABCs?
steps.explanation: **Abstract Base Classes** define interfaces in Python. They cannot be instantiated and can require subclasses to implement certain methods. ABCs bring some of the benefits of static typing to Python.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "ABC Purpose"
        A["Define interface contract"]
        B["Cannot be instantiated"]
        C["Force method implementation"]
        D["Enable isinstance/issubclass"]
    end
    ABC["Abstract Base Class"] --> A
    ABC --> B
    ABC --> C
    ABC --> D
    style ABC fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Creating an ABC
steps.explanation: Inherit from `ABC` and use `@abstractmethod` to declare methods that subclasses must implement. Attempting to instantiate a class with unimplemented abstract methods raises `TypeError`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Define ABC" Code["from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Shape(ABC):
@abstractmethod
def area(self):
pass

@abstractmethod
def perimeter(self):
pass"] end subgraph "Instantiation" I["s = Shape() # TypeError!
Cannot instantiate abstract class"] end Code --> I style I fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Implementing an ABC
steps.explanation: Subclasses must implement **all** abstract methods to be instantiable. Once all abstract methods are implemented, the class is concrete and can be instantiated.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Concrete Implementation" Code["class Circle(Shape):
def __init__(self, radius):
self.radius = radius

def area(self):
return 3.14 * self.radius ** 2

def perimeter(self):
return 2 * 3.14 * self.radius"] end subgraph "Now Valid" V["c = Circle(5) # Works!
c.area() - 78.5"] end Code --> V style V fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Partial Implementation
steps.explanation: If a subclass only implements some abstract methods, it's still abstract. This is useful for creating intermediate abstract classes in a hierarchy.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Hierarchy" A["Shape (ABC)
@abstractmethod area
@abstractmethod perimeter"] B["Polygon (ABC)
implements perimeter
@abstractmethod area"] C["Rectangle
implements area"] end A --> B --> C subgraph "Instantiation" I1["Shape() - TypeError"] I2["Polygon() - TypeError"] I3["Rectangle() - OK!"] end style A fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#FFF3E0 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Abstract Properties
steps.explanation: Use `@abstractmethod` with `@property` to require property implementations. The `@abstractmethod` must be the innermost decorator.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Abstract Property" Code["class Vehicle(ABC):
@property
@abstractmethod
def wheels(self):
pass

class Car(Vehicle):
@property
def wheels(self):
return 4"] end subgraph "Note" N["@property BEFORE @abstractmethod
(decorators apply bottom-up)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Virtual Subclasses
steps.explanation: Use `register()` to mark a class as a 'virtual subclass' without actual inheritance. The registered class passes `isinstance`/`issubclass` checks but doesn't inherit any methods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Virtual Subclass" Code["from abc import ABC

class MySequence(ABC):
pass

MySequence.register(tuple) # tuple is now 'subclass'

isinstance((), MySequence) # True!
issubclass(tuple, MySequence) # True!"] end subgraph "Note" N["tuple doesn't inherit
any MySequence methods!"] end Code --> N style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style N fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: __subclasshook__
steps.explanation: `__subclasshook__` lets you define custom `issubclass` logic. Return `True` if the class qualifies, `False` if not, `NotImplemented` for normal behavior.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Custom isinstance Logic" Code["class Drawable(ABC):
@classmethod
def __subclasshook__(cls, C):
if hasattr(C, 'draw'):
return True
return NotImplemented

class Circle:
def draw(self): pass

isinstance(Circle(), Drawable) # True!"] end subgraph "Duck Typing" D["If it has draw(),
it's Drawable!"] end Code --> D style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Built-in ABCs
steps.explanation: `collections.abc` provides ABCs for common types: `Iterable`, `Iterator`, `Sequence`, `Mapping`, `Set`, etc. These let you check capabilities without checking specific types.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "collections.abc" A["Iterable - has __iter__"] B["Iterator - has __next__"] C["Sequence - has __getitem__, __len__"] D["Mapping - has __getitem__, keys, items"] E["Set - has __contains__, __iter__"] end subgraph "Usage" Code["from collections.abc import Sequence

def process(items: Sequence):
'''Works with list, tuple, str, ...'''
for item in items:
..."] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ABCs vs Protocols
steps.explanation: Python 3.8+ adds `Protocol` for structural subtyping (duck typing with type hints). ABCs require explicit inheritance; Protocols match based on methods. Use Protocols for looser coupling.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "ABC (Nominal)" ABC["class Drawable(ABC):
@abstractmethod
def draw(self): ...

class Circle(Drawable): # Must inherit!
def draw(self): ..."] end subgraph "Protocol (Structural)" P["class Drawable(Protocol):
def draw(self): ...

class Circle: # No inheritance needed!
def draw(self): ...

# Circle is Drawable if it has draw()"] end style ABC fill:#FFF3E0 style P fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Composition vs Inheritance
fields.description: Design pattern decision tree

steps.title: Inheritance vs Composition
steps.explanation: **Inheritance** = 'is-a' relationship (Dog is an Animal). **Composition** = 'has-a' relationship (Car has an Engine). The mantra: 'Favor composition over inheritance.'
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Inheritance (is-a)"
        A1["Animal"]
        A2["Dog"]
        A1 --> A2
    end
    subgraph "Composition (has-a)"
        B1["Car"]
        B2["Engine"]
        B1 -->|has| B2
    end
    style A2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Inheritance Example
steps.explanation: Inheritance shares behavior through the class hierarchy. The child class gets all parent methods and can override them.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Inheritance" Code["class Animal:
def speak(self):
raise NotImplementedError

class Dog(Animal):
def speak(self):
return 'Woof!'

class Cat(Animal):
def speak(self):
return 'Meow!'"] end subgraph "Usage" U["dog = Dog()
