dog.speak() # 'Woof!'"] end Code --> U style Code fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Composition Example
steps.explanation: Composition achieves code reuse by containing instances of other classes. The containing class delegates work to its components.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Composition" Code["class Engine:
def start(self):
return 'Engine started'

class Car:
def __init__(self):
self.engine = Engine() # Has-an engine

def start(self):
return self.engine.start()"] end subgraph "Usage" U["car = Car()
car.start() # 'Engine started'"] end Code --> U style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Inheritance Problems
steps.explanation: Inheritance can lead to: **tight coupling** (changes ripple through hierarchy), **fragile base class** (parent changes break children), **inflexible hierarchies** (hard to change structure).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Inheritance Problems" P1["Tight Coupling
Child depends on parent internals"] P2["Fragile Base Class
Parent changes break children"] P3["Rigid Hierarchy
Can't easily restructure"] P4["Diamond Problem
Multiple inheritance issues"] end I["Deep Inheritance"] --> P1 I --> P2 I --> P3 I --> P4 style I fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Composition Benefits
steps.explanation: Composition offers: **loose coupling** (components are independent), **flexibility** (swap components at runtime), **easier testing** (mock components), **clearer relationships**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Composition Benefits" B1["Loose Coupling
Components are independent"] B2["Flexibility
Swap components at runtime"] B3["Testability
Easy to mock components"] B4["Clarity
Explicit relationships"] end C["Composition"] --> B1 C --> B2 C --> B3 C --> B4 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Decision Tree
steps.explanation: Use this decision tree to choose between inheritance and composition. The key question: is it truly an 'is-a' relationship that won't change?

steps.title: Strategy Pattern (Composition)
steps.explanation: The Strategy pattern uses composition to swap algorithms at runtime. Instead of inheriting behavior, inject it as a component.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Strategy Pattern" Code["class PaymentProcessor:
def __init__(self, strategy):
self.strategy = strategy

def process(self, amount):
return self.strategy.pay(amount)

class CreditCard:
def pay(self, amount): ...

class PayPal:
def pay(self, amount): ...

# Swap strategies at runtime!
processor = PaymentProcessor(CreditCard())
processor.strategy = PayPal()"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Mixin Pattern (Hybrid)
steps.explanation: **Mixins** combine inheritance and composition benefits. They're small classes that add specific functionality without being a full parent class. Use sparingly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Mixin Pattern" Code["class JSONMixin:
'''Adds JSON serialization'''
def to_json(self):
return json.dumps(self.__dict__)

class LogMixin:
'''Adds logging'''
def log(self, msg):
print(f'{self.__class__.__name__}: {msg}')

class User(JSONMixin, LogMixin):
def __init__(self, name):
self.name = name"] end subgraph "Usage" U["user = User('Alice')
user.to_json() # From JSONMixin
user.log('hi') # From LogMixin"] end style Code fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Real-World Guidelines
steps.explanation: In practice: use inheritance for frameworks (Django models), abstract interfaces (ABCs), and clear taxonomies. Use composition for everything else—it's almost always more flexible.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Use Inheritance" I1["Framework base classes
(Django Model)"] I2["Abstract interfaces
(ABCs)"] I3["True taxonomies
(Exception hierarchy)"] end subgraph "Use Composition" C1["Feature injection
(strategies, plugins)"] C2["Runtime flexibility
(configurable behavior)"] C3["Code reuse
(shared components)"] C4["When in doubt"] end style I1 fill:#FFF3E0 style I2 fill:#FFF3E0 style I3 fill:#FFF3E0 style C1 fill:#E8F5E9 style C2 fill:#E8F5E9 style C3 fill:#E8F5E9 style C4 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Type Hierarchy
fields.description: object - type - concrete types relationship

steps.title: Everything Inherits from object
steps.explanation: In Python, **every class inherits from `object`**. This is the root of the type hierarchy. Even built-in types like `int`, `str`, and `list` inherit from `object`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    O["object"]
    O --> I["int"]
    O --> S["str"]
    O --> L["list"]
    O --> D["dict"]
    O --> T["type"]
    O --> U["User-defined classes"]
    style O fill:#FFE082

steps.title: type Creates Classes
steps.explanation: `type` is the metaclass of all classes. Every class is an instance of `type`. Even `type` itself is an instance of `type`! This circular relationship is fundamental to Python.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Instance-Of Relationships" T["type"] C["class MyClass"] I["instance"] end T -->|"instance of"| T C -->|"instance of"| T I -->|"instance of"| C subgraph "Check" Check["type(MyClass) == type
type(type) == type
isinstance(MyClass, type) == True"] end style T fill:#FFE082

steps.title: object and type Relationship
steps.explanation: `object` is an instance of `type`, and `type` inherits from `object`. This creates a chicken-and-egg bootstrap that's resolved at interpreter startup.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Bootstrap" O["object"] T["type"] end T -->|"inherits from"| O O -->|"instance of"| T T -->|"instance of"| T subgraph "Verify" V["isinstance(object, type) # True
issubclass(type, object) # True
type(object) == type # True"] end style O fill:#E8F5E9 style T fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Numeric Type Hierarchy
steps.explanation: Python's numeric types form a hierarchy defined in `numbers` module: `Number` - `Complex` - `Real` - `Rational` - `Integral`. `int` is an `Integral`, `float` is a `Real`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "numbers Module ABCs"
        N["Number"]
        C["Complex"]
        R["Real"]
        Ra["Rational"]
        In["Integral"]
    end
    N --> C --> R --> Ra --> In

    subgraph "Concrete Types"
        INT["int (Integral)"]
        FLOAT["float (Real)"]
        COMP["complex (Complex)"]
    end
    In --> INT
    R --> FLOAT
    C --> COMP
    style In fill:#E8F5E9
    style R fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Collection Type Hierarchy
steps.explanation: `collections.abc` defines the hierarchy for containers: `Iterable` - `Collection` - `Sequence`/`Set`/`Mapping`. This enables duck-typed container operations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "collections.abc Hierarchy"
        IT["Iterable"]
        CON["Container"]
        SZ["Sized"]
        COLL["Collection"]
        SEQ["Sequence"]
        MAP["Mapping"]
        SET["Set"]
    end
    IT --> COLL
    CON --> COLL
    SZ --> COLL
    COLL --> SEQ
    COLL --> MAP
    COLL --> SET

    subgraph "Concrete"
        L["list - Sequence"]
        D["dict - Mapping"]
        S["set - Set"]
    end
    style COLL fill:#FFE082

steps.title: Special Type Objects
steps.explanation: The `typing` module provides special type objects: `Any` (any type), `None` (NoneType), `NoReturn` (never returns), `Never` (impossible type). These aren't real classes but type hints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Special Types" ANY["Any
Compatible with everything"] NONE["None
Only None value"] NR["NoReturn
Function never returns"] NEV["Never
Impossible/bottom type"] end subgraph "Usage" U1["def log(x: Any) -> None:"] U2["def fail() -> NoReturn:
raise Exception"] U3["def impossible() -> Never:
# unreachable"] end style ANY fill:#E3F2FD style NONE fill:#E8F5E9 style NR fill:#FFCDD2 style NEV fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Type vs type
steps.explanation: `type` (lowercase) is the built-in metaclass. `Type[X]` from `typing` is for type hints indicating a class object itself (not an instance). Python 3.9+ allows `type[X]`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "type (builtin)" B["type(obj) # Returns class
type('MyClass', bases, dict) # Creates class"] end subgraph "Type[X] (typing)" T["def factory(cls: Type[Animal]) -> Animal:
return cls() # cls is the CLASS, not instance"] end subgraph "Python 3.9+" N["def factory(cls: type[Animal]) -> Animal:
return cls() # lowercase type works now"] end style B fill:#FFF3E0 style T fill:#E8F5E9 style N fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Inheritance Checking
steps.explanation: Use `isinstance()` to check if an object is an instance of a class. Use `issubclass()` to check if a class inherits from another. Both work with ABCs and Protocols.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "isinstance()" I1["isinstance(5, int) # True"] I2["isinstance(5, (int, str)) # True (any)"] I3["isinstance([], Sequence) # True (ABC)"] end subgraph "issubclass()" S1["issubclass(bool, int) # True"] S2["issubclass(list, Sequence) # True"] S3["issubclass(int, str) # False"] end subgraph "Note" N["isinstance checks object type
issubclass checks class hierarchy"] end style I1 fill:#E8F5E9 style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Generic Types
fields.description: TypeVar, Generic[T], variance (covariant/contravariant)

steps.title: Why Generics?
steps.explanation: **Generics** let you write type-safe code that works with multiple types. Instead of `list` (any items), you write `list[int]` (only integers). The type checker knows what's inside.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without Generics" A["def first(items: list):
return items[0] # What type?"] end subgraph "With Generics" B["def first(items: list[int]) -> int:
return items[0] # Type checker knows!"] end A --> C["Type checker: ???"] B --> D["Type checker: int"] style A fill:#FFCDD2 style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Built-in Generic Types
steps.explanation: Python 3.9+ allows using built-in types as generics: `list[T]`, `dict[K, V]`, `set[T]`, `tuple[T, ...]`. Before 3.9, use `List`, `Dict`, etc. from `typing`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Python 3.9+" A["list[int]"] B["dict[str, int]"] C["set[str]"] D["tuple[int, str, float]"] end subgraph "Python 3.8 and earlier" E["from typing import List, Dict, Set, Tuple

List[int]
Dict[str, int]
Set[str]
Tuple[int, str, float]"] end style A fill:#E8F5E9 style E fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: TypeVar: Type Variables
steps.explanation: `TypeVar` creates a placeholder for a type that will be determined later. It's like a variable but for types. The same TypeVar in a signature means the same type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Creating TypeVar" Code["from typing import TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T') # Any type
N = TypeVar('N', int, float) # Constrained"] end subgraph "Using TypeVar" Use["def identity(x: T) -> T:
return x

identity(5) # T = int, returns int
identity('hi') # T = str, returns str"] end Code --> Use style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Generic Classes
steps.explanation: Inherit from `Generic[T]` to create generic classes. The type parameter can be used in method signatures and attribute types throughout the class.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Generic Class" Code["from typing import Generic, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')

class Stack(Generic[T]):
def __init__(self) -> None:
self.items: list[T] = []

def push(self, item: T) -> None:
self.items.append(item)

def pop(self) -> T:
return self.items.pop()"] end subgraph "Usage" Use["int_stack: Stack[int] = Stack()
int_stack.push(42) # OK
int_stack.push('hi') # Type error!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Bounded TypeVars
steps.explanation: Use `bound=` to restrict a TypeVar to a specific type or its subclasses. This is useful when you need certain methods or attributes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Bounded TypeVar" Code["from typing import TypeVar

class Animal:
def speak(self) -> str: ...

A = TypeVar('A', bound=Animal)

def make_speak(animal: A) -> str:
return animal.speak() # Safe! A has speak()"] end subgraph "Behavior" B1["make_speak(Dog()) # OK, Dog <= Animal"] B2["make_speak(Car()) # Error! Car not Animal"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style B2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Variance: Covariance
steps.explanation: **Covariant** types preserve subtyping direction. If `Dog` is subtype of `Animal`, then `Container[Dog]` is subtype of `Container[Animal]`. Use for **read-only** containers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Covariance" A["Animal"] D["Dog (subtype)"] D --> A CA["Container[Animal]"] CD["Container[Dog]"] CD -->|"subtype if covariant"| CA end subgraph "Code" Code["T_co = TypeVar('T_co', covariant=True)

class Reader(Generic[T_co]):
def read(self) -> T_co: ... # Only output

def process(r: Reader[Animal]) -> None: ...
dog_reader: Reader[Dog] = ...
process(dog_reader) # OK! Covariant"] end style CD fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Variance: Contravariance
steps.explanation: **Contravariant** types reverse subtyping direction. If `Dog` is subtype of `Animal`, then `Handler[Animal]` is subtype of `Handler[Dog]`. Use for **write-only** containers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Contravariance" A["Animal"] D["Dog (subtype)"] D --> A HA["Handler[Animal]"] HD["Handler[Dog]"] HA -->|"subtype if contravariant"| HD end subgraph "Code" Code["T_contra = TypeVar('T_contra', contravariant=True)

class Handler(Generic[T_contra]):
def handle(self, x: T_contra) -> None: ... # Only input

def setup(h: Handler[Dog]) -> None: ...
animal_handler: Handler[Animal] = ...
setup(animal_handler) # OK! Can handle any Animal"] end style HA fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Invariance (Default)
steps.explanation: By default, TypeVars are **invariant**: `Container[Dog]` is NOT a subtype of `Container[Animal]`. This is the safe default because mutable containers can both read and write.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Invariance" LA["list[Animal]"] LD["list[Dog]"] LA ---|"NOT subtypes!"| LD end subgraph "Why?" W["def add_cat(animals: list[Animal]):
animals.append(Cat())

dogs: list[Dog] = [Dog()]
add_cat(dogs) # If allowed...
# dogs now contains a Cat!"] end style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Variance Summary
steps.explanation: Choose variance based on how the type parameter is used: **input only** = contravariant, **output only** = covariant, **both** = invariant.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Decision Tree" Q{How is T used?} Q -->|"Only output (return)"| COV["Covariant
T_co = TypeVar('T_co', covariant=True)"] Q -->|"Only input (param)"| CONTRA["Contravariant
T_contra = TypeVar('T_contra', contravariant=True)"] Q -->|"Both"| INV["Invariant (default)
T = TypeVar('T')"] end style COV fill:#E8F5E9 style CONTRA fill:#E3F2FD style INV fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Union and Optional
fields.description: X | None vs Optional[X] vs Union[X, Y]

steps.title: Union: Multiple Possible Types
steps.explanation: `Union[X, Y]` means a value can be either type X or type Y. Use it when a function accepts or returns different types. Python 3.10+ allows `X | Y` syntax.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Union Syntax"
        OLD["Union[int, str]  # Python 3.9 and earlier"]
        NEW["int | str  # Python 3.10+"]
    end
    OLD <-->|"Equivalent"| NEW
    subgraph "Meaning"
        M["Value can be int OR str"]
    end
    style NEW fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Optional: Maybe None
steps.explanation: `Optional[X]` is just `Union[X, None]`. Use it when a value might be `None`. Python 3.10+ allows `X | None` which is clearer.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "All Equivalent" A["Optional[str]"] B["Union[str, None]"] C["str | None"] end A <--> B <--> C subgraph "Meaning" M["Value is str OR None"] end subgraph "Example" E["def find(key: str) -> str | None:
# Returns str if found, None otherwise"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Use `X | None` for nullable values. Use `X | Y` for genuinely different types. Avoid unions of many types—often a sign of design issues.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Use Optional/X | None" O1["Return value might not exist"] O2["Parameter has default None"] O3["Database field can be NULL"] end subgraph "Use Union/X | Y" U1["Function handles multiple input types"] U2["Return type varies by input"] U3["Serialization/API responses"] end subgraph "Avoid" A["Union[A, B, C, D, E]
Too many types = design smell"] end style O1 fill:#E8F5E9 style U1 fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Type Narrowing with Union
steps.explanation: Type checkers understand `isinstance()` checks and narrow Union types. After checking, you can use type-specific methods safely.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Type Narrowing" Code["def process(x: int | str) -> str:
if isinstance(x, int):
# Here x is known to be int
return str(x * 2)
else:
# Here x is known to be str
return x.upper()"] end subgraph "Type Checker Understands" TC["Before check: int | str
After isinstance(x, int): int
In else branch: str"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: None Checks
steps.explanation: For `X | None`, checking `if x is None` or `if x is not None` narrows the type. Type checkers recognize these patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "None Narrowing" Code["def greet(name: str | None) -> str:
if name is None:
return 'Hello, stranger!'
# Here name is str (not None)
return f'Hello, {name.upper()}!'"] end subgraph "Also Works" A["if name: # Truthy check"] B["if name is not None: # Explicit"] C["name or 'default' # Falsy fallback"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Union in Return Types
steps.explanation: When a function returns different types based on input, use Union. Consider using `@overload` for better type inference at call sites.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Simple Union Return" S["def parse(s: str) -> int | None:
try:
return int(s)
except ValueError:
return None"] end subgraph "With @overload (better)" O["@overload
def get(key: str, default: None = None) -> T | None: ...

@overload
def get(key: str, default: T) -> T: ...

def get(key: str, default: T | None = None) -> T | None:
..."] end style S fill:#E3F2FD style O fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Union Simplification
steps.explanation: Type checkers automatically simplify unions: `Union[int, int]` becomes `int`, `Union[int, str, int]` becomes `Union[int, str]`. Nested unions flatten.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Simplification Rules" A["Union[int, int] = int"] B["Union[int, str, int] = int | str"] C["Union[int, Union[str, float]] = int | str | float"] D["Union[int, object] = object"] end subgraph "Note" N["Subtype absorbed by supertype:
Union[int, bool] = int
(bool is subclass of int)"] end style N fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Use `X | None` for nullable, `X | Y` for two distinct types. Avoid large unions. Consider generics or protocols for flexibility instead of unions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good" G1["str | None # Nullable string"] G2["int | float # Numeric types"] G3["Success | Error # Result types"] end subgraph "Consider Alternatives" A1["Instead of Union[A,B,C,D]
Use a Protocol or ABC"] A2["Instead of Union[Handler1, Handler2]
Use base Handler class"] end style G1 fill:#E8F5E9 style G2 fill:#E8F5E9 style G3 fill:#E8F5E9 style A1 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Protocol Classes
fields.description: Structural subtyping flow

steps.title: Nominal vs Structural Typing
steps.explanation: **Nominal typing**: types match by name/inheritance (ABCs). **Structural typing**: types match by structure/methods (Protocols). Protocols bring static duck typing to Python.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Nominal (ABC)" N1["class Drawable(ABC):
@abstractmethod
def draw(self): ..."] N2["class Circle(Drawable): # Must inherit!
def draw(self): ..."] end subgraph "Structural (Protocol)" S1["class Drawable(Protocol):
def draw(self): ..."] S2["class Circle: # No inheritance!
def draw(self): ..."] S3["Circle matches Drawable"] end N1 --> N2 S1 --> S3 S2 --> S3 style N2 fill:#FFF3E0 style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Defining a Protocol
steps.explanation: Inherit from `Protocol` and define the required methods/attributes. Classes don't need to inherit from the Protocol—they just need to have matching members.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Protocol Definition" Code["from typing import Protocol

class Closeable(Protocol):
def close(self) -> None:
..."] end subgraph "Matching Classes" F["class File:
def close(self) -> None: ...
# Matches Closeable!"] S["class Socket:
def close(self) -> None: ...
# Also matches!"] end Code --> F Code --> S style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Using Protocols in Type Hints
steps.explanation: Use Protocols in function signatures to accept any object with matching structure. The type checker verifies callers pass objects with required methods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Function Signature" Code["def cleanup(resource: Closeable) -> None:
resource.close()"] end subgraph "Valid Calls" V1["cleanup(File()) # OK"] V2["cleanup(Socket()) # OK"] V3["cleanup(Database()) # OK if has close()"] end subgraph "Invalid" I["cleanup(42) # Error: int has no close()"] end Code --> V1 Code --> V2 Code --> I style V1 fill:#E8F5E9 style I fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Protocol with Attributes
steps.explanation: Protocols can require attributes, not just methods. Declare them with type annotations at class level (no default value).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Protocol with Attribute" Code["class Named(Protocol):
name: str # Required attribute

class Person:
def __init__(self, name: str):
self.name = name # Has name attr!

def greet(obj: Named) -> str:
return f'Hello, {obj.name}'"] end subgraph "Matches" M1["Person('Alice') # Has name: str"] M2["Dog('Buddy') # If has name: str"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: runtime_checkable Protocols
steps.explanation: By default, Protocols only work with static type checkers. Add `@runtime_checkable` to enable `isinstance()` checks at runtime.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without Decorator" W["class Drawable(Protocol):
def draw(self): ...

isinstance(obj, Drawable) # TypeError!"] end subgraph "With Decorator" D["@runtime_checkable
class Drawable(Protocol):
def draw(self): ...

isinstance(obj, Drawable) # Works!"] end subgraph "Limitation" L["Only checks method EXISTS
Doesn't check signatures"] end style W fill:#FFCDD2 style D fill:#E8F5E9 style L fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Generic Protocols
steps.explanation: Protocols can be generic. Define a TypeVar and use it in method signatures to create flexible, reusable interface definitions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Generic Protocol" Code["T = TypeVar('T')

class Container(Protocol[T]):
def get(self) -> T: ...
def set(self, value: T) -> None: ...

class Box:
def get(self) -> int: ...
def set(self, value: int) -> None: ...

box: Container[int] = Box() # OK!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Built-in Protocols
steps.explanation: Python has implicit built-in protocols: `Iterable` (has `__iter__`), `Sized` (has `__len__`), `Hashable` (has `__hash__`). These are defined in `typing` and `collections.abc`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Built-in Protocols" IT["Iterable - __iter__"] SZ["Sized - __len__"] HA["Hashable - __hash__"] CA["Callable - __call__"] CM["ContextManager - __enter__, __exit__"] SU["SupportsInt - __int__"] end subgraph "Usage" U["def count(items: Sized) -> int:
return len(items) # Works with any Sized"] end style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Protocol vs ABC Decision
steps.explanation: Use **Protocol** for loose coupling (don't control all implementations). Use **ABC** when you control the hierarchy and want shared implementation code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use Protocol"
        P1["Third-party code must match"]
        P2["Duck typing scenarios"]
        P3["Flexibility over strictness"]
        P4["No shared implementation"]
    end
    subgraph "Use ABC"
        A1["You control all subclasses"]
        A2["Want shared default methods"]
        A3["Runtime isinstance checks"]
        A4["Explicit inheritance required"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style A2 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: TypedDict vs dataclass
fields.description: When to use which

steps.title: The Problem They Solve
steps.explanation: Both TypedDict and dataclass provide typed structured data. **TypedDict** types dictionaries. **dataclass** creates classes with auto-generated methods. Choose based on your use case.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypedDict" TD["Typed dictionary
Still a dict at runtime
For JSON/API data"] end subgraph "dataclass" DC["Class with attributes
Generated __init__, __repr__, etc.
For internal data models"] end subgraph "Plain dict" PD["dict[str, Any]
No type safety
Avoid for structured data"] end style TD fill:#E3F2FD style DC fill:#E8F5E9 style PD fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: TypedDict Definition
steps.explanation: TypedDict creates a type for dictionaries with specific keys and value types. At runtime it's just a `dict`. Type checkers validate key access.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypedDict" Code["from typing import TypedDict

class User(TypedDict):
name: str
age: int
email: str

user: User = {
'name': 'Alice',
'age': 30,
'email': 'alice@example.com'
}"] end subgraph "Access" A["user['name'] # str
user['age'] # int
user['foo'] # Type error!"] end style Code fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: dataclass Definition
steps.explanation: dataclass decorator generates `__init__`, `__repr__`, `__eq__`, and more. Attributes become parameters. It creates a proper class, not a dict.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "dataclass" Code["from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class User:
name: str
age: int
email: str

user = User(name='Alice', age=30, email='alice@example.com')"] end subgraph "Auto-generated" A["__init__(self, name, age, email)"] B["__repr__(self) -> 'User(...)'"] C["__eq__(self, other)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Key Differences
steps.explanation: TypedDict is a dict with type hints. dataclass is a class with generated methods. They serve different purposes and have different runtime behavior.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "TypedDict"
        T1["type(user) == dict"]
        T2["Access: user['name']"]
        T3["JSON-compatible"]
        T4["No methods"]
        T5["No attribute access"]
    end
    subgraph "dataclass"
        D1["type(user) == User"]
        D2["Access: user.name"]
        D3["Needs serialization"]
        D4["Has methods"]
        D5["Can add methods"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Optional Fields
steps.explanation: TypedDict has `total=False` for optional keys and `NotRequired`. dataclass uses `field(default=...)` or `Optional` with `None` default.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypedDict Optional" TD["class User(TypedDict, total=False):
name: str # Optional
age: int # Optional

# Or use NotRequired (Python 3.11+)
class User(TypedDict):
name: str
age: NotRequired[int]"] end subgraph "dataclass Optional" DC["@dataclass
class User:
name: str
age: int = 0 # Default value
email: str | None = None # Optional"] end style TD fill:#E3F2FD style DC fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Use TypedDict For
steps.explanation: Use TypedDict for external data: JSON responses, API payloads, configuration dicts. It preserves dict compatibility for serialization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypedDict Use Cases" U1["JSON API responses"] U2["Config dictionaries"] U3["kwargs typing"] U4["Legacy dict code"] end subgraph "Example" Code["class APIResponse(TypedDict):
status: str
data: list[dict]

response: APIResponse = json.loads(text)"] end U1 --> Code style Code fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Use dataclass For
steps.explanation: Use dataclass for internal data models: domain objects, value objects, DTOs. You get clean attribute access, methods, and immutability options.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "dataclass Use Cases" U1["Domain models"] U2["Value objects"] U3["Internal DTOs"] U4["Config objects"] end subgraph "Example" Code["@dataclass(frozen=True) # Immutable!
class Point:
x: float
y: float

def distance(self, other: 'Point') -> float:
return ((self.x - other.x)**2 + (self.y - other.y)**2)**0.5"] end U1 --> Code style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Ask: Is it external data (JSON/API)? Use TypedDict. Is it internal domain model? Use dataclass. Need methods? dataclass. Need dict compatibility? TypedDict.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A{What's the source?}
    A -->|"JSON/API/External"| TD["TypedDict"]
    A -->|"Internal/Domain"| DC["dataclass"]

    B{Need methods?}
    B -->|Yes| DC2["dataclass"]
    B -->|No| C{Need dict?}
    C -->|Yes| TD2["TypedDict"]
    C -->|No| DC3["dataclass"]

    style TD fill:#E3F2FD
    style DC fill:#E8F5E9
    style TD2 fill:#E3F2FD
    style DC2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style DC3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Type Narrowing
fields.description: How isinstance(), assert, and guards narrow types

steps.title: What is Type Narrowing?
steps.explanation: **Type narrowing** is when a type checker recognizes that a value has a more specific type within a code block. Checks like `isinstance()` or `if x is not None` narrow the type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before Narrowing" B["x: int | str # Could be either"] end subgraph "After Check" A["if isinstance(x, int):
# x is now int"] end B --> A style B fill:#FFF3E0 style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: isinstance() Narrowing
steps.explanation: `isinstance()` is the most common way to narrow types. Type checkers understand it and narrow the type in the if/else branches.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "isinstance Narrowing" Code["def process(x: int | str | list) -> str:
if isinstance(x, int):
return str(x * 2) # x: int
elif isinstance(x, str):
return x.upper() # x: str
else:
return str(len(x)) # x: list"] end subgraph "Multiple Types" M["isinstance(x, (int, float)) # x: int | float"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: None Checks
steps.explanation: Checking `x is None` or `x is not None` narrows `X | None` to `None` or `X`. This is essential for handling optional values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "None Narrowing" Code["def greet(name: str | None) -> str:
if name is None:
# name: None
return 'Hello!'
# name: str
return f'Hello, {name.upper()}!'"] end subgraph "Patterns" P1["if x is None: # x: None"] P2["if x is not None: # x: X"] P3["if x: # x: X (truthy narrow)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: assert Narrowing
steps.explanation: `assert` narrows types by assuming the condition is true. Type checkers use this for narrowing but beware—asserts can be disabled with `-O`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "assert Narrowing" Code["def process(x: int | None) -> int:
assert x is not None
# x: int (narrowed)
return x * 2"] end subgraph "Warning" W["assert removed with python -O
Use assert for dev checks, not logic"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9 style W fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Equality Narrowing
steps.explanation: Comparing to literal values narrows to that specific type or value. Works with `==`, `is`, and `in` for collections.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Equality Narrowing" E1["if x == 'foo':
# x: Literal['foo']"] E2["if x in ('a', 'b'):
# x: Literal['a'] | Literal['b']"] end subgraph "Enum Narrowing" Code["class Color(Enum):
RED = 1
BLUE = 2

if c == Color.RED:
# c: Literal[Color.RED]"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: TypeGuard Functions
steps.explanation: `TypeGuard` lets you create custom type narrowing functions. Return `True` to indicate the argument is the specified type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypeGuard" Code["from typing import TypeGuard

def is_str_list(val: list) -> TypeGuard[list[str]]:
return all(isinstance(x, str) for x in val)

def process(items: list) -> None:
if is_str_list(items):
# items: list[str]
for s in items:
print(s.upper())"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: TypeIs (Python 3.13+)
steps.explanation: `TypeIs` is stricter than `TypeGuard`. It narrows in both True AND False branches, while TypeGuard only narrows in True branch.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "TypeIs vs TypeGuard" TG["TypeGuard:
if check(x): # x: NarrowType
else: # x: OriginalType (not narrowed!)"] TI["TypeIs:
if check(x): # x: NarrowType
else: # x: OriginalType - NarrowType"] end subgraph "Example" Code["def is_int(x: int | str) -> TypeIs[int]:
return isinstance(x, int)

if is_int(x):
# x: int
else:
# x: str (narrowed both ways!)"] end style TI fill:#E8F5E9 style TG fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Narrowing Limitations
steps.explanation: Type narrowing doesn't persist across function calls or mutable operations. Each scope needs its own narrowing checks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Narrowing Lost" Code["def check(x: int | None) -> None:
if x is not None:
do_something() # Might mutate x?
print(x * 2) # May warn: x could be None"] end subgraph "Solution" S["Assign to local variable
or re-check after call"] end subgraph "Works" W["if x is not None:
val = x # val: int
do_something()
print(val * 2) # val still int"] end style Code fill:#FFF3E0 style W fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Callable Types
fields.description: Callable[[Args], Return] and ParamSpec

steps.title: The Callable Type
steps.explanation: `Callable[[ArgTypes], ReturnType]` describes function signatures. The first list contains argument types, the second is the return type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Callable Syntax" C["Callable[[Arg1Type, Arg2Type], ReturnType]"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Callable[[int, str], bool]
# func(int, str) -> bool"] E2["Callable[[], None]
# func() -> None"] E3["Callable[[int], int]
# func(int) -> int"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Using Callable in Type Hints
steps.explanation: Use `Callable` to type function parameters that accept callbacks, decorators, or any callable object (functions, lambdas, classes with `__call__`).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Function Parameter" Code["from typing import Callable

def apply(
func: Callable[[int], int],
value: int
) -> int:
return func(value)

apply(lambda x: x * 2, 5) # OK
apply(str, 5) # Error: str returns str"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Callable with Any Arguments
steps.explanation: Use `Callable[..., ReturnType]` when you don't care about argument types. The `...` (Ellipsis) means any arguments are accepted.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Any Arguments" Code["Callable[..., int]
# Any function returning int

def run(f: Callable[..., int]) -> int:
return f() # or f(1) or f(1, 2, x=3)"] end subgraph "Common Use" U["Callbacks where args vary
Decorators preserving signatures"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ParamSpec: Preserving Parameters
steps.explanation: `ParamSpec` captures a function's parameters so decorators can preserve the original signature. It's like TypeVar but for parameter lists.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "ParamSpec" Code["from typing import ParamSpec, TypeVar, Callable

P = ParamSpec('P')
R = TypeVar('R')

def logging(f: Callable[P, R]) -> Callable[P, R]:
def wrapper(*args: P.args, **kwargs: P.kwargs) -> R:
print(f'Calling {f.__name__}')
return f(*args, **kwargs)
return wrapper"] end subgraph "Result" Res["@logging
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int: ...

# add still shows (a: int, b: int) -> int"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ParamSpec.args and ParamSpec.kwargs
steps.explanation: `P.args` and `P.kwargs` are special attributes for typing `*args` and `**kwargs` that match the ParamSpec.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Using P.args and P.kwargs" Code["P = ParamSpec('P')

def decorator(f: Callable[P, R]) -> Callable[P, R]:
def wrapper(
*args: P.args, # Positional args
**kwargs: P.kwargs # Keyword args
) -> R:
return f(*args, **kwargs)
return wrapper"] end subgraph "Note" N["P.args and P.kwargs are
only valid inside ParamSpec context"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Concatenate: Adding Parameters
steps.explanation: `Concatenate` lets you add new parameters to a ParamSpec. Useful for decorators that inject arguments.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Concatenate" Code["from typing import Concatenate

P = ParamSpec('P')
R = TypeVar('R')

def with_db(
f: Callable[Concatenate[DB, P], R]
) -> Callable[P, R]:
def wrapper(*args: P.args, **kwargs: P.kwargs) -> R:
db = get_db()
return f(db, *args, **kwargs) # Inject db
return wrapper"] end subgraph "Usage" U["@with_db
def query(db: DB, sql: str) -> list:
...

query('SELECT *') # db is injected"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Callable Classes
steps.explanation: Any class with `__call__` is callable. You can type hint them using `Callable` or use Protocol for more precision.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Callable Class" Code["class Multiplier:
def __init__(self, factor: int):
self.factor = factor

def __call__(self, x: int) -> int:
return x * self.factor

m: Callable[[int], int] = Multiplier(3)
m(5) # 15"] end subgraph "Protocol Alternative" P["class IntTransform(Protocol):
def __call__(self, x: int) -> int: ..."] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: These are common Callable patterns: callback types, factory functions, decorator signatures, and higher-order function types.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Patterns" CB["# Callback
Callback = Callable[[Event], None]"] FAC["# Factory
Factory = Callable[[], T]"] PRED["# Predicate
Predicate = Callable[[T], bool]"] MAP["# Mapper
Mapper = Callable[[T], U]"] end subgraph "Decorator Type" DEC["F = TypeVar('F', bound=Callable)
Decorator = Callable[[F], F]"] end style CB fill:#E8F5E9 style DEC fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Literal Types
fields.description: Constraining to specific values

steps.title: What Are Literal Types?
steps.explanation: `Literal` constrains a type to specific values, not just types. Instead of `str`, you can say `Literal['red', 'green', 'blue']`—only those exact strings are allowed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Regular Type" R["color: str
# Any string allowed"] end subgraph "Literal Type" L["color: Literal['red', 'green', 'blue']
# Only these 3 values allowed"] end R -->|"Less precise"| L style L fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Basic Literal Usage
steps.explanation: Import `Literal` from `typing` and specify allowed values. Works with strings, integers, booleans, bytes, and enums.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Literal Examples" S["Literal['GET', 'POST', 'PUT'] # Strings"] I["Literal[0, 1, 2] # Integers"] B["Literal[True, False] # Booleans"] N["Literal[None] # Just None"] end subgraph "Usage" Code["def request(method: Literal['GET', 'POST']) -> Response:
...

request('GET') # OK
request('DELETE') # Type error!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Literal vs Enum
steps.explanation: Both `Literal` and `Enum` constrain values. Use `Literal` for simple cases (no runtime import needed). Use `Enum` when values have meaning or behavior.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Literal" L["Literal['red', 'green', 'blue']
+ No runtime overhead
+ Simple cases
- No methods"] end subgraph "Enum" E["class Color(Enum):
RED = 'red'
GREEN = 'green'
+ Methods, attributes
+ Runtime existence
- Import required"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Literal in Function Overloads
steps.explanation: `Literal` with `@overload` lets you return different types based on literal argument values. Common in APIs with mode parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Overload with Literal" Code["from typing import overload, Literal

@overload
def fetch(url: str, format: Literal['json']) -> dict: ...

@overload
def fetch(url: str, format: Literal['text']) -> str: ...

def fetch(url: str, format: str):
...

result = fetch(url, 'json') # result: dict
result = fetch(url, 'text') # result: str"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: LiteralString (Python 3.11+)
steps.explanation: `LiteralString` accepts any literal string value (compile-time constant). Useful for SQL injection prevention—only allows known safe strings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "LiteralString" Code["from typing import LiteralString

def execute(sql: LiteralString) -> None:
# Only literal strings, not user input
...

execute('SELECT * FROM users') # OK
user_input = input()
execute(user_input) # Type error!"] end subgraph "Security" S["Prevents injection by
requiring compile-time strings"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Final: Constant Values
steps.explanation: `Final` marks a variable that shouldn't be reassigned. Combined with `Literal`, the type checker knows the exact value.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Final Constants" Code["from typing import Final

MAX_SIZE: Final = 100 # Can't reassign
API_VERSION: Final[str] = '2.0'

# Type checker infers Literal[100]
MAX_SIZE = 200 # Type error!"] end subgraph "Benefit" B["Type checker knows exact value
Enables better inference"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Combining Literals
steps.explanation: Combine `Literal` with `Union` (or `|`) for multiple allowed values. `Literal['a', 'b']` is equivalent to `Literal['a'] | Literal['b']`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Equivalent" E1["Literal['a', 'b', 'c']"] E2["Literal['a'] | Literal['b'] | Literal['c']"] end E1 <-->|"Same"| E2 subgraph "Type Alias" Code["HttpMethod = Literal['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE']

def request(method: HttpMethod) -> Response:
..."] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Use Literal for mode flags, API parameters, and status codes. Avoid for large value sets (use Enum). Combine with @overload for precise return types.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Uses"
        G1["Mode flags: Literal['sync', 'async']"]
        G2["Status codes: Literal[200, 404, 500]"]
        G3["HTTP methods: Literal['GET', 'POST']"]
    end
    subgraph "Prefer Enum"
        E["Many values (10+)"]
        E2["Need methods/attributes"]
        E3["Values have meaning"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style E fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Self Type
fields.description: Return type annotations for method chaining

steps.title: The Problem Self Solves
steps.explanation: When a method returns `self`, what's the return type? Using the class name breaks inheritance—subclass methods would return the parent type. `Self` always refers to the actual class.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Problem" Code["class Builder:
def set_name(self, name: str) -> 'Builder':
self.name = name
return self

class AdvancedBuilder(Builder):
def set_advanced(self) -> 'AdvancedBuilder': ...

adv = AdvancedBuilder()
adv.set_name('x') # Returns Builder, not AdvancedBuilder!"] end style Code fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Using Self
steps.explanation: `Self` (Python 3.11+) refers to the actual class type, not a specific class. Subclasses automatically get the correct return type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With Self" Code["from typing import Self

class Builder:
def set_name(self, name: str) -> Self:
self.name = name
return self

class AdvancedBuilder(Builder):
pass

adv = AdvancedBuilder()
result = adv.set_name('x') # Returns AdvancedBuilder!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Method Chaining / Fluent Interface
steps.explanation: `Self` enables fluent interfaces where methods can be chained. Each method returns `self`, and the type checker knows the exact type throughout.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Fluent Interface" Code["class QueryBuilder:
def select(self, *cols: str) -> Self:
self.columns = cols
return self

def where(self, condition: str) -> Self:
self.conditions.append(condition)
return self

def limit(self, n: int) -> Self:
self.limit_val = n
return self

result = QueryBuilder()
.select('name', 'age')
.where('age > 18')
.limit(10)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Self in Classmethods
steps.explanation: `Self` works in classmethods too. It represents the class being called, so factory methods return the correct subclass type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Classmethod with Self" Code["class Animal:
@classmethod
def create(cls) -> Self:
return cls()

class Dog(Animal):
pass

dog = Dog.create() # Returns Dog, not Animal!"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Before Self: TypeVar Workaround
steps.explanation: Before Python 3.11, you needed a TypeVar bound to the class. This was verbose and easy to get wrong. `Self` is simpler.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Old Way (TypeVar)" Old["T = TypeVar('T', bound='Builder')

class Builder:
def set_name(self: T, name: str) -> T:
self.name = name
return self"] end subgraph "New Way (Self)" New["from typing import Self

class Builder:
def set_name(self, name: str) -> Self:
self.name = name
return self"] end Old -->|"Simpler"| New style Old fill:#FFF3E0 style New fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Self vs cls/self Parameter
steps.explanation: `Self` is the type annotation, not a parameter name. `self` (lowercase) is the instance, `Self` (capitalized) is its type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Distinction" S1["self # The parameter (instance)"] S2["Self # The type (class type of self)"] end subgraph "In Code" Code["class MyClass:
# self is the instance
# Self is its type
def copy(self) -> Self:
return MyClass() # or self.__class__()"] end style S1 fill:#E3F2FD style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Use Cases
steps.explanation: `Self` is useful for: builder patterns, copy methods, factory classmethods, context managers returning self, and any method chaining.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Builder pattern - fluent methods"] U2["Copy/clone methods"] U3["Factory classmethods"] U4["Context managers (__enter__)"] U5["Setters returning self"] end subgraph "Example" Code["def __enter__(self) -> Self:
return self

def copy(self) -> Self:
return self.__class__(...)"] end style U1 fill:#E8F5E9 style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Type Checking Flow
fields.description: How mypy/pyright analyze code

steps.title: What Type Checkers Do
steps.explanation: Type checkers (mypy, pyright) analyze your code **without running it**. They read type hints, infer types, and report errors before runtime.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR A["Source Code
+ Type Hints"] --> B["Type Checker
(mypy/pyright)"] B --> C{Errors?} C -->|Yes| D["Report Type Errors"] C -->|No| E["Code is Type-Safe"] style B fill:#E8F5E9 style D fill:#FFCDD2 style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Type Inference
steps.explanation: Type checkers infer types from assignments and expressions. You don't need hints everywhere—checkers figure out obvious types from context.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Type Inference"
        A["x = 5  # Inferred: int"]
        B["y = 'hello'  # Inferred: str"]
        C["z = [1, 2, 3]  # Inferred: list[int]"]
        D["d = {'a': 1}  # Inferred: dict[str, int]"]
    end
    subgraph "When Hints Needed"
        E["Empty containers: list[int] = []"]
        F["Function parameters"]
        G["Complex expressions"]
    end
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style E fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Analysis Phases
steps.explanation: Type checkers analyze code in phases: parse AST, collect definitions, infer types, check compatibility, report errors.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Source
    participant Parser
    participant Collector
    participant Inferrer
    participant Checker
    participant Output

    Source->>Parser: Parse to AST
    Parser->>Collector: Collect definitions
    Collector->>Inferrer: Infer variable types
    Inferrer->>Checker: Check type compatibility
    Checker->>Output: Report errors

steps.title: Cross-Module Analysis
steps.explanation: Type checkers analyze imports and follow type information across modules. They use `.pyi` stub files for libraries without inline hints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Your Code"
        M["main.py"]
        U["utils.py"]
    end
    subgraph "Type Info Sources"
        S1["Inline type hints"]
        S2[".pyi stub files"]
        S3["typeshed (stdlib stubs)"]
        S4["Library type packages"]
    end
    M --> U
    U --> S1
    M --> S3
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Error Detection
steps.explanation: Type checkers catch: wrong argument types, missing return values, attribute errors, incompatible assignments, and more—all before you run the code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Errors Caught" E1["Wrong argument types"] E2["Wrong return type"] E3["Missing attributes"] E4["Incompatible assignment"] E5["Unreachable code"] E6["Missing Optional handling"] end subgraph "Example" Code["def greet(name: str) -> str:
return name.upper()

greet(42) # Error: int is not str"] end style E1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Gradual Typing
steps.explanation: Python typing is gradual—you can add types incrementally. `Any` acts as an escape hatch. Type checkers are lenient with untyped code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Gradual Adoption" L1["No types: --ignore-missing-imports"] L2["Some types: typed functions"] L3["Strict: --strict mode"] end subgraph "Any Escape" Code["from typing import Any

def process(x: Any) -> Any:
# No type checking here
return x.whatever() # No error"] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 style L3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: mypy vs pyright
steps.explanation: mypy is the original Python type checker. pyright (Microsoft) is faster and stricter. Both are widely used; pyright powers VS Code's Pylance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "mypy"
        M1["Original type checker"]
        M2["Python implementation"]
        M3["Configurable strictness"]
        M4["Mature ecosystem"]
    end
    subgraph "pyright"
        P1["Fast (TypeScript-based)"]
        P2["Stricter by default"]
        P3["Powers Pylance/VS Code"]
        P4["Better inference"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Configuration
steps.explanation: Configure type checkers via pyproject.toml, mypy.ini, or pyrightconfig.json. Control strictness, ignored errors, and per-module settings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "mypy.ini" M["[mypy]
strict = true
ignore_missing_imports = true

[mypy-tests.*]
ignore_errors = true"] end subgraph "pyproject.toml" P["[tool.mypy]
strict = true

[tool.pyright]
typeCheckingMode = 'strict'"] end style M fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: GIL and Threading
fields.description: Why the GIL exists, when it releases

steps.title: What is the GIL?
steps.explanation: The **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)** is a mutex that protects access to Python objects. Only one thread can execute Python bytecode at a time, even on multi-core CPUs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With GIL" T1["Thread 1"] -->|"Holds GIL"| PY["Python Interpreter"] T2["Thread 2"] -->|"Waiting"| WAIT["Blocked"] T3["Thread 3"] -->|"Waiting"| WAIT end subgraph "Only One Active" NOTE["Only ONE thread executes
Python code at a time"] end style PY fill:#E8F5E9 style WAIT fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Why Does the GIL Exist?
steps.explanation: The GIL simplifies CPython's memory management. Reference counting (Python's main GC) isn't thread-safe. The GIL prevents race conditions on refcounts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without GIL (Problem)" A["Thread 1: x.refcount++"] B["Thread 2: x.refcount++"] C["Race condition!
Lost update"] end subgraph "With GIL (Safe)" D["Thread 1 (has GIL): x.refcount++"] E["Thread 2 (waiting): blocked"] F["No race condition"] end A --> C B --> C D --> F style C fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When Does the GIL Release?
steps.explanation: The GIL is released during **I/O operations** (file, network, sleep) and certain **C extensions**. This allows I/O-bound programs to benefit from threading.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "GIL Released"
        I1["File I/O"]
        I2["Network I/O"]
        I3["time.sleep()"]
        I4["NumPy operations"]
        I5["Database queries"]
    end
    subgraph "GIL Held"
        H1["Python bytecode"]
        H2["Pure Python loops"]
        H3["Object manipulation"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H1 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: I/O-Bound vs CPU-Bound
steps.explanation: **I/O-bound**: Waiting for external resources (network, disk). Threads help! **CPU-bound**: Heavy computation. GIL hurts—use multiprocessing instead.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "I/O-Bound" IO["Web requests, file reads
Database queries"] IORES["Threading works!
GIL releases during I/O"] end subgraph "CPU-Bound" CPU["Math, image processing
Data crunching"] CPURES["Threading limited
Use multiprocessing"] end IO --> IORES CPU --> CPURES style IORES fill:#E8F5E9 style CPURES fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Threading for I/O
steps.explanation: Use `threading` for I/O-bound tasks. Multiple threads can wait for I/O simultaneously while one executes Python code.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread 1
    participant T2 as Thread 2
    participant GIL
    participant Network

    T1->>GIL: Acquire
    T1->>Network: Send request (releases GIL)
    T2->>GIL: Acquire (while T1 waits)
    T2->>T2: Process data
    Network-->>T1: Response ready
    T1->>GIL: Re-acquire when T2 done
    T1->>T1: Process response

steps.title: Multiprocessing for CPU
steps.explanation: `multiprocessing` bypasses the GIL by using separate Python processes. Each process has its own GIL, enabling true parallelism.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multiprocessing" P1["Process 1
(own GIL)"] P2["Process 2
(own GIL)"] P3["Process 3
(own GIL)"] end subgraph "True Parallelism" CPU1["Core 1"] CPU2["Core 2"] CPU3["Core 3"] end P1 --> CPU1 P2 --> CPU2 P3 --> CPU3 style CPU1 fill:#E8F5E9 style CPU2 fill:#E8F5E9 style CPU3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Concurrency Options Summary
steps.explanation: Choose based on workload: **threading** for I/O-bound, **multiprocessing** for CPU-bound, **asyncio** for high-concurrency I/O, **concurrent.futures** for simple parallelism.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{Workload type?} Q -->|I/O-bound| IO{Many connections?} IO -->|Yes| ASYNC["asyncio"] IO -->|No| THREAD["threading"] Q -->|CPU-bound| MULTI["multiprocessing"] subgraph "concurrent.futures" CF["ThreadPoolExecutor - I/O
ProcessPoolExecutor - CPU"] end style ASYNC fill:#E8F5E9 style THREAD fill:#E3F2FD style MULTI fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: GIL-Free Future?
steps.explanation: Python 3.13+ experiments with **free-threaded Python** (no GIL). It's optional and may change Python's concurrency story. Watch PEP 703.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Current (CPython)" CUR["GIL required
Thread-safe refcounting"] end subgraph "Future (PEP 703)" FUT["Optional no-GIL mode
Biased reference counting
Per-object locks"] end subgraph "Alternatives Now" ALT["PyPy (different GIL)
Jython (no GIL)
multiprocessing (bypass)"] end CUR --> FUT style FUT fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Memory Management
fields.description: Reference counting + generational GC

steps.title: Python's Memory Model
steps.explanation: Python uses **reference counting** as the primary memory management mechanism, plus a **cyclic garbage collector** to handle reference cycles that refcounting can't detect.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Memory Management" RC["Reference Counting
(Primary)"] GC["Cyclic Garbage Collector
(Backup)"] end RC -->|"Handles most objects"| FREE["Immediate Cleanup"] GC -->|"Handles cycles"| CYCLE["Detect & Break Cycles"] style RC fill:#E8F5E9 style GC fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Reference Counting
steps.explanation: Every object has a reference count. When it reaches zero, the object is immediately freed. Assignments, function calls, and container operations change refcounts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Reference Counting" A["x = [1, 2, 3] # refcount = 1"] B["y = x # refcount = 2"] C["del x # refcount = 1"] D["del y # refcount = 0 - FREED!"] end A --> B --> C --> D subgraph "sys.getrefcount()" S["import sys
sys.getrefcount(obj) # Note: +1 for arg"] end style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Reference Cycles Problem
steps.explanation: Reference counting can't handle cycles. If A references B and B references A, both have refcount > 0 even when unreachable. This is where the GC helps.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Cycle Problem" A["Object A
refcount: 1"] B["Object B
refcount: 1"] end A -->|"A.ref = B"| B B -->|"B.ref = A"| A subgraph "Unreachable but not freed" NOTE["del A, del B
Still refcount=1 each!
Memory leak without GC"] end style NOTE fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Generational Garbage Collection
steps.explanation: Python's GC is **generational**: objects are grouped by age. Young objects (gen 0) are checked often; old objects (gen 2) rarely. Most objects die young.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Generation 0 (Young)" G0["New objects
Collected frequently"] end subgraph "Generation 1" G1["Survived 1 collection
Collected less often"] end subgraph "Generation 2 (Old)" G2["Long-lived
Collected rarely"] end G0 -->|"Survives"| G1 G1 -->|"Survives"| G2 style G0 fill:#FFCDD2 style G2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When GC Runs
steps.explanation: GC runs automatically when allocations exceed a threshold. You can also trigger it manually with `gc.collect()` or disable it with `gc.disable()`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GC Triggers" T1["Automatic: allocation threshold"] T2["Manual: gc.collect()"] T3["Exit: interpreter shutdown"] end subgraph "Control" C["import gc
gc.collect() # Force collection
gc.disable() # Turn off GC
gc.get_threshold() # View thresholds"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Memory Optimization Tips
steps.explanation: Use `__slots__` to reduce instance memory. Prefer generators over lists for large sequences. Use `weakref` to avoid preventing collection.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Optimization Techniques" S["__slots__: No instance __dict__"] G["Generators: Lazy evaluation"] W["weakref: Don't increase refcount"] P["Object pools: Reuse objects"] end subgraph "Example" Code["class Point:
__slots__ = ('x', 'y') # Saves ~100 bytes/instance

import weakref
ref = weakref.ref(obj) # Won't prevent GC"] end style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Memory Profiling
steps.explanation: Use `tracemalloc` to track allocations, `sys.getsizeof()` for object size, and `memory_profiler` for line-by-line memory usage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Profiling Tools" TM["tracemalloc
Track allocations"] GS["sys.getsizeof()
Object size"] MP["memory_profiler
@profile decorator"] OP["objgraph
Reference graphs"] end subgraph "Example" Code["import tracemalloc
tracemalloc.start()
# ... code ...
snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
top_stats = snapshot.statistics('lineno')"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Memory Issues
steps.explanation: Watch for: large object accumulation, unclosed resources, circular references with __del__, and global caches that grow unbounded.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Common Issues"
        I1["Large lists/dicts in memory"]
        I2["Unclosed files/connections"]
        I3["__del__ preventing GC"]
        I4["Global cache growth"]
    end
    subgraph "Solutions"
        S1["Use generators"]
        S2["Context managers (with)"]
        S3["Avoid __del__"]
        S4["LRU cache with maxsize"]
    end
    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S2
    I3 --> S3
    I4 --> S4
    style I1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Import System
fields.description: Module search path, __init__.py, namespace packages

steps.title: Import Search Order
steps.explanation: When you `import foo`, Python searches in order: 1) Built-in modules 2) `sys.path` locations (current dir, PYTHONPATH, installed packages).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A["import foo"] --> B{Built-in?}
    B -->|Yes| C["Use built-in module"]
    B -->|No| D["Search sys.path"]
    D --> E["Current directory"]
    D --> F["PYTHONPATH"]
    D --> G["site-packages"]
    E --> H{Found?}
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I["Load module"]
    H -->|No| J["ImportError"]
    style J fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: sys.path Explained
steps.explanation: `sys.path` is a list of directories Python searches. Modify it to add custom locations. The first entry is usually the script's directory or empty string (CWD).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "sys.path Contents" P1["'' # Current directory"] P2["PYTHONPATH entries"] P3["Standard library"] P4["site-packages"] end subgraph "Modify" Code["import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '/my/modules')
# Now imports search here first"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Module vs Package
steps.explanation: A **module** is a single .py file. A **package** is a directory with `__init__.py`. Packages can contain modules and sub-packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Module"
        MOD["utils.py  # Single file"]
    end
    subgraph "Package"
        PKG["mypackage/"]
        INIT["__init__.py"]
        M1["module1.py"]
        M2["module2.py"]
        SUB["subpackage/"]
        SINIT["__init__.py"]
    end
    PKG --> INIT
    PKG --> M1
    PKG --> M2
    PKG --> SUB
    SUB --> SINIT
    style INIT fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: __init__.py Purpose
steps.explanation: `__init__.py` makes a directory a package. It runs when the package is imported. Use it to expose APIs, set `__all__`, or initialize package state.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__init__.py Contents" Code["# mypackage/__init__.py

from .module1 import func1
from .module2 import Class2

__all__ = ['func1', 'Class2']

__version__ = '1.0.0'"] end subgraph "Effect" E1["from mypackage import func1 # Works"] E2["Exposes clean public API"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Namespace Packages (PEP 420)
steps.explanation: Python 3.3+ supports **namespace packages**: packages without `__init__.py`. Multiple directories can contribute to the same package. Useful for plugins.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Namespace Package" D1["path1/mypkg/module1.py"] D2["path2/mypkg/module2.py"] end subgraph "Result" R["import mypkg.module1 # From path1
import mypkg.module2 # From path2

mypkg combines both!"] end D1 --> R D2 --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Relative vs Absolute Imports
steps.explanation: **Absolute imports** use full path from project root. **Relative imports** use dots (`.` = current package, `..` = parent). Relative imports only work inside packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Absolute Import" A["from mypackage.utils import helper"] end subgraph "Relative Import" R1["from . import sibling # Same package"] R2["from .utils import helper # Same package"] R3["from .. import parent # Parent package"] R4["from ..other import func # Sibling of parent"] end subgraph "Best Practice" BP["Use absolute for clarity
Relative OK within package"] end style A fill:#E8F5E9 style BP fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Import Caching (sys.modules)
steps.explanation: Python caches imported modules in `sys.modules`. Subsequent imports return the cached module. This ensures modules initialize once.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "First Import" F1["import foo"] F2["Find foo.py"] F3["Execute code"] F4["Store in sys.modules"] end subgraph "Second Import" S1["import foo"] S2["Check sys.modules"] S3["Return cached module"] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 S1 --> S2 --> S3 subgraph "Force Reload" R["import importlib
importlib.reload(foo)"] end style S3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Import Patterns
steps.explanation: Follow these patterns: use absolute imports, lazy imports for heavy modules, conditional imports for optional dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good Patterns" G1["from mypackage import Module # Absolute"] G2["def func():
import heavy # Lazy import"] G3["try:
import optional
except ImportError:
optional = None"] end subgraph "Avoid" A1["from module import * # Pollutes namespace"] A2["Circular imports # A imports B imports A"] end style G1 fill:#E8F5E9 style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Context Managers
fields.description: __enter__/__exit__ protocol flow

steps.title: The with Statement
steps.explanation: The `with` statement ensures cleanup happens even if errors occur. It calls `__enter__` on entry and `__exit__` on exit (success or exception).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A["with open('file') as f:"] --> B["f.__enter__()"]
    B --> C["Execute block"]
    C --> D["f.__exit__()"]
    D --> E["Cleanup done!"]

    subgraph "Even on error"
        ERR["Exception raised"]
        EX["__exit__ still called!"]
    end
    C --> ERR
    ERR --> EX
    EX --> D
    style E fill:#E8F5E9
    style EX fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Protocol
steps.explanation: Context managers implement `__enter__` (returns value for 'as' clause) and `__exit__` (handles cleanup, receives exception info if any).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__enter__" EN["def __enter__(self):
# Setup
return self # or other value"] end subgraph "__exit__" EX["def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
# Cleanup
# Return True to suppress exception
return False"] end subgraph "Parameters" P1["exc_type: Exception class or None"] P2["exc_val: Exception instance or None"] P3["exc_tb: Traceback or None"] end style EN fill:#E8F5E9 style EX fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Simple Example
steps.explanation: A context manager that times code execution. `__enter__` starts the timer, `__exit__` stops it and prints duration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Timer Context Manager" Code["import time

class Timer:
def __enter__(self):
self.start = time.time()
return self

def __exit__(self, *args):
elapsed = time.time() - self.start
print(f'Took {elapsed:.2f}s')
return False"] end subgraph "Usage" Use["with Timer():
do_work() # Prints timing"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Exception Handling in __exit__
steps.explanation: If the block raises an exception, `__exit__` receives it. Return `True` to suppress it, `False` (or nothing) to let it propagate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "__exit__ Exception Flow" A["Exception in block"] B["__exit__(exc_type, exc_val, tb)"] C{Return True?} C -->|Yes| D["Exception suppressed"] C -->|No| E["Exception re-raised"] end subgraph "Example" Code["def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, tb):
if exc_type is ValueError:
print(f'Caught: {exc_val}')
return True # Suppress
return False # Re-raise others"] end style D fill:#E8F5E9 style E fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: @contextmanager Decorator
steps.explanation: Use `@contextmanager` from `contextlib` to create context managers with generators. Yield separates setup from cleanup.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "@contextmanager" Code["from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def timer():
start = time.time() # Setup
try:
yield start # Value for 'as'
finally:
print(f'Took {time.time()-start:.2f}s') # Cleanup"] end subgraph "Simpler Than" CLS["No need for class!
Just a function with yield"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Nested Context Managers
steps.explanation: You can nest `with` statements or use multiple managers in one line. Python 3.10+ allows parenthesized multi-line syntax.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multiple Managers" M1["# Python 3.9+
with open('a') as f1, open('b') as f2:
..."] M2["# Python 3.10+
with (
open('a') as f1,
open('b') as f2,
):
..."] end subgraph "contextlib.ExitStack" ES["with ExitStack() as stack:
files = [stack.enter_context(open(f))
for f in filenames]"] end style M2 fill:#E8F5E9 style ES fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Context Managers
steps.explanation: Standard library provides many: file objects, locks, database connections, `contextlib.suppress`, `contextlib.redirect_stdout`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Built-in"
        B1["open('file')  # Auto-close"]
        B2["threading.Lock()  # Auto-release"]
        B3["decimal.localcontext()  # Temp precision"]
    end
    subgraph "contextlib"
        C1["suppress(Exception)  # Ignore errors"]
        C2["redirect_stdout(f)  # Redirect print"]
        C3["closing(obj)  # Ensure .close()"]
        C4["nullcontext()  # No-op manager"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Async Context Managers
steps.explanation: For async code, use `async with` and implement `__aenter__`/`__aexit__`. These are async methods that can await.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Async Context Manager" Code["class AsyncDB:
async def __aenter__(self):
await self.connect()
return self

async def __aexit__(self, *args):
await self.disconnect()

async with AsyncDB() as db:
await db.query(...)"] end subgraph "@asynccontextmanager" AC["@asynccontextmanager
async def db_session():
db = await connect()
try:
yield db
finally:
await disconnect()"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Iterator Protocol
fields.description: __iter__/__next__ state machine

steps.title: Iterable vs Iterator
steps.explanation: **Iterable**: has `__iter__()` that returns an iterator. **Iterator**: has `__next__()` that returns items, raises `StopIteration` when done.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Iterable" IT["Has __iter__()
Returns an iterator"] EX1["list, tuple, str, dict"] end subgraph "Iterator" ITR["Has __next__()
Returns next item
Raises StopIteration"] EX2["list_iterator, generator"] end IT -->|"__iter__()"| ITR style IT fill:#E3F2FD style ITR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: How for Loops Work
steps.explanation: The `for` loop calls `iter()` on the iterable, then repeatedly calls `next()` until `StopIteration` is raised. This is why you can't iterate an iterator twice.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant for as for loop
    participant iterable as list
    participant iterator as list_iterator

    for->>iterable: iter(iterable)
    iterable-->>for: iterator
    loop Until StopIteration
        for->>iterator: next(iterator)
        iterator-->>for: item
        for->>for: Execute loop body
    end
    iterator-->>for: StopIteration
    for->>for: Exit loop

steps.title: Implementing an Iterator
steps.explanation: Create a class with `__iter__` (returns self) and `__next__` (returns next item or raises `StopIteration`). Maintain state to track position.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Iterator Class" Code["class CountUp:
def __init__(self, limit):
self.limit = limit
self.current = 0

def __iter__(self):
return self # Iterator is its own iterable

def __next__(self):
if self.current >= self.limit:
raise StopIteration
self.current += 1
return self.current"] end subgraph "Usage" U["for n in CountUp(3):
print(n) # 1, 2, 3"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Iterators Are One-Use
steps.explanation: Iterators can only be consumed once. After exhaustion, they keep raising `StopIteration`. To iterate again, get a new iterator from the iterable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "One-Use Problem" A["nums = iter([1, 2, 3])"] B["list(nums) # [1, 2, 3]"] C["list(nums) # [] Exhausted!"] end subgraph "Solution" S["nums = [1, 2, 3] # Iterable, not iterator
list(nums) # [1, 2, 3]
list(nums) # [1, 2, 3] Works!"] end A --> B --> C style C fill:#FFCDD2 style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: iter() and next() Functions
steps.explanation: `iter(obj)` calls `obj.__iter__()`. `next(iterator)` calls `iterator.__next__()`. Use `next(it, default)` to avoid StopIteration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Built-in Functions" I["iter(obj) # Returns iterator
iter(obj.__iter__())"] N["next(it) # Next item or StopIteration
next(it, default) # Default if exhausted"] end subgraph "Example" Code["nums = iter([1, 2])
next(nums) # 1
next(nums) # 2
next(nums, 'done') # 'done'"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Separating Iterable and Iterator
steps.explanation: For reusable iteration, separate iterable (has `__iter__`) from iterator (has `__next__`). The iterable creates a fresh iterator each time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Separate Classes" IT["class Range:
def __init__(self, n):
self.n = n

def __iter__(self):
return RangeIterator(self.n)"] ITR["class RangeIterator:
def __init__(self, n):
self.n = n
self.i = 0

def __next__(self):
if self.i >= self.n:
raise StopIteration
self.i += 1
return self.i - 1"] end IT -->|"Creates new"| ITR style IT fill:#E3F2FD style ITR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Iterator Tools
steps.explanation: `itertools` provides powerful iterator utilities: `chain`, `cycle`, `islice`, `zip_longest`, `groupby`, and more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "itertools" C["chain(a, b) # Concat iterables"] CY["cycle(it) # Infinite cycle"] IS["islice(it, n) # First n items"] ZL["zip_longest(a, b) # Zip with fill"] G["groupby(it, key) # Group consecutive"] end subgraph "Example" Code["from itertools import islice, cycle

colors = cycle(['red', 'green', 'blue'])
first_5 = list(islice(colors, 5))
# ['red', 'green', 'blue', 'red', 'green']"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Async Iterators
steps.explanation: For async iteration, implement `__aiter__` and `__anext__`. Use `async for` to iterate. The async versions can await.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Async Iterator" Code["class AsyncRange:
def __init__(self, n):
self.n = n
self.i = 0

def __aiter__(self):
return self

async def __anext__(self):
if self.i >= self.n:
raise StopAsyncIteration
await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
self.i += 1
return self.i"] end subgraph "Usage" U["async for n in AsyncRange(3):
print(n)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Generator Execution
fields.description: yield suspension and resumption

steps.title: What is a Generator?
steps.explanation: A generator is a function that uses `yield` instead of `return`. Calling it returns a generator object, which is an iterator. Values are produced lazily on demand.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Generator Function" GF["def count():
yield 1
yield 2
yield 3"] end subgraph "Calling It" C["gen = count() # Returns generator object"] N1["next(gen) # 1"] N2["next(gen) # 2"] N3["next(gen) # 3"] N4["next(gen) # StopIteration"] end GF --> C --> N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 style GF fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: yield Suspension
steps.explanation: When `yield` is hit, execution suspends and the value is returned. The function's state (local variables, instruction pointer) is preserved until `next()` is called again.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant Generator

    Caller->>Generator: next(gen)
    Generator->>Generator: Execute until yield
    Generator-->>Caller: yielded value
    Note over Generator: Suspended (state saved)

    Caller->>Generator: next(gen)
    Generator->>Generator: Resume from yield
    Generator->>Generator: Execute until next yield
    Generator-->>Caller: yielded value

steps.title: Generator State Machine
steps.explanation: A generator progresses through states: created (not started), suspended (at yield), running (executing), closed (finished or closed).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    A["Created"] -->|"next()"| B["Running"]
    B -->|"yield"| C["Suspended"]
    C -->|"next()"| B
    B -->|"return/end"| D["Closed"]
    C -->|"close()"| D
    A -->|"close()"| D
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#E8F5E9
    style D fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Lazy Evaluation Benefits
steps.explanation: Generators are memory-efficient. They produce values one at a time, not all at once. Perfect for large/infinite sequences.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "List (Eager)" L["[x**2 for x in range(1000000)]
Creates 1M items in memory"] end subgraph "Generator (Lazy)" G["(x**2 for x in range(1000000))
Creates items one at a time"] end L --> LM["High memory"] G --> GM["Low memory"] style LM fill:#FFCDD2 style GM fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Generator Expressions
steps.explanation: Generator expressions are like list comprehensions but with parentheses. They create generators without defining a function.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "List Comprehension" LC["[x**2 for x in range(10)]
Returns list"] end subgraph "Generator Expression" GE["(x**2 for x in range(10))
Returns generator"] end subgraph "Usage" U["sum(x**2 for x in range(10)) # No extra parens needed"] end style GE fill:#E8F5E9 style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: yield from
steps.explanation: `yield from` delegates to another iterable/generator. It's cleaner than a for loop and properly handles .send() and .throw().
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without yield from" W["def chain(a, b):
for x in a:
yield x
for x in b:
yield x"] end subgraph "With yield from" Y["def chain(a, b):
yield from a
yield from b"] end W --> Y subgraph "Benefits" B["Cleaner syntax
Proper exception handling
send/throw delegation"] end style Y fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Generator Methods
steps.explanation: Generators have `.send()`, `.throw()`, and `.close()` methods. `.send()` sends a value INTO the generator (yield becomes an expression).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Generator Methods" S["gen.send(value)
Resume and send value"] T["gen.throw(exception)
Raise exception at yield"] C["gen.close()
Close generator"] end subgraph "send() Example" Code["def echo():
while True:
received = yield
print(f'Got: {received}')

g = echo()
next(g) # Start generator
g.send('hello') # Got: hello"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Async Generators
steps.explanation: `async def` with `yield` creates async generators. Use `async for` to iterate. They can await inside and yield values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Async Generator" Code["async def fetch_pages(urls):
for url in urls:
response = await fetch(url)
yield response.text

async for page in fetch_pages(urls):
process(page)"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Async I/O between yields"] B2["Lazy async iteration"] B3["Memory efficient streaming"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Decorator Execution Order
fields.description: Stacked decorators evaluation

steps.title: Decorator Basics
steps.explanation: A decorator is a function that wraps another function. `@decorator` before a function definition is syntactic sugar for `func = decorator(func)`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Decorator Syntax" A["@decorator
def func(): ..."] end subgraph "Equivalent To" B["def func(): ...
func = decorator(func)"] end A <-->|"Same!"| B style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Stacked Decorators
steps.explanation: Multiple decorators stack bottom-up at definition time, but execute wrappers top-down at call time. The order matters!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Definition Order" D["@A
@B
@C
def func(): ..."] EQ["func = A(B(C(func)))"] end D --> EQ subgraph "Application Order" O1["C wraps func"] O2["B wraps C's result"] O3["A wraps B's result"] end O1 --> O2 --> O3 style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Call Time Execution
steps.explanation: When calling the decorated function, wrappers execute **top-down** (outer to inner), then the original function, then return **bottom-up** (inner to outer).
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant A as @A wrapper
    participant B as @B wrapper
    participant func as original func

    Caller->>A: func()
    A->>B: call wrapped
    B->>func: call original
    func-->>B: return
    B-->>A: return (modified?)
    A-->>Caller: return (modified?)

steps.title: Example: Logging Order
steps.explanation: With `@log_entry` and `@log_exit` stacked, you'll see entry logs top-down and exit logs bottom-up.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" Code["@log_entry # Logs 'Entering A'
@log_exit # Logs 'Exiting B'
def func(): ...
