# Call: func()"] end subgraph "Output Order" O1["1. Entering A (outer wrapper entry)"] O2["2. Entering B (inner wrapper entry)"] O3["3. func() executes"] O4["4. Exiting B (inner wrapper exit)"] O5["5. Exiting A (outer wrapper exit)"] end O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> O5 style O1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Ordering Issues
steps.explanation: Order matters for things like authentication before validation, or caching before timing. Get the order wrong and you get bugs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correct Order" C["@login_required # Check auth first
@validate_input # Then validate
@cache_result # Cache validated
def api_endpoint(): ..."] end subgraph "Wrong Order" W["@cache_result # Caches auth errors!
@login_required
@validate_input
def api_endpoint(): ..."] end style C fill:#E8F5E9 style W fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Decorators with Arguments
steps.explanation: Decorators with arguments are actually factories: they return the real decorator. This adds another layer of function calls.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With Arguments" D["@repeat(3)
def greet(): print('Hi')"] EQ["# Equivalent to:
greet = repeat(3)(greet)"] end subgraph "Implementation" Code["def repeat(n):
def decorator(func):
def wrapper(*args):
for _ in range(n):
func(*args)
return wrapper
return decorator"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: functools.wraps
steps.explanation: Use `@functools.wraps(func)` on wrapper functions to preserve the original function's metadata (`__name__`, `__doc__`, etc.).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without wraps" W1["decorated.__name__ # 'wrapper'
decorated.__doc__ # wrapper's doc"] end subgraph "With wraps" Code["from functools import wraps

def my_decorator(func):
@wraps(func) # Preserve metadata
def wrapper(*args):
return func(*args)
return wrapper

decorated.__name__ # Original name!"] end style W1 fill:#FFCDD2 style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Class-Based Decorators
steps.explanation: Classes with `__call__` can be decorators. `__init__` receives the function or arguments, `__call__` is invoked when calling the decorated function.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Class Decorator" Code["class CountCalls:
def __init__(self, func):
self.func = func
self.count = 0

def __call__(self, *args):
self.count += 1
return self.func(*args)

@CountCalls
def greet(): print('Hi')

greet() # count = 1
greet() # count = 2"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Exception Handling
fields.description: Try/except/else/finally control flow

steps.title: Exception Flow Overview
steps.explanation: `try/except/else/finally` provides complete exception handling. `else` runs if no exception, `finally` ALWAYS runs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    A["try block"] --> B{Exception?}
    B -->|Yes| C["except block"]
    B -->|No| D["else block"]
    C --> E["finally block"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Continue execution"]
    style D fill:#E8F5E9
    style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Basic try/except
steps.explanation: The `try` block contains risky code. The `except` block catches and handles exceptions. Without except, exceptions propagate up.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Basic Pattern" Code["try:
risky_operation()
except ValueError as e:
print(f'Error: {e}')
except TypeError:
print('Type error')"] end subgraph "Multiple Types" M["except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
# Handle both"] end subgraph "Catch All" CA["except Exception as e:
# Catch most exceptions
# Avoid bare 'except:'"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The else Clause
steps.explanation: `else` runs ONLY if no exception was raised in try. Use it for code that should run on success but isn't part of the risky operation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With else" Code["try:
result = parse(data)
except ParseError:
result = default
else:
# Only if parse succeeded
process(result)
log_success()"] end subgraph "Why else?" W["Separates success logic
from error handling"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The finally Clause
steps.explanation: `finally` ALWAYS runs—after try, except, or else. Use it for cleanup (close files, release locks). It runs even if there's a return!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "finally Always Runs" A["After success"] B["After exception"] C["After return"] D["After break/continue"] end subgraph "Example" Code["try:
f = open('file')
return f.read()
except IOError:
return 'default'
finally:
f.close() # Always runs!"] end A --> F["finally"] B --> F C --> F D --> F style F fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Exception Hierarchy
steps.explanation: Exceptions form a hierarchy. Catching a parent catches all children. `BaseException` is root; `Exception` is for most errors; `SystemExit`/`KeyboardInterrupt` should rarely be caught.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB BE["BaseException"] SE["SystemExit"] KI["KeyboardInterrupt"] GE["GeneratorExit"] EX["Exception"] VE["ValueError"] TE["TypeError"] RE["RuntimeError"] BE --> SE BE --> KI BE --> GE BE --> EX EX --> VE EX --> TE EX --> RE subgraph "Tip" T["Catch Exception, not BaseException
Let SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt propagate"] end style EX fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Raising Exceptions
steps.explanation: Use `raise` to throw exceptions. `raise Exception('msg')` creates new. `raise` alone re-raises current. `raise ... from ...` chains exceptions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Raising" R1["raise ValueError('Invalid') # New exception"] R2["raise # Re-raise current exception"] R3["raise NewError() from original # Chain"] end subgraph "Chaining" Code["try:
process()
except IOError as e:
raise ProcessingError('Failed') from e
# Shows both in traceback"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Custom Exceptions
steps.explanation: Create custom exceptions by inheriting from `Exception`. Add attributes for extra context. Use a hierarchy for your domain.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Custom Exception" Code["class APIError(Exception):
'''Base API exception'''
def __init__(self, message, status_code):
super().__init__(message)
self.status_code = status_code

class NotFoundError(APIError):
def __init__(self, resource):
super().__init__(f'{resource} not found', 404)"] end subgraph "Usage" U["try:
get_user(id)
except NotFoundError as e:
print(f'{e}: {e.status_code}')"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Context Manager Pattern
steps.explanation: For cleanup, prefer context managers (`with`) over `try/finally`. They're cleaner and can't forget the cleanup.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "try/finally" TF["f = open('file')
try:
data = f.read()
finally:
f.close()"] end subgraph "Context Manager" CM["with open('file') as f:
data = f.read()
# Auto-closed!"] end TF -->|"Prefer"| CM style CM fill:#E8F5E9 style TF fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: ETL vs ELT Pipeline
fields.description: Compare extract-transform-load vs extract-load-transform approaches

steps.title: ETL vs ELT Overview
steps.explanation: **ETL** (Extract-Transform-Load) transforms data before loading into the target. **ELT** (Extract-Load-Transform) loads raw data first, then transforms using the target system's compute power. The choice depends on your infrastructure and data volume.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph ETL["ETL - Traditional"]
        E1["Extract"] --> T1["Transform"] --> L1["Load"]
    end
    subgraph ELT["ELT - Modern"]
        E2["Extract"] --> L2["Load"] --> T2["Transform"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style T2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ETL: Transform Before Loading
steps.explanation: In ETL, an **intermediate server** handles transformations. Data is cleaned, normalized, and aggregated before reaching the warehouse. This was necessary when storage was expensive and compute was limited in target systems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Sources S1["Database"] S2["API"] S3["Files"] end subgraph "ETL Server" E["Extract"] T["Transform
• Clean
• Normalize
• Aggregate"] L["Load"] end subgraph Target DW["Data Warehouse"] end S1 & S2 & S3 --> E --> T --> L --> DW style T fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: ELT: Transform After Loading
steps.explanation: In ELT, raw data lands in a **staging area** first, then powerful cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks) handle transformations. This leverages cheap storage and scalable compute in modern platforms.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph Sources S1["Database"] S2["API"] S3["Files"] end subgraph "Cloud Platform" subgraph "Raw Zone" R["Raw/Staging"] end subgraph "Transform" T["dbt / SQL
Spark / Python"] end subgraph "Curated" C["Clean Tables"] end end S1 & S2 & S3 --> R --> T --> C style T fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: When to Use ETL
steps.explanation: ETL is preferred when: data must be **cleaned before storage** (compliance), target system has **limited compute**, you need **real-time transformations**, or working with **legacy on-prem systems**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use ETL When"
        A["Compliance requires clean data only"]
        B["Target has limited compute"]
        C["Real-time transformation needed"]
        D["Legacy on-prem infrastructure"]
        E["Sensitive data must be masked early"]
    end
    subgraph "Examples"
        Ex1["Oracle Data Integrator"]
        Ex2["Informatica PowerCenter"]
        Ex3["SSIS"]
    end
    style A fill:#FFF3E0
    style B fill:#FFF3E0
    style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: When to Use ELT
steps.explanation: ELT shines with **cloud data platforms**, **big data volumes**, when you need to **preserve raw data**, or when transformation logic **changes frequently**. Modern data stacks almost always use ELT.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use ELT When"
        A["Cloud warehouse available"]
        B["Large data volumes"]
        C["Need raw data preserved"]
        D["Transformation logic evolves"]
        E["Multiple teams need different views"]
    end
    subgraph "Examples"
        Ex1["Fivetran + Snowflake + dbt"]
        Ex2["Airbyte + BigQuery + dbt"]
        Ex3["Databricks Lakehouse"]
    end
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style B fill:#E8F5E9
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Modern Data Stack
steps.explanation: The modern ELT stack separates concerns: **ingestion tools** (Fivetran, Airbyte) extract and load, **transformation tools** (dbt) handle SQL transforms, **orchestrators** (Airflow, Dagster) manage workflows.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Ingestion F["Fivetran
Airbyte
Stitch"] end subgraph Storage S["Snowflake
BigQuery
Databricks"] end subgraph Transform T["dbt
SQL
Spark"] end subgraph Orchestration O["Airflow
Dagster
Prefect"] end F -->|"E+L"| S S -->|"T"| T O -.->|"orchestrate"| F & S & T style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Comparison Summary
steps.explanation: ETL transforms in transit (separate compute), ELT transforms in place (warehouse compute). ELT is dominant in modern cloud architectures due to scalability and flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Comparison
        subgraph ETL
            E1["Transform: Separate server"]
            E2["Storage: Only clean data"]
            E3["Flexibility: Low"]
            E4["Cost: Higher compute"]
        end
        subgraph ELT
            L1["Transform: In warehouse"]
            L2["Storage: Raw + clean"]
            L3["Flexibility: High"]
            L4["Cost: Cheap storage"]
        end
    end
    style E1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style L1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L4 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Data Lake vs Warehouse vs Lakehouse
fields.description: Architecture comparison and trade-offs between data storage paradigms

steps.title: Three Paradigms
steps.explanation: Data storage has evolved: **Data Warehouses** (structured, schema-on-write), **Data Lakes** (raw, schema-on-read), and **Lakehouses** (combining both). Each solves different problems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "1990s-2000s" DW["Data Warehouse
Structured"] end subgraph "2010s" DL["Data Lake
Raw/Unstructured"] end subgraph "2020s" LH["Lakehouse
Best of Both"] end DW --> DL --> LH style DW fill:#E3F2FD style DL fill:#FFF3E0 style LH fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Data Warehouse
steps.explanation: A **Data Warehouse** stores structured, processed data optimized for BI and analytics. Schema is defined **before** loading (schema-on-write). SQL is the primary interface.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Data Warehouse"
        subgraph Characteristics
            A["Structured data only"]
            B["Schema-on-write"]
            C["Optimized for BI/SQL"]
            D["Expensive storage"]
            E["ACID compliant"]
        end
        subgraph Examples
            Ex1["Snowflake"]
            Ex2["BigQuery"]
            Ex3["Redshift"]
            Ex4["Teradata"]
        end
    end
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style B fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Data Lake
steps.explanation: A **Data Lake** stores raw data in any format (structured, semi-structured, unstructured). Schema is applied **when reading** (schema-on-read). Cheap storage, flexible, but can become a 'data swamp'.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Data Lake"
        subgraph "Any Format"
            J["JSON"]
            P["Parquet"]
            I["Images"]
            V["Video"]
            L["Logs"]
        end
        subgraph Characteristics
            A["Schema-on-read"]
            B["Cheap object storage"]
            C["No ACID (originally)"]
            D["Risk of data swamp"]
        end
        subgraph Examples
            S3["AWS S3"]
            ADLS["Azure ADLS"]
            GCS["GCS"]
        end
    end
    style A fill:#FFF3E0
    style B fill:#FFF3E0
    style D fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: The Data Swamp Problem
steps.explanation: Without governance, data lakes become **data swamps**: no metadata, no quality, no discoverability. Organizations struggled to get value from their lakes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Data Lake"
        D1["Raw data"]
    end
    subgraph "Data Swamp"
        S1["No documentation"]
        S2["Unknown quality"]
        S3["Duplicate data"]
        S4["Stale data"]
        S5["No lineage"]
    end
    D1 -->|"Without governance"| S1 & S2 & S3 & S4 & S5
    style S1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style S5 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Lakehouse Architecture
steps.explanation: A **Lakehouse** combines cheap lake storage with warehouse features: **ACID transactions**, **schema enforcement**, **time travel**, and **unified governance**. Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Hudi enable this.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Lakehouse" subgraph "Storage Layer" S["Object Storage
(S3, ADLS, GCS)"] end subgraph "Table Format" T["Delta Lake / Iceberg / Hudi"] end subgraph "Features" A["ACID Transactions"] B["Schema Evolution"] C["Time Travel"] D["Unified Batch + Stream"] end subgraph "Compute" Q["SQL + ML + Python"] end end S --> T --> Q T --- A & B & C & D style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Table Formats Enable Lakehouse
steps.explanation: Open table formats like **Delta Lake**, **Apache Iceberg**, and **Apache Hudi** add a metadata layer on top of Parquet files, enabling ACID, schema evolution, and time travel.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Table Formats" D["Delta Lake
(Databricks)"] I["Apache Iceberg
(Netflix)"] H["Apache Hudi
(Uber)"] end subgraph "They Provide" A["Transaction log"] B["Snapshot isolation"] C["Schema evolution"] D2["Compaction"] E["Time travel"] end D & I & H --> A & B & C & D2 & E style D fill:#E8F5E9 style I fill:#E3F2FD style H fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Architecture Comparison
steps.explanation: Each architecture makes different trade-offs. Warehouses prioritize reliability and SQL. Lakes prioritize flexibility and cost. Lakehouses aim for both.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Comparison"
        subgraph "Warehouse"
            W1["Structured only"]
            W2["Schema-on-write"]
            W3["ACID: Yes"]
            W4["Cost: $$$"]
        end
        subgraph "Lake"
            L1["Any format"]
            L2["Schema-on-read"]
            L3["ACID: No*"]
            L4["Cost: $"]
        end
        subgraph "Lakehouse"
            H1["Any format"]
            H2["Schema enforcement"]
            H3["ACID: Yes"]
            H4["Cost: $"]
        end
    end
    style W3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L4 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Choose based on your needs: **Warehouse** for BI-heavy workloads with existing tooling. **Lake** for ML and unstructured data. **Lakehouse** for unified analytics + ML on modern platforms.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Choose Warehouse"
        W["Heavy BI/SQL usage"]
        W2["Existing Snowflake/BQ"]
        W3["Small-medium data"]
    end
    subgraph "Choose Lake"
        L["ML/AI workloads"]
        L2["Unstructured data"]
        L3["Cost-sensitive"]
    end
    subgraph "Choose Lakehouse"
        H["Unified analytics + ML"]
        H2["Large scale"]
        H3["Need flexibility + reliability"]
    end
    style H fill:#E8F5E9
    style H2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Batch vs Stream Processing
fields.description: When to use each processing paradigm and Lambda/Kappa architectures

steps.title: Processing Paradigms
steps.explanation: **Batch processing** handles data in chunks at scheduled intervals. **Stream processing** handles data continuously as it arrives. Each has different latency, complexity, and use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Batch["Batch Processing"]
        B1["Collect data"] --> B2["Process chunk"] --> B3["Store results"]
        B4["Latency: Minutes to hours"]
    end
    subgraph Stream["Stream Processing"]
        S1["Event arrives"] --> S2["Process immediately"] --> S3["Update state"]
        S4["Latency: Milliseconds to seconds"]
    end
    style B4 fill:#FFF3E0
    style S4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Batch Processing
steps.explanation: Batch processes bounded datasets—you know where it starts and ends. Run on schedules (hourly, daily). Simpler to reason about, easier to rerun, and more cost-efficient for large volumes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Batch Characteristics"
        A["Bounded data"]
        B["Scheduled runs"]
        C["High throughput"]
        D["Simpler logic"]
        E["Easy reprocessing"]
    end
    subgraph "Tools"
        T1["Apache Spark"]
        T2["Hadoop MapReduce"]
        T3["dbt"]
        T4["Airflow jobs"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Cases"
        U1["Daily reports"]
        U2["ML training"]
        U3["ETL pipelines"]
        U4["Data warehouse loads"]
    end
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style B fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Stream Processing
steps.explanation: Stream processes unbounded, continuous data. Events are processed as they arrive. Required for real-time dashboards, fraud detection, and live recommendations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Stream Characteristics"
        A["Unbounded data"]
        B["Continuous processing"]
        C["Low latency"]
        D["Complex state management"]
        E["Harder to reprocess"]
    end
    subgraph "Tools"
        T1["Apache Kafka"]
        T2["Apache Flink"]
        T3["Spark Streaming"]
        T4["Apache Pulsar"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Cases"
        U1["Real-time dashboards"]
        U2["Fraud detection"]
        U3["Live recommendations"]
        U4["IoT monitoring"]
    end
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Lambda Architecture
steps.explanation: **Lambda Architecture** runs batch and stream in parallel. The **batch layer** provides accuracy, the **speed layer** provides low latency. A **serving layer** merges results. Downside: maintaining two codebases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    D["Data Source"] --> B & S
    subgraph "Batch Layer"
        B["Batch Processing"]
        BV["Batch Views"]
    end
    subgraph "Speed Layer"
        S["Stream Processing"]
        SV["Real-time Views"]
    end
    subgraph "Serving Layer"
        SL["Merged Query"]
    end
    B --> BV --> SL
    S --> SV --> SL
    SL --> Q["Query"]
    style B fill:#E3F2FD
    style S fill:#E8F5E9
    style SL fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Kappa Architecture
steps.explanation: **Kappa Architecture** simplifies Lambda by using **only stream processing**. Historical data is replayed through the same stream pipeline. Single codebase, but requires a replayable log (Kafka).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB D["Data Source"] --> K["Kafka
(Replayable Log)"] K --> SP["Stream Processing"] SP --> SV["Serving Layer"] subgraph "Reprocessing" R["Replay from offset 0
Same code path"] end K -.-> R -.-> SP style SP fill:#E8F5E9 style K fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Lambda vs Kappa
steps.explanation: Lambda: two systems, proven pattern, complex maintenance. Kappa: one system, simpler, requires good stream infrastructure. Many modern teams prefer Kappa with tools like Flink or Spark Structured Streaming.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lambda
        L1["Two codebases"]
        L2["Batch + Stream"]
        L3["Complex merging"]
        L4["Proven at scale"]
    end
    subgraph Kappa
        K1["One codebase"]
        K2["Stream only"]
        K3["Simpler architecture"]
        K4["Needs replayable log"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style L3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style K1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style K3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Unified Batch + Stream
steps.explanation: Modern frameworks like **Apache Flink** and **Spark Structured Streaming** unify batch and stream with the same API. Write once, run as batch or stream. This is the current best practice.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Unified Processing" API["Single API
(DataFrames / Tables)"] B["Batch Execution"] S["Stream Execution"] end subgraph "Frameworks" F1["Apache Flink"] F2["Spark Structured Streaming"] F3["Apache Beam"] end API --> B API --> S F1 & F2 & F3 --> API style API fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Choosing Your Approach
steps.explanation: Start with **batch** unless you have real-time requirements. Add **streaming** for specific low-latency needs. Use **unified frameworks** to minimize code duplication.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{Need real-time?} Q1 -->|No| B["Start with Batch
Simpler, cheaper"] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{How complex?} Q2 -->|Simple| S["Add Stream layer"] Q2 -->|Complex| U["Unified framework
Flink / Spark"] B --> M["Migrate to unified
as needs grow"] style B fill:#E3F2FD style U fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: OLTP vs OLAP Systems
fields.description: Transactional vs analytical database design patterns

steps.title: Two Types of Systems
steps.explanation: **OLTP** (Online Transaction Processing) handles day-to-day operations—orders, payments, updates. **OLAP** (Online Analytical Processing) handles analysis—reports, aggregations, trends. Different purposes, different designs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph OLTP["OLTP - Transactional"]
        T1["Insert order"]
        T2["Update inventory"]
        T3["Process payment"]
    end
    subgraph OLAP["OLAP - Analytical"]
        A1["Monthly revenue"]
        A2["Customer trends"]
        A3["Product analysis"]
    end
    style OLTP fill:#E3F2FD
    style OLAP fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: OLTP Characteristics
steps.explanation: OLTP systems are optimized for **fast writes**, **row-level operations**, and **ACID compliance**. They're normalized (3NF) to minimize redundancy and ensure data integrity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "OLTP Design" A["Row-oriented storage"] B["Highly normalized (3NF)"] C["Many small transactions"] D["Low latency writes"] E["ACID compliant"] end subgraph "Examples" Ex1["PostgreSQL"] Ex2["MySQL"] Ex3["Oracle DB"] Ex4["SQL Server"] end subgraph "Queries" Q["SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE id = 12345"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: OLAP Characteristics
steps.explanation: OLAP systems are optimized for **fast reads**, **aggregations**, and **complex queries**. They're often denormalized (star/snowflake schema) for query performance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "OLAP Design" A["Column-oriented storage"] B["Denormalized schemas"] C["Complex aggregations"] D["High read throughput"] E["Historical data"] end subgraph "Examples" Ex1["Snowflake"] Ex2["BigQuery"] Ex3["Redshift"] Ex4["ClickHouse"] end subgraph "Queries" Q["SELECT region, SUM(revenue)
FROM sales
GROUP BY region"] end style A fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Row vs Column Storage
steps.explanation: OLTP uses **row storage** (all columns for a row together)—fast for fetching entire records. OLAP uses **column storage** (all values for a column together)—fast for aggregating specific columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Row-Oriented (OLTP)" R["Row 1: [id, name, price, qty]
Row 2: [id, name, price, qty]
Row 3: [id, name, price, qty]"] RQ["Get order #123
= Fast (1 disk read)"] end subgraph "Column-Oriented (OLAP)" C["id: [1, 2, 3, ...]
name: [A, B, C, ...]
price: [10, 20, 30, ...]"] CQ["SUM(price)
= Fast (read 1 column)"] end style RQ fill:#E3F2FD style CQ fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Data Flow
steps.explanation: OLTP systems feed OLAP systems. Data flows from operational databases through ETL/ELT pipelines into analytical warehouses. This separation prevents analytical queries from slowing down operations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Operations" O1["Web App"] O2["Mobile App"] O3["APIs"] end subgraph "OLTP" DB["PostgreSQL
MySQL"] end subgraph "Pipeline" P["ETL/ELT"] end subgraph "OLAP" DW["Snowflake
BigQuery"] end subgraph "Analytics" A1["Dashboards"] A2["Reports"] A3["ML Models"] end O1 & O2 & O3 --> DB --> P --> DW --> A1 & A2 & A3 style P fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Comparison Table
steps.explanation: The fundamental trade-offs: OLTP optimizes for operational correctness and speed, OLAP optimizes for analytical throughput and flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Comparison
        subgraph "OLTP"
            T1["Purpose: Operations"]
            T2["Queries: Simple, fast"]
            T3["Data: Current"]
            T4["Schema: Normalized"]
            T5["Size: GBs"]
        end
        subgraph "OLAP"
            A1["Purpose: Analysis"]
            A2["Queries: Complex, slow"]
            A3["Data: Historical"]
            A4["Schema: Denormalized"]
            A5["Size: TBs-PBs"]
        end
    end
    style T1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: HTAP: Hybrid Approach
steps.explanation: **HTAP** (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing) systems handle both workloads in one database. Examples: TiDB, SingleStore, CockroachDB. Useful when you can't tolerate ETL latency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "HTAP System"
        H["Single Database"]
        T["Transactional Engine"]
        A["Analytical Engine"]
        H --> T & A
    end
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B1["No ETL delay"]
        B2["Single source of truth"]
        B3["Simplified architecture"]
    end
    subgraph "Trade-offs"
        X1["More expensive"]
        X2["Less specialized"]
        X3["Newer technology"]
    end
    style H fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Star vs Snowflake Schema
fields.description: Dimensional modeling patterns for data warehouses

steps.title: Dimensional Modeling
steps.explanation: Dimensional modeling organizes data into **facts** (measurements) and **dimensions** (context). This denormalized structure is optimized for analytical queries, unlike the normalized structures in OLTP.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Key Concepts" F["Fact Table
Sales, Events, Metrics"] D["Dimension Tables
Who, What, When, Where"] end F --> D subgraph "Schema Types" S["Star Schema"] SF["Snowflake Schema"] end style F fill:#E3F2FD style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Fact Tables
steps.explanation: **Fact tables** store measurable events—sales transactions, page views, sensor readings. They contain foreign keys to dimensions and numeric measures (amounts, counts, durations).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "sales_fact"
        F1["sale_id (PK)"]
        F2["date_key (FK)"]
        F3["product_key (FK)"]
        F4["customer_key (FK)"]
        F5["store_key (FK)"]
        F6["---"]
        F7["quantity"]
        F8["unit_price"]
        F9["total_amount"]
    end
    subgraph "Characteristics"
        C1["Many rows (millions)"]
        C2["Numeric measures"]
        C3["Foreign keys"]
        C4["Grain: one event"]
    end
    style F7 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F8 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F9 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Dimension Tables
steps.explanation: **Dimension tables** provide context for facts—who bought it, what product, when, where. They have descriptive attributes used for filtering, grouping, and labeling.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "product_dim"
        P1["product_key (PK)"]
        P2["product_name"]
        P3["category"]
        P4["subcategory"]
        P5["brand"]
        P6["unit_cost"]
    end
    subgraph "Characteristics"
        C1["Fewer rows (thousands)"]
        C2["Descriptive attributes"]
        C3["Used for GROUP BY"]
        C4["Denormalized"]
    end
    style P2 fill:#E3F2FD
    style P3 fill:#E3F2FD
    style P4 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Star Schema
steps.explanation: In a **Star Schema**, the fact table sits at the center with dimension tables around it. Dimensions are **fully denormalized**—all attributes in one table. Simple, fast queries, but some redundancy.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    D1["dim_date"] --> F["fact_sales"]
    D2["dim_product"] --> F
    D3["dim_customer"] --> F
    D4["dim_store"] --> F
    style F fill:#E3F2FD
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Snowflake Schema
steps.explanation: In a **Snowflake Schema**, dimensions are **normalized** into sub-dimensions. Reduces redundancy but requires more JOINs. Named because the diagram looks like a snowflake.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    D1["dim_date"] --> F["fact_sales"]
    D2["dim_product"] --> F
    D3["dim_customer"] --> F
    D4["dim_store"] --> F

    subgraph "Normalized Dimensions"
        C["dim_category"] --> D2
        B["dim_brand"] --> D2
        CT["dim_city"] --> D4
        R["dim_region"] --> CT
    end
    style F fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#FFF3E0
    style B fill:#FFF3E0
    style CT fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Star vs Snowflake Comparison
steps.explanation: Star is simpler and faster for queries. Snowflake saves storage and maintains data integrity. Modern warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) make storage cheap, so star schema is usually preferred.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Star Schema"
        S1["Denormalized dims"]
        S2["Fewer JOINs"]
        S3["Faster queries"]
        S4["More storage"]
        S5["Simpler SQL"]
    end
    subgraph "Snowflake Schema"
        F1["Normalized dims"]
        F2["More JOINs"]
        F3["Slower queries"]
        F4["Less storage"]
        F5["Complex SQL"]
    end
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S5 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Example Query: Star Schema
steps.explanation: Star schema queries are straightforward—join fact to dimensions, filter on dimension attributes, aggregate measures. This simplicity is why analysts prefer star schemas.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Star Schema Query" Q["SELECT
d.year,
p.category,
SUM(f.total_amount)
FROM fact_sales f
JOIN dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key
JOIN dim_product p ON f.product_key = p.product_key
WHERE d.year = 2024
GROUP BY d.year, p.category"] end subgraph "Only 2 JOINs" J["fact → dim_date
fact → dim_product"] end style Q fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Use **star schema** as the default. Use **snowflake** only when storage is extremely limited or dimension integrity is critical. Consider **one big table (OBT)** for very simple analytics.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{Storage costly?} Q -->|Yes| SF["Snowflake
Normalize dims"] Q -->|No| Q2{Query simplicity?} Q2 -->|Important| ST["Star Schema
Denormalize dims"] Q2 -->|Very simple| OBT["One Big Table
No dims"] style ST fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Data Mesh Architecture
fields.description: Decentralized domain-oriented data ownership

steps.title: The Problem with Centralized Data
steps.explanation: Centralized data teams become bottlenecks. Domain teams wait for the data team to build pipelines. The data team lacks domain expertise. **Data Mesh** flips this by decentralizing ownership.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Centralized (Traditional)"
        D1["Sales Domain"] --> DT["Central Data Team"]
        D2["Marketing Domain"] --> DT
        D3["Finance Domain"] --> DT
        DT --> DW["Data Warehouse"]
    end
    subgraph "Problems"
        P1["Bottleneck"]
        P2["Lack of domain knowledge"]
        P3["Slow delivery"]
    end
    DT -.-> P1 & P2 & P3
    style DT fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Four Principles of Data Mesh
steps.explanation: Data Mesh is built on four principles: **domain ownership**, **data as a product**, **self-serve platform**, and **federated governance**. It's organizational, not just technical.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Data Mesh Principles" P1["Domain Ownership
Domains own their data"] P2["Data as a Product
Treat data like a product"] P3["Self-Serve Platform
Enable autonomy"] P4["Federated Governance
Standards + autonomy"] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 style P1 fill:#E3F2FD style P2 fill:#E8F5E9 style P3 fill:#FFF3E0 style P4 fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Domain Ownership
steps.explanation: Each domain team owns their **data products**. Sales owns sales data, Marketing owns marketing data. The team that generates data is responsible for serving it to consumers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sales Domain" S1["Operational DB"] S2["Data Product:
Sales Transactions"] S1 --> S2 end subgraph "Marketing Domain" M1["Operational DB"] M2["Data Product:
Campaign Metrics"] M1 --> M2 end subgraph "Finance Domain" F1["Operational DB"] F2["Data Product:
Revenue Reports"] F1 --> F2 end S2 & M2 --> F2 style S2 fill:#E8F5E9 style M2 fill:#E8F5E9 style F2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Data as a Product
steps.explanation: Data products must be **discoverable**, **addressable**, **trustworthy**, **self-describing**, and **secure**. Treat data consumers as customers—they need reliable, well-documented data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Data Product Characteristics" D["Discoverable
Can be found"] A["Addressable
Unique identifier"] T["Trustworthy
Quality guaranteed"] S["Self-describing
Schema + docs"] SE["Secure
Access controlled"] end subgraph "Product Thinking" P1["Who are my consumers?"] P2["What do they need?"] P3["How do I measure success?"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD style T fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Self-Serve Data Platform
steps.explanation: A platform team provides **infrastructure as a service**: storage, compute, pipelines, monitoring. Domain teams use this platform without needing deep infrastructure expertise.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Platform Team Provides"
        P1["Storage (S3, Delta Lake)"]
        P2["Compute (Spark, dbt)"]
        P3["Orchestration (Airflow)"]
        P4["Catalog (DataHub)"]
        P5["Monitoring (Monte Carlo)"]
    end
    subgraph "Domain Teams Use"
        D1["Sales Data Products"]
        D2["Marketing Data Products"]
        D3["Finance Data Products"]
    end
    P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 & P5 --> D1 & D2 & D3
    style P1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style P2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Federated Governance
steps.explanation: Global standards ensure interoperability: naming conventions, quality requirements, security policies. But domains have **autonomy** within these standards. Balance central control with local flexibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Global Standards"
        G1["Naming conventions"]
        G2["Data quality SLAs"]
        G3["Security policies"]
        G4["Schema standards"]
    end
    subgraph "Local Autonomy"
        L1["Domain-specific schemas"]
        L2["Pipeline implementations"]
        L3["Technology choices"]
    end
    subgraph "Governance Council"
        GC["Representatives from each domain"]
    end
    GC --> G1 & G2 & G3 & G4
    GC -.-> L1 & L2 & L3
    style GC fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Data Mesh Architecture
steps.explanation: The full picture: domain teams build data products on a shared platform, governed by federated standards. A data catalog makes products discoverable across the organization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Platform Layer" PL["Self-Serve Data Platform"] end subgraph "Domain Layer" S["Sales
Data Products"] M["Marketing
Data Products"] F["Finance
Data Products"] end subgraph "Governance" G["Federated
Standards"] end subgraph "Discovery" C["Data Catalog"] end PL --> S & M & F G -.-> S & M & F S & M & F --> C style PL fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Data Mesh
steps.explanation: Data Mesh suits **large organizations** with multiple domains and scale problems. Small teams don't need it—the overhead outweighs benefits. It's primarily an **organizational change**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Fit"
        G1["Large organization"]
        G2["Multiple business domains"]
        G3["Central team is bottleneck"]
        G4["Domain expertise matters"]
    end
    subgraph "Poor Fit"
        P1["Small team (<50 people)"]
        P2["Single domain"]
        P3["Early-stage company"]
        P4["Limited data maturity"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style G3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style P4 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Change Data Capture (CDC)
fields.description: Log-based, trigger-based, and timestamp-based CDC methods

steps.title: What is CDC?
steps.explanation: **Change Data Capture (CDC)** tracks changes (inserts, updates, deletes) in source systems and propagates them to downstream systems. It's essential for keeping data warehouses in sync without full reloads.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph Source DB["Database"] C["Changes:
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE"] end subgraph CDC CDC1["Capture Changes"] end subgraph Target DW["Data Warehouse"] ST["Streaming"] end DB --> C --> CDC1 --> DW & ST style CDC1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Why Not Full Loads?
steps.explanation: Full table scans are expensive for large tables. CDC captures only what changed, reducing load on source systems and enabling near-real-time sync. Essential for high-volume OLTP systems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Full Load"
        F1["Scan entire table"]
        F2["Transfer all rows"]
        F3["High source load"]
        F4["Hours of latency"]
    end
    subgraph "CDC"
        C1["Track changes only"]
        C2["Transfer deltas"]
        C3["Low source load"]
        C4["Seconds of latency"]
    end
    style F3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style F4 fill:#FFCDD2
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Log-Based CDC
steps.explanation: **Log-based CDC** reads the database transaction log (WAL/binlog). Non-invasive, captures all changes, includes deletes. The gold standard for CDC. Tools: Debezium, Fivetran, Airbyte.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Database" T["Tables"] W["Transaction Log
(WAL/binlog)"] T -->|writes| W end subgraph "CDC Tool" D["Debezium
Reads log"] end subgraph "Output" K["Kafka"] DW["Data Warehouse"] end W --> D --> K --> DW style W fill:#E8F5E9 style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Trigger-Based CDC
steps.explanation: **Trigger-based CDC** uses database triggers to capture changes. Writes to a shadow table. More invasive (adds triggers), can impact performance, but works when log access isn't available.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Database" T["Table"] TR["Triggers
AFTER INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE"] S["Shadow Table
(changes)"] T --> TR --> S end subgraph "CDC Process" P["Poll shadow table"] end subgraph "Target" DW["Data Warehouse"] end S --> P --> DW style TR fill:#FFF3E0 style S fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Timestamp-Based CDC
steps.explanation: **Timestamp-based CDC** queries for rows where `updated_at > last_sync`. Simple to implement but **misses deletes** and requires timestamp columns on all tables.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE updated_at > '2024-01-01 00:00:00'"] end subgraph "Limitations" L1["Cannot detect deletes"] L2["Requires updated_at column"] L3["Clock skew issues"] L4["Misses in-place updates"] end subgraph "Good For" G1["Append-only tables"] G2["Simple use cases"] end style L1 fill:#FFCDD2 style L2 fill:#FFCDD2 style G1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: CDC Event Format
steps.explanation: CDC events typically include: **operation type** (c/u/d), **before** image (old values), **after** image (new values), and **metadata** (timestamp, source). This enables full replay.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "CDC Event" E["{'op': 'u',
'before': {'id': 1, 'name': 'Old'},
'after': {'id': 1, 'name': 'New'},
'ts_ms': 1704067200000,
'source': {'table': 'users'}}"] end subgraph "Operations" C["c = create (INSERT)"] U["u = update (UPDATE)"] D["d = delete (DELETE)"] end style E fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: CDC Architecture with Kafka
steps.explanation: A common pattern: Debezium reads database logs, publishes to Kafka topics (one per table), consumers process events. Kafka provides durability, replay, and multiple consumers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sources
        P["PostgreSQL"]
        M["MySQL"]
    end
    subgraph "Debezium"
        D["Connectors"]
    end
    subgraph "Kafka"
        T1["users topic"]
        T2["orders topic"]
    end
    subgraph Consumers
        DW["Data Warehouse"]
        ES["Elasticsearch"]
        C["Cache"]
    end
    P & M --> D --> T1 & T2 --> DW & ES & C
    style D fill:#E8F5E9
    style T1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style T2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: CDC Method Comparison
steps.explanation: Log-based is best for production systems. Trigger-based when logs unavailable. Timestamp-based for simple, append-only scenarios. Choose based on your constraints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Log-Based"
        L1["Non-invasive"]
        L2["Captures deletes"]
        L3["Low latency"]
        L4["Requires log access"]
    end
    subgraph "Trigger-Based"
        T1["Works anywhere"]
        T2["Captures deletes"]
        T3["Performance impact"]
        T4["Complex setup"]
    end
    subgraph "Timestamp-Based"
        TS1["Simple to implement"]
        TS2["No log access needed"]
        TS3["Misses deletes"]
        TS4["Requires columns"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style T3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style TS3 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)
fields.description: Type 1, 2, 3 strategies for handling dimension changes

steps.title: The Problem
steps.explanation: Dimension attributes change over time: customers move, products get recategorized, employees change departments. **How do we track these changes** while maintaining analytical accuracy?
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Example" C1["Customer: John
City: New York
(Jan 2023)"] C2["Customer: John
City: Boston
(Jun 2023)"] end subgraph "Question" Q["Sales in NYC for 2023?
Which John do we use?"] end C1 --> C2 style Q fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: SCD Type 0: No Change
steps.explanation: **Type 0**: Never update dimension values. The original value is retained forever. Used for attributes that should never change (e.g., original signup date).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Type 0"
        O["Original value preserved"]
        N["Changes ignored"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Cases"
        U1["Date of birth"]
        U2["Original signup date"]
        U3["Immutable IDs"]
    end
    style O fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: SCD Type 1: Overwrite
steps.explanation: **Type 1**: Overwrite the old value with the new value. No history is kept. Simple, but **loses historical context**. Past transactions appear with current values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before Update" B["customer_id: 1
name: John
city: New York"] end subgraph "After Update" A["customer_id: 1
name: John
city: Boston"] end B -->|"UPDATE"| A subgraph "Problem" P["NYC sales now show
as Boston sales!"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: SCD Type 2: Add New Row
steps.explanation: **Type 2**: Insert a new row for each change. Add `effective_date`, `end_date`, and `is_current` columns. **Preserves full history** but increases table size.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Type 2 Table" R1["sk: 1, id: 1, city: NYC
eff: 2023-01, end: 2023-05, current: N"] R2["sk: 2, id: 1, city: Boston
eff: 2023-06, end: 9999-12, current: Y"] end subgraph "Surrogate Keys" S["sk = surrogate key (unique per version)
id = natural key (business key)"] end subgraph "Benefit" B["Historical queries
join on date range"] end style R2 fill:#E8F5E9 style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: SCD Type 2: Joining to Facts
steps.explanation: With Type 2, fact tables reference the **surrogate key** (not natural key). This links each transaction to the dimension values **at that point in time**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "fact_sales" F["sale_id: 100
customer_sk: 1
amount: 500"] F2["sale_id: 200
customer_sk: 2
amount: 300"] end subgraph "dim_customer" D1["sk: 1, city: NYC"] D2["sk: 2, city: Boston"] end F --> D1 F2 --> D2 style D1 fill:#E3F2FD style D2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: SCD Type 3: Add Column
steps.explanation: **Type 3**: Add columns for previous values (`previous_city`, `current_city`). Tracks limited history (usually just one previous value). Rarely used.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Type 3 Table" R["customer_id: 1
name: John
current_city: Boston
previous_city: New York"] end subgraph "Limitation" L["Only tracks 1 previous value
Loses older history"] end subgraph "Use Case" U["When you only need
current vs previous comparison"] end style L fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: SCD Type 4: Mini-Dimension
steps.explanation: **Type 4**: Separate frequently changing attributes into a **mini-dimension**. The main dimension stays stable; the mini-dimension handles rapid changes (e.g., customer status).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Main Dimension" D["dim_customer
customer_id, name, address"] end subgraph "Mini-Dimension" M["dim_customer_status
status_sk, tier, segment, score"] end subgraph "Fact Table" F["fact_sales
customer_id, status_sk, ..."] end D --> F M --> F style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Choosing SCD Type
steps.explanation: Type 1 for corrections and non-critical attributes. Type 2 for full history (most common). Type 3 rarely. Type 4 for high-change attributes. Often **combine types** in one dimension.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{Need history?} Q1 -->|No| T1["Type 1
Overwrite"] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{How much?} Q2 -->|Full| T2["Type 2
New rows"] Q2 -->|Just previous| T3["Type 3
Add column"] Q2 -->|Rapid changes| T4["Type 4
Mini-dimension"] style T2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Idempotent Pipeline Design
fields.description: Ensuring repeatable, safe reruns in data pipelines

steps.title: What is Idempotency?
steps.explanation: An **idempotent** operation produces the same result whether run once or multiple times. For pipelines: **rerunning a job should not corrupt data** or produce duplicates.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Idempotent"
        I1["Run 1: Result = X"]
        I2["Run 2: Result = X"]
        I3["Run 3: Result = X"]
    end
    subgraph "Non-Idempotent"
        N1["Run 1: Result = X"]
        N2["Run 2: Result = X + X"]
        N3["Run 3: Result = X + X + X"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style I2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style I3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style N3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Why Idempotency Matters
steps.explanation: Pipelines fail and need retries. Backfills require re-processing historical data. Without idempotency, you get **duplicates**, **partial updates**, and **inconsistent data**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Real-World Scenarios"
        S1["Job fails mid-way → retry"]
        S2["Backfill historical data"]
        S3["Fix bug → rerun"]
        S4["Test in production"]
    end
    subgraph "Without Idempotency"
        P["Duplicates, corruption"]
    end
    subgraph "With Idempotency"
        G["Safe reruns, same result"]
    end
    S1 & S2 & S3 & S4 --> P
    S1 & S2 & S3 & S4 -.-> G
    style P fill:#FFCDD2
    style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pattern 1: Delete + Insert
steps.explanation: Before inserting, **delete existing data** for that partition/date. Ensures reruns replace rather than append. Simple and effective for batch pipelines.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Delete + Insert" D["DELETE FROM sales
WHERE date = '2024-01-15'"] I["INSERT INTO sales
SELECT ... WHERE date = '2024-01-15'"] D --> I end subgraph "Result" R["Always same rows
for that date"] end style D fill:#FFF3E0 style I fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pattern 2: MERGE / Upsert
steps.explanation: Use **MERGE** (or upsert) to insert new rows and update existing ones. Based on a unique key. Works well when you can't delete partitions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "MERGE Pattern" M["MERGE INTO target t
USING source s
ON t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT"] end subgraph "Behavior" B1["Existing rows: updated"] B2["New rows: inserted"] B3["No duplicates"] end style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pattern 3: Partition Overwrite
steps.explanation: Write to a **partition** and overwrite the entire partition. Common with Spark/Hive. The partition acts as the unit of idempotency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Spark Example" S["df.write
.mode('overwrite')
.partitionBy('date')
.saveAsTable('sales')"] end subgraph "Effect" E["Overwrites only
date=2024-01-15 partition"] end subgraph "Other Partitions" O["Untouched"] end style S fill:#E8F5E9 style O fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Pattern 4: Deduplication
steps.explanation: Include **deduplication logic** in your pipeline. Use ROW_NUMBER() or DISTINCT on business keys. Handles duplicates from any source.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Dedup Query" Q["WITH ranked AS (
SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
PARTITION BY id
ORDER BY updated_at DESC
) as rn
FROM staging
)
SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1"] end subgraph "Result" R["Latest record per id"] end style Q fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Checkpointing & State
steps.explanation: For streaming pipelines, use **checkpointing** to track progress. If a job restarts, it resumes from the checkpoint, avoiding reprocessing or duplicates.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Streaming Pipeline"
        S["Source (Kafka)"]
        P["Processor"]
        T["Target"]
        C["Checkpoint Store"]
    end
    S --> P --> T
    P -->|"Save offset"| C
    C -->|"Resume from"| P
    style C fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Always design for reruns. Use **deterministic logic** (no random(), no current_timestamp() without override). Process data in **partitions**. Test by running the same job twice.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Do"
        D1["Parameterize dates"]
        D2["Use deterministic IDs"]
        D3["Partition by date"]
        D4["Delete before insert"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't"
        N1["Use CURRENT_TIMESTAMP"]
        N2["Append without dedup"]
        N3["Use random() for IDs"]
        N4["Assume single run"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style N2 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Backfill Strategies
fields.description: Historical data reprocessing patterns

steps.title: What is Backfilling?
steps.explanation: **Backfilling** is reprocessing historical data—running a pipeline for past dates. Needed when: fixing bugs, adding new columns, onboarding new data sources, or recovering from outages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Normal Run" N["Process today's data"] end subgraph "Backfill" B["Process Jan 1-31
(historical)"] end N -.-> B style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Backfill
steps.explanation: Common scenarios requiring backfills: **logic changes** (fixed a bug), **schema changes** (added a column), **new pipeline** (need historical data), **data recovery** (outage, corruption).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Backfill Triggers"
        T1["Bug fix in transformation"]
        T2["New column added"]
        T3["New downstream table"]
        T4["Pipeline outage recovery"]
        T5["Source data correction"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style T2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style T4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Prerequisites
steps.explanation: Before backfilling: ensure **idempotent pipelines**, parameterize by date, have source data available for the historical period, and understand downstream dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Checklist"
        C1["Pipeline is idempotent?"]
        C2["Date is parameterized?"]
        C3["Source data available?"]
        C4["Dependencies mapped?"]
        C5["Downstream impact known?"]
    end
    C1 & C2 & C3 & C4 & C5 --> G["Ready to backfill"]
    style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Airflow Backfill
steps.explanation: Airflow supports backfills natively with `catchup=True` or CLI. It runs missed DAG runs for historical dates, respecting dependencies and parallelism.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Airflow CLI" CLI["airflow dags backfill
-s 2024-01-01
-e 2024-01-31
my_dag"] end subgraph "DAG Config" D["DAG(
catchup=True,
start_date=datetime(2024,1,1)
)"] end subgraph "Execution" E["Runs 31 DAG instances
in parallel"] end CLI --> E D --> E style CLI fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Chunked Backfill
steps.explanation: For large backfills, process in **chunks** (e.g., one month at a time). Reduces memory pressure, allows progress tracking, and makes failures easier to handle.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Full Year Backfill" F["Jan 1 - Dec 31
(365 days at once)"] end subgraph "Chunked Backfill" C1["Jan 1-31"] --> C2["Feb 1-28"] --> C3["Mar 1-31"] C3 --> C4["..."] --> C5["Dec 1-31"] end F -->|"Risk: OOM, long failure"| X["Fail"] C1 -->|"Safer, trackable"| G["Success"] style F fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Dependency-Aware Backfill
steps.explanation: When backfilling, process **upstream tables first**, then downstream. Respect the DAG order. Otherwise, downstream tables have stale/missing upstream data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Pipeline Order"
        A["raw_events"] --> B["cleaned_events"] --> C["aggregated_events"]
    end
    subgraph "Backfill Order"
        O1["1. Backfill raw_events"]
        O2["2. Backfill cleaned_events"]
        O3["3. Backfill aggregated_events"]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3
    end
    style O1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style O2 fill:#E3F2FD
    style O3 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Parallel vs Sequential
steps.explanation: Run dates in **parallel** if independent. Run **sequentially** if there are cross-day dependencies (e.g., running totals, window functions over multiple days).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parallel (Independent Days)"
        P1["Jan 1"]
        P2["Jan 2"]
        P3["Jan 3"]
        P1 & P2 & P3 --> R1["Fast"]
    end
    subgraph "Sequential (Dependencies)"
        S1["Jan 1"] --> S2["Jan 2"] --> S3["Jan 3"]
        S3 --> R2["Slower but correct"]
    end
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R2 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Monitoring Backfills
steps.explanation: Track progress, set alerts, and plan for long-running backfills. Consider off-peak hours to reduce load on production systems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Best Practices" B1["Run during off-peak"] B2["Track progress (dates done)"] B3["Set completion alerts"] B4["Validate results"] B5["Document the backfill"] end subgraph "Validation" V["Row counts match?
No duplicates?
Values reasonable?"] end style V fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Data Quality Gates
fields.description: Validation checkpoints in data pipelines

steps.title: What are Quality Gates?
steps.explanation: **Quality gates** are validation checkpoints in your pipeline that verify data meets expectations before proceeding. They catch issues early, before bad data reaches downstream consumers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR E["Extract"] --> Q1["Quality
Gate 1"] Q1 --> T["Transform"] T --> Q2["Quality
Gate 2"] Q2 --> L["Load"] L --> Q3["Quality
Gate 3"] style Q1 fill:#E8F5E9 style Q2 fill:#E8F5E9 style Q3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Types of Quality Checks
steps.explanation: Common checks: **completeness** (no nulls), **uniqueness** (no duplicates), **validity** (correct format/range), **freshness** (recent data), **consistency** (cross-table relationships).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Quality Dimensions" C["Completeness
No unexpected nulls"] U["Uniqueness
No duplicates on keys"] V["Validity
Values in expected range"] F["Freshness
Data is recent enough"] A["Accuracy
Values are correct"] CO["Consistency
Cross-table integrity"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style U fill:#E3F2FD style V fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Example Quality Checks
steps.explanation: Concrete examples of data quality assertions you might implement in a pipeline.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "orders Table"
        C1["order_id is unique"]
        C2["order_date not null"]
        C3["amount >= 0"]
        C4["status in ['pending','shipped','delivered']"]
        C5["customer_id exists in customers"]
    end
    subgraph "Freshness"
        F1["max(created_at) > now() - 1 hour"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Gate Actions: Warn vs Block
steps.explanation: **Warn**: Log the issue, send alert, but continue pipeline. **Block**: Stop pipeline, prevent bad data from loading. Choose based on severity and downstream impact.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q["Quality Check"] Q --> P{Pass?} P -->|Yes| C["Continue"] P -->|No| S{Severity?} S -->|Low| W["Warn
Log & alert
Continue"] S -->|High| B["Block
Fail pipeline
Notify"] style W fill:#FFF3E0 style B fill:#FFCDD2 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Great Expectations
steps.explanation: **Great Expectations** is a popular Python framework for data quality. Define expectations as code, run validations, get reports. Integrates with Airflow, Spark, and more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Great Expectations" E["Expectations Suite
(YAML/Python)"] V["Validator
Runs checks"] D["Data Docs
HTML reports"] end subgraph "Example" Code["expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('order_id')
expect_column_values_to_be_unique('order_id')
expect_column_values_to_be_between('amount', 0, 10000)"] end E --> V --> D style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: dbt Tests
steps.explanation: **dbt** includes built-in data tests. Define in YAML, run with `dbt test`. Tests include unique, not_null, accepted_values, and relationships (foreign keys).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "dbt schema.yml" Y["models:
- name: orders
columns:
- name: order_id
tests:
- unique
- not_null
- name: status
tests:
- accepted_values:
values: ['pending','shipped']"] end subgraph "Run" R["dbt test"] end Y --> R style Y fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pipeline Integration
steps.explanation: Place quality gates at strategic points: **after extraction** (source issues), **after transformation** (logic errors), **before load** (final validation). Use orchestrator to gate progression.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Airflow DAG"
        E["extract_task"]
        Q1["quality_check_raw"]
        T["transform_task"]
        Q2["quality_check_transformed"]
        L["load_task"]
    end
    E --> Q1 --> T --> Q2 --> L
    Q1 -->|"Fail"| F1["Alert & stop"]
    Q2 -->|"Fail"| F2["Alert & stop"]
    style Q1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Q2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Monitoring & Observability
steps.explanation: Beyond gates, use **data observability** tools (Monte Carlo, Bigeye, Soda) for ongoing monitoring: anomaly detection, freshness tracking, schema changes, lineage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Data Observability" A["Anomaly Detection
Unusual row counts, values"] F["Freshness Monitoring
Data arriving on time?"] S["Schema Tracking
Columns added/removed?"] L["Lineage
Impact analysis"] end subgraph "Tools" T1["Monte Carlo"] T2["Bigeye"] T3["Soda"] T4["Elementary"] end style A fill:#E3F2FD style F fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Orchestration Patterns
fields.description: DAG execution, dependencies, retries, and workflow design

steps.title: What is Orchestration?
steps.explanation: **Orchestration** coordinates the execution of data pipelines: scheduling, dependencies, retries, monitoring. Tools like Airflow, Dagster, and Prefect manage complex workflows as DAGs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Orchestrator"
        S["Scheduler"]
        E["Executor"]
        M["Monitor"]
    end
    subgraph "DAG"
        T1["Task 1"] --> T2["Task 2"] --> T3["Task 3"]
        T1 --> T4["Task 4"] --> T3
    end
    S --> E --> DAG
    DAG --> M
    style S fill:#E3F2FD
    style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: DAG Fundamentals
steps.explanation: A **DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) defines tasks and dependencies. **Directed**: edges have direction (A→B means A runs before B). **Acyclic**: no cycles (A→B→A is invalid).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Valid DAG"
        A1["A"] --> B1["B"] --> C1["C"]
        A1 --> D1["D"] --> C1
    end
    subgraph "Invalid (Cycle)"
        A2["A"] --> B2["B"]
        B2 --> C2["C"]
        C2 --> A2
    end
    style A2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style B2 fill:#FFCDD2
    style C2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Dependency Patterns
steps.explanation: Common dependency patterns: **sequential** (A→B→C), **parallel** (A→[B,C]→D), **fan-out** (A→[B,C,D]), **fan-in** ([A,B,C]→D).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Sequential"
        S1["A"] --> S2["B"] --> S3["C"]
    end
    subgraph "Parallel"
        P1["A"]
        P2["B"]
        P3["C"]
        P1 & P2 & P3 --> P4["D"]
    end
    subgraph "Fan-out"
        F1["A"] --> F2["B"] & F3["C"] & F4["D"]
    end
    subgraph "Fan-in"
        I1["A"] & I2["B"] & I3["C"] --> I4["D"]
    end

steps.title: Scheduling Strategies
steps.explanation: DAGs run on schedules: **cron** (0 * * * *), **intervals** (@hourly, @daily), or **event-driven** (trigger on data arrival). Choose based on latency needs and source availability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Cron Schedule" CR["0 2 * * *
Every day at 2 AM"] end subgraph "Interval" IN["@hourly
timedelta(hours=1)"] end subgraph "Event-Driven" EV["Trigger when:
- File arrives
- API webhook
- Upstream DAG completes"] end style EV fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Retry & Failure Handling
steps.explanation: Configure **retries** for transient failures. Set **retry_delay** for backoff. Use **on_failure_callback** for alerts. Design tasks to be **idempotent** for safe retries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Retry Config" R["retries=3
retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5)
retry_exponential_backoff=True"] end subgraph "Failure Flow" T["Task"] --> F{Failed?} F -->|Yes| C{Retries left?} C -->|Yes| W["Wait"] --> T C -->|No| A["Alert & fail"] F -->|No| S["Success"] end style R fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Task Communication
steps.explanation: Tasks communicate via **XComs** (Airflow), **outputs** (Dagster), or external storage (S3, database). Pass small metadata; use storage for large data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Small Data: XCom"
        T1["Task 1"] -->|"push: row_count=1000"| X["XCom"]
        X -->|"pull: row_count"| T2["Task 2"]
    end
    subgraph "Large Data: Storage"
        T3["Task 3"] -->|"Write df to S3"| S["S3"]
        S -->|"Read from S3"| T4["Task 4"]
    end
    style X fill:#FFF3E0
    style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Dynamic DAGs
steps.explanation: Generate tasks dynamically based on config or data. Useful for processing multiple tables, files, or partitions. Airflow 2.0+ supports dynamic task mapping.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Dynamic Tasks" S["Start"] S --> T1["Process table_a"] S --> T2["Process table_b"] S --> T3["Process table_c"] T1 & T2 & T3 --> E["End"] end subgraph "Airflow 2.0" Code["@task
def process(table):
...

process.expand(table=tables)"] end style Code fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Orchestrator Comparison
steps.explanation: **Airflow**: Industry standard, powerful, steep learning curve. **Dagster**: Software-defined assets, great for data teams. **Prefect**: Pythonic, modern. **Dagster & Prefect** gaining traction.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Airflow"
        A1["Industry standard"]
        A2["Large ecosystem"]
        A3["Complex setup"]
    end
    subgraph "Dagster"
        D1["Asset-centric"]
        D2["Great testing"]
        D3["Newer ecosystem"]
    end
    subgraph "Prefect"
        P1["Pythonic"]
        P2["Easy local dev"]
        P3["Cloud offering"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P1 fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Serialization Formats Comparison
fields.description: Parquet vs Avro vs ORC vs JSON trade-offs

steps.title: Why File Format Matters
steps.explanation: File format affects **query performance**, **storage cost**, **compatibility**, and **schema evolution**. Choosing the right format is critical for data lakes and pipelines.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Format Choice Affects"
        A["Query speed"]
        B["Storage size"]
        C["Schema flexibility"]
        D["Tool compatibility"]
    end
    subgraph "Formats"
        F1["Parquet"]
        F2["Avro"]
        F3["ORC"]
        F4["JSON"]
    end
    style F1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Row vs Column Orientation
steps.explanation: **Row-oriented** (Avro, JSON): all columns for a row together. Good for full-row access. **Column-oriented** (Parquet, ORC): all values for a column together. Good for analytical queries on few columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Row-Oriented" R["Row1: [id, name, age]
Row2: [id, name, age]
Row3: [id, name, age]"] RU["Good for: INSERT, full row reads"] end subgraph "Column-Oriented" C["id: [1, 2, 3]
name: ['a', 'b', 'c']
age: [20, 30, 40]"] CU["Good for: SELECT col, aggregations"] end style RU fill:#FFF3E0 style CU fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Parquet
steps.explanation: **Parquet**: Column-oriented, compressed, schema embedded. The standard for analytics. Excellent for Spark, Athena, BigQuery. Supports nested data, predicate pushdown.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parquet"
        P1["Column-oriented"]
        P2["Highly compressed"]
        P3["Schema in file"]
        P4["Nested data support"]
        P5["Predicate pushdown"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B["Data lakes"]
        B2["Analytics queries"]
        B3["Spark/BigQuery/Athena"]
    end
    subgraph "Compression"
        C["Snappy (fast)"]
        C2["Gzip (smaller)"]
        C3["Zstd (balanced)"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Avro
steps.explanation: **Avro**: Row-oriented, schema in JSON, compact binary format. Great for **streaming** and **schema evolution**. Kafka's preferred format. Schema registry integration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Avro"
        A1["Row-oriented"]
                        A2["JSON schema definition"]
        A3["Binary compact format"]
        A4["Excellent schema evolution"]
        A5["Schema registry support"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B["Kafka messages"]
        B2["Data in transit"]
        B3["Write-heavy workloads"]
    end
    style A4 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A5 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ORC
steps.explanation: **ORC** (Optimized Row Columnar): Column-oriented, designed for Hive. Similar to Parquet but with ACID transaction support. Strong in Hive ecosystem, less portable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "ORC"
        O1["Column-oriented"]
        O2["ACID transaction support"]
        O3["Excellent compression"]
        O4["Hive-optimized"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B["Hive data warehouse"]
        B2["ACID requirements"]
        B3["Heavy Hadoop use"]
    end
    subgraph "Trade-off"
        T["Less portable than Parquet"]
    end
    style O2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style T fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: JSON & CSV
steps.explanation: **JSON/CSV**: Human-readable, no schema enforcement, no compression by default. Easy for interchange but inefficient for analytics. Use for APIs, configs, small datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "JSON/CSV"
        J1["Human-readable"]
        J2["No schema enforcement"]
        J3["No built-in compression"]
        J4["Verbose (larger files)"]
    end
    subgraph "Use For"
        U["API responses"]
        U2["Config files"]
        U3["Small datasets"]
        U4["Data interchange"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid For"
        A["Data lakes"]
        A2["Large-scale analytics"]
    end
    style J1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Format Comparison
steps.explanation: Side-by-side comparison of key characteristics. Choose based on your primary use case: Parquet for analytics, Avro for streaming, ORC for Hive, JSON for compatibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Comparison"
        subgraph "Parquet"
            P1["Analytics: Excellent"]
            P2["Streaming: Good"]
            P3["Size: Small"]
        end
        subgraph "Avro"
            A1["Analytics: Fair"]
            A2["Streaming: Excellent"]
            A3["Size: Medium"]
        end
        subgraph "JSON"
            J1["Analytics: Poor"]
            J2["Streaming: Good"]
            J3["Size: Large"]
        end
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style J3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Quick guide: **Parquet** for data lakes and analytics. **Avro** for Kafka and event streaming. **ORC** for Hive. **JSON** only for APIs and small files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{Use case?}
    Q1 -->|"Data lake/Analytics"| P["Parquet"]
    Q1 -->|"Kafka/Streaming"| A["Avro"]
    Q1 -->|"Hive/ACID"| O["ORC"]
    Q1 -->|"API/Config"| J["JSON"]
    style P fill:#E8F5E9
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style O fill:#FFF3E0
    style J fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Partitioning Strategies
fields.description: Time-based, hash, and range partitioning patterns

steps.title: Why Partition?
steps.explanation: **Partitioning** divides large datasets into smaller, manageable chunks. Benefits: **faster queries** (scan less data), **efficient writes** (target specific partitions), **better manageability** (drop old partitions).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without Partitioning" W["Scan all 1TB"] end subgraph "With Partitioning" P["Scan only 10GB
(relevant partition)"] end W -->|"Query for Jan 2024"| S1["100x slower"] P -->|"Query for Jan 2024"| S2["100x faster"] style S1 fill:#FFCDD2 style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Time-Based Partitioning
steps.explanation: Most common strategy. Partition by **date** or **hour**. Queries with date filters only scan relevant partitions. Great for time-series data, logs, events.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Partition by Date" T["events/"] P1["date=2024-01-01/"] P2["date=2024-01-02/"] P3["date=2024-01-03/"] T --> P1 & P2 & P3 end subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT * FROM events
WHERE date = '2024-01-02'"] R["Only scans P2"] end Q --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Multi-Level Partitioning
steps.explanation: Partition by multiple columns: `year/month/day` or `region/date`. Useful for very large tables. Be careful of creating too many small partitions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multi-Level" R["data/"] Y["year=2024/"] M1["month=01/"] M2["month=02/"] D1["day=01/"] D2["day=02/"] R --> Y --> M1 & M2 M1 --> D1 & D2 end subgraph "Alternative" A["region=US/
date=2024-01-01/"] end style Y fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Hash Partitioning
steps.explanation: Distribute rows based on **hash of a column** (e.g., user_id). Ensures even distribution across partitions. Good for joins and avoiding hot partitions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Hash Partitioning"
        H["hash(user_id) % 10"]
        P0["partition_0"]
        P1["partition_1"]
        P9["partition_9"]
        H --> P0 & P1 & P9
    end
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B1["Even distribution"]
        B2["No hot partitions"]
        B3["Efficient joins"]
    end
    style H fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Range Partitioning
steps.explanation: Partition by **value ranges**: IDs 1-1000, 1001-2000, etc. Useful when you query specific ranges. Common for ordered data like timestamps or sequential IDs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Range Partitioning" T["orders"] R1["id: 1-10000"] R2["id: 10001-20000"] R3["id: 20001-30000"] T --> R1 & R2 & R3 end subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE id BETWEEN 15000 AND 18000"] S["Only scans R2"] end Q --> S style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Partition Pruning
steps.explanation: **Partition pruning** skips irrelevant partitions during queries. Requires filters on partition columns. The optimizer must know which partitions to skip.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Query" Q["SELECT * FROM sales
WHERE date = '2024-01-15'
AND region = 'US'"] end subgraph "Partitions" P1["date=2024-01-14"] P2["date=2024-01-15"] P3["date=2024-01-16"] end Q -->|"Prune"| P1 Q -->|"Scan"| P2 Q -->|"Prune"| P3 style P1 fill:#FFCDD2 style P2 fill:#E8F5E9 style P3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Avoiding Small Files
steps.explanation: Too many partitions = too many small files. Small files hurt performance (overhead per file). Aim for files **100MB-1GB**. Compact small files regularly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problem" S["1000 partitions
1MB each"] P["Poor performance
File overhead"] end subgraph "Solution" C["Compact small files"] R["Repartition/coalesce"] L["Use larger partitions"] end S --> P C & R & L --> G["Good performance"] style P fill:#FFCDD2 style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Choosing Partition Keys
steps.explanation: Choose partition keys based on **query patterns**. Filter columns in WHERE clauses make good partition keys. Avoid high-cardinality columns (too many partitions).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Partition Keys"
        G1["date (common filter)"]
        G2["region (10s of values)"]
        G3["year/month"]
    end
    subgraph "Bad Partition Keys"
        B1["user_id (millions)"]
        B2["transaction_id (unique)"]
        B3["timestamp (seconds)"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style G2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style B1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style B2 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Compression Trade-offs
fields.description: Speed vs size vs query pattern considerations

steps.title: The Compression Trade-off
steps.explanation: Compression reduces storage costs but adds CPU overhead. The trade-off: **smaller files** (cheaper storage, less I/O) vs **slower decompression** (more CPU time). Choose based on your bottleneck.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Trade-off"
        S["Storage Cost"]
        C["CPU Cost"]
        I["I/O Bandwidth"]
    end
    subgraph "More Compression"
        M1["Smaller files"]
        M2["Less I/O"]
        M3["More CPU"]
    end
    subgraph "Less Compression"
        L1["Larger files"]
        L2["More I/O"]
        L3["Less CPU"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Compression Codecs
steps.explanation: **Snappy**: Fast compression/decompression, moderate ratio. **Gzip**: High ratio, slower. **Zstd**: Best of both, newer. **LZ4**: Fastest, lower ratio.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Speed vs Ratio" LZ4["LZ4
Fastest, low ratio"] SN["Snappy
Fast, moderate ratio"] ZS["Zstd
Balanced"] GZ["Gzip
Slow, high ratio"] end LZ4 -->|"More speed"| SN --> ZS --> GZ GZ -->|"More compression"| LZ4 style ZS fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When Speed Matters
steps.explanation: Choose **Snappy/LZ4** when: queries are interactive, CPU is the bottleneck, data is frequently accessed, or you have fast storage (SSD/NVMe).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Choose Fast Compression"
        F1["Interactive queries"]
        F2["CPU-bound workloads"]
        F3["Hot data (frequent access)"]
        F4["Fast storage (SSD)"]
    end
    subgraph "Codecs"
        C["Snappy"]
        L["LZ4"]
    end
    F1 & F2 & F3 & F4 --> C & L
    style C fill:#E8F5E9
    style L fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When Size Matters
steps.explanation: Choose **Gzip/Zstd** when: storage cost is a concern, data is cold/archival, network bandwidth is limited, or data will be transferred frequently.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Choose High Compression"
        H1["Storage cost sensitive"]
        H2["Cold/archival data"]
        H3["Limited bandwidth"]
        H4["Data transfer heavy"]
    end
    subgraph "Codecs"
        G["Gzip"]
        Z["Zstd"]
    end
    H1 & H2 & H3 & H4 --> G & Z
    style Z fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Zstd: The Modern Choice
steps.explanation: **Zstd** (Zstandard) offers the best balance: high compression ratios with fast decompression. Becoming the default for modern data platforms. Supports compression levels 1-22.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Zstd Benefits"
        B1["High compression ratio"]
        B2["Fast decompression"]
        B3["Configurable levels"]
        B4["Wide adoption"]
    end
    subgraph "Compression Levels"
        L1["Level 1-3: Fast"]
        L2["Level 10-15: Balanced"]
        L3["Level 19-22: Max ratio"]
    end
    style L2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Parquet + Compression
steps.explanation: Parquet files contain row groups, each compressed independently. Column-oriented layout compresses well (similar values together). Snappy is the typical default; consider Zstd for better ratios.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Parquet Structure" F["Parquet File"] RG1["Row Group 1
(compressed)"] RG2["Row Group 2
(compressed)"] F --> RG1 & RG2 end subgraph "Defaults" D["Spark: Snappy"] D2["PyArrow: Snappy"] D3["BigQuery: Snappy"] end subgraph "Better" B["Consider Zstd
for 20-30% smaller files"] end style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Codec Comparison
steps.explanation: Benchmark comparison (approximate, varies by data). Zstd offers the best overall balance for most data engineering workloads.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Compression Ratio (higher = smaller)"
        R1["Gzip: 1.0 (baseline)"]
        R2["Zstd: 0.95"]
        R3["Snappy: 0.70"]
        R4["LZ4: 0.65"]
    end
    subgraph "Decompression Speed"
        S1["LZ4: Fastest"]
        S2["Snappy: Fast"]
        S3["Zstd: Fast"]
        S4["Gzip: Slow"]
    end
    style R2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Decision Framework
steps.explanation: Quick decision guide based on your primary constraint. When in doubt, start with Zstd level 3 for a good balance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q{Primary constraint?}
    Q -->|"Query speed"| S["Snappy / LZ4"]
    Q -->|"Storage cost"| G["Gzip / Zstd high"]
    Q -->|"Balanced"| Z["Zstd level 3"]
    Q -->|"Not sure"| Z
    style Z fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Schema Evolution
fields.description: Forward/backward compatibility strategies for data schemas

steps.title: The Schema Evolution Problem
