    subgraph "Synthetic (Self-Instruct)"
        S1["LLM generates data"]
        S2["Scalable, cheap"]
        S3["Quality varies"]
    end
    subgraph "Open Datasets"
        O1["FLAN Collection"]
        O2["Dolly 15K"]
        O3["OpenAssistant"]
    end
    style H1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Task Diversity
steps.explanation: Include **diverse tasks**: summarization, QA, coding, math, creative writing, etc. Diversity helps the model generalize. The FLAN collection has 1800+ task types.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Task Types" T1["Summarization"] T2["Question Answering"] T3["Code Generation"] T4["Translation"] T5["Math/Reasoning"] T6["Creative Writing"] T7["Extraction"] T8["Classification"] end subgraph "Goal" G["Diverse tasks →
General instruction-following"] end style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Training Process
steps.explanation: Standard supervised fine-tuning. Train to predict response given instruction. Use chat template formatting. Typically 1-3 epochs over the dataset.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Training" D["Instruction Dataset"] F["Format with chat template"] T["Train (cross-entropy loss)"] M["Instruction-Tuned Model"] end D --> F --> T --> M subgraph "Hyperparameters" H["LR: ~2e-5
Epochs: 1-3
Batch size: varies"] end style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Chat Templates
steps.explanation: Models expect specific **chat templates**. Format must match what model was trained on. Templates define how roles (user, assistant, system) are marked.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Llama 2 Template" L["[INST] <>
System prompt
<>

User message [/INST]
Assistant response"] end subgraph "ChatML Template" C["<|im_start|>system
System prompt<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
Message<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant"] end style L fill:#E3F2FD style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Quality vs Quantity
steps.explanation: Research shows: **small, high-quality datasets** often beat large, noisy ones. LIMA paper: 1000 carefully curated examples rivaled much larger datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "LIMA Finding" L["1000 high-quality examples
≈ Much larger datasets"] end subgraph "Implications" I1["Curate carefully"] I2["Quality > quantity"] I3["Remove bad examples"] I4["Diverse but clean"] end style L fill:#E8F5E9 style I2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Evaluation
steps.explanation: Evaluate instruction-tuned models with: **human evaluation** (gold standard), **LLM-as-judge** (GPT-4 ranks outputs), **benchmarks** (MT-Bench, AlpacaEval).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Evaluation Methods" H["Human Evaluation
(expensive, gold standard)"] L["LLM-as-Judge
(GPT-4 rates outputs)"] B["Benchmarks
(MT-Bench, AlpacaEval)"] end subgraph "What to Measure" M1["Helpfulness"] M2["Harmlessness"] M3["Honesty"] M4["Following instructions"] end style H fill:#E8F5E9 style L fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: RLHF Pipeline
fields.description: Reward modeling and PPO training for alignment

steps.title: What is RLHF?
steps.explanation: **RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) aligns models with human preferences. Humans rank outputs, a reward model learns these preferences, and RL optimizes the LLM to maximize rewards.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Step 1" S1["Collect human
preferences"] end subgraph "Step 2" S2["Train reward
model"] end subgraph "Step 3" S3["RL training
(PPO)"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 style S3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 1: Collect Preferences
steps.explanation: Generate multiple responses to the same prompt. Have humans **rank** them (A > B > C). This creates preference pairs for training. Expensive but critical for quality.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Generate Responses"
        P["Prompt"]
        R1["Response A"]
        R2["Response B"]
        R3["Response C"]
        P --> R1 & R2 & R3
    end
    subgraph "Human Ranking"
        H["Human: B > A > C"]
    end
    subgraph "Preference Pairs"
        PP["(B, A), (B, C), (A, C)"]
    end
    R1 & R2 & R3 --> H --> PP
    style H fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 2: Train Reward Model
steps.explanation: The **reward model** learns to score responses like humans. It's typically initialized from the SFT model. Trained on preference pairs using Bradley-Terry loss.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Reward Model Training"
        SFT["SFT Model (init)"]
        D["Preference Data"]
        L["Loss: log σ(r(better) - r(worse))"]
        RM["Reward Model"]
    end
    SFT & D --> L --> RM
    subgraph "Output"
        O["(prompt, response) → scalar score"]
    end
    RM --> O
    style RM fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 3: PPO Training
steps.explanation: **PPO** (Proximal Policy Optimization) fine-tunes the LLM to maximize reward. The model generates responses, reward model scores them, PPO updates the model. Includes KL penalty to prevent diverging from SFT model.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "PPO Loop" P["Sample prompts"] G["Generate responses"] R["Get reward scores"] U["PPO update"] KL["KL penalty
(stay close to SFT)"] end P --> G --> R --> U --> G KL --> U subgraph "Objective" O["Maximize: reward - β × KL"] end style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: KL Penalty Importance
steps.explanation: Without KL penalty, the model can **hack** the reward model—finding outputs that score high but are nonsensical. KL keeps the model close to the well-behaved SFT policy.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without KL"
        W1["Model finds reward hacks"]
        W2["Outputs become weird"]
        W3["Reward gaming"]
    end
    subgraph "With KL"
        K1["Stay close to SFT"]
        K2["Balanced optimization"]
        K3["Prevents collapse"]
    end
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style K2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: RLHF Challenges
steps.explanation: RLHF is **complex and unstable**. Reward hacking, mode collapse, sensitivity to hyperparameters. Requires careful tuning and monitoring. Alternatives like DPO are gaining popularity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Challenges"
        C1["Training instability"]
        C2["Reward hacking"]
        C3["Expensive (4 models!)"]
        C4["Hyperparameter sensitive"]
    end
    subgraph "4 Models Needed"
        M1["Actor (LLM being trained)"]
        M2["Critic (value function)"]
        M3["Reference (SFT frozen)"]
        M4["Reward model"]
    end
    style C3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: DPO: A Simpler Alternative
steps.explanation: **DPO** (Direct Preference Optimization) removes the reward model. Directly optimize on preferences with a closed-form loss. Single training stage, more stable, often competitive with RLHF.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "RLHF"
        R1["Train reward model"]
        R2["PPO with reward"]
        R3["4 models, complex"]
    end
    subgraph "DPO"
        D1["Direct optimization"]
        D2["Single training stage"]
        D3["1 model, stable"]
    end
    subgraph "DPO Loss"
        L["Maximize: log σ(β × (log π(win) - log π(lose)))"]
    end
    style D3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use RLHF vs DPO
steps.explanation: **RLHF**: Maximum control, iterative improvement, when you have engineering resources. **DPO**: Simpler, stable, good first choice. Most teams start with DPO now.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Choose RLHF"
        R1["Need iterative refinement"]
        R2["Have engineering resources"]
        R3["Want maximum control"]
    end
    subgraph "Choose DPO"
        D1["Simpler setup"]
        D2["Faster iteration"]
        D3["Good default choice"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Quantization
fields.description: INT8, INT4, GPTQ, AWQ trade-offs for efficient inference

steps.title: Why Quantization?
steps.explanation: LLMs use 16-bit or 32-bit floats per weight. A 70B model needs ~140GB in FP16. **Quantization** reduces precision to 8-bit or 4-bit, shrinking memory by 2-4x with minimal quality loss.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Memory per Parameter"
        F32["FP32: 4 bytes"]
        F16["FP16: 2 bytes"]
        I8["INT8: 1 byte"]
        I4["INT4: 0.5 bytes"]
    end
    subgraph "70B Model Size"
        S32["FP32: 280GB"]
        S16["FP16: 140GB"]
        S8["INT8: 70GB"]
        S4["INT4: 35GB"]
    end
    style I4 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: How Quantization Works
steps.explanation: Map continuous float values to discrete integers. Store a **scale** and **zero-point** to reconstruct approximate values. Trade precision for memory/speed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Quantization" F["Float: [-1.5, 0.3, 2.1]"] S["Scale, zero-point"] Q["Quantized: [0, 127, 255]"] end F --> S --> Q subgraph "Dequantization" D["Reconstruct: [-1.5, 0.3, 2.1]
(approximately)"] end Q --> D style Q fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: INT8 Quantization
steps.explanation: **INT8** uses 8 bits per value (256 levels). Very little quality loss for most models. 2x memory reduction. Well-supported by hardware (NVIDIA Tensor Cores).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "INT8"
        B["8 bits = 256 values"]
        M["2x memory reduction"]
        Q["~0-1% quality loss"]
        H["Good hardware support"]
    end
    subgraph "Libraries"
        L1["bitsandbytes"]
        L2["TensorRT"]
        L3["ONNX Runtime"]
    end
    style Q fill:#E8F5E9
    style H fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: INT4 Quantization
steps.explanation: **INT4** uses 4 bits (16 levels). 4x memory reduction but more quality loss. Requires careful calibration. Popular methods: GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "INT4"
        B["4 bits = 16 values"]
        M["4x memory reduction"]
        Q["~1-5% quality loss"]
        C["Needs calibration data"]
    end
    subgraph "Methods"
        G["GPTQ: Post-training quant"]
        A["AWQ: Activation-aware"]
        GG["GGUF: CPU-optimized"]
    end
    style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: GPTQ vs AWQ
steps.explanation: **GPTQ**: Layer-by-layer quantization with calibration. Fast inference. **AWQ**: Protects salient weights (important for activations). Often better quality. Both widely used.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GPTQ" G1["Layer-by-layer quant"] G2["Faster to quantize"] G3["Good quality"] end subgraph "AWQ" A1["Activation-aware"] A2["Protects important weights"] A3["Often better quality"] end subgraph "Choose" C["AWQ: slightly better quality
GPTQ: more widely supported"] end style A3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: GGUF and llama.cpp
steps.explanation: **GGUF** is a format for CPU inference via llama.cpp. Various quant levels (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, etc.). Run LLMs on MacBooks and consumer hardware. Huge community.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GGUF Quantization Levels" Q4["Q4_K_M: ~4.5 bits, good balance"] Q5["Q5_K_M: ~5.5 bits, better quality"] Q8["Q8_0: 8 bits, minimal loss"] end subgraph "Use Case" U["Run 7B-70B models
on consumer hardware"] end subgraph "Tools" T["llama.cpp"] T2["Ollama"] T3["LM Studio"] end style Q4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Quality vs Size Trade-off
steps.explanation: Higher quantization = smaller but lower quality. INT8 is usually safe. INT4 requires evaluation. Some tasks (reasoning, math) are more sensitive to quantization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Trade-off" F16["FP16: Baseline quality"] I8["INT8: ~99% quality"] I4["INT4: ~95-98% quality"] I3["INT3: ~90% quality"] end subgraph "Sensitive Tasks" S["Math, reasoning, coding
may degrade more"] end subgraph "Recommendation" R["Always benchmark
your use case"] end style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Practical Guide
steps.explanation: Start with INT8 (safest). Try INT4 if memory-constrained. Use AWQ or GPTQ for GPU, GGUF for CPU. Always evaluate on your specific tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Decision"
        Q1{Memory constrained?}
        Q1 -->|No| F["FP16 or INT8"]
        Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{GPU or CPU?}
        Q2 -->|GPU| G["AWQ or GPTQ"]
        Q2 -->|CPU| GG["GGUF (Q4_K_M)"]
    end
    subgraph "Always"
        A["Benchmark your tasks!"]
    end
    style A fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Speculative Decoding
fields.description: Draft models for faster generation

steps.title: The Inference Bottleneck
steps.explanation: Autoregressive generation is **sequential**: one token at a time. Each token requires a full forward pass. The GPU is underutilized—memory-bound, not compute-bound.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR T1["Token 1"] --> T2["Token 2"] --> T3["Token 3"] --> T4["..."] subgraph "Problem" P["Each token = full forward pass
Sequential bottleneck
GPU underutilized"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Speculative Decoding Idea
steps.explanation: Use a **small, fast draft model** to generate candidate tokens. The **large model verifies** multiple tokens in parallel. Accept correct guesses, reject wrong ones. Net speedup if draft is accurate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Draft" D["Small model generates
k candidate tokens"] end subgraph "Verify" V["Large model checks
all k tokens in parallel"] end subgraph "Accept/Reject" A["Accept correct prefix
Reject from first error"] end D --> V --> A style D fill:#E8F5E9 style V fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: How It Works
steps.explanation: 1) Draft model generates k tokens quickly. 2) Large model scores all k tokens in ONE forward pass. 3) Accept tokens until first mismatch. 4) Repeat. If draft is ~80% accurate, huge speedups.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Step 1: Draft" G["Draft: 'The cat sat on the mat'"] end subgraph "Step 2: Verify (parallel)" V["Large model checks all 6 tokens
in single forward pass"] end subgraph "Step 3: Accept" A["Accept: 'The cat sat on'
Reject: 'the mat' (wrong)"] end G --> V --> A style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Speedup Analysis
steps.explanation: Speedup depends on **draft model acceptance rate**. If draft predicts 80% of tokens correctly, you get ~3-4x speedup. Higher accuracy = more speedup. Wrong guesses cost time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Speedup Factors"
        A["Draft acceptance rate"]
        S["Draft model speed"]
        K["Speculation length k"]
    end
    subgraph "Typical Results"
        R1["70-80% acceptance: 2-3x speedup"]
        R2["80-90% acceptance: 3-4x speedup"]
        R3["<50% acceptance: May be slower!"]
    end
    style R2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Choosing Draft Models
steps.explanation: Draft model should be: **same tokenizer** as target, **much smaller** (7x-10x), **trained similarly** (same distribution). Options: smaller variant, distilled model, or same model with early exit.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Draft Model Options" V1["Smaller variant
(Llama-7B → Llama-70B)"] V2["Distilled model"] V3["N-gram model
(simple but fast)"] V4["Same model, fewer layers"] end subgraph "Requirements" R["Same tokenizer
Similar distribution
Much faster"] end style V1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Self-Speculative Decoding
steps.explanation: No separate draft model needed! Use **early exit** from the same model—stop at an earlier layer for draft tokens. Or use **Medusa**: add extra heads that predict future tokens.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Early Exit" E["Use layer 10 output
as draft for layer 32"] end subgraph "Medusa" M["Extra prediction heads
on the same model"] end subgraph "Benefit" B["No separate model needed"] end style E fill:#E8F5E9 style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use
steps.explanation: Best for: long generations, low-latency requirements, when draft model is available. Not worth it for: short outputs, batch processing (already efficient), very diverse outputs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Fit"
        G1["Long text generation"]
        G2["Latency-critical apps"]
        G3["Good draft model available"]
    end
    subgraph "Poor Fit"
        P1["Short outputs"]
        P2["High batch sizes"]
        P3["Very creative/diverse outputs"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Implementation
steps.explanation: Available in: **vLLM** (native support), **Hugging Face** (transformers), **TensorRT-LLM**. Set draft model and speculation length. Monitor acceptance rate to tune.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Libraries" V["vLLM: --speculative-model"] H["Hugging Face: assistant_model"] T["TensorRT-LLM: draft config"] end subgraph "Tune" TU["Monitor acceptance rate
Adjust k (speculation length)"] end style V fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Batching Strategies
fields.description: Continuous batching and vLLM approach for throughput

steps.title: Why Batching Matters
steps.explanation: GPUs are efficient when processing **multiple requests together**. Batching amortizes memory transfer costs and increases throughput. But LLM batching is tricky due to variable sequence lengths.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "No Batching" N["1 request at a time
GPU underutilized"] end subgraph "Batching" B["Multiple requests together
Higher throughput"] end subgraph "Challenge" C["Variable output lengths
Some finish before others"] end style B fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Static Batching Problem
steps.explanation: **Static batching**: wait for all requests in batch to complete. Short requests wait for long ones. Wastes compute. New requests must wait for next batch.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Static Batch" R1["Request 1: 10 tokens"] R2["Request 2: 100 tokens"] R3["Request 3: 20 tokens"] W["R1, R3 wait for R2
to finish!"] end subgraph "Problem" P["Wasted compute
High latency for short requests"] end style W fill:#FFCDD2 style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Continuous Batching
steps.explanation: **Continuous batching**: As soon as a request finishes, replace it with a new one. Batch is always full. Maximizes GPU utilization. Key innovation behind vLLM.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Continuous Batching" I["Iteration 1: [R1, R2, R3]"] F["R1 finishes"] N["R4 joins immediately"] I2["Iteration 2: [R4, R2, R3]"] end I --> F --> N --> I2 subgraph "Benefit" B["Batch always full
No waiting"] end style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: PagedAttention (vLLM)
steps.explanation: **PagedAttention** manages KV-cache like virtual memory. Allocates memory in blocks, not contiguous. Reduces fragmentation. Enables efficient continuous batching.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Traditional KV-Cache" T["Pre-allocate max length
per request"] TW["Wastes memory on
short sequences"] end subgraph "PagedAttention" P["Allocate blocks
as needed"] PE["Share blocks
across requests"] end style TW fill:#FFCDD2 style P fill:#E8F5E9 style PE fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: vLLM Benefits
steps.explanation: vLLM combines PagedAttention + continuous batching. Result: **2-24x higher throughput** than HuggingFace. Now the standard for production serving.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "vLLM"
        V1["PagedAttention"]
        V2["Continuous batching"]
        V3["Efficient memory"]
    end
    subgraph "Results"
        R1["2-24x throughput vs HF"]
        R2["Lower latency"]
        R3["Higher batch sizes"]
    end
    V1 & V2 & V3 --> R1 & R2 & R3
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Prefix Caching
steps.explanation: Many requests share the same **system prompt**. **Prefix caching** computes the system prompt KV-cache once and reuses it. Huge savings for chatbots and agents.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without Prefix Caching" W["Compute system prompt
for every request"] end subgraph "With Prefix Caching" P1["Compute once"] P2["Reuse for all requests
with same prefix"] end subgraph "Savings" S["Save 100s-1000s of tokens
per request"] end style P2 fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Serving Framework Comparison
steps.explanation: **vLLM**: Best throughput, production standard. **TensorRT-LLM**: NVIDIA optimized, complex. **Text Generation Inference (TGI)**: HuggingFace, good defaults. **Ollama**: Local/dev use.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Production"
        V["vLLM: Best general choice"]
        T["TensorRT-LLM: Max perf, complex"]
        TG["TGI: HuggingFace, easy"]
    end
    subgraph "Development"
        O["Ollama: Local dev"]
        LM["LM Studio: GUI"]
    end
    style V fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Throughput vs Latency
steps.explanation: Larger batches = higher throughput but higher latency. Tune based on your priority. For chat: lower latency. For batch processing: higher throughput.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Trade-off" T["Batch size ↑ = Throughput ↑"] T2["Batch size ↑ = Latency ↑"] end subgraph "Chat/Interactive" C["Smaller batches
Lower latency priority"] end subgraph "Batch Processing" B["Larger batches
Throughput priority"] end style C fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Agents & Tool Use
fields.description: Function calling and ReAct pattern for autonomous agents

steps.title: Beyond Text Generation
steps.explanation: LLMs can do more than generate text—they can **use tools**. By calling APIs, searching the web, or running code, models become **agents** that take actions in the world.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Basic LLM" B["Text in → Text out"] end subgraph "LLM with Tools" A["Text in"] T["Call tools
(APIs, code, search)"] R["Text + Actions out"] A --> T --> R end style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Function Calling
steps.explanation: **Function calling** lets you define functions the model can invoke. Model outputs structured arguments. You execute the function and return results. Built into OpenAI, Claude, and others.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Define Tools" D["get_weather(location)
search_web(query)
send_email(to, body)"] end subgraph "Model Decides" M["User: 'Weather in NYC?'
Model: Call get_weather('NYC')"] end subgraph "Execute & Return" E["Run get_weather('NYC')
Return: 'Sunny, 72°F'"] end subgraph "Final Response" F["Model: 'It's sunny and 72°F in NYC'"] end D --> M --> E --> F style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The Agent Loop
steps.explanation: Agents run in a **loop**: observe, think, act, observe result, repeat. Continue until task is complete or max steps reached. The model decides when to stop.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    S["Start: User task"]
    T["Think: What to do?"]
    A["Act: Call tool"]
    O["Observe: Get result"]
    D{Done?}
    F["Final answer"]
    S --> T --> A --> O --> D
    D -->|No| T
    D -->|Yes| F
    style T fill:#E8F5E9
    style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: ReAct Pattern
steps.explanation: **ReAct** (Reason + Act): Model explicitly states its reasoning before acting. Makes decisions interpretable. Improves accuracy on complex tasks. Widely used in agent frameworks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "ReAct Format" R["Thought: I need to find the weather
Action: get_weather('NYC')
Observation: Sunny, 72°F
Thought: I have the answer
Answer: It's sunny and 72°F"] end subgraph "Benefits" B1["Interpretable reasoning"] B2["Better accuracy"] B3["Easier debugging"] end style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Tool Design Best Practices
steps.explanation: Good tools: **clear names**, **detailed descriptions**, **simple parameters**, **helpful error messages**. The model uses descriptions to decide when and how to call tools.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good Tool Design" G1["Clear, descriptive name"] G2["Detailed description"] G3["Simple parameters"] G4["Good error handling"] end subgraph "Example" E["name: 'search_knowledge_base'
desc: 'Search internal docs for
company policies and procedures'
params: query (string)"] end style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Agent Frameworks
steps.explanation: **LangChain**: Popular, flexible, many integrations. **LlamaIndex**: Data-focused, great for RAG. **AutoGPT/CrewAI**: Autonomous multi-agent. **Custom**: Often simpler for specific use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Frameworks" L["LangChain
General purpose"] LI["LlamaIndex
Data & RAG focused"] A["AutoGPT/CrewAI
Autonomous agents"] C["Custom code
Simple use cases"] end subgraph "Choose" CH["Start simple,
add complexity as needed"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9 style CH fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Agent Challenges
steps.explanation: Agents can: **loop forever**, **make wrong tool calls**, **leak sensitive data**, **cost a lot** (many LLM calls). Implement guardrails, limits, and monitoring.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Risks"
        R1["Infinite loops"]
        R2["Wrong tool calls"]
        R3["Data leakage"]
        R4["High costs"]
    end
    subgraph "Mitigations"
        M1["Max step limits"]
        M2["Tool confirmation"]
        M3["Input/output filtering"]
        M4["Budget controls"]
    end
    style R1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style M1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Agents
steps.explanation: Use agents for: **multi-step tasks**, **information gathering**, **automation workflows**. Don't use for: simple Q&A, deterministic processes, high-stakes without human oversight.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Fit"
        G1["Research tasks"]
        G2["Data analysis"]
        G3["Workflow automation"]
        G4["Code execution"]
    end
    subgraph "Poor Fit"
        P1["Simple Q&A"]
        P2["Deterministic processes"]
        P3["High stakes w/o oversight"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P3 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Hallucination & Grounding
fields.description: Detection and mitigation strategies for factual errors

steps.title: What is Hallucination?
steps.explanation: **Hallucination**: Model generates plausible but incorrect information. Makes up facts, cites non-existent sources, or confidently states falsehoods. A core limitation of LLMs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Hallucination Examples" H1["Citing papers that don't exist"] H2["Wrong dates/statistics"] H3["Fabricated quotes"] H4["Confident but wrong answers"] end subgraph "Root Cause" R["LLMs predict plausible text
Not retrieve verified facts"] end style H1 fill:#FFCDD2 style H2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Types of Hallucination
steps.explanation: **Factual**: Wrong facts about the world. **Faithfulness**: Contradicts provided context. **Instruction**: Doesn't follow given instructions. Each needs different mitigation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Factual Hallucination" F["Incorrect world knowledge
'Paris is in Germany'"] end subgraph "Faithfulness Hallucination" FA["Contradicts source document
'Doc says A, model says B'"] end subgraph "Instruction Hallucination" I["Ignores constraints
'Only use these sources' violated"] end style F fill:#FFCDD2 style FA fill:#FFF3E0 style I fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Mitigation: RAG
steps.explanation: **RAG** (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) grounds answers in retrieved documents. Model answers based on provided context, not just training data. Reduces but doesn't eliminate hallucination.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR Q["Query"] R["Retrieve
relevant docs"] C["Docs as context"] M["Model generates
grounded answer"] Q --> R --> C --> M subgraph "Benefit" B["Answers grounded
in real documents"] end style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Mitigation: Prompting
steps.explanation: Prompt strategies: **ask for sources**, **'I don't know' instruction**, **confidence calibration**, **chain-of-thought** for reasoning transparency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Prompting Strategies" S1["'If unsure, say I don't know'"] S2["'Cite your sources'"] S3["'Rate your confidence 1-10'"] S4["'Think step by step'"] end subgraph "Example" E["Only answer based on the
provided context. If the
answer isn't in the context,
say 'I don't have that information.'"] end style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Detection Methods
steps.explanation: Detecting hallucinations: **consistency checking** (ask multiple times), **retrieval verification** (check against sources), **classifier models** (trained to detect hallucination).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Detection Methods" C["Consistency Check
Ask same question 5x"] R["Retrieval Verify
Check claims against sources"] M["Classifier Model
Trained hallucination detector"] end subgraph "Libraries" L["Guardrails, NeMo Guardrails"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9 style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Faithfulness Evaluation
steps.explanation: For RAG systems, measure **faithfulness**: does the answer match the retrieved context? Use NLI models or LLM-as-judge to verify claims are supported.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Faithfulness Check" C["Retrieved Context"] A["Generated Answer"] V["Verifier
(NLI or LLM)"] S["Score: supported/unsupported"] end C & A --> V --> S subgraph "Metrics" M["% claims supported
by context"] end style V fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Real-time Guardrails
steps.explanation: Production systems need **real-time checks**. Validate outputs before returning to users. Block or flag potential hallucinations. Balance thoroughness vs latency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR G["Generate
response"] C["Check
guardrails"] D{Pass?} R["Return
to user"] F["Flag or
regenerate"] G --> C --> D D -->|Yes| R D -->|No| F style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Managing Expectations
steps.explanation: Hallucination can't be fully eliminated. Set appropriate user expectations. Use disclaimers. Design UX for verification. Human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Best Practices" B1["Set user expectations"] B2["Add source citations"] B3["Enable fact-checking"] B4["Human review for high stakes"] end subgraph "Accept" A["Some hallucination
is unavoidable"] end subgraph "Design" D["Systems that are
robust to occasional errors"] end style D fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Guardrails Architecture
fields.description: Safety layers and content filtering for production LLMs

steps.title: Why Guardrails?
steps.explanation: LLMs can generate **harmful, biased, or off-topic content**. **Guardrails** are safety layers that filter inputs and outputs, ensuring models behave appropriately in production.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Risks" R1["Harmful content"] R2["Prompt injection"] R3["Data leakage"] R4["Off-topic responses"] end subgraph "Guardrails" G["Safety layers to
prevent/detect issues"] end R1 & R2 & R3 & R4 --> G style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Guardrails Architecture
steps.explanation: Guardrails wrap the LLM: **input guardrails** filter user messages, **output guardrails** filter model responses. Both should be fast and reliable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR U["User Input"] IG["Input
Guardrails"] M["LLM"] OG["Output
Guardrails"] R["Response"] U --> IG --> M --> OG --> R IG -->|"Block"| B1["Reject"] OG -->|"Block"| B2["Regenerate"] style IG fill:#E8F5E9 style OG fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Input Guardrails
steps.explanation: **Input guardrails** protect against: prompt injection, toxic content, PII in prompts, off-topic queries. Block or sanitize before reaching the model.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Input Checks"
        I1["Prompt injection detection"]
        I2["Toxicity/hate speech"]
        I3["PII detection"]
        I4["Topic classification"]
        I5["Rate limiting"]
    end
    subgraph "Actions"
        A1["Block request"]
        A2["Sanitize input"]
        A3["Add warnings"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style I3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Output Guardrails
steps.explanation: **Output guardrails** catch: harmful generated content, leaked system prompts, hallucinated PII, off-brand messaging. Block, regenerate, or modify before returning.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Output Checks"
        O1["Content safety"]
        O2["System prompt leakage"]
        O3["PII in response"]
        O4["Factuality check"]
        O5["Brand compliance"]
    end
    subgraph "Actions"
        A1["Block response"]
        A2["Regenerate"]
        A3["Redact sensitive parts"]
    end
    style O1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style O2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Implementation Options
steps.explanation: **Rule-based**: regex, keyword lists. **ML classifiers**: trained content moderators. **LLM-as-judge**: another LLM evaluates. Trade-offs between speed, accuracy, and cost.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Approaches" R["Rule-based
Fast, limited"] C["ML Classifiers
Balanced"] L["LLM-as-Judge
Accurate, slow"] end subgraph "Choose Based On" CH["Latency requirements
Accuracy needs
Cost budget"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Guardrails Frameworks
steps.explanation: **NeMo Guardrails** (NVIDIA): Programmable rails. **Guardrails AI**: Schema validation + LLM checks. **Lakera**: API-based detection. **Custom**: Often needed for specific requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Frameworks" N["NeMo Guardrails
Programmable rails"] G["Guardrails AI
Validation + LLM"] L["Lakera Guard
API service"] C["Custom
Specific needs"] end subgraph "Features" F["Input/output filtering
Hallucination detection
Custom policies"] end style N fill:#E8F5E9 style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Layered Defense
steps.explanation: No single guardrail is perfect. Use **defense in depth**: multiple checks at different layers. Fast checks first (rules), then accurate checks (ML/LLM) as needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Layer 1: Fast"
        L1["Keyword blocklist"]
        L1B["Regex patterns"]
    end
    subgraph "Layer 2: ML"
        L2["Toxicity classifier"]
        L2B["PII detector"]
    end
    subgraph "Layer 3: LLM"
        L3["Semantic check"]
        L3B["Policy compliance"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3
    style L1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style L2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L3 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Monitoring & Iteration
steps.explanation: Guardrails need monitoring: false positives (blocking good content), false negatives (missing bad content). Continuously improve based on real-world feedback.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Monitor"
        M1["False positive rate"]
        M2["False negative rate"]
        M3["Latency impact"]
        M4["User complaints"]
    end
    subgraph "Iterate"
        I1["Tune thresholds"]
        I2["Add new patterns"]
        I3["Retrain classifiers"]
    end
    M1 & M2 & M3 & M4 --> I1 & I2 & I3
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: App & Function Decorators
fields.description: How @app.function, @app.local_entrypoint, and @app.cls work together in Modal

steps.title: Modal App Structure
steps.explanation: Every Modal application starts with an **App** object. The app is a container that groups related functions, classes, and resources together for deployment.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "modal_app.py"
        A["app = modal.App('my-app')"]
        F1["@app.function()"]
        F2["@app.cls()"]
        F3["@app.local_entrypoint()"]
    end
    A --> F1 & F2 & F3
    style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: @app.function Decorator
steps.explanation: **@app.function()** marks a function to run remotely in Modal's cloud. You can specify compute resources, image, secrets, and more. Call with `.remote()` to execute in the cloud.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Definition" D["@app.function(
gpu='A10G',
image=my_image
)"] F["def process(data):"] end subgraph "Invocation" L["Local: process.local(x)"] R["Remote: process.remote(x)"] end D --> F F --> L & R R --> C["Modal Cloud"] style R fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: @app.cls Decorator
steps.explanation: **@app.cls()** creates a remote class. Use `@modal.method()` for methods that run remotely. Great for ML models where you load weights once in `__enter__` and run inference in methods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Remote Class" C["@app.cls(gpu='A100')"] CLS["class Model:"] E["def __enter__(self):
self.model = load()"] M["@modal.method()
def predict(self, x):"] end C --> CLS --> E --> M subgraph "Usage" U["model = Model()
model.predict.remote(x)"] end M --> U style E fill:#FFF3E0 style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: @app.local_entrypoint
steps.explanation: **@app.local_entrypoint()** marks the function that runs locally when you execute `modal run`. It's your script's entry point that orchestrates remote function calls.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Local Machine"
        E["@app.local_entrypoint()"]
        M["def main():"]
        C1["result = func1.remote()"]
        C2["results = func2.map(items)"]
    end
    subgraph "Modal Cloud"
        F1["func1()"]
        F2["func2() × N"]
    end
    E --> M --> C1 & C2
    C1 --> F1
    C2 --> F2
    style E fill:#E3F2FD
    style F1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: A typical Modal app combines all three: an App object, functions with compute specs, and a local entrypoint. Run with `modal run app.py`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "app.py" A["app = modal.App('ml-pipeline')"] I["image = modal.Image.debian_slim()
.pip_install('torch')"] F["@app.function(image=image, gpu='A10G')
def train(data): ..."] E["@app.local_entrypoint()
def main():
train.remote(data)"] end A --> I --> F --> E subgraph "Execution" CMD["$ modal run app.py"] L["main() runs locally"] R["train() runs on GPU"] end CMD --> L --> R style A fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Container Lifecycle
fields.description: Cold start → warm → execution → shutdown flow in Modal containers

steps.title: Container States
steps.explanation: Modal containers go through distinct states: **Cold** (not running), **Starting** (initializing), **Warm** (ready for requests), and **Shutdown** (cleaning up).
steps.diagram_data:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Cold
    Cold --> Starting: Request arrives
    Starting --> Warm: Container ready
    Warm --> Warm: Process requests
    Warm --> Shutdown: Idle timeout
    Shutdown --> [*]

    note right of Cold: No container exists
    note right of Warm: Fast response

steps.title: Cold Start Process
steps.explanation: A **cold start** happens when no warm container is available. Modal must: provision compute, pull/build image, start container, run initialization code. This takes seconds.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    R["Request arrives"] --> C{"Warm container?"}
    C -->|No| P["Provision compute"]
    P --> I["Pull/build image"]
    I --> S["Start container"]
    S --> INIT["Run __enter__ / imports"]
    INIT --> EXEC["Execute function"]
    C -->|Yes| EXEC

    style P fill:#FFF3E0
    style I fill:#FFF3E0
    style S fill:#FFF3E0
    style INIT fill:#FFF3E0
    style EXEC fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Warm Container Reuse
steps.explanation: After handling a request, containers stay **warm** for a period. Subsequent requests reuse the warm container, skipping cold start entirely. Much faster!
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant M as Modal
    participant W as Warm Container

    Note over W: Container already warm
    C->>M: Request 1
    M->>W: Execute
    W-->>M: Result
    M-->>C: Response (fast)

    Note over W: Still warm
    C->>M: Request 2
    M->>W: Execute
    W-->>M: Result
    M-->>C: Response (fast)

steps.title: __enter__ and __exit__
steps.explanation: For `@app.cls()` classes, `__enter__` runs once at container start (load models here). `__exit__` runs at shutdown for cleanup. Methods run many times on the warm container.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Container Start (once)" E["__enter__(self)
self.model = load_model()"] end subgraph "Handle Requests (many)" M1["predict(x1) → y1"] M2["predict(x2) → y2"] M3["predict(x3) → y3"] end subgraph "Container Stop (once)" X["__exit__()
cleanup()"] end E --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> X style E fill:#FFF3E0 style M1 fill:#E8F5E9 style M2 fill:#E8F5E9 style M3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Idle Timeout & Shutdown
steps.explanation: Warm containers shut down after an idle period (default ~5 min). You can use `container_idle_timeout` to adjust. Use `keep_warm` to maintain containers even without requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Warm Container" W["Waiting for requests"] end W -->|"Idle > timeout"| S["Shutdown"] W -->|"New request"| W subgraph "Configuration" K["keep_warm=1
Always 1 container ready"] T["container_idle_timeout=300
5 min idle before shutdown"] end style W fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Lifecycle Timeline
steps.explanation: Complete timeline: cold start (seconds), warm execution (milliseconds per request), idle period, then shutdown. Optimize by minimizing cold start work and using keep_warm.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Cold Start (2-30s)"
        A1["Provision"]
        A2["Image"]
        A3["Init"]
    end
    subgraph "Warm (ms per req)"
        B1["Req 1"]
        B2["Req 2"]
        B3["..."]
    end
    subgraph "Idle"
        C1["Waiting..."]
    end
    subgraph "Shutdown"
        D1["Cleanup"]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> D1
    style A1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style A2 fill:#FFF3E0
    style A3 fill:#FFF3E0
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style B2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Image Building & Layers
fields.description: How Modal images are constructed with .pip_install(), .run_commands(), and layer caching

steps.title: Modal Image Basics
steps.explanation: Modal images define the environment your code runs in. Start from a base image and chain methods to add dependencies. Each method creates a cached layer.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    B["modal.Image.debian_slim()"]
    P[".pip_install('torch', 'numpy')"]
    R[".run_commands('apt-get install -y ffmpeg')"]
    C[".copy_local_file('model.py')"]

    B --> P --> R --> C

    style B fill:#E3F2FD
    style P fill:#E8F5E9
    style R fill:#E8F5E9
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Base Images
steps.explanation: Modal provides optimized base images. Choose based on your needs: **debian_slim** (minimal), **micromamba** (conda), or start from a **Dockerfile**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Base Image Options" D["modal.Image.debian_slim()
Minimal Debian, fast"] M["modal.Image.micromamba()
Conda support"] F["modal.Image.from_dockerfile()
Custom Dockerfile"] R["modal.Image.from_registry()
'pytorch/pytorch:2.0'"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD style M fill:#E8F5E9 style F fill:#FFF3E0 style R fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Layer Methods
steps.explanation: Each method adds a layer. **pip_install** for Python packages, **run_commands** for shell commands, **apt_install** for system packages, **copy** for files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Python Packages"
        P1[".pip_install('torch')"]
        P2[".pip_install_from_requirements('req.txt')"]
        P3[".pip_install_from_pyproject('.')"]
    end
    subgraph "System Packages"
        A1[".apt_install('ffmpeg', 'git')"]
        R1[".run_commands('wget ...')"]
    end
    subgraph "Files"
        C1[".copy_local_file('model.py')"]
        C2[".copy_local_dir('./src', '/app')"]
        C3[".add_local_python_source('mypackage')"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style C1 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Layer Caching
steps.explanation: Modal caches each layer. If a layer hasn't changed, it's reused. **Order matters**: put stable layers first (system deps), changing layers last (your code).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good Order (cache-friendly)" G1["debian_slim()"] --> G2["apt_install('ffmpeg')"] G2 --> G3["pip_install('torch')"] G3 --> G4["copy_local_file('app.py')"] end subgraph "Cache Behavior" CB["app.py changes →
Only layer 4 rebuilds"] end style G1 fill:#E8F5E9 style G2 fill:#E8F5E9 style G3 fill:#E8F5E9 style G4 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: GPU-Optimized Images
steps.explanation: For GPU workloads, use CUDA-enabled images. Modal handles CUDA/cuDNN installation. Chain with .pip_install for ML frameworks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GPU Image" B["modal.Image.debian_slim(
python_version='3.11'
)"] P[".pip_install(
'torch',
'transformers',
'accelerate'
)"] end B --> P subgraph "Usage" F["@app.function(
image=gpu_image,
gpu='A100'
)"] end P --> F style B fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#E8F5E9 style F fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Build-time vs Run-time
steps.explanation: Some operations run at **build time** (creating the image), others at **run time** (in your function). Use `.run_function()` to run Python at build time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Build Time"
        B1["pip_install → installs packages"]
        B2["run_commands → shell commands"]
        B3["run_function → Python code"]
    end
    subgraph "Run Time"
        R1["Your @app.function code"]
        R2["__enter__ initialization"]
    end

    B1 --> B2 --> B3 -->|"Image ready"| R1
    R1 --> R2

    style B1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style B2 fill:#E3F2FD
    style B3 fill:#E3F2FD
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Local vs Remote Execution
fields.description: Understanding what runs locally vs in Modal's cloud

steps.title: Two Execution Contexts
steps.explanation: Modal code runs in two places: **locally** (your machine) and **remotely** (Modal's cloud). Understanding which code runs where is crucial.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Your Machine"
        L["Local execution"]
    end
    subgraph "Modal Cloud"
        R["Remote execution"]
    end
    L <-->|".remote()"| R
    style L fill:#E3F2FD
    style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: What Runs Locally
steps.explanation: **@app.local_entrypoint()** functions run on your machine. They orchestrate remote calls, handle CLI args, and process results. No GPU needed locally!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Local (Your Machine)" E["@app.local_entrypoint()
def main():"] A[" args = parse_args()"] C[" result = process.remote(args)"] P[" print(result)"] end E --> A --> C --> P style E fill:#E3F2FD style A fill:#E3F2FD style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: What Runs Remotely
steps.explanation: **@app.function()** code runs in Modal's cloud when called with `.remote()`. This is where GPUs, large memory, and compute happen.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Remote (Modal Cloud)"
        F["@app.function(gpu='A100')"]
        D["def train(data):"]
        M["  model = load_model()"]
        T["  model.fit(data)"]
        R["  return model"]
    end
    F --> D --> M --> T --> R
    style F fill:#E8F5E9
    style M fill:#E8F5E9
    style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: .remote() vs .local()
steps.explanation: Call `.remote()` to run in Modal's cloud. Call `.local()` to run locally (useful for debugging). The function code is the same, but execution context differs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB F["@app.function()
def compute(x):"] subgraph "Invocation Options" R["compute.remote(x)
→ Runs in Modal cloud"] L["compute.local(x)
→ Runs on your machine"] end F --> R & L style R fill:#E8F5E9 style L fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Data Flow
steps.explanation: Arguments are **serialized** and sent to Modal. Results are serialized back. Keep data transfer minimal for performance. Use Volumes for large data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Local"
        A["args (Python objects)"]
        R["results"]
    end
    subgraph "Network"
        S1["Serialize →"]
        S2["← Serialize"]
    end
    subgraph "Remote"
        F["function(args)"]
        RES["return value"]
    end
    A --> S1 --> F
    F --> RES --> S2 --> R
    style S1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style S2 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Complete Flow
steps.explanation: Typical flow: local entrypoint parses args → calls remote functions → remote functions do heavy compute → results return to local for display/storage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Local"
        E["main()"]
        P["Parse args"]
        D["Display results"]
    end
    subgraph "Remote (Modal)"
        F1["preprocess.remote()"]
        F2["train.remote()"]
        F3["evaluate.remote()"]
    end
    E --> P --> F1
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    F3 --> D
    style E fill:#E3F2FD
    style P fill:#E3F2FD
    style D fill:#E3F2FD
    style F1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: GPU Provisioning
fields.description: A10G vs A100 vs H100, memory tiers, and GPU selection strategies

steps.title: Available GPUs
steps.explanation: Modal offers various GPU types. Each has different memory, compute power, and cost. Choose based on your workload requirements.

16GB • Budget"] L4["L4
24GB • Balanced"] A10["A10G
24GB • Popular"] A100["A100
40/80GB • High-end"] H100["H100
80GB • Cutting-edge"] end T4 --> L4 --> A10 --> A100 --> H100 style T4 fill:#E3F2FD style A10 fill:#E8F5E9 style A100 fill:#FFF3E0 style H100 fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Specifying GPUs
steps.explanation: Use the `gpu` parameter in `@app.function()`. Specify by name string or use `modal.gpu` module for more control.

flowchart TB
    subgraph "Simple String"
        S1["gpu='T4'"]
        S2["gpu='A10G'"]

    end

steps.title: Memory Tiers
steps.explanation: A100 comes in 40GB and 80GB variants. Specify with `size` parameter. Larger models need more VRAM. H100 is always 80GB.

flowchart LR subgraph "A100 Variants" A40["A100-40GB
Faster availability"] A80["A100-80GB
Large models"] end subgraph "Specification" C1["modal.gpu.A100() # 40GB default"] C2["modal.gpu.A100(size='80GB')"] end A40 --- C1 A80 --- C2 style A40 fill:#E8F5E9 style A80 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Multi-GPU
steps.explanation: For large models or faster training, use multiple GPUs with `count`. Your code must use distributed training (PyTorch DDP, etc.).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multi-GPU Config" C["gpu=modal.gpu.A100(count=4)"] end subgraph "Container" G1["GPU 0"] G2["GPU 1"] G3["GPU 2"] G4["GPU 3"] end C --> G1 & G2 & G3 & G4 subgraph "Your Code" D["torch.distributed
or accelerate"] end G1 & G2 & G3 & G4 --> D style C fill:#E3F2FD style G1 fill:#E8F5E9 style G2 fill:#E8F5E9 style G3 fill:#E8F5E9 style G4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: GPU Selection Strategy
steps.explanation: Choose GPU based on: **model size** (VRAM needed), **latency requirements**, **cost sensitivity**. Start small, scale up if needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{"Model size?"} Q1 -->|"< 7B params"| T4["T4/L4
16-24GB"] Q1 -->|"7B-30B"| A10["A10G/A100-40
24-40GB"] Q1 -->|"30B-70B"| A100["A100-80GB
80GB"] Q1 -->|"70B+"| MULTI["Multi-GPU
or H100"] style T4 fill:#E3F2FD style A10 fill:#E8F5E9 style A100 fill:#FFF3E0 style MULTI fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Fallback with Any()
steps.explanation: Use `modal.gpu.Any()` to accept any available GPU. Good for flexible workloads. Faster scheduling but less predictable performance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Flexible" A["gpu=modal.gpu.Any()"] O["Gets whatever is available:
T4, L4, A10G, etc."] end subgraph "Specific" S["gpu='A100'"] W["May wait for availability"] end A --> O S --> W style A fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Auto-Scaling & Concurrency
fields.description: How Modal scales containers and handles concurrent requests

steps.title: Auto-Scaling Basics
steps.explanation: Modal automatically scales containers based on demand. More requests → more containers. No manual scaling needed. Scale to zero when idle.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Low Traffic"
        L1["Container 1"]
    end
    subgraph "High Traffic"
        H1["Container 1"]
        H2["Container 2"]
        H3["Container 3"]
        H4["Container N"]
    end
    subgraph "No Traffic"
        Z["Zero containers"]
    end
    style L1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style H1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style H3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style Z fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Concurrency Limit
steps.explanation: **concurrency_limit** sets max containers running simultaneously. Useful for rate limiting, resource control, or API quotas. Default is no limit.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Without Limit" W1["1000 requests → 1000 containers"] end subgraph "With concurrency_limit=10" C["Max 10 containers"] Q["Queue for excess requests"] end L["@app.function(
concurrency_limit=10
)"] L --> C & Q style C fill:#E8F5E9 style Q fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Allow Concurrent Inputs
steps.explanation: **allow_concurrent_inputs** lets one container handle multiple requests simultaneously. Great for I/O-bound work. Set a number or use True for default (4).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "allow_concurrent_inputs=1 (default)" C1["Container handles
1 request at a time"] Q1["Others queue"] end subgraph "allow_concurrent_inputs=10" C2["Container handles
up to 10 requests"] E2["Concurrent execution"] end style C1 fill:#E3F2FD style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Scaling Formula
steps.explanation: Containers needed = requests ÷ concurrent_inputs. With 100 requests and concurrent_inputs=10, you need ~10 containers instead of 100.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "100 Requests"
        R["Incoming"]
    end
    subgraph "concurrent_inputs=1"
        C1["100 containers needed"]
    end
    subgraph "concurrent_inputs=10"
        C2["10 containers needed"]
    end
    R --> C1
    R --> C2
    style C1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Use Cases
steps.explanation: Use **concurrency_limit** for resource caps. Use **allow_concurrent_inputs** for I/O-bound functions. Combine for fine-grained control.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "CPU-bound (ML inference)" CPU["allow_concurrent_inputs=1
Full CPU per request"] end subgraph "I/O-bound (API calls)" IO["allow_concurrent_inputs=20
Many concurrent requests"] end subgraph "Rate Limited API" RL["concurrency_limit=5
Max 5 concurrent calls"] end style CPU fill:#E3F2FD style IO fill:#E8F5E9 style RL fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Combine settings: limit total containers AND handle multiple inputs per container. Optimize for your specific workload pattern.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Configuration" F["@app.function(
concurrency_limit=20,
allow_concurrent_inputs=5
)"] end subgraph "Result" M["Max 20 containers"] C["5 requests each"] T["= 100 concurrent requests max"] end F --> M --> C --> T style F fill:#E3F2FD style T fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Cold Starts vs Warm Pools
fields.description: Understanding keep_warm, container reuse, and latency implications

steps.title: Cold vs Warm Starts
steps.explanation: **Cold start**: No container ready, must provision (seconds). **Warm start**: Container ready, immediate execution (milliseconds). Warm is much faster.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Cold Start (2-30s)"
        C1["Provision"]
        C2["Pull image"]
        C3["Start container"]
        C4["Run __enter__"]
        C5["Execute"]
    end
    subgraph "Warm Start (ms)"
        W1["Execute"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5
    style C1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style C2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style C3 fill:#FFEBEE
    style C4 fill:#FFEBEE
    style W1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Why Cold Starts Happen
steps.explanation: Cold starts occur when: first request arrives, traffic spikes beyond warm containers, containers have been idle and shut down, or code/image changes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Causes of Cold Starts"
        F["First request ever"]
        S["Traffic spike"]
        I["Idle shutdown"]
        D["New deployment"]
    end
    F & S & I & D --> CS["Cold Start"]
    style CS fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: keep_warm Parameter
steps.explanation: **keep_warm=N** maintains N containers always running. Eliminates cold starts for initial requests. Costs money but guarantees low latency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without keep_warm"
        W0["0 containers when idle"]
        CS["First request = cold start"]
    end
    subgraph "With keep_warm=2"
        W2["2 containers always ready"]
        WS["First 2 requests = instant"]
    end

    F["@app.function(keep_warm=2)"]
    F --> W2 --> WS
    style W0 fill:#FFEBEE
    style W2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style WS fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Container Idle Timeout
steps.explanation: After handling requests, containers stay warm for a timeout period. Adjust with **container_idle_timeout**. Longer = more warm hits, higher cost.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR R["Request handled"] W["Container warm"] T["Timeout countdown"] S["Shutdown"] N["New request"] R --> W --> T T -->|"Timeout expires"| S T -->|"Request arrives"| W C["container_idle_timeout=600
(10 minutes)"] style W fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Optimizing Cold Starts
steps.explanation: Minimize cold start time: use smaller images, lazy load heavy imports, load models in __enter__ for classes, cache to Volumes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Slow"
        S1["Large image"]
        S2["Heavy imports at top"]
        S3["Download model every time"]
    end
    subgraph "Fast"
        F1["Minimal image"]
        F2["Lazy imports"]
        F3["Cache model in Volume"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style S2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style S3 fill:#FFEBEE
    style F1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style F3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Strategy by Use Case
steps.explanation: Choose strategy based on latency requirements and budget: batch jobs tolerate cold starts, APIs need warm pools, production needs keep_warm.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Batch Processing" B["Cold starts OK
keep_warm=0"] end subgraph "Development API" D["Some cold starts OK
container_idle_timeout=300"] end subgraph "Production API" P["No cold starts
keep_warm=2+"] end style B fill:#E3F2FD style D fill:#FFF3E0 style P fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Container Scheduling
fields.description: How Modal schedules containers across its infrastructure

steps.title: Scheduling Overview
steps.explanation: When you call `.remote()`, Modal's scheduler finds the best place to run your container. It considers: GPU availability, region, warm containers, and resource requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    R["func.remote(x)"]
    S["Modal Scheduler"]
    subgraph "Infrastructure"
        N1["Node Pool 1"]
        N2["Node Pool 2"]
        N3["Node Pool N"]
    end
    R --> S
    S --> N1 & N2 & N3
    style S fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Resource Matching
steps.explanation: Scheduler matches your requirements (GPU type, memory, CPU) to available nodes. Specific GPU requests may wait longer than flexible requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Your Request" REQ["gpu='A100'
memory=32GB
cpu=4"] end subgraph "Scheduler Checks" C1["A100 available?"] C2["32GB RAM free?"] C3["4 CPUs free?"] end subgraph "Match Found" M["Schedule on Node X"] end REQ --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> M style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Warm Container Priority
steps.explanation: Scheduler prefers **warm containers** over cold starts. If a warm container matches your function, it's reused immediately.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB R["Request arrives"] C{"Warm container
available?"} C -->|Yes| W["Use warm container
(instant)"] C -->|No| COLD["Cold start
(seconds)"] style W fill:#E8F5E9 style COLD fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Queue Behavior
steps.explanation: When resources are scarce or concurrency_limit is hit, requests queue. Modal processes queue FIFO. Long queues indicate need for more capacity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Incoming" R1["Req 1"] R2["Req 2"] R3["Req 3"] end subgraph "Queue" Q["Waiting..."] end subgraph "Running" C["Container
(at capacity)"] end R1 & R2 & R3 --> Q --> C style Q fill:#FFF3E0 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Region Selection
steps.explanation: Modal runs in multiple cloud regions. By default, it picks the best region. You can specify region for data locality or compliance needs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Regions"
        US["us-east"]
        EU["eu-west"]
        ASIA["asia-pacific"]
    end
    subgraph "Selection"
        AUTO["Auto: nearest to you"]
        SPEC["Explicit: region='us-east'"]
    end
    AUTO --> US & EU & ASIA
    SPEC --> US
    style US fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Scheduling Insights
steps.explanation: Monitor scheduling with Modal dashboard. See container starts, queue times, and resource utilization. Optimize based on patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Metrics to Watch"
        M1["Cold start frequency"]
        M2["Queue wait time"]
        M3["Container utilization"]
        M4["GPU availability"]
    end
    subgraph "Actions"
        A1["Increase keep_warm"]
        A2["Adjust concurrency"]
        A3["Use different GPU"]
    end
    M1 --> A1
    M2 --> A2
    M4 --> A3
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Volumes & NetworkFileSystem
fields.description: Persistent storage options and their use cases in Modal

steps.title: Storage Options
steps.explanation: Modal provides two persistent storage types: **Volume** (fast, eventually consistent) and **NetworkFileSystem** (POSIX-compliant, strongly consistent). Choose based on your needs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Storage Types" V["modal.Volume
Fast, eventually consistent"] N["modal.NetworkFileSystem
POSIX, strongly consistent"] end style V fill:#E8F5E9 style N fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Volume Basics
steps.explanation: **Volume** is high-performance storage. Create once, attach to functions. Great for caching models, storing outputs. Changes propagate eventually (seconds).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Create Volume" C["vol = modal.Volume.from_name(
'my-volume',
create_if_missing=True
)"] end subgraph "Attach to Function" F["@app.function(
volumes={'/data': vol}
)"] end subgraph "Use in Code" U["with open('/data/file.txt') as f:"] end C --> F --> U style C fill:#E3F2FD style F fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: NetworkFileSystem
steps.explanation: **NetworkFileSystem** provides POSIX semantics. Strongly consistent—changes visible immediately. Better for shared state between concurrent containers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Create NFS" C["nfs = modal.NetworkFileSystem.from_name(
'my-nfs',
create_if_missing=True
)"] end subgraph "Attach" F["@app.function(
network_file_systems={'/shared': nfs}
)"] end subgraph "Concurrent Access" C1["Container 1: write"] C2["Container 2: read
(sees write immediately)"] end C --> F C1 --> C2 style C fill:#E3F2FD style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Volume vs NFS
steps.explanation: Choose **Volume** for: model caching, artifacts, single-writer. Choose **NFS** for: shared state, concurrent writes, POSIX compliance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Volume (Best For)"
        V1["Model weights cache"]
        V2["Output artifacts"]
        V3["Single container writing"]
        V4["High throughput reads"]
    end
    subgraph "NFS (Best For)"
        N1["Shared checkpoints"]
        N2["Concurrent writes"]
        N3["POSIX tools (rsync, etc)"]
        N4["Strong consistency needed"]
    end
    style V1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style V2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style N2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Caching Models
steps.explanation: Common pattern: cache downloaded models in Volume. First run downloads, subsequent runs use cache. Eliminates repeated downloads.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "First Run"
        F1["Check cache"] --> F2["Not found"]
        F2 --> F3["Download model"]
        F3 --> F4["Save to Volume"]
    end
    subgraph "Later Runs"
        L1["Check cache"] --> L2["Found!"]
        L2 --> L3["Load from Volume"]
    end
    F4 -->|"Volume persists"| L1
    style F3 fill:#FFF3E0
    style L3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Volume Commit
steps.explanation: Volume writes need **commit()** to persist. Changes are batched for performance. Without commit, data may be lost when container stops.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Write Pattern" W["with open('/data/out.txt', 'w') as f:
f.write(data)"] C["vol.commit() # Persist changes"] end subgraph "Result" P["Data persisted to Volume"] end W --> C --> P style C fill:#E8F5E9 style P fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Secrets Management
fields.description: modal.Secret, environment injection, and security model

steps.title: Secrets Overview
steps.explanation: **modal.Secret** securely stores API keys, credentials, and sensitive data. Secrets are encrypted at rest and injected as environment variables at runtime.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Modal Dashboard" S["Secret: 'openai-key'
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-..."] end subgraph "Your Function" E["os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY']"] end S -->|"Injected at runtime"| E style S fill:#F3E5F5 style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Creating Secrets
steps.explanation: Create secrets via Modal dashboard or CLI. Each secret can contain multiple key-value pairs. Reference by name in your code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Dashboard" D["Create secret 'my-api-keys'
API_KEY=xxx
API_SECRET=yyy"] end subgraph "CLI" C["modal secret create my-api-keys
API_KEY=xxx API_SECRET=yyy"] end subgraph "Code Reference" R["modal.Secret.from_name('my-api-keys')"] end D --> R C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Using Secrets
steps.explanation: Attach secrets to functions with the `secrets` parameter. Multiple secrets can be attached. Access via `os.environ` in your function.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Function Definition" F["@app.function(
secrets=[
modal.Secret.from_name('openai'),
modal.Secret.from_name('db-creds')
]
)"] end subgraph "Function Body" B["def my_func():
key = os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY']
db_pass = os.environ['DB_PASSWORD']"] end F --> B style F fill:#E3F2FD style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Built-in Secret Types
steps.explanation: Modal provides templates for common services. Use `modal.Secret.from_name()` for custom secrets or built-in patterns for known services.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Custom" C["modal.Secret.from_name('my-secret')"] end subgraph "Environment Dict" E["modal.Secret.from_dict({
'KEY': 'value'
})"] end subgraph "Local Env" L["modal.Secret.from_local_environ(
['OPENAI_API_KEY']
)"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9 style E fill:#E3F2FD style L fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Security Model
steps.explanation: Secrets are: encrypted at rest, never logged, only available to your functions, scoped to your workspace. Never hardcode secrets in code!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Secure"
        S1["Stored encrypted"]
        S2["Injected at runtime"]
        S3["Not in logs"]
        S4["Workspace-scoped"]
    end
    subgraph "Never Do"
        N1["Hardcode in source"]
        N2["Log secret values"]
        N3["Commit to git"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style N2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style N3 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Local Development
steps.explanation: For local testing, use `.local()` which can read from your local environment. Or use `modal.Secret.from_local_environ()` to mirror local env vars.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Local Testing"
        L["my_func.local()"]
        LE["Reads local env vars"]
    end
    subgraph "Remote Execution"
        R["my_func.remote()"]
        RS["Reads Modal secrets"]
    end
    L --> LE
    R --> RS
    style LE fill:#E3F2FD
    style RS fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Mount Patterns
fields.description: modal.Mount for code/data and when to use each approach

steps.title: What Are Mounts?
steps.explanation: **Mounts** copy local files into your Modal container at build time. Use for code, configs, and small data files that are part of your project.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Local"
        L["./src/"]
        C["./config.yaml"]
    end
    subgraph "Mount"
        M["modal.Mount"]
    end
    subgraph "Container"
        R["/root/src/"]
        RC["/root/config.yaml"]
    end
    L --> M --> R
    C --> M --> RC
    style M fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Mount Types
steps.explanation: **Mount.from_local_dir()** for directories, **Mount.from_local_file()** for single files. Specify local path and remote path.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Directory Mount" D["modal.Mount.from_local_dir(
'./src',
remote_path='/app/src'
)"] end subgraph "File Mount" F["modal.Mount.from_local_file(
'./config.yaml',
remote_path='/app/config.yaml'
)"] end style D fill:#E8F5E9 style F fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Using Mounts
steps.explanation: Attach mounts via the `mounts` parameter. Files are available at the specified remote paths. Great for local code that isn't pip-installable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Definition" M["code_mount = modal.Mount.from_local_dir(
'./mypackage',
remote_path='/root/mypackage'
)"] F["@app.function(mounts=[code_mount])"] end subgraph "Function" I["import sys
sys.path.append('/root')
from mypackage import utils"] end M --> F --> I style F fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Mount vs Image Copy
steps.explanation: **Mount**: synced each run, good for development. **Image copy**: baked into image, cached, good for production. Choose based on change frequency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Mount (Development)"
        MD["Files synced every run"]
        MC["Changes reflected immediately"]
        MS["Slower cold start"]
    end
    subgraph "Image Copy (Production)"
        ID[".copy_local_file() in image"]
        IC["Baked into cached layers"]
        IS["Faster cold start"]
    end
    style MD fill:#FFF3E0
    style ID fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Python Source Mount
steps.explanation: For Python packages, use **add_local_python_source()** on the image. This is the recommended pattern for your own packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Image Method (Recommended)" I["image = modal.Image.debian_slim()
.pip_install('deps')
.add_local_python_source('mypackage')"] end subgraph "Result" R["mypackage importable
from anywhere in function"] end I --> R style I fill:#E8F5E9 style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: Use **Mounts** for: rapid iteration, config files, data. Use **Image** for: dependencies, stable code, production. Use **Volumes** for: large files, persistence.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Mounts"
        M1["Config files"]
        M2["Development iteration"]
        M3["Small data files"]
    end
    subgraph "Image Copy"
        I1["Python packages"]
        I2["Stable source code"]
        I3["System dependencies"]
    end
    subgraph "Volumes"
        V1["Large datasets"]
        V2["Model weights"]
        V3["Persisted outputs"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style V1 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Sync vs Async Calls
fields.description: .remote() vs .remote.aio(), blocking vs non-blocking execution

steps.title: Sync vs Async Overview
steps.explanation: **.remote()** is synchronous—blocks until result. **.remote.aio()** is async—returns awaitable, doesn't block. Choose based on your calling context.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Synchronous"
        S["result = func.remote(x)"]
        SB["Blocks until complete"]
    end
    subgraph "Asynchronous"
        A["result = await func.remote.aio(x)"]
        AB["Non-blocking, awaitable"]
    end
    S --> SB
    A --> AB
    style S fill:#E3F2FD
    style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Synchronous Calls
steps.explanation: **.remote()** is simple: call and wait. Good for scripts, CLI tools, simple workflows. The call blocks until the function completes and returns.
steps.diagram_data:
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Local
    participant M as Modal

    L->>M: func.remote(x)
    Note over L: Waiting...
    M-->>L: result
    Note over L: Continue execution

steps.title: Async Calls
steps.explanation: **.remote.aio()** for async code. Returns immediately with awaitable. Run multiple calls concurrently with `asyncio.gather()`. Much more efficient for parallel work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Async Pattern" C["async def main():"] T[" tasks = [
func.remote.aio(x1),
func.remote.aio(x2),
func.remote.aio(x3)
]"] G[" results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)"] end C --> T --> G style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Parallel Execution
steps.explanation: Async calls start immediately without waiting. With **gather()**, all run in parallel. Total time ≈ slowest call, not sum of all calls.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Sequential (Sync)"
        S1["func1: 2s"] --> S2["func2: 3s"] --> S3["func3: 2s"]
        ST["Total: 7s"]
    end
    subgraph "Parallel (Async)"
        P1["func1: 2s"]
        P2["func2: 3s"]
        P3["func3: 2s"]
        PT["Total: 3s"]
    end
    style ST fill:#FFEBEE
    style PT fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Spawn Pattern
steps.explanation: Use **.spawn()** to fire-and-forget. Returns a handle to check status or get result later. Good for background jobs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Spawn"
        SP["handle = func.spawn(x)"]
        C["# Do other work..."]
        R["result = handle.get()  # When ready"]
    end
    SP --> C --> R
    style SP fill:#E8F5E9
    style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: **Sync**: Simple scripts, one call at a time. **Async**: Multiple concurrent calls, web servers. **Spawn**: Background jobs, fire-and-forget.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph ".remote() (Sync)"
        S["CLI scripts"]
        S2["Simple pipelines"]
        S3["One call at a time"]
    end
    subgraph ".remote.aio() (Async)"
        A["Web servers"]
        A2["Parallel batch jobs"]
        A3["High concurrency"]
    end
    subgraph ".spawn()"
        SP["Background tasks"]
        SP2["Fire and forget"]
        SP3["Long-running jobs"]
    end
    style S fill:#E3F2FD
    style A fill:#E8F5E9
    style SP fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Map & Starmap Parallelism
fields.description: Fan-out patterns for batch processing in Modal

steps.title: Map Overview
steps.explanation: **.map()** runs your function on many inputs in parallel. Each input gets its own container. Results returned in order. The simplest way to parallelize.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Input"
        I["items = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]"]
    end
    subgraph "Map"
        M["results = func.map(items)"]
    end
    subgraph "Containers"
        C1["func(1)"]
        C2["func(2)"]
        C3["func(3)"]
        C4["func(4)"]
        C5["func(5)"]
    end
    I --> M
    M --> C1 & C2 & C3 & C4 & C5
    style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Basic Map Example
steps.explanation: Call `.map()` with an iterable. Returns a generator of results. Wrap in `list()` to get all results. Containers spin up automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" D["@app.function()
def process(x):
return x * 2"] C["items = range(100)"] M["results = list(process.map(items))"] end subgraph "Result" R["[0, 2, 4, 6, ..., 198]"] end D --> C --> M --> R style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Starmap for Multiple Args
steps.explanation: **.starmap()** unpacks arguments. Each item is a tuple of arguments. Use when your function takes multiple parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Function" F["def process(x, y):
return x + y"] end subgraph "Starmap" I["args = [(1, 2), (3, 4), (5, 6)]"] S["results = process.starmap(args)"] end subgraph "Calls" C1["process(1, 2) → 3"] C2["process(3, 4) → 7"] C3["process(5, 6) → 11"] end F --> I --> S S --> C1 & C2 & C3 style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Order Modes
steps.explanation: **order_outputs=True** (default): results in input order. **order_outputs=False**: results as they complete (faster for variable workloads).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "order_outputs=True"
        O1["Input: [A, B, C]"]
        O2["Wait for order"]
        O3["Output: [A_result, B_result, C_result]"]
    end
    subgraph "order_outputs=False"
        U1["Input: [A, B, C]"]
        U2["Return as ready"]
        U3["Output: [C_result, A_result, B_result]"]
    end
    O1 --> O2 --> O3
    U1 --> U2 --> U3
    style O3 fill:#E3F2FD
    style U3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Controlling Parallelism
steps.explanation: Map can spawn many containers. Use **concurrency_limit** on the function to cap parallel containers. Prevents overwhelming downstream services.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without Limit"
        W["10000 items → 10000 containers"]
    end
    subgraph "With concurrency_limit=100"
        L["10000 items → max 100 containers"]
        Q["Excess items queue"]
    end

    F["@app.function(concurrency_limit=100)"]
    F --> L & Q
    style L fill:#E8F5E9
    style Q fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Map with Return Exceptions
steps.explanation: By default, map raises on first error. Use **return_exceptions=True** to continue and collect all results/errors. Handle errors after.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "return_exceptions=False (default)"
        D["First error raises immediately"]
        S["Other results lost"]
    end
    subgraph "return_exceptions=True"
        C["Continue on errors"]
        R["Results include Exception objects"]
        H["Handle errors after"]
    end
    C --> R --> H
    style D fill:#FFEBEE
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Web Endpoints
fields.description: @app.web_endpoint, @app.asgi_app, and FastAPI integration

steps.title: Web Endpoint Types
steps.explanation: Modal supports web endpoints: **@web_endpoint** for simple functions, **@asgi_app** for full ASGI apps (FastAPI, Starlette). Both get auto-generated URLs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Simple Endpoint" W["@app.function()
@modal.web_endpoint()"] end subgraph "Full App" A["@app.function()
@modal.asgi_app()"] end subgraph "Result" U["https://your-app--endpoint.modal.run"] end W --> U A --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Simple Web Endpoint
steps.explanation: **@modal.web_endpoint()** wraps a function as HTTP endpoint. Return dict for JSON, or use Response objects. Quick way to expose a function as API.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Definition" F["@app.function()
@modal.web_endpoint(method='POST')
def predict(data: dict):
result = model.predict(data)
return {'prediction': result}"] end subgraph "Access" C["POST https://...modal.run
Body: {'input': [1,2,3]}"] R["Response: {'prediction': 0.95}"] end F --> C --> R style F fill:#E3F2FD style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: FastAPI Integration
steps.explanation: **@modal.asgi_app()** wraps full ASGI applications. Use FastAPI for complex APIs with validation, docs, middleware. Full framework features available.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "FastAPI App" FA["from fastapi import FastAPI
web_app = FastAPI()

@web_app.post('/predict')
def predict(data: Input):
return model(data)"] end subgraph "Modal Wrapper" M["@app.function()
@modal.asgi_app()
def fastapi_app():
return web_app"] end FA --> M style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: GPU Web Endpoints
steps.explanation: Combine web endpoints with GPU compute. Load model once in class, serve via endpoint. Perfect for ML inference APIs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "GPU-Powered API" C["@app.cls(gpu='A10G')"] E["class Model:"] I[" def __enter__(self):
self.model = load_model()"] W[" @modal.web_endpoint()
def predict(self, x):
return self.model(x)"] end C --> E --> I --> W style C fill:#E3F2FD style W fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Custom Domains
steps.explanation: Modal provides auto-generated URLs. You can also configure custom domains via the dashboard for production deployments.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Auto URL"
        A["your-app--endpoint.modal.run"]
    end
    subgraph "Custom Domain"
        C["api.yourcompany.com"]
        DNS["Configure DNS CNAME"]
    end
    A --> C
    DNS --> C
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Deployment
