steps.explanation: Use **modal deploy** for persistent web endpoints. Unlike `modal run`, deployed apps stay running and get stable URLs for production use.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Development" R["modal run app.py"] RT["Temporary URL
Stops when you exit"] end subgraph "Production" D["modal deploy app.py"] DT["Permanent URL
Runs continuously"] end R --> RT D --> DT style RT fill:#FFF3E0 style DT fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Scheduled Functions
fields.description: @app.function(schedule=...) cron patterns for automated jobs

steps.title: Scheduled Functions Overview
steps.explanation: Modal can run functions on a schedule using **cron** patterns. Perfect for periodic jobs: data syncs, reports, cleanup tasks. No external scheduler needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Schedule Config" S["@app.function(
schedule=modal.Cron('0 9 * * *')
)"] end subgraph "Execution" E["Runs every day at 9 AM UTC"] end S --> E style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Cron Syntax
steps.explanation: Standard cron format: **minute hour day month weekday**. Modal also supports **Period** for simple intervals. Use crontab.guru to build expressions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Cron Format" F["* * * * *
│ │ │ │ └─ Day of week (0-6)
│ │ │ └─── Month (1-12)
│ │ └───── Day of month (1-31)
│ └─────── Hour (0-23)
└───────── Minute (0-59)"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["'0 * * * *' → Every hour"] E2["'0 9 * * 1' → Mon 9 AM"] E3["'*/15 * * * *' → Every 15 min"] end style E1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Period Schedules
steps.explanation: **modal.Period** is simpler for regular intervals. Specify days, hours, minutes. Good for "every N minutes" patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Period Examples" P1["modal.Period(minutes=30)
Every 30 minutes"] P2["modal.Period(hours=2)
Every 2 hours"] P3["modal.Period(days=1)
Daily"] end subgraph "Usage" U["@app.function(
schedule=modal.Period(hours=1)
)"] end P1 --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Scheduled Function Example
steps.explanation: Complete example: daily data sync job. Runs automatically, logs results, handles errors. Deploy once and forget.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Definition" F["@app.function(
schedule=modal.Cron('0 6 * * *'),
secrets=[modal.Secret.from_name('db')]
)"] D["def daily_sync():
data = fetch_data()
process(data)
upload_results()"] end subgraph "Deployment" DEP["modal deploy sync.py"] R["Runs daily at 6 AM UTC"] end F --> D --> DEP --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Monitoring Scheduled Jobs
steps.explanation: View scheduled job history in Modal dashboard. See run times, success/failure, logs. Set up alerts for failures.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Dashboard"
        H["Run history"]
        L["Logs per run"]
        S["Success/failure status"]
        N["Next scheduled run"]
    end
    subgraph "Alerts"
        A["Configure notifications"]
        E["Email on failure"]
    end
    H --> L --> S
    S --> A --> E
    style H fill:#E3F2FD
    style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Timezone Considerations
steps.explanation: Cron times are in **UTC** by default. Plan accordingly for local time requirements. Period schedules are relative to deployment time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "UTC Default"
        U["'0 9 * * *' = 9 AM UTC"]
        L["= 4 AM EST / 1 AM PST"]
    end
    subgraph "Tips"
        T1["Convert local to UTC"]
        T2["Document timezone in code"]
        T3["Test with manual runs first"]
    end
    U --> L
    style T1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style T2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Model Serving Patterns
fields.description: Loading models in __enter__, inference in methods

steps.title: The Pattern
steps.explanation: For ML serving: load model **once** in `__enter__`, run inference **many times** in methods. Model stays loaded across requests on warm containers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Container Lifecycle"
        E["__enter__: Load model (once)"]
        M["method(): Inference (many)"]
        X["__exit__: Cleanup (once)"]
    end
    E -->|"Model in memory"| M
    M -->|"Reuse for requests"| M
    M --> X
    style E fill:#FFF3E0
    style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Basic Structure
steps.explanation: Use **@app.cls()** for stateful serving. `__enter__` runs at container start. Store model on `self`. Methods access `self.model` for inference.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Class Structure" C["@app.cls(gpu='A10G')"] D["class Predictor:"] E[" def __enter__(self):
self.model = load_model()
return self"] M[" @modal.method()
def predict(self, x):
return self.model(x)"] end C --> D --> E --> M style E fill:#FFF3E0 style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Why This Pattern?
steps.explanation: Model loading is slow (seconds-minutes). Inference is fast (milliseconds). Loading once and inferring many times maximizes throughput on warm containers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Without Pattern"
        B1["Load: 10s"]
        B2["Infer: 50ms"]
        B3["Load: 10s"]
        B4["Infer: 50ms"]
        BT["20.1s for 2 requests"]
    end
    subgraph "With Pattern"
        G1["Load: 10s (once)"]
        G2["Infer: 50ms"]
        G3["Infer: 50ms"]
        GT["10.1s for 2 requests"]
    end
    style BT fill:#FFEBEE
    style GT fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Caching Model Weights
steps.explanation: Combine with **Volumes** to cache downloaded weights. First container downloads, subsequent containers load from cache. Even faster cold starts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "__enter__ with Cache"
        C["Check Volume for model"]
        D{"Found?"}
        D -->|No| DL["Download model"]
        DL --> S["Save to Volume"]
        D -->|Yes| L["Load from Volume"]
        S --> L
        L --> R["self.model = loaded"]
    end
    style L fill:#E8F5E9
    style S fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Web Endpoint Serving
steps.explanation: Combine class pattern with web endpoints. Model loads once, serves HTTP requests. Full ML API with one decorator.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Full Pattern" C["@app.cls(gpu='A10G', keep_warm=1)"] CL["class API:"] E[" def __enter__(self): ..."] W[" @modal.web_endpoint(method='POST')
def predict(self, data: dict):
return self.model(data['input'])"] end C --> CL --> E --> W subgraph "Result" U["https://...modal.run
POST with JSON
GPU inference"] end W --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Production-ready pattern: class with cached weights, GPU, keep_warm for latency, web endpoint for API access.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Definition" D["vol = modal.Volume.from_name('models')

@app.cls(
gpu='A10G',
volumes={'/cache': vol},
keep_warm=2
)
class LLM:
def __enter__(self):
self.model = load('/cache/model')

@modal.web_endpoint()
def generate(self, prompt):"] end subgraph "Deploy" DEP["modal deploy → Production API"] end D --> DEP style DEP fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Batch Inference Pipeline
fields.description: Combining volumes, GPUs, and map for large-scale inference

steps.title: Batch Inference Overview
steps.explanation: Process thousands of items with **map()**. Each item gets its own GPU container. Modal handles scheduling, scaling, and result collection.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Input"
        I["1000s of images/texts"]
    end
    subgraph "Modal Map"
        M["process.map(items)"]
    end
    subgraph "GPU Containers"
        G1["GPU 1"]
        G2["GPU 2"]
        G3["GPU N"]
    end
    subgraph "Output"
        O["All results collected"]
    end
    I --> M --> G1 & G2 & G3 --> O
    style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pipeline Architecture
steps.explanation: Typical batch pipeline: read data → fan out to GPUs → collect results → write output. Use Volumes for data storage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Stage 1" R["Read from Volume
or external source"] end subgraph "Stage 2" P["process.map(items)
GPU inference"] end subgraph "Stage 3" W["Write results
to Volume"] end R --> P --> W style P fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Processing Function
steps.explanation: Each map invocation processes one item. Load model in `__enter__` (or use cached weights). Return result for collection.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Batch Processor" C["@app.cls(gpu='A10G')"] CL["class Processor:"] E[" def __enter__(self):
self.model = load_model()"] M[" @modal.method()
def process_item(self, item):
return self.model(item)"] end C --> CL --> E --> M style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Orchestration
steps.explanation: Local entrypoint orchestrates: load items, call map, collect results, save. Handles errors with return_exceptions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Orchestrator" E["@app.local_entrypoint()"] M["def main():"] L[" items = load_items()"] P[" processor = Processor()"] R[" results = list(processor.process_item.map(
items,
return_exceptions=True
))"] S[" save_results(results)"] end E --> M --> L --> P --> R --> S style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Optimizations
steps.explanation: **Batching within containers**: process multiple items per invocation. **Chunking**: reduce map overhead. **Streaming**: yield results as ready.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Item-per-call"
        I1["map(1000 items)"]
        I2["1000 function calls"]
    end
    subgraph "Batch-per-call"
        B1["map(chunks of 10)"]
        B2["100 function calls"]
        B3["Each processes batch"]
    end
    style I2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style B3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Complete Pipeline
steps.explanation: Production batch pipeline: cached model, chunked inputs, error handling, results to Volume. Scales to millions of items.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Setup"
        V["vol = modal.Volume.from_name('data')"]
        I["image with model cached"]
    end
    subgraph "Process"
        CL["@app.cls(gpu, volumes, image)"]
        PR["Batch processor class"]
    end
    subgraph "Orchestrate"
        OR["Local entrypoint"]
        CH["Chunk inputs"]
        MP["Map over chunks"]
        SV["Save to Volume"]
    end
    V --> I --> CL --> PR
    OR --> CH --> MP --> SV
    style MP fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Fine-Tuning Workflow
fields.description: Data loading → training → checkpointing → serving on Modal

steps.title: Fine-Tuning Overview
steps.explanation: Fine-tune models on Modal: upload data to Volume, train on GPUs, checkpoint progress, serve the result. All serverless, pay for what you use.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Data"
        D["Upload to Volume"]
    end
    subgraph "Train"
        T["GPU Training"]
    end
    subgraph "Save"
        S["Checkpoint to Volume"]
    end
    subgraph "Serve"
        SV["Deploy API"]
    end
    D --> T --> S --> SV
    style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Data Preparation
steps.explanation: Upload training data to a Volume. Accessible from any function. Can also stream from external sources (S3, HuggingFace).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Local" L["Training data"] end subgraph "Upload Function" U["@app.function(volumes={'/data': vol})
def upload_data():
# Copy files to /data
vol.commit()"] end subgraph "Volume" V["/data/train.jsonl
/data/val.jsonl"] end L --> U --> V style V fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Training Function
steps.explanation: Training runs on GPU. Load data from Volume, run training loop, save checkpoints back to Volume. Use appropriate GPU (A100 for large models).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Training Setup" F["@app.function(
gpu='A100',
volumes={
'/data': data_vol,
'/checkpoints': ckpt_vol
},
timeout=3600
)"] end subgraph "Training Loop" L["def train():
model = load_base_model()
data = load('/data/train.jsonl')
for epoch in range(N):
train_epoch(model, data)
save('/checkpoints/epoch_N')"] end F --> L style L fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Checkpointing
steps.explanation: Save checkpoints to Volume during training. Enables resume on failure, keeps best models. Use vol.commit() to ensure persistence.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Save Checkpoint"
        S["torch.save(model, '/ckpt/model.pt')"]
        C["ckpt_vol.commit()"]
    end
    subgraph "Resume"
        R["model = torch.load('/ckpt/model.pt')"]
        T["Continue training"]
    end
    S --> C
    R --> T
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Multi-GPU Training
steps.explanation: For large models, use multiple GPUs. Set gpu count, use distributed training frameworks (PyTorch DDP, DeepSpeed, Accelerate).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multi-GPU Config" F["@app.function(
gpu=modal.gpu.A100(count=4)
)"] end subgraph "Distributed Code" D["from accelerate import Accelerator
accelerator = Accelerator()
model = accelerator.prepare(model)"] end F --> D style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Serving Fine-Tuned Model
steps.explanation: After training, serve the model. Load from checkpoint Volume, deploy as API. Same pattern as model serving.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Serving Class" C["@app.cls(
gpu='A10G',
volumes={'/model': ckpt_vol}
)"] CL["class FineTunedModel:"] E[" def __enter__(self):
self.model = load('/model/best.pt')"] W[" @modal.web_endpoint()
def predict(self, x): ..."] end C --> CL --> E --> W subgraph "Deploy" D["modal deploy → Production API"] end W --> D style D fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Anti-Join & Semi-Join
fields.description: Left anti, left semi joins for filtering with joins in PySpark

steps.title: Anti-Join vs Semi-Join
steps.explanation: **Anti-join**: Returns rows from left that have NO match in right. **Semi-join**: Returns rows from left that HAVE a match in right. Both return only left columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Left Anti Join"
        LA["Keep rows with NO match"]
        LAR["Returns: left columns only"]
    end
    subgraph "Left Semi Join"
        LS["Keep rows WITH match"]
        LSR["Returns: left columns only"]
    end
    style LA fill:#FFEBEE
    style LS fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Visual Comparison
steps.explanation: Think of it as filtering: semi-join is like `WHERE EXISTS`, anti-join is like `WHERE NOT EXISTS`. Neither returns columns from the right table.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Left Table"
        L1["Alice"]
        L2["Bob"]
        L3["Carol"]
    end
    subgraph "Right Table"
        R1["Alice"]
        R2["Carol"]
    end
    subgraph "Left Semi"
        S1["Alice ✓"]
        S2["Carol ✓"]
    end
    subgraph "Left Anti"
        A1["Bob ✓"]
    end
    L1 --> S1
    L3 --> S2
    L2 --> A1
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Left Anti Join Syntax
steps.explanation: Use `join(other, on, 'left_anti')` to find rows in left that don't exist in right. Perfect for finding missing records, orphans, or exclusions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["# Find customers who never ordered
customers.join(
orders,
on='customer_id',
how='left_anti'
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["Customers with no orders"] end C --> R style R fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Left Semi Join Syntax
steps.explanation: Use `join(other, on, 'left_semi')` to filter left table by existence in right. Like an efficient IN clause without duplicating rows.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["# Find customers who have ordered
customers.join(
orders,
on='customer_id',
how='left_semi'
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["Customers with at least one order
(no duplicates!)"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Semi vs Inner Join
steps.explanation: **Inner join** can duplicate left rows if multiple matches exist. **Semi join** returns each left row at most once, regardless of how many matches.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Data"
        L["Customer: Alice"]
        R["Orders: 3 orders by Alice"]
    end
    subgraph "Inner Join"
        I["Alice (× 3 rows!)"]
    end
    subgraph "Semi Join"
        S["Alice (× 1 row)"]
    end
    L --> I
    L --> S
    style I fill:#FFEBEE
    style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Use Cases
steps.explanation: **Anti-join**: Find missing data, data quality checks, exclusion lists. **Semi-join**: Filter by existence, validate relationships, efficient IN subqueries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Anti-Join Use Cases"
        A1["Find orphan records"]
        A2["Data quality: missing FKs"]
        A3["Exclude blacklisted items"]
        A4["Find new records (not in history)"]
    end
    subgraph "Semi-Join Use Cases"
        S1["Filter by whitelist"]
        S2["Active users (have activity)"]
        S3["Products in stock"]
        S4["Efficient EXISTS check"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Performance Tips
steps.explanation: Anti/semi joins are often more efficient than alternatives. Spark optimizes them well. Avoid NOT IN with nulls (use anti-join instead).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Avoid"
        B1["df.filter(~col('id').isin(list))"]
        B2["NOT IN with subquery"]
        B3["Left join + filter nulls"]
    end
    subgraph "Prefer"
        G1["left_anti join"]
        G2["Optimized by Catalyst"]
        G3["Handles nulls correctly"]
    end
    B1 --> G1
    B2 --> G1
    B3 --> G1
    style B1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Join Types Comparison
fields.description: Inner, left, right, full outer joins with visual examples

steps.title: Join Types Overview
steps.explanation: PySpark supports all standard SQL join types. Each determines which rows are included when combining two DataFrames based on a condition.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Join Types"
        I["inner - matching rows only"]
        L["left/left_outer - all left + matching right"]
        R["right/right_outer - all right + matching left"]
        F["full/full_outer - all rows from both"]
        C["cross - cartesian product"]
    end
    style I fill:#E8F5E9
    style L fill:#E3F2FD
    style R fill:#FFF3E0
    style F fill:#F3E5F5

steps.title: Sample Data
steps.explanation: Let's use two simple tables: **Employees** (id, name) and **Departments** (id, dept). Some employees have no department, some departments have no employees.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Employees"
        E1["1, Alice"]
        E2["2, Bob"]
        E3["3, Carol"]
    end
    subgraph "Departments"
        D1["1, Engineering"]
        D2["3, Sales"]
        D3["4, Marketing"]
    end

steps.title: Inner Join
steps.explanation: **Inner join** returns only rows where the key exists in BOTH tables. Rows without matches are excluded entirely.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Inner Join Result"
        R1["1, Alice, Engineering"]
        R2["3, Carol, Sales"]
    end
    subgraph "Excluded"
        X1["Bob (no dept)"]
        X2["Marketing (no emp)"]
    end
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style X1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style X2 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Left Outer Join
steps.explanation: **Left join** keeps ALL rows from left table. Matching right columns filled in, non-matching get NULL. Never loses left rows.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Left Join Result" R1["1, Alice, Engineering"] R2["2, Bob, NULL"] R3["3, Carol, Sales"] end subgraph "Note" N["All employees kept
Marketing excluded"] end style R1 fill:#E8F5E9 style R2 fill:#FFF3E0 style R3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Right Outer Join
steps.explanation: **Right join** keeps ALL rows from right table. Matching left columns filled in, non-matching get NULL. Mirror of left join.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Right Join Result" R1["1, Alice, Engineering"] R2["3, Carol, Sales"] R3["NULL, NULL, Marketing"] end subgraph "Note" N["All departments kept
Bob excluded"] end style R1 fill:#E8F5E9 style R2 fill:#E8F5E9 style R3 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Full Outer Join
steps.explanation: **Full outer join** keeps ALL rows from BOTH tables. NULLs fill in where no match exists. Most inclusive join type.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Full Outer Result" R1["1, Alice, Engineering"] R2["2, Bob, NULL"] R3["3, Carol, Sales"] R4["NULL, NULL, Marketing"] end subgraph "Note" N["Everyone included
NULLs where no match"] end style R1 fill:#E8F5E9 style R2 fill:#FFF3E0 style R3 fill:#E8F5E9 style R4 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Syntax Examples
steps.explanation: Use the `how` parameter to specify join type. Default is inner. Can also use SQL-style syntax with `spark.sql()`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "DataFrame API" D1["df1.join(df2, 'id', 'inner')"] D2["df1.join(df2, 'id', 'left')"] D3["df1.join(df2, 'id', 'right')"] D4["df1.join(df2, 'id', 'full')"] end subgraph "SQL" S1["SELECT * FROM t1
INNER JOIN t2 ON ..."] end style D1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Broadcast vs Shuffle Join
fields.description: When to use broadcast joins for performance optimization

steps.title: Two Join Strategies
steps.explanation: Spark has two main join strategies: **Shuffle join** (default for large tables) and **Broadcast join** (for small + large table). Choice dramatically affects performance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Shuffle Join"
        S["Both tables shuffled"]
        SS["Data moves across network"]
    end
    subgraph "Broadcast Join"
        B["Small table broadcast to all"]
        BS["No shuffle needed"]
    end
    style S fill:#FFEBEE
    style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Shuffle Join (Sort-Merge)
steps.explanation: Default for large tables. Both DataFrames shuffled by join key so matching keys land on same partition. Expensive network I/O.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Table A (100GB)" A1["Partition 1"] A2["Partition 2"] A3["Partition 3"] end subgraph "Table B (50GB)" B1["Partition 1"] B2["Partition 2"] end subgraph "Shuffle" SH["Network transfer
Sort by key
Merge matching"] end A1 & A2 & A3 --> SH B1 & B2 --> SH style SH fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Broadcast Join
steps.explanation: Small table sent to all executors, kept in memory. Large table stays in place—no shuffle! Much faster when one table is small.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Small Table (100MB)"
        S["Entire table"]
    end
    subgraph "Broadcast"
        BC["Copy to all executors"]
    end
    subgraph "Executors"
        E1["Executor 1: Large + Small"]
        E2["Executor 2: Large + Small"]
        E3["Executor 3: Large + Small"]
    end
    S --> BC --> E1 & E2 & E3
    style BC fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Auto Broadcast Threshold
steps.explanation: Spark auto-broadcasts tables smaller than **spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold** (default 10MB). Set to -1 to disable auto-broadcast.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Auto Decision" C{"Table size < 10MB?"} C -->|Yes| B["Broadcast join"] C -->|No| S["Shuffle join"] end subgraph "Config" CF["spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold
Default: 10485760 (10MB)"] end style B fill:#E8F5E9 style S fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Explicit Broadcast Hint
steps.explanation: Use **broadcast()** function to force broadcast join even for larger tables (if they fit in memory). Override auto-threshold.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

large_df.join(
broadcast(small_df),
'key'
)"] end subgraph "Effect" E["Forces broadcast
regardless of size"] end C --> E style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use Each
steps.explanation: **Broadcast**: One table fits in executor memory (< 1-2GB typically). **Shuffle**: Both tables large. Wrong choice = OOM or slow performance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use Broadcast"
        B1["Dimension tables"]
        B2["Lookup tables"]
        B3["Small aggregates"]
        B4["< 1GB typically safe"]
    end
    subgraph "Use Shuffle"
        S1["Both tables huge"]
        S2["Small table > memory"]
        S3["Many-to-many joins"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S1 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Check Execution Plan
steps.explanation: Use `explain()` to verify join strategy. Look for **BroadcastHashJoin** vs **SortMergeJoin** in the plan.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Explain Output"
        E["df.explain(True)"]
    end
    subgraph "Look For"
        B["BroadcastHashJoin ✓"]
        S["SortMergeJoin"]
        SH["ShuffledHashJoin"]
    end
    E --> B & S & SH
    style B fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Join Strategies & Hints
fields.description: Sort-merge, shuffle hash, broadcast hash joins and how to control them

steps.title: Join Strategy Overview
steps.explanation: Spark chooses join strategy based on table sizes, data properties, and hints. Three main strategies: **Broadcast Hash**, **Shuffle Hash**, and **Sort-Merge**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Strategies" BH["Broadcast Hash Join
Small + Large"] SH["Shuffle Hash Join
Medium tables"] SM["Sort-Merge Join
Large + Large"] end BH --> SH --> SM style BH fill:#E8F5E9 style SM fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Broadcast Hash Join
steps.explanation: Fastest strategy. Small table broadcast to all nodes, hash table built in memory. O(n) for large table scan. Best for small dimension tables.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Small Table" S["Build hash table"] end subgraph "Broadcast" BC["Send to all executors"] end subgraph "Large Table" L["Probe hash table
per partition"] end S --> BC --> L style BC fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Shuffle Hash Join
steps.explanation: Both tables shuffled by key, then hash join per partition. No sorting needed. Good when one side fits in partition memory.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Table A" A["Shuffle by key"] end subgraph "Table B" B["Shuffle by key"] end subgraph "Per Partition" P["Build hash table
Probe other side"] end A --> P B --> P style P fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Sort-Merge Join
steps.explanation: Default for large tables. Both sides shuffled and sorted by key. Merge sorted streams. Handles huge tables but requires sorting.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Table A" AS["Shuffle → Sort by key"] end subgraph "Table B" BS["Shuffle → Sort by key"] end subgraph "Merge" M["Walk through sorted streams
Match keys in order"] end AS --> M BS --> M style M fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Join Hints
steps.explanation: Use hints to override Spark's choice. Available hints: **BROADCAST**, **MERGE**, **SHUFFLE_HASH**, **SHUFFLE_REPLICATE_NL**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Hint Syntax" H1["df1.hint('broadcast').join(df2)"] H2["df1.join(df2.hint('merge'))"] end subgraph "SQL Syntax" S["SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(t1) */
FROM t1 JOIN t2"] end style H1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Strategy Selection Logic
steps.explanation: Spark picks strategy based on: table sizes, broadcast threshold, available memory, and any hints provided. Hints override defaults.

steps.title: Performance Comparison
steps.explanation: Choose wisely based on data characteristics. Wrong choice can be 10-100x slower. Always check explain() plan.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Speed (typical)"
        S1["Broadcast Hash: Fastest"]
        S2["Shuffle Hash: Fast"]
        S3["Sort-Merge: Slower but reliable"]
    end
    subgraph "Memory"
        M1["Broadcast: Must fit in memory"]
        M2["Shuffle Hash: Per-partition fit"]
        M3["Sort-Merge: Spills to disk OK"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S3 fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: Window Functions Anatomy
fields.description: OVER, PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frame specification in PySpark

steps.title: What Are Window Functions?
steps.explanation: Window functions compute values across a set of rows **related to the current row**. Unlike GROUP BY, they don't collapse rows—each row keeps its identity.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "GROUP BY"
        G1["Collapses rows"]
        G2["One output per group"]
    end
    subgraph "Window Function"
        W1["Keeps all rows"]
        W2["Adds computed column"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style W1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Window Specification
steps.explanation: A window has three parts: **PARTITION BY** (groups), **ORDER BY** (row order within partition), and **Frame** (which rows to include).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Window Spec" P["PARTITION BY dept"] O["ORDER BY salary DESC"] F["ROWS BETWEEN ..."] end subgraph "Result" R["Window per department
Ordered by salary
Frame defines scope"] end P --> O --> F --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Creating a Window
steps.explanation: Use `Window` class to define window specification. Chain `partitionBy()`, `orderBy()`, and frame methods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["from pyspark.sql.window import Window

w = Window\
.partitionBy('dept')\
.orderBy('salary')"] end subgraph "Usage" U["df.withColumn(
'rank',
rank().over(w)
)"] end C --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Frame Types
steps.explanation: Frame defines which rows relative to current row. **ROWS**: physical offset. **RANGE**: logical value range. Default depends on ORDER BY presence.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Row Frame"
        R1["ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING"]
        R2["AND CURRENT ROW"]
        R3["Exactly 3 rows"]
    end
    subgraph "Range Frame"
        RA1["RANGE BETWEEN 100 PRECEDING"]
        RA2["AND CURRENT ROW"]
        RA3["All within value range"]
    end
    style R1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style RA1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Frame Boundaries
steps.explanation: Boundaries can be: **UNBOUNDED PRECEDING** (start), **N PRECEDING**, **CURRENT ROW**, **N FOLLOWING**, **UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING** (end).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Frame Example" U1["UNBOUNDED
PRECEDING"] P2["2 PRECEDING"] P1["1 PRECEDING"] CR["CURRENT
ROW"] F1["1 FOLLOWING"] UF["UNBOUNDED
FOLLOWING"] end U1 --> P2 --> P1 --> CR --> F1 --> UF style CR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Common window specs: running total (unbounded to current), moving average (N preceding to current), entire partition (unbounded to unbounded).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Running Total" RT[".rowsBetween(
Window.unboundedPreceding,
Window.currentRow
)"] end subgraph "Moving Average (3)" MA[".rowsBetween(-2, 0)"] end subgraph "Entire Partition" EP[".rowsBetween(
Window.unboundedPreceding,
Window.unboundedFollowing
)"] end style RT fill:#E8F5E9 style MA fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Example: Running Sum
steps.explanation: Calculate running sum of sales by department, ordered by date. Each row shows cumulative total up to that point.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["w = Window\
.partitionBy('dept')\
.orderBy('date')\
.rowsBetween(
Window.unboundedPreceding,
Window.currentRow
)

df.withColumn('running_total',
sum('sales').over(w))"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Ranking & Analytics Functions
fields.description: row_number, rank, dense_rank, lead, lag, and running totals

steps.title: Ranking Functions
steps.explanation: Three ranking functions with different tie-handling: **row_number** (unique), **rank** (gaps after ties), **dense_rank** (no gaps).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Scores: 100, 90, 90, 80"
        RN["row_number: 1, 2, 3, 4"]
        R["rank: 1, 2, 2, 4"]
        DR["dense_rank: 1, 2, 2, 3"]
    end
    style RN fill:#E3F2FD
    style R fill:#E8F5E9
    style DR fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: row_number()
steps.explanation: Assigns unique sequential numbers. Ties get arbitrary order (unless ORDER BY is deterministic). Great for deduplication.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Use Case: Keep Latest" C["w = Window.partitionBy('user_id')\
.orderBy(desc('timestamp'))

df.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))\
.filter(col('rn') == 1)"] end subgraph "Result" R["One row per user
(most recent)"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: rank() vs dense_rank()
steps.explanation: **rank()**: Ties share rank, next rank skips. **dense_rank()**: Ties share rank, next rank is consecutive. Choose based on needs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Data: 100, 90, 90, 80" D["Four scores with tie"] end subgraph "rank()" R["1, 2, 2, 4
Skips 3"] end subgraph "dense_rank()" DR["1, 2, 2, 3
No gaps"] end D --> R & DR style R fill:#E3F2FD style DR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: lead() and lag()
steps.explanation: **lag()**: Access previous row's value. **lead()**: Access next row's value. Great for comparing consecutive records.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "lag(col, n)"
        L["Value from n rows BEFORE"]
        LE["lag('price', 1) → previous price"]
    end
    subgraph "lead(col, n)"
        LD["Value from n rows AFTER"]
        LDE["lead('price', 1) → next price"]
    end
    style L fill:#E3F2FD
    style LD fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: lead/lag Example
steps.explanation: Calculate day-over-day change. Use lag() to get previous day's value, then compute difference.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["w = Window.partitionBy('stock')\
.orderBy('date')

df.withColumn('prev_price',
lag('price', 1).over(w))\
.withColumn('change',
col('price') - col('prev_price'))"] end subgraph "Result" R["date | price | prev | change
D1 | 100 | null | null
D2 | 105 | 100 | 5
D3 | 102 | 105 | -3"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Aggregate Window Functions
steps.explanation: Standard aggregates work as window functions: **sum**, **avg**, **min**, **max**, **count**. Add running/moving calculations without GROUP BY.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Running Aggregates"
        S["sum('amount').over(w)"]
        A["avg('amount').over(w)"]
        C["count('*').over(w)"]
    end
    subgraph "With Frame"
        F["Moving avg: .rowsBetween(-6, 0)"]
    end
    style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ntile() and percent_rank()
steps.explanation: **ntile(n)**: Divide into n buckets (quartiles, deciles). **percent_rank()**: Relative rank as percentage (0 to 1).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "ntile(4)" N["Divide into quartiles
1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4"] end subgraph "percent_rank()" PR["0.0 = first
0.5 = middle
1.0 = last"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U["ntile: Bucketing customers"] U2["percent_rank: Percentiles"] end N --> U PR --> U2 style N fill:#E8F5E9 style PR fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: GroupBy & Aggregations
fields.description: groupBy vs agg, multiple aggregations, and pivot operations

steps.title: GroupBy Basics
steps.explanation: **groupBy()** partitions data by key columns. Follow with aggregation functions to compute summaries per group. Similar to SQL GROUP BY.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Input" I["dept | salary
Eng | 100
Eng | 120
Sales| 90"] end subgraph "GroupBy" G["groupBy('dept')"] end subgraph "Aggregate" A["sum('salary')"] end subgraph "Output" O["Eng: 220
Sales: 90"] end I --> G --> A --> O style O fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Simple Aggregations
steps.explanation: Chain aggregation methods directly: **count()**, **sum()**, **avg()**, **min()**, **max()**. Quick for single aggregations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Direct Methods"
        C["df.groupBy('dept').count()"]
        S["df.groupBy('dept').sum('salary')"]
        A["df.groupBy('dept').avg('salary')"]
        M["df.groupBy('dept').max('salary')"]
    end
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Multiple Aggregations with agg()
steps.explanation: Use **agg()** for multiple aggregations at once. Pass column expressions with aggregate functions. More flexible and efficient.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["from pyspark.sql import functions as F

df.groupBy('dept').agg(
F.count('*').alias('count'),
F.sum('salary').alias('total'),
F.avg('salary').alias('avg_sal'),
F.max('salary').alias('max_sal')
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["dept | count | total | avg | max"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Aggregation Functions
steps.explanation: PySpark provides many aggregation functions: statistical, collection, and conditional aggregates.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Statistical"
        S1["sum, avg, mean"]
        S2["min, max"]
        S3["stddev, variance"]
        S4["percentile_approx"]
    end
    subgraph "Collection"
        C1["count, countDistinct"]
        C2["collect_list, collect_set"]
        C3["first, last"]
    end
    subgraph "Conditional"
        CD["sum(when(...))"]
        CD2["count(when(...))"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C1 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Grouping by Multiple Columns
steps.explanation: Pass multiple columns to groupBy for hierarchical grouping. Results in one row per unique combination of group keys.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["df.groupBy('dept', 'year').agg(
F.sum('sales').alias('total')
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["dept | year | total
Eng | 2023 | 500K
Eng | 2024 | 600K
Sales | 2023 | 300K"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pivot Tables
steps.explanation: **pivot()** rotates rows into columns. Group → Pivot → Aggregate. Creates wide tables from long data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Long Format" L["dept | qtr | sales
Eng | Q1 | 100
Eng | Q2 | 120
Sales| Q1 | 80"] end subgraph "Pivot Code" P["df.groupBy('dept')\
.pivot('qtr')\
.sum('sales')"] end subgraph "Wide Format" W["dept | Q1 | Q2
Eng | 100 | 120
Sales | 80 | null"] end L --> P --> W style W fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Performance Tips
steps.explanation: GroupBy triggers shuffle. Reduce data before grouping when possible. Use approximate functions for large datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Expensive"
        E1["countDistinct on huge data"]
        E2["collect_list (memory)"]
    end
    subgraph "Alternatives"
        A1["approx_count_distinct"]
        A2["Limit collect size"]
        A3["Filter before groupBy"]
    end
    E1 --> A1
    E2 --> A2
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Explode & Flatten
fields.description: Working with arrays, maps, and nested structures

steps.title: Why Explode?
steps.explanation: **explode()** converts array/map elements into separate rows. Essential for working with nested JSON, repeated fields, and denormalizing data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before" B["id | tags
1 | [a, b, c]"] end subgraph "explode" E["explode('tags')"] end subgraph "After" A["id | tag
1 | a
1 | b
1 | c"] end B --> E --> A style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: explode() Function
steps.explanation: Basic explode creates one row per array element. Original row duplicated for each element. Array column replaced with single values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["from pyspark.sql.functions import explode

df.select(
'id',
explode('items').alias('item')
)"] end subgraph "Note" N["Empty arrays → row dropped
Null arrays → row dropped"] end C --> N style N fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: explode_outer()
steps.explanation: **explode_outer()** preserves rows with empty/null arrays. Exploded column becomes NULL. Use when you don't want to lose rows.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "explode"
        E1["[a,b] → a, b"]
        E2["[] → (dropped)"]
        E3["null → (dropped)"]
    end
    subgraph "explode_outer"
        O1["[a,b] → a, b"]
        O2["[] → null"]
        O3["null → null"]
    end
    style E2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style O2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: posexplode()
steps.explanation: **posexplode()** also returns the position (index) of each element. Useful when order matters or you need element indices.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["df.select(
'id',
posexplode('items').alias('pos', 'item')
)"] end subgraph "Result" R["id | pos | item
1 | 0 | a
1 | 1 | b
1 | 2 | c"] end C --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Exploding Maps
steps.explanation: Explode works on maps too. Returns key-value pairs as separate columns. Use for JSON objects stored as MapType.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Map Data" M["id | props
1 | {a:1, b:2}"] end subgraph "Explode" E["explode('props')"] end subgraph "Result" R["id | key | value
1 | a | 1
1 | b | 2"] end M --> E --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Flatten Nested Structs
steps.explanation: For nested structs, use **dot notation** to access fields. Or **select with star** to flatten. No explode needed for structs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Nested Struct" N["id | address.city | address.zip"] end subgraph "Access" A1["df.select('address.city')"] A2["df.select('address.*')"] end subgraph "Flatten All" F["df.select('id',
col('address.city').alias('city'),
col('address.zip').alias('zip')
)"] end N --> A1 & A2 & F style F fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Multiple Explosions
steps.explanation: Exploding multiple arrays creates cartesian product. Usually explode one at a time with separate selects. Or use **arrays_zip()** first.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problem" P["Two arrays: [a,b] and [1,2]
Double explode = 4 rows"] end subgraph "Solution: arrays_zip" Z["arrays_zip(arr1, arr2)"] E["Then explode once"] R["Paired: (a,1), (b,2)"] end P --> Z --> E --> R style R fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: UDFs vs Built-in Functions
fields.description: Performance comparison and when to use each approach

steps.title: Built-in vs UDF
steps.explanation: **Built-in functions** run in JVM, optimized by Catalyst. **UDFs** run in Python, require serialization. Built-ins are 10-100x faster!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Built-in Functions"
        B1["Run in JVM"]
        B2["Catalyst optimized"]
        B3["Vectorized execution"]
    end
    subgraph "Python UDFs"
        U1["Run in Python"]
        U2["Serialize data to Python"]
        U3["Row-by-row execution"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style U1 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Built-in Functions
steps.explanation: PySpark has 200+ built-in functions. Check `pyspark.sql.functions` before writing UDF. Most common operations are covered.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "String"
        S["upper, lower, trim"]
        S2["concat, substring"]
        S3["regexp_extract, split"]
    end
    subgraph "Date/Time"
        D["date_add, datediff"]
        D2["year, month, dayofweek"]
        D3["to_timestamp, date_format"]
    end
    subgraph "Math"
        M["round, abs, sqrt"]
        M2["log, exp, pow"]
    end
    style S fill:#E8F5E9
    style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Creating a UDF
steps.explanation: When built-ins aren't enough, define a UDF. Specify return type. Register with @udf decorator or udf() function.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Decorator Style" D["from pyspark.sql.functions import udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

@udf(StringType())
def my_func(x):
return x.upper()[:3]"] end subgraph "Function Style" F["my_udf = udf(my_func, StringType())

df.withColumn('new', my_udf('col'))"] end style D fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: UDF Performance Problem
steps.explanation: Each row serialized Python ↔ JVM. Breaks Catalyst optimization. Processes row-by-row instead of vectorized. Can be 100x slower!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "JVM"
        J["DataFrame rows"]
    end
    subgraph "Serialize"
        S["Row → Python object"]
    end
    subgraph "Python"
        P["Run UDF"]
    end
    subgraph "Deserialize"
        D["Result → JVM"]
    end
    J --> S --> P --> D --> J
    style S fill:#FFEBEE
    style D fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Pandas UDFs (Vectorized)
steps.explanation: **Pandas UDFs** process batches using Arrow, much faster than regular UDFs. Use @pandas_udf decorator. Best when you need custom logic.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pandas UDF" C["from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf

@pandas_udf(DoubleType())
def normalize(s: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
return (s - s.mean()) / s.std()"] end subgraph "Benefit" B["Batched processing"] B2["Arrow serialization"] B3["10-100x faster than UDF"] end C --> B & B2 & B3 style B3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use What
steps.explanation: Decision tree: built-in first, then Pandas UDF, regular UDF only as last resort. SQL expressions also optimized.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{"Built-in exists?"} Q1 -->|Yes| B["Use built-in ✓"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Can combine built-ins?"} Q2 -->|Yes| C["Chain built-ins ✓"] Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Vectorizable?"} Q3 -->|Yes| P["Pandas UDF"] Q3 -->|No| U["Regular UDF
(last resort)"] style B fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#E8F5E9 style P fill:#E3F2FD style U fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Optimization Tips
steps.explanation: If UDF needed: cache inputs, use broadcast for lookups, consider Scala UDF. Always profile and compare alternatives.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Optimize UDFs"
        O1["Cache repeated UDF inputs"]
        O2["Broadcast lookup tables"]
        O3["Filter before UDF"]
        O4["Consider Scala UDF"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["UDF in filter (after!)"]
        A2["UDF on huge columns"]
        A3["Nested UDF calls"]
    end
    style O1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#FFEBEE

fields.title: Catalyst Optimizer
fields.description: Logical plan → physical plan → code generation pipeline

steps.title: What is Catalyst?
steps.explanation: **Catalyst** is Spark's query optimizer. Transforms your DataFrame operations into optimized execution plans. Works automatically—you benefit without knowing it.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Your Code"
        C["df.filter().join().select()"]
    end
    subgraph "Catalyst"
        CA["Optimizer"]
    end
    subgraph "Execution"
        E["Optimized physical plan"]
    end
    C --> CA --> E
    style CA fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Optimization Pipeline
steps.explanation: Four phases: **Analysis** (resolve names), **Logical Optimization** (rule-based), **Physical Planning** (choose algorithms), **Code Generation** (generate JVM bytecode).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Phase 1" A["Analysis
Resolve names, types"] end subgraph "Phase 2" L["Logical Optimization
Apply rules"] end subgraph "Phase 3" P["Physical Planning
Choose algorithms"] end subgraph "Phase 4" CG["Code Generation
Whole-stage codegen"] end A --> L --> P --> CG style L fill:#E8F5E9 style CG fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Logical Optimizations
steps.explanation: Rule-based transformations: **predicate pushdown**, **projection pruning**, **constant folding**, **filter reordering**. Applied automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Key Rules" PP["Predicate Pushdown
Move filters early"] PR["Projection Pruning
Only read needed columns"] CF["Constant Folding
Compute constants at compile"] CO["Combine Operators
Merge adjacent ops"] end style PP fill:#E8F5E9 style PR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Predicate Pushdown
steps.explanation: Filters pushed down to data source. Read less data from disk. Works with Parquet, JDBC, Delta Lake. Huge performance gain.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before Optimization"
        R1["Read all data"]
        F1["Then filter"]
    end
    subgraph "After Optimization"
        F2["Filter pushed to read"]
        R2["Read only matching"]
    end
    R1 --> F1
    F2 --> R2
    style R1 fill:#FFEBEE
    style R2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Physical Planning
steps.explanation: Chooses algorithms: broadcast vs shuffle join, hash vs sort aggregate. Uses statistics (table sizes, cardinality) when available.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Join Decision"
        J["Join operation"]
    end
    subgraph "Options"
        BH["BroadcastHashJoin"]
        SM["SortMergeJoin"]
        SH["ShuffleHashJoin"]
    end
    subgraph "Based On"
        S["Table sizes"]
        H["Hints"]
    end
    J --> BH & SM & SH
    S --> J
    H --> J
    style BH fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Whole-Stage Code Generation
steps.explanation: **Tungsten** generates optimized JVM bytecode. Fuses operators together. Avoids virtual function calls. Runs at near-native speed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Traditional" T1["Operator 1 → output"] T2["Operator 2 → output"] T3["Virtual calls overhead"] end subgraph "Code Gen" CG["Fused code
No intermediate outputs
CPU cache friendly"] end style T3 fill:#FFEBEE style CG fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: View the Plan
steps.explanation: Use **explain()** to see optimization results. **explain(True)** shows all phases. Check that optimizations applied as expected.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Commands"
        E1["df.explain()  # Physical only"]
        E2["df.explain(True)  # All phases"]
        E3["df.explain('formatted')  # Pretty"]
    end
    subgraph "Look For"
        L1["PushedFilters"]
        L2["ReadSchema (pruned)"]
        L3["WholeStageCodegen"]
    end
    style E2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style L1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)
fields.description: Dynamic optimization, skew handling, and partition coalescing

steps.title: What is AQE?
steps.explanation: **Adaptive Query Execution** optimizes during runtime based on actual data statistics. Fixes problems Catalyst can't predict at compile time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Catalyst"
        C["Compile-time optimization"]
    end
    subgraph "AQE"
        A["Runtime optimization"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefit"
        B["Adapts to real data"]
    end
    C --> A --> B
    style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: AQE Features
steps.explanation: Three main features: **Coalesce Shuffle Partitions**, **Convert Sort-Merge to Broadcast**, **Handle Skew Joins**. Enabled by default in Spark 3.2+.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "AQE Capabilities" CS["Coalesce small partitions"] BC["Switch to broadcast join"] SK["Handle skewed joins"] end subgraph "Config" CF["spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true
(default in 3.2+)"] end CS & BC & SK --> CF style CF fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Coalesce Partitions
steps.explanation: After shuffle, AQE checks partition sizes. Combines small partitions to reduce task overhead. No manual repartition needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before AQE"
        B1["200 partitions"]
        B2["Many tiny partitions"]
        B3["Task overhead"]
    end
    subgraph "With AQE"
        A1["Check actual sizes"]
        A2["Combine small ones"]
        A3["Fewer, larger tasks"]
    end
    B1 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    style A3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Dynamic Join Selection
steps.explanation: At runtime, AQE checks actual table sizes after filters. Can switch from sort-merge to broadcast join if table becomes small enough.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Compile Time"
        C["Large table → Sort-Merge planned"]
    end
    subgraph "Runtime"
        R["After filter: only 5MB"]
        D["AQE: Switch to Broadcast!"]
    end
    C --> R --> D
    style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Skew Join Handling
steps.explanation: Data skew: some partitions have way more data. AQE detects skewed partitions and splits them into smaller pieces for parallel processing.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Skewed"
        S1["Partition 1: 100 rows"]
        S2["Partition 2: 1M rows"]
        S3["Task 2 takes forever"]
    end
    subgraph "AQE Fix"
        A1["Detect skew"]
        A2["Split partition 2"]
        A3["Process in parallel"]
    end
    S2 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3
    style A3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S2 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: AQE Configuration
steps.explanation: Key configs to tune AQE behavior. Defaults work well for most cases. Adjust for specific workloads.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Key Configs" C1["spark.sql.adaptive.enabled"] C2["spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled"] C3["spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled"] C4["spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor"] end subgraph "Thresholds" T1["advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes
Target partition size"] T2["skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes
Skew detection"] end style C1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Monitoring AQE
steps.explanation: Check Spark UI for AQE effects. Look for coalesced partitions, changed join types, split skewed partitions in the query plan.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Spark UI" U1["SQL tab → Query plan"] U2["Stage details"] end subgraph "Look For" L1["CustomShuffleReader
(coalescing)"] L2["BroadcastHashJoin
(converted)"] L3["Skewed partition splits"] end U1 --> L1 & L2 & L3 style L1 fill:#E8F5E9 style L2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Cache vs Persist
fields.description: Storage levels, when to cache, and memory management

steps.title: Why Cache?
steps.explanation: Spark recomputes DataFrames on each action (lazy evaluation). **Cache** stores results in memory to avoid recomputation. Essential for iterative algorithms.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without Cache"
        W1["Action 1 → Compute all"]
        W2["Action 2 → Compute all again!"]
    end
    subgraph "With Cache"
        C1["Cache → Compute once"]
        C2["Action 1 → Use cached"]
        C3["Action 2 → Use cached"]
    end
    style W2 fill:#FFEBEE
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: cache() vs persist()
steps.explanation: **cache()** = shorthand for persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK). **persist()** allows choosing storage level. Both are lazy—actual caching happens on first action.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "cache()"
        C["df.cache()"]
        CE["= persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK)"]
    end
    subgraph "persist()"
        P["df.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)"]
        PE["Choose storage level"]
    end
    C --> CE
    P --> PE
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Storage Levels
steps.explanation: Different levels trade off memory, disk, serialization, and replication. Choose based on available resources and recomputation cost.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Levels" M["MEMORY_ONLY
Fast, evicts if no space"] MD["MEMORY_AND_DISK
Spills to disk if needed"] D["DISK_ONLY
All on disk"] MS["MEMORY_ONLY_SER
Serialized, less memory"] end subgraph "With Replication" R["*_2 variants
Copy on 2 nodes"] end style MD fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Cache
steps.explanation: Cache when DataFrame is: **expensive to compute** AND **used multiple times**. Don't cache everything—memory is limited!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good to Cache"
        G1["Joins of large tables"]
        G2["After complex transforms"]
        G3["ML feature DataFrames"]
        G4["Iterative algorithms"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't Cache"
        B1["One-time use DataFrames"]
        B2["Simple transformations"]
        B3["Everything (OOM!)"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style B3 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Unpersist
steps.explanation: Always **unpersist()** when done. Frees memory for other operations. Cached data stays until unpersist or Spark evicts it.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Lifecycle"
        C["df.cache()"]
        A["df.count()  # Actually caches"]
        U["Use cached data..."]
        UP["df.unpersist()  # Free memory"]
    end
    C --> A --> U --> UP
    style UP fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Checkpointing
steps.explanation: **checkpoint()** writes to reliable storage and breaks lineage. Use for very long DAGs. More reliable than cache for fault tolerance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Cache"
        CA["In memory/disk"]
        CA2["Keeps lineage"]
        CA3["May recompute on failure"]
    end
    subgraph "Checkpoint"
        CP["To HDFS/S3"]
        CP2["Breaks lineage"]
        CP3["Reliable recovery"]
    end
    style CA fill:#E3F2FD
    style CP fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Monitoring Cache
steps.explanation: Check Spark UI Storage tab. See what's cached, how much memory used, fraction cached. Helps debug memory issues.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Spark UI - Storage"
        S1["Cached DataFrames list"]
        S2["Size in memory/disk"]
        S3["Fraction cached"]
        S4["Partitions cached"]
    end
    subgraph "Warning Signs"
        W1["Fraction < 100%"]
        W2["Spilled to disk"]
        W3["Evicted entries"]
    end
    S1 --> W1 & W2 & W3
    style W1 fill:#FFF3E0

fields.title: File Formats Comparison
fields.description: Parquet, ORC, Delta, CSV, JSON trade-offs

steps.title: Format Overview
steps.explanation: Different formats for different needs. **Columnar** (Parquet, ORC) for analytics. **Row-based** (CSV, JSON) for compatibility. **Delta** for ACID transactions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Columnar"
        P["Parquet"]
        O["ORC"]
    end
    subgraph "Row-based"
        C["CSV"]
        J["JSON"]
    end
    subgraph "Transactional"
        D["Delta Lake"]
    end
    style P fill:#E8F5E9
    style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Parquet
steps.explanation: **Parquet**: Default for Spark. Columnar, compressed, schema embedded. Best for analytical queries. Excellent predicate pushdown.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parquet Pros"
        P1["Columnar = fast analytics"]
        P2["Built-in compression"]
        P3["Schema in file"]
        P4["Predicate pushdown"]
    end
    subgraph "Parquet Cons"
        C1["Not human-readable"]
        C2["Append-only (no update)"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style P2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Delta Lake
steps.explanation: **Delta Lake**: Parquet + transaction log. Enables ACID, time travel, schema enforcement. Best for data lakes needing reliability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Delta Features"
        D1["ACID transactions"]
        D2["Time travel"]
        D3["Schema evolution"]
        D4["Upserts (MERGE)"]
        D5["Z-ordering"]
    end
    subgraph "Usage"
        U["df.write.format('delta').save()"]
    end
    D1 & D2 & D3 & D4 & D5 --> U
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style D2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ORC
steps.explanation: **ORC**: Optimized Row Columnar. Similar to Parquet. Better Hive integration. Slightly better for Hive workloads, Parquet for Spark.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "ORC"
        O1["Columnar format"]
        O2["Optimized for Hive"]
        O3["Built-in indexes"]
    end
    subgraph "Parquet vs ORC"
        P["Parquet: Spark ecosystem"]
        OR["ORC: Hive ecosystem"]
        B["Both: Good compression"]
    end
    style P fill:#E8F5E9
    style OR fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: CSV and JSON
steps.explanation: **CSV/JSON**: Human-readable, universal. Slow to read, no schema, no compression. Use for interchange, not production analytics.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "CSV/JSON Pros"
        P1["Human readable"]
        P2["Universal format"]
        P3["Easy debugging"]
    end
    subgraph "CSV/JSON Cons"
        C1["No built-in schema"]
        C2["No compression"]
        C3["Full file scan"]
        C4["No predicate pushdown"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C4 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Performance Comparison
steps.explanation: Columnar formats are 10-100x faster for analytics. Delta adds ~5-10% overhead for transactional features. Worth it for reliability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Speed (Analytics)"
        S1["Parquet: ⚡⚡⚡⚡⚡"]
        S2["ORC: ⚡⚡⚡⚡⚡"]
        S3["Delta: ⚡⚡⚡⚡"]
        S4["CSV: ⚡"]
        S5["JSON: ⚡"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style S3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Use What
steps.explanation: Decision guide based on use case. Default to Parquet/Delta for production. CSV/JSON for data exchange.

fields.title: Schema Handling
fields.description: Inference vs explicit schema, evolution, and mergeSchema

steps.title: Schema Basics
steps.explanation: Schema defines column names and types. Spark can **infer** schema (automatic) or you can **define explicitly** (recommended for production).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Schema Inference"
        I["Spark samples data"]
        I2["Guesses types"]
        I3["Can be wrong!"]
    end
    subgraph "Explicit Schema"
        E["You define types"]
        E2["Faster read"]
        E3["Type safety"]
    end
    style I3 fill:#FFEBEE
    style E3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Schema Inference
steps.explanation: Default behavior: Spark reads sample of data to infer schema. Works but has pitfalls: wrong types, extra I/O, inconsistent across files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Inference Process"
        R["Read sample"]
        G["Guess types"]
        A["Apply to all"]
    end
    subgraph "Problems"
        P1["Integer vs Long"]
        P2["Null columns = string"]
        P3["Date vs String"]
    end
    R --> G --> A
    style P1 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Defining Explicit Schema
steps.explanation: Use **StructType** and **StructField** to define schema. Specify column name, type, and nullable. Apply when reading.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Define Schema" S["from pyspark.sql.types import *

schema = StructType([
StructField('id', LongType(), False),
StructField('name', StringType(), True),
StructField('price', DoubleType(), True)
])"] end subgraph "Apply" A["spark.read.schema(schema)\
.csv('data.csv')"] end S --> A style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Available Types
steps.explanation: PySpark has types for all common data. Use appropriate precision for numbers. Complex types for nested data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Numeric"
        N["IntegerType, LongType"]
        N2["FloatType, DoubleType"]
        N3["DecimalType(p, s)"]
    end
    subgraph "String/Binary"
        S["StringType"]
        B["BinaryType"]
    end
    subgraph "Date/Time"
        D["DateType, TimestampType"]
    end
    subgraph "Complex"
        C["ArrayType, MapType"]
        C2["StructType (nested)"]
    end
    style N fill:#E8F5E9
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Schema Evolution
steps.explanation: When schema changes over time (new columns, type changes). **mergeSchema** combines schemas from different files. Delta Lake handles this best.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problem" F1["File 1: {a, b}"] F2["File 2: {a, b, c}"] R["Read together?"] end subgraph "Solution" M["spark.read\
.option('mergeSchema', 'true')\
.parquet(...)"] end F1 & F2 --> R --> M style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Schema Enforcement
steps.explanation: Ensure data matches expected schema. Use **mode** options: FAILFAST (error on mismatch), DROPMALFORMED (skip bad), PERMISSIVE (nullify bad).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Read Modes" F["FAILFAST
Error on bad data"] D["DROPMALFORMED
Skip bad rows"] P["PERMISSIVE
Null for bad values"] end subgraph "Code" C["spark.read\
.schema(my_schema)\
.option('mode', 'FAILFAST')"] end style F fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Always define explicit schemas in production. Store schema definitions in code/config. Use Delta for automatic schema evolution.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Do"
        D1["Define schemas explicitly"]
        D2["Version control schemas"]
        D3["Use Delta Lake"]
        D4["Validate early"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't"
        N1["Rely on inference in prod"]
        N2["Ignore schema changes"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFEBEE

fields.title: Structured Streaming
fields.description: Triggers, watermarks, output modes, and exactly-once semantics

steps.title: Structured Streaming Overview
steps.explanation: Treat streaming data as an unbounded table. Use same DataFrame API as batch. Spark handles incremental processing, fault tolerance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Source" S["Kafka, Files, Socket"] end subgraph "Processing" P["DataFrame operations
(same as batch!)"] end subgraph "Sink" K["Kafka, Files, Console"] end S --> P --> K style P fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Basic Structure
steps.explanation: Read with **readStream**, transform with DataFrame ops, write with **writeStream**. Specify trigger and output mode.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C["df = spark.readStream\
.format('kafka')\
.load()

result = df.groupBy('key').count()

query = result.writeStream\
.outputMode('complete')\
.format('console')\
.start()"] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Output Modes
steps.explanation: Three modes for how results are written: **Append** (new rows only), **Complete** (full result each time), **Update** (changed rows only).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Append"
        A["Only new rows output"]
        A2["For non-aggregation queries"]
    end
    subgraph "Complete"
        C["Full result table"]
        C2["For aggregations"]
    end
    subgraph "Update"
        U["Only changed rows"]
        U2["Efficient for updates"]
    end
    style A fill:#E3F2FD
    style C fill:#E8F5E9
    style U fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Triggers
steps.explanation: Control how often processing runs. **Default**: as fast as possible. **ProcessingTime**: fixed interval. **Once**: single batch. **AvailableNow**: all available, then stop.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Trigger Types" T1["Default: continuous"] T2["processingTime='10 seconds'"] T3["once=True (one batch)"] T4["availableNow=True"] end subgraph "Usage" U["writeStream.trigger(
processingTime='1 minute'
)"] end T2 --> U style T2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Watermarks
steps.explanation: Handle late data in event-time processing. **Watermark** defines how late data can arrive. Data older than watermark is dropped.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problem" P["Events arrive late"] P2["Can't wait forever"] end subgraph "Watermark" W["df.withWatermark(
'timestamp',
'10 minutes'
)"] W2["Accept data up to 10 min late"] end P --> W style W fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Checkpointing
steps.explanation: **checkpointLocation** enables fault tolerance and exactly-once. Stores progress and state. Required for production streaming.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Checkpoint" C1["Stores offsets"] C2["Stores state (aggregations)"] C3["Enables recovery"] end subgraph "Usage" U["writeStream\
.option('checkpointLocation',
'/path/to/checkpoint')"] end C1 & C2 & C3 --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Common Sources & Sinks
steps.explanation: Built-in connectors for common systems. Kafka most popular for production. File sources good for testing.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Sources"
        S1["Kafka"]
        S2["File (JSON, Parquet, CSV)"]
        S3["Socket (testing)"]
        S4["Rate (testing)"]
    end
    subgraph "Sinks"
        K1["Kafka"]
        K2["File"]
        K3["Console (debug)"]
        K4["Memory (debug)"]
        K5["foreach (custom)"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style K1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: EDA Workflow Overview
fields.description: The systematic process: understand → clean → explore → visualize → insights

steps.title: What is EDA?
steps.explanation: **Exploratory Data Analysis** is the process of understanding your data before modeling. Coined by John Tukey. Goal: discover patterns, spot anomalies, test hypotheses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "EDA Goals"
        G1["Understand structure"]
        G2["Find patterns"]
        G3["Detect anomalies"]
        G4["Form hypotheses"]
    end
    G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4
    style G1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: EDA Workflow
steps.explanation: Systematic approach: **Load** → **Understand** → **Clean** → **Explore** → **Visualize** → **Document**. Iterative—often loop back as you learn more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    L["1. Load Data"]
    U["2. Understand Structure"]
    C["3. Clean & Prepare"]
    E["4. Explore Relationships"]
    V["5. Visualize Findings"]
    D["6. Document Insights"]

    L --> U --> C --> E --> V --> D
    E -->|"Found issues"| C
    V -->|"New questions"| E
    style L fill:#E3F2FD
    style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 1: Load & First Look
steps.explanation: Load data and get initial overview. Check shape, columns, data types. First few rows. This sets the stage for everything else.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "First Look"
        S["df.shape"]
        C["df.columns"]
        D["df.dtypes"]
        H["df.head()"]
        I["df.info()"]
    end
    subgraph "Questions"
        Q1["How many rows/columns?"]
        Q2["What are the features?"]
        Q3["What types of data?"]
    end
    S & C & D & H & I --> Q1 & Q2 & Q3
    style H fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 2: Understand Structure
steps.explanation: Dig deeper: summary statistics, missing values, unique counts, data quality issues. Build mental model of the data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Understand"
        DE["df.describe()"]
        MI["df.isnull().sum()"]
        NU["df.nunique()"]
        VC["df['col'].value_counts()"]
    end
    subgraph "Learn"
        L1["Distributions"]
        L2["Missing patterns"]
        L3["Cardinality"]
    end
    DE --> L1
    MI --> L2
    NU --> L3
    style DE fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 3: Clean & Prepare
steps.explanation: Handle issues found: missing values, duplicates, outliers, type conversions. Document decisions. Clean data enables reliable analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Common Tasks"
        M["Handle missing values"]
        D["Remove duplicates"]
        O["Address outliers"]
        T["Fix data types"]
        F["Feature engineering"]
    end
    subgraph "Methods"
        M1["Drop, impute, flag"]
        D1["df.drop_duplicates()"]
        O1["Cap, remove, transform"]
    end
    M --> M1
    D --> D1
    O --> O1
    style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Step 4-5: Explore & Visualize
steps.explanation: Univariate analysis (one variable), bivariate (two variables), multivariate (many). Use appropriate visualizations for each.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Univariate"
        U1["Histograms"]
        U2["Box plots"]
        U3["Bar charts"]
    end
    subgraph "Bivariate"
        B1["Scatter plots"]
        B2["Correlation"]
        B3["Grouped stats"]
    end
    subgraph "Multivariate"
        M1["Pair plots"]
        M2["Heatmaps"]
        M3["PCA"]
    end
    style U1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style B1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style M2 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Step 6: Document Insights
steps.explanation: Record findings, decisions, and next steps. EDA informs modeling choices. Share with stakeholders. Reproducibility matters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Document"
        F["Key findings"]
        D["Data quality issues"]
        A["Assumptions made"]
        R["Recommendations"]
    end
    subgraph "Outputs"
        N["Notebook/report"]
        V["Key visualizations"]
        P["Prepared dataset"]
    end
    F & D & A & R --> N & V & P
    style N fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Summary Statistics
fields.description: Mean, median, mode, std, quartiles, skewness, kurtosis explained

steps.title: Why Summary Statistics?
steps.explanation: Condense data into key numbers. Understand **central tendency** (typical value), **spread** (variability), and **shape** (distribution form).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Summary Stats Tell You" CT["Central Tendency
What's typical?"] SP["Spread
How variable?"] SH["Shape
What distribution?"] end style CT fill:#E8F5E9 style SP fill:#E3F2FD style SH fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Central Tendency
steps.explanation: **Mean**: arithmetic average. **Median**: middle value (robust to outliers). **Mode**: most frequent value. Each tells different story.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Data: 1, 2, 2, 3, 100" D["Five values with outlier"] end subgraph "Measures" ME["Mean = 21.6
(pulled by outlier)"] MD["Median = 2
(middle value)"] MO["Mode = 2
(most frequent)"] end D --> ME & MD & MO style ME fill:#FFEBEE style MD fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Spread / Dispersion
steps.explanation: **Std Dev**: average distance from mean. **Variance**: std² . **Range**: max - min. **IQR**: Q3 - Q1 (robust).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Spread Measures" S["Std Dev (σ)
Typical deviation"] V["Variance (σ²)
Squared deviation"] R["Range
Max - Min"] I["IQR
Q3 - Q1"] end subgraph "Rule of Thumb" RT["68% within 1σ
95% within 2σ
99.7% within 3σ"] end S --> RT style S fill:#E8F5E9 style I fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Quartiles & Percentiles
steps.explanation: Divide data into parts. **Q1** (25%), **Q2/Median** (50%), **Q3** (75%). Percentiles generalize to any percentage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Sorted Data" MIN["Min"] Q1["Q1
25%"] Q2["Q2
Median"] Q3["Q3
75%"] MAX["Max"] end MIN --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> MAX style Q2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Skewness
steps.explanation: Measures asymmetry. **Positive skew**: tail right (mean > median). **Negative skew**: tail left (mean < median). **Zero**: symmetric.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Left Skew (Negative)" LS["Tail to left
Mean < Median"] end subgraph "Symmetric" SY["No skew
Mean ≈ Median"] end subgraph "Right Skew (Positive)" RS["Tail to right
Mean > Median"] end style LS fill:#E3F2FD style SY fill:#E8F5E9 style RS fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Kurtosis
steps.explanation: Measures **tailedness**. High kurtosis = heavy tails, more outliers. Low kurtosis = light tails. Normal distribution = 3 (excess = 0).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Kurtosis Types" LP["Leptokurtic (>3)
Heavy tails, peaked"] ME["Mesokurtic (=3)
Normal distribution"] PL["Platykurtic (<3)
Light tails, flat"] end style ME fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pandas describe()
steps.explanation: **df.describe()** gives count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max. Quick overview of all numeric columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "df.describe()"
        C["count - non-null values"]
        M["mean - average"]
        S["std - standard deviation"]
        MI["min - minimum"]
        Q1["25% - first quartile"]
        Q2["50% - median"]
        Q3["75% - third quartile"]
        MA["max - maximum"]
    end
    C --> M --> S --> MI --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> MA
    style C fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Missing Value Analysis
fields.description: Patterns, MCAR/MAR/MNAR, visualization and handling techniques

steps.title: Why Missing Values Matter
steps.explanation: Missing data is common and impacts analysis. Understanding **why** data is missing determines how to handle it. Wrong approach biases results.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Impact"
        I1["Reduced sample size"]
        I2["Biased estimates"]
        I3["Invalid conclusions"]
    end
    subgraph "Questions"
        Q1["How much is missing?"]
        Q2["Why is it missing?"]
        Q3["Is there a pattern?"]
    end
    I1 & I2 & I3 --> Q1 & Q2 & Q3
    style Q2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Types of Missingness
steps.explanation: **MCAR**: Missing Completely At Random. **MAR**: Missing At Random (depends on observed). **MNAR**: Missing Not At Random (depends on unobserved).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "MCAR" M1["Completely random"] M1E["Example: Data entry typo"] end subgraph "MAR" M2["Depends on other columns"] M2E["Example: Income missing
more for younger people"] end subgraph "MNAR" M3["Depends on the value itself"] M3E["Example: High earners
don't report income"] end style M1 fill:#E8F5E9 style M2 fill:#FFF3E0 style M3 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Detecting Missing Values
steps.explanation: Use pandas to count and visualize missing data. Check percentages, patterns across columns, and correlations between missingness.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Detection Methods"
        C1["df.isnull().sum()"]
        C2["df.isnull().mean() * 100"]
        C3["df.info()"]
    end
    subgraph "Visualization"
        V1["Heatmap of nulls"]
        V2["Bar chart by column"]
        V3["missingno library"]
    end
    C1 --> V1
    C2 --> V2
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Missing Value Patterns
steps.explanation: Look for patterns: random scatter, blocks, correlations. Pattern informs handling strategy. Use matrix visualizations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Patterns" P1["Random (MCAR)
Scattered nulls"] P2["Column-wise
Entire column sparse"] P3["Row-wise
Certain rows incomplete"] P4["Correlated
Nulls appear together"] end style P1 fill:#E8F5E9 style P4 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Handling Strategies
steps.explanation: **Drop**: Remove rows/columns. **Impute**: Fill with value. **Flag**: Create indicator column. **Model**: Predict missing values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Drop"
        D1["dropna() - remove rows"]
        D2["drop() - remove columns"]
        D3["When: <5% missing, MCAR"]
    end
    subgraph "Impute"
        I1["fillna(mean/median/mode)"]
        I2["Forward/backward fill"]
        I3["When: MAR, need all rows"]
    end
    subgraph "Advanced"
        A1["Create missing indicator"]
        A2["KNN/model imputation"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Imputation Methods
steps.explanation: **Mean/Median**: Simple, distorts variance. **Mode**: For categorical. **Interpolation**: Time series. **KNN/Regression**: Preserve relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Simple"
        S1["Mean - distorts distribution"]
        S2["Median - robust to outliers"]
        S3["Mode - for categorical"]
    end
    subgraph "Advanced"
        A1["KNN - uses similar rows"]
        A2["Regression - model-based"]
        A3["MICE - multiple imputation"]
    end
    style S2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Document missing data before and after handling. Test sensitivity of conclusions. Consider multiple approaches. Never hide the problem.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Do"
        D1["Document missing %"]
        D2["Analyze patterns first"]
        D3["Compare imputation methods"]
        D4["Report what you did"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't"
        N1["Ignore missing data"]
        N2["Always drop rows"]
        N3["Impute then forget"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFEBEE

fields.title: Histogram & KDE
fields.description: Binning strategies, kernel density estimation, choosing bin width

steps.title: Why Histograms?
steps.explanation: Histograms show **distribution shape**. See where values concentrate, spread, and outliers. Essential first look at any numeric variable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Histogram Shows"
        S1["Distribution shape"]
        S2["Central tendency"]
        S3["Spread/range"]
        S4["Outliers"]
        S5["Modality (peaks)"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: How Histograms Work
steps.explanation: Divide data range into **bins** (intervals). Count values in each bin. Height = count (or density). Bin choice affects appearance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Process" R["Find range (min to max)"] B["Divide into bins"] C["Count per bin"] D["Draw bars"] end R --> B --> C --> D subgraph "Example" E["Data: 1-100
10 bins: 1-10, 11-20, ..."] end style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Choosing Bin Width
steps.explanation: Too few bins: lose detail. Too many: noisy. Rules of thumb: **Sturges** (log-based), **Freedman-Diaconis** (IQR-based), or experiment.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Too Few Bins" TF["Hides patterns
Over-smoothed"] end subgraph "Just Right" JR["Shows true shape
Clear patterns"] end subgraph "Too Many Bins" TM["Noisy
Hard to interpret"] end style TF fill:#FFEBEE style JR fill:#E8F5E9 style TM fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Bin Count Rules
steps.explanation: Common rules: **Sturges**: k = 1 + log₂(n). **Sqrt**: k = √n. **Freedman-Diaconis**: width = 2 × IQR / n^(1/3). Try multiple!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Rules" R1["Sturges: 1 + log₂(n)"] R2["Square root: √n"] R3["Freedman-Diaconis:
Uses IQR"] end subgraph "Pandas" P["df['col'].hist(bins='auto')"] P2["df['col'].hist(bins=30)"] end R1 & R2 & R3 --> P style P fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Kernel Density Estimation
