steps.explanation: **KDE** is a smooth version of histogram. Places kernel (bump) at each point, sums them. No bin choice needed. Shows continuous density.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "KDE Process"
        K1["Place kernel at each point"]
        K2["Kernels overlap and sum"]
        K3["Result: smooth curve"]
    end
    subgraph "Bandwidth"
        B1["Small: wiggly (overfit)"]
        B2["Large: smooth (underfit)"]
        B3["Just right: captures shape"]
    end
    K1 --> K2 --> K3
    style K3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Histogram vs KDE
steps.explanation: Both show distribution. Histogram: discrete bins, count-based. KDE: continuous, probability density. Often plot both together.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Histogram" H1["Discrete bins"] H2["Shows counts"] H3["Bin choice matters"] end subgraph "KDE" K1["Continuous curve"] K2["Shows density"] K3["Bandwidth choice matters"] end subgraph "Best Practice" BP["Plot both!
df['col'].plot(kind='hist',
density=True)
df['col'].plot(kind='kde')"] end style BP fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Code Examples
steps.explanation: Pandas and seaborn make histograms easy. Use `histplot` for modern approach with KDE overlay option.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pandas" P1["df['col'].hist(bins=30)"] P2["df['col'].plot.kde()"] end subgraph "Seaborn" S1["sns.histplot(df['col'])"] S2["sns.histplot(df['col'],
kde=True)"] S3["sns.kdeplot(df['col'])"] end style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Box Plot Anatomy
fields.description: Quartiles, whiskers, outliers, and comparing groups

steps.title: What is a Box Plot?
steps.explanation: Box plot (box-and-whisker) shows **five-number summary** plus outliers. Compact way to see distribution, spread, and compare groups.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Box Plot Shows"
        S1["Median (center line)"]
        S2["Quartiles (box edges)"]
        S3["Range (whiskers)"]
        S4["Outliers (points)"]
    end
    subgraph "Great For"
        G1["Comparing groups"]
        G2["Spotting outliers"]
        G3["Quick distribution view"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Anatomy of Box Plot
steps.explanation: **Box**: Q1 to Q3 (IQR). **Line**: Median. **Whiskers**: extend to min/max within 1.5×IQR. **Points**: outliers beyond whiskers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Components (bottom to top)" OUT1["Outliers below"] WL["Lower whisker
(Q1 - 1.5×IQR or min)"] Q1["Q1 (25th percentile)"] MED["Median (50th)"] Q3["Q3 (75th percentile)"] WU["Upper whisker
(Q3 + 1.5×IQR or max)"] OUT2["Outliers above"] end OUT1 --> WL --> Q1 --> MED --> Q3 --> WU --> OUT2 style MED fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: The IQR Rule
steps.explanation: **IQR** = Q3 - Q1 (interquartile range). Outliers defined as points beyond Q1 - 1.5×IQR or Q3 + 1.5×IQR. Robust outlier detection.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "IQR Calculation"
        I["IQR = Q3 - Q1"]
    end
    subgraph "Outlier Boundaries"
        L["Lower = Q1 - 1.5 × IQR"]
        U["Upper = Q3 + 1.5 × IQR"]
    end
    subgraph "Classification"
        IN["Within bounds: Normal"]
        OUT["Outside bounds: Outlier"]
    end
    I --> L & U
    L & U --> IN & OUT
    style OUT fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Reading Distribution Shape
steps.explanation: Box position and size reveal distribution. Symmetric: median centered. Skewed: median off-center. Wide box: high variability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Symmetric" S["Median in center
Whiskers equal length"] end subgraph "Right Skewed" R["Median toward bottom
Upper whisker longer"] end subgraph "Left Skewed" L["Median toward top
Lower whisker longer"] end style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Comparing Groups
steps.explanation: Box plots excel at group comparison. Side-by-side boxes show differences in median, spread, and outliers across categories.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Group Comparison" C1["Different medians?
→ Different centers"] C2["Different box sizes?
→ Different spreads"] C3["Overlap?
→ Similar distributions"] end subgraph "Code" CO["sns.boxplot(x='group',
y='value', data=df)"] end C1 & C2 & C3 --> CO style CO fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Variations
steps.explanation: **Violin plot**: box + KDE. **Swarm plot**: show all points. **Box + strip**: box with jittered points. Choose based on data size.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Variations" V1["Violin plot
Shows full distribution"] V2["Swarm plot
All points visible"] V3["Box + strip
Summary + detail"] end subgraph "When to Use" W1["Small n: show points"] W2["Large n: box/violin"] end style V3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Code Examples
steps.explanation: Easy to create with pandas, matplotlib, or seaborn. Horizontal or vertical orientation. Group by categorical variable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pandas" P["df.boxplot(column='value')"] P2["df.boxplot(column='value',
by='group')"] end subgraph "Seaborn" S["sns.boxplot(y=df['value'])"] S2["sns.boxplot(x='group',
y='value', data=df)"] S3["sns.violinplot(...)"] end style S2 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Distribution Shapes
fields.description: Normal, skewed, bimodal, uniform - recognizing and interpreting patterns

steps.title: Why Shape Matters
steps.explanation: Distribution shape guides analysis choices. Affects which statistics to use, whether transformations are needed, and model assumptions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Shape Determines" D1["Which average?
Mean vs Median"] D2["Which tests?
Parametric vs Non-parametric"] D3["Need transform?
Log, sqrt, etc."] end style D1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Normal Distribution
steps.explanation: **Bell curve**: symmetric, mean=median=mode. Many natural phenomena. Basis for many statistical tests. Check with histogram or Q-Q plot.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Normal (Gaussian)"
        N1["Symmetric bell shape"]
                        N2["Mean = Median = Mode"]
        N3["68-95-99.7 rule"]
    end
    subgraph "Examples"
        E1["Heights, test scores"]
        E2["Measurement errors"]
    end
    style N1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Skewed Distributions
steps.explanation: **Right skew**: long tail right, mean > median. **Left skew**: long tail left, mean < median. Common in real data (income, prices).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Right Skew (Positive)"
        RS1["Tail extends right"]
        RS2["Mean > Median"]
        RS3["Example: Income, prices"]
    end
    subgraph "Left Skew (Negative)"
        LS1["Tail extends left"]
        LS2["Mean < Median"]
        LS3["Example: Age at death"]
    end
    style RS1 fill:#FFF3E0
    style LS1 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Bimodal & Multimodal
steps.explanation: **Two or more peaks**. Often indicates mixed populations or subgroups. Investigate why—may need to analyze groups separately.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Bimodal" B1["Two distinct peaks"] B2["Suggests two groups"] end subgraph "Causes" C1["Mixed populations"] C2["Different conditions"] C3["Time of day effects"] end subgraph "Action" A["Split and analyze
groups separately"] end B1 --> C1 --> A style A fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Uniform Distribution
steps.explanation: **Flat**: all values equally likely. Rare in nature. May indicate random sampling, discretization issues, or artificial data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Uniform"
        U1["Flat, no peaks"]
        U2["All values equal probability"]
    end
    subgraph "Examples"
        E1["Random number generator"]
        E2["Dice rolls"]
        E3["Rounding artifacts"]
    end
    style U1 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Detecting Shape
steps.explanation: Use **histograms** and **KDE** to see shape. **Skewness** statistic quantifies asymmetry. **Q-Q plot** checks normality.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Visual"
        V1["Histogram"]
        V2["KDE plot"]
        V3["Q-Q plot"]
    end
    subgraph "Statistics"
        S1["skew(): > 0 right, < 0 left"]
        S2["kurtosis(): tail heaviness"]
    end
    subgraph "Tests"
        T["Shapiro-Wilk (normality)"]
    end
    V1 & V2 --> S1
    V3 --> T
    style V1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Handling Non-Normal
steps.explanation: If data is skewed: **transform** (log, sqrt), use **robust statistics** (median, IQR), or **non-parametric tests**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Transformations"
        T1["Log: for right skew"]
        T2["Sqrt: moderate skew"]
        T3["Box-Cox: automatic"]
    end
    subgraph "Robust Stats"
        R1["Median instead of mean"]
        R2["IQR instead of std"]
    end
    subgraph "Non-parametric"
        N["Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Scatter Plot & Correlation
fields.description: Linear relationships, Pearson vs Spearman, interpreting correlation coefficient

steps.title: Why Scatter Plots?
steps.explanation: Scatter plots show **relationship between two numeric variables**. See patterns, trends, clusters, and outliers. Essential for bivariate analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Scatter Shows"
        S1["Direction (positive/negative)"]
        S2["Strength (tight/loose)"]
        S3["Form (linear/curved)"]
        S4["Outliers"]
    end
    style S1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Correlation Coefficient
steps.explanation: **r** measures linear relationship strength. Ranges -1 to +1. **+1**: perfect positive. **-1**: perfect negative. **0**: no linear relationship.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Correlation Values"
        P1["r = +1: Perfect positive"]
        P2["r = +0.7: Strong positive"]
        P3["r = 0: No correlation"]
        N2["r = -0.7: Strong negative"]
        N1["r = -1: Perfect negative"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> N2 --> N1
    style P3 fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Pearson vs Spearman
steps.explanation: **Pearson**: linear relationships, sensitive to outliers. **Spearman**: monotonic relationships, rank-based, robust to outliers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Pearson"
        PE1["Measures linear relationship"]
        PE2["Assumes normality"]
        PE3["Affected by outliers"]
    end
    subgraph "Spearman"
        SP1["Measures monotonic relationship"]
        SP2["Rank-based"]
        SP3["Robust to outliers"]
    end
    subgraph "When to Use"
        W1["Linear + normal → Pearson"]
        W2["Non-linear/ordinal → Spearman"]
    end
    style W1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style W2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Interpreting Strength
steps.explanation: Rules of thumb for |r|: **< 0.3** weak, **0.3-0.7** moderate, **> 0.7** strong. Context matters—in some fields 0.3 is strong!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Strength Guidelines" W["|r| < 0.3
Weak"] M["|r| 0.3-0.7
Moderate"] S["|r| > 0.7
Strong"] end subgraph "Caution" C["Context matters!
Field-specific standards"] end W --> M --> S style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Correlation ≠ Causation
steps.explanation: **Correlation does not imply causation**. Third variables (confounders) may explain relationship. Need experiments for causation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correlated" A["Ice cream sales"] B["Drowning deaths"] end subgraph "Confounder" C["Hot weather"] end C --> A C --> B A -.->|"Correlation
(not causation)"| B style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Non-linear Patterns
steps.explanation: Correlation only measures **linear** relationships. Curved patterns may have r ≈ 0 but strong relationship. Always visualize first!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Linear"
        L["r captures relationship"]
    end
    subgraph "U-shaped"
        U["r ≈ 0 but related!"]
    end
    subgraph "Exponential"
        E["r underestimates strength"]
    end
    subgraph "Lesson"
        LE["ALWAYS plot first!"]
    end
    L & U & E --> LE
    style LE fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Code Examples
steps.explanation: Calculate correlation and create scatter plots with pandas, matplotlib, and seaborn. Add regression line to visualize trend.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Correlation"
        C1["df['x'].corr(df['y'])"]
        C2["df.corr()  # all pairs"]
        C3["df.corr(method='spearman')"]
    end
    subgraph "Visualization"
        V1["plt.scatter(df['x'], df['y'])"]
        V2["sns.scatterplot(x='x', y='y')"]
        V3["sns.regplot(...)  # with line"]
    end
    style V3 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Correlation Matrix & Heatmaps
fields.description: Multicollinearity detection, feature selection insights, visualization

steps.title: Correlation Matrix
steps.explanation: Matrix showing correlation between **all pairs** of numeric variables. Diagonal is always 1 (self-correlation). Symmetric matrix.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Matrix Structure" M[" A B C
A 1.0 0.8 0.2
B 0.8 1.0 0.3
C 0.2 0.3 1.0"] end subgraph "Properties" P1["Diagonal = 1"] P2["Symmetric"] P3["Values: -1 to +1"] end style M fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Creating Heatmaps
steps.explanation: Visualize correlation matrix with **color**. Red/blue for positive/negative. Intensity shows strength. Quickly spot patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code" C1["corr = df.corr()"] C2["sns.heatmap(corr,
annot=True,
cmap='coolwarm')"] end subgraph "Color Scheme" CS["Red: positive
Blue: negative
White: zero"] end C1 --> C2 style C2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Detecting Multicollinearity
steps.explanation: **Multicollinearity**: features highly correlated with each other. Problematic for regression. Look for off-diagonal values > 0.8.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Signs" S1["High correlation (> 0.8)"] S2["Between features"] S3["Not with target"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Unstable coefficients"] P2["Hard to interpret"] P3["Inflated variance"] end subgraph "Solution" SO["Remove one of the
correlated features"] end S1 --> P1 --> SO style SO fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Feature Selection Insights
steps.explanation: Use correlation with **target** to identify predictive features. High correlation with target = potentially useful. But beware non-linear relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "With Target" T1["High |r| with target
→ Potentially useful"] T2["Low |r| with target
→ May not help (linearly)"] end subgraph "Between Features" F1["High correlation
→ Redundant info"] F2["Keep one, drop other"] end style T1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Heatmap Best Practices
steps.explanation: Show only lower triangle (avoid redundancy). Add annotations for values. Use diverging colormap. Mask insignificant correlations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Improvements" I1["Lower triangle only"] I2["Add value annotations"] I3["Diverging colormap"] I4["Mask small values"] end subgraph "Code" C["mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr))
sns.heatmap(corr,
mask=mask,
annot=True)"] end I1 & I2 & I3 & I4 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Pair Plots
steps.explanation: **Pair plot**: scatter plots for all pairs + histograms on diagonal. See relationships and distributions together. Great for small feature sets.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pair Plot" P1["Scatter: all pairs"] P2["Histogram: diagonal"] P3["Color by category"] end subgraph "Code" C["sns.pairplot(df,
hue='category')"] end P1 & P2 & P3 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Clustermap
steps.explanation: **Clustermap**: heatmap with hierarchical clustering. Groups similar features together. Reveals structure in correlation patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Clustermap" CM1["Reorders rows/columns"] CM2["Groups similar features"] CM3["Shows dendrograms"] end subgraph "Code" C["sns.clustermap(df.corr(),
cmap='coolwarm',
annot=True)"] end CM1 & CM2 & CM3 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Categorical vs Numerical
fields.description: Grouped box plots, violin plots, bar charts with error bars

steps.title: The Question
steps.explanation: How does a **numeric variable** differ across **categories**? E.g., salary by department, age by gender, scores by treatment group.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Question Type" Q["How does Y (numeric)
vary by X (category)?"] end subgraph "Examples" E1["Salary by department"] E2["Height by gender"] E3["Score by treatment"] end Q --> E1 & E2 & E3 style Q fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Grouped Box Plots
steps.explanation: Box plot per category. Compare medians, spreads, outliers across groups. Most common choice for this analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Grouped Boxplot" B1["One box per category"] B2["Compare medians"] B3["Compare spreads"] B4["Spot outliers"] end subgraph "Code" C["sns.boxplot(x='category',
y='value', data=df)"] end B1 & B2 & B3 & B4 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Violin Plots
steps.explanation: Box plot + KDE. Shows **full distribution shape** per group. Better than boxplot when distribution shape matters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Violin Plot" V1["Width = density"] V2["Shows distribution shape"] V3["Can include box inside"] end subgraph "Code" C["sns.violinplot(x='category',
y='value', data=df)"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["Bimodal distributions"] W2["Sample size varies"] end V1 & V2 & V3 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Bar Charts with Error Bars
steps.explanation: Bar height = mean (or other stat). Error bars show **uncertainty** (std, SE, or CI). Good for presenting summary statistics.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Bar + Error" B1["Height = mean"] B2["Error bar = std or CI"] end subgraph "Code" C["sns.barplot(x='category',
y='value', data=df,
ci=95)"] end subgraph "Caution" CA["Hides distribution shape"] end B1 & B2 --> C style CA fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Strip and Swarm Plots
steps.explanation: Show **all individual points**. Strip: random jitter. Swarm: no overlap. Great for small datasets to see actual data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Strip Plot"
        ST["Points with jitter"]
        ST2["Can overlap"]
    end
    subgraph "Swarm Plot"
        SW["Points arranged"]
        SW2["No overlap"]
        SW3["Slow for large n"]
    end
    subgraph "Combo"
        CO["Box + Strip: best of both"]
    end
    style CO fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Statistical Testing
steps.explanation: Visual differences may not be significant. Use **t-test** (2 groups), **ANOVA** (3+ groups), or non-parametric alternatives.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "2 Groups"
        T1["t-test (normal)"]
        T2["Mann-Whitney (non-normal)"]
    end
    subgraph "3+ Groups"
        A1["ANOVA (normal)"]
        A2["Kruskal-Wallis (non-normal)"]
    end
    subgraph "Post-hoc"
        PH["Tukey HSD, Bonferroni"]
    end
    A1 --> PH
    style T1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style A1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Code Examples
steps.explanation: Seaborn makes grouped visualizations easy. Combine with groupby for numeric summaries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Visualizations" V1["sns.boxplot(x, y)"] V2["sns.violinplot(x, y)"] V3["sns.barplot(x, y)"] V4["sns.stripplot(x, y)"] end subgraph "Numeric Summary" N["df.groupby('category')['value']\
.agg(['mean', 'std', 'count'])"] end style N fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Outlier Detection Methods
fields.description: IQR, Z-score, isolation forest, and visual identification

steps.title: What Are Outliers?
steps.explanation: **Outliers**: data points significantly different from others. Could be errors, rare events, or genuinely extreme values. Important to investigate!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Outlier Types" E["Errors
(data entry, measurement)"] R["Rare events
(legitimate but unusual)"] I["Interesting
(worth investigating)"] end subgraph "Impact" IM["Affect mean, std, regression"] end E & R & I --> IM style IM fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: IQR Method
steps.explanation: **Box plot rule**: outliers are beyond Q1 - 1.5×IQR or Q3 + 1.5×IQR. Robust—based on quartiles, not mean/std.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "IQR Method" I1["IQR = Q3 - Q1"] I2["Lower = Q1 - 1.5 × IQR"] I3["Upper = Q3 + 1.5 × IQR"] I4["Outside = outlier"] end subgraph "Code" C["Q1 = df['col'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['col'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
outliers = df[(df['col'] < Q1-1.5*IQR) |
(df['col'] > Q3+1.5*IQR)]"] end I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 style I4 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Z-Score Method
steps.explanation: **Z-score**: how many standard deviations from mean. |Z| > 2 or 3 often flagged as outlier. Assumes roughly normal distribution.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Z-Score" Z1["Z = (x - mean) / std"] Z2["|Z| > 3: extreme outlier"] Z3["|Z| > 2: moderate outlier"] end subgraph "Code" C["from scipy import stats
z = np.abs(stats.zscore(df['col']))
outliers = df[z > 3]"] end Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Visual Detection
steps.explanation: **See outliers** with box plots, scatter plots, histograms. Often the best first step—context helps interpretation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Visualizations"
        V1["Box plot: points beyond whiskers"]
        V2["Scatter plot: distant points"]
        V3["Histogram: extreme bars"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B["See context"]
        B2["Judge severity"]
        B3["Spot patterns"]
    end
    V1 & V2 & V3 --> B & B2 & B3
    style V1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Multivariate Outliers
steps.explanation: Point may be normal in each dimension but outlier in combination. Use **Isolation Forest**, **LOF**, or **Mahalanobis distance**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Methods" IF["Isolation Forest
Tree-based, scalable"] LO["Local Outlier Factor
Density-based"] MA["Mahalanobis Distance
Accounts for correlation"] end subgraph "Code (IF)" C["from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
iso = IsolationForest(contamination=0.1)
outliers = iso.fit_predict(df)"] end style IF fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Method Comparison
steps.explanation: Different methods catch different outliers. IQR: robust. Z-score: assumes normality. ML methods: multivariate. Use multiple approaches.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "IQR"
        I1["✓ Robust"]
        I2["✓ Simple"]
        I3["✗ Univariate only"]
    end
    subgraph "Z-Score"
        Z1["✓ Simple"]
        Z2["✗ Assumes normal"]
        Z3["✗ Sensitive to outliers"]
    end
    subgraph "ML Methods"
        M1["✓ Multivariate"]
        M2["✓ Complex patterns"]
        M3["✗ Harder to interpret"]
    end
    style I1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style M1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Investigation Workflow
steps.explanation: Detect → Investigate → Decide. Don't automatically remove! Understand why they're outliers first.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    D["Detect outliers"]
    I["Investigate cause"]
    Q{"Error or real?"}
    Q -->|Error| R["Fix or remove"]
    Q -->|Real| K["Keep or transform"]
    D --> I --> Q
    style I fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Handling Outliers
fields.description: Remove, cap, transform - when each approach is appropriate

steps.title: Handling Options
steps.explanation: Four main approaches: **Remove**, **Cap/Winsorize**, **Transform**, or **Keep**. Choice depends on cause and analysis goals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Options" R["Remove
Delete outlier rows"] C["Cap/Winsorize
Limit to threshold"] T["Transform
Log, sqrt, etc."] K["Keep
Use robust methods"] end style R fill:#FFEBEE style C fill:#FFF3E0 style T fill:#E3F2FD style K fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: When to Remove
steps.explanation: Remove if outlier is **clearly an error**: impossible values, data entry mistakes, measurement failures. Document the removal!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Remove When" R1["Clearly impossible values"] R2["Known data entry errors"] R3["Measurement failures"] end subgraph "Code" C["# Remove impossible ages
df = df[df['age'] <= 120]

# Remove based on IQR
df = df[~((df['x'] < lower) |
(df['x'] > upper))]"] end R1 & R2 & R3 --> C style R1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Capping / Winsorizing
steps.explanation: **Cap** extreme values at a threshold. Preserves all rows, reduces impact. **Winsorizing**: cap at percentiles (e.g., 1st and 99th).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Capping" C1["Set max/min limits"] C2["Values beyond → set to limit"] end subgraph "Winsorizing" W1["Cap at percentiles"] W2["e.g., 1st and 99th"] end subgraph "Code" CO["# Cap at percentiles
lower = df['x'].quantile(0.01)
upper = df['x'].quantile(0.99)
df['x'] = df['x'].clip(lower, upper)"] end C1 & W1 --> CO style CO fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Transformation
steps.explanation: **Transform** to reduce outlier impact. Log, sqrt, Box-Cox compress right skew. Doesn't remove outliers—makes them less extreme.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Transformations" T1["Log: strong compression"] T2["Sqrt: moderate"] T3["Box-Cox: automatic"] end subgraph "When" W["Right-skewed data"] W2["Multiplicative relationships"] end subgraph "Code" C["df['log_x'] = np.log1p(df['x'])
df['sqrt_x'] = np.sqrt(df['x'])"] end T1 & T2 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Keep with Robust Methods
steps.explanation: If outliers are **real and important**, keep them but use **robust methods**: median instead of mean, MAD instead of std, robust regression.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Robust Alternatives"
        R1["Median vs Mean"]
        R2["MAD vs Std"]
        R3["Huber regression"]
        R4["Trimmed mean"]
    end
    subgraph "When"
        W["Outliers are genuine"]
        W2["Can't justify removal"]
    end
    R1 & R2 --> W
    style R1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Decision Framework
steps.explanation: Systematic approach: Is it an error? → Remove. Is it extreme but real? → Transform or robust methods. Does model need it? → Cap or keep.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Is it an error?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R["Remove"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is distribution skewed?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| T["Transform"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Need all observations?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| K["Keep + robust methods"]
    Q3 -->|No| C["Cap/Winsorize"]
    style R fill:#FFEBEE
    style T fill:#E3F2FD
    style K fill:#E8F5E9
    style C fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Document decisions. Compare results with/without outliers. Report sensitivity. Never hide what you did!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Do"
        D1["Document all decisions"]
        D2["Justify your choice"]
        D3["Check sensitivity"]
        D4["Report transparently"]
    end
    subgraph "Don't"
        N1["Remove silently"]
        N2["Cherry-pick results"]
        N3["Ignore outliers"]
    end
    style D1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style N1 fill:#FFEBEE

fields.title: Data Profiling
fields.description: Automated EDA tools, ydata-profiling, what to look for

steps.title: What is Data Profiling?
steps.explanation: **Automated analysis** of dataset characteristics. Generates comprehensive report: types, stats, missing, distributions, correlations. Saves hours of manual EDA.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Profiling Provides"
                        P1["Data types & stats"]
        P2["Missing value analysis"]
        P3["Distribution plots"]
        P4["Correlation analysis"]
        P5["Duplicate detection"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefit"
        B["Hours of EDA → Minutes"]
    end
    P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 & P5 --> B
    style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: ydata-profiling (pandas-profiling)
steps.explanation: Most popular profiling library. One line of code generates full HTML report. Formerly called pandas-profiling.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Installation" I["pip install ydata-profiling"] end subgraph "Usage" U["from ydata_profiling import ProfileReport

profile = ProfileReport(df)
profile.to_file('report.html')"] end I --> U style U fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Report Contents
steps.explanation: Report includes: **Overview** (size, types), **Variables** (per-column analysis), **Interactions** (correlations), **Missing**, **Duplicates**, **Sample**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sections" O["Overview
Dataset stats"] V["Variables
Per-column deep dive"] I["Interactions
Correlations, pairs"] M["Missing
Patterns, counts"] D["Duplicates
Identical rows"] S["Sample
First/last rows"] end O --> V --> I --> M --> D --> S style V fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Per-Variable Analysis
steps.explanation: For each column: type, distinct count, missing %, statistics, histogram/bar chart, frequent values, extreme values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Numeric Variable"
        N1["Min, max, mean, median"]
        N2["Std, variance, range"]
        N3["Histogram + stats"]
        N4["Zeros, negatives"]
    end
    subgraph "Categorical Variable"
        C1["Unique count"]
        C2["Top values + frequency"]
        C3["Bar chart"]
    end
    style N3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Alerts & Warnings
steps.explanation: Profiler flags issues: high cardinality, high correlation, many zeros, constant columns, skewness, missing values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Alerts" A1["HIGH CARDINALITY
Too many unique values"] A2["HIGH CORRELATION
Multicollinearity"] A3["MISSING
Significant nulls"] A4["CONSTANT
Single value"] A5["ZEROS
Many zero values"] end style A1 fill:#FFF3E0 style A2 fill:#FFF3E0 style A3 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Configuration Options
steps.explanation: Customize for large datasets: minimal mode, sampling, disable expensive computations. Control what's included.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Modes" M1["Default: Full analysis"] M2["Minimal: Fast, basic"] M3["Explorative: Interactive"] end subgraph "Options" O["ProfileReport(df,
minimal=True,
samples={'head': 10},
correlations=None)"] end M1 & M2 & M3 --> O style O fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Other Profiling Tools
steps.explanation: Alternatives: **Sweetviz** (comparison reports), **D-Tale** (interactive), **Great Expectations** (validation). Each has strengths.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Alternatives" S["Sweetviz
Compare datasets"] D["D-Tale
Interactive web UI"] G["Great Expectations
Data validation"] L["Lux
Auto visualizations"] end subgraph "Choose Based On" C["Comparison needed → Sweetviz"] C2["Interactive → D-Tale"] C3["Validation → Great Expectations"] end style S fill:#E3F2FD style D fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Data Type Analysis
fields.description: Numeric vs categorical, date parsing, type coercion issues

steps.title: Why Types Matter
steps.explanation: Correct data types enable proper analysis. Wrong types cause errors or wrong results. **First step**: verify all columns have correct types.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problems" P1["Numbers as strings
→ Can't calculate"] P2["Dates as strings
→ Can't sort/diff"] P3["Categories as int
→ Wrong statistics"] end subgraph "Solution" S["Check and fix types
before analysis"] end P1 & P2 & P3 --> S style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Main Data Types
steps.explanation: **Numeric**: int, float (continuous or discrete). **Categorical**: object, category (nominal or ordinal). **Temporal**: datetime. **Boolean**: True/False.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Numeric"
        N1["int64: whole numbers"]
        N2["float64: decimals"]
    end
    subgraph "Categorical"
        C1["object: strings"]
        C2["category: efficient"]
        C3["bool: True/False"]
    end
    subgraph "Temporal"
        T1["datetime64"]
        T2["timedelta64"]
    end
    style N1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style T1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Checking Types
steps.explanation: Use **dtypes**, **info()**, and **select_dtypes()** to inspect. Look for mismatches: numbers stored as object, dates as string.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Inspection"
        D["df.dtypes"]
        I["df.info()"]
        S["df.select_dtypes(include='number')"]
    end
    subgraph "Red Flags"
        R1["Numeric column → object"]
        R2["Date column → object"]
        R3["ID column → int64"]
    end
    D & I --> R1 & R2 & R3
    style R1 fill:#FFEBEE

steps.title: Converting Types
steps.explanation: Use **astype()** for simple conversions. **to_numeric()**, **to_datetime()** handle errors. **category** for efficient categoricals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Conversion Methods" A["df['col'].astype('int64')"] N["pd.to_numeric(df['col'],
errors='coerce')"] D["pd.to_datetime(df['col'])"] C["df['col'].astype('category')"] end subgraph "errors='coerce'" E["Invalid → NaN
(useful for cleaning)"] end N --> E style N fill:#E8F5E9 style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Category Type Benefits
steps.explanation: **category** dtype: less memory, faster operations, preserves order (ordinal). Use for low-cardinality string columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Benefits" B1["Memory efficient"] B2["Faster groupby"] B3["Order preserved (ordinal)"] end subgraph "When to Use" W["Few unique values"] W2["Repeated values"] W3["Natural ordering"] end subgraph "Code" C["df['size'] = pd.Categorical(
df['size'],
categories=['S', 'M', 'L'],
ordered=True
)"] end B1 & B2 & B3 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Date Parsing Issues
steps.explanation: Dates are tricky: different formats, timezones, invalid values. Always specify **format** when known. Handle parsing errors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Issues" I1["Mixed formats"] I2["Invalid dates"] I3["Timezone confusion"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Specify format"] S2["errors='coerce'"] S3["tz_localize/convert"] end subgraph "Code" C["pd.to_datetime(df['date'],
format='%Y-%m-%d',
errors='coerce')"] end I1 --> S1 --> C style C fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Type Inference Pitfalls
steps.explanation: Pandas infers types on read. Can be wrong: IDs as int, zip codes losing leading zeros, mixed types. Always verify!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Pitfalls" P1["ID '001' → 1 (loses zero)"] P2["Zip '07001' → 7001"] P3["Mixed types → object"] end subgraph "Prevention" PR["pd.read_csv(file,
dtype={'id': str,
'zip': str})"] end P1 & P2 --> PR style PR fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Choosing the Right Chart
fields.description: Decision tree for chart selection based on data type and question

steps.title: Chart Selection Matters
steps.explanation: Right chart reveals insights. Wrong chart hides or misleads. Choose based on: **data type**, **question asked**, and **audience**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Consider" D["Data type"] Q["Question to answer"] A["Audience"] end subgraph "Goal" G["Clear, honest
communication"] end D & Q & A --> G style G fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: One Numeric Variable
steps.explanation: Show **distribution**. Use: **Histogram** (binned counts), **KDE** (smooth density), **Box plot** (summary stats).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Distribution Charts" H["Histogram
Shape, bins"] K["KDE
Smooth density"] B["Box plot
Summary + outliers"] end subgraph "When" W1["Histogram: default choice"] W2["KDE: smooth appearance"] W3["Box: compare groups"] end style H fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: One Categorical Variable
steps.explanation: Show **frequency/counts**. Use: **Bar chart** (counts per category), **Pie chart** (parts of whole—use sparingly!).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Categorical Charts" BA["Bar chart
Compare counts"] PI["Pie chart
Parts of whole"] end subgraph "Guidelines" G1["Bar: almost always better"] G2["Pie: only 2-5 categories"] G3["Order bars by value"] end style BA fill:#E8F5E9 style PI fill:#FFF3E0

steps.title: Two Numeric Variables
steps.explanation: Show **relationship**. Use: **Scatter plot** (individual points), **Line plot** (ordered/time data), **Hexbin** (many points).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Relationship Charts" SC["Scatter plot
Correlation, clusters"] LI["Line plot
Trends over time"] HX["Hexbin/2D hist
Many points"] end subgraph "When" W1["Scatter: < 10k points"] W2["Line: sequential data"] W3["Hexbin: overplotting"] end style SC fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Numeric by Category
steps.explanation: **Compare distributions** across groups. Use: **Grouped box plots**, **Violin plots**, **Bar + error bars** (means).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Comparison Charts"
        GB["Grouped box plots"]
        VI["Violin plots"]
        BE["Bar + error bars"]
    end
    subgraph "Choose"
        C1["Box: quick comparison"]
        C2["Violin: show full shape"]
        C3["Bar: means only"]
    end
    style GB fill:#E8F5E9
    style VI fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Many Variables
steps.explanation: Show **all relationships**. Use: **Pair plot** (all scatter plots), **Heatmap** (correlation matrix), **Parallel coordinates**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Multivariate Charts" PA["Pair plot
All pairs"] HM["Heatmap
Correlation matrix"] PC["Parallel coordinates
Row patterns"] end subgraph "When" W1["Pair: < 8 variables"] W2["Heatmap: many variables"] W3["Parallel: cluster patterns"] end style PA fill:#E8F5E9 style HM fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Decision Flowchart
steps.explanation: Quick reference: How many variables? What types? What question? Follow the flow to choose.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{"How many
variables?"} Q1 -->|1| Q2{"Type?"} Q2 -->|Numeric| H["Histogram/Box"] Q2 -->|Category| B["Bar chart"] Q1 -->|2| Q3{"Types?"} Q3 -->|Both numeric| SC["Scatter plot"] Q3 -->|Num + Cat| GB["Grouped box"] Q3 -->|Both cat| HM["Heatmap/Mosaic"] Q1 -->|Many| PA["Pair plot/Heatmap"] style H fill:#E8F5E9 style SC fill:#E8F5E9 style GB fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: EDA with Pandas
fields.description: Key pandas methods: describe(), value_counts(), corr(), plot()

steps.title: Pandas EDA Toolkit
steps.explanation: Pandas has everything for EDA built-in. Learn key methods and you can explore any dataset quickly and effectively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Core EDA Methods"
        I["info(), dtypes"]
        D["describe()"]
        V["value_counts()"]
        C["corr()"]
        P["plot()"]
    end
    style I fill:#E8F5E9
    style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: First Look Methods
steps.explanation: **shape**, **head()**, **info()**, **dtypes**. Get oriented with size, columns, types, memory usage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Structure" SH["df.shape
(rows, cols)"] HE["df.head()
First 5 rows"] TA["df.tail()
Last 5 rows"] end subgraph "Types & Memory" IN["df.info()
Types + non-null"] DT["df.dtypes
Just types"] end SH --> HE --> IN style IN fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: describe() Deep Dive
steps.explanation: **describe()** gives count, mean, std, min, quartiles, max. Use **include** parameter for non-numeric columns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Basic" D1["df.describe()
Numeric only"] end subgraph "Include All" D2["df.describe(include='all')
All columns"] end subgraph "Specific Types" D3["df.describe(include='object')
Strings only"] end D1 --> D2 --> D3 style D2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: value_counts() Power
steps.explanation: **value_counts()** shows frequency of each value. Essential for categorical columns. Options: normalize, bins for numeric.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Basic" V1["df['col'].value_counts()"] end subgraph "Options" V2["normalize=True
Percentages"] V3["dropna=False
Include NaN"] V4["bins=5
Bin numeric"] end V1 --> V2 & V3 & V4 style V1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Missing Value Methods
steps.explanation: **isnull()**, **isna()** detect missing. Chain with **sum()** or **mean()** for counts/percentages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Detection" M1["df.isnull().sum()
Count per column"] M2["df.isnull().mean() * 100
Percent missing"] M3["df.isnull().any()
Any missing?"] end subgraph "Handling" H1["df.dropna()"] H2["df.fillna(value)"] end M1 --> M2 style M1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Correlation & GroupBy
steps.explanation: **corr()** for correlation matrix. **groupby()** for group statistics. Essential for relationship analysis.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correlation" C1["df.corr()
All pairs"] C2["df['a'].corr(df['b'])
Two columns"] end subgraph "GroupBy" G1["df.groupby('cat')['val'].mean()"] G2["df.groupby('cat').agg(['mean', 'std'])"] end style C1 fill:#E8F5E9 style G2 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Quick Plotting
steps.explanation: **plot()** method for quick visualizations. Histogram, box, scatter, line—all built into pandas.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Plot Methods"
        P1["df['col'].plot.hist()"]
        P2["df['col'].plot.box()"]
        P3["df.plot.scatter(x='a', y='b')"]
        P4["df['col'].plot.line()"]
    end
    subgraph "Or"
        A["df.plot(kind='hist')"]
    end
    P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 --> A
    style P1 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Grammar of Graphics
fields.description: Understanding the layered grammar that underpins ggplot2, plotnine, and modern visualization libraries.

steps.title: The Grammar of Graphics
steps.explanation: The **Grammar of Graphics** (Wilkinson, 2005) decomposes visualizations into independent components that can be combined. This enables systematic chart creation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Grammar Layers" D["📊 DATA
The dataset"] A["🎨 AESTHETICS
Map variables to visuals"] G["📐 GEOMETRIES
Visual marks (points, bars, lines)"] S["📈 STATISTICS
Transformations (binning, smoothing)"] C["🗺️ COORDINATES
Cartesian, polar, geo"] F["📋 FACETS
Small multiples"] T["🎯 THEME
Non-data styling"] end D --> A --> G --> S --> C --> F --> T style D fill:#BBDEFB style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Data Layer
steps.explanation: **Data** is the foundation. A tidy dataframe where each row is an observation and each column is a variable. The data layer feeds all other layers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Tidy Data Principles" R["Each ROW = one observation"] C["Each COLUMN = one variable"] V["Each CELL = one value"] end subgraph "Example" T["| species | bill_length | body_mass |
| Adelie | 39.1 | 3750 |
| Gentoo | 46.1 | 5200 |"] end R & C & V --> T style T fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Aesthetics Layer
steps.explanation: **Aesthetics** map data variables to visual properties: x, y positions, color, size, shape, alpha (transparency). This is where data becomes visual.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Data Variables"
        V1["bill_length"]
        V2["body_mass"]
        V3["species"]
        V4["island"]
    end
    subgraph "Visual Channels"
        A1["x position"]
        A2["y position"]
        A3["color"]
        A4["shape"]
    end
    V1 --> A1
    V2 --> A2
    V3 --> A3
    V4 --> A4
    style A3 fill:#FFCDD2
    style A4 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Geometries Layer
steps.explanation: **Geometries (geoms)** are the visual marks representing data points. Each geom has required and optional aesthetics.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Geoms" P["geom_point()
Scatter plots"] L["geom_line()
Line charts"] B["geom_bar()
Bar charts"] H["geom_histogram()
Distributions"] BX["geom_boxplot()
Box plots"] A["geom_area()
Area charts"] end subgraph "Required Aesthetics" P --> PA["x, y"] L --> LA["x, y"] B --> BA["x (y computed)"] end style P fill:#E8F5E9 style L fill:#E8F5E9 style B fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Statistics Layer
steps.explanation: **Statistics** transform raw data before plotting. Examples: binning for histograms, smoothing for trend lines, counting for bar charts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Raw Data" D["Individual values"] end subgraph "Statistical Transform" S1["stat_bin
→ histogram counts"] S2["stat_smooth
→ trend line"] S3["stat_count
→ bar heights"] S4["stat_boxplot
→ quartiles"] end subgraph "Geom" G["Visual output"] end D --> S1 & S2 & S3 & S4 --> G style S2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Coordinates & Facets
steps.explanation: **Coordinates** define the plotting space. **Facets** create small multiples—same chart repeated for subsets of data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Coordinate Systems" C1["coord_cartesian
Standard x-y"] C2["coord_polar
Pie/radar charts"] C3["coord_flip
Swap x and y"] C4["coord_map
Geographic"] end subgraph "Faceting" F1["facet_wrap(~var)
Wrap by one variable"] F2["facet_grid(row~col)
Grid by two variables"] end style C1 fill:#E8F5E9 style F1 fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: Putting it all together: data + aesthetics + geom + statistics + coordinates + facets + theme = complete visualization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code Structure" C["ggplot(data=penguins) +
aes(x=bill_length, y=body_mass, color=species) +
geom_point() +
geom_smooth(method='lm') +
facet_wrap(~island) +
theme_minimal()"] end subgraph "Layer Breakdown" L1["DATA: penguins df"] L2["AES: x, y, color mapped"] L3["GEOM: points + smooth line"] L4["FACET: by island"] L5["THEME: minimal styling"] end C --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 style C fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Chart Type Decision Tree
fields.description: A flowchart guide for selecting the right visualization based on your data and goals.

steps.title: Start: What's Your Goal?
steps.explanation: Chart selection starts with your **communication goal**. Are you comparing values, showing trends, distributions, relationships, or composition?
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB START["🎯 What do you want to show?"] START --> COMP["📊 Comparison
Compare values across categories"] START --> TREND["📈 Trend
Show change over time"] START --> DIST["📉 Distribution
Show spread of values"] START --> REL["🔗 Relationship
Show correlation"] START --> PART["🥧 Composition
Show parts of a whole"] style START fill:#BBDEFB style COMP fill:#C8E6C9 style TREND fill:#FFF9C4 style DIST fill:#FFCCBC style REL fill:#E1BEE7 style PART fill:#B2EBF2

steps.title: Comparison Charts
steps.explanation: For **comparing values** across categories: use bar charts (few categories), grouped bars (comparing groups), or dot plots (many categories).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB COMP["📊 COMPARISON"] COMP --> FEW{"How many
categories?"} FEW -->|"< 10"| BAR["Bar Chart
✅ Clear, familiar"] FEW -->|"10-30"| DOT["Dot Plot / Lollipop
✅ Less ink, cleaner"] FEW -->|"> 30"| TABLE["Consider a table
or highlight key items"] COMP --> GROUPS{"Comparing
groups?"} GROUPS -->|"2-4 groups"| GROUPED["Grouped Bar Chart"] GROUPS -->|"Many groups"| FACET["Small Multiples"] style BAR fill:#C8E6C9 style DOT fill:#C8E6C9 style GROUPED fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Trend Charts
steps.explanation: For **showing trends over time**: line charts for continuous data, area charts for cumulative totals, bar charts for discrete periods.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB TREND["📈 TREND OVER TIME"] TREND --> CONT{"Continuous
or discrete?"} CONT -->|"Continuous"| LINE["Line Chart
✅ Best for trends"] CONT -->|"Discrete periods"| TBAR["Bar Chart
✅ Clear boundaries"] LINE --> MULTI{"Multiple
series?"} MULTI -->|"2-5 series"| MLINE["Multi-line Chart"] MULTI -->|"> 5 series"| SM["Small Multiples
or highlight key series"] LINE --> STACK{"Show
cumulative?"} STACK -->|"Yes"| AREA["Stacked Area"] style LINE fill:#FFF9C4 style MLINE fill:#FFF9C4 style AREA fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Distribution Charts
steps.explanation: For **showing distributions**: histograms for one variable, box plots for comparisons, violin plots for detailed shape.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB DIST["📉 DISTRIBUTION"] DIST --> ONE{"One variable
or compare?"} ONE -->|"One variable"| HIST["Histogram
✅ Shows frequency"] ONE -->|"One, smooth"| KDE["KDE / Density
✅ Smooth shape"] ONE -->|"Compare groups"| CMP{"How many
groups?"} CMP -->|"2-7 groups"| BOX["Box Plot
✅ Compact summary"] CMP -->|"Show shape"| VIOLIN["Violin Plot
✅ Distribution shape"] CMP -->|"Many groups"| RIDGE["Ridgeline Plot"] style HIST fill:#FFCCBC style BOX fill:#FFCCBC style VIOLIN fill:#FFCCBC

steps.title: Relationship Charts
steps.explanation: For **showing relationships**: scatter plots for two variables, bubble charts for three, heatmaps for correlation matrices.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB REL["🔗 RELATIONSHIP"] REL --> VARS{"How many
variables?"} VARS -->|"2 numeric"| SCATTER["Scatter Plot
✅ Classic correlation"] VARS -->|"3 numeric"| BUBBLE["Bubble Chart
Size = 3rd variable"] VARS -->|"2 + category"| SCAT_COL["Scatter + Color
Color by category"] REL --> MANY{"Many variable
pairs?"} MANY -->|"Correlation matrix"| HEAT["Heatmap
✅ Overview of all pairs"] MANY -->|"Pairwise plots"| PAIR["Pair Plot / SPLOM"] style SCATTER fill:#E1BEE7 style HEAT fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Composition Charts
steps.explanation: For **showing parts of a whole**: pie charts (few parts, one time), stacked bars (compare over time), treemaps (hierarchical).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB PART["🥧 COMPOSITION"] PART --> TIME{"Over time
or static?"} TIME -->|"Static, one point"| STATIC{"How many
parts?"} STATIC -->|"2-5 parts"| PIE["Pie / Donut
✅ Intuitive"] STATIC -->|"> 5 parts"| TREE["Treemap
✅ Many categories"] TIME -->|"Over time"| CHANGE{"Relative or
absolute?"} CHANGE -->|"Relative %"| STACK100["100% Stacked Bar"] CHANGE -->|"Absolute"| STACKBAR["Stacked Bar"] CHANGE -->|"Show flow"| SANKEY["Sankey Diagram"] style PIE fill:#B2EBF2 style TREE fill:#B2EBF2 style STACK100 fill:#B2EBF2

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Summary decision matrix: match your goal and data type to the right chart.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Goal → Chart"
        C1["Compare categories → Bar Chart"]
        C2["Trend over time → Line Chart"]
        C3["Distribution → Histogram / Box Plot"]
        C4["Relationship → Scatter Plot"]
        C5["Part of whole → Pie / Stacked Bar"]
        C6["Geographic → Map / Choropleth"]
        C7["Ranking → Bar Chart (sorted)"]
        C8["Flow/process → Sankey / Flowchart"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9
    style C4 fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: Chart Anatomy
fields.description: The essential components of a well-designed chart: axes, labels, legends, titles, and annotations.

steps.title: Chart Components Overview
steps.explanation: A well-designed chart has multiple components working together. Each element has a specific purpose in communicating data effectively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Chart Components" T["📝 TITLE
What the chart shows"] S["📝 SUBTITLE
Additional context"] P["📊 PLOT AREA
Where data lives"] X["↔️ X-AXIS
Horizontal scale"] Y["↕️ Y-AXIS
Vertical scale"] L["🎨 LEGEND
Decode colors/shapes"] A["💬 ANNOTATIONS
Call out insights"] G["📏 GRIDLINES
Reading aid"] SRC["📌 SOURCE
Data attribution"] end T --> P X --> P Y --> P L --> P style T fill:#BBDEFB style P fill:#C8E6C9 style L fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Title & Subtitle
steps.explanation: The **title** should be descriptive and convey the main insight. The **subtitle** adds context, source, or methodology notes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good Titles" G1["✅ 'Sales doubled after
product launch'
(Insight-driven)"] G2["✅ 'Monthly revenue by region,
2020-2024'
(Descriptive)"] end subgraph "Bad Titles" B1["❌ 'Chart 1'
(Meaningless)"] B2["❌ 'Sales'
(Too vague)"] end subgraph "Subtitle Uses" S1["Data source"] S2["Date range"] S3["Units of measure"] S4["Methodology notes"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style G2 fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#FFCDD2 style B2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Axes & Labels
steps.explanation: **Axes** define the scale and range. Always label axes with units. Start y-axis at zero for bar charts (usually).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Axis Best Practices" A1["✅ Label both axes"] A2["✅ Include units (%, $, kg)"] A3["✅ Use readable tick intervals"] A4["✅ Consider axis breaks carefully"] end subgraph "Common Mistakes" M1["❌ Truncated y-axis
exaggerates differences"] M2["❌ Missing units
unclear meaning"] M3["❌ Too many tick marks
visual clutter"] M4["❌ Rotated labels
hard to read"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style M1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Legends
steps.explanation: **Legends** decode visual encodings (colors, shapes, sizes). Place near the data. Consider direct labeling instead.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Legend Placement"
        P1["Top: Good for horizontal space"]
        P2["Right: Traditional, works well"]
        P3["Inside plot: Saves space"]
        P4["Direct labels: Best! No lookup needed"]
    end
    subgraph "Legend Tips"
        T1["✅ Order matches data"]
        T2["✅ Short, clear labels"]
        T3["✅ Visible symbol size"]
        T4["❌ Avoid legends for 1 series"]
    end
    style P4 fill:#C8E6C9
    style T4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Gridlines & Reference Lines
steps.explanation: **Gridlines** help readers trace values. Use sparingly—too many create visual noise. Reference lines highlight benchmarks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Gridline Guidelines"
        G1["✅ Light gray, subtle"]
        G2["✅ Major gridlines only"]
        G3["✅ Match important tick marks"]
        G4["❌ Dark or thick gridlines"]
        G5["❌ Both major AND minor"]
    end
    subgraph "Reference Lines"
        R1["Average / Mean line"]
        R2["Target / Goal line"]
        R3["Baseline (e.g., zero)"]
        R4["Threshold (e.g., limit)"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Annotations
steps.explanation: **Annotations** guide attention to key insights. Use sparingly and purposefully—highlight what matters most.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Annotation Types" A1["📍 Point labels
Specific values"] A2["📝 Text callouts
Explain anomalies"] A3["🔲 Highlight regions
Shade important areas"] A4["➡️ Arrows
Point to features"] end subgraph "When to Annotate" W1["Peak or trough values"] W2["Events (launches, crises)"] W3["Outliers or anomalies"] W4["Key comparisons"] end style A2 fill:#FFF9C4 style W2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Complete Anatomy
steps.explanation: All components working together create a clear, informative visualization that tells a story at a glance.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Complete Chart"
        T["TITLE: Sales Growth Accelerated After Launch"]
        ST["Subtitle: Monthly revenue, Jan 2023 - Dec 2024"]
        PLOT["[Plot Area with Data]"]
        XL["X-Axis: Month"]
        YL["Y-Axis: Revenue ($M)"]
        LEG["Legend: Product A, Product B"]
        ANN["Annotation: 'Launch date →'"]
        SRC["Source: Internal sales data"]
    end
    T --> ST --> PLOT
    XL --> PLOT
    YL --> PLOT
    LEG --> PLOT
    ANN --> PLOT
    PLOT --> SRC
    style T fill:#BBDEFB
    style PLOT fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Coordinate Systems
fields.description: Understanding Cartesian, polar, and geographic coordinate systems for different visualization needs.

steps.title: Why Coordinate Systems Matter
steps.explanation: The **coordinate system** defines how data positions map to screen positions. Different systems suit different data types and visualization goals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Coordinate Systems" CART["📐 CARTESIAN
x-y grid, most common"] POLAR["🎯 POLAR
Angle + radius"] GEO["🌍 GEOGRAPHIC
Lat/lon projections"] PAR["📊 PARALLEL
High-dimensional data"] end subgraph "Choose Based On" D1["Data type"] D2["Relationships to show"] D3["Audience familiarity"] end CART --> D1 style CART fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Cartesian Coordinates
steps.explanation: **Cartesian (rectangular)** coordinates use perpendicular x and y axes. The most intuitive and widely used system for most charts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Cartesian Properties"
        P1["Perpendicular x-y axes"]
        P2["Linear or log scale"]
        P3["Easy to read exact values"]
        P4["Natural for time series"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Line charts"]
        B2["Bar charts"]
        B3["Scatter plots"]
        B4["Heatmaps"]
    end
    subgraph "Variations"
        V1["coord_flip: Swap x/y"]
        V2["coord_fixed: Equal aspect ratio"]
        V3["coord_trans: Log or other transform"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Polar Coordinates
steps.explanation: **Polar** coordinates use angle (theta) and radius. Good for cyclical data (time of day, seasons) and part-to-whole relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Polar Properties" P1["Position = angle + distance"] P2["Natural for cycles"] P3["Can distort perception"] P4["Pie = bar in polar coords"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["🥧 Pie/donut charts
(bar → polar)"] U2["🕐 Clock/time visualizations"] U3["🧭 Radar/spider charts"] U4["🌹 Rose diagrams (wind)"] end subgraph "Caution" C1["⚠️ Angles harder to compare
than lengths"] C2["⚠️ Area distortion in
outer rings"] end style U1 fill:#FFF9C4 style C1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Geographic Projections
steps.explanation: **Geographic** coordinates project the 3D Earth onto 2D. Different projections preserve different properties (area, shape, distance).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common Projections" P1["Mercator
Preserves shape, distorts area"] P2["Equal-area (Albers)
Preserves area, distorts shape"] P3["Equidistant
Preserves distance from center"] P4["Robinson
Compromise, good for world maps"] end subgraph "Mercator Problem" M["Greenland appears same
size as Africa
(Africa is 14x larger!)"] end subgraph "Choose Based On" C1["Choropleth → Equal-area"] C2["Navigation → Mercator"] C3["General display → Robinson"] end style P2 fill:#C8E6C9 style M fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Parallel Coordinates
steps.explanation: **Parallel coordinates** handle high-dimensional data by placing axes side by side. Each observation is a line crossing all axes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR subgraph "Structure" A1["Axis 1
Var 1"] A2["Axis 2
Var 2"] A3["Axis 3
Var 3"] A4["Axis 4
Var 4"] end A1 ---|"line per
observation"| A2 A2 --- A3 A3 --- A4 subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Multivariate exploration"] U2["Cluster identification"] U3["Outlier detection"] end style A1 fill:#E1BEE7 style A2 fill:#E1BEE7 style A3 fill:#E1BEE7 style A4 fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Coordinate Transformations
steps.explanation: **Transformations** change how values map to positions. Log scale for exponential data, sqrt for counts, reversed for rankings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Scale Transforms" L["Log scale
Exponential growth,
wide value ranges"] S["Sqrt scale
Count data,
reduces skew"] R["Reverse scale
Rankings (1st at top)"] end subgraph "When to Use Log" W1["Data spans orders of magnitude"] W2["Multiplicative relationships"] W3["Exponential trends"] end subgraph "Caution" C["⚠️ Log scale can mislead
if audience expects linear"] end style L fill:#FFF9C4 style C fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Choosing the Right System
steps.explanation: Match coordinate system to your data and message. Default to Cartesian; use others when they provide clear benefits.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Decision Guide"
        Q1{"Data type?"}
        Q1 -->|"Most data"| CART["Cartesian ✓"]
        Q1 -->|"Cyclical (hours, seasons)"| POLAR["Polar"]
        Q1 -->|"Geographic locations"| GEO["Map projection"]
        Q1 -->|"Many dimensions"| PAR["Parallel coordinates"]

        Q2{"Part-to-whole?"}
        Q2 -->|"Few parts"| PIE["Polar (pie)"]
        Q2 -->|"Compare changes"| STACK["Cartesian (stacked bar)"]
    end
    style CART fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Bar Chart Variants
fields.description: Grouped, stacked, normalized, and horizontal bar charts for different comparison needs.

steps.title: Bar Chart Overview
steps.explanation: **Bar charts** are the workhorse of data visualization. Different variants serve different comparison goals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Bar Chart Family" S["Simple Bar
One category"] G["Grouped Bar
Compare groups"] ST["Stacked Bar
Show composition"] N["100% Stacked
Compare proportions"] H["Horizontal Bar
Long labels"] L["Lollipop
Less ink"] end subgraph "Key Rule" R["✅ Always start y-axis at zero
(bar length = value)"] end style S fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Simple Bar Chart
steps.explanation: The **simple bar chart** compares values across one categorical variable. Sort bars by value for easier comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Simple Bar Best Practices"
        B1["✅ Sort by value (usually desc)"]
        B2["✅ Start axis at zero"]
        B3["✅ Consistent bar width"]
        B4["✅ Direct labels if few bars"]
        B5["✅ Single color (or highlight one)"]
    end
    subgraph "When to Use"
        W1["Compare values across categories"]
        W2["Rankings"]
        W3["Single time point"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Grouped Bar Chart
steps.explanation: **Grouped (clustered) bars** compare multiple series across categories. Best for 2-4 groups; more becomes cluttered.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Grouped Bar Structure" direction LR G1["Group A
■ ■ ■"] G2["Group B
■ ■ ■"] G3["Group C
■ ■ ■"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["✅ Limit to 2-4 series"] B2["✅ Use color for series"] B3["✅ Legend near data"] B4["❌ Too many groups = confusing"] end subgraph "Comparison Focus" C1["Within group: Easy"] C2["Across groups: Harder"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B4 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Stacked Bar Chart
steps.explanation: **Stacked bars** show composition within categories. Good for part-to-whole when total matters. Hard to compare middle segments.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Stacked Bar" S["Category A: ■■■■
Category B: ■■■■■■
Category C: ■■■"] end subgraph "Pros" P1["✅ Shows total AND parts"] P2["✅ Part-to-whole within category"] P3["✅ Good for 2-4 segments"] end subgraph "Cons" C1["❌ Hard to compare non-bottom segments"] C2["❌ Middle segments have no common baseline"] C3["⚠️ Order segments by importance"] end style P1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: 100% Stacked Bar
steps.explanation: **Normalized (100%) stacked bars** focus on proportions, not absolute values. Great for comparing composition across categories.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "100% Stacked" S["Category A: 25% 50% 25%
Category B: 33% 33% 33%
Category C: 10% 60% 30%"] end subgraph "When to Use" W1["Compare proportions"] W2["Composition over time"] W3["Survey responses"] end subgraph "Limitations" L1["❌ Loses absolute values"] L2["✅ Add total labels if needed"] end style W1 fill:#C8E6C9 style L1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Horizontal Bar Chart
steps.explanation: **Horizontal bars** work better when category labels are long or when showing rankings (highest at top).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "When to Use Horizontal"
        W1["Long category labels"]
        W2["Many categories > 10"]
        W3["Rankings top to bottom"]
        W4["Gantt charts"]
    end
    subgraph "Orientation Rule"
        R1["Labels longer than bars? → Horizontal"]
        R2["Time dimension? → Vertical x-axis"]
    end
    style W1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style R1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Variant Selection Guide
steps.explanation: Choose the bar chart variant based on what comparison you need to make.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"What to compare?"}
    Q1 -->|"Single values"| SIMPLE["Simple Bar"]
    Q1 -->|"Groups side-by-side"| GROUPED["Grouped Bar"]
    Q1 -->|"Part-to-whole + total"| STACKED["Stacked Bar"]
    Q1 -->|"Just proportions"| NORM["100% Stacked"]

    Q2{"Long labels?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| HORIZ["→ Make it horizontal"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| VERT["→ Keep vertical"]

    Q3{"Many categories?"}
    Q3 -->|">15"| LOLLIPOP["Consider Lollipop"]

    style SIMPLE fill:#C8E6C9
    style GROUPED fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Pie vs Donut vs Treemap
fields.description: Part-to-whole visualizations: when to use each and common perception issues.

steps.title: Part-to-Whole Visualizations
steps.explanation: These charts show how parts contribute to a whole. Each has different strengths and perception challenges.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Part-to-Whole Options" PIE["PIE CHART
Simple, familiar"] DON["DONUT CHART
Cleaner center"] TREE["TREEMAP
Many categories"] WAF["WAFFLE CHART
Precise percentages"] end subgraph "Key Question" Q["Do parts sum to 100%?
If not, don't use these!"] end PIE --> Q DON --> Q TREE --> Q style Q fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Pie Chart Basics
steps.explanation: **Pie charts** are intuitive for simple part-to-whole. Limited to 2-5 slices. Humans are bad at comparing angles.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Pie Chart Rules"
        R1["✅ Limit to 2-5 slices"]
        R2["✅ Start at 12 o'clock"]
        R3["✅ Order by size largest first"]
        R4["✅ Use direct labels not legend"]
        R5["✅ Slices must sum to 100%"]
    end
    subgraph "Problems"
        P1["❌ Angles hard to compare"]
        P2["❌ 3D pie charts distort"]
        P3["❌ Many slices unreadable"]
        P4["❌ Cannot compare across pies"]
    end
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style P2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Angle Perception Problem
steps.explanation: Humans struggle to accurately compare **angles and areas**. We perceive them differently than lengths.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Perception Accuracy" L["1. LENGTH most accurate"] P["2. Position along scale"] A["3. Angle less accurate"] AR["4. Area least accurate"] end subgraph "The Problem" EX["25% vs 30% slice
Very hard to distinguish!

Bar chart: Easy to see"] end subgraph "When Pie Works" W1["Few slices 2-3"] W2["One dominant category"] W3["Exact values labeled"] end style L fill:#C8E6C9 style AR fill:#FFCDD2 style EX fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Donut Chart
steps.explanation: **Donut charts** are pies with a hole. The center can display totals or labels. Same angle perception issues apply.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Donut Advantages" A1["Center space for text/number"] A2["Looks more modern"] A3["Can nest multiple rings"] end subgraph "Donut Disadvantages" D1["Same angle perception issues"] D2["Even harder with thin rings"] D3["Arc length varies by radius"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Use center for:
Total value or Main label"] end style B fill:#C8E6C9 style D1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Treemap
steps.explanation: **Treemaps** use nested rectangles to show hierarchy and size. Great for many categories that pie charts can't handle.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Treemap Structure" T["Rectangles sized by value
Nested for hierarchy
Color for category/metric"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["✅ Handles many categories"] A2["✅ Shows hierarchy"] A3["✅ Efficient use of space"] A4["✅ Area easier than angles"] end subgraph "Disadvantages" D1["❌ Hard to compare non-adjacent"] D2["❌ Small rectangles hard to label"] D3["❌ Shape varies tall vs wide"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style A2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Waffle Chart
steps.explanation: **Waffle charts** use a 10x10 grid of squares. Each square = 1%. Very accurate for showing percentages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Waffle Chart" W["100 squares = 100%
Each square = 1%
Easy to count"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["✅ Easy to read exact %"] A2["✅ Compare multiple categories"] A3["✅ Works for icon arrays too"] end subgraph "Best For" B1["Survey results 73% agree"] B2["Proportion of population"] B3["Goal progress 75% complete"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Selection Guide
steps.explanation: Choose based on number of categories, precision needs, and whether you're showing hierarchy.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{"How many parts?"} Q1 -->|"2-3"| PIE["Pie/Donut OK"] Q1 -->|"4-6"| BAR["Bar chart better"] Q1 -->|">6"| TREE["Treemap"] Q2{"Need exact %?"} Q2 -->|"Yes"| WAFFLE["Waffle chart"] Q2 -->|"General sense"| PIE2["Pie/Donut OK"] Q3{"Hierarchical?"} Q3 -->|"Yes"| TREE2["Treemap"] Q3 -->|"No"| OTHER["Pie/Bar"] subgraph "Default Advice" D["When in doubt,
use a bar chart instead!"] end style D fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Waffle & Icon Charts
fields.description: Grid-based visualizations for accurate part-to-whole representation.

steps.title: Grid-Based Visualizations
steps.explanation: **Waffle and icon charts** represent data using countable units. Each unit represents a fixed amount, making percentages easy to grasp.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Grid Chart Types" W["WAFFLE CHART
100 squares = 100%"] I["ICON ARRAY
Pictograms represent units"] U["UNIT CHART
Symbols for counts"] end subgraph "Key Benefit" B["Countable = Accurate
No area/angle distortion"] end style B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Waffle Chart Basics
steps.explanation: A **waffle chart** is typically a 10x10 grid. Each cell = 1%. Colored cells show the proportion.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Waffle Structure" G["10 x 10 Grid
= 100 cells
= 100%"] end subgraph "Example: 73%" E["73 colored cells
27 empty/gray cells

Instantly readable!"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["✅ Round to whole numbers"] B2["✅ Use consistent colors"] B3["✅ Add percentage label"] B4["✅ Fill top-to-bottom or L-to-R"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9 style B4 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Icon Arrays Pictograms
steps.explanation: **Icon arrays** use symbols (people, objects) to represent units. Each icon = a fixed count. Very intuitive for general audiences.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Icon Array Example" I["People icons in grid
Each person = 10,000 people

Highlighted = affected group"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["✅ Instantly relatable"] A2["✅ Works for non-technical audiences"] A3["✅ Great for risk communication"] A4["✅ Memorable ISOTYPE legacy"] end subgraph "Guidelines" G1["1 icon = round number"] G2["Show scale in legend"] G3["Use simple recognizable icons"] end style I fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Comparing Proportions
steps.explanation: Multiple waffle charts or icon arrays can compare proportions across groups—more accurately than multiple pies.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Multi-Waffle Comparison"
        W1["Group A: 45%"]
        W2["Group B: 72%"]
        W3["Group C: 23%"]
    end
    subgraph "Why Better Than Pies"
        B1["Same baseline 100 units"]
        B2["Count cells to compare"]
        B3["No angle distortion"]
    end
    style W2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Risk Communication
steps.explanation: Icon arrays excel at **risk communication**. 'X out of 100' is more intuitive than '25% chance.'
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Medical Example" M["'25% chance of side effect'
vs
'25 out of 100 patients
experience side effects'"] end subgraph "Research Shows" R1["Icon arrays improve
risk understanding"] R2["Especially for low-numeracy
audiences"] end style M fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Design Considerations
steps.explanation: Careful design ensures waffle and icon charts communicate clearly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Design"
        G1["✅ Clear value labels"]
        G2["✅ Obvious unit scale"]
        G3["✅ High contrast colors"]
        G4["✅ Logical fill direction"]
        G5["✅ Consistent icon size"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Too many colors"]
        A2["❌ Complex icons"]
        A3["❌ Fractional icons"]
        A4["❌ Random fill patterns"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: When to Use
steps.explanation: Waffle and icon charts shine in specific scenarios.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Best Use Cases" U1["Showing percentages"] U2["Risk/probability communication"] U3["Part-to-whole comparisons"] U4["Public-facing dashboards"] U5["Replacing pie charts"] end subgraph "Not Ideal For" N1["Precise values use bar"] N2["Time series use line"] N3["Many categories > 4"] N4["Very small percentages < 1%"] end subgraph "Tools" T["matplotlib, seaborn, plotly,
d3.js, Tableau"] end style U1 fill:#C8E6C9 style U2 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Line Chart Best Practices
fields.description: Time series visualization, multiple series, and avoiding dual-axis pitfalls.

steps.title: Line Chart Fundamentals
steps.explanation: **Line charts** show trends over time or continuous variables. The slope conveys rate of change; position shows absolute value.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Line Chart Anatomy"
        L["Lines connect data points"]
        T["X-axis: usually time"]
        V["Y-axis: measured value"]
        S["Slope = rate of change"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Time series data"]
        B2["Trend visualization"]
        B3["Rate of change"]
        B4["Continuous x-axis"]
    end
    style L fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Single Series Best Practices
steps.explanation: For a single line: keep it simple. Avoid markers unless highlighting specific points. Use consistent intervals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Practices"
        G1["✅ Clear axis labels with units"]
        G2["✅ Meaningful title"]
        G3["✅ Consistent time intervals"]
        G4["✅ Start y at zero (usually)"]
        G5["✅ Annotate key events"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Too many markers"]
        A2["❌ 3D effects"]
        A3["❌ Misleading truncated y-axis"]
        A4["❌ Excessive gridlines"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Multiple Series
steps.explanation: When comparing multiple lines, limit to 4-5 series. Use distinct colors and consider direct labeling.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Multiple Line Guidelines"
        M1["✅ Limit to 4-5 lines"]
        M2["✅ Use distinct colors"]
        M3["✅ Direct label lines at end"]
        M4["✅ Highlight most important"]
        M5["✅ Consider small multiples"]
    end
    subgraph "Too Many Lines?"
        T1["Use small multiples/facets"]
        T2["Interactive highlighting"]
        T3["Show just key comparisons"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style T1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Dual Axis Dangers
steps.explanation: **Dual y-axes** are often misleading. The relationship between scales is arbitrary and can imply false correlations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Dual Axis Problems"
        P1["❌ Scale relationship is arbitrary"]
        P2["❌ Easy to manipulate perception"]
        P3["❌ Implies correlation where none exists"]
        P4["❌ Confusing for readers"]
    end
    subgraph "Better Alternatives"
        A1["✅ Two separate charts stacked"]
        A2["✅ Index both series to 100"]
        A3["✅ Percent change chart"]
        A4["✅ Difference/ratio chart"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Aspect Ratio Matters
steps.explanation: The **aspect ratio** (width:height) affects perception of slope. Banking to 45 degrees is a classic rule.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Aspect Ratio Effect" W["Wide chart = flatter slopes"] T["Tall chart = steeper slopes"] B["Balanced = slopes near 45 deg"] end subgraph "Guidelines" G1["Bank to 45 degrees"] G2["Match importance of pattern"] G3["Time series: often 3:1 to 4:1"] end subgraph "Cleveland's Rule" C["Average absolute slope
should be around 45 degrees"] end style B fill:#C8E6C9 style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Handling Missing Data
steps.explanation: Missing values in time series need clear treatment. Don't just connect across gaps—make them visible.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Missing Data Options"
        O1["Break the line (honest)"]
        O2["Dashed line through gap"]
        O3["Interpolate with note"]
        O4["Show NA markers"]
    end
    subgraph "Never Do"
        N1["❌ Connect across gaps silently"]
        N2["❌ Fill with zero"]
        N3["❌ Ignore without disclosure"]
    end
    style O1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Line vs Area vs Bar
steps.explanation: Choose line, area, or bar based on what you want to emphasize.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "When to Use What" LINE["LINE: Trend, rate of change,
compare series"] AREA["AREA: Cumulative totals,
magnitude over time"] BAR["BAR: Discrete periods,
individual values matter"] end subgraph "Decision" D1{"Continuous time?"} D1 -->|"Yes"| LINE D1 -->|"No/Discrete"| BAR D2{"Show volume/magnitude?"} D2 -->|"Yes"| AREA end style LINE fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Area Charts
fields.description: Stacked areas, streamgraphs, and when to choose area over line charts.

steps.title: Area Chart Types
steps.explanation: **Area charts** fill the space between a line and the axis. They emphasize magnitude and volume over time.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Area Chart Variants" S["SIMPLE AREA
One series, filled"] ST["STACKED AREA
Multiple series, cumulative"] N["100% STACKED
Proportions over time"] SM["STREAMGRAPH
Symmetric flow"] end subgraph "Key Difference vs Line" D["Area emphasizes MAGNITUDE
Line emphasizes TREND"] end style S fill:#C8E6C9 style D fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Simple Area Chart
steps.explanation: A single series with fill. Good for emphasizing volume or cumulative values. Same rules as line charts apply.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "When to Use" W1["Emphasize magnitude"] W2["Show cumulative total"] W3["Single series only"] end subgraph "Design Tips" T1["✅ Use light fill color"] T2["✅ Add line on top edge"] T3["✅ Start y-axis at zero"] T4["✅ Semi-transparent fill"] end subgraph "Caution" C["Area exaggerates differences
compared to line chart"] end style W1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Stacked Area Chart
steps.explanation: **Stacked areas** show part-to-whole over time. Each band represents a category; total height is the sum.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Stacked Area" S["Series stacked on top
Total = sum of all
Each band = one category"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["✅ Shows total AND parts"] A2["✅ Good for 3-5 categories"] A3["✅ Clear composition shifts"] end subgraph "Limitations" L1["❌ Hard to compare non-bottom bands"] L2["❌ No common baseline"] L3["❌ Order affects perception"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style L1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: 100% Stacked Area
steps.explanation: **Normalized stacked area** shows proportions over time. Each vertical slice sums to 100%.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "100% Stacked" N["Each time point = 100%
Shows proportion shifts
Loses absolute values"] end subgraph "Best For" B1["Market share over time"] B2["Composition changes"] B3["Relative importance"] end subgraph "Limitation" L["Cannot see if total is
growing or shrinking"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9 style L fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Streamgraph
steps.explanation: **Streamgraphs** center the baseline, creating a flowing river effect. Aesthetically pleasing but less precise.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Streamgraph" S["Symmetric around center
Flowing organic shape
ThemeRiver variant"] end subgraph "Pros" P1["Aesthetically engaging"] P2["Good for music/cultural data"] P3["Shows volume and flow"] end subgraph "Cons" C1["❌ No fixed baseline"] C2["❌ Hard to read exact values"] C3["❌ Difficult comparisons"] end style P1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Ordering Categories
steps.explanation: The **stacking order** significantly affects readability. Place most important or most stable at bottom.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Ordering Strategies" O1["Largest at bottom
(most stable baseline)"] O2["Most important at bottom
(easiest to track)"] O3["Alphabetical
(consistent, findable)"] O4["Inside-out
(for streamgraphs)"] end subgraph "Impact" I["Bottom series is easiest to read
Middle series hardest"] end style O1 fill:#C8E6C9 style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Area vs Line Decision
steps.explanation: Choose area when magnitude matters; choose line when trend matters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"What matters most?"}
    Q1 -->|"Volume/magnitude"| AREA["Use Area Chart"]
    Q1 -->|"Trend/rate"| LINE["Use Line Chart"]
    Q1 -->|"Parts of whole"| STACK["Stacked Area"]

    Q2{"Multiple series?"}
    Q2 -->|"2-5 categories"| STACK2["Stacked OK"]
