    Q2 -->|">5 categories"| SM["Small Multiples"]
    Q2 -->|"Overlap OK"| TRANS["Transparent Areas"]

    style AREA fill:#C8E6C9
    style LINE fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Heatmaps & Tile Plots
fields.description: Color-encoded matrices for showing patterns in two-dimensional data.

steps.title: Heatmap Fundamentals
steps.explanation: **Heatmaps** use color intensity to represent values in a matrix. Great for spotting patterns in two-dimensional data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Heatmap Structure"
        R["Rows = one dimension"]
        C["Columns = other dimension"]
        CL["Color = value"]
    end
    subgraph "Common Uses"
        U1["Correlation matrices"]
        U2["Time-of-day patterns"]
        U3["Geographic data on grid"]
        U4["Confusion matrices"]
        U5["Gene expression"]
    end
    style CL fill:#C8E6C9
    style U1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Color Scale Selection
steps.explanation: The **color scale** is critical. Match the scale to your data type: sequential, diverging, or categorical.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sequential" S["Low to High
One color gradient
White → Blue"] SE["Use for: counts, amounts,
non-negative values"] end subgraph "Diverging" D["Negative - Zero - Positive
Two colors meet at midpoint
Blue ← White → Red"] DE["Use for: correlations,
deviations, change"] end subgraph "Categorical" C["Distinct colors
No implied order"] end style S fill:#BBDEFB style D fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Correlation Heatmap
steps.explanation: The most common heatmap shows **pairwise correlations**. Use diverging colors centered at zero.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Correlation Heatmap" M["Symmetric matrix
Diagonal = 1.0
Values from -1 to +1"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["✅ Diverging colormap"] B2["✅ Center at zero"] B3["✅ Add value annotations"] B4["✅ Cluster similar variables"] B5["✅ Consider masking diagonal"] end subgraph "Seaborn Example" E["sns.heatmap(df.corr(),
annot=True,
cmap='RdBu_r',
center=0)"] end style B1 fill:#C8E6C9 style E fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Calendar Heatmap
steps.explanation: **Calendar heatmaps** show daily patterns. GitHub contribution graphs are a famous example.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Calendar Structure" R["Rows = days of week"] C["Columns = weeks"] CL["Color = activity level"] end subgraph "Use Cases" U1["Daily activity patterns"] U2["Website traffic"] U3["Sales by day"] U4["Habit tracking"] end subgraph "GitHub Style" G["52 weeks x 7 days
Green intensity = commits"] end style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Design Considerations
steps.explanation: Careful design prevents heatmap from becoming a wall of color that's hard to interpret.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Design"
        G1["✅ Perceptually uniform colormap"]
        G2["✅ Colorbar legend"]
        G3["✅ Readable cell labels"]
        G4["✅ Meaningful ordering"]
        G5["✅ Adequate cell size"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Rainbow colormaps"]
        A2["❌ Too many cells"]
        A3["❌ Missing legend"]
        A4["❌ Random row/col order"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Clustering & Ordering
steps.explanation: **Clustering** rows and columns reveals hidden patterns. Dendrograms show cluster relationships.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Ordering Options" O1["Alphabetical (findable)"] O2["By row/column totals"] O3["Hierarchical clustering"] O4["Domain-specific order"] end subgraph "Clustermap" C["Heatmap + dendrograms
Groups similar rows/cols
Reveals structure"] end subgraph "Seaborn" S["sns.clustermap(data)"] end style C fill:#C8E6C9 style S fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Alternatives to Heatmaps
steps.explanation: Heatmaps can become overwhelming. Consider alternatives for specific use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "When to Use Alternatives"
        A1["Few comparisons → Bar chart"]
        A2["Exact values matter → Table"]
        A3["Geographic → Choropleth map"]
        A4["Time patterns → Line chart"]
    end
    subgraph "Heatmap Best For"
        B1["Pattern discovery"]
        B2["Many pairwise relationships"]
        B3["Identifying clusters"]
        B4["Overview then drill-down"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Histogram vs Density vs Violin
fields.description: Distribution visualization options and their trade-offs.

steps.title: Distribution Visualization Options
steps.explanation: Multiple chart types show distributions. Each makes different trade-offs between detail, comparison, and space.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Distribution Charts" H["HISTOGRAM
Binned counts"] K["KDE / DENSITY
Smooth estimate"] V["VIOLIN
Density + mirror"] B["BOX PLOT
Summary stats"] R["RIDGELINE
Stacked densities"] end subgraph "Trade-offs" T1["Detail vs Simplicity"] T2["One vs Many groups"] T3["Exact shape vs Summary"] end style H fill:#C8E6C9 style K fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Histogram
steps.explanation: **Histograms** bin continuous data and show frequency. The bin width significantly affects the shape.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Histogram Basics"
        B["Divide range into bins"]
        C["Count observations per bin"]
        D["Height = count or density"]
    end
    subgraph "Bin Width Effect"
        W1["Too few bins: Miss detail"]
        W2["Too many bins: Noisy"]
        W3["Just right: Clear shape"]
    end
    subgraph "Guidelines"
        G1["✅ Start with sqrt(n) bins"]
        G2["✅ Try multiple widths"]
        G3["✅ Use density for comparison"]
    end
    style W3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: KDE / Density Plot
steps.explanation: **Kernel Density Estimation** creates a smooth curve estimate of the distribution. No binning artifacts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "KDE Concept" K["Place kernel at each point"] S["Sum all kernels"] R["Result: smooth curve"] end subgraph "Advantages" A1["✅ No bin edge artifacts"] A2["✅ Easy to overlay multiple"] A3["✅ Shows shape clearly"] end subgraph "Bandwidth Parameter" B1["Too narrow: spiky"] B2["Too wide: oversmoothed"] B3["Default usually good"] end subgraph "Caution" C["⚠️ Can imply values outside
actual data range"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style C fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Violin Plot
steps.explanation: **Violin plots** combine KDE with mirroring, often adding box plot elements. Compact comparison of distributions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Violin Structure"
        V["Mirrored KDE on sides"]
        M["Width = density"]
        B["Often includes box/median"]
    end
    subgraph "Advantages"
        A1["✅ Shows full distribution shape"]
        A2["✅ Compare many groups"]
        A3["✅ More info than box plot"]
    end
    subgraph "Disadvantages"
        D1["❌ Unfamiliar to some audiences"]
        D2["❌ Can look unusual"]
        D3["❌ Needs enough data points"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Box Plot Comparison
steps.explanation: **Box plots** are compact summaries: median, quartiles, whiskers. Trade detail for comparison efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Box Plot Shows" M["Median (center line)"] Q["IQR (box: Q1 to Q3)"] W["Whiskers (1.5x IQR)"] O["Outliers (beyond whiskers)"] end subgraph "vs Violin" B1["Box: 5-number summary"] B2["Violin: Full shape"] B3["Box: Familiar to all"] B4["Violin: More detail"] end subgraph "Hybrid" H["Box inside violin
combines both benefits"] end style M fill:#C8E6C9 style H fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Ridgeline Plot
steps.explanation: **Ridgeline (joy) plots** stack multiple KDEs vertically. Great for comparing many distributions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Ridgeline Structure"
        R["Stacked density curves"]
        O["Slight overlap allowed"]
        Y["Y-axis = category"]
    end
    subgraph "Best For"
        B1["Many groups (5-20+)"]
        B2["Seeing overall patterns"]
        B3["Time-based evolution"]
    end
    subgraph "Design Tips"
        T1["✅ Order meaningfully"]
        T2["✅ Use light fill"]
        T3["✅ Consistent x-axis"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Selection Guide
steps.explanation: Choose based on audience, number of groups, and whether detail or comparison matters more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"How many groups?"}
    Q1 -->|"1"| HIST["Histogram or KDE"]
    Q1 -->|"2-5"| VB["Violin or Box"]
    Q1 -->|"5-20"| RIDGE["Ridgeline"]
    Q1 -->|">20"| SM["Small multiples"]

    Q2{"Audience?"}
    Q2 -->|"Technical"| VIOLIN["Violin OK"]
    Q2 -->|"General"| BOX["Box or Histogram"]

    Q3{"Need shape detail?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| V2["Violin / KDE"]
    Q3 -->|"Summary OK"| B2["Box plot"]

    style HIST fill:#C8E6C9
    style VB fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Small Multiples / Faceting
fields.description: Repeating charts for comparing across categories—the most powerful comparison technique.

steps.title: What Are Small Multiples?
steps.explanation: **Small multiples** (Tufte) repeat the same chart structure for different subsets of data. Enables parallel comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Concept" S["Same chart type"] D["Different data subsets"] C["Consistent scales"] G["Grid arrangement"] end subgraph "Also Called" N["Facets (ggplot2)"] T["Trellis plots"] P["Panel charts"] end subgraph "Power" PW["Compare many groups
while showing full detail"] end style PW fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Why Small Multiples Work
steps.explanation: Our eyes easily compare shapes when they're aligned. Small multiples leverage this cognitive advantage.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Cognitive Benefits"
        B1["Eye movement is efficient"]
        B2["Pattern detection is natural"]
        B3["Memory not overloaded"]
        B4["Each panel is simple"]
    end
    subgraph "vs Alternatives"
        A1["vs Many-line chart: Less clutter"]
        A2["vs Animation: All visible at once"]
        A3["vs Color only: More detail"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Types of Faceting
steps.explanation: **Facet wrap** arranges by one variable; **facet grid** creates a matrix from two variables.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Facet Wrap"
        W["One variable"]
        WE["Wraps into grid"]
        WX["facet_wrap(~category)"]
    end
    subgraph "Facet Grid"
        G["Two variables"]
        GE["Rows x Columns matrix"]
        GX["facet_grid(row ~ col)"]
    end
    subgraph "Example"
        E["Wrap: by country (12 panels)"]
        E2["Grid: year rows x region cols"]
    end
    style W fill:#C8E6C9
    style G fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Scale Decisions
steps.explanation: Should all panels share the same axis scales? Depends on comparison goals.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Fixed Scales" F1["✅ Easy absolute comparison"] F2["✅ Consistent perception"] F3["❌ Small values hard to see"] end subgraph "Free Scales" FR1["✅ Each panel optimized"] FR2["✅ See patterns in small groups"] FR3["❌ Harder to compare across"] end subgraph "Guideline" G["Default to fixed scales
Free only if necessary"] end style G fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Ordering Panels
steps.explanation: Panel order affects interpretation. Order by data (e.g., totals) or by logic (time, geography).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Ordering Options" O1["Alphabetical (findable)"] O2["By value (ranked)"] O3["By time (chronological)"] O4["Geographic (spatial)"] O5["Custom/domain order"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Order should support
the story you're telling"] end style O2 fill:#C8E6C9 style B fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Design Guidelines
steps.explanation: Effective small multiples require careful attention to labeling, spacing, and simplification.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Design"
        G1["✅ Clear panel labels"]
        G2["✅ Minimal within-panel elements"]
        G3["✅ Consistent colors"]
        G4["✅ Shared legend (not per panel)"]
        G5["✅ Adequate whitespace"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Too many panels (>25)"]
        A2["❌ Overcrowded panels"]
        A3["❌ Inconsistent styling"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: When to Use
steps.explanation: Small multiples are powerful but not always the best choice.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Use Small Multiples When" U1["Comparing across categories"] U2["3-25 groups to compare"] U3["Pattern in each matters"] U4["Overlapping would confuse"] end subgraph "Consider Alternatives When" A1["Only 2-3 groups (overlay OK)"] A2["Many groups (>30)"] A3["Only totals matter"] A4["Interactive possible"] end subgraph "Tufte's Advice" T["Small multiples are
the best design solution
for a wide range of problems"] end style U1 fill:#C8E6C9 style T fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Radar/Spider Charts
fields.description: Multivariate displays on radial axes—use cases and limitations.

steps.title: What Is a Radar Chart?
steps.explanation: **Radar charts** (spider, star, web diagrams) display multiple variables on axes starting from a center point.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Structure"
        C["Center = minimum"]
        A["Axes radiate outward"]
        P["Points connected by polygon"]
        S["Each axis = one variable"]
    end
    subgraph "Also Called"
        N["Spider chart"]
        N2["Star plot"]
        N3["Web diagram"]
        N4["Kiviat diagram"]
    end
    style C fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When Radar Charts Work
steps.explanation: Radar charts can be effective in specific situations, but these cases are limited.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Good Use Cases" G1["Comparing profiles
(athlete skills, product ratings)"] G2["Identifying outliers
in multivariate data"] G3["Familiar domain
(games, sports stats)"] G4["Few variables (5-8)"] end subgraph "Requirements" R1["All variables comparable"] R2["Same scale across axes"] R3["Order of axes meaningful"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Perception Problems
steps.explanation: Radar charts have significant perceptual issues that limit their effectiveness.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Problems" P1["❌ Area is misleading"] P2["❌ Values hard to read exactly"] P3["❌ Axis order affects shape"] P4["❌ Overlapping polygons confuse"] P5["❌ Connection implies relationship"] end subgraph "Area Illusion" A["Polygon area doesn't represent
anything meaningful!
Reordering axes changes it"] end style P1 fill:#FFCDD2 style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Better Alternatives
steps.explanation: Most radar chart use cases are better served by simpler visualizations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Alternatives"
        A1["Bar chart (one observation)"]
        A2["Grouped bar (compare 2-3)"]
        A3["Parallel coordinates (many vars)"]
        A4["Small multiples"]
        A5["Heatmap (matrix view)"]
    end
    subgraph "Why Bar is Better"
        B1["Easier to read values"]
        B2["Easy comparison"]
        B3["No distortion"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: If You Must Use Radar
steps.explanation: If a radar chart is required (stakeholder preference, domain convention), follow these guidelines.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["✅ Limit to 5-8 variables"]
        B2["✅ All same scale"]
        B3["✅ Order axes meaningfully"]
        B4["✅ Max 2-3 overlays"]
        B5["✅ Add value labels"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Many overlapping polygons"]
        A2["❌ Different scales"]
        A3["❌ Implying area = total"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Parallel Coordinates Alternative
steps.explanation: **Parallel coordinates** handle multivariate data better—especially for many variables or observations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parallel Coordinates"
        P["Axes side by side"]
        L["Lines connect values"]
        M["Can show many observations"]
    end
    subgraph "Advantages"
        A1["No area distortion"]
        A2["Works with many variables"]
        A3["Clusters visible"]
        A4["Interactive filtering"]
    end
    subgraph "vs Radar"
        V["Radar: 1-3 profiles"]
        V2["Parallel: many profiles"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Decision Summary
steps.explanation: Before using a radar chart, consider if alternatives would work better.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q1{"Compare multivariate?"} Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"How many observations?"} Q2 -->|"1"| BAR["Bar chart"] Q2 -->|"2-3"| Q3{"Must be radar?"} Q3 -->|"No"| GBAR["Grouped bar"] Q3 -->|"Stakeholder wants"| RADAR["Radar (careful!)"] Q2 -->|"Many"| PAR["Parallel coords"] subgraph "Radar Rule" R["Only use if domain expects it
AND few variables AND profiles"] end style BAR fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Color Scales
fields.description: Sequential, diverging, and categorical color palettes with colorblind-safe options.

steps.title: Color Scale Types
steps.explanation: Match the **color scale type** to your data type. Wrong choice leads to misinterpretation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Three Main Types" S["SEQUENTIAL
Low to High
One color gradient"] D["DIVERGING
Low - Mid - High
Two colors meet at center"] C["CATEGORICAL
Distinct values
No implied order"] end subgraph "Match to Data" S --> SD["Counts, amounts, magnitudes"] D --> DD["Deviations, correlations, change"] C --> CD["Categories, labels, groups"] end style S fill:#BBDEFB style D fill:#E8F5E9 style C fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Sequential Scales
steps.explanation: **Sequential scales** go from light to dark (or vice versa). Use for data with a natural ordering from low to high.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sequential Examples" V["viridis (colorblind-safe)"] B["Blues, Greens, Oranges"] Y["YlOrRd (yellow to red)"] P["plasma, inferno, magma"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["✅ Light = low, dark = high"] B2["✅ Perceptually uniform"] B3["✅ Works in grayscale"] B4["✅ Colorblind-safe preferred"] end subgraph "Use For" U["Counts, temperatures,
population density, values ≥ 0"] end style V fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Diverging Scales
steps.explanation: **Diverging scales** have a meaningful center point. Two colors diverge from neutral middle (often white/gray).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Diverging Examples" R["RdBu (Red-Blue)"] RY["RdYlGn (Red-Yellow-Green)"] PR["PuOr (Purple-Orange)"] C["coolwarm"] end subgraph "Key Requirement" K["Must have meaningful midpoint!
Zero, average, baseline"] end subgraph "Use For" U["Correlations (-1 to +1)"] U2["Change (decrease/increase)"] U3["Deviations from mean"] end style K fill:#FFF9C4 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Categorical Scales
steps.explanation: **Categorical scales** use distinct colors for different groups. No implied order—colors should be equally distinguishable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Categorical Palettes" T["tab10, tab20 (matplotlib)"] S["Set1, Set2, Set3 (ColorBrewer)"] P["Paired (for pairs)"] D["Dark2, Pastel1"] end subgraph "Guidelines" G1["✅ Max 8-10 distinct colors"] G2["✅ Equal visual weight"] G3["✅ No color implies order"] G4["✅ Consider brand colors"] end subgraph "Problem" PR["Too many categories?
Use shapes/patterns or faceting"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style PR fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Colorblind-Safe Palettes
steps.explanation: ~8% of men have color vision deficiency. Always check your palette works for colorblind viewers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Safe Palettes"
        V["viridis, plasma, inferno"]
        C["Cividis (colorblind-optimized)"]
        CB["ColorBrewer qualitative"]
        OK["Okabe-Ito palette"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Red-Green only distinction"]
        A2["❌ Rainbow colormap"]
        A3["❌ jet (matplotlib default)"]
    end
    subgraph "Testing Tools"
        T["Coblis simulator"]
        T2["Color Oracle"]
        T3["Chromatic Vision Simulator"]
    end
    style V fill:#C8E6C9
    style A2 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Perceptual Uniformity
steps.explanation: In **perceptually uniform** scales, equal data differences appear as equal visual differences. Critical for accurate reading.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Uniform vs Not"
        U["Uniform: Equal steps look equal"]
        N["Non-uniform: Some steps pop more"]
    end
    subgraph "Perceptually Uniform"
        P1["viridis, magma, inferno, plasma"]
        P2["Cividis"]
        P3["Scientific colormaps (cmocean)"]
    end
    subgraph "NOT Uniform (avoid)"
        A["jet, rainbow, hsv"]
        A2["Creates false patterns!"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Color Scale Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Quick reference for choosing the right color scale.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Data type?"}
    Q1 -->|"Ordered, low→high"| SEQ["Sequential"]
    Q1 -->|"Centered, ±deviation"| DIV["Diverging"]
    Q1 -->|"Categories, no order"| CAT["Categorical"]

    Q2{"How many colors?"}
    Q2 -->|"Continuous"| CONT["Sequential/Diverging"]
    Q2 -->|"< 8 categories"| CAT2["Categorical OK"]
    Q2 -->|"> 8 categories"| PROB["Rethink encoding"]

    subgraph "Defaults"
        D["Sequential: viridis"]
        D2["Diverging: RdBu"]
        D3["Categorical: tab10"]
    end
    style D fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Perceptual Ranking
fields.description: Position beats length beats angle beats area beats color—choosing the right visual channel.

steps.title: Cleveland & McGill's Ranking
steps.explanation: Research shows some visual encodings are perceived more **accurately** than others. Use the best encoding for your most important variable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Accuracy Ranking (Best to Worst)" P1["1. Position along common scale"] P2["2. Position along non-aligned scale"] P3["3. Length"] P4["4. Angle / Slope"] P5["5. Area"] P6["6. Volume / Curvature"] P7["7. Color saturation / Density"] end subgraph "Implication" I["Use position for most
important comparisons!"] end style P1 fill:#C8E6C9 style I fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Position: Most Accurate
steps.explanation: **Position along a common scale** (bar chart, dot plot) is most accurately perceived. Aligned baselines enable precise comparison.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Position Encodings" SC["Scatter plot (x, y)"] DOT["Dot plot"] BAR["Bar chart"] LI["Line chart (y position)"] end subgraph "Why Best" W1["Common baseline"] W2["Easy to compare"] W3["Precise reading"] end subgraph "Example" E["Bar chart: which is larger?
Immediately obvious"] end style BAR fill:#C8E6C9 style E fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Length: Second Best
steps.explanation: **Length** (without a common baseline) is still good but harder than aligned position. Stacked bar segments use length.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Length Examples" SB["Stacked bar (non-bottom)"] GA["Gantt chart bars"] end subgraph "Problem" P["No common baseline
= harder comparison"] end subgraph "vs Position" V["Position: A vs B easy"] V2["Length: A vs B harder
when not aligned"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Angle: Poor
steps.explanation: **Angle** perception is surprisingly poor. Pie chart slices are hard to compare accurately.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Angle Encodings" PIE["Pie chart slices"] RAD["Radar chart axes"] end subgraph "Problems" P1["Hard to judge differences"] P2["25% vs 30%: Can you tell?"] P3["No common reference"] end subgraph "Implication" I["Prefer bar chart
over pie chart"] end style P1 fill:#FFCDD2 style I fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Area: Problematic
steps.explanation: **Area** perception is systematically biased. We underestimate larger areas. Bubble charts suffer from this.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Area Encodings" BUB["Bubble chart (size)"] TREE["Treemap rectangles"] PIE2["Pie chart (implicitly)"] end subgraph "Bias" B["We underestimate larger areas"] B2["Doubling area ≠ looks double"] end subgraph "Mitigation" M["If using area:
Add value labels!"] end style B fill:#FFCDD2 style M fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Color: Least Precise
steps.explanation: **Color** (hue, saturation, lightness) is least precisely perceived. Good for categories, poor for exact values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Color Uses"
        HUE["Hue: Categories (good)"]
        SAT["Saturation: Magnitude (poor)"]
        LI["Lightness: Better than saturation"]
    end
    subgraph "Good For"
        G["Distinguishing groups"]
        G2["Highlighting attention"]
        G3["Showing patterns"]
    end
    subgraph "Bad For"
        B["Reading exact values"]
        B2["Fine distinctions"]
    end
    style G fill:#C8E6C9
    style B fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Practical Application
steps.explanation: Map your most important variable to the most accurate encoding.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Design Strategy" S1["Most important → Position"] S2["Secondary → Length or Color"] S3["Tertiary → Size or Shape"] end subgraph "Example: Scatter Plot" E1["x, y: Position (most important)"] E2["color: Category (grouping)"] E3["size: Third variable (least precise)"] end subgraph "Rule" R["Don't waste position
on less important variables"] end style S1 fill:#C8E6C9 style R fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Visual Encoding Channels
fields.description: Mapping data variables to visual properties: position, size, color, shape, and more.

steps.title: What Is Visual Encoding?
steps.explanation: **Visual encoding** maps data variables to visual properties (channels). This is the core of visualization design.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Data"
        D1["Temperature"]
        D2["Country"]
        D3["Population"]
        D4["Year"]
    end
    subgraph "Visual Channels"
        V1["Y Position"]
        V2["Color Hue"]
        V3["Size"]
        V4["X Position"]
    end
    D1 --> V1
    D2 --> V2
    D3 --> V3
    D4 --> V4
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style V2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Quantitative Channels
steps.explanation: **Quantitative (ordered) channels** encode numeric values. Position and length are most accurate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Quantitative Channels"
        P["Position (x, y)"]
        L["Length"]
        A["Angle"]
        AR["Area"]
        LI["Lightness/Saturation"]
    end
    subgraph "Best for Numbers"
        B1["Position: Exact values"]
        B2["Length: Bar heights"]
        B3["Size: Third variable"]
    end
    subgraph "Accuracy"
        AC["Position > Length > Area > Color"]
    end
    style P fill:#C8E6C9
    style AC fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Categorical Channels
steps.explanation: **Categorical (identity) channels** distinguish groups without implying order.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Categorical Channels"
        HU["Color Hue"]
        SH["Shape"]
        TX["Texture/Pattern"]
        SP["Spatial Grouping"]
    end
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["Hue: 5-8 categories max"]
        B2["Shape: 5-6 max"]
        B3["Combine hue + shape for more"]
    end
    subgraph "Not Categorical"
        NC["Size implies order!"]
        NC2["Saturation implies order!"]
    end
    style HU fill:#C8E6C9
    style NC fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Combining Channels
steps.explanation: Multiple channels can encode different variables. This creates multivariate displays.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Scatter Plot Example" X["X: Variable 1"] Y["Y: Variable 2"] C["Color: Category"] S["Size: Variable 3"] SH["Shape: Category 2"] end subgraph "Channel Independence" I1["Position channels interact"] I2["Color + Size independent"] I3["Shape + Color independent"] end subgraph "Limit" L["3-4 encoded variables max
for readability"] end style L fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Redundant Encoding
steps.explanation: **Redundant encoding** maps one variable to multiple channels. Increases emphasis and accessibility.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Redundancy Example"
        E1["Category → Color AND Shape"]
        E2["Value → Position AND Label"]
    end
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B1["Colorblind accessibility"]
        B2["Emphasis/attention"]
        B3["Print compatibility"]
    end
    subgraph "When to Use"
        W["Critical distinctions"]
        W2["Accessibility requirements"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Channel-Data Type Matching
steps.explanation: Match channel type to data type. Quantitative data needs ordered channels; categorical needs identity channels.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Correct Matching"
        C1["Numeric → Position/Length/Size"]
        C2["Categorical → Hue/Shape"]
        C3["Ordered categorical → Saturation"]
        C4["Time → X position"]
    end
    subgraph "Wrong Matching"
        W1["❌ Category → Size"]
        W2["❌ Number → Random shapes"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style W1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Encoding Decision Framework
steps.explanation: Systematic approach to choosing visual encodings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Variable type?"}
    Q1 -->|"Quantitative"| QC["Position > Length > Size"]
    Q1 -->|"Categorical"| CC["Hue > Shape > Texture"]
    Q1 -->|"Ordered cat"| OC["Lightness > Saturation"]

    Q2{"Most important var?"}
    Q2 --> MI["Give it Position"]

    Q3{"How many vars?"}
    Q3 -->|"1-2"| S["Simple chart"]
    Q3 -->|"3-4"| M["Multi-encoding"]
    Q3 -->|">4"| C["Consider faceting"]

    style MI fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Interactive Techniques
fields.description: Tooltips, zoom, filter, brush, and linked views for dynamic data exploration.

steps.title: Why Interactivity?
steps.explanation: **Interactive visualizations** let users explore data at their own pace. They enable overview first, then zoom and filter, then details on demand.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Shneiderman's Mantra"
        O["OVERVIEW first"]
        Z["ZOOM and filter"]
        D["DETAILS on demand"]
    end
    O --> Z --> D
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B1["Handle complex data"]
        B2["Personalized exploration"]
        B3["Reduce visual clutter"]
    end
    style O fill:#C8E6C9
    style Z fill:#FFF9C4
    style D fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Tooltips & Hover
steps.explanation: **Tooltips** show details when hovering over data points. Most fundamental interaction—add to every chart.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Tooltip Content"
        T1["Exact values"]
        T2["Labels and context"]
        T3["Formatted numbers"]
        T4["Mini-charts (sparklines)"]
    end
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["✅ Near mouse cursor"]
        B2["✅ Don't obscure data"]
        B3["✅ Fast response (< 100ms)"]
        B4["✅ Consistent formatting"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Zoom & Pan
steps.explanation: **Zooming** reveals detail in dense visualizations. Pan allows navigation. Essential for geographic maps and large datasets.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Zoom Types" G["Geometric zoom
(magnify everything)"] S["Semantic zoom
(show more detail)"] end subgraph "Controls" C1["Mouse wheel / pinch"] C2["Click buttons (+/-)"] C3["Double-click to zoom in"] C4["Shift+drag to pan"] end subgraph "Design Tip" T["Always provide reset/home button"] end style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Filtering
steps.explanation: **Filtering** removes data points from view. Use sliders, dropdowns, or checkboxes to control which data is shown.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Filter Types"
        R["Range slider (numeric)"]
        D["Dropdown (categorical)"]
        CB["Checkboxes (multi-select)"]
        SE["Search/text filter"]
    end
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["✅ Show count of filtered items"]
        B2["✅ Clear all filters button"]
        B3["✅ Preview effect before apply"]
        B4["✅ Remember filter state"]
    end
    style R fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Brushing & Selection
steps.explanation: **Brushing** selects a region of data. Selected points highlight across linked views. Powerful for multivariate exploration.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Brush Types" R["Rectangle brush"] L["Lasso (free-form)"] I["Interval (1D range)"] end subgraph "Brush Effects" E1["Highlight selected"] E2["Fade non-selected"] E3["Update linked charts"] E4["Show selection stats"] end subgraph "Use Case" U["Select outliers in scatter →
See them in histogram"] end style R fill:#C8E6C9 style U fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Linked Views
steps.explanation: **Linked views** (coordinated views) show the same data in multiple charts. Selection in one propagates to all.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Linkage Types" S["Selection linking
(brush propagates)"] F["Filter linking
(filter applies to all)"] H["Highlight linking
(hover highlights all)"] end subgraph "Example Dashboard" E1["Map: select region"] E2["Bar: highlights that region"] E3["Line: filters to region"] end E1 --> E2 E1 --> E3 style S fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Add Interactivity
steps.explanation: Interactivity adds complexity. Use it when exploration is valuable; skip it for simple messages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Add Interactivity When" A1["Data is large/complex"] A2["Users need to explore"] A3["Multiple questions possible"] A4["Details vary by user"] end subgraph "Skip Interactivity When" S1["Single clear message"] S2["Print/static medium"] S3["Simple comparison"] S4["Low-tech audience"] end subgraph "Tools" T["Plotly, Altair, D3.js,
Tableau, Power BI, Bokeh"] end style A1 fill:#C8E6C9 style S1 fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Dashboard Layout Patterns
fields.description: Grid systems, information hierarchy, and arranging multiple visualizations effectively.

steps.title: Dashboard Purpose
steps.explanation: A **dashboard** combines multiple visualizations to monitor, analyze, or communicate. Design depends on the primary purpose.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Dashboard Types" OP["OPERATIONAL
Monitor in real-time"] AN["ANALYTICAL
Explore and discover"] ST["STRATEGIC
High-level KPIs"] end subgraph "Design Implications" OP --> OI["Glanceable, alerts, live data"] AN --> AI["Filters, drill-down, detail"] ST --> SI["Summary metrics, trends"] end style OP fill:#C8E6C9 style AN fill:#FFF9C4 style ST fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Information Hierarchy
steps.explanation: Organize from **most important to least**. Top-left gets most attention. Big numbers/KPIs up top.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Visual Hierarchy" T["TOP: KPIs, summary metrics"] M["MIDDLE: Main visualizations"] B["BOTTOM: Details, tables"] end subgraph "Reading Pattern" R["F-pattern or Z-pattern"] R2["Top-left = most seen"] R3["Bottom-right = least seen"] end subgraph "Tip" TIP["Put critical info where
eyes naturally land"] end style T fill:#C8E6C9 style R2 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Grid Systems
steps.explanation: Use a **consistent grid** to align elements. Common: 12-column grid, card-based layout.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Grid Benefits" G1["Alignment creates order"] G2["Consistent spacing"] G3["Responsive adaptation"] end subgraph "Common Layouts" L1["2x2: Four equal panels"] L2["1+2: Hero + two smaller"] L3["Sidebar + main"] L4["Cards in flex grid"] end subgraph "Spacing" S["Consistent margins/padding
8px, 16px, 24px system"] end style G1 fill:#C8E6C9 style S fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: KPI Cards
steps.explanation: **KPI cards** show key metrics prominently. Big number + label + trend indicator.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "KPI Card Anatomy" N["BIG NUMBER
The metric value"] L["Label
What it measures"] T["Trend
Up/down arrow, %"] SP["Sparkline
Recent history"] end subgraph "Best Practices" B1["✅ 3-6 KPIs max"] B2["✅ Most important first"] B3["✅ Color for good/bad"] B4["✅ Consistent formatting"] end style N fill:#C8E6C9 style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Filters & Controls
steps.explanation: **Filters** should be visible but not dominant. Top or sidebar placement is common.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Filter Placement" T["Top bar
(affects all)"] S["Sidebar
(many options)"] I["Inline
(chart-specific)"] end subgraph "Filter Design" D1["✅ Show current state"] D2["✅ Clear all option"] D3["✅ Indicate when filtered"] D4["❌ Don't hide critical filters"] end style T fill:#C8E6C9 style D1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Responsive Considerations
steps.explanation: Dashboards must work on different screen sizes. Plan for reflow and prioritization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Responsive Strategies" R1["Reflow: Cards stack"] R2["Prioritize: Show less on mobile"] R3["Resize: Charts adapt"] R4["Collapse: Filters hide"] end subgraph "Mobile-First" M["Design for mobile first,
then enhance for desktop"] end subgraph "Breakpoints" B["Mobile: 1 column"] B2["Tablet: 2 columns"] B3["Desktop: 3-4 columns"] end style M fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Dashboard Anti-Patterns
steps.explanation: Common mistakes that make dashboards ineffective.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Avoid"
        A1["❌ Too many charts (>10)"]
        A2["❌ No clear hierarchy"]
        A3["❌ Inconsistent styling"]
        A4["❌ 3D charts, chartjunk"]
        A5["❌ Too many colors"]
        A6["❌ No context/labels"]
    end
    subgraph "Good Dashboard"
        G1["✅ Clear purpose"]
        G2["✅ Scannable layout"]
        G3["✅ Consistent design system"]
        G4["✅ Actionable insights"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Common Visualization Pitfalls
fields.description: Truncated axes, 3D effects, dual axes, chartjunk, and other mistakes to avoid.

steps.title: Why Pitfalls Matter
steps.explanation: Bad visualizations **mislead** viewers, whether intentionally or not. Learn to spot and avoid common problems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Consequences"
        C1["Wrong conclusions"]
        C2["Lost credibility"]
        C3["Poor decisions"]
    end
    subgraph "Common Pitfalls"
        P1["Truncated axes"]
        P2["3D effects"]
        P3["Dual y-axes"]
        P4["Chartjunk"]
        P5["Cherry-picked data"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style P1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Truncated Y-Axis
steps.explanation: Not starting bar charts at zero **exaggerates differences**. A 5% change can look like 500%.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Problem" P["Y-axis starts at 95
98 vs 100 looks HUGE
Actually 2% difference"] end subgraph "When OK" OK1["Line charts (sometimes)"] OK2["Stock prices"] OK3["When range is labeled clearly"] end subgraph "Rule for Bars" R["Bar charts: ALWAYS start at zero
Bar length = value"] end style P fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: 3D Effects
steps.explanation: **3D charts** distort perception. Depth, perspective, and occlusion make values harder to compare.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "3D Problems" P1["Perspective distorts size"] P2["Back items occluded"] P3["Depth is meaningless"] P4["Angle perception worse"] end subgraph "Worst Offenders" W1["3D pie charts"] W2["3D bar charts"] W3["3D area charts"] end subgraph "Rule" R["NEVER use 3D for statistical charts
Zero exceptions"] end style P1 fill:#FFCDD2 style R fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Dual Y-Axes
steps.explanation: **Dual axes** let you manipulate the apparent relationship between two series. Scale choice is arbitrary.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "The Problem" P1["Which scale shows truth?"] P2["Crossing lines = meaningful?"] P3["Implies correlation"] end subgraph "Manipulation" M["You can make ANY two series
appear correlated by
adjusting the scales"] end subgraph "Alternatives" A1["✅ Two separate charts"] A2["✅ Index to same baseline"] A3["✅ Percent change"] end style M fill:#FFCDD2 style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Chartjunk
steps.explanation: **Chartjunk** (Tufte's term) is non-data visual elements that don't help understanding. Maximize data-ink ratio.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Chartjunk Examples" J1["Decorative backgrounds"] J2["Excessive gridlines"] J3["Gratuitous icons"] J4["Drop shadows/bevels"] J5["Unnecessary legends"] end subgraph "Data-Ink Ratio" D["Data-ink = ink used for data
Maximize: data-ink / total ink"] end subgraph "Tufte's Rule" R["Erase non-data-ink
within reason"] end style J1 fill:#FFCDD2 style D fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Misleading Color & Scale
steps.explanation: Color and scale choices can **distort perception** even with accurate data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Color Problems" C1["Red = bad? (cultural bias)"] C2["Rainbow hides patterns"] C3["Non-uniform perception"] end subgraph "Scale Problems" S1["Unequal intervals"] S2["Missing zero (maps)"] S3["Inverted scales"] end subgraph "Fix" F["Use perceptually uniform colors
Consistent scales
Clear legends"] end style C2 fill:#FFCDD2 style F fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Pitfall Checklist
steps.explanation: Review every visualization against this checklist before publishing.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before Publishing" CH1["☐ Y-axis starts at zero (bars)"] CH2["☐ No 3D effects"] CH3["☐ No dual axes"] CH4["☐ Minimal chartjunk"] CH5["☐ Colorblind-safe"] CH6["☐ Clear labels & title"] CH7["☐ Source cited"] CH8["☐ Honest representation"] end subgraph "Ask Yourself" Q["Would I be embarrassed if
someone pointed out a flaw?"] end style CH1 fill:#C8E6C9 style Q fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Accessibility in Visualization
fields.description: Colorblind-safe palettes, screen reader support, alt text, and inclusive design.

steps.title: Why Accessibility Matters
steps.explanation: ~8% of men and ~0.5% of women have color vision deficiency. Accessibility benefits everyone and is often legally required.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Who Benefits"
        B1["Color-blind viewers"]
        B2["Screen reader users"]
        B3["Low vision"]
        B4["Cognitive differences"]
        B5["Everyone (good design!)"]
    end
    subgraph "Requirements"
        R1["WCAG 2.1 guidelines"]
        R2["ADA / Section 508"]
        R3["Organizational policies"]
    end
    style B5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Color Vision Deficiency
steps.explanation: **Protanopia** and **deuteranopia** (red-green confusion) are most common. Never rely on color alone.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "CVD Types" P["Protanopia: No red cones"] D["Deuteranopia: No green cones"] T["Tritanopia: No blue cones (rare)"] end subgraph "Problem" PR["Red vs Green = Same!
Pink vs Gray = Same!"] end subgraph "Solutions" S1["Use colorblind-safe palettes"] S2["Add patterns/textures"] S3["Direct labeling"] S4["Redundant encoding"] end style PR fill:#FFCDD2 style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Colorblind-Safe Design
steps.explanation: Use **tested palettes** or simulate color blindness to verify your choices work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Safe Palettes" V["viridis, cividis"] O["Okabe-Ito palette"] CB["ColorBrewer (marked safe)"] end subgraph "Testing Tools" T1["Coblis (online simulator)"] T2["Color Oracle (desktop)"] T3["Chrome DevTools CVD"] end subgraph "Best Practice" B["Test with all three
CVD types before publishing"] end style V fill:#C8E6C9 style B fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Beyond Color: Multi-Modal
steps.explanation: Don't rely on color alone. Use **multiple channels** to convey information.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Redundant Encoding" R1["Color + Shape"] R2["Color + Pattern/Texture"] R3["Color + Position"] R4["Color + Direct Labels"] end subgraph "Example" E["Categories: Different colors
AND different shapes
AND labeled directly"] end subgraph "Print Test" P["Would it work in
black and white?"] end style R4 fill:#C8E6C9 style P fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Alt Text & Screen Readers
steps.explanation: **Alt text** describes visualizations for screen reader users. Be concise but include key insights.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Alt Text Structure" S1["Chart type"] S2["What it shows"] S3["Key insight/trend"] S4["Data source (if needed)"] end subgraph "Example" E["'Bar chart showing monthly sales.
Sales increased 25% from
January to June 2024.'"] end subgraph "Avoid" A["❌ 'Chart' or 'Image'"] A2["❌ Reading every data point"] end style E fill:#C8E6C9 style A fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Interactive Accessibility
steps.explanation: Interactive visualizations need **keyboard navigation** and proper ARIA labels.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Keyboard Access" K1["Tab to navigate elements"] K2["Enter/Space to activate"] K3["Arrow keys for navigation"] K4["Escape to close"] end subgraph "ARIA Labels" A1["role='img' for static charts"] A2["aria-label for description"] A3["Live regions for updates"] end subgraph "Data Tables" T["Provide data table alternative
for complex visualizations"] end style K1 fill:#C8E6C9 style T fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Accessibility Checklist
steps.explanation: Use this checklist for every visualization.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Accessibility Checklist" C1["☐ Colorblind-safe palette"] C2["☐ Not color-only encoding"] C3["☐ Sufficient contrast (4.5:1)"] C4["☐ Alt text provided"] C5["☐ Keyboard navigable (if interactive)"] C6["☐ Text is readable size"] C7["☐ Data table available"] end subgraph "Mantra" M["Accessible design
is good design"] end style C1 fill:#C8E6C9 style M fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Virtual Environment Creation
fields.description: Step-by-step visualization of what happens when you run python -m venv

steps.title: The Command
steps.explanation: When you run `python -m venv myenv`, Python's venv module creates an isolated environment. This prevents package conflicts between projects and keeps your global Python clean.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    CMD["python -m venv myenv"]
    PY["Python Interpreter"]
    MOD["venv module"]
    CMD --> PY --> MOD
    style CMD fill:#E3F2FD
    style MOD fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Create Root Directory
steps.explanation: First, the venv module creates the root directory with your chosen name. This will contain all the virtual environment files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Your Project"
        PROJ["project/"]
        MYENV["myenv/"]
        PROJ --> MYENV
    end
    style MYENV fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:2px

steps.title: Create pyvenv.cfg
steps.explanation: A configuration file `pyvenv.cfg` is created containing the Python version, home directory, and whether system site-packages are included.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "myenv/"
        CFG["pyvenv.cfg"]
    end
    subgraph "pyvenv.cfg contents"
        C1["home = /usr/bin"]
        C2["version = 3.12.0"]
        C3["include-system-site-packages = false"]
    end
    CFG --> C1
    CFG --> C2
    CFG --> C3
    style CFG fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Create bin/ Directory (Unix) or Scripts/ (Windows)
steps.explanation: The `bin/` directory (or `Scripts/` on Windows) is created. This will hold the Python executable symlink and activation scripts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "myenv/"
        CFG["pyvenv.cfg"]
        BIN["bin/"]
    end
    subgraph "bin/ contents"
        PY["python → symlink"]
        ACT["activate"]
        ACTF["activate.fish"]
        ACTC["activate.csh"]
    end
    BIN --> PY
    BIN --> ACT
    BIN --> ACTF
    BIN --> ACTC
    style BIN fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Symlink Python Executable
steps.explanation: A symlink to the system Python executable is created. This ensures the venv uses the same Python version that created it.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "myenv/bin/"
        PY["python"]
        PY3["python3"]
    end
    subgraph "System"
        SYS["/usr/bin/python3.12"]
    end
    PY -->|symlink| SYS
    PY3 -->|symlink| SYS
    style SYS fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Create lib/ Directory
steps.explanation: The `lib/` directory is created with a Python version subdirectory containing `site-packages/`. This is where pip will install packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "myenv/"
        LIB["lib/"]
        subgraph "lib/python3.12/"
            SP["site-packages/"]
        end
    end
    LIB --> SP
    style SP fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:2px

steps.title: Create include/ Directory
steps.explanation: The `include/` directory is created for C header files. This is used when compiling Python extensions that need to link against Python.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "myenv/" INC["include/"] end NOTE["Used for C extensions
compilation headers"] INC --> NOTE style INC fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Final Structure
steps.explanation: The complete virtual environment is ready! It contains isolated Python binaries, activation scripts, and an empty site-packages waiting for your dependencies.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "myenv/"
        CFG["pyvenv.cfg"]
        subgraph "bin/"
            PY["python"]
            PIP["pip"]
            ACT["activate"]
        end
        subgraph "lib/python3.12/"
            SP["site-packages/"]
        end
        INC["include/"]
    end
    style CFG fill:#FFF9C4
    style SP fill:#C8E6C9
    style ACT fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Activation Process
fields.description: What happens when you activate a virtual environment

steps.title: Before Activation
steps.explanation: Before activating, your shell uses the system Python and global packages. The PATH points to system directories first.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Current Shell"
        PROMPT["$ "]
        PATH["PATH=/usr/bin:..."]
    end
    subgraph "System Python"
        SYS["/usr/bin/python3"]
        GLOB["Global packages"]
    end
    PROMPT --> SYS
    style SYS fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Run Activation Command
steps.explanation: You source the activate script. On Unix: `source myenv/bin/activate`. On Windows: `myenv\Scripts\activate.bat`. The 'source' command runs the script in the current shell.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["source myenv/bin/activate"]
    SCRIPT["activate script"]
    SHELL["Current Shell"]
    CMD --> SCRIPT
    SCRIPT -->|"modifies"| SHELL
    style CMD fill:#E3F2FD
    style SCRIPT fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Save Original PATH
steps.explanation: The activate script saves your original PATH to `_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH`. This allows the deactivate command to restore it later.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    OLD["PATH=/usr/bin:/usr/local/bin"]
    SAVE["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH"]
    OLD -->|"saved to"| SAVE
    style SAVE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Prepend venv to PATH
steps.explanation: The script prepends `myenv/bin` to PATH. Now when you type `python`, the shell finds `myenv/bin/python` BEFORE `/usr/bin/python`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "New PATH"
        direction LR
        V["myenv/bin"]
        U["/usr/bin"]
        L["/usr/local/bin"]
        V --> U --> L
    end
    NOTE["Shell searches left to right"]
    V --> NOTE
    style V fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Set VIRTUAL_ENV Variable
steps.explanation: The `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable is set to the venv's absolute path. Tools like pip use this to know they're in a virtual environment.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    VAR["VIRTUAL_ENV=/home/user/project/myenv"]
    PIP["pip"]
    TOOLS["Other tools"]
    VAR --> PIP
    VAR --> TOOLS
    NOTE["Tools check this variable"]
    style VAR fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Modify Shell Prompt
steps.explanation: The script modifies your `PS1` prompt to show the venv name in parentheses. This visual indicator reminds you which environment is active.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Before"
        B["user@host:~/project$"]
    end
    subgraph "After"
        A["(myenv) user@host:~/project$"]
    end
    B --> A
    style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Define deactivate Function
steps.explanation: A shell function called `deactivate` is defined. This function will restore the original PATH and prompt when called.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    FUNC["deactivate()"]
    R1["Restore _OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH"]
    R2["Restore PS1 prompt"]
    R3["Unset VIRTUAL_ENV"]
    FUNC --> R1
    FUNC --> R2
    FUNC --> R3
    style FUNC fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Activation Complete
steps.explanation: The virtual environment is now active! All Python commands use the venv's interpreter and packages are installed to the venv's site-packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Active Environment"
        PROMPT["(myenv) $"]
        PY["python → myenv/bin/python"]
        PIP["pip → myenv/bin/pip"]
        SP["Packages → myenv/lib/site-packages"]
    end
    PROMPT --> PY
    PROMPT --> PIP
    PIP --> SP
    style PROMPT fill:#C8E6C9
    style SP fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: PATH Manipulation
fields.description: Before/after comparison of PATH showing how venv's Python takes priority

steps.title: What is PATH?
steps.explanation: PATH is an environment variable containing a list of directories. When you type a command, the shell searches these directories in order to find the executable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    CMD["python"]
    SHELL["Shell"]
    PATH["Search PATH"]
    EXE["Found executable"]
    CMD --> SHELL --> PATH --> EXE
    style PATH fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: System PATH (No venv)
steps.explanation: Without a virtual environment, PATH typically contains system directories. The shell finds Python in `/usr/bin` or `/usr/local/bin`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "PATH search order"
        direction LR
        P1["/usr/local/bin"]
        P2["/usr/bin"]
        P3["/bin"]
        P4["/usr/sbin"]
    end
    PY["python3"]
    FOUND["/usr/bin/python3"]
    PY --> P1
    P1 -->|not found| P2
    P2 -->|"FOUND!"| FOUND
    style FOUND fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Activation Prepends venv
steps.explanation: When you activate a venv, its `bin/` directory is **prepended** (added to the front) of PATH. This makes it the first place the shell looks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    OLD["/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"]
    NEW["myenv/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"]
    OLD -->|"activate"| NEW
    style NEW fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: PATH with Active venv
steps.explanation: Now when you type `python`, the shell finds it in `myenv/bin` FIRST, before even looking at system directories.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "PATH search order"
        direction LR
        V["myenv/bin"]
        P1["/usr/local/bin"]
        P2["/usr/bin"]
        P3["/bin"]
    end
    PY["python"]
    FOUND["myenv/bin/python"]
    PY --> V
    V -->|"FOUND!"| FOUND
    style V fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:3px
    style FOUND fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Same for pip
steps.explanation: The same applies to `pip`. The venv's pip is found first, ensuring packages install to the venv's site-packages, not globally.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "PATH search"
        V["myenv/bin"]
        SYS["/usr/bin"]
    end
    subgraph "pip locations"
        VP["myenv/bin/pip"]
        SP["/usr/bin/pip"]
    end
    V -->|"found first"| VP
    SYS --> SP
    style VP fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:2px
    style SP fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: View Your PATH
steps.explanation: You can inspect your PATH anytime using `echo $PATH` (Unix) or `echo %PATH%` (Windows). The directories are separated by colons (Unix) or semicolons (Windows).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD1["echo $PATH"]
    OUT1["/home/user/myenv/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin"]
    CMD1 --> OUT1
    NOTE["venv directory appears first"]
    OUT1 --> NOTE
    style OUT1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Why Prepending Matters
steps.explanation: By prepending instead of appending, the venv guarantees its Python is found first. If it were appended, the system Python would still be found first, defeating the purpose.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Prepend (correct)"
        direction LR
        V1["myenv/bin"]
        S1["/usr/bin"]
        V1 -->|"search"| S1
    end
    subgraph "Append (wrong)"
        direction LR
        S2["/usr/bin"]
        V2["myenv/bin"]
        S2 -->|"search"| V2
    end
    R1["✓ venv Python found"]
    R2["✗ System Python found"]
    V1 --> R1
    S2 --> R2
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style R2 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Package Isolation
fields.description: How pip install puts packages in local site-packages vs global

steps.title: The Problem: Dependency Conflicts
steps.explanation: Without virtual environments, all projects share the same packages. Project A needs `requests==2.25` but Project B needs `requests==2.28`. They can't coexist globally!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Global Python" PKG["site-packages"] REQ["requests==???"] end PA["Project A
needs 2.25"] PB["Project B
needs 2.28"] PA -->|conflict!| REQ PB -->|conflict!| REQ style REQ fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: The Solution: Isolated site-packages
steps.explanation: Each virtual environment has its own `site-packages` directory. Packages installed in one venv are completely invisible to others.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Project A venv"
        SPA["site-packages"]
        RA["requests==2.25"]
    end
    subgraph "Project B venv"
        SPB["site-packages"]
        RB["requests==2.28"]
    end
    SPA --> RA
    SPB --> RB
    style RA fill:#C8E6C9
    style RB fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Where pip Installs
steps.explanation: When a venv is active, `pip install` automatically installs to the venv's site-packages. No special flags needed!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PIP["pip install requests"]
    CHECK{"VIRTUAL_ENV set?"}
    VENV["myenv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/"]
    GLOB["/usr/lib/python3.12/site-packages/"]
    PIP --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"Yes"| VENV
    CHECK -->|"No"| GLOB
    style VENV fill:#C8E6C9
    style GLOB fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Checking Installation Location
steps.explanation: Use `pip show <package>` to see where a package is installed. The 'Location' field shows the site-packages path.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["pip show requests"]
    subgraph "Output"
        N["Name: requests"]
        V["Version: 2.28.0"]
        L["Location: /home/user/myenv/lib/python3.12/site-packages"]
    end
    CMD --> N
    CMD --> V
    CMD --> L
    style L fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Import Resolution
steps.explanation: When Python imports a module, it searches `sys.path`. In a venv, the venv's site-packages appears before (or instead of) the global one.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    IMP["import requests"]
    subgraph "sys.path search"
        P1["./"]
        P2["myenv/lib/python3.12/site-packages"]
        P3["/usr/lib/python3.12/site-packages"]
    end
    IMP --> P1
    P1 -->|"not found"| P2
    P2 -->|"FOUND!"| FOUND["requests module"]
    style P2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: System Packages Option
steps.explanation: By default, venvs are isolated. But you can create one with `--system-site-packages` to also see globally installed packages (not recommended).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Default venv"
        D["Only sees venv packages"]
    end
    subgraph "--system-site-packages"
        S["Sees venv + global packages"]
    end
    CMD1["python -m venv myenv"]
    CMD2["python -m venv myenv --system-site-packages"]
    CMD1 --> D
    CMD2 --> S
    style D fill:#C8E6C9
    style S fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Complete Isolation
steps.explanation: With venvs, each project has exactly the dependencies it needs. No conflicts, no pollution of the global environment, easy to reproduce.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Project A" VA["venv_a/"] PA["Django==4.0
requests==2.25"] end subgraph "Project B" VB["venv_b/"] PB["Flask==2.0
requests==2.28"] end subgraph "Global Python" G["Clean! No project packages"] end style PA fill:#C8E6C9 style PB fill:#BBDEFB style G fill:#E8F5E9

fields.title: venv vs Global Python
fields.description: Side-by-side comparison of package resolution with/without venv active

steps.title: Two Pythons, Two Worlds
steps.explanation: When you run `python` without a venv, you use the system Python. With a venv active, you use an isolated Python with its own packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Global Python"
        GP["/usr/bin/python3"]
        GSP["/usr/lib/python3/site-packages"]
    end
    subgraph "venv Python"
        VP["myenv/bin/python"]
        VSP["myenv/lib/python3/site-packages"]
    end
    GP --> GSP
    VP --> VSP
    style GSP fill:#FFCDD2
    style VSP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Which Python Am I Using?
steps.explanation: Use `which python` (Unix) or `where python` (Windows) to see which Python executable is active. This is the most reliable way to check.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "No venv"
        C1["which python"]
        R1["/usr/bin/python"]
        C1 --> R1
    end
    subgraph "venv active"
        C2["which python"]
        R2["/home/user/myenv/bin/python"]
        C2 --> R2
    end
    style R1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style R2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: sys.prefix Shows the Truth
steps.explanation: `sys.prefix` in Python tells you the base directory. For global Python it's `/usr`. For a venv, it's the venv directory.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["python -c 'import sys; print(sys.prefix)'"]
    subgraph "No venv"
        G["/usr"]
    end
    subgraph "venv active"
        V["/home/user/myenv"]
    end
    style G fill:#FFCDD2
    style V fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Package Visibility Comparison
steps.explanation: Packages installed globally are NOT visible in a venv (by default). Packages installed in a venv are NOT visible globally. Complete isolation!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Global" G["pip list"] GP["numpy, pandas
(globally installed)"] end subgraph "venv" V["pip list"] VP["requests, flask
(venv installed)"] end G --> GP V --> VP X1["❌ Can't see venv packages"] X2["❌ Can't see global packages"] GP --> X1 VP --> X2 style GP fill:#E3F2FD style VP fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: pip install Behavior
steps.explanation: The same `pip install` command behaves differently based on context. Always know which environment is active before installing!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["pip install requests"]
    subgraph "No venv active"
        G["/usr/lib/python3/site-packages/requests"]
        W["⚠️ May need sudo"]
    end
    subgraph "venv active"
        V["myenv/lib/python3/site-packages/requests"]
        OK["✓ No sudo needed"]
    end
    CMD --> G
    CMD --> V
    G --> W
    V --> OK
    style W fill:#FFCDD2
    style OK fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Import Behavior Comparison
steps.explanation: When you `import` a module, Python searches `sys.path`. The venv modifies this to prioritize venv packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    IMP["import requests"]
    subgraph "Global sys.path"
        GP1["./"]
        GP2["/usr/lib/python3/site-packages"]
    end
    subgraph "venv sys.path"
        VP1["./"]
        VP2["myenv/lib/python3/site-packages"]
        VP3["/usr/lib/python3/site-packages"]
    end
    NOTE["venv site-packages searched first"]
    VP2 --> NOTE
    style VP2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Quick Environment Check
steps.explanation: These commands help you verify which environment you're in. Make it a habit to check before installing packages!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Check Commands" C1["which python"] C2["echo $VIRTUAL_ENV"] C3["python -c 'import sys; print(sys.prefix)'"] C4["pip -V"] end R["All should point to
your venv directory"] C1 --> R C2 --> R C3 --> R C4 --> R style R fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: venv Directory Structure
fields.description: Tree diagram of a typical venv folder

steps.title: Overview
steps.explanation: A virtual environment is just a directory with a specific structure. Understanding this structure helps you troubleshoot issues and understand how venv works.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    VENV["myenv/"]
    VENV --> BIN
    VENV --> INCLUDE
    VENV --> LIB
    VENV --> CFG
    BIN["bin/"]
    INCLUDE["include/"]
    LIB["lib/"]
    CFG["pyvenv.cfg"]
    style VENV fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: pyvenv.cfg - Configuration
steps.explanation: This plain text file stores venv configuration. It tells Python where the base interpreter is and the Python version used to create the venv.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CFG["pyvenv.cfg"]
    subgraph "Contents"
        H["home = /usr/bin"]
        V["version = 3.12.0"]
        I["include-system-site-packages = false"]
    end
    CFG --> H
    CFG --> V
    CFG --> I
    style CFG fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: bin/ Directory (Scripts/ on Windows)
steps.explanation: Contains Python executable symlinks, pip, and activation scripts. This is what gets added to your PATH when you activate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    BIN["bin/"]
    BIN --> PY["python → symlink"]
    BIN --> PY3["python3 → symlink"]
    BIN --> PIP["pip"]
    BIN --> PIP3["pip3"]
    BIN --> ACT["activate"]
    BIN --> ACTF["activate.fish"]
    BIN --> ACTC["activate.csh"]
    BIN --> ACTPS["Activate.ps1"]
    style BIN fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Python Symlinks
steps.explanation: The `python` and `python3` files are symlinks pointing to the system Python that created the venv. They're not copies, saving disk space.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "myenv/bin/"
        PY["python"]
        PY3["python3"]
        PY312["python3.12"]
    end
    SYS["/usr/bin/python3.12"]
    PY -->|"symlink"| SYS
    PY3 -->|"symlink"| SYS
    PY312 -->|"symlink"| SYS
    style SYS fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: lib/ Directory
steps.explanation: Contains Python version-specific directories and most importantly, `site-packages/` where pip installs all your packages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    LIB["lib/"]
    PV["python3.12/"]
    SP["site-packages/"]
    LIB --> PV --> SP
    subgraph "site-packages/"
        P1["pip/"]
        P2["setuptools/"]
        P3["requests/"]
        P4["..."]
    end
    SP --> P1
    SP --> P2
    SP --> P3
    SP --> P4
    style SP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: site-packages/ Contents
steps.explanation: Each installed package gets a directory here. Metadata is stored in `.dist-info` directories. This is where Python looks when you `import` something.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    SP["site-packages/"]
    subgraph "Package"
        R["requests/"]
        RI["requests-2.28.0.dist-info/"]
    end
    subgraph "Metadata"
        M["METADATA"]
        REC["RECORD"]
        TOP["top_level.txt"]
    end
    SP --> R
    SP --> RI
    RI --> M
    RI --> REC
    RI --> TOP
    style SP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: include/ Directory
steps.explanation: Contains Python header files for compiling C extensions. Usually empty or contains symlinks. Needed when pip builds packages from source.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB INC["include/"] PV["python3.12/"] INC --> PV NOTE["Header files for
C extension compilation"] PV --> NOTE style INC fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Complete Structure
steps.explanation: Here's the complete picture. The venv is self-contained - you can even zip it up and move it (though paths may break). Delete the directory to remove the venv.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB VENV["myenv/"] CFG["pyvenv.cfg"] BIN["bin/
python, pip, activate"] INC["include/
C headers"] LIB["lib/python3.12/
site-packages/"] VENV --> CFG VENV --> BIN VENV --> INC VENV --> LIB style VENV fill:#E3F2FD style CFG fill:#FFF9C4 style BIN fill:#BBDEFB style LIB fill:#C8E6C9 style INC fill:#FFE0B2

fields.title: Deactivation Flow
fields.description: What deactivate does to restore the original environment

steps.title: Before Deactivation
steps.explanation: You have an active virtual environment. Your PATH starts with the venv's bin directory, VIRTUAL_ENV is set, and your prompt shows the venv name.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Current State"
        PATH["PATH=myenv/bin:/usr/bin:..."]
        VE["VIRTUAL_ENV=/path/to/myenv"]
        PS1["(myenv) user@host$"]
    end
    subgraph "Saved by activate"
        OLD["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH=/usr/bin:..."]
        OLDPS["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PS1=user@host$"]
    end
    style PATH fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Run deactivate
steps.explanation: Type `deactivate` in your shell. This runs the deactivate function that was defined when you activated the venv.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    CMD["deactivate"]
    FUNC["deactivate() function"]
    SHELL["Modifies shell"]
    CMD --> FUNC --> SHELL
    style CMD fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Restore Original PATH
steps.explanation: The deactivate function restores PATH from `_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH`. The venv's bin directory is removed from the front.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Before"
        B["PATH=myenv/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/local/bin"]
    end
    subgraph "After"
        A["PATH=/usr/bin:/usr/local/bin"]
    end
    OLD["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH"]
    OLD -->|"restore"| A
    B --> A
    style B fill:#C8E6C9
    style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Unset VIRTUAL_ENV
steps.explanation: The VIRTUAL_ENV environment variable is removed. Tools will no longer detect that a virtual environment is active.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Before"
        VE["VIRTUAL_ENV=/path/to/myenv"]
    end
    subgraph "After"
        UNSET["VIRTUAL_ENV=(unset)"]
    end
    VE -->|"unset"| UNSET
    style VE fill:#C8E6C9
    style UNSET fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Restore Shell Prompt
steps.explanation: The original prompt (PS1) is restored from `_OLD_VIRTUAL_PS1`. The venv name prefix disappears from your prompt.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Before"
        B["(myenv) user@host:~$"]
    end
    subgraph "After"
        A["user@host:~$"]
    end
    B --> A
    style B fill:#C8E6C9
    style A fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Clean Up Variables
steps.explanation: The helper variables (`_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH`, `_OLD_VIRTUAL_PS1`) are unset. The deactivate function itself is also removed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Removed"
        V1["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PATH"]
        V2["_OLD_VIRTUAL_PS1"]
        V3["VIRTUAL_ENV"]
        F["deactivate function"]
    end
    CLEAN["Shell is clean"]
    V1 --> CLEAN
    V2 --> CLEAN
    V3 --> CLEAN
    F --> CLEAN
    style CLEAN fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: After Deactivation
steps.explanation: You're back to using the system Python. The venv still exists on disk - you can reactivate it anytime.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Current State"
        PY["python → /usr/bin/python3"]
        PIP["pip → /usr/bin/pip"]
        PKG["Packages → global site-packages"]
    end
    subgraph "venv directory"
        VENV["myenv/ still exists"]
        NOTE["Ready to reactivate"]
    end
    VENV --> NOTE
    style PY fill:#E3F2FD
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Alternative: Just Close Terminal
steps.explanation: You can also just close your terminal. Environment variables and PATH changes only exist in that shell session. A new terminal starts fresh.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Terminal 1"
        T1["(myenv) active"]
        C["Close terminal"]
    end
    subgraph "Terminal 2 (new)"
        T2["No venv active"]
        F["Fresh shell environment"]
    end
    T1 --> C
    C -->|"open new"| T2
    T2 --> F
    style T1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style T2 fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: venv vs virtualenv vs conda
fields.description: Comparison flowchart showing when to use each tool

steps.title: Three Popular Tools
steps.explanation: Python has several tools for managing environments: `venv` (built-in), `virtualenv` (third-party), and `conda` (from Anaconda). Each has strengths and use cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Environment Tools" VENV["venv
Built into Python 3.3+"] VE["virtualenv
Third-party package"] CONDA["conda
Anaconda/Miniconda"] end style VENV fill:#C8E6C9 style VE fill:#BBDEFB style CONDA fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: venv - The Standard Choice
steps.explanation: `venv` is included in Python's standard library since 3.3. No installation needed. It's lightweight, simple, and perfect for most Python projects.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    VENV["venv"]
    subgraph "Pros"
        P1["✓ Built-in, no install"]
        P2["✓ Lightweight"]
        P3["✓ Official Python tool"]
        P4["✓ Simple & reliable"]
    end
    subgraph "Cons"
        C1["✗ Python-only"]
        C2["✗ Can't select Python version"]
        C3["✗ Fewer features"]
    end
    VENV --> P1
    VENV --> C1
    style VENV fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: virtualenv - The Veteran
steps.explanation: `virtualenv` predates venv and works with Python 2.x. It's faster, has more features, and can discover Python versions on your system.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    VE["virtualenv"]
    subgraph "Pros"
        P1["✓ Faster than venv"]
        P2["✓ More features"]
        P3["✓ Python 2 support"]
        P4["✓ Can find Python versions"]
    end
    subgraph "Cons"
        C1["✗ Requires pip install"]
        C2["✗ External dependency"]
    end
    VE --> P1
    VE --> C1
    style VE fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: conda - The Data Science Powerhouse
steps.explanation: `conda` manages both Python AND non-Python dependencies (C libraries, etc.). Essential for data science where packages need compiled binaries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CONDA["conda"]
    subgraph "Pros"
        P1["✓ Manages any language"]
        P2["✓ Handles C/C++ deps"]
        P3["✓ Pre-built binaries"]
        P4["✓ Multiple Python versions"]
    end
    subgraph "Cons"
        C1["✗ Large install size"]
        C2["✗ Slower"]
        C3["✗ Different from pip"]
    end
    CONDA --> P1
    CONDA --> C1
    style CONDA fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Decision: Pure Python Project?
steps.explanation: If your project is pure Python (no complex C dependencies), `venv` is the simplest choice. It's built-in and works perfectly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Pure Python project?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| VENV["Use venv ✓"]
    Q1 -->|"No, has C deps"| NEXT["Consider conda"]
    style VENV fill:#C8E6C9
    style NEXT fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Decision: Data Science Stack?
steps.explanation: For data science (NumPy, SciPy, TensorFlow, etc.), `conda` shines. It handles tricky binary dependencies and provides optimized builds.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{"Using NumPy/SciPy/
TensorFlow/PyTorch?"} Q -->|"Yes, many deps"| CONDA["conda recommended"] Q -->|"Light usage"| VENV["venv + pip works"] style CONDA fill:#FFE0B2 style VENV fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Decision: Need Multiple Python Versions?
steps.explanation: `venv` uses whichever Python created it. `virtualenv` and `conda` can switch between Python versions easily.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB Q{"Need Python 3.9, 3.10,
3.11 simultaneously?"} Q -->|"Yes"| OPT["Use pyenv + venv
or conda"] Q -->|"No, one version"| VENV["venv is fine"] style OPT fill:#BBDEFB style VENV fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Quick Reference
steps.explanation: Here's a simple decision guide. When in doubt, start with `venv` - it's always available and works for most cases.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB START["Which tool?"] Q1{"Data science
heavy project?"} Q2{"Need special
features?"} START --> Q1 Q1 -->|"Yes"| CONDA["conda"] Q1 -->|"No"| Q2 Q2 -->|"Yes"| VE["virtualenv"] Q2 -->|"No"| VENV["venv"] DEFAULT["Default choice:
venv"] VENV --> DEFAULT style VENV fill:#C8E6C9 style CONDA fill:#FFE0B2 style VE fill:#BBDEFB style DEFAULT fill:#E8F5E9

steps.title: Commands Comparison
steps.explanation: Here are the basic commands for each tool. The concepts are similar - create, activate, install, deactivate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "venv"
        V1["python -m venv env"]
        V2["source env/bin/activate"]
        V3["pip install pkg"]
    end
    subgraph "virtualenv"
        VE1["virtualenv env"]
        VE2["source env/bin/activate"]
        VE3["pip install pkg"]
    end
    subgraph "conda"
        C1["conda create -n env"]
        C2["conda activate env"]
        C3["conda install pkg"]
    end
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style VE1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style C1 fill:#FFE0B2

fields.title: String Encoding (ASCII/UTF-8/Unicode)
fields.description: How characters map to bytes, encoding vs decoding

steps.title: Characters vs Bytes
steps.explanation: Computers store everything as bytes (numbers 0-255). **Encoding** converts characters to bytes. **Decoding** converts bytes back to characters. The encoding scheme determines the mapping.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR CHAR["Characters
'Hello'"] ENCODE["Encode"] BYTES["Bytes
48 65 6c 6c 6f"] DECODE["Decode"] CHAR2["Characters
'Hello'"] CHAR --> ENCODE --> BYTES --> DECODE --> CHAR2 style CHAR fill:#C8E6C9 style BYTES fill:#BBDEFB style CHAR2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: ASCII - The Original
steps.explanation: ASCII uses 7 bits (0-127) to represent 128 characters: letters, digits, punctuation, and control characters. It only covers English - no accents, no other scripts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "ASCII Table (sample)" A["'A' = 65"] B["'B' = 66"] Z["'Z' = 90"] a["'a' = 97"] zero["'0' = 48"] space["' ' = 32"] end LIMIT["Only 0-127
English only!"] style LIMIT fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Unicode - The Universal Standard
steps.explanation: Unicode assigns a unique **code point** (number) to every character in every language. Code points are written as U+XXXX. Unicode defines WHAT characters exist.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Unicode Code Points" A["'A' = U+0041"] EURO["'€' = U+20AC"] SMILE["'😀' = U+1F600"] ZH["'中' = U+4E2D"] end NOTE["1.1 million+ code points
All languages covered"] style NOTE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: UTF-8 - The Encoding
steps.explanation: UTF-8 is an **encoding** that converts Unicode code points to bytes. It uses 1-4 bytes per character. ASCII characters use 1 byte (backward compatible!).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "UTF-8 Byte Lengths"
        B1["1 byte: ASCII (0-127)"]
        B2["2 bytes: Latin, Greek, etc."]
        B3["3 bytes: Chinese, Japanese"]
        B4["4 bytes: Emoji, rare chars"]
    end
    EX1["'A' → 0x41 (1 byte)"]
    EX2["'é' → 0xC3 0xA9 (2 bytes)"]
    EX3["'中' → 0xE4 0xB8 0xAD (3 bytes)"]
    EX4["'😀' → 0xF0 0x9F 0x98 0x80 (4 bytes)"]
    B1 --> EX1
    B2 --> EX2
    B3 --> EX3
    B4 --> EX4
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Python 3 Strings
steps.explanation: In Python 3, `str` is always Unicode. Use `.encode()` to get bytes, `.decode()` to get string back. Default encoding is UTF-8.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR STR["str
'Hello'"] ENC[".encode('utf-8')"] BYTES["bytes
b'Hello'"] DEC[".decode('utf-8')"] STR2["str
'Hello'"] STR --> ENC --> BYTES --> DEC --> STR2 style STR fill:#C8E6C9 style BYTES fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Encoding Errors
steps.explanation: Using the wrong encoding causes errors or garbled text (mojibake). Always know your data's encoding! UTF-8 is the safe default for most modern applications.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    DATA["Bytes: 0xC3 0xA9"]
    subgraph "Correct"
        UTF8["UTF-8 decode"]
        GOOD["'é' ✓"]
    end
    subgraph "Wrong"
        LATIN["Latin-1 decode"]
        BAD["'Ã©' ✗"]
    end
    DATA --> UTF8 --> GOOD
    DATA --> LATIN --> BAD
    style GOOD fill:#C8E6C9
    style BAD fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Common Encodings
steps.explanation: UTF-8 dominates the web (95%+). You may encounter legacy encodings in old files or specific regions. Always specify encoding explicitly when reading files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Modern (use these)" UTF8["UTF-8
