        U1["myapp run app.py --debug"]
        U2["myapp build --output dist/"]
    end
    P --> SP --> R
    SP --> B
    style SP fill:#E3F2FD

fields.title: Mutually Exclusive Groups
fields.description: --verbose vs --quiet - only one allowed

steps.title: What Are Mutually Exclusive Groups?
steps.explanation: Some arguments conflict - you can't be `--verbose` AND `--quiet`. Mutually exclusive groups enforce "only one of these".
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Conflict"
        V["--verbose"]
        Q["--quiet"]
        X["Can't use both!"]
    end
    V --> X
    Q --> X
    style X fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Creating a Group
steps.explanation: Use `add_mutually_exclusive_group()` to create a group. Add arguments to the group, not the parser.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PARSER["parser"]
    GROUP["group = parser.add_mutually_exclusive_group()"]
    A1["group.add_argument('-v', '--verbose')"]
    A2["group.add_argument('-q', '--quiet')"]
    PARSER --> GROUP --> A1
    GROUP --> A2
    style GROUP fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Enforcement
steps.explanation: argparse automatically errors if user provides more than one argument from the group.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CMD["$ myapp --verbose --quiet"] ERR["error: argument -q/--quiet:
not allowed with argument -v/--verbose"] EXIT["sys.exit(2)"] CMD --> ERR --> EXIT style ERR fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Valid Usage
steps.explanation: Users can provide zero or one argument from the group (unless `required=True`).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "All valid"
        V1["myapp --verbose"]
        V2["myapp --quiet"]
        V3["myapp (neither)"]
    end
    subgraph "Invalid"
        I["myapp --verbose --quiet"]
    end
    style V1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style V2 fill:#C8E6C9
    style V3 fill:#C8E6C9
    style I fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: required=True
steps.explanation: Force users to pick exactly one option from the group with `required=True`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["group = parser.add_mutually_exclusive_group(
required=True)"] subgraph "Must choose one" V1["--verbose ✓"] V2["--quiet ✓"] V3["(neither) ✗"] end CODE --> V1 CODE --> V3 style V3 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Common Patterns
steps.explanation: Typical use cases for mutually exclusive groups: verbosity levels, output formats, operation modes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Verbosity"
        A1["--verbose | --quiet"]
    end
    subgraph "Format"
        A2["--json | --csv | --table"]
    end
    subgraph "Mode"
        A3["--dry-run | --force"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Help Text
steps.explanation: Mutually exclusive groups show in help with brackets and pipes indicating the choices.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB HELP["usage: myapp [-v | -q]"] NOTE["Brackets = optional
Pipe = or"] HELP --> NOTE style HELP fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Multiple Groups
steps.explanation: You can have multiple mutually exclusive groups. Each group is independent.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Group 1: Verbosity"
        G1A["-v | -q"]
    end
    subgraph "Group 2: Format"
        G2A["--json | --csv"]
    end
    VALID["myapp -v --json ✓"]
    INVALID["myapp -v -q ✗"]
    G1A --> VALID
    G1A --> INVALID
    style VALID fill:#C8E6C9
    style INVALID fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Argument Groups
fields.description: Organizing help output into logical sections

steps.title: Why Argument Groups?
steps.explanation: For CLIs with many options, the help text becomes overwhelming. **Argument groups** organize related arguments under labeled sections.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without groups"
        W["All options in one list"]
    end
    subgraph "With groups"
        G1["Input options:"]
        G2["Output options:"]
        G3["Debug options:"]
    end
    style W fill:#FFF9C4
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Creating Groups
steps.explanation: Use `add_argument_group()` with a title. Then add arguments to the group instead of the parser.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PARSER["parser"]
    GROUP["input_group = parser.add_argument_group('Input')"]
    ARG["input_group.add_argument('--input')"]
    PARSER --> GROUP --> ARG
    style GROUP fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Multiple Groups
steps.explanation: Create multiple groups to organize your CLI logically. Each group appears as a section in `--help`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PARSER["parser"]
    G1["parser.add_argument_group('Input')"]
    G2["parser.add_argument_group('Output')"]
    G3["parser.add_argument_group('Logging')"]
    PARSER --> G1
    PARSER --> G2
    PARSER --> G3
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style G2 fill:#BBDEFB
    style G3 fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Help Output
steps.explanation: Groups create labeled sections in `--help` output. Much easier to scan!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "--help output"
        H1["Input:"]
        A1["  --input FILE"]
        A2["  --format FMT"]
        H2["Output:"]
        A3["  --output FILE"]
        H3["Logging:"]
        A4["  --verbose"]
        A5["  --log-file FILE"]
    end
    style H1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style H2 fill:#BBDEFB
    style H3 fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Group Description
steps.explanation: Add a description to provide context for each group of arguments.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["parser.add_argument_group(
'Input',
'Options for specifying input data')"] HELP["Input:
Options for specifying input data

--input FILE ..."] CODE --> HELP style HELP fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Default Groups
steps.explanation: argparse automatically creates two groups: 'positional arguments' and 'options'. Your groups appear after these.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Default groups"
        D1["positional arguments"]
        D2["options (--help, etc.)"]
    end
    subgraph "Your groups"
        Y1["Input"]
        Y2["Output"]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> Y1 --> Y2
    style D1 fill:#E3F2FD
    style D2 fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Groups vs Mutually Exclusive
steps.explanation: Don't confuse them! **Argument groups** are for organization (help text). **Mutually exclusive** groups enforce logic (only one allowed).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Argument Group"
        AG["Organizes help text"]
        AG2["No logic enforcement"]
    end
    subgraph "Mutually Exclusive"
        ME["Enforces one-of"]
        ME2["Also affects help"]
    end
    style AG fill:#C8E6C9
    style ME fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Practical Example
steps.explanation: A well-organized CLI with multiple argument groups for different concerns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "myapp --help"
        P["usage: myapp ..."]
        POS["positional arguments:"]
        OPT["options:"]
        IN["Input options:"]
        OUT["Output options:"]
        DBG["Debug options:"]
    end
    P --> POS --> OPT --> IN --> OUT --> DBG
    style IN fill:#C8E6C9
    style OUT fill:#BBDEFB
    style DBG fill:#FFE0B2

fields.title: Default Values & Required
fields.description: default=, required=True, and their interactions

steps.title: The Basics
steps.explanation: `default` sets the value when argument is absent. `required` forces the user to provide it. They interact in specific ways.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parameters"
        D["default=value"]
        R["required=True"]
    end
    D --> D1["Value if absent"]
    R --> R1["Must be provided"]
    style D fill:#C8E6C9
    style R fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Default Values
steps.explanation: If an optional argument isn't provided, it gets the `default` value. Default default is `None`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CODE["add_argument('--count', default=10)"]
    subgraph "Usage"
        U1["(absent) → args.count = 10"]
        U2["--count 5 → args.count = 5"]
    end
    CODE --> U1
    CODE --> U2
    style U1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Required Optional Arguments
steps.explanation: Normally, `--flags` are optional. Use `required=True` to make them mandatory.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CODE["add_argument('--config', required=True)"]
    subgraph "Must provide"
        U1["$ myapp → error!"]
        U2["$ myapp --config file.yaml ✓"]
    end
    CODE --> U1
    CODE --> U2
    style U1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style U2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Positional Defaults
steps.explanation: Positional arguments are required by default. Use `nargs='?'` with `default` to make them optional.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Required (default)"
        C1["add_argument('file')"]
        R1["Must provide file"]
    end
    subgraph "Optional positional"
        C2["add_argument('file', nargs='?', default='input.txt')"]
        R2["file is optional"]
    end
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R2
    style R2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: default=SUPPRESS
steps.explanation: `default=argparse.SUPPRESS` means the attribute won't exist if not provided. Useful for sparse configs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--verbose',
action='store_true',
default=argparse.SUPPRESS)"] subgraph "If absent" A["hasattr(args, 'verbose') → False"] end subgraph "If present" P["args.verbose → True"] end CODE --> A CODE --> P style A fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: None vs default
steps.explanation: Without `default`, value is `None`. This lets you distinguish 'not provided' from 'explicitly set'.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--count', type=int)"] subgraph "Usage" U1["(absent) → None"] U2["--count 0 → 0"] end LOGIC["if args.count is None:
# not provided"] CODE --> U1 --> LOGIC style LOGIC fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Environment Variable Defaults
steps.explanation: A common pattern: use environment variable as default, CLI can override.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--api-key',
default=os.environ.get('API_KEY'))"] subgraph "Priority" P1["1. CLI: --api-key xxx"] P2["2. Env: API_KEY=xxx"] P3["3. None"] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Summary Table
steps.explanation: Quick reference for optional vs positional and their default behaviors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Positional"
        POS1["Required by default"]
        POS2["nargs='?' → optional"]
    end
    subgraph "Optional (--flag)"
        OPT1["Optional by default"]
        OPT2["required=True → mandatory"]
    end
    subgraph "Defaults"
        DEF1["default=X → use X if absent"]
        DEF2["No default → None"]
    end
    style POS1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style OPT1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Help & Documentation
fields.description: help=, description=, epilog=, metavar=

steps.title: Auto-Generated Help
steps.explanation: argparse automatically generates `--help` output. You control it with description, epilog, help text, and metavar.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    HELP["--help"]
    subgraph "Output sections"
        DESC["Description"]
        USAGE["Usage line"]
        ARGS["Arguments"]
        EPILOG["Epilog"]
    end
    HELP --> DESC --> USAGE --> ARGS --> EPILOG
    style HELP fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Parser Description & Epilog
steps.explanation: `description` appears before arguments, `epilog` appears after. Use for overview and examples.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["ArgumentParser(
description='Process files.',
epilog='Example: myapp input.txt')"] subgraph "--help" D["Process files."] U["usage: ..."] A["arguments..."] E["Example: myapp input.txt"] end D --> U --> A --> E style D fill:#C8E6C9 style E fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Argument help=
steps.explanation: Each argument can have `help` text explaining what it does. This is crucial for usability.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--output', '-o',
help='Output file path')"] HELP["-o, --output OUTPUT Output file path"] CODE --> HELP style HELP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: metavar for Display
steps.explanation: `metavar` controls how the argument value appears in help. Doesn't affect `dest`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Without metavar"
        W["--output OUTPUT"]
    end
    subgraph "With metavar='FILE'"
        M["--output FILE"]
    end
    CODE["add_argument('--output', metavar='FILE')"]
    CODE --> M
    style M fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Format Specifiers
steps.explanation: Use `%(prog)s`, `%(default)s`, `%(type)s` in help strings for dynamic values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--count',
type=int, default=10,
help='Number of items (default: %(default)s)')"] RESULT["--count N Number of items (default: 10)"] CODE --> RESULT NOTE["%(default)s is replaced"] style RESULT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Suppressing Arguments
steps.explanation: `help=argparse.SUPPRESS` hides an argument from help. Useful for deprecated or internal options.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--internal',
help=argparse.SUPPRESS)"] subgraph "--help output" VISIBLE["--verbose Show details"] HIDDEN["(--internal not shown)"] end NOTE["Still works, just hidden"] style HIDDEN fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Custom Formatter
steps.explanation: Use `formatter_class` to control help formatting: raw text, default values shown, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Formatter Classes" F1["RawDescriptionHelpFormatter
Preserve newlines in description"] F2["RawTextHelpFormatter
Preserve all whitespace"] F3["ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter
Show defaults automatically"] end CODE["ArgumentParser(
formatter_class=ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter)"] style F3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: A well-documented CLI with all help components.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "$ myapp --help"
        DESC["Process and transform data files."]
        USAGE["usage: myapp [-h] [-v] -o FILE input"]
        POS["positional arguments:"]
        A1["  input         Input file to process"]
        OPT["options:"]
        A2["  -h, --help    show this help"]
        A3["  -v, --verbose Enable verbose output"]
        A4["  -o, --output FILE  Output file (required)"]
        EPILOG["Examples: myapp data.csv -o out.json"]
    end
    style DESC fill:#C8E6C9
    style EPILOG fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Environment Variable Fallbacks
fields.description: Pattern for CLI args with env var defaults

steps.title: The Pattern
steps.explanation: CLI tools often read from environment variables as defaults, letting command-line override them. This gives flexibility for both scripting and interactive use.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Priority (highest first)"
        P1["1. Command line: --api-key XXX"]
        P2["2. Environment: API_KEY=XXX"]
        P3["3. Hardcoded default"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Basic Implementation
steps.explanation: Use `os.environ.get()` as the default value. CLI arg overrides if provided.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--api-key',
default=os.environ.get('API_KEY'),
help='API key (or set API_KEY env var)')"] subgraph "Resolution" C["CLI provided? → use CLI"] E["Else env set? → use env"] N["Else → None"] end CODE --> C --> E --> N style C fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: With Fallback Default
steps.explanation: Chain `os.environ.get()` with a default for a three-tier fallback.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CODE["default=os.environ.get('PORT', '8080')"]
    subgraph "Resolution"
        C["CLI: --port 3000 → 3000"]
        E["Env: PORT=9000 → 9000"]
        D["Neither → 8080"]
    end
    CODE --> C
    CODE --> E
    CODE --> D
    style D fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Required with Env Fallback
steps.explanation: You can require an argument but allow env var to satisfy it. Check after parsing.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--api-key',
default=os.environ.get('API_KEY'))"] CHECK["if not args.api_key:
parser.error('--api-key or API_KEY required')"] CODE --> CHECK style CHECK fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Type Conversion
steps.explanation: Remember: env vars are strings. Apply type conversion if needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--port',
type=int,
default=int(os.environ.get('PORT', '8080')))"] NOTE["Convert env var to int"] CODE --> NOTE style NOTE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Boolean Env Vars
steps.explanation: Boolean env vars need special handling. Common pattern: check for truthy strings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Helper function"
        F1["def env_bool(key, default=False):"]
        F2["    val = os.environ.get(key, '')"]
        F3["    return val.lower() in ('1', 'true', 'yes')"]
    end
    USE["default=env_bool('DEBUG')"]
    F3 --> USE
    style USE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Document in Help
steps.explanation: Always tell users about env var support in the help text!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CODE["add_argument('--api-key',
default=os.environ.get('API_KEY'),
help='API key (env: API_KEY)')"] HELP["--api-key KEY API key (env: API_KEY)"] CODE --> HELP style HELP fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Complete Pattern
steps.explanation: A reusable pattern for environment variable fallbacks with proper documentation.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Setup"
        S1["Read env vars"]
        S2["Set as defaults"]
        S3["Document in help"]
    end
    subgraph "Usage"
        U1["export API_KEY=xxx"]
        U2["myapp --other-flag"]
        U3["API key from env ✓"]
    end
    S3 --> U1 --> U2 --> U3
    style U3 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Config File + CLI Pattern
fields.description: Loading from config file, overriding with CLI args

steps.title: The Challenge
steps.explanation: Complex CLIs need many options. Users want: 1) config file for defaults, 2) CLI to override specific values. argparse doesn't do this natively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Priority"
        P1["1. CLI args (highest)"]
        P2["2. Config file"]
        P3["3. Hardcoded defaults"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Two-Phase Parsing
steps.explanation: Parse in two phases: first get the config file path, then load config and parse again with defaults from config.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB PHASE1["Phase 1: Get --config path"] LOAD["Load config file"] PHASE2["Phase 2: Parse all args
with config as defaults"] PHASE1 --> LOAD --> PHASE2 style PHASE2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: parse_known_args
steps.explanation: Use `parse_known_args()` in phase 1 to get the config file without errors for other args.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CODE["args, remaining = parser.parse_known_args()"]
    subgraph "Result"
        A["args.config → 'settings.yaml'"]
        R["remaining → ['--port', '8080', ...]"]
    end
    CODE --> A
    CODE --> R
    style A fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Loading Config
steps.explanation: Load config from YAML, JSON, or TOML. Then use `set_defaults()` to apply config values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    LOAD["config = yaml.safe_load(open(args.config))"]
    SET["parser.set_defaults(**config)"]
    PARSE["args = parser.parse_args()"]
    LOAD --> SET --> PARSE
    NOTE["CLI args still override!"]
    style PARSE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Config File Example
steps.explanation: A YAML config file with the same keys as CLI arguments (without dashes).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "config.yaml"
        Y1["port: 8080"]
        Y2["verbose: true"]
        Y3["output: results/"]
    end
    subgraph "CLI"
        C1["--port 8080"]
        C2["--verbose"]
        C3["--output results/"]
    end
    Y1 --> C1
    style Y1 fill:#BBDEFB
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Full Implementation
steps.explanation: The complete pattern: parse config path, load file, set defaults, parse all args.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Code flow" S1["parser.add_argument('--config')"] S2["args, _ = parser.parse_known_args()"] S3["if args.config:
config = load(args.config)"] S4["parser.set_defaults(**config)"] S5["args = parser.parse_args()"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 style S5 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Override Behavior
steps.explanation: CLI args override config values. Absent CLI args fall back to config. Absent config falls back to defaults.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "config.yaml" CF["port: 8080
verbose: true"] end subgraph "CLI" CL["--port 3000"] end subgraph "Result" R1["port: 3000 (CLI wins)"] R2["verbose: true (from config)"] end CF --> R2 CL --> R1 style R1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Alternative: fromfile_prefix_chars
steps.explanation: argparse has built-in `fromfile_prefix_chars`. Use `@config.txt` to read args from file. Simpler but less flexible.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CODE["ArgumentParser(fromfile_prefix_chars='@')"]
    subgraph "config.txt"
        F1["--port"]
        F2["8080"]
        F3["--verbose"]
    end
    CMD["$ myapp @config.txt --output out/"]
    NOTE["Args read from file"]
    CODE --> CMD
    style CMD fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Tips for config file + CLI patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["Use same names in config and CLI"]
        B2["Document config file format"]
        B3["Show loaded config path in --verbose"]
        B4["Support multiple config locations"]
        B5["Validate config file contents"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Conversation & Tool Call Flow
fields.description: How user input → Claude → tool calls → results → response works

steps.title: The Agentic Loop
steps.explanation: Claude Code operates in an **agentic loop**: you send a message, Claude thinks and may call tools, tools return results, Claude continues until the task is complete.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    USER["User Input"]
    CLAUDE["Claude Thinks"]
    TOOLS["Tool Calls"]
    RESULTS["Tool Results"]
    RESPONSE["Response to User"]
    USER --> CLAUDE
    CLAUDE --> TOOLS
    TOOLS --> RESULTS
    RESULTS --> CLAUDE
    CLAUDE --> RESPONSE
    style CLAUDE fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: User Sends Message
steps.explanation: You type a message or question. Claude Code sends this to the Claude API along with conversation history and system instructions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR USER["'Fix the bug in auth.py'"] CONTEXT["+ Conversation history
+ System prompt
+ Tool definitions"] API["Claude API"] USER --> CONTEXT --> API style USER fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Claude Analyzes & Plans
steps.explanation: Claude reads your request, considers the context, and decides what to do. It may need to read files, run commands, or ask clarifying questions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB INPUT["User request"] THINK["Claude analyzes:
- What's being asked?
- What info do I need?
- Which tools to use?"] DECIDE{"Need tools?"} INPUT --> THINK --> DECIDE DECIDE -->|Yes| TOOLS["Make tool calls"] DECIDE -->|No| RESPOND["Respond directly"] style THINK fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Tool Calls
steps.explanation: Claude can call multiple tools in one turn. Each tool call specifies the tool name and parameters. Tools run locally on your machine.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CLAUDE["Claude"]
    subgraph "Tool Calls"
        T1["Read: auth.py"]
        T2["Grep: 'error'"]
        T3["Bash: git status"]
    end
    CLAUDE --> T1
    CLAUDE --> T2
    CLAUDE --> T3
    NOTE["Tools run on YOUR machine"]
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Tool Execution
steps.explanation: Claude Code executes the tool calls locally. Some tools may require permission (like writing files or running commands).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CALL["Tool Call"]
    CHECK{"Needs permission?"}
    ASK["Ask user"]
    EXEC["Execute tool"]
    RESULT["Return result"]
    CALL --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|Yes| ASK --> EXEC
    CHECK -->|No| EXEC
    EXEC --> RESULT
    style EXEC fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Results Back to Claude
steps.explanation: Tool results are sent back to Claude. It sees file contents, command output, search results - everything needed to continue.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Tool Results"
        R1["File: 200 lines of code"]
        R2["Grep: 3 matches found"]
        R3["Bash: 'clean working tree'"]
    end
    CLAUDE["Claude processes results"]
    R1 --> CLAUDE
    R2 --> CLAUDE
    R3 --> CLAUDE
    style CLAUDE fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Iterative Processing
steps.explanation: Claude may need multiple rounds of tool calls. Read a file → find an issue → edit the file → run tests → fix test failures. This continues until done.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Turn 1"
        T1A["Read file"]
        T1B["Find bug"]
    end
    subgraph "Turn 2"
        T2A["Edit file"]
        T2B["Fix bug"]
    end
    subgraph "Turn 3"
        T3A["Run tests"]
        T3B["Verify fix"]
    end
    T1B --> T2A
    T2B --> T3A
    style T3B fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Final Response
steps.explanation: Once Claude has completed the task (or needs your input), it responds with text explaining what was done, what was found, or asking for clarification.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB DONE["Task complete"] RESPONSE["Claude responds:
'I fixed the bug in auth.py line 42.
The issue was a missing null check.
Tests now pass.'"] USER["User sees response"] DONE --> RESPONSE --> USER style RESPONSE fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Tool Architecture
fields.description: Overview of all tools: Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Task, etc.

steps.title: Tools Overview
steps.explanation: Claude Code has access to a set of **tools** - functions it can call to interact with your system. Each tool has a specific purpose and set of parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CLAUDE["Claude"]
    subgraph "Tools"
        FILES["File Tools"]
        SEARCH["Search Tools"]
        EXEC["Execution Tools"]
        AGENT["Agent Tools"]
    end
    CLAUDE --> FILES
    CLAUDE --> SEARCH
    CLAUDE --> EXEC
    CLAUDE --> AGENT
    style CLAUDE fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: File Reading Tools
steps.explanation: **Read** reads file contents. **Glob** finds files by pattern. These are the primary ways Claude explores your codebase.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Reading" READ["Read
Read file contents
Supports images, PDFs, notebooks"] GLOB["Glob
Find files by pattern
'**/*.py', 'src/**/*.ts'"] end EX1["Read: /path/to/file.py"] EX2["Glob: **/*.test.js"] READ --> EX1 GLOB --> EX2 style READ fill:#C8E6C9 style GLOB fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: File Writing Tools
steps.explanation: **Write** creates/overwrites files. **Edit** makes surgical replacements. Edit is preferred for modifying existing files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Writing" WRITE["Write
Create new file or
overwrite entirely"] EDIT["Edit
Replace specific text
Surgical changes"] end EX1["Write: Create new config.py"] EX2["Edit: Fix line 42 in auth.py"] WRITE --> EX1 EDIT --> EX2 style EDIT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Search Tools
steps.explanation: **Grep** searches file contents with regex. **Glob** finds files by name pattern. Together they help locate relevant code.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Search" GREP["Grep
Search file contents
Regex support, context lines"] GLOB["Glob
Search file names
Pattern matching"] end EX1["Grep: 'def authenticate' in **/*.py"] EX2["Glob: **/test_*.py"] GREP --> EX1 GLOB --> EX2 style GREP fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Execution Tools
steps.explanation: **Bash** runs shell commands. Powerful but sandboxed by default. Used for git, npm, pytest, docker, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB BASH["Bash
Run shell commands
Sandboxed by default"] subgraph "Common uses" U1["git status, commit, push"] U2["npm install, npm test"] U3["pytest, ruff check"] U4["docker, kubectl"] end BASH --> U1 BASH --> U2 BASH --> U3 BASH --> U4 style BASH fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Agent Tools
steps.explanation: **Task** spawns sub-agents for complex work. Different agent types: Explore, Plan, Bash specialist, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB TASK["Task
Spawn sub-agents"] subgraph "Agent Types" EXPLORE["Explore
Codebase exploration"] PLAN["Plan
Implementation planning"] BASH_AGENT["Bash
Command specialist"] GENERAL["General
Multi-step tasks"] end TASK --> EXPLORE TASK --> PLAN TASK --> BASH_AGENT TASK --> GENERAL style TASK fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Interaction Tools
steps.explanation: Tools for interacting with you and the web: **AskUserQuestion**, **WebSearch**, **WebFetch**.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Interaction" ASK["AskUserQuestion
Get user input/choices"] SEARCH["WebSearch
Search the web"] FETCH["WebFetch
Fetch URL content"] end EX1["Ask: Which auth method?"] EX2["Search: React 19 new features"] EX3["Fetch: API documentation"] ASK --> EX1 SEARCH --> EX2 FETCH --> EX3 style ASK fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Planning & Tracking Tools
steps.explanation: Tools for organizing work: **TodoWrite** for task tracking, **EnterPlanMode**/**ExitPlanMode** for planning workflows.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Planning" TODO["TodoWrite
Track task progress"] ENTER["EnterPlanMode
Start planning"] EXIT["ExitPlanMode
Finish planning"] end subgraph "Workflow" W1["Create todo list"] W2["Mark tasks complete"] W3["Plan before coding"] end TODO --> W1 TODO --> W2 ENTER --> W3 style TODO fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Tool Selection
steps.explanation: Claude chooses tools based on the task. It prefers specialized tools (Read over cat, Edit over sed) for better results.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TASK["Task: Read a file"]
    subgraph "Prefer"
        GOOD["Read tool ✓"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        BAD["Bash: cat file ✗"]
    end
    TASK --> GOOD
    TASK -.-> BAD
    NOTE["Specialized tools > generic Bash"]
    style GOOD fill:#C8E6C9
    style BAD fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Context Window Management
fields.description: How conversation history is managed, truncated, and summarized

steps.title: The Context Window
steps.explanation: Claude has a **context window** - the maximum amount of text it can process at once. Claude Code manages this automatically so conversations can continue indefinitely.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Context Window"
        SYS["System prompt"]
        HIST["Conversation history"]
        TOOLS["Tool definitions"]
        CURR["Current message"]
    end
    LIMIT["~200K tokens max"]
    style LIMIT fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: What's in Context
steps.explanation: Each API call includes: system instructions, tool definitions, conversation history (messages + tool results), and your current input.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Sent to Claude" S1["System prompt
(instructions, rules)"] S2["Tool schemas
(available tools)"] S3["Prior messages
(user + assistant)"] S4["Tool results
(file contents, etc.)"] S5["Current input"] end S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 style S4 fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Context Fills Up
steps.explanation: Long conversations accumulate history. File contents, command outputs, and tool results can be large. Eventually context fills up.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Growing Context"
        M1["Message 1"]
        M2["Message 2"]
        M3["+ Large file read"]
        M4["Message 3"]
        M5["+ Command output"]
        M6["Message 4..."]
    end
    FULL["Context getting full!"]
    M6 --> FULL
    style FULL fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Automatic Summarization
steps.explanation: When context gets too large, Claude Code automatically **summarizes** older parts of the conversation. Key information is preserved, verbatim details are condensed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Before" B1["Full message 1"] B2["Full file contents"] B3["Full message 2"] end SUMMARIZE["Summarize"] subgraph "After" A1["Summary: 'Discussed auth bug,
read auth.py, found issue on line 42'"] end B1 --> SUMMARIZE B2 --> SUMMARIZE B3 --> SUMMARIZE SUMMARIZE --> A1 style A1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: What Gets Preserved
steps.explanation: Summarization preserves: what tasks were completed, key decisions made, important findings, and current state. Recent messages stay verbatim.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Preserved"
        P1["✓ Tasks completed"]
        P2["✓ Key decisions"]
        P3["✓ Important findings"]
        P4["✓ Current state"]
    end
    subgraph "Condensed"
        C1["○ Exact file contents"]
        C2["○ Full command outputs"]
        C3["○ Exploration attempts"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style C1 fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Unlimited Conversations
steps.explanation: Thanks to summarization, conversations can continue indefinitely. You won't hit a wall - Claude Code handles context management transparently.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    START["Start"]
    WORK["Work..."]
    SUMMARIZE["Auto-summarize"]
    MORE["More work..."]
    SUMMARIZE2["Auto-summarize"]
    CONTINUE["Continue..."]
    START --> WORK --> SUMMARIZE --> MORE --> SUMMARIZE2 --> CONTINUE
    style SUMMARIZE fill:#BBDEFB
    style SUMMARIZE2 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Help Claude Code manage context: be specific in requests, don't paste huge files in chat (let Claude read them), and start new conversations for unrelated tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good"
        G1["'Read auth.py and fix the bug'"]
        G2["Let Claude use Read tool"]
        G3["New chat for new project"]
    end
    subgraph "Avoid"
        B1["Pasting 1000 lines in chat"]
        B2["Unrelated tasks in one chat"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#FFCDD2

fields.title: Agent/Subagent System
fields.description: How the Task tool spawns specialized agents (Explore, Plan, Bash, etc.)

steps.title: Why Subagents?
steps.explanation: Complex tasks benefit from **specialized agents**. Instead of one agent doing everything, Claude Code can spawn focused sub-agents for specific work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    MAIN["Main Claude"]
    subgraph "Subagents"
        E["Explore Agent"]
        P["Plan Agent"]
        B["Bash Agent"]
    end
    MAIN -->|"spawn"| E
    MAIN -->|"spawn"| P
    MAIN -->|"spawn"| B
    style MAIN fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: The Task Tool
steps.explanation: The **Task** tool spawns subagents. You specify the agent type, a prompt, and the agent works autonomously, returning results when done.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TASK["Task Tool"]
    subgraph "Parameters"
        TYPE["subagent_type: 'Explore'"]
        PROMPT["prompt: 'Find auth code'"]
        BG["run_in_background: false"]
    end
    TASK --> TYPE
    TASK --> PROMPT
    TASK --> BG
    style TASK fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Explore Agent
steps.explanation: **Explore** agent is optimized for codebase exploration. Use it for finding files, understanding structure, searching for patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    EXPLORE["Explore Agent"]
    subgraph "Good for"
        U1["Finding files by pattern"]
        U2["Searching code for keywords"]
        U3["Understanding codebase structure"]
        U4["Answering 'where is X?'"]
    end
    EXPLORE --> U1
    EXPLORE --> U2
    EXPLORE --> U3
    style EXPLORE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Plan Agent
steps.explanation: **Plan** agent designs implementation strategies. Use it before coding to think through architecture, identify files to change, consider trade-offs.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PLAN["Plan Agent"]
    subgraph "Good for"
        U1["Designing implementations"]
        U2["Identifying critical files"]
        U3["Considering trade-offs"]
        U4["Step-by-step plans"]
    end
    PLAN --> U1
    PLAN --> U2
    PLAN --> U3
    style PLAN fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Bash Agent
steps.explanation: **Bash** agent specializes in command execution. Use for git operations, running builds, executing terminal tasks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    BASH["Bash Agent"]
    subgraph "Good for"
        U1["Git operations"]
        U2["Running builds"]
        U3["Terminal tasks"]
        U4["Command sequences"]
    end
    BASH --> U1
    BASH --> U2
    BASH --> U3
    style BASH fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: General-Purpose Agent
steps.explanation: **General-purpose** agent handles complex multi-step tasks. It has access to all tools and works autonomously.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB GENERAL["General-Purpose Agent"] subgraph "Has access to" T1["All tools"] T2["Full context"] T3["Multi-step reasoning"] end USE["Use for complex
autonomous tasks"] GENERAL --> T1 GENERAL --> USE style GENERAL fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Background Agents
steps.explanation: Agents can run in the **background** with `run_in_background: true`. Main conversation continues while agent works. Check results later.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB MAIN["Main conversation"] BG["Background agent
(running tests)"] CONTINUE["Continue chatting..."] CHECK["Check agent results"] MAIN --> BG MAIN --> CONTINUE CONTINUE --> CHECK style BG fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Agent Communication
steps.explanation: Agents return results to the main conversation. The main Claude sees what the agent found/did and incorporates it into its response.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB MAIN["Main Claude"] SPAWN["Spawn Explore agent"] AGENT["Agent explores..."] RESULT["Returns: 'Found auth in
src/auth/handler.py'"] USE["Main Claude uses result"] MAIN --> SPAWN --> AGENT --> RESULT --> USE style RESULT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Parallel Agents
steps.explanation: Multiple agents can run in **parallel** when tasks are independent. Claude Code launches them simultaneously for efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    MAIN["Main Claude"]
    subgraph "Parallel"
        A1["Agent 1: Search frontend"]
        A2["Agent 2: Search backend"]
        A3["Agent 3: Check tests"]
    end
    MAIN --> A1
    MAIN --> A2
    MAIN --> A3
    RESULTS["All results combined"]
    A1 --> RESULTS
    A2 --> RESULTS
    A3 --> RESULTS
    style RESULTS fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Sandbox Model
fields.description: Filesystem and network restrictions, when sandbox is bypassed

steps.title: Why Sandbox?
steps.explanation: Claude Code runs commands on your machine. The **sandbox** provides safety by restricting what commands can access - limiting filesystem and network by default.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["Bash command"]
    SANDBOX["Sandbox"]
    subgraph "Restricted"
        FS["Filesystem access"]
        NET["Network access"]
    end
    CMD --> SANDBOX --> FS
    SANDBOX --> NET
    style SANDBOX fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Filesystem Restrictions
steps.explanation: By default, commands can only **write** to the current project directory and `/tmp/claude`. Reading is more permissive but still restricted.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Write Allowed"
        W1["Current directory (.)"]
        W2["/tmp/claude/"]
    end
    subgraph "Write Denied"
        D1["Home directory (~)"]
        D2["System files"]
        D3[".env files"]
    end
    style W1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Network Restrictions
steps.explanation: Network access is limited to an allowlist of hosts. Common development services are allowed (GitHub, PyPI, npm). Others are blocked.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Allowed Hosts"
        A1["github.com"]
        A2["api.github.com"]
        A3["pypi.org"]
        A4["api.anthropic.com"]
    end
    subgraph "Blocked"
        B1["Random URLs"]
        B2["Internal networks"]
    end
    style A1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style B1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Sandbox Violations
steps.explanation: When a command hits sandbox restrictions, it fails with 'Operation not permitted'. Claude Code detects this and can retry with sandbox disabled (with your permission).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["Command runs"]
    BLOCK["Sandbox blocks"]
    ERR["'Operation not permitted'"]
    DETECT["Claude detects violation"]
    RETRY["Retry without sandbox?"]
    CMD --> BLOCK --> ERR --> DETECT --> RETRY
    style ERR fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Disabling Sandbox
steps.explanation: Claude can set `dangerouslyDisableSandbox: true` to bypass restrictions. This requires **your permission** via a prompt.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CLAUDE["Claude requests"]
    PARAM["dangerouslyDisableSandbox: true"]
    PROMPT["User prompted for permission"]
    ALLOW{"Allow?"}
    RUN["Command runs unrestricted"]
    DENY["Command blocked"]
    CLAUDE --> PARAM --> PROMPT --> ALLOW
    ALLOW -->|Yes| RUN
    ALLOW -->|No| DENY
    style PROMPT fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: When Sandbox is Bypassed
steps.explanation: Sandbox should only be disabled when: 1) You explicitly ask, or 2) A command failed due to sandbox restrictions. Claude explains why it's needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Bypass OK"
        B1["User explicitly requests"]
        B2["Command failed with permission error"]
    end
    subgraph "Should Not Bypass"
        N1["Just because it's convenient"]
        N2["Without clear reason"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Managing Sandbox Settings
steps.explanation: Use `/sandbox` command to manage restrictions. Add allowed paths, hosts, or see current settings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["/sandbox"]
    subgraph "Options"
        O1["View current settings"]
        O2["Add allowed paths"]
        O3["Add allowed hosts"]
        O4["Reset to defaults"]
    end
    CMD --> O1
    CMD --> O2
    style CMD fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Security Best Practices
steps.explanation: Keep sandbox enabled by default. Only disable for specific operations. Review what Claude wants to do before allowing unrestricted access.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        P1["Keep sandbox ON by default"]
        P2["Review before allowing bypass"]
        P3["Don't add sensitive paths to allowlist"]
        P4["Use /sandbox to check settings"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Permission Flow
fields.description: How Claude Code requests and handles user permissions

steps.title: Permission System
steps.explanation: Claude Code asks for permission before potentially dangerous operations. This keeps you in control while allowing Claude to work efficiently.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    ACTION["Claude wants to act"]
    CHECK{"Needs permission?"}
    ASK["Ask user"]
    ALLOW{"Allowed?"}
    EXEC["Execute"]
    SKIP["Skip/modify"]
    ACTION --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|Yes| ASK --> ALLOW
    CHECK -->|No| EXEC
    ALLOW -->|Yes| EXEC
    ALLOW -->|No| SKIP
    style ASK fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: What Requires Permission
steps.explanation: Operations that modify your system typically require permission: writing files, running bash commands (especially without sandbox), making network requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Needs Permission"
        P1["Write/Edit files"]
        P2["Bash commands"]
        P3["Sandbox bypass"]
        P4["Some MCP tools"]
    end
    subgraph "No Permission Needed"
        N1["Read files"]
        N2["Glob/Grep search"]
        N3["Ask questions"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFF9C4
    style N1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Permission Prompt
steps.explanation: When permission is needed, Claude Code shows what it wants to do and waits for your approval. You can approve, deny, or modify.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PROMPT["Claude wants to edit auth.py"]
    subgraph "Your options"
        Y["Yes - allow"]
        N["No - deny"]
        A["Always - allow this type"]
        E["Edit - modify first"]
    end
    PROMPT --> Y
    PROMPT --> N
    PROMPT --> A
    style PROMPT fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: Auto-Allow Rules
steps.explanation: Selecting 'Always' creates an auto-allow rule. That operation type won't ask again. Rules can be project-specific or global.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    ALWAYS["Select 'Always'"]
    RULE["Rule created"]
    FUTURE["Future same actions"]
    AUTO["Auto-approved"]
    ALWAYS --> RULE --> FUTURE --> AUTO
    NOTE["Check .claude/settings.json"]
    style AUTO fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Permission Modes
steps.explanation: Claude Code can run in different permission modes: ask for everything, auto-allow safe ops, or full auto (use with caution).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Modes"
        M1["Default: Ask for writes/bash"]
        M2["Yolo mode: Less asking"]
        M3["Custom allowlists"]
    end
    SETTING["Configure in settings"]
    M1 --> SETTING
    M2 --> SETTING
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Viewing Permissions
steps.explanation: Check current permission settings with `/permissions` or look at `.claude/settings.json` in your project.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CHECK["/permissions"]
    subgraph "Shows"
        S1["Auto-allowed operations"]
        S2["Denied patterns"]
        S3["Current mode"]
    end
    FILE[".claude/settings.json"]
    CHECK --> S1
    FILE --> S1
    style CHECK fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Revoking Permissions
steps.explanation: You can revoke auto-allow rules anytime. Edit settings or use commands to tighten permissions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    REVOKE["Want to revoke"]
    subgraph "Methods"
        M1["Edit .claude/settings.json"]
        M2["Use /permissions command"]
        M3["Delete and recreate rules"]
    end
    REVOKE --> M1
    REVOKE --> M2
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: Review before allowing. Use 'Always' sparingly. Keep sensitive operations requiring approval. Balance safety with workflow efficiency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good Practices"
        G1["Review what Claude wants to do"]
        G2["Use 'Always' for safe, common ops"]
        G3["Keep bash commands requiring approval"]
        G4["Project-specific rules when possible"]
    end
    style G1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Hooks System
fields.description: Pre/post hooks for tool calls, user prompt hooks

steps.title: What Are Hooks?
steps.explanation: **Hooks** are shell commands that run automatically at certain points. They let you customize Claude Code's behavior - run linters, format code, trigger builds, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    EVENT["Event occurs"]
    HOOK["Hook runs"]
    CONTINUE["Continue"]
    EVENT --> HOOK --> CONTINUE
    EX["e.g., After file edit → run formatter"]
    style HOOK fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Hook Types
steps.explanation: Several hook points are available: before/after tool calls, on user prompt submit, and more. Each triggers at a different moment.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Hook Points" H1["PreToolCall
Before tool runs"] H2["PostToolCall
After tool completes"] H3["UserPromptSubmit
When user sends message"] end NOTE["Configure in settings"] style H2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: PreToolCall Hooks
steps.explanation: Run **before** a tool executes. Can validate, log, or even block the operation. Receives tool name and parameters.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TOOL["Tool: Edit auth.py"]
    PRE["PreToolCall hook"]
    subgraph "Hook can"
        V["Validate params"]
        L["Log operation"]
        B["Block if needed"]
    end
    EXEC["Tool executes"]
    TOOL --> PRE --> V
    PRE --> EXEC
    style PRE fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: PostToolCall Hooks
steps.explanation: Run **after** a tool completes. Perfect for: running formatters after edits, running tests after changes, notifications.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TOOL["Edit completes"]
    POST["PostToolCall hook"]
    subgraph "Common uses"
        F["Run prettier/black"]
        T["Run tests"]
        N["Send notification"]
    end
    TOOL --> POST --> F
    style POST fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Configuring Hooks
steps.explanation: Hooks are configured in `.claude/settings.json`. Specify the event, matcher (which tools), and command to run.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "settings.json" H["hooks: [
{
event: 'PostToolCall',
matcher: 'Edit',
command: 'npm run format'
}
]"] end RESULT["Runs formatter after every Edit"] H --> RESULT style H fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Hook Matchers
steps.explanation: Matchers filter which tools trigger the hook. Match by tool name, file patterns, or other criteria.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Matcher Examples"
        M1["'Edit' → all Edit calls"]
        M2["'Edit:*.py' → Python edits"]
        M3["'Bash' → all bash commands"]
        M4["'*' → all tools"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Hook Output
steps.explanation: Hook output is shown to Claude. If a hook fails (non-zero exit), Claude sees the error and can respond. Hooks can provide feedback!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    HOOK["Hook runs"]
    OUTPUT["Output/errors"]
    CLAUDE["Claude sees output"]
    RESPOND["Claude responds accordingly"]
    HOOK --> OUTPUT --> CLAUDE --> RESPOND
    EX["e.g., 'Lint errors found on line 42'"]
    style OUTPUT fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Common Hook Patterns
steps.explanation: Popular hook setups: auto-format on edit, run tests on file changes, lint before commit, notify on completion.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Popular Hooks"
        P1["PostEdit → prettier/black"]
        P2["PostEdit → eslint/ruff"]
        P3["PostBash:git commit → run tests"]
        P4["UserPromptSubmit → log prompts"]
    end
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Settings Hierarchy
fields.description: User settings vs project settings vs managed settings

steps.title: Multiple Settings Levels
steps.explanation: Claude Code has settings at multiple levels: global user settings, project-specific settings, and organization-managed settings. They're merged with specific precedence.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Settings Levels"
        M["Managed (org)"]
        U["User (~/.claude)"]
        P["Project (.claude/)"]
    end
    MERGE["Merged settings"]
    M --> MERGE
    U --> MERGE
    P --> MERGE
    style P fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: User Settings
steps.explanation: Global settings in `~/.claude/settings.json`. Apply to all projects. Set your preferences, default behaviors, API keys.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PATH["~/.claude/settings.json"]
    subgraph "Contains"
        S1["Default model preferences"]
        S2["Global permissions"]
        S3["Theme/display settings"]
        S4["API configuration"]
    end
    PATH --> S1
    style PATH fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Project Settings
steps.explanation: Project-specific settings in `.claude/settings.json`. Override user settings for this project. Can be committed to git for team sharing.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PATH[".claude/settings.json"]
    subgraph "Contains"
        S1["Project-specific rules"]
        S2["Allowed operations"]
        S3["Hooks for this project"]
        S4["Custom instructions"]
    end
    GIT["Can commit to git!"]
    PATH --> S1
    PATH --> GIT
    style PATH fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Local Settings
steps.explanation: `.claude/settings.local.json` for personal project settings that shouldn't be shared. Gitignored by default.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PATH[".claude/settings.local.json"]
    subgraph "Use for"
        S1["Personal preferences"]
        S2["Local paths"]
        S3["Secrets/tokens"]
    end
    IGNORE["Gitignored - not shared"]
    PATH --> S1
    PATH --> IGNORE
    style IGNORE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Managed Settings
steps.explanation: Organizations can push managed settings. These take highest precedence and can't be overridden locally. For enterprise policies.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    ORG["Organization admin"]
    MANAGED["Managed settings"]
    POLICY["Enforced policies"]
    LOCAL["Can't override locally"]
    ORG --> MANAGED --> POLICY
    MANAGED --> LOCAL
    style MANAGED fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Precedence Order
steps.explanation: Settings merge with this precedence (highest to lowest): Managed → Project local → Project → User. Higher levels override lower.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Precedence (high to low)"
        P1["1. Managed (org)"]
        P2["2. Project local"]
        P3["3. Project shared"]
        P4["4. User global"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    NOTE["Higher overrides lower"]
    style P1 fill:#E1BEE7
    style P3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: CLAUDE.md Files
steps.explanation: `CLAUDE.md` files provide project instructions to Claude. They're read automatically and inform Claude about the codebase.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "CLAUDE.md locations"
        ROOT["./CLAUDE.md"]
        SUB["./subdir/CLAUDE.md"]
        PARENT["../CLAUDE.md"]
    end
    CLAUDE["Claude reads these"]
    ROOT --> CLAUDE
    SUB --> CLAUDE
    NOTE["Project-specific instructions"]
    style ROOT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Viewing Effective Settings
steps.explanation: See the merged effective settings with `/config` or by checking what Claude knows about your settings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["/config"]
    subgraph "Shows"
        S1["Effective merged settings"]
        S2["Where each setting comes from"]
        S3["Current permissions"]
    end
    CMD --> S1
    style CMD fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: MCP Server Architecture
fields.description: How Model Context Protocol servers extend capabilities

steps.title: What is MCP?
steps.explanation: **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** is a standard for connecting AI assistants to external tools and data sources. MCP servers extend Claude Code's capabilities.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    CLAUDE["Claude Code"]
    MCP["MCP Protocol"]
    subgraph "MCP Servers"
        S1["Database"]
        S2["APIs"]
        S3["Custom tools"]
    end
    CLAUDE --> MCP --> S1
    MCP --> S2
    MCP --> S3
    style MCP fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: MCP Components
steps.explanation: MCP has three main components: **Servers** (provide tools/data), **Clients** (consume tools), and the **Protocol** (how they communicate).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "MCP Architecture" CLIENT["Client
(Claude Code)"] PROTOCOL["Protocol
(JSON-RPC)"] SERVER["Server
(provides tools)"] end CLIENT --> PROTOCOL --> SERVER style PROTOCOL fill:#E3F2FD

steps.title: What MCP Servers Provide
steps.explanation: MCP servers can provide: **Tools** (functions Claude can call), **Resources** (data/context), and **Prompts** (predefined instructions).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB SERVER["MCP Server"] subgraph "Provides" T["Tools
Functions to call"] R["Resources
Data/context"] P["Prompts
Instructions"] end SERVER --> T SERVER --> R SERVER --> P style T fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Example MCP Servers
steps.explanation: Popular MCP servers: filesystem access, database queries, GitHub integration, Slack, web browsing, and many more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Common MCP Servers" S1["Filesystem
Read/write files"] S2["PostgreSQL
Query databases"] S3["GitHub
Issues, PRs, repos"] S4["Slack
Messages, channels"] S5["Puppeteer
Web automation"] end style S3 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Configuring MCP Servers
steps.explanation: Add MCP servers in settings. Specify the server command, arguments, and any environment variables needed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "settings.json" CONFIG["mcpServers: {
'github': {
command: 'npx',
args: ['@mcp/github'],
env: { GITHUB_TOKEN: '...' }
}
}"] end RESULT["GitHub tools available"] CONFIG --> RESULT style CONFIG fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: How Claude Uses MCP Tools
steps.explanation: MCP tools appear alongside built-in tools. Claude can call them the same way - they're seamlessly integrated.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CLAUDE["Claude"] subgraph "Available Tools" BUILTIN["Built-in: Read, Edit, Bash"] MCP["MCP: github_create_issue,
db_query, slack_post"] end CLAUDE --> BUILTIN CLAUDE --> MCP style MCP fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: MCP Server Lifecycle
steps.explanation: MCP servers start when needed and can stay running. Claude Code manages the connection automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    START["Claude needs tool"]
    SPAWN["Start MCP server"]
    CONNECT["Establish connection"]
    USE["Call tools"]
    KEEP["Keep alive for reuse"]
    START --> SPAWN --> CONNECT --> USE --> KEEP
    style USE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Building Custom MCP Servers
steps.explanation: You can build custom MCP servers for your specific needs. SDKs available for Python, TypeScript, and more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB subgraph "Build with" PY["Python SDK
mcp-python"] TS["TypeScript SDK
@modelcontextprotocol/sdk"] end CUSTOM["Custom server for
your internal tools"] PY --> CUSTOM TS --> CUSTOM style CUSTOM fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Plan Mode Workflow
fields.description: EnterPlanMode → explore → plan → ExitPlanMode → implement

steps.title: What is Plan Mode?
steps.explanation: **Plan Mode** is for designing before coding. Claude explores the codebase, creates a plan, and gets your approval before implementing. Prevents wasted effort!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    TASK["Complex task"]
    PLAN["Plan Mode"]
    APPROVE["User approves"]
    IMPLEMENT["Implement"]
    TASK --> PLAN --> APPROVE --> IMPLEMENT
    style PLAN fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: When to Use Plan Mode
steps.explanation: Use Plan Mode for: new features, architectural changes, multi-file modifications, unclear requirements. Skip for simple fixes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Use Plan Mode"
        U1["New features"]
        U2["Architecture changes"]
        U3["Multiple files affected"]
        U4["Unclear requirements"]
    end
    subgraph "Skip Plan Mode"
        S1["Simple bug fixes"]
        S2["Single-line changes"]
        S3["Clear, small tasks"]
    end
    style U1 fill:#E1BEE7
    style S1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Entering Plan Mode
steps.explanation: Claude uses `EnterPlanMode` tool (or you can request it). This shifts Claude to planning-focused behavior - no code changes yet.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    REQUEST["'Plan how to add auth'"]
    ENTER["EnterPlanMode"]
    MODE["Now in Plan Mode"]
    FOCUS["Focus: explore & design"]
    REQUEST --> ENTER --> MODE --> FOCUS
    style MODE fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: Exploration Phase
steps.explanation: In Plan Mode, Claude explores: reads relevant files, understands existing patterns, identifies what needs to change. No modifications yet!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Plan Mode Activities"
        E1["Read existing code"]
        E2["Understand patterns"]
        E3["Identify files to change"]
        E4["Consider trade-offs"]
    end
    NO["NO edits or writes"]
    E4 --> NO
    style NO fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Writing the Plan
steps.explanation: Claude writes a detailed plan to a file (often `PLAN.md`). Lists steps, files to modify, approach, and any questions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PLAN["Plan document"]
    subgraph "Contents"
        C1["Overview"]
        C2["Files to modify"]
        C3["Step-by-step approach"]
        C4["Open questions"]
    end
    PLAN --> C1
    PLAN --> C2
    PLAN --> C3
    style PLAN fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Exiting Plan Mode
steps.explanation: Claude calls `ExitPlanMode` to signal the plan is ready. You review and approve (or request changes) before implementation begins.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    EXIT["ExitPlanMode"]
    REVIEW["You review plan"]
    DECIDE{"Approve?"}
    IMPLEMENT["Start implementing"]
    REVISE["Revise plan"]
    EXIT --> REVIEW --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -->|Yes| IMPLEMENT
    DECIDE -->|No| REVISE
    style DECIDE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Implementation Phase
steps.explanation: After approval, Claude implements the plan step-by-step. The plan serves as a checklist. Progress is visible and trackable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    PLAN["Approved plan"]
    subgraph "Implementation"
        I1["Step 1: Create model ✓"]
        I2["Step 2: Add routes ✓"]
        I3["Step 3: Write tests..."]
    end
    PLAN --> I1 --> I2 --> I3
    style I1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style I2 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Benefits of Plan Mode
steps.explanation: Plan Mode: catches issues early, ensures alignment, provides clear progress tracking, and creates documentation of decisions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Benefits"
        B1["Catch issues early"]
        B2["Align on approach"]
        B3["Track progress"]
        B4["Document decisions"]
        B5["Avoid rework"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Git Commit Flow
fields.description: How Claude Code handles git operations safely

steps.title: Git Safety Protocol
steps.explanation: Claude Code follows strict git safety rules: only commits when asked, never force pushes, respects hooks, and follows conventional commit style.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    GIT["Git Operations"]
    subgraph "Safety Rules"
        R1["Only commit when asked"]
        R2["Never force push"]
        R3["Respect git hooks"]
        R4["No destructive ops"]
    end
    GIT --> R1
    GIT --> R2
    style R1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When Claude Commits
steps.explanation: Claude only creates commits when you explicitly ask. It won't proactively commit changes - you stay in control of your git history.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Will Commit"
        Y1["'Commit these changes'"]
        Y2["'Create a commit'"]
        Y3["'Save this to git'"]
    end
    subgraph "Won't Commit"
        N1["After making edits"]
        N2["Automatically"]
    end
    style Y1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Pre-Commit Checks
steps.explanation: Before committing, Claude checks: git status, staged changes, recent commit style. Ensures the commit is appropriate.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    REQUEST["'Commit the changes'"]
    subgraph "Checks"
        C1["git status"]
        C2["git diff --staged"]
        C3["git log (commit style)"]
    end
    ANALYZE["Analyze changes"]
    REQUEST --> C1 --> ANALYZE
    REQUEST --> C2 --> ANALYZE
    REQUEST --> C3 --> ANALYZE
    style ANALYZE fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Commit Message Style
steps.explanation: Claude follows **conventional commits**: `type(scope): description`. Adds Co-Authored-By line. Explains 'why' not just 'what'.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Message Format"
        F1["type(scope): short summary"]
        F2[""]
        F3["Body explains WHY"]
        F4[""]
        F5["Co-Authored-By: Claude..."]
    end
    EX["feat(auth): add password reset flow"]
    style EX fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Commit Types
steps.explanation: Standard types: feat (new feature), fix (bug fix), refactor (code change), test (tests), docs (documentation), chore (maintenance).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Commit Types"
        T1["feat → new feature"]
        T2["fix → bug fix"]
        T3["refactor → restructure"]
        T4["test → add tests"]
        T5["docs → documentation"]
        T6["chore → maintenance"]
    end
    style T1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style T2 fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Handling Pre-Commit Hooks
steps.explanation: If pre-commit hooks modify files (formatters, linters), Claude handles it gracefully. May need a follow-up commit for hook changes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    COMMIT["Commit attempt"]
    HOOK["Pre-commit hook runs"]
    MODIFY{"Files modified?"}
    SUCCESS["Commit succeeds"]
    AMEND["Amend to include changes"]
    COMMIT --> HOOK --> MODIFY
    MODIFY -->|No| SUCCESS
    MODIFY -->|Yes| AMEND
    style HOOK fill:#FFE0B2

steps.title: Dangerous Operations Avoided
steps.explanation: Claude never does: force push, hard reset, rebase published commits, skip hooks, or modify git config. These require explicit user action.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Claude NEVER does"
        N1["git push --force"]
        N2["git reset --hard"]
        N3["git rebase (published)"]
        N4["--no-verify"]
        N5["Modify .gitconfig"]
    end
    SAFE["Your history is safe"]
    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2
    style SAFE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Pull Request Creation
steps.explanation: Claude can create PRs using `gh` CLI. Follows a template: summary, test plan, and links. Never pushes without asking.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    REQUEST["'Create a PR'"]
    CHECK["Check branch status"]
    PUSH["Push if needed (asks first)"]
    PR["gh pr create"]
    DONE["PR created with summary"]
    REQUEST --> CHECK --> PUSH --> PR --> DONE
    style DONE fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Multi-file Edit Pattern
fields.description: Read → understand → Edit → verify workflow

steps.title: The Pattern
steps.explanation: When modifying code, Claude follows a disciplined pattern: **Read** files first, **Understand** the context, **Edit** carefully, **Verify** the changes work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    READ["Read"]
    UNDERSTAND["Understand"]
    EDIT["Edit"]
    VERIFY["Verify"]
    READ --> UNDERSTAND --> EDIT --> VERIFY
    style READ fill:#BBDEFB
    style EDIT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Read First
steps.explanation: Claude **always** reads files before modifying them. This ensures it understands current code, doesn't break existing functionality, and maintains consistency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TASK["Modify auth.py"]
    READ["Read auth.py"]
    UNDERSTAND["See current implementation"]
    EDIT["Make informed edits"]
    TASK --> READ --> UNDERSTAND --> EDIT
    RULE["NEVER edit without reading!"]
    style RULE fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Understanding Context
steps.explanation: After reading, Claude analyzes: coding style, patterns used, related functions, imports needed, test coverage. This informs the edit strategy.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    READ["File contents"]
    subgraph "Analyze"
        A1["Coding style"]
        A2["Existing patterns"]
        A3["Dependencies"]
        A4["Test coverage"]
    end
    PLAN["Plan the edit"]
    READ --> A1 --> PLAN
    READ --> A2 --> PLAN
    style PLAN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Surgical Edits
steps.explanation: Claude uses the **Edit** tool for precise changes - replace specific text, not rewrite entire files. This minimizes diff size and risk.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Edit Tool"
        OLD["old_string: exact text"]
        NEW["new_string: replacement"]
    end
    RESULT["Only changed lines modified"]
    OLD --> NEW --> RESULT
    BETTER["Better than Write for existing files"]
    style BETTER fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Multiple Related Edits
steps.explanation: When changes span multiple files, Claude plans all edits together. Maintains consistency across files - imports match exports, types align.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TASK["Add new feature"]
    subgraph "Coordinated Edits"
        E1["Edit model.py"]
        E2["Edit views.py"]
        E3["Edit tests.py"]
    end
    TASK --> E1
    TASK --> E2
    TASK --> E3
    CONSISTENT["All changes consistent"]
    E3 --> CONSISTENT
    style CONSISTENT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Verification
steps.explanation: After editing, Claude verifies: runs tests, checks for syntax errors, ensures the change works. Catches problems immediately.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    EDIT["Edits complete"]
    subgraph "Verify"
        V1["Run tests"]
        V2["Check syntax"]
        V3["Lint code"]
    end
    PASS{"Pass?"}
    DONE["Complete"]
    FIX["Fix issues"]
    EDIT --> V1 --> PASS
    PASS -->|Yes| DONE
    PASS -->|No| FIX
    style DONE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Handling Edit Failures
steps.explanation: If an edit fails (string not found, not unique), Claude reads the file again to understand current state and retries with correct context.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    EDIT["Edit attempt"]
    FAIL["'String not unique'"]
    REREAD["Re-read file"]
    UNDERSTAND["Find correct context"]
    RETRY["Retry with more context"]
    EDIT --> FAIL --> REREAD --> UNDERSTAND --> RETRY
    style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2
    style RETRY fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Best Practices
steps.explanation: For reliable multi-file edits: read before editing, use Edit not Write for existing files, verify changes, keep edits focused.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Best Practices"
        B1["Always read first"]
        B2["Use Edit for existing files"]
        B3["Keep changes minimal"]
        B4["Verify after editing"]
        B5["Handle failures gracefully"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Parallel Tool Execution
fields.description: When and how tools run in parallel vs sequential

steps.title: Parallel vs Sequential
steps.explanation: Claude Code can run multiple tools in **parallel** (simultaneously) or **sequential** (one after another). Choosing correctly improves speed and correctness.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Parallel"
        P1["Tool A"]
        P2["Tool B"]
        P3["Tool C"]
    end
    subgraph "Sequential"
        S1["Tool A"] --> S2["Tool B"] --> S3["Tool C"]
    end
    FAST["Faster"]
    SAFE["Safer for dependencies"]
    P1 --> FAST
    S3 --> SAFE
    style FAST fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Parallelize
steps.explanation: Run tools in **parallel** when they're independent - no tool needs another's output. Reading multiple files, searching in different places.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Independent → Parallel"
        R1["Read auth.py"]
        R2["Read users.py"]
        R3["Read tests.py"]
    end
    NOTE["None depends on others"]
    PARALLEL["Run all at once!"]
    R1 --> PARALLEL
    R2 --> PARALLEL
    R3 --> PARALLEL
    style PARALLEL fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Sequence
steps.explanation: Run tools **sequentially** when outputs feed into inputs. Read file → Edit file. Create directory → Write file inside it.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Dependent → Sequential"
        S1["Read file"]
        S2["Edit based on contents"]
        S3["Run tests on edited file"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    NOTE["Each needs previous result"]
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: How Claude Decides
steps.explanation: Claude analyzes tool calls: if outputs are needed as inputs, they're sequential. If independent, they can parallelize.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CALLS["Multiple tool calls"]
    CHECK{"Dependencies?"}
    SEQ["Run sequential"]
    PAR["Run parallel"]
    CALLS --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"A needs B's output"| SEQ
    CHECK -->|"Independent"| PAR
    style PAR fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Parallel Example
steps.explanation: Searching codebase: Grep in src/, Grep in tests/, Glob for configs. All independent - run in parallel for speed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    SEARCH["Find all usages"]
    subgraph "Parallel searches"
        G1["Grep src/"]
        G2["Grep tests/"]
        G3["Glob configs"]
    end
    COMBINE["Combine results"]
    SEARCH --> G1 --> COMBINE
    SEARCH --> G2 --> COMBINE
    SEARCH --> G3 --> COMBINE
    style COMBINE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Sequential Example
steps.explanation: Making changes: Read file → Plan edit → Edit file → Run tests. Each step needs the previous result.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    R["Read"]
    P["Plan"]
    E["Edit"]
    T["Test"]
    R --> P --> E --> T
    NOTE["Must be sequential"]
    style NOTE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Mixed Approach
steps.explanation: Often Claude uses both: parallel for gathering info, then sequential for modifications. Best of both worlds.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Phase 1: Parallel"
        R1["Read file A"]
        R2["Read file B"]
        R3["Search codebase"]
    end
    ANALYZE["Analyze all"]
    subgraph "Phase 2: Sequential"
        E1["Edit A"]
        E2["Edit B"]
        T["Test"]
    end
    R1 --> ANALYZE
    R2 --> ANALYZE
    R3 --> ANALYZE
    ANALYZE --> E1 --> E2 --> T
    style ANALYZE fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: User-Requested Parallel
steps.explanation: You can ask Claude to run things in parallel. Say 'run these in parallel' and Claude will batch independent operations together.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    USER["'Run tests and lint in parallel'"]
    subgraph "Parallel execution"
        T["pytest"]
        L["ruff check"]
    end
    RESULTS["Both results returned"]
    USER --> T --> RESULTS
    USER --> L --> RESULTS
    style RESULTS fill:#C8E6C9

fields.title: Background Tasks
fields.description: Running commands in background, checking output later

steps.title: Background Execution
steps.explanation: Long-running commands can run in the **background**. Claude continues working while the command runs. Check results later.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["Long command"]
    BG["Run in background"]
    CONTINUE["Continue conversation"]
    LATER["Check results later"]
    CMD --> BG
    BG --> CONTINUE
    BG --> LATER
    style CONTINUE fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: When to Use Background
steps.explanation: Use background for: long test suites, builds, installations, any command where you don't need immediate results.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Good for Background"
        B1["Test suites (pytest)"]
        B2["Build processes"]
        B3["npm install"]
        B4["Long-running scripts"]
    end
    subgraph "Keep Foreground"
        F1["Quick commands"]
        F2["Need result immediately"]
    end
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Starting Background Tasks
steps.explanation: Claude sets `run_in_background: true` on the Bash tool. The command starts, and Claude gets a task ID to check later.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB CALL["Bash: pytest
run_in_background: true"] START["Command starts"] ID["Returns task_id"] RUNNING["Tests running..."] CALL --> START --> ID START --> RUNNING style ID fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Checking Task Status
steps.explanation: Use **TaskOutput** tool to check on background tasks. Can check status without waiting, or block until complete.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CHECK["TaskOutput"]
    subgraph "Options"
        BLOCK["block: true → wait for completion"]
        NOBLOCK["block: false → check current status"]
    end
    RESULT["Get output/status"]
    CHECK --> BLOCK --> RESULT
    CHECK --> NOBLOCK --> RESULT
    style RESULT fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Task Status Flow
steps.explanation: Background tasks go through states: running → completed (or failed). Output is captured for later retrieval.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    START["Started"]
    RUNNING["Running"]
    COMPLETE["Completed"]
    FAILED["Failed"]
    START --> RUNNING
    RUNNING --> COMPLETE
    RUNNING --> FAILED
    style COMPLETE fill:#C8E6C9
    style FAILED fill:#FFCDD2

steps.title: Background Agents
steps.explanation: The Task tool can also run agents in background. Useful for long explorations or parallel investigations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TASK["Task tool"]
    PARAM["run_in_background: true"]
    AGENT["Agent works autonomously"]
    MAIN["Main conversation continues"]
    LATER["Check agent results"]
    TASK --> PARAM --> AGENT
    PARAM --> MAIN --> LATER
    style MAIN fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Listing Tasks
steps.explanation: Use `/tasks` command to see all running and completed background tasks. Shows task IDs and status.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["/tasks"]
    subgraph "Output"
        T1["task_123: pytest (running)"]
        T2["task_124: build (completed)"]
        T3["task_125: agent (running)"]
    end
    CMD --> T1
    style CMD fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Killing Background Tasks
steps.explanation: Long-running tasks can be killed with **KillShell** tool. Stops the command and frees resources.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    RUNNING["Task running too long"]
    KILL["KillShell: task_id"]
    STOPPED["Task terminated"]
    RUNNING --> KILL --> STOPPED
    style KILL fill:#FFCDD2
    style STOPPED fill:#FFF9C4

fields.title: Skill/Command System
fields.description: How /slash commands and skills work

steps.title: Slash Commands
steps.explanation: **Slash commands** (like `/help`, `/clear`, `/commit`) are shortcuts for common actions. Type `/` to see available commands.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    SLASH["/command"]
    subgraph "Types"
        BUILTIN["Built-in commands"]
        SKILLS["Custom skills"]
    end
    SLASH --> BUILTIN
    SLASH --> SKILLS
    style SLASH fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Built-in Commands
steps.explanation: Core commands built into Claude Code: `/help`, `/clear`, `/config`, `/permissions`, `/tasks`, and more.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Built-in Commands"
        C1["/help → show help"]
        C2["/clear → clear conversation"]
        C3["/config → show settings"]
        C4["/permissions → manage permissions"]
        C5["/tasks → list background tasks"]
        C6["/sandbox → sandbox settings"]
    end
    style C1 fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: What Are Skills?
steps.explanation: **Skills** are user-defined commands that expand into prompts. Create custom workflows: `/review-pr`, `/commit`, `/deploy`, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    SKILL["/commit"]
    EXPAND["Expands to full prompt"]
    INSTRUCTIONS["Detailed commit instructions"]
    EXECUTE["Claude follows instructions"]
    SKILL --> EXPAND --> INSTRUCTIONS --> EXECUTE
    style SKILL fill:#E1BEE7

steps.title: How Skills Work
steps.explanation: When you type `/skill-name`, Claude Code looks up the skill definition and executes it using the Skill tool. The skill's prompt guides Claude.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    USER["/commit"]
    LOOKUP["Find skill definition"]
    TOOL["Skill tool invoked"]
    PROMPT["Skill prompt loaded"]
    EXECUTE["Claude executes"]
    USER --> LOOKUP --> TOOL --> PROMPT --> EXECUTE
    style TOOL fill:#BBDEFB

steps.title: Defining Custom Skills
steps.explanation: Skills are defined in settings or CLAUDE.md files. Specify name, description, and the prompt/instructions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Skill Definition"
        NAME["name: 'review-pr'"]
        DESC["description: 'Review a PR'"]
        PROMPT["prompt: 'Review the PR...'"]
    end
    WHERE["Define in settings or CLAUDE.md"]
    NAME --> WHERE
    style WHERE fill:#FFF9C4

steps.title: Skill with Arguments
steps.explanation: Skills can accept arguments. `/review-pr 123` passes '123' to the skill. The prompt can use this value.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["/review-pr 123"]
    subgraph "Parsed"
        SKILL["skill: review-pr"]
        ARGS["args: '123'"]
    end
    USE["Prompt uses PR number"]
    CMD --> SKILL
    CMD --> ARGS --> USE
    style ARGS fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Project-Specific Skills
steps.explanation: Define skills in `.claude/` for project-specific workflows. Team shares them via git. Consistent processes!
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Project Skills"
        S1["/deploy → deploy to staging"]
        S2["/db-migrate → run migrations"]
        S3["/test-e2e → run E2E tests"]
    end
    GIT["Committed to repo"]
    TEAM["Whole team uses them"]
    S3 --> GIT --> TEAM
    style TEAM fill:#C8E6C9

steps.title: Discovering Skills
steps.explanation: Type `/` and start typing to see available skills. The Skill tool's description shows what's available in current context.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TYPE["/"]
    LIST["Available commands appear"]
    subgraph "Shows"
        B["Built-in commands"]
        P["Project skills"]
        U["User skills"]
    end
    TYPE --> LIST --> B
    LIST --> P
    style LIST fill:#BBDEFB

fields.title: Request/Response Lifecycle
fields.description: How a request flows through FastAPI from ASGI server to response

steps.title: Initial State
steps.explanation: A FastAPI application runs on an **ASGI server** (like Uvicorn). When a client sends an HTTP request, it enters the ASGI pipeline and flows through multiple layers before reaching your endpoint handler.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
