    Client["Client Browser/App"]
    ASGI["ASGI Server\n(Uvicorn/Hypercorn)"]
    App["FastAPI Application"]
    Handler["Endpoint Handler"]
    
    Client -.->|HTTP Request| ASGI
    ASGI -.-> App
    App -.-> Handler
    
    style Client fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style ASGI fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style App fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Handler fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666

steps.title: Request Hits ASGI Server
steps.explanation: The **ASGI server** (Uvicorn) receives the raw HTTP request. It parses headers, method, path, and body into an ASGI scope dictionary. This is the entry point for all async Python web frameworks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Client["Client Browser/App"]
    ASGI["ASGI Server\n(Uvicorn/Hypercorn)"]
    App["FastAPI Application"]
    Handler["Endpoint Handler"]
    
    Client ==>|"POST /users\nHeaders, Body"| ASGI
    ASGI -.-> App
    App -.-> Handler
    
    style Client fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style ASGI fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style App fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Handler fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666

steps.title: Middleware Processing
steps.explanation: Request passes through the **middleware stack** in order. Each middleware can inspect/modify the request, or short-circuit the response. Common middleware: CORS, authentication, logging, compression.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Client["Client"]
    ASGI["ASGI Server"]
    MW1["CORS Middleware"]
    MW2["Auth Middleware"]
    MW3["Logging Middleware"]
    App["FastAPI Router"]
    
    Client --> ASGI
    ASGI ==> MW1
    MW1 ==> MW2
    MW2 ==> MW3
    MW3 -.-> App
    
    style MW1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style MW2 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style MW3 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Client fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style ASGI fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Route Matching
steps.explanation: FastAPI's **router** matches the request path and method to a registered endpoint. It uses Starlette's routing under the hood. Path parameters are extracted (e.g., `/users/{user_id}`).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Request["POST /users/123"]
    Router["FastAPI Router"]
    Route1["GET /users"]
    Route2["POST /users/{user_id}"]
    Route3["DELETE /users/{user_id}"]
    Match["Match Found!\nuser_id=123"]
    
    Request ==> Router
    Router --> Route1
    Router ==>|"✓ Match"| Route2
    Router --> Route3
    Route2 ==> Match
    
    style Request fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Router fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Route2 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style Match fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Dependency Resolution
steps.explanation: Before calling the handler, FastAPI resolves all **dependencies** declared with `Depends()`. Dependencies form a tree and are resolved recursively. Results are cached within the request.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["create_user()"]
    Dep1["Depends(get_db)"]
    Dep2["Depends(get_current_user)"]
    Dep3["Depends(get_token)"]
    DB[("Database Session")]
    User["Current User"]
    
    Handler --> Dep1
    Handler --> Dep2
    Dep2 --> Dep3
    Dep1 ==>|"Resolved"| DB
    Dep3 ==>|"Resolved"| Dep2
    Dep2 ==>|"Resolved"| User
    
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Dep1 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Dep2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Dep3 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style DB fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style User fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Request Validation
steps.explanation: FastAPI uses **Pydantic** to validate path parameters, query parameters, headers, and request body against your type hints. Invalid data triggers automatic 422 Validation Error responses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Body["{name: 'John', age: 'invalid'}"]
    Pydantic["Pydantic Validation"]
    Schema["class User:\n  name: str\n  age: int"]
    Valid["Valid Data"]
    Error["422 Validation Error"]
    
    Body ==> Pydantic
    Schema --> Pydantic
    Pydantic -->|"✓ Valid"| Valid
    Pydantic ==>|"✗ 'invalid' not int"| Error
    
    style Body fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style Pydantic fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Schema fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Error fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Handler Execution
steps.explanation: With dependencies resolved and data validated, the **endpoint handler** executes. For `async def` handlers, FastAPI awaits them directly. For regular `def` handlers, they run in a thread pool.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["@app.post('/users')\nasync def create_user(...)"]
    Logic["Business Logic\ndb.add(user)\nawait db.commit()"]
    Result["Return Value\nUserResponse(id=1, ...)"]
    
    Handler ==>|"await"| Logic
    Logic ==> Result
    
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style Logic fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Result fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Response Serialization
steps.explanation: The return value is serialized using the **response_model** Pydantic schema. Fields are filtered based on `response_model_exclude`, `response_model_include`. JSON encoding handles datetime, UUID, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Return["UserResponse(\n  id=1,\n  name='John',\n  password='secret'\n)"]
    Model["response_model=UserOut\nexclude={'password'}"]
    JSON["{\n  'id': 1,\n  'name': 'John'\n}"]
    
    Return ==> Model
    Model ==>|"Serialize & Filter"| JSON
    
    style Return fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style JSON fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Response Returns
steps.explanation: The JSON response travels back through middleware (in reverse order), then through ASGI server to the client. Middleware can modify response headers, compress body, or log the response.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["Handler"]
    MW3["Logging MW\n(log response)"]
    MW2["Auth MW"]
    MW1["CORS MW\n(add headers)"]
    ASGI["ASGI Server"]
    Client["Client"]
    
    Handler ==>|"200 OK"| MW3
    MW3 ==> MW2
    MW2 ==> MW1
    MW1 ==> ASGI
    ASGI ==>|"HTTP Response"| Client
    
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style MW3 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style MW2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style MW1 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style ASGI fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Client fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px

fields.title: Path Operations & Routing
fields.description: Understanding FastAPI decorators, path/query parameters, and request body handling

steps.title: Path Operation Decorators
steps.explanation: FastAPI uses **decorators** to register endpoint handlers. Each decorator corresponds to an HTTP method: `@app.get()`, `@app.post()`, `@app.put()`, `@app.delete()`, `@app.patch()`, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    App["FastAPI()"]
    Get["@app.get('/items')"]
    Post["@app.post('/items')"]
    Put["@app.put('/items/{id}')"]
    Delete["@app.delete('/items/{id}')"]
    
    App --> Get
    App --> Post
    App --> Put
    App --> Delete
    
    style App fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style Get fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Post fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style Put fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed
    style Delete fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Path Parameters
steps.explanation: **Path parameters** are extracted from the URL using curly braces `{param}`. They must be declared as function arguments with type hints. FastAPI validates and converts them automatically.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    URL["/users/123/posts/456"]
    Path["/users/{user_id}/posts/{post_id}"]
    Func["def get_post(\n  user_id: int,\n  post_id: int\n)"]
    Values["user_id = 123\npost_id = 456"]
    
    URL --> Path
    Path --> Func
    Func --> Values
    
    style URL fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Path fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Func fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Values fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Query Parameters
steps.explanation: **Query parameters** are function arguments NOT in the path. They come from `?key=value` in the URL. Use `Optional` or default values to make them optional.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    URL["/items?skip=10&limit=20"]
    Func["def get_items(\n  skip: int = 0,\n  limit: int = 10\n)"]
    Values["skip = 10\nlimit = 20"]
    
    URL --> Func
    Func --> Values
    
    subgraph Optional["Optional with defaults"]
        Func
    end
    
    style URL fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Func fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Values fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Optional fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666

steps.title: Request Body
steps.explanation: **Request body** is declared using Pydantic models as type hints. FastAPI reads JSON from the request body, validates it against the model, and passes the parsed object to your handler.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    JSON["{\n  'name': 'Widget',\n  'price': 29.99\n}"]
    Model["class Item(BaseModel):\n  name: str\n  price: float"]
    Func["def create_item(\n  item: Item\n)"]
    Access["item.name → 'Widget'\nitem.price → 29.99"]
    
    JSON --> Model
    Model --> Func
    Func --> Access
    
    style JSON fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Func fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Access fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Combining Parameters
steps.explanation: You can combine **path, query, and body** parameters in a single endpoint. FastAPI automatically determines which is which based on the function signature and type hints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Request["PUT /items/5?notify=true\nBody: {name: 'New'}"]
    Func["def update_item(\n  item_id: int,      # path\n  notify: bool,      # query\n  item: ItemUpdate   # body\n)"]
    Path["item_id = 5"]
    Query["notify = True"]
    Body["item = ItemUpdate(...)"]
    
    Request --> Func
    Func --> Path
    Func --> Query
    Func --> Body
    
    style Request fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Func fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Path fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Query fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Body fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: Path Parameter Validation
steps.explanation: Use **Path()** for advanced path parameter validation: constraints, metadata, examples. You can set `ge`, `le`, `gt`, `lt` for numeric constraints, or regex patterns for strings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Func["def get_item(\n  item_id: int = Path(\n    ...,\n    ge=1,\n    le=1000,\n    title='Item ID'\n  )\n)"]
    Valid["/items/500 → ✓"]
    Invalid1["/items/0 → 422"]
    Invalid2["/items/9999 → 422"]
    
    Func --> Valid
    Func --> Invalid1
    Func --> Invalid2
    
    style Func fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Invalid1 fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Invalid2 fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Query Parameter Validation
steps.explanation: Use **Query()** for query parameter validation and metadata. Supports `min_length`, `max_length`, `regex` for strings, numeric constraints, and deprecation warnings.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Func["def search(\n  q: str = Query(\n    ...,\n    min_length=3,\n    max_length=50,\n    regex='^[a-z]+$'\n  )\n)"]
    Valid["?q=hello → ✓"]
    Short["?q=ab → 422\n'too short'"]
    Invalid["?q=Hello123 → 422\n'regex mismatch'"]
    
    Func --> Valid
    Func --> Short
    Func --> Invalid
    
    style Func fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Short fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Invalid fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Multiple Values
steps.explanation: Query parameters can accept **multiple values** using `list[]` type hints. This handles URLs like `?tag=python&tag=fastapi`. Path params with regex can capture complex patterns.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    URL["?tag=python&tag=fastapi&tag=api"]
    Func["def get_items(\n  tag: list[str] = Query([])\n)"]
    Result["tag = ['python', 'fastapi', 'api']"]
    
    URL --> Func
    Func --> Result
    
    style URL fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Func fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Result fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Route Priority
steps.explanation: Routes are matched in **declaration order**. Put specific routes before parameterized ones. `/users/me` must come before `/users/{user_id}` or it will never match.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Request["/users/me"]
    Route1["@app.get('/users/me')\n# FIRST - specific"]
    Route2["@app.get('/users/{user_id}')\n# SECOND - generic"]
    Match["Matches /users/me"]
    NoMatch["Never reached for 'me'"]
    
    Request --> Route1
    Route1 ==>|"✓ Match"| Match
    Route2 -.->|"Would match 'me' as ID"| NoMatch
    
    style Request fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Route1 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style Route2 fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Match fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style NoMatch fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

fields.title: Dependency Injection System
fields.description: How FastAPI's Depends() works for reusable components and dependency trees

steps.title: What is Dependency Injection?
steps.explanation: **Dependency Injection** is a pattern where functions receive their dependencies as parameters rather than creating them. FastAPI's `Depends()` automates this, calling dependency functions and passing results to your handlers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Without["Without DI"]
        H1["def handler():\n  db = get_db()\n  user = get_user()"]
    end
    
    subgraph With["With FastAPI DI"]
        H2["def handler(\n  db = Depends(get_db),\n  user = Depends(get_user)\n)"]
    end
    
    Without --> With
    
    style Without fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style With fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style H1 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626
    style H2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Basic Dependency
steps.explanation: A dependency is any **callable** (function, class, lambda). FastAPI calls it before your handler, and injects the return value as a parameter. Dependencies can have their own parameters too.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Dep["def get_db():
  db = SessionLocal()
  try:
    yield db
  finally:
    db.close()"]
    Handler["def get_users(
  db = Depends(get_db)
):
  return db.query(User)"]
    Call["1. FastAPI calls get_db()
2. Yields db session
3. Injects into handler
4. Handler executes
5. Finally closes db"]
    
    Dep --> Handler
    Handler --> Call
    
    style Dep fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Call fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Dependency Tree
steps.explanation: Dependencies can depend on other dependencies, forming a **tree**. FastAPI resolves them in the correct order. Each dependency is called once per request and cached.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["create_post()"]
    Auth["Depends(get_current_user)"]
    DB["Depends(get_db)"]
    Token["Depends(get_token)"]
    Settings["Depends(get_settings)"]
    
    Handler --> Auth
    Handler --> DB
    Auth --> Token
    Auth --> DB
    Token --> Settings
    
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px
    style Auth fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style DB fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Token fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed
    style Settings fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Class Dependencies
steps.explanation: Classes can be dependencies too. FastAPI calls `__init__` and injects the instance. This is useful for dependencies with multiple parameters or shared state.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Class["class Pagination:\n  def __init__(\n    self,\n    skip: int = 0,\n    limit: int = 10\n  ):\n    self.skip = skip\n    self.limit = limit"]
    Handler["def get_items(\n  pagination: Pagination = Depends()\n):\n  return items[p.skip:p.skip+p.limit]"]
    URL["?skip=20&limit=5"]
    Result["pagination.skip = 20\npagination.limit = 5"]
    
    URL --> Class
    Class --> Handler
    Handler --> Result
    
    style Class fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style URL fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Result fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Yield Dependencies
steps.explanation: Use **yield** for dependencies that need cleanup (database sessions, file handles, locks). Code after yield runs after the response is sent. Essential for resource management.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lifecycle["Dependency Lifecycle"]
        Before["1. Setup\ndb = SessionLocal()"]
        Yield["2. yield db\n(handler runs)"]
        After["3. Cleanup\ndb.close()"]
    end
    
    Before --> Yield
    Yield --> After
    
    Handler["Handler executes\nwith db session"]
    Yield -.->|"Inject"| Handler
    Handler -.->|"Done"| After
    
    style Before fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Yield fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style After fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Handler fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: Path Operation Dependencies
steps.explanation: Apply dependencies to **all routes** in a path operation using the `dependencies` parameter. Useful for authentication checks that don't need return values.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Router["router = APIRouter(\n  dependencies=[\n    Depends(verify_token),\n    Depends(log_request)\n  ]\n)"]
    Route1["@router.get('/users')"]
    Route2["@router.post('/users')"]
    Route3["@router.delete('/users/{id}')"]
    
    Router --> Route1
    Router --> Route2
    Router --> Route3
    
    Check["verify_token runs\nfor ALL routes"]
    Router -.-> Check
    
    style Router fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Route1 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Route2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Route3 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Check fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Global Dependencies
steps.explanation: Apply dependencies to the **entire application** using the `dependencies` parameter on `FastAPI()`. These run for every single request to your API.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    App["app = FastAPI(\n  dependencies=[\n    Depends(verify_api_key)\n  ]\n)"]
    All["All Routes"]
    R1["/users"]
    R2["/items"]
    R3["/orders"]
    Check["verify_api_key()\nruns on EVERY request"]
    
    App --> All
    All --> R1
    All --> R2
    All --> R3
    App -.-> Check
    
    style App fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Check fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Dependency Caching
steps.explanation: By default, dependencies are **cached per request**. If multiple handlers/dependencies use `Depends(get_db)`, the same instance is reused. Use `use_cache=False` to disable.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["handler()"]
    Dep1["Depends(get_db)"]
    Dep2["Depends(get_user)"]
    SubDep["get_user uses\nDepends(get_db)"]
    DB[("Single DB Session\n(cached)")]
    
    Handler --> Dep1
    Handler --> Dep2
    Dep2 --> SubDep
    Dep1 --> DB
    SubDep --> DB
    
    Note["Same session\nreused!"]
    DB -.-> Note
    
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style DB fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Note fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Testing with Dependencies
steps.explanation: Override dependencies in tests using `app.dependency_overrides`. Map original dependency to mock function. This makes testing easy without hitting real databases or services.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Real["def get_db():\n  return real_database"]
    Mock["def mock_db():\n  return fake_database"]
    Override["app.dependency_overrides[\n  get_db\n] = mock_db"]
    Test["# Tests use mock_db\nclient.get('/users')"]
    
    Real --> Override
    Mock --> Override
    Override --> Test
    
    style Real fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Mock fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Override fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Test fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

fields.title: Pydantic Model Validation
fields.description: Request body validation, response models, and automatic serialization

steps.title: Pydantic Overview
steps.explanation: FastAPI uses **Pydantic** for data validation. Define models with type hints, and Pydantic handles validation, conversion, and serialization automatically. Invalid data triggers 422 errors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    JSON["{
  'name': 'Widget',
  'price': 29.99,
  'qty': '10'
}"]
    Model["class Item(BaseModel):
  name: str
  price: float
  qty: int"]
    Valid["Item(
  name='Widget',
  price=29.99,
  qty=10  # converted!
)"]
    
    JSON --> Model
    Model --> Valid
    
    style JSON fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Required vs Optional Fields
steps.explanation: Fields without defaults are **required**. Use `Optional` or `| None` with a default of `None` for optional fields. Use `= ...` to explicitly mark as required.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Model["class User(BaseModel):\n  name: str              # required\n  email: str             # required\n  bio: str | None = None # optional\n  age: int = 18          # default"]
    
    Valid["{name: 'John', email: 'j@x.com'}"]
    Missing["{name: 'John'}"]
    
    Valid -->|"✓ Valid"| OK["bio=None, age=18"]
    Missing -->|"✗ Invalid"| Err["422: email required"]
    
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Missing fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style OK fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Err fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Field Validation
steps.explanation: Use **Field()** for constraints: `min_length`, `max_length`, `ge`, `le`, `gt`, `lt`, `regex`, `multiple_of`. Also adds metadata for OpenAPI docs (title, description, example).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Model["class Product(BaseModel):
  name: str = Field(
    min_length=1,
    max_length=50
  )
  price: float = Field(
    gt=0,
    description='USD'
  )
  sku: str = Field(
    regex='^[A-Z]{3}[0-9]{4}$'
  )"]
    
    Valid["name='Widget'
price=29.99
sku='ABC1234'"]
    Invalid["name=''
price=-10
sku='abc'"]
    
    Model --> Valid
    Model --> Invalid
    
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Invalid fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Nested Models
steps.explanation: Pydantic models can contain other models for **nested validation**. Use `list[Model]` for arrays of nested objects. Validation happens recursively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    JSON["{\n  'name': 'Order 1',\n  'items': [\n    {'name': 'A', 'qty': 2},\n    {'name': 'B', 'qty': 1}\n  ]\n}"]
    
    Item["class Item(BaseModel):\n  name: str\n  qty: int"]
    Order["class Order(BaseModel):\n  name: str\n  items: list[Item]"]
    
    JSON --> Order
    Order --> Item
    
    Result["Order(\n  name='Order 1',\n  items=[Item(...), Item(...)]\n)"]
    Order --> Result
    
    style JSON fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Order fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Item fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed
    style Result fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Custom Validators
steps.explanation: Use **@field_validator** for custom validation logic. Access other fields with **@model_validator**. Raise `ValueError` to fail validation with a custom message.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Model["class User(BaseModel):\n  password: str\n  password_confirm: str\n\n  @model_validator(mode='after')\n  def passwords_match(self):\n    if self.password != self.password_confirm:\n      raise ValueError('No match')\n    return self"]
    
    Valid["{pw: 'secret', confirm: 'secret'}"]
    Invalid["{pw: 'secret', confirm: 'wrong'}"]
    
    Valid -->|"✓"| OK["Validation passes"]
    Invalid -->|"✗"| Err["422: No match"]
    
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Valid fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Invalid fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Response Models
steps.explanation: Use **response_model** to define output schema. FastAPI filters the response to include only declared fields. Great for hiding sensitive data like passwords.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    DB["User from DB:\n  id=1\n  email='j@x.com'\n  password='hashed'\n  created_at=..."]
    
    Model["class UserOut(BaseModel):\n  id: int\n  email: str"]
    
    Route["@app.get('/user',\n  response_model=UserOut\n)"]
    
    Response["{\n  'id': 1,\n  'email': 'j@x.com'\n}\n# password hidden!"]
    
    DB --> Route
    Model --> Route
    Route --> Response
    
    style DB fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Response fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Multiple Models Pattern
steps.explanation: Use separate models for **Create, Update, Read** operations. Create model has required fields, Update model has all optional, Read model includes DB fields like id.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Base["class UserBase(BaseModel):\n  email: str\n  name: str"]
    
    Create["class UserCreate(UserBase):\n  password: str"]
    Update["class UserUpdate(BaseModel):\n  email: str | None = None\n  name: str | None = None"]
    Read["class UserRead(UserBase):\n  id: int\n  created_at: datetime"]
    
    Base --> Create
    Base --> Read
    
    style Base fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:3px
    style Create fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Update fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style Read fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: Config & JSON Aliases
steps.explanation: Use **model_config** (Pydantic v2) for model-wide settings. `from_attributes=True` enables ORM mode. Use `alias` to map JSON keys to different Python names.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    JSON["{\n  'user-name': 'john',\n  'emailAddress': 'j@x.com'\n}"]
    
    Model["class User(BaseModel):\n  model_config = ConfigDict(\n    from_attributes=True\n  )\n  name: str = Field(alias='user-name')\n  email: str = Field(alias='emailAddress')"]
    
    Python["User(\n  name='john',\n  email='j@x.com'\n)"]
    
    JSON --> Model
    Model --> Python
    
    style JSON fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Python fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Validation Error Format
steps.explanation: When validation fails, FastAPI returns a **422 Unprocessable Entity** with detailed errors. Each error includes location (body/query/path), field name, message, and error type.

fields.title: Authentication Flow
fields.description: OAuth2, JWT tokens, API keys, and security dependencies

steps.title: Security Options
steps.explanation: FastAPI supports multiple **authentication methods**: OAuth2 with Password/Bearer tokens, API Keys (header, query, cookie), HTTP Basic Auth, and OpenID Connect. Choose based on your use case.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Auth["FastAPI Security"]
    OAuth["OAuth2\nPassword + Bearer"]
    API["API Key\nHeader/Query/Cookie"]
    Basic["HTTP Basic\nUsername:Password"]
    OIDC["OpenID Connect\nGoogle, Auth0, etc."]
    
    Auth --> OAuth
    Auth --> API
    Auth --> Basic
    Auth --> OIDC
    
    style Auth fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style OAuth fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style API fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Basic fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed
    style OIDC fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: OAuth2 Password Flow
steps.explanation: The **OAuth2 Password flow** is common for first-party apps. User sends username/password to `/token` endpoint, receives JWT access token, then uses it for subsequent requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Client["Client App"]
    Login["POST /token\nusername=john\npassword=secret"]
    Verify["Verify credentials\nHash password check"]
    Token["Generate JWT\n{sub: 'john', exp: ...}"]
    Response["{\n  access_token: 'eyJ...',\n  token_type: 'bearer'\n}"]
    
    Client --> Login
    Login --> Verify
    Verify --> Token
    Token --> Response
    Response --> Client
    
    style Login fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Token fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Response fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: JWT Token Structure
steps.explanation: **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** have three parts: Header (algorithm), Payload (claims like user_id, expiration), and Signature (verification). They're base64-encoded and signed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    JWT["eyJhbGc.eyJzdWI.SflKx"]
    
    Header["Header:\n{\n  'alg': 'HS256',\n  'typ': 'JWT'\n}"]
    Payload["Payload:\n{\n  'sub': 'user123',\n  'exp': 1699999999,\n  'role': 'admin'\n}"]
    Sig["Signature:\nHMAC-SHA256(\n  header + payload,\n  SECRET_KEY\n)"]
    
    JWT --> Header
    JWT --> Payload
    JWT --> Sig
    
    style JWT fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Header fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Payload fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Sig fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: OAuth2PasswordBearer
steps.explanation: **OAuth2PasswordBearer** extracts the token from the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. Use it as a dependency to get the raw token string.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Request["GET /users/me\nAuthorization: Bearer eyJ..."]
    
    Scheme["oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(\n  tokenUrl='token'\n)"]
    
    Handler["async def get_me(\n  token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)\n):\n  # token = 'eyJ...'"]
    
    Request --> Scheme
    Scheme --> Handler
    
    style Request fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Scheme fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: get_current_user Dependency
steps.explanation: Create a **get_current_user** dependency that decodes the JWT, validates it, and returns the user object. This is the standard pattern for authenticated endpoints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Token["token = 'eyJ...'"]
    Decode["jwt.decode(\n  token,\n  SECRET_KEY,\n  algorithms=['HS256']\n)"]
    Payload["{sub: 'john', exp: ...}"]
    Query["db.query(User).filter(\n  User.username == 'john'\n).first()"]
    User["User object"]
    
    Token --> Decode
    Decode --> Payload
    Payload --> Query
    Query --> User
    
    style Token fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Decode fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style User fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Protected Endpoints
steps.explanation: Use `Depends(get_current_user)` on any endpoint that requires authentication. The user object is automatically injected. Unauthenticated requests get 401 Unauthorized.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Dep["async def get_current_user(...):\n  # decode token, get user\n  return user"]
    
    Handler["@app.get('/users/me')\nasync def read_me(\n  user: User = Depends(get_current_user)\n):\n  return user"]
    
    Success["200 OK\n{id: 1, name: 'john'}"]
    Fail["401 Unauthorized\n{detail: 'Not authenticated'}"]
    
    Dep --> Handler
    Handler -->|"Valid token"| Success
    Handler -->|"Invalid/missing"| Fail
    
    style Dep fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Success fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Fail fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Role-Based Access Control
steps.explanation: Extend authentication with **role checks**. Create dependency functions that verify the user has required roles/permissions. Raise HTTPException for forbidden access.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    User["get_current_user"]
    Admin["def require_admin(\n  user: User = Depends(get_current_user)\n):\n  if user.role != 'admin':\n    raise HTTPException(403)\n  return user"]
    
    Handler["@app.delete('/users/{id}')\nasync def delete_user(\n  user: User = Depends(require_admin)\n)"]
    
    User --> Admin
    Admin --> Handler
    
    style User fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Admin fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: API Key Authentication
steps.explanation: For service-to-service auth, use **API Keys**. FastAPI provides `APIKeyHeader`, `APIKeyQuery`, and `APIKeyCookie` for different locations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Scheme["api_key_header = APIKeyHeader(\n  name='X-API-Key',\n  auto_error=False\n)"]
    
    Dep["async def get_api_key(\n  key: str = Depends(api_key_header)\n):\n  if key != API_KEY:\n    raise HTTPException(403)\n  return key"]
    
    Request["GET /data\nX-API-Key: sk-12345"]
    
    Request --> Scheme
    Scheme --> Dep
    
    style Scheme fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Dep fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Request fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Complete Auth Flow
steps.explanation: The complete flow: Client authenticates at `/token`, receives JWT, includes it in subsequent requests. Server validates token, extracts user, checks permissions, then executes handler.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Login["1. POST /token\n   username + password"]
    JWT["2. Receive JWT"]
    Request["3. GET /protected\n   Authorization: Bearer"]
    Validate["4. Validate JWT"]
    GetUser["5. Load User"]
    CheckRole["6. Check Permissions"]
    Execute["7. Execute Handler"]
    Response["8. Return Response"]
    
    Login --> JWT
    JWT --> Request
    Request --> Validate
    Validate --> GetUser
    GetUser --> CheckRole
    CheckRole --> Execute
    Execute --> Response
    
    style Login fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style JWT fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Validate fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Execute fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

fields.title: Middleware Pipeline
fields.description: Request/response interception, middleware ordering, and common patterns

steps.title: What is Middleware?
steps.explanation: **Middleware** intercepts every request before it reaches your endpoint, and every response before it's sent to the client. Used for logging, CORS, authentication, compression, timing, etc.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    Client["Client"]
    MW1["Middleware 1"]
    MW2["Middleware 2"]
    MW3["Middleware 3"]
    App["Endpoint"]
    
    Client -->|"Request"| MW1
    MW1 --> MW2
    MW2 --> MW3
    MW3 --> App
    App -->|"Response"| MW3
    MW3 --> MW2
    MW2 --> MW1
    MW1 --> Client
    
    style MW1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style MW2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style MW3 fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: Adding Middleware
steps.explanation: Use **@app.middleware('http')** decorator or `app.add_middleware()`. The decorated function receives the request and a `call_next` function to continue the chain.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Decorator["@app.middleware('http')
async def my_middleware(
  request: Request,
  call_next
):
  # before request
  response = await call_next(request)
  # after response
  return response"]
    
    AddMethod["app.add_middleware(
  MyMiddlewareClass,
  param1='value'
)"]
    
    style Decorator fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style AddMethod fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Execution Order
steps.explanation: Middleware executes in **reverse order** of registration for requests, and registration order for responses. Last added middleware is closest to your endpoint.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Add["# Registration order:\napp.add_middleware(A)  # First\napp.add_middleware(B)  # Second\napp.add_middleware(C)  # Third"]
    
    subgraph Request["Request Flow"]
        direction LR
        R1["A"] --> R2["B"] --> R3["C"] --> Endpoint
    end
    
    subgraph Response["Response Flow"]
        direction LR
        Endpoint2["Endpoint"] --> S3["C"] --> S2["B"] --> S1["A"]
    end
    
    style Add fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style R1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style R3 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Timing Middleware
steps.explanation: A common pattern: measure **request processing time** and add it to response headers. Capture start time before `call_next`, calculate duration after.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.middleware('http')\nasync def timing(request, call_next):\n  start = time.time()\n  response = await call_next(request)\n  duration = time.time() - start\n  response.headers['X-Process-Time'] = str(duration)\n  return response"]
    
    Request["GET /users"]
    Response["200 OK\nX-Process-Time: 0.045"]
    
    Request --> Code
    Code --> Response
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Response fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: CORS Middleware
steps.explanation: **CORSMiddleware** handles Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. Configure allowed origins, methods, headers, and credentials. Essential for browser-based API clients.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware\n\napp.add_middleware(\n  CORSMiddleware,\n  allow_origins=['https://app.com'],\n  allow_credentials=True,\n  allow_methods=['*'],\n  allow_headers=['*'],\n)"]
    
    Preflight["OPTIONS /api/users\nOrigin: https://app.com"]
    Response["200 OK\nAccess-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.com\nAccess-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, ..."]
    
    Code --> Preflight
    Preflight --> Response
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Preflight fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Response fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: GZip Middleware
steps.explanation: **GZipMiddleware** compresses responses larger than a minimum size. Reduces bandwidth for JSON/HTML responses. Client must send `Accept-Encoding: gzip`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["from fastapi.middleware.gzip import GZipMiddleware\n\napp.add_middleware(\n  GZipMiddleware,\n  minimum_size=1000  # bytes\n)"]
    
    Original["Response: 50KB JSON"]
    Compressed["Response: 8KB gzipped\nContent-Encoding: gzip"]
    
    Code --> Original
    Original --> Compressed
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Original fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Compressed fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Request ID Middleware
steps.explanation: Add a **unique request ID** to every request for tracing and debugging. Include it in logs and response headers. Essential for distributed systems.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.middleware('http')\nasync def request_id(request, call_next):\n  req_id = str(uuid4())\n  request.state.request_id = req_id\n  response = await call_next(request)\n  response.headers['X-Request-ID'] = req_id\n  return response"]
    
    Request["GET /users"]
    Log["logger.info(\n  'Processing',\n  request_id=request.state.request_id\n)"]
    Response["X-Request-ID: abc-123-def"]
    
    Request --> Code
    Code --> Log
    Code --> Response
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Log fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Error Handling in Middleware
steps.explanation: Middleware can **catch exceptions** from downstream handlers. Wrap `call_next` in try/except to handle errors uniformly, log them, and return custom error responses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.middleware('http')\nasync def error_handler(request, call_next):\n  try:\n    return await call_next(request)\n  except Exception as e:\n    logger.error(f'Error: {e}')\n    return JSONResponse(\n      status_code=500,\n      content={'error': 'Internal error'}\n    )"]
    
    Handler["raise ValueError()"]
    Response["500 Internal Error\n{error: 'Internal error'}"]
    
    Handler --> Code
    Code --> Response
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Response fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706

steps.title: Middleware vs Dependencies
steps.explanation: **Middleware** runs on ALL requests regardless of route. **Dependencies** run only on matched routes. Use middleware for cross-cutting concerns, dependencies for route-specific logic.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph MW["Middleware"]
        direction TB
        MW1["✓ All requests"]
        MW2["✓ Before routing"]
        MW3["✓ Access raw request"]
        MW4["✗ No dependency injection"]
    end
    
    subgraph Dep["Dependencies"]
        direction TB
        D1["✓ Per-route"]
        D2["✓ After routing"]
        D3["✓ Full DI support"]
        D4["✓ Access path params"]
    end
    
    style MW fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style Dep fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

fields.title: Background Tasks
fields.description: BackgroundTasks for fire-and-forget operations and comparison with Celery

steps.title: What are Background Tasks?
steps.explanation: **Background tasks** run after the response is sent to the client. Use them for operations that don't need to block the response: sending emails, writing logs, cleanup tasks, notifications.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    Request["POST /order"]
    Handler["Process order\nReturn 201"]
    Response["201 Created"]
    BG["Background:\nSend email\nUpdate analytics"]
    
    Request --> Handler
    Handler --> Response
    Handler -.->|"After response"| BG
    
    style Request fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Response fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style BG fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: Using BackgroundTasks
steps.explanation: Inject **BackgroundTasks** as a dependency, then call `add_task()` with a function and arguments. The task runs after the response is sent.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["@app.post('/signup')
async def signup(
  email: str,
  background_tasks: BackgroundTasks
):
  user = create_user(email)
  background_tasks.add_task(
    send_welcome_email,
    email
  )
  return {'status': 'created'}"]
    
    Timeline["1. Create user
2. Return response
3. Send email (background)"]
    
    Handler --> Timeline
    
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Timeline fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Multiple Tasks
steps.explanation: Add **multiple background tasks** in a single handler. They execute in order, one after another. Each task is a separate function call.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["background_tasks.add_task(send_email, user.email)\nbackground_tasks.add_task(notify_admin, user)\nbackground_tasks.add_task(update_metrics, 'signup')\nreturn {'ok': True}"]
    
    Seq["After response:\n1. send_email()\n2. notify_admin()\n3. update_metrics()"]
    
    Handler --> Seq
    
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Seq fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Tasks with Parameters
steps.explanation: Pass **arguments to tasks** as positional or keyword arguments after the function. The task function receives them when executed.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    TaskDef["def write_log(\n  message: str,\n  level: str = 'INFO'\n):\n  logger.log(level, message)"]
    
    AddTask["background_tasks.add_task(\n  write_log,\n  'User created',  # positional\n  level='DEBUG'    # keyword\n)"]
    
    Exec["Executes:\nwrite_log('User created', level='DEBUG')"]
    
    TaskDef --> AddTask
    AddTask --> Exec
    
    style TaskDef fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style AddTask fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Exec fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Tasks in Dependencies
steps.explanation: Background tasks work in **dependencies** too. Add tasks from a dependency function, and they'll run after the response. Useful for cleanup or logging.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Dep["async def log_request(
  request: Request,
  background_tasks: BackgroundTasks
):
  background_tasks.add_task(
    log_to_db,
    request.url.path,
    request.method
  )"]
    
    Handler["@app.get('/users')
async def get_users(
  _: None = Depends(log_request)
):
  return users"]
    
    Dep --> Handler
    
    style Dep fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706

steps.title: Async Task Functions
steps.explanation: Task functions can be **async or sync**. Async tasks run on the event loop. Sync tasks run in a thread pool. Both work seamlessly with BackgroundTasks.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Async["async def send_email(to: str):\n  async with aiosmtplib.SMTP() as smtp:\n    await smtp.send(...)"]
    
    Sync["def write_file(path: str, data: str):\n  with open(path, 'w') as f:\n    f.write(data)"]
    
    Add["background_tasks.add_task(send_email, 'a@b.com')\nbackground_tasks.add_task(write_file, '/tmp/log', 'data')"]
    
    Async --> Add
    Sync --> Add
    
    style Async fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Sync fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Add fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Limitations
steps.explanation: BackgroundTasks are **fire-and-forget**. No retries, no result tracking, no persistence. If the server crashes, tasks are lost. For critical tasks, use Celery or similar.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Limitations["BackgroundTasks Limitations"]
        L1["✗ No retry on failure"]
        L2["✗ No result storage"]
        L3["✗ Lost on server restart"]
        L4["✗ No task scheduling"]
    end
    
    subgraph Good["Good For"]
        G1["✓ Sending emails"]
        G2["✓ Writing logs"]
        G3["✓ Quick cleanup"]
        G4["✓ Non-critical tasks"]
    end
    
    style Limitations fill:#fecaca,stroke:#dc2626
    style Good fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534

steps.title: BackgroundTasks vs Celery
steps.explanation: **BackgroundTasks**: simple, in-process, no infrastructure. **Celery**: distributed, persistent, retries, scheduling. Choose based on requirements.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph BG["BackgroundTasks"]
        B1["In-process"]
        B2["No extra infra"]
        B3["Fire-and-forget"]
        B4["Simple use cases"]
    end
    
    subgraph Celery["Celery"]
        C1["Distributed workers"]
        C2["Redis/RabbitMQ broker"]
        C3["Retries & monitoring"]
        C4["Complex workflows"]
    end
    
    Simple["Simple email?"] --> BG
    Complex["Payment processing?"] --> Celery
    
    style BG fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Celery fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Error Handling
steps.explanation: Background task **errors don't affect the response** (already sent). Wrap tasks in try/except to handle errors gracefully and log failures.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Task["async def risky_task():\n  try:\n    await do_something()\n  except Exception as e:\n    logger.error(f'Task failed: {e}')\n    # Optionally: send alert, retry logic"]
    
    Handler["Handler returns 200"]
    Error["Task fails silently\n(response already sent)"]
    Log["Error logged for debugging"]
    
    Handler --> Task
    Task -->|"Exception"| Error
    Error --> Log
    
    style Task fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Error fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

fields.title: WebSocket Connections
fields.description: Real-time bidirectional communication with WebSocket lifecycle and broadcasting

steps.title: What are WebSockets?
steps.explanation: **WebSockets** provide full-duplex, persistent connections between client and server. Unlike HTTP (request/response), both sides can send messages anytime. Perfect for chat, live updates, gaming.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph HTTP["HTTP"]
        C1["Client"] -->|"Request"| S1["Server"]
        S1 -->|"Response"| C1
    end
    
    subgraph WS["WebSocket"]
        C2["Client"] <-->|"Bidirectional"| S2["Server"]
    end
    
    style HTTP fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style WS fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Basic WebSocket Endpoint
steps.explanation: Use **@app.websocket()** decorator. Accept the connection with `await websocket.accept()`, then loop to receive and send messages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.websocket('/ws')
async def websocket_endpoint(
  websocket: WebSocket
):
  await websocket.accept()
  while True:
    data = await websocket.receive_text()
    await websocket.send_text(f'Echo: {data}')"]
    
    Flow["1. Client connects
2. Server accepts
3. Loop: receive → send
4. Connection persists"]
    
    Code --> Flow
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Flow fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Connection Lifecycle
steps.explanation: WebSocket connections have a lifecycle: **connect → open → messages → close**. Handle each phase properly for robust applications.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Connect["Client: new WebSocket('/ws')"]
    Accept["Server: await websocket.accept()"]
    Open["Connection Open"]
    Send["send_text() / send_json()"]
    Receive["receive_text() / receive_json()"]
    Close["Connection Closed"]
    
    Connect --> Accept
    Accept --> Open
    Open --> Send
    Open --> Receive
    Send --> Open
    Receive --> Open
    Open -->|"disconnect"| Close
    
    style Connect fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Accept fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Open fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Close fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Handling Disconnection
steps.explanation: Clients can disconnect unexpectedly. Use **try/except** with `WebSocketDisconnect` to handle graceful and abrupt disconnections.

steps.title: Connection Manager Pattern
steps.explanation: For multiple clients, use a **ConnectionManager** class to track active connections. Enable broadcasting messages to all or specific clients.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Manager["class ConnectionManager:
  def __init__(self):
    self.connections: list[WebSocket] = []

  async def connect(self, ws: WebSocket):
    await ws.accept()
    self.connections.append(ws)

  def disconnect(self, ws: WebSocket):
    self.connections.remove(ws)

  async def broadcast(self, message: str):
    for conn in self.connections:
      await conn.send_text(message)"]
    
    style Manager fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Broadcasting Messages
steps.explanation: With ConnectionManager, **broadcast** a message to all connected clients. One client sends, everyone receives. Essential for chat rooms and live updates.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Client1["Client 1\nsends: 'Hello'"]
    Server["ConnectionManager\nbroadcast('Hello')"]
    Client2["Client 2\nreceives: 'Hello'"]
    Client3["Client 3\nreceives: 'Hello'"]
    Client4["Client 4\nreceives: 'Hello'"]
    
    Client1 --> Server
    Server --> Client1
    Server --> Client2
    Server --> Client3
    Server --> Client4
    
    style Server fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Client1 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Path & Query Parameters
steps.explanation: WebSocket routes support **path and query parameters** just like HTTP routes. Use them for room IDs, user tokens, or other connection-specific data.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    URL["ws://server/ws/room123?token=abc"]
    
    Code["@app.websocket('/ws/{room_id}')\nasync def ws(\n  websocket: WebSocket,\n  room_id: str,\n  token: str = Query(None)\n):\n  # room_id = 'room123'\n  # token = 'abc'"]
    
    URL --> Code
    
    style URL fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: JSON Messages
steps.explanation: Use **receive_json()** and **send_json()** for structured data. Pydantic models can validate incoming JSON messages.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Receive["data = await websocket.receive_json()\n# data = {'type': 'chat', 'msg': 'Hi'}"]
    
    Process["if data['type'] == 'chat':\n  message = data['msg']"]
    
    Send["await websocket.send_json({\n  'type': 'response',\n  'status': 'ok'\n})"]
    
    Receive --> Process
    Process --> Send
    
    style Receive fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Process fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Send fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Dependencies in WebSockets
steps.explanation: WebSocket endpoints support **dependencies**, but with limitations. Use them for authentication, database access, but note that yield dependencies behave differently.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Auth["async def get_user(
  websocket: WebSocket,
  token: str = Query(...)
):
  user = verify_token(token)
  if not user:
    await websocket.close(code=4001)
    raise WebSocketException(...)
  return user"]
    
    Handler["@app.websocket('/ws')
async def ws(
  websocket: WebSocket,
  user: User = Depends(get_user)
):
  # user authenticated"]
    
    Auth --> Handler
    
    style Auth fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:3px
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706

fields.title: ASGI & Uvicorn
fields.description: Understanding the async server gateway interface and Uvicorn configuration

steps.title: What is ASGI?
steps.explanation: **ASGI (Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface)** is the spec for async Python web apps. It's the successor to WSGI, supporting async/await, WebSockets, and HTTP/2. FastAPI is an ASGI framework.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sync["WSGI (Sync)"]
        Flask["Flask"]
        Django["Django (traditional)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Async["ASGI (Async)"]
        FastAPI["FastAPI"]
        Starlette["Starlette"]
        DjangoAsync["Django (async)"]
    end
    
    WSGI["WSGI Servers\nGunicorn, uWSGI"] --> Sync
    ASGI["ASGI Servers\nUvicorn, Hypercorn"] --> Async
    
    style Sync fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Async fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: ASGI Application Interface
steps.explanation: An ASGI app is a callable that receives **scope** (connection info), **receive** (to get messages), and **send** (to send messages). FastAPI abstracts this away.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Interface["async def app(\n  scope: dict,   # connection info\n  receive: Callable,  # get messages\n  send: Callable      # send messages\n)"]
    
    Scope["scope = {\n  'type': 'http',\n  'method': 'GET',\n  'path': '/users',\n  'headers': [...]\n}"]
    
    Interface --> Scope
    
    FastAPI["FastAPI handles this\nfor you automatically!"]
    Interface -.-> FastAPI
    
    style Interface fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style FastAPI fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Uvicorn Overview
steps.explanation: **Uvicorn** is the recommended ASGI server for FastAPI. It's lightning-fast, built on uvloop and httptools. Use it in development and production.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Uvicorn["Uvicorn"]
    uvloop["uvloop\n(fast event loop)"]
    httptools["httptools\n(fast HTTP parser)"]
    websockets["websockets\n(WS support)"]
    
    Uvicorn --> uvloop
    Uvicorn --> httptools
    Uvicorn --> websockets
    
    Features["Features:\n• Hot reload\n• HTTP/1.1 & WebSocket\n• Multiple workers\n• SSL/TLS support"]
    Uvicorn --> Features
    
    style Uvicorn fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Running Uvicorn
steps.explanation: Run Uvicorn from command line or programmatically. Use `--reload` for development (auto-restart on code changes).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CLI["# Command line
uvicorn main:app --reload --port 8000"]
    
    Code["# Programmatic
import uvicorn

if __name__ == '__main__':
  uvicorn.run(
    'main:app',
    host='0.0.0.0',
    port=8000,
    reload=True
  )"]
    
    CLI --> Server["Server running at
http://127.0.0.1:8000"]
    Code --> Server
    
    style CLI fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Server fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Worker Processes
steps.explanation: For production, run **multiple worker processes** to utilize all CPU cores. Each worker handles requests independently. Use `--workers` flag.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["uvicorn main:app --workers 4"]
    
    Master["Master Process"]
    W1["Worker 1\n(handles requests)"]
    W2["Worker 2\n(handles requests)"]
    W3["Worker 3\n(handles requests)"]
    W4["Worker 4\n(handles requests)"]
    
    CMD --> Master
    Master --> W1
    Master --> W2
    Master --> W3
    Master --> W4
    
    style CMD fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Master fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style W1 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style W2 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style W3 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style W4 fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Gunicorn + Uvicorn
steps.explanation: In production, use **Gunicorn as process manager** with Uvicorn workers. Gunicorn handles worker lifecycle, Uvicorn handles ASGI. Best of both worlds.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    CMD["gunicorn main:app \\\n  -w 4 \\\n  -k uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker \\\n  --bind 0.0.0.0:8000"]
    
    Gunicorn["Gunicorn\n(process manager)"]
    U1["Uvicorn Worker 1"]
    U2["Uvicorn Worker 2"]
    U3["Uvicorn Worker 3"]
    U4["Uvicorn Worker 4"]
    
    CMD --> Gunicorn
    Gunicorn --> U1
    Gunicorn --> U2
    Gunicorn --> U3
    Gunicorn --> U4
    
    style CMD fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Gunicorn fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Event Loop
steps.explanation: Uvicorn runs an **asyncio event loop**. All async code executes on this loop. Use `uvloop` (default on Unix) for 2-4x better performance than default asyncio.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Loop["Event Loop"]
    T1["Task: handle request 1"]
    T2["Task: handle request 2"]
    T3["Task: await db query"]
    T4["Task: send response"]
    
    Loop --> T1
    Loop --> T2
    Loop --> T3
    Loop --> T4
    
    Note["Single thread,\nmultiple concurrent tasks\n(cooperative multitasking)"]
    Loop -.-> Note
    
    style Loop fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Note fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666

steps.title: Configuration Options
steps.explanation: Uvicorn has many **configuration options**: host, port, SSL, timeouts, headers, access logs, and more. Configure via CLI flags or config file.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Config["uvicorn main:app\n  --host 0.0.0.0\n  --port 8000\n  --workers 4\n  --ssl-keyfile key.pem\n  --ssl-certfile cert.pem\n  --timeout-keep-alive 30\n  --access-log\n  --log-level info"]
    
    subgraph Options["Common Options"]
        O1["--reload: dev mode"]
        O2["--workers: parallelism"]
        O3["--ssl-*: HTTPS"]
        O4["--log-level: verbosity"]
    end
    
    Config --> Options
    
    style Config fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Lifespan Events
steps.explanation: Use **lifespan events** for startup/shutdown logic: initialize database pools, load ML models, close connections. Defined with async context manager.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
  # Startup
  await init_db()
  load_model()
  yield
  # Shutdown
  await close_db()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)"]
    
    Flow["Server start → Startup code → Ready
Server stop → Shutdown code → Exit"]
    
    Code --> Flow
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Flow fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

fields.title: APIRouter Organization
fields.description: Modular app structure with routers, prefixes, and tags

steps.title: Why Use Routers?
steps.explanation: **APIRouter** allows you to split your FastAPI app into multiple files/modules. Each router handles a group of related endpoints. Essential for large applications.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Monolithic["Without Routers"]
        Main1["main.py\n500+ lines\nall endpoints"]
    end
    
    subgraph Modular["With Routers"]
        Main2["main.py"]
        Users["routers/users.py"]
        Items["routers/items.py"]
        Orders["routers/orders.py"]
        Main2 --> Users
        Main2 --> Items
        Main2 --> Orders
    end
    
    style Monolithic fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Modular fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Creating a Router
steps.explanation: Create an **APIRouter** instance and define routes using the same decorators as FastAPI app. Export the router to include in main app.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    File["# routers/users.py"]
    Code["from fastapi import APIRouter

router = APIRouter()

@router.get('/users')
async def get_users():
  return []

@router.post('/users')
async def create_user(user: User):
  return user"]
    
    File --> Code
    
    style File fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Including Routers
steps.explanation: Use **app.include_router()** to mount routers on the main app. Routes from the router become part of the application.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Main["# main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from routers import users, items

app = FastAPI()

app.include_router(users.router)
app.include_router(items.router)"]
    
    Routes["/users
/users/{id}
/items
/items/{id}"]
    
    Main --> Routes
    
    style Main fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Routes fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Path Prefixes
steps.explanation: Add a **prefix** to all routes in a router. Define routes without the prefix, it's added automatically when including.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Router["# routers/users.py\n@router.get('/')      # becomes /api/v1/users/\n@router.get('/{id}')  # becomes /api/v1/users/{id}"]
    
    Include["app.include_router(\n  users.router,\n  prefix='/api/v1/users'\n)"]
    
    Result["/api/v1/users/\n/api/v1/users/{id}"]
    
    Router --> Include
    Include --> Result
    
    style Router fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Include fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Result fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Tags for Documentation
steps.explanation: Use **tags** to group routes in OpenAPI docs. Set tags on the router or per-route. Routes appear grouped in Swagger UI.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Router["router = APIRouter(\n  prefix='/users',\n  tags=['Users']\n)"]
    
    Include["# Or when including:\napp.include_router(\n  router,\n  tags=['Users']\n)"]
    
    Swagger["Swagger UI:\n▼ Users\n  GET /users\n  POST /users\n  GET /users/{id}"]
    
    Router --> Swagger
    Include --> Swagger
    
    style Router fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Swagger fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Router Dependencies
steps.explanation: Apply **dependencies to all routes** in a router. Perfect for authentication that applies to an entire module.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Router["router = APIRouter(
  prefix='/admin',
  tags=['Admin'],
  dependencies=[
    Depends(require_admin)
  ]
)"]
    
    Routes["@router.get('/stats')
@router.delete('/users/{id}')
@router.post('/broadcast')

# All require admin!"]
    
    Router --> Routes
    
    style Router fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Routes fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Nested Routers
steps.explanation: Routers can **include other routers** for hierarchical organization. Build API versions or nested resources naturally.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    V1["v1_router = APIRouter(prefix='/v1')"]
    Users["users_router"]
    Items["items_router"]
    
    V1 -->|"include_router"| Users
    V1 -->|"include_router"| Items
    
    App["app.include_router(v1_router)"]
    V1 --> App
    
    Result["/v1/users/...\n/v1/items/..."]
    App --> Result
    
    style V1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Users fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Items fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Project Structure
steps.explanation: Organize routers in a **routers/** directory with `__init__.py` for clean imports. Group related endpoints in separate files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Root["app/"]
    Main["main.py"]
    Routers["routers/"]
    Init["__init__.py"]
    Users["users.py"]
    Items["items.py"]
    Auth["auth.py"]
    
    Root --> Main
    Root --> Routers
    Routers --> Init
    Routers --> Users
    Routers --> Items
    Routers --> Auth
    
    InitCode["# routers/__init__.py\nfrom .users import router as users_router\nfrom .items import router as items_router"]
    Init -.-> InitCode
    
    style Root fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Routers fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Complete Example
steps.explanation: A complete router setup with prefix, tags, dependencies, and responses. This is a production-ready pattern.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Router["router = APIRouter(
  prefix='/items',
  tags=['Items'],
  dependencies=[Depends(verify_token)],
  responses={404: {'description': 'Not found'}}
)"]
    
    Include["app.include_router(
  router,
  prefix='/api/v1'
)"]
    
    Final["Final routes:
/api/v1/items/
/api/v1/items/{id}

All authenticated
All tagged 'Items'
All have 404 response"]
    
    Router --> Include
    Include --> Final
    
    style Router fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Include fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Final fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

fields.title: Exception Handling
fields.description: Custom exception handlers, HTTPException, and validation errors

steps.title: Exception Types
steps.explanation: FastAPI handles several **exception types**: HTTPException (your explicit errors), RequestValidationError (Pydantic failures), and Python exceptions (500 errors by default).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Exceptions["Exceptions"]
    HTTP["HTTPException\n(4xx, 5xx you raise)"]
    Validation["RequestValidationError\n(422 from Pydantic)"]
    Python["Exception\n(500 Internal Error)"]
    
    Exceptions --> HTTP
    Exceptions --> Validation
    Exceptions --> Python
    
    style Exceptions fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style HTTP fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Validation fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
    style Python fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: HTTPException
steps.explanation: Raise **HTTPException** to return error responses. Set status code, detail message, and optional headers. FastAPI converts it to JSON response.

steps.title: Custom Exception Classes
steps.explanation: Create **custom exception classes** for domain-specific errors. They can carry extra data. Register handlers to format responses.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Exception["class ItemNotFoundError(Exception):\n  def __init__(self, item_id: int):\n    self.item_id = item_id"]
    
    Raise["# In handler:\nraise ItemNotFoundError(item_id=42)"]
    
    Handler["# Needs custom handler\n# (next slide)"]
    
    Exception --> Raise
    Raise --> Handler
    
    style Exception fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Raise fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Exception Handlers
steps.explanation: Use **@app.exception_handler()** to define custom responses for exception types. The handler receives request and exception, returns a Response.

steps.title: Override Default Handlers
steps.explanation: Override FastAPI's **built-in handlers** for HTTPException or RequestValidationError to customize error response format across your entire API.

steps.title: Validation Error Details
steps.explanation: **RequestValidationError** contains detailed error info. Access `.errors()` to get list of validation failures with location, message, and type.

steps.title: Catch-All Handler
steps.explanation: Add a **catch-all handler** for unhandled exceptions. Log the error, return a generic 500 response. Never expose internal errors to clients.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Handler["@app.exception_handler(Exception)
async def catch_all(
  request: Request,
  exc: Exception
):
  logger.error(f'Unhandled: {exc}')
  return JSONResponse(
    status_code=500,
    content={
      'error': 'internal_error',
      'message': 'Something went wrong'
    }
  )"]
    
    Safe["Sensitive info
stays in logs,
not in response"]
    
    Handler --> Safe
    
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Safe fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Starlette HTTPException
steps.explanation: FastAPI's HTTPException extends Starlette's. To catch **both**, handle Starlette's exception. Important when using Starlette middleware that raises exceptions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Starlette["from starlette.exceptions import HTTPException as StarletteHTTPException"]
    
    Handler["@app.exception_handler(StarletteHTTPException)\nasync def http_exception_handler(\n  request: Request,\n  exc: StarletteHTTPException\n):\n  return JSONResponse(\n    status_code=exc.status_code,\n    content={'detail': exc.detail}\n  )"]
    
    Catches["Catches BOTH:\n• FastAPI HTTPException\n• Starlette HTTPException"]
    
    Starlette --> Handler
    Handler --> Catches
    
    style Starlette fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Handler fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Error Response Pattern
steps.explanation: Define a **consistent error format** across your API. Include error code, message, and optional details. Document it in OpenAPI.

fields.title: OpenAPI Auto-Documentation
fields.description: How Swagger/ReDoc documentation is generated from your code

steps.title: Automatic Documentation
steps.explanation: FastAPI **automatically generates** OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation from your code. Type hints, docstrings, and decorators become API docs. Zero extra work.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    Code["Your Code\n• Type hints\n• Pydantic models\n• Decorators\n• Docstrings"]
    
    OpenAPI["OpenAPI Schema\n/openapi.json"]
    
    Swagger["Swagger UI\n/docs"]
    ReDoc["ReDoc\n/redoc"]
    
    Code --> OpenAPI
    OpenAPI --> Swagger
    OpenAPI --> ReDoc
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style OpenAPI fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Swagger fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style ReDoc fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Documentation URLs
steps.explanation: Three documentation endpoints are available by default: **/docs** (Swagger UI), **/redoc** (ReDoc), and **/openapi.json** (raw schema).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    App["FastAPI app"]
    
    Docs["/docs\nSwagger UI\nInteractive testing"]
    ReDoc["/redoc\nReDoc UI\nClean reading"]
    JSON["/openapi.json\nRaw OpenAPI schema\nFor code generation"]
    
    App --> Docs
    App --> ReDoc
    App --> JSON
    
    style App fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Docs fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style ReDoc fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style JSON fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#7c3aed

steps.title: API Metadata
steps.explanation: Configure **API metadata** in the FastAPI constructor: title, description, version, terms of service, contact, license. Appears in docs header.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["app = FastAPI(
  title='My API',
  description='A cool API',
  version='1.0.0',
  contact={
    'name': 'Support',
    'email': 'help@api.com'
  },
  license_info={
    'name': 'MIT'
  }
)"]
    
    Header["Swagger UI Header:
My API v1.0.0
A cool API
Support: help@api.com"]
    
    Code --> Header
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Header fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Operation Documentation
steps.explanation: Document individual endpoints with **docstrings** (description), **summary**, **response_description**, and **operation_id**. All appear in OpenAPI spec.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.get(
  '/users/{id}',
  summary='Get user by ID',
  response_description='The user object'
)
async def get_user(id: int):
  '''
  Retrieve a single user by their ID.

  - **id**: The unique user identifier
  '''
  return user"]
    
    Docs["Swagger shows:
GET /users/{id}
'Get user by ID'

Description from docstring
Response description shown"]
    
    Code --> Docs
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Docs fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Tags for Grouping
steps.explanation: Use **tags** to organize endpoints into groups. Define tag metadata for descriptions. Routes appear grouped in Swagger sidebar.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Tags["app = FastAPI()

tags_metadata = [
  {
    'name': 'Users',
    'description': 'User management'
  },
  {
    'name': 'Items',
    'description': 'Item operations'
  }
]

app.openapi_tags = tags_metadata"]
    
    Routes["@app.get('/users', tags=['Users'])
@app.get('/items', tags=['Items'])"]
    
    Swagger["▼ Users
  GET /users
  POST /users
▼ Items
  GET /items"]
    
    Tags --> Routes
    Routes --> Swagger
    
    style Tags fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Routes fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Swagger fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Response Documentation
steps.explanation: Document **multiple response types** using the `responses` parameter. Define status codes, descriptions, and response models.

steps.title: Schema Examples
steps.explanation: Add **examples** to Pydantic models or individual fields. Examples appear in Swagger UI request/response bodies.

steps.title: Deprecating Endpoints
steps.explanation: Mark endpoints as **deprecated** to indicate they'll be removed. Shows strikethrough in docs. Clients can still use them.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.get(\n  '/old-endpoint',\n  deprecated=True,\n  summary='Use /new-endpoint instead'\n)\nasync def old_endpoint():\n  return {'msg': 'Still works'}"]
    
    Swagger["~~GET /old-endpoint~~\n(strikethrough)\n'Use /new-endpoint instead'"]
    
    Code --> Swagger
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Swagger fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Custom OpenAPI Schema
steps.explanation: Override **openapi()** method to customize the generated schema. Add security schemes, servers, or modify any part of the spec.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["def custom_openapi():
  if app.openapi_schema:
    return app.openapi_schema
  schema = get_openapi(
    title='My API',
    version='1.0.0',
    routes=app.routes
  )
  # Customize schema here
  schema['servers'] = [
    {'url': 'https://api.example.com'}
  ]
  app.openapi_schema = schema
  return schema

app.openapi = custom_openapi"]
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

fields.title: Database Patterns
fields.description: SQLAlchemy integration, async sessions, and dependency injection for databases

steps.title: Database Options
steps.explanation: FastAPI works with any database. Common choices: **SQLAlchemy** (ORM), **databases** (async), **SQLModel** (FastAPI creator's ORM), or raw drivers (asyncpg, motor).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    FastAPI["FastAPI"]
    SQLAlchemy["SQLAlchemy\n(sync + async)"]
    SQLModel["SQLModel\n(Pydantic + SQLAlchemy)"]
    Databases["databases\n(async query builder)"]
    Raw["Raw drivers\n(asyncpg, motor)"]
    
    FastAPI --> SQLAlchemy
    FastAPI --> SQLModel
    FastAPI --> Databases
    FastAPI --> Raw
    
    style FastAPI fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style SQLAlchemy fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: SQLAlchemy Setup
steps.explanation: Create **engine** and **sessionmaker**. Use `create_engine` for sync, `create_async_engine` for async. Session handles transactions.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Engine["from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker

engine = create_engine(
  'postgresql://user:pass@localhost/db',
  pool_size=5,
  max_overflow=10
)

SessionLocal = sessionmaker(
  bind=engine,
  autocommit=False,
  autoflush=False
)"]
    
    style Engine fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Declarative Models
steps.explanation: Define **SQLAlchemy models** using declarative base. Models map to database tables. Define columns with types and constraints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Base["from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
  pass"]
    
    Model["class User(Base):
  __tablename__ = 'users'

  id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
  email: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(unique=True)
  name: Mapped[str]
  is_active: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(default=True)"]
    
    Base --> Model
    
    style Base fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Model fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Database Dependency
steps.explanation: Create a **get_db dependency** that yields a session. Use `yield` for proper cleanup - session closes after request, even on errors.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Dep["def get_db():
  db = SessionLocal()
  try:
    yield db
  finally:
    db.close()"]
    
    Handler["@app.get('/users')
async def get_users(
  db: Session = Depends(get_db)
):
  return db.query(User).all()"]
    
    Flow["1. Session created
2. Handler uses db
3. Session closed
   (even on error)"]
    
    Dep --> Handler
    Handler --> Flow
    
    style Dep fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Flow fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: CRUD Operations
steps.explanation: Implement **Create, Read, Update, Delete** operations. Use session methods: `add`, `query`, `commit`, `delete`. Return Pydantic models.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Create["# Create\nuser = User(email='a@b.com', name='Alice')\ndb.add(user)\ndb.commit()\ndb.refresh(user)"]
    
    Read["# Read\nuser = db.query(User).filter(\n  User.id == user_id\n).first()"]
    
    Update["# Update\nuser.name = 'Bob'\ndb.commit()"]
    
    Delete["# Delete\ndb.delete(user)\ndb.commit()"]
    
    style Create fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Read fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Update fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706
    style Delete fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Async SQLAlchemy
steps.explanation: For async, use **create_async_engine** and **async_sessionmaker**. Sessions are async context managers. Use `await` for queries.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Engine["from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import (
  create_async_engine,
  async_sessionmaker,
  AsyncSession
)

engine = create_async_engine(
  'postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@host/db'
)

async_session = async_sessionmaker(
  engine, expire_on_commit=False
)"]
    
    Dep["async def get_db():
  async with async_session() as session:
    yield session"]
    
    Engine --> Dep
    
    style Engine fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Dep fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Async Queries
steps.explanation: Async queries use **select()** and **await session.execute()**. Access results with `.scalars()` for ORM objects. Different syntax than sync.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Sync["# Sync
users = db.query(User).filter(
  User.is_active == True
).all()"]
