    Async["# Async
from sqlalchemy import select

result = await session.execute(
  select(User).where(
    User.is_active == True
  )
)
users = result.scalars().all()"]
    
    Sync --> Async
    
    style Sync fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Async fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Pydantic + SQLAlchemy
steps.explanation: Convert between **SQLAlchemy models** and **Pydantic schemas**. Use `model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)` for automatic conversion.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    SQLAlchemy["# SQLAlchemy model
class UserDB(Base):
  id: Mapped[int]
  email: Mapped[str]
  password: Mapped[str]"]
    
    Pydantic["# Pydantic schema
class UserOut(BaseModel):
  model_config = ConfigDict(
    from_attributes=True
  )
  id: int
  email: str
  # password excluded!"]
    
    Convert["# Convert
user_db = db.query(UserDB).first()
user_out = UserOut.model_validate(user_db)"]
    
    SQLAlchemy --> Convert
    Pydantic --> Convert
    
    style SQLAlchemy fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Pydantic fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Convert fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Transactions
steps.explanation: SQLAlchemy sessions **auto-commit on close** by default. Use explicit `commit()` and `rollback()` for control. Handle errors properly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Pattern["async def create_order(
  db: AsyncSession,
  order: OrderCreate
):
  try:
    db_order = Order(**order.dict())
    db.add(db_order)
    
    # Update inventory
    for item in order.items:
      await update_stock(db, item)
    
    await db.commit()
    return db_order
  except Exception:
    await db.rollback()
    raise"]
    
    style Pattern fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

fields.title: CORS Configuration
fields.description: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing setup and common configurations

steps.title: What is CORS?
steps.explanation: **CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)** is a browser security feature. It blocks requests from different origins by default. Your API must explicitly allow cross-origin requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Browser["Browser at\nhttps://app.com"]
    API["API at\nhttps://api.com"]
    
    Request["fetch('https://api.com/data')"]
    Block["CORS Error!\nBlocked by browser"]
    
    Browser --> Request
    Request -->|"Different origin"| Block
    
    Note["Same-origin: OK\nDifferent origin: Needs CORS"]
    
    style Browser fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style API fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Block fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Adding CORSMiddleware
steps.explanation: Use **CORSMiddleware** to handle CORS. Configure allowed origins, methods, headers, and credentials. Add it early in middleware stack.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
  CORSMiddleware,
  allow_origins=['https://app.com'],
  allow_credentials=True,
  allow_methods=['*'],
  allow_headers=['*'],
)"]
    
    Result["API now accepts requests
from https://app.com"]
    
    Code --> Result
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Result fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Preflight Requests
steps.explanation: For complex requests, browsers send a **preflight OPTIONS request** first. CORS middleware handles this automatically, responding with allowed methods/headers.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Browser["Browser"]
    Preflight["OPTIONS /api/data\nOrigin: https://app.com\nAccess-Control-Request-Method: POST"]
    Response["200 OK\nAccess-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.com\nAccess-Control-Allow-Methods: POST, GET"]
    Actual["POST /api/data\n(actual request)"]
    
    Browser --> Preflight
    Preflight --> Response
    Response --> Actual
    
    style Preflight fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Response fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Actual fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Allow All Origins
steps.explanation: Use `allow_origins=['*']` to allow **any origin**. Simple but less secure. Cannot use with `allow_credentials=True`.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    AllOrigins["allow_origins=['*']"]
    
    Any["Allows:\nhttps://good-site.com ✓\nhttps://evil-site.com ✓\nhttp://localhost ✓"]
    
    Warning["⚠️ Cannot combine with\nallow_credentials=True"]
    
    AllOrigins --> Any
    AllOrigins --> Warning
    
    style AllOrigins fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Warning fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Specific Origins
steps.explanation: List **specific allowed origins** for production. Include protocol and port. Each origin is checked exactly.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["allow_origins=[\n  'https://app.example.com',\n  'https://admin.example.com',\n  'http://localhost:3000',  # dev\n]"]
    
    Allowed["✓ https://app.example.com\n✓ https://admin.example.com\n✓ http://localhost:3000"]
    
    Blocked["✗ https://evil.com\n✗ https://app.example.com:8080\n✗ http://app.example.com"]
    
    Code --> Allowed
    Code --> Blocked
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Allowed fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Blocked fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Origin Regex
steps.explanation: Use **allow_origin_regex** for pattern matching. Useful for subdomains or dynamic origins. Regex is matched against full origin URL.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["app.add_middleware(\n  CORSMiddleware,\n  allow_origin_regex=r'https://.*\.example\.com',\n  allow_methods=['*'],\n)"]
    
    Matches["Matches:\n✓ https://app.example.com\n✓ https://api.example.com\n✓ https://dev.staging.example.com"]
    
    Code --> Matches
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Matches fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Credentials
steps.explanation: Set **allow_credentials=True** to allow cookies/auth headers. Requires specific origins (not `*`). Browser sends credentials with requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["app.add_middleware(\n  CORSMiddleware,\n  allow_origins=['https://app.com'],\n  allow_credentials=True,  # ✓\n)"]
    
    JS["// Frontend JS\nfetch('/api/data', {\n  credentials: 'include'  // send cookies\n})"]
    
    Works["Cookies and\nAuth headers\nsent with request"]
    
    Code --> JS
    JS --> Works
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style JS fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Works fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Exposed Headers
steps.explanation: By default, browsers hide most response headers. Use **expose_headers** to make custom headers accessible to JavaScript.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["app.add_middleware(
  CORSMiddleware,
  allow_origins=['*'],
  expose_headers=[
    'X-Request-ID',
    'X-Total-Count',
    'X-Page-Number'
  ]
)"]
    
    JS["// Frontend can access:
response.headers.get('X-Request-ID')
response.headers.get('X-Total-Count')"]
    
    Code --> JS
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style JS fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Common Configuration
steps.explanation: A **production-ready CORS configuration** with specific origins, credentials, and reasonable defaults.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["# Production config
origins = [
  os.getenv('FRONTEND_URL'),
  'https://app.example.com',
]

app.add_middleware(
  CORSMiddleware,
  allow_origins=origins,
  allow_credentials=True,
  allow_methods=['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],
  allow_headers=['Authorization', 'Content-Type'],
  expose_headers=['X-Request-ID'],
  max_age=600,  # cache preflight 10 min
)"]
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

fields.title: Async vs Sync Endpoints
fields.description: When to use async def vs def and how FastAPI handles each

steps.title: Two Types of Endpoints
steps.explanation: FastAPI supports both **async def** (runs on event loop) and regular **def** (runs in thread pool). Choose based on what your endpoint does.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Async["async def endpoint()"]
        A1["Runs on event loop"]
        A2["Non-blocking I/O"]
        A3["Use with await"]
    end
    
    subgraph Sync["def endpoint()"]
        S1["Runs in thread pool"]
        S2["Blocking is OK"]
        S3["No await needed"]
    end
    
    style Async fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Sync fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: When to Use Async
steps.explanation: Use **async def** when calling async libraries: aiohttp, asyncpg, motor, aioredis, httpx (async mode). The event loop handles concurrency.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Async["Use async def when:"]
    
    DB["Async database\n(asyncpg, motor)"]
    HTTP["Async HTTP\n(httpx, aiohttp)"]
    Redis["Async cache\n(aioredis)"]
    Files["Async files\n(aiofiles)"]
    
    Async --> DB
    Async --> HTTP
    Async --> Redis
    Async --> Files
    
    Example["async def get_data():\n  data = await httpx.get(url)\n  return data"]
    
    style Async fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Example fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: When to Use Sync
steps.explanation: Use **def** when calling blocking/sync libraries: SQLAlchemy (sync), requests, file operations, CPU-bound work. FastAPI runs it in a thread pool.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Sync["Use def when:"]
    
    DB["Sync database\n(SQLAlchemy sync)"]
    HTTP["Sync HTTP\n(requests)"]
    CPU["CPU-bound\n(pandas, numpy)"]
    Files["Regular file I/O"]
    
    Sync --> DB
    Sync --> HTTP
    Sync --> CPU
    Sync --> Files
    
    Example["def process_data():\n  df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')\n  return df.to_dict()"]
    
    style Sync fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Example fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: How FastAPI Handles Sync
steps.explanation: For **def** endpoints, FastAPI runs them in an **external thread pool**. This prevents blocking the event loop while still allowing concurrent requests.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Request1["Request 1"]
    Request2["Request 2"]
    EventLoop["Event Loop"]
    ThreadPool["Thread Pool"]
    T1["Thread 1\nhandles req 1"]
    T2["Thread 2\nhandles req 2"]
    
    Request1 --> EventLoop
    Request2 --> EventLoop
    EventLoop --> ThreadPool
    ThreadPool --> T1
    ThreadPool --> T2
    
    style EventLoop fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style ThreadPool fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: The Blocking Problem
steps.explanation: Never use **blocking calls in async def**! They freeze the entire event loop. All other requests wait until the blocking call completes.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Bad["# BAD - blocks event loop!\nasync def bad_endpoint():\n  time.sleep(10)  # BLOCKS!\n  data = requests.get(url)  # BLOCKS!\n  return data"]
    
    Frozen["Event loop FROZEN\nAll requests wait 10+ seconds"]
    
    Bad --> Frozen
    
    style Bad fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:3px
    style Frozen fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: Correct Patterns
steps.explanation: Match your function type to your I/O type. **Async I/O → async def**. **Sync I/O → def**. Never mix blocking calls with async def.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Good["✓ Correct"]
        G1["async def + await httpx.get()"]
        G2["def + requests.get()"]
        G3["async def + await asyncpg.fetch()"]
        G4["def + sqlalchemy.query()"]
    end
    
    subgraph Bad["✗ Wrong"]
        B1["async def + requests.get()"]
        B2["async def + time.sleep()"]
    end
    
    style Good fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style Bad fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626

steps.title: run_in_threadpool
steps.explanation: If you must call sync code from async, use **run_in_threadpool**. It runs the sync function in a thread, returning a coroutine you can await.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["from starlette.concurrency import run_in_threadpool

async def endpoint():
  # Run sync function in thread pool
  result = await run_in_threadpool(
    sync_heavy_function,
    arg1,
    arg2
  )
  return result"]
    
    How["Sync function runs in thread
Event loop stays responsive"]
    
    Code --> How
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style How fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Performance Comparison
steps.explanation: **Async** is more efficient for I/O-bound work (handles thousands of concurrent connections). **Sync** is fine for CPU-bound or simple endpoints.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    subgraph AsyncPerf["Async Performance"]
        A1["Single thread\nThousands of connections"]
        A2["No thread overhead"]
        A3["Best for I/O-bound"]
    end
    
    subgraph SyncPerf["Sync Performance"]
        S1["Thread per request"]
        S2["Limited by thread pool"]
        S3["Fine for CPU-bound"]
    end
    
    style AsyncPerf fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style SyncPerf fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Decision Guide
steps.explanation: Quick decision guide: **async def** for async I/O and high concurrency. **def** for sync libraries, CPU work, or when unsure (safer default).
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Start["What does your endpoint do?"]
    
    AsyncIO["Calls async libraries?\n(httpx, asyncpg)"]
    SyncIO["Calls sync libraries?\n(requests, SQLAlchemy sync)"]
    CPU["CPU-bound work?\n(pandas, ML inference)"]
    Simple["Simple logic?\nNo I/O?"]
    
    UseAsync["Use async def"]
    UseSync["Use def"]
    
    Start --> AsyncIO
    Start --> SyncIO
    Start --> CPU
    Start --> Simple
    
    AsyncIO -->|"Yes"| UseAsync
    SyncIO -->|"Yes"| UseSync
    CPU -->|"Yes"| UseSync
    Simple -->|"Either works"| UseSync
    
    style UseAsync fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style UseSync fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

fields.title: Response Streaming
fields.description: StreamingResponse, file downloads, and Server-Sent Events

steps.title: Why Streaming?
steps.explanation: **Streaming** sends response data in chunks instead of all at once. Essential for large files, real-time data, and memory efficiency. Client receives data progressively.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Normal["Normal Response"]
        N1["Server builds\nentire response"]
        N2["Send all at once"]
        N3["Client waits"]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3
    end
    
    subgraph Stream["Streaming Response"]
        S1["Send chunk 1"]
        S2["Send chunk 2"]
        S3["Send chunk N"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end
    
    style Normal fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#666
    style Stream fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: StreamingResponse
steps.explanation: Use **StreamingResponse** with a generator function. Yields chunks of data. Set media_type for content type. Memory-efficient for large data.

steps.title: File Downloads
steps.explanation: Stream **large files** without loading into memory. Use `FileResponse` for simple cases, `StreamingResponse` for dynamic content or very large files.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    FileResp["# Simple file download
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse

@app.get('/download/{filename}')
async def download(filename: str):
  return FileResponse(
    path=f'/files/{filename}',
    filename=filename,
    media_type='application/octet-stream'
  )"]
    
    StreamFile["# Stream large file
async def iter_file(path: str):
  async with aiofiles.open(path, 'rb') as f:
    while chunk := await f.read(8192):
      yield chunk"]
    
    style FileResp fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534
    style StreamFile fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
steps.explanation: **SSE** streams events from server to client over HTTP. Perfect for notifications, live updates, progress bars. Client uses EventSource API.

steps.title: SSE Event Format
steps.explanation: SSE has a specific format: **event**, **data**, **id**, **retry** fields. Each message ends with double newline. Data field is required.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Format["# SSE Format
event: message
   # event type (optional)
data: {'text': 'hello'}
   # payload (required)
id: 123
   # event ID (optional)
retry: 5000
   # reconnect ms (optional)

   # double newline = end of event"]
    
    Code["yield f'''event: notification
data: {json.dumps(payload)}
id: {event_id}

'''"]
    
    Format --> Code
    
    style Format fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px

steps.title: Streaming JSON Lines
steps.explanation: Stream **newline-delimited JSON** for large datasets. Each line is a complete JSON object. Common for log streaming and bulk data export.

steps.title: Progress Streaming
steps.explanation: Stream **progress updates** for long-running operations. Client shows progress bar. Useful for file processing, ML training, bulk operations.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["async def process_with_progress():\n  total = 100\n  for i in range(total):\n    await process_item(i)\n    progress = (i + 1) / total * 100\n    yield f'data: {{\"progress\": {progress}}}\\n\\n'\n  yield 'data: {\"status\": \"complete\"}\\n\\n'"]
    
    Client["Progress: [████████░░] 80%"]
    
    Code --> Client
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Client fill:#4ade80,stroke:#166534

steps.title: Headers and Caching
steps.explanation: Set appropriate **headers** for streaming. Disable buffering, set content type, handle caching. Some headers must be set before streaming starts.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["@app.get('/stream')
async def stream():
  return StreamingResponse(
    generate_data(),
    media_type='text/event-stream',
    headers={
      'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
      'Connection': 'keep-alive',
      'X-Accel-Buffering': 'no',  # nginx
    }
  )"]
    
    Notes["• no-cache: Don't cache stream
• keep-alive: Persistent connection
• X-Accel-Buffering: Disable nginx buffering"]
    
    Code --> Notes
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Notes fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#2563eb

steps.title: Error Handling
steps.explanation: Handle **errors in generators** carefully. Once streaming starts, you can't change status code. Send error as data or use background cleanup.
steps.diagram_data:
flowchart TB
    Code["async def safe_generator():
  try:
    async for item in source:
      yield format_item(item)
  except Exception as e:
    # Can't change status code now!
    # Send error as event instead
    yield f'event: error\ndata: {str(e)}\n\n'
  finally:
    # Cleanup resources
    await source.close()"]
    
    Warning["⚠️ Status code set before
first chunk is sent"]
    
    Code --> Warning
    
    style Code fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:3px
    style Warning fill:#f87171,stroke:#dc2626
